title: It’s not just a No 10 in the north, it’s an engine for growth throughout the whole UK | Andy Burnham | The Guardian
description: Power has been too centralised in Britain and tackling that is key to progress and prosperity. This initiative is ambitious: we’re remaking the state, says UK prime minister Andy Burnham
Author: Andy Burnham
Headline: Power has been too centralised in Britain and tackling that is key to progress and prosperity. This initiative is ambitious: we’re remaking the state, says UK prime minister Andy Burnham
Content: Andy Burnham confirming the No 10 plan at his first cabinet meeting, London. 21 July 2026.Photograph: WPA/Getty ImagesView image in fullscreenAndy Burnham confirming the No 10 plan at his first cabinet meeting, London. 21 July 2026.Photograph: WPA/Getty ImagesOpinionAndy BurnhamIt’s not just a No 10 in the north, it’s an engine for growth throughout the whole UKAndy BurnhamPower has been too centralised in Britain and tackling that is key to progress and prosperity. This initiative is ambitious: we’re remaking the stateAndy Burnham is prime minister of the United KingdomThu 23 Jul 2026 17.30 EDTShareOn Monday, while the cameras were fixed on that famous black door in Downing Street, something important was happening 165 miles away. A small team of officials and advisers were walking into an office building in Manchester to set about the remaking of the British state.This was the beginning ofNo 10 North: the first major act of this government and what I hope will be seen as its most defining.Over the course of the past 40 years, Britain has taken a series of wrong turns. In the 1980s, political power was centralised, economic power was privatised and communities were deindustrialised. Many of the places most affected have not recovered since and are still in decline. They have been unable to turn things around because they simply do not have the necessary power to do so. This feeling of neglect has lain underneath the increasingly fractious politics that has bedevilled Britain in recent times.Solving this problem is crucial for the cohesion of our country. It is something with which it has wrestled for a long time but never got right. We have seen gimmicks, such aslevelling up. We have seen the shifting of bits of the British state around the map to no great effect.Why has none of it worked? It is partly because, even though Whitehall departments have set up outposts, decision-making power has remained in London. It is also because, in the very centre of government itself, Nos 10 and 11 have not been sufficiently aligned – particularly on growth.This is what No 10 North is seeking to correct and, if we succeed, it could bring the much-needed sea change in the way Britain is run. It is about driving growth from the most powerful office in the land and doing so in close alignment with the Treasury. It is about taking power down and placing it in the hands of people who can use it best.No 10 North will be responsible foraccelerating devolution. It will drive reindustrialisation and growth across every postcode of the UK. It will remove barriers to regeneration, from council housebuilding to public transport infrastructure. Crucially, No 10 North will bring the whole of Whitehall together behind places rather than standing in their way. Our long-term ambition is clear: to achieve equivalent living standards across all the regions and nations of the UK.Giving places their own power matters because, without it, they cannot do those things. Growth cannot be imposed from the top down, but instead has to be nurtured from the bottom up. Britain has spent decades trying unsuccessfully to do the former and now, through No 10 North, it is about to give the latter a proper go.People will have heard me talk about wanting the nation to face the same way and pull together. That example has to be set by government. Whitehall departments need to stop fighting turf wars and start pulling together behind the places that desperately need their support.I know change is not always easy and that this is the biggest rebalancing of power our country has seen. When I entered Downing Street, I told staff that the era of fighting devolution is over. No one in government loses when power is shared. When you lift up a place, you lift up Britain.I know the power of devolution is real. A decade ago, I left Westminster to go back home and try to change things for the better. And we did. We brought buses back under public control. We created new opportunities for young people. We built a city-region withtwice the economic growthof anywhere else in the UK. What we did in Greater Manchester, we can do across our nation.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |WeeklySign up toMatters of OpinionGuardian columnists and writers on what they’ve been debating, thinking about, reading, and morePreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionThe Treasury will check Andy Burnham’s ambitions – unless old orthodoxies are challenged | Larry ElliottRead moreTomorrow, from No 10 North, I will chair the first meeting of the National Economic Council, bringing together senior ministers and regional mayors. Through this forum, we will ensure every department in Whitehall gets behind the growth and devolution ambitions of every region.I want Britain to believe again. That means creating a country where everybody feels a part of what is happening and everybody feels heard. This government will demonstrate to everyone that they are part of the story.Later this year, we will bring forward a 10-year plan for Britain, laying out a path from where we are now to where I believe we all want the country to be. Our plan will set out how we bring the essentials in life back under strongerpublic controlto make them affordable again; how we reindustrialise every part of Britain; and how we build more council homes and solve the housing crisis.I know people cannot wait forever to see change. So we will do smaller things too to provide some breathing space. We have already done this with two tax cuts: one onVAT on electricity bills for householdsover winter and another onbusiness rates for pubs, social clubs and live music venues.The new government has shown in its first five days how it is prepared to take the small steps to make life easier now and the big ones to put Britain back on the right long-term path. Both are needed simultaneously if we are to carry people with us.I am under no illusions about how hard this is all going to be, but I have a clear plan and I know how to get things done. And this week has left me in no doubt that the civil service is ready to come on this journey with me too.Andy Burnham is the prime minister of the United KingdomDo you have an opinion on the issues raised in this article? If you would like to submit a response of up to 300 words by email to be considered for publication in ourletterssection, pleaseclick here.Explore more on these topicsAndy BurnhamOpinionDevolutionLabourcommentShareReuse this content

title: On the ground at India's 'Cockroach' protest | India | The Guardian
description: The Guardian’s south Asia correspondent Hannah Ellis-Petersen is on the ground in Delhi, where thousands still thronged the streets, many having travelled hundreds of miles to be there.
Author: Augusta Anthony
Headline: The Guardian’s south Asia correspondent Hannah Ellis-Petersen is on the ground in Delhi, where thousands still thronged the streets, many having travelled hundreds of miles to be there.
Content: On the ground at India's 'Cockroach' protestIndiaOn the ground at India's 'Cockroach' protestThe unprecedented expression of dissent against India's government, headed by the prime minister, Narendra Modi, has shown no sign of abating, as protests by the youth-led “cockroach” protest movement entered its fourth day.The Guardian’s south Asia correspondent Hannah Ellis-Petersen was on the ground in Delhi, where thousands still thronged the streets, many having travelled hundreds of miles to be there.While the Cockroach Janta party has primarily focused on accountability for the devastating recent leaks of exam papers and called for the resignation of the education minister, Dharmendra Pradhan, the overriding feeling as the week develops is that this is now bigger than one issue.For some, this is the moment that India’s youth – totalling more than 600 million – found its political voice.Hannah Ellis-Petersen,Tom Stevens, Ahmer Khan andAugusta AnthonyThu 23 Jul 2026 12.27 EDTShareExplore more on these topicsIndia

title: How to Launder Money But Not Like Me, by George Cottrell (as imagined by John Crace) | John Crace | The Guardian
description: The Guardian’s parliamentary sketch writer imagines Nigel Farage’s right-hand man’s thinking behind his book, How to Launder Money
Author: John Crace
Headline: The Guardian’s parliamentary sketch writer imagines Nigel Farage’s right-hand man’s thinking behind his book, How to Launder Money
Content: George Cottrell launches his book, How to Launder Money.Photograph: X/Biteback PublishingView image in fullscreenGeorge Cottrell launches his book, How to Launder Money.Photograph: X/Biteback PublishingThe politics sketchUK newsHow to Launder Money But Not Like Me, by George Cottrell (as imagined by John Crace)John CraceThe Guardian’s parliamentary sketch writer imagines Nigel Farage’s right-hand man’s thinking behind his book, How to Launder MoneyThu 23 Jul 2026 10.38 EDTLast modified on Thu 23 Jul 2026 21.30 EDTShareAnti-money-laundering laws are supposed to stop the illicit flow of money. So far, they seem to have stopped next to nothing. About 5% of the global economy is illicit and only 0.05% is stopped by AML laws.Which makes me, George, something of a rarity. The idiot member of the 1% who actually got done for conspiring to launder money. Just think, while 99% of criminals were getting away with it, I had the bright idea of telling an audience of undercover members of the US Internal Revenue Service in Las Vegas how I could clean up their drug money for them. Much to my surprise, they didn’t take me up on my offer. I was arrested on money-laundering charges at Chicago airport. Still, I did a plea bargain and the charges were reduced to wire fraud and I did eight months in prison. So no harm done.Anyway, that’s all water under the bridge. And in my book How to Launder Money I tell you how you can get away with it by not making the same mistakes as me.Er, excuse me, co-author Lawrence here. I thought we were supposed to be writing a book to inform the authorities on how to prevent money laundering.Gosh, sorry Lozza. There seems to have been a misunderstanding. Of course. We’re all definitely against money laundering. I’m all for cleaning up the global economy. It would be terrible if the system got so opaque that it was nigh on impossible to discover how political parties were being funded. Not that it would be illegal. Obvs.First, we need to think of the nature of money. Money is something you can spend onthings. Not many people realise that. In my time I have spent money on lots of things. Like renting a house near Buckingham Palace that I can make available toNigel Farage– or Daddy, as I choose to call him – whenever he needs somewhere to stay. I like having lots of money. The key to having a small fortune, as my friend Dicky Tice likes to remind me, is to start with a large one.We should also be clear that there is a big difference between money laundering and grifting. Grifting is entirely legal. That is what Daddy does when he offers to make personalised Cameo videos for £80. As he often likes to say, every little counts. Of course, the best money is the money you get for doing nothing.Or in Daddy’s case, a Thai crypto billionaire who coincidentally gifts you £5m just a short while after you have said you need £1m a year for the next five years if you are going to stand for parliament. Any suggestion that Daddy might owe income tax on this should be referred to his accountants. And it’s obviously deeply distressing to hear the £5m wasreported to the National Crime Agency (NCA) over money laundering concerns.Anyway, back to me. Here are several hundred pages of really boring anecdotes of money laundering from Roman times to the present day. Try to look on this book as a cure for insomnia.One easy way to rinse dirty money is to get someone to paint a fake Van Gogh. No one will notice if another picture of some sunflowers comes on to the market. Then you buy it yourself at an international auction house and donate the painting to a museum. Lo and behold you have suddenly transformed a few thousand quid in dirty money into an asset worth tens of millions. Job done. What could possibly go wrong? Another thing that can work well is bribing people. Though you have to make sure the people you are trying to bribe aren’t undercover agents! If you get my drift.Perhaps the best way of cleaning money is to become prime minister. That way you don’t have to worry about going through customs with gold ingots or large bundles of high denomination notes. Instead you can just walk on to the private jet with a couple of suitcases and hand them over to Vladimir Putin during a bilateral meeting at which no special advisers are present. With the right person in No 10, the UK could become the world centre of illegal finance. Just think of the boost that could give to our economy.Of all cryptocurrencies, 99.9% are an outright scam. But don’t let that bother you. Just buy the ones that Daddy recommends as they are obviously legit because they are being run by Kwasi Kwarteng. And don’t think that any monies being donated toReform UKare dodgy just because they are subject to a Suspicious Activity Report to the NCA.Make no mistake, no one would be more horrified than me to discover that money from overseas – Montenegro, say – had been used to fund a British political party.The desire to be loved is a natural human emotion. I long for Daddy’s attention and was thrilled when he allowed me to answer his emails even when I definitely had nothing to do with the day-to-day activities of the Brexit party and Reform. But sometimes it is best to seek anonymity in your financial dealings, regardless of whether your activities are illegal or not. That’s why when I was offering to launder drug money in Vegas I went under the pseudonym Graham Cottrell. No one would ever think of linking that name with my own.Everything else is quite straightforward. Buy some houses via shell and offshore companies – not that there is anything wrong with these – and leave a trail that is too complicated for anyone to bother to trace. Best of all, you can place bets on the crypto polymarket that you know you are going to lose. That way your illegal money instantly disappears. And the Feds never suspect you of deliberately squandering your assets. This also works well with making legal money disappear. I recently lost £1m on the date of Keir Starmer’s resignation and Donald Trump bombing Iran.So that’s it. All you need to know to get your criminal activities up and running. I mean, for the authorities to clamp down on money laundering. Thank God my own financial affairs are now so transparent. Happy washing day everyone.Explore more on these topicsUK newsThe politics sketchReform UKNigel FaragecommentShareReuse this content

title: The roaring 20s: how the current decade revolutionised cinema | Movies | The Guardian
description: Pre-Covid, the movie landscape was dramatically different: YouTubers weren’t dominating the box office, video game spin-offs rarely made an impact and Winnie-the-Pooh wasn’t yet the star of a slasher movie. Why such a flurry of innovation – and what will be next?
Author: Stuart Heritage
Headline: Pre-Covid, the movie landscape was dramatically different: YouTubers weren’t dominating the box office, video game spin-offs rarely made an impact and Winnie-the-Pooh wasn’t yet the star of a slasher movie. Why such a flurry of innovation – and what will be next?
Content: Horror adaptation … David Howard Thornton as Steamboat Willie in Screamboat.Photograph: SKLAE/Signature EntertainmentView image in fullscreenHorror adaptation … David Howard Thornton as Steamboat Willie in Screamboat.Photograph: SKLAE/Signature EntertainmentMoviesThe roaring 20s: how the current decade revolutionised cinemaPre-Covid, the movie landscape was dramatically different: YouTubers weren’t dominating the box office, video game spin-offs rarely made an impact and Winnie-the-Pooh wasn’t yet the star of a slasher movie. Why such a flurry of innovation – and what will be next?Stuart HeritageThu 23 Jul 2026 11.00 EDTLast modified on Thu 23 Jul 2026 12.21 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleCinema has always been a medium of research and development. At any given point, the industry is searching for yet another breakthrough that can drive it into the future. Synchronised sound in the 1920s. Technicolor in the 1930s. The invention of the modern blockbuster in the 1970s.Conversely, the consensus is that, if the 2020s has a defining trend, then it is one of decline. The theatrical experience is under attack from all angles: streaming,YouTube, a once-in-a-century health crisis that shut cinemas down entirely. And yet, if you look closer, this has actually been one of the most innovative decades that cinema has ever seen. Whether down to technology or desperation, the film industry has spent these last few years figuring out new ways to make films that simply could not have existed even at the turn of the decade.Currently, the most notable of these is the way that cinema has become a conversation with YouTube. Back in 2005, when Jawed Karim uploaded the first video (the 19-second Me at the Zoo) to the platform, YouTube felt like a novelty. It was cheap and cheerful, a collection of cat videos and people falling over. How things have changed. Not only has it become the largest streaming platform in the world, but the first generation of adults who grew up with YouTube has come of age.View image in fullscreenCreepypasta comes good … Chiwetel Ejiofor in Backrooms.Photograph: A24/APAs we speak, the seventh and eighth biggest films of 2026 have been directed by people who learned their trade making content for YouTube. Obsession (worldwide gross: $442m) was directed by Curry Barker, a 26-year-old who dropped out of film school to make YouTube videos and TikToks. Meanwhile, Backrooms (worldwide gross: $384m) is even more explicitly internet-inspired. Directed by the then 20-year-old Kane Parsons, the film is based on a series of shorts he began uploading to YouTube in 2022, which in turn were inspired by somecreepypasta(web-based horror content) that has been floating around online since 2019.A decade ago, studios would have got this disastrously wrong. Ask them to adapt a YouTube video and they would have hired Michael Bay and Rihanna to makeDavid After Dentist: The Motion Picture; it would have baffled anyone who didn’t watch YouTube – and appalled anyone who did. This is the first time that the industry has trusted natives to make the films they want to make. Clearly, given the enormous financial returns, it will not be the last. Steven Spielberg is now producingAlex Kister’s The Mandela Catalogue, based on a terrifying analogue horror series that has run on YouTube since 2021.This would also be a good time to mention the rise of the copyright expiry movie. In 1998, the Copyright Term Extension Act was passed in the US, which extended protection to works to 95 years after they were first published. (In the UK, the rules are different; copyright extends to 70 years after the creator’s death.) This effectively meant that two decades went by without anything going out of copyright. When things finally caught up in 2019, freeing up anything made in 1923, there was a goldrush.View image in fullscreenRuining your childhood … Winnie-the-Pooh: Blood and Honey 2.Photograph: Publicity imageNow, let it be said that it wasn’t a particularlygoodgoldrush. First out of the blocks was 2023’sWinnie-the-Pooh: Blood and Honey, a low-rent slasher movie whose only original thought was to have Winnie-the-Pooh in it. Then cameThe Mouse TrapandScreamboat(both based on Steamboat Willie, the first element of the Mickey Mouse characterto have lost copyright protection), along with Popeye the Slayer Man andBambi: The Reckoning. Without exception, these films are cheap, exploitative and burn out their premise long before the end credits – but more are to come.Next year, if you can wait that long, will see the release of something calledPoohniverse: Monsters Assemble, which will star – deep breath – Winnie-the-Pooh, Tigger, Bambi, Peter Pan, Tinker Bell, Pinocchio, Sleeping Beauty and the Cheshire Cat. It is also worth pointing out that King Kong enters the public domain in 2029, which will be brilliant news for anyone who has been longing to see a low-rent horror movie starring an unusually large gorilla.Now, here is where I may lose you, because I am about to name Sonic the Hedgehog as the most important movie of the decade. Please bear with me. One of the most pervasive trends of this decade has been the way that, for good or ill, audiences have hijacked movies to their own ends. The biggest example is obviouslyBarbenheimer, in which Barbie and Oppenheimer were released on the same day, kickstarting a phenomenon where people bought tickets to see one straight after the other. Nobody in the film industry expected this, but the publicity from this grassroots celebration drove receipts of both films through the roof.View image in fullscreenDoomed by irony … Morbius.Photograph: Landmark Media/AlamyThe flipside to this isMorbius, a terrible film that inspired no end of ironic online mockery – but what was hilarious was that Sony mistook the irony for sincere support, andrereleased the film on 1,000 screensto cash in. It goes without saying that nobody watched it. Also, think of all the extra teenagers who bought tickets for Minions: The Rise of Gru, purely so they could watch dressed in suits and babble nonsense to participate in theGentleminions trend, or the people who went to see A Minecraft Movie exclusively to scream and chuck stuff about whenJack Black said the words “chicken jockey”.No film, arguably, has benefited from fan reaction quite like Sonic the Hedgehog. The first we saw of it was anightmarish trailerfeaturing an unrecognisable main character. Sonic had bizarrely human proportions, as well as tiny eyes and teeth that looked as if they had been stolen from someone’s mouth while they slept. It was awful, and the internet wasted no time in saying that it was awful. In times gone by, studios would have simply taken the hit and released the film as quietly as possible, writing it off as another godawful video game adaptation destined to die on impact. But this time, producers listened. The trailer was released on Tuesday. By Thursday, the director had tweeted that the character would be completely redesigned. Six months after that,New Sonic was unveiled, and fans were delighted.View image in fullscreen‘Teeth stolen from someone’s sleeping mouth’ … a still from the first Sonic the Hedgehog trailer.Photograph: Paramount PicturesWith a character that people could actually stand to look at, Sonic the Hedgehog went on to become the sixth biggest film of 2020. Nobody saw this coming. Until Sonic, video game adaptations were universally written off as the lowest form of cinema. Think of thedismal live action Super Mario Bros movie, or Mark Wahlberg’sbaffling Max Payne, or Uwe Boll’s irredeemablePostal. About 46 English language video game adaptations were released between 1993 and 2019, and they made a combined total of $5.4bn. But A Minecraft Movie made close to a $1bn on its own. The animated Super Mario Movie released in 2023 ended the year second only to Barbie. At time of writing,The Super Mario Galaxy Movie is the biggest film of 2026so far worldwide. By demonstrating to other film-makers how to give people what they actually want to see, Sonic has turned the video game adaptation into a licence to print money.As you will agree, this makesSonic the Hedgehogby far the most important film of the decade. And the future will only bear this out further, once Mr Beast signs on to direct Sonic the Hedgehog 5 and someone makes a rubbish horror film about a blue hedgehog, as soon as the rights lapse in 2087.Explore more on these topicsFilmHorror filmsSonic the HedgehogBackroomsYouTubeGamesfeaturesShareReuse this content

title: ​’Ian Curtis didn’t have a chance’: the Durutti Column on departed friends, Factory Records and fun with Morrissey | Pop and rock | The Guardian
description: As they release their first album in 16 years, the Vini Reilly-fronted band answer your questions on sandpaper record sleeves, having Harry Styles as a fan and inventing a new style of guitar music
Author: Dave Simpson
Headline: As they release their first album in 16 years, the Vini Reilly-fronted band answer your questions on sandpaper record sleeves, having Harry Styles as a fan and inventing a new style of guitar music
Content: ‘Asked what kind of music we played, I piped up: “Avant garde jazz classical.” It was a joke, but it stuck’ … Vini Reilly, centre, with producer Keir Stewart, left, and drummer Bruce Mitchell.Photograph: Michael SteffView image in fullscreen‘Asked what kind of music we played, I piped up: “Avant garde jazz classical.” It was a joke, but it stuck’ … Vini Reilly, centre, with producer Keir Stewart, left, and drummer Bruce Mitchell.Photograph: Michael SteffFilm & Music reader interviewPop and rockInterview​’Ian Curtis didn’t have a chance’: the Durutti Column on departed friends, Factory Records and fun with MorrisseyAs told toDave SimpsonAs they release their first album in 16 years, the Vini Reilly-fronted band answer your questions on sandpaper record sleeves, having Harry Styles as a fan and inventing a new style of guitar musicThu 23 Jul 2026 11.16 EDTLast modified on Thu 23 Jul 2026 11.26 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleVini, you’ve created some of the most beautiful, ethereal music of the last 40 years. Is it true that in your youth you were in a violent Manchester street gang?Songy24Vini Reilly [guitars, vocals, keyboards]:Gangs then had a different meaning. It was just “their lot” and “our lot”. We’d sling insults at each other. Then you’d play football in the park and realise you were playing with the local thugs. They were actually very funny, and I could have fun with them because I was so little. At the time I’d been going through a recurring depressive illness and felt I didn’t fit in at home, so I left without any clean clothing. I wanted to end my life so I decided to go to Moss Side, where the gangsters would shoot me. Of course they didn’t. I’d had a very happy childhood until my father died when I was doing my O-levels, but I’ve come to the conclusion that the really bad things gave me more than they could ever take. How can you know happiness until you’ve known deep sadness?View image in fullscreen‘I wanted to create something which wasn’t a recognisable form’ … Reilly in April 2021.Photograph: Michael SteffBruce Mitchell [drums]:Vini does kick off, though, properly battles. We had to section him once, and six people had to hold him down.Legend has it that Joy Division helped cut and glue the sandpaper sheetson to the famous original cover ofThe Return of the Durutti Column. Were you involved?Gomgom71VR:I walked into Factory Records to find Joy Division gluing them all. It was mortifying, so I escaped as quickly as I could.BM:Vini denies this, but Hooky [Peter Hook] always says that Vini quickly stopped gluing them so he didn’t damage his nails. Joy Division had no cash, so they were paid to do it, which they did while watching porno movies. Alan Erasmus at Factory Records had got a load of thrown-away sleeves from an album of steam train noises: you can tell it’s an original edition because if you peel the sandpaper back you’ll find a steam train.Is there a story behind your Blood Orange collaboration, The Field?nobblehobbleKeir Stewart [producer]:Dev Hynes [Blood Orange] has been a fan for a long time, and he sampled [the 1998 track] Sing to Me and made a tune out of it. He asked permission for the sample, and when we met we had this two-hour babble about the Durutti Column and all sorts. Durutti have all these famous covert fans: Stewart Lee, Chris Morris, Max Richter, Richard Ayoade, John Frusciante from the Red Hot Chili Peppers, Chris Martin from Coldplay, AG Cook, Caroline Polachek, loads of artists on the hyperpop scene. It’s great that Vini’s been able to harvest this recognition in his lifetime, but he keeps it all outside his door. He wants to remain unpolluted.VR:I’ve never heard of most of these people, but it’s very nice.Which guitarists did you listen to growing up?TroykatooVR:One of my favourite guitarists was Al Casey, who played with Thomas “Fats” Waller: really sparse melodies and harmonic progressions. I had this notion that whether you went to a big orchestral performance at the Free Trade Hall or a folk club in Sale, you knew exactly what the audience would look like, because people identify with genre. So I wanted to create something that wasn’t a recognisable form. When we were trying to get into Canada, the customs guy asked what kind of music we played and I piped up: “Avant garde jazz classical.” It was a joke, but it stuck.View image in fullscreenDurutti Column play on the TV show The Tube.Photograph: ITV/ShutterstockHow do you feel about the recentFor Vini – A Tribute to the Durutti Columnshow in London?NickJMorganKS:Harry Styles told Radio 1 that the biggest influences on his new album are LCD Soundsystem and the Durutti Column. Then he was asked to curate the Meltdown festival, so we were asked if there was any way Vini could do a show. There wasn’t, so we got the musicians together for the tribute. I played guitar – I can’t play like Vini but put in a lot of practice! When Bruce came on to play drums, everyone was cheering. A promoter’s already asked if we could do a UK tour, so we’ll see.VR:It’s fantastic that they’re doing it, but I couldn’t bear to see it. If Bruce is involved, it’s always right. Whenever we played together we didn’t rehearse, but we had such chemistry he always knew when a song would end. It worked every time.Could you tell us your memories of Ian Curtis and the inspiration behind the song The Missing Boy?PhilshawknightVR:Ian and I were getting to know each other – we both worked for different parts of the civil service. We were planning to do some music together but never told anyone in case people like Mark E Smith called it nepotism. Then I was staying with Tony [Wilson, Factory boss] and Lindsay [Reade, Wilson’s then wife]. I answered the phone and it was Ian. He wasn’t making any sense. He fluctuated from being really positive to the opposite. He was angry that Tony had called him a poet. When I saw all the pills Ian had been prescribed for epilepsy, I knew he didn’t have a chance. Also his marriage was falling apart, he had a little girl … I wasn’t surprised when he killed himself: it’s all there in the songs, but nobody – not even Tony – ever asked him about them. There’s an anger to The Missing Boy because it’s like I’m gritting my teeth.BM:I was living round the corner. Vini said he needed someone to go in the studio with him to record a memorial for someone who had died. He played it for the engineer, then played once more, leaning over to play the grand piano while his guitar went through a space echo. That second take is on the record.Who is Paul inDetail for Paul?UncleheinrichagainVR:A friend I had for a long time who’d been at art school. Really intelligent, good guy. Then he got a job in advertising which went against his socialist principles. One day he just didn’t show up. I miss him. People always assume my songs are named after girlfriends, which they’re not at all. Jacqueline is Bruce’s wife, a fantastic artist. Quite a few are named after my nieces or cousins. Do they feel honoured? Oh no. My family are terribly insulted.What was the best and worst thing about Tony Wilson?dramaaghVR:There wasn’t a worst. The best is that he was a true altruist, and that never changed. Factory was conceived with the money his mother bequeathed to him after her death, and he was determined he was gonna do something out of respect for her. He made loads of recordings possible. A lot are very precious to me, because without him people would have never got to hear them.View image in fullscreenReilly plays Brussels.Photograph: David Corio/RedfernsWithout Mercyis absolutely beautiful – a modern classical classic. How do you rate it among your work?FarmyardManureVR:It was a nightmare to make, but I don’t regret it. Tony said: “Will you do an album forme?” How could I say no? He’d decided that Keats’s poem Without Mercy [La Belle Dame sans Merci] was the same formula as a pop song – boy meets girl, girl dumps boy – and wanted a musical interpretation. I don’t really like it [laughs], but I’m glad that Tony did, because it was a way of trying to thank him.Was the name the Durutti Column deliberately or accidentally misspelt from theSpanishcivilwar anarchist Buenaventura DurrutiandLa Colonna Durruti?somna123VR:The name was Tony’s idea. He felt that since anarchy was the basis of punk, we should go back to that idea. But he decided not to explain that to people, which was great for me because it meant I could waffle on talking absolute nonsense about it. It was probably misspelled by Factory as a mistake, but even in Spain some people think it looks better with one “r”.View image in fullscreenBruce Mitchell and Vini Reilly in Manchester in 2023.Photograph: Christopher Thomond/The Guardian‘I’ve played for 60 years. That’s long enough’: guitar hero Vini Reilly on PTSD, life on the streets and the little girl who saved himRead moreWhat was it like to work onViva Hate with Morrissey,and would you work with him again?deFaoiteKVR:Certainly, because he’s so ridiculously gifted. I remember him talking about the Moors murderers and how he could have been one of those [murdered] kids, which never left him. On the song Late Night, Maudlin Street you really felt that doomy power. But also we never stopped laughing.BM:I dropped Vini off at the studio half-expecting to pick him up again after half an hour, because all the musicians and engineers were eating at this long table, and right at the end on his own was Morrissey. But they got on so well that when I eventually picked him up, Vini said: “Can we take Mozza?” They were nattering in the back seat all the way. It was like listening to two old ladies on Coronation Street.Renascent is released via London Records on 31 JulyExplore more on these topicsPop and rockFilm & Music reader interviewIndieinterviewsShareReuse this content

title: Meet the man whose groundbreaking book is being devoured by the left | Politics | The Guardian
description: Anton Jäger has been lauded for his exceptional ability to make sense of these strange political times – and he’s only 32. The writer and academic explains why we’re living in an age of hyperpolitics
Author: Zoe Williams
Headline: Anton Jäger has been lauded for his exceptional ability to make sense of these strange political times – and he’s only 32. The writer and academic explains why we’re living in an age of hyperpolitics
Content: ‘Even with all the baggage and connotations, I still think the easiest thing is to call myself a Marxist’ … Anton Jäger.Photograph: Fred Debrock/IDView image in fullscreen‘Even with all the baggage and connotations, I still think the easiest thing is to call myself a Marxist’ … Anton Jäger.Photograph: Fred Debrock/IDPoliticsMeet the man whose groundbreaking book is being devoured by the leftAnton Jäger has been lauded for his exceptional ability to make sense of these strange political times – and he’s only 32. The writer and academic explains why we’re living in an age of hyperpoliticsZoe WilliamsThu 23 Jul 2026 05.00 EDTLast modified on Thu 23 Jul 2026 18.29 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on Google‘Iam acutely conscious,” said Andy Burnham outside 10 Downing Street on Monday, “that I am the sixth person in the last 10 years to walk up this street.” It was good to hear him say it because, beneath all the cabinet gossip and rune-reading, there is a feeling of terror about the immediate future and also about ourselves, the electorate. Is this who we are now? Wildly enthusiastic one minute, seething the next; as passionate about the change we want as we are convinced that those who promise it are incapable of enacting it?It is perhaps not surprising that Anton Jäger’s recent book, Hyperpolitics: Extreme Politicization without Political Consequences, is being read by Labour watchers and leftwing thinkers. As the New York Times wrote, it’s “among the best and most dazzling efforts to model the political present in all its maddening strangeness”. Still, I think Jäger was surprised to have his honeymoon interrupted so I could interview him about it. Life comes at everyone quite fast in a hyperpolitical age.Jäger, 32, raised in Brussels by parents who work in the arts (“bohemian class?” I ask, and he says carefully that they are boho “adjacent”), educated at the University of Essex, then Cambridge, is now a lecturer in the history of political thought and theory at University College, Oxford. He has an intense, angular face and a calm, almost self-effacing manner. His philosophical reference points are Deleuze and Guattari, Mouffe and Laclau, but asked to situate himself in the firmament of the European left, he says simply: “Even with all the baggage and connotations, I still think the easiest thing is to call myself a Marxist.”His thinking runs as follows (don’t get hung up on the dates, though some, like 1989, are non-negotiable). The years 1914 to 1989 mark the age of mass politics, when politicisation was high and institutionalisation was also high – so people engaged with politics electorally and on the streets. They were joiners – of political parties, unions, congregations, working men’s clubs, reading groups; they participated in multiple interlacing institutions. Not by coincidence, this was also a time when political struggle resulted in concrete gains. Then, 1989 to the financial crash in 2008 (the years often called the “long 90s”) was the age of post-politics, when institutionalisation was low (we emphatically did not join clubs then, wewentto clubs), and politicisation was through the floor. It was a time when technocrats took care of business while everyone else went to a rave, and we said history was over. From the crash through to about 2020 (sometimes earlier, this boundary is the most fluid) was the age of anti-politics – a resurgent Tea Party in the US, Beppe Grillo and anti-establishment protests in Italy, the Arab spring, the Occupy movement. Politicisation in this period came back with a vengeance, and even while institutionalisation hasn’t recovered, these movements still have institutional ambitions. Protest movements in Spain and Greece bore political parties, Podemos and Syriza; protest candidates in the US, France and the UK, had their eyes on retaking traditional parties from which the revolutionary intent had long been stripped away.View image in fullscreenA Black Lives Matter protest in London on 6 June 2020 after the killing of George Floyd.Photograph: Andy Rain/EPAHyperpolitics is what came next: intense politicisation, with neither institutional roots nor ambitions. “On the surface,” Jäger writes, “the Black Lives Matter protests would seem to have little in common with the mob that stormed the Capitol in January 2021. Morally, they are worlds apart … Organisationally, however, these movements exhibit a striking set of similarities: fleeting in duration, they maintain no membership rolls and struggle to impose any real discipline on their adherents.” As a consequence, everything explodes and nothing changes; movements surge suddenly forward and yet no territory is taken. He quotes political theorist Paolo Gerbaudo’s evocative phrase “bodies without organs”. Modern political movements, he writes, are “concentrated and muscular, yet without an internal metabolism”.He found this pattern across the Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development and he only stopped there, he says, because “I don’t have the linguistic and cultural reach to fully tell this story globally”. His descriptions are powerful. He writes of climate strikers and anti-lockdown demonstrators alike as “a myriad animated by brief, potent stimuli, stirred to action by charismatic influencers and digital demagogues”. And that, you have to admit, is exactly what it looks like.These four eras – mass politics, post-politics, antipolitics and hyperpolitics – “are not just concepts,” Jäger says, “they have some empirical data behind them. ‘How institutionalised is society?’ is basically a question of, how does civil society look? How are membership organisations looking? What are the affiliations, what are the types of bonds that people have? That’s not very difficult to quantify. You can look at union membership, you can look at church attendance, you can look at less overtly political forms of belonging. The political axis is more difficult to measure, but not impossible – namely, how many people attend elections, how many people strike, how many people engage in political violence, how many people engage in political communication, write letters to editors or now post online?”He argues that the height of mass politics was between the wars. To illustrate this in the book, he lists all the civic organisations during the Great Depression in the small Rhenish hamlet of Marienthal – they spanned all human activity, from work to thought to rabbit-breeding. I won’t list them all here but there were many. Considering the effect these organisations might have on the collective psyche, Jäger considers the history of his own family: “On the Flemish side, this meant that the Christian Democratic or the Catholic party was not just a party that said how you were to vote, or which union you joined, but also had a very strong say in what happened in the bedroom. It was a total doctrine, not something that purely applied to your political deportment.” There was little boundary between public and private, and no such thing as a political identity without a membership card. It was personally quite constraining, but it got shit done. (Sidebar, I’ve seen a nascent institutionalised-politics like this, in Manchester, in 2019; the local Momentum group was campaigning for Jeremy Corbyn, but it also had a running club, a knitting group and a book group. I was overcome by the sense of seeing a trade union movement reborn. Needless to say, Corbyn did not win that election. Institution-building can’t be done on the fly.)The destruction of political organisations was “not monocausal”, Jäger says, though in the book he puts it squarely on the shoulders of the union busting by Margaret Thatcher and Ronald Reagan. People lost faith in organisations because the crunchy bit of their leverage – the bit where you get what you want by withdrawing your labour, the bit before all-out war – was taken away. Unions were defanged and workers impoverished, and many ultimately saw their industries collapse in the process.Jäger says there were also cultural and generational shifts over decades – people in the 60s and 70s seeking a different relationship with collective institutions, for example, and looking instead for emancipation and equality – so it takes a while for the picture to cohere, and Communist parties remained tenacious through the 1980s in Italy, for instance. “In 1989, suddenly it’s as if there’s a day and night difference,” he says. With the fall of the Berlin Wall, the end of the cold war, he says: “Suddenly everyone deserves to party.”View image in fullscreenCrowds climb onto the Berlin Wall on the night of 9 November 1989.Photograph: Robert Wallis/Corbis/Getty ImagesIt is a strange sensation to have the 90s narrated back to you by someone who was born in 1994, but it is mainly unsettling because it is so accurate. Tony Blair did describe globalisation as a process as inexorable as the weather, and that is also how neoliberals described the economy – a thing that happened to you. “This is the ambiguity of the post-politics of the 90s,” Jäger says. “Politics is no longer something that you see as effecting large-scale social change, and the forms of liberty that are available to you are now more private, more intimate, more individual. At the same time, the idea that you can collectively bundle together with other individuals and change society starts to appear very, very remote.”There was also a lot of beauty to that time, which he describes through the photography of Wolfgang Tillmans and the novels of Annie Ernaux and Michel Houellebecq. We were obsessed with Houellebecq in the 90s, but we will have to talk about why, and the direction his novels took, and whether we will ever again live in a world whose best cultural artefact is the novel, another time. “The fact that Tillmans as a gay man is able to express and experience the rave culture of the 90s and 00s as a new form of collectivity that wasn’t available to him in the 70s and 80s, I don’t think we should deny the liberatory value of this. It very clearly is the case that there are individuals who did not feel at home in the families, or the communities or the neighbourhoods in which they grew up, in which they finally find a kind of new community that is much less demanding and is less exigent than what they had before. The ambiguity is, of course, that it also implies a profound disenchantment and cynicism around what politics can do.” That’s politics with a capital P – you might see a community animated about an issue, but with no faith in parliamentary channels.View image in fullscreenArtist and photographer Wolfgang Tillmans at his home and studio in Bethnal Green in 2003.Photograph: Antonio Olmos/The ObserverThere is a critical and rarely mentioned adjunct to the neoliberal destruction of institutions and the post and antipolitics that came afterwards, which is that it didn’t only come for the left. “Thatcher basically did a controlled demolition of popular Conservatism,” Jäger says, “and replaced much of this with PR spin, and also a lot of private funding, because the idea was: ‘We can get rid of our mass party because we’re in control of the media, we can win elections with that.’” The right today, he says, is just as inconsequential as the left, and for exactly the same reason, that it’s hyper but not formally organised. “The people who go on marches subscribe to the same podcast, might follow the same pages on Instagram, might be on the same Telegram channels, but they don’t have an organisational history they shared before they entered the march. This is different even to the BNP, where marchers had a shared history in football hooliganism, and the National Front. That shared subculture is no longer there.”He says this is clear with Reform UK, “which is so heavily leader-dependent. The entire movement is at risk with this scandal of the crypto-billionaire, because there’s no party infrastructure. There is just Nigel and a smartphone and then a couple of private donors.” To me, this fails to take into account that the right is different to the left: they have more money. “Maybe I understated the availability of private funding,” he says mildly. “The fact that we can even talk of a trillionaire, such as Elon Musk, means there’s a complete secession of that individual from what we could even call society. That is a variable in explaining why hyperpolitics on the right thrives and why hyperpolitics on the left has found it much more difficult to achieve tangible results.” They are not more organised or more ideologically coherent or more strategic; they are just better funded – which puts quite a lot on the shoulders of capital itself as the overarching organising principle of global politics. Money might not be the only thing talking, but it’s the only thing getting what it wants.View image in fullscreen‘What we’re looking at, with Trump, is “Bonapartism.”’Photograph: Jim Watson/AFP/Getty ImagesThe more immediately controversial proposition of the book, though, is that what we are watching in Trump’s US is not fascism, because the right only travels to that extreme in the face of a direct threat of organised socialism. “The left is doing itself too many favours by considering its opponents fascists,” he says. “You’re overestimating your strength. The contemporary far right is more a function of the crisis of liberalism than it is a function of the strength of the left.” What we’re looking at instead, with Trump, is “Bonapartism” says Jäger. He writes that Trump, and everyone else who has ever got ahead by championing the “left behind”, isn’t representing a strong mass movement or class, but, like Napoleon, relying on “the largely passive support of impoverished peasants … not a collective but ‘formed by the simple addition of isomorphous magnitudes, much as potatoes in a sack form a sack of potatoes’.” (That bit about potatoes is from Karl Marx’s The Eighteenth Brumaire of Louis Bonaparte.) We skirmish a little about the US Immigration and Customs Enforcement as a standing army that looks extremely fascist, until Jäger concludes: “The spectrum from fascism to Bonapartism can flip into something more radical. At the same time, I’m still more struck by the differences than the similarities with these 20th-century phenomena.”Neal Lawson, guru of Manchesterism and an Anton Jäger fan, lays these arguments over the predicament of our new prime minister. “It isn’t just lack of money Burnham has to contend with but the absence of secure political footings. Temporary hyper-political swarms have taken the place of substantial and secure alliances. He must find a way to surf these volatile waves or be another victim of them.” But we, the volatile waves, also have to figure out how to re-engage with institutions and build new ones, and is that possible? Jäger pauses, dispiritingly, like a mechanic who is about to give you bad news about your fanbelt. “This is an interesting and scary question. We do live in a society in which the incentives for leaving rather than entering organisations are much higher. Recent experience, whether joining a party or joining a union, often militates against the idea that there’s any sense in collective action, and the rational response is helplessness. And yet, repoliticisation is good. Hyperpolitics has increased people’s consciousness of the fact that politics matters.”Hyperpolitics by Anton Jäger (Verso Books, £11.99). To support the Guardian, order your copy atguardianbookshop.com. Delivery charges may apply.Explore more on these topicsPoliticsPolitics booksUS politicsEuropefeaturesShareReuse this content

title: Regional mayors to take key roles in UK policy decisions as part of No 10 North plans | Politics | The Guardian
description: Burnham says ‘no one in government loses when power is shared’ as he revives Brown-era national economic council
Author: Pippa Crerar
Headline: Burnham says ‘no one in government loses when power is shared’ as he revives Brown-era national economic council
Content: Then mayor of Greater Manchester Andy Burnham (third from left) and other regional mayors outside No 10 in July 2024.Photograph: ZUMA Press, Inc./AlamyView image in fullscreenThen mayor of Greater Manchester Andy Burnham (third from left) and other regional mayors outside No 10 in July 2024.Photograph: ZUMA Press, Inc./AlamyPoliticsRegional mayors to take key roles in UK policy decisions as part of No 10 North plansBurnham says ‘no one in government loses when power is shared’ as he revives Brown-era national economic councilIt’s not just a No 10 in the north, it’s an engine for growth throughout the whole UK | Andy BurnhamPippa CrerarPolitical editorThu 23 Jul 2026 17.30 EDTLast modified on Thu 23 Jul 2026 18.59 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleRegional mayors are to be given a leading role in the UK’s economic policy,Andy Burnhamhas said, as he called the opening of No 10 North “the biggest rebalancing of power our country has seen”.The new prime minister has revived the national economic council [NEC], first set up byGordon Brownto put the government on a war footing during the 2008 financial crash, arguing that economic growth requires the same level of urgency, focus and collective action.In anarticle for the Guardian, he wrote: “When I entered Downing Street, I told staff that the era of fighting devolution is over. No one in government loses when power is shared.”Burnham will chair the inaugural meeting of the council on Friday, bringing together senior ministers and regional mayors to put devolution at the heart of his government’s mission.Burnham wrote: “Even though Whitehall departments have set up outposts, decision-making power has remained in London … This is what No 10 North is seeking to correct and, if we succeed, it could bring the much-needed sea change in the way Britain is run.“It is about driving growth from the most powerful office in the land and doing so in close alignment with the Treasury. It is about taking power down and placing it in the hands of people who can use it best.”View image in fullscreenHeron House, in central Manchester, will provide an interim HQ for Andy Burnham’s No 10 North.Photograph: Peter Byrne/PAAs he becomes the first British leader to run the government on a weekly basis fromManchester, Burnham will face calls to prove that the operation is not tokenistic.Earlier this week, he created a powerful new prime minister’s department taking in parts of the Cabinet Office, Treasury and housing ministry to become the “dominant driver of the UK’s economy” – an apparent tension with the No 10 North plan.In one of his first acts,Burnham set up the Office for the Prime Minister and the Cabinet, to be led by his ally and enforcerLouise Haigh, which will oversee No 10 North in Manchester, the No 10 operation in London, and parts of the Cabinet Office.Haigh, along with the education secretary, Lucy Powell, and the communities secretary, Angela Rayner – who both haveGreater Manchesterseats – are planning to spend some of their time working from No 10 North, while teams of civil servants are expected to be relocated there.With regional mayors invited to sit round the table, Downing Street argues that devolution will be cemented at the centre of economic decision-making, maintaining the prime minister’s pledge that decisions will be taken with local leaders, not just for them.View image in fullscreenAngela Rayner has returned to her previous role as the housing and communities secretary in Burnham’s cabinet.Photograph: Tolga Akmen/EPAOperating from Heron House in Manchester’s city centre, the home of GCHQ in the north-west, No 10 North is intended to bring together the levers of local economic growth and devolution under one roof for the first time, working from a single plan rather than competing ones.Burnham is expected to work from No 10 North each week so that decisions about the country’s economic future will no longer exclusively be made in Westminster, with other ministers also working regularly from the building.Dave Penman, the general secretary of the FDA union which represents senior civil servants, has welcomed the changes to the structure of government but cautioned that relocation was a big decision for staff.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |Every weekdaySign up toFirst EditionOur morning email breaks down the key stories of the day, telling you what’s happening and why it mattersPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionBusiness rates cut for pubs, clubs and music venues in England only ‘a small move’, say criticsRead moreAt least seven Greater Manchester Combined Authority employees, including Burnham’s deputy chief of staff, Caroline Simpson, and longstanding aide Kevin Lee, are already moving into the temporary building, while a brownfield site is developed. The Heron House office will be led by Sam Lister, a senior civil servant who has worked across several departments.The NEC’s attenders will change at each meeting, depending on the issues under consideration, bringing together business leaders, trade unions and other partners where their expertise can help drive decision-making.Under Gordon Brown, the body met twice a week and acted as the focus of his government’s effort to stave off recession, the global crisis in the financial markets and volatile prices, although there were concerns at the time that it could clip the wings of the Treasury.The new committee was quickly described as an economic version of the Cobra emergency committee. It included key figures such as the then chancellor Alistair Darling and former business secretary Peter Mandelson, and was instrumental in coordinating emergency banking sector bailouts.11:01Gary Lineker calls for Burnham to introduce wealth tax - The LatestIn his article, Burnham suggested that the Manchester department would be crucial to his aim to “drive reindustrialisation and growth across every postcode of the UK”.He went on: “It will remove barriers to regeneration, from council house building to public transport infrastructure. Crucially, No 10 North will bring the whole of Whitehall together behind places rather than standing in their way.“Our long-term ambition is clear: to achieve equivalent living standards across all the regions and nations of the UK.”Explore more on these topicsPoliticsAndy BurnhamManchesterGordon BrownLabourDevolutionLouise HaighnewsShareReuse this content

title: Southport killer Axel Rudakubana moved to Broadmoor psychiatric hospital | Southport attack | The Guardian
description: Exclusive: Clinical assessments concluded Rudakubana could not be safely managed in prison, sources say
Author: Rajeev Syal
Headline: Exclusive: Clinical assessments concluded Rudakubana could not be safely managed in prison, sources say
Content: Rudakubana was moved from Belmarsh prison to Broadmoor high-security hospital (above) this week.Photograph: Neil Hall/ShutterstockView image in fullscreenRudakubana was moved from Belmarsh prison to Broadmoor high-security hospital (above) this week.Photograph: Neil Hall/ShutterstockSouthport attackSouthport killer Axel Rudakubana moved to Broadmoor psychiatric hospitalExclusive: Clinical assessments concluded Rudakubana could not be safely managed in prison, sources sayRajeev SyalHome affairs editorThu 23 Jul 2026 13.51 EDTLast modified on Thu 23 Jul 2026 18.59 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleThe Southport killer Axel Rudakubana has been moved from prison to a psychiatric hospital after clinical assessment, the Guardian can disclose.He was transferred to Broadmoor high-security hospital from the category A prison Belmarsh this week after it was concluded that he could not be managed safely in a prison environment, sources confirmed.People in psychiatric units such as Broadmoor are treated as patients rather than prisoners. A Ministry of Justice spokesperson said he would remain locked up and is unlikely to ever be released.The move is likely to anger many of the families of his victims, who will argue he was assessed as fit to stand trial in January 2025.It means the cost of his incarceration will rise. Costs for patients in hospital secure units are more than £300,000 each a year, compared with about £60,000 for prisoners, Whitehall sources said.Rudakubana, 19, from Banks, Lancashire, was jailed for life in January 2025, with a minimum sentence of 52 years, for the “sadistic” murders of Elsie Dot Stancombe, seven, Bebe King, six, and nine-year-old Alice da Silva Aguiar.View image in fullscreenLeft to right: Elsie Dot Stancombe, seven, Bebe King, six, and Alice da Silva Aguiar, nine.Composite: Merseyside policeHe attacked eight other children, the class instructor Leanne Lucas, and businessman John Hayes, who had tried to disarm him at a Taylor Swift-themed dance class on 29 July 2024 in Southport. He was also found guilty of producing the biological toxin ricin and of possessing an al-Qaida training manual, an offence under the Terrorism Act.Alder Hey children’s NHS foundation trust confirmed Rudakubana had been under its care as a mental health patient between 2019 and 2023, when he “stopped engaging”. He was referred to them after taking a knife to school and later saying he had done so “to use it”.He was also referred to the UK’s counter-extremism programme, Prevent, because he had been researching school massacres on a computer at his next school, the Acorns.There were two other referrals to Prevent, but none were progressed as Rudakubana had no clear ideology behind his obsession with violence.In May 2025, Rudakubana attacked a prison officer with boiling water, sparking an internal inquiry at Belmarsh.View image in fullscreenAn artist’s sketch of Axel Rudakubana appearing via video link during a court appearance.Photograph: Julia Quenzler/ReutersResponding to the move to Broadmoor, Mark Fairhurst, the national chair of the POA union, which represents prison, correctional, and secure psychiatric workers, said his members believed he was “playing the system” so he could move to a more comfortable environment.He said: “[Rudakubana] is not mentally unwell. He is playing the system and is nothing more than evil. He should spend his entire sentence in solitary confinement staring at four walls.“It is appalling to think that he will now enjoy all the freedoms a secure hospital is forced to give him and even more unjustified is the taxpayer actually paying him benefits because he is now classed as mentally unwell.“It is POA members who will now have to carry out the wishes of those who think he can be cured. This is a sickening development and my thoughts are with the families of the victims and all those who have been affected by his atrocious crime.”Broadmoor, in Crowthorne, Berkshire, is the oldest of England’s three high security psychiatric hospitals. It has been used to hold some of the UK’s most notorious murderers, including Peter Sutcliffe, the Yorkshire Ripper.Patients remain locked up, with conditions heavily regulated by strict physical security including locked wards, monitored by staff and 24-hour CCTV. They receive mandatory therapeutic and rehabilitative programmes. If offenders no longer require treatment in hospital, the 1983 Mental Health Act provides for a swift return to prison.If an offender presents an increased risk and cannot be managed safely in a prison environment because of a mental disorder, a request to be moved to a secure psychiatric unit can be made by clinical professionals.Sources said the act and the clinical assessments meant ministers had no choice but to accept the request to transfer the prisoner in this instance.A Ministry of Justice spokesperson said: “The families of those murdered and injured in Southport will be in this country’s heart for ever and our thoughts remain with them.“The offender who carried out these vile crimes will rightly spend the entirety of his 52-year custodial sentence locked up, and is unlikely to ever be released.“High secure hospitals are not places of freedom. He will remain under constant supervision in a high-security psychiatric unit, and be returned to prison as soon as possible.“The transfer of an offender follows a robust independent clinical assessment.”Explore more on these topicsSouthport attackPrisons and probationUK criminal justiceEnglandnewsShareReuse this content

title: Donald Trump says decision on ‘massive attack’ against Iran is close | US-Israel war on Iran | The Guardian
description: President’s remarks follow Houthi strikes on Saudi tankers in the Red Sea and oil prices rising past $100 a barrel again
Author: Peter Beaumont
Headline: President’s remarks follow Houthi strikes on Saudi tankers in the Red Sea and oil prices rising past $100 a barrel again
Content: Donald Trump said: ‘I am considering a massive attack. Bigger than ever before.’Photograph: Julia Demaree Nikhinson/APView image in fullscreenDonald Trump said: ‘I am considering a massive attack. Bigger than ever before.’Photograph: Julia Demaree Nikhinson/APUS-Israel war on IranDonald Trump says decision on ‘massive attack’ against Iran is closePresident’s remarks follow Houthi strikes on Saudi tankers in the Red Sea and oil prices rising past $100 a barrel againPeter BeaumontSenior international correspondentThu 23 Jul 2026 12.58 EDTLast modified on Thu 23 Jul 2026 22.42 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleDonald Trump has said he is close to deciding whether to launch “a massive attack” against Iran, on a scale larger than strikes already witnessed during the months-long conflict with Tehran.Speaking to the Axios news site, the US president said: “I am considering a massive attack. Bigger than ever before. I am close to making a decision. We are all set for it.”Trump made his remarks on Thursday asoil prices once again surged past the symbolic $100for a barrel of Brent crude, the international benchmark, and amid indications the price could go higher still.He acknowledged that such a decision would have consequences but said he had not yet made a final determination. Israel “would join in two minutes if I ask them to”, he added, before saying: “We don’t need anybody.”Hours later the US carried out a 13th consecutive night of strikes onIranlasting more ⁠than two ​hours. Iranian state media reported explosions along the strait of Hormuz in Qeshm and Bandar Abbas, as well as to the north-west of the country and in Tehran.The strikes however appeared to broadly match the scope and intensity of those the US has carried throughout the most recent escalation of violence.Trump’s comments on Thursday followed the earlier threat of “major military punishment” against Iran and the Houthis, after the Iran-backedYemeni militia’s attacks on two Saudi Arabian oil tankersin the Red Sea.Underlining the sense of a region increasingly approaching an abyss, the UN secretary general, António Guterres, told the security council that the Middle East was being pushed to the “edge of the unimaginable” amid mounting fears over the trajectory of the conflict.“The situation is getting out of control. It is teetering on the edge of the unimaginable. The region is being pulled into an ever-widening circle of confrontation. One crisis feeds another. One escalation triggers the next,” Guterres told a security council meeting.“As this dynamic spreads, the political objectives are becoming increasingly obscured by the confrontation itself.”The UK announced it was temporarilywithdrawing diplomatic staff from Iranon Wednesday, and the German military announced on Thursday that it would withdraw two vessels from the Red Sea.And as Israeli cities opened bomb shelters, the US state department warned Americans living in the region that flights may be cancelled.In a separate development, Trump clarified on Thursday that an announced agreement thatthe US would help Saudi Arabia with its civilian nuclear programmewould require the kingdom to normalise relations with Israel through the Abraham Accords, a move Saudi Arabia has long resisted until there is demonstrable movement on Palestinian rights and statehood.It emerged later that Trump had not spoken toSaudi Arabiaabout the new condition.The Houthi attacks threatened the safety of a second major maritime route connecting the Suez canal to the Red Sea and Gulf of Aden. They also came amid reports that Iran had sent military advisers and drone parts to theHouthis10 days ago.Quoting four sources, including two Iranians, Reuters reported on Thursday that Iran had transferred ​Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps personnel and military-related equipment on a flight from Tehran toYemenon 13 July, as Tehran was calling for the Houthis to blockade the Bab el-Mandeb strait.Ships had begun turning back from sailing through the narrow strait this week after the Houthis announced a blockade of Saudi ports, most notably the Red Sea port of Yanbu, which Riyadh has been using to ship oil to Asia during the conflict that has all but halted shipping through the strait of Hormuz.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |Every weekdaySign up toFirst ThingOur US morning briefing breaks down the key stories of the day, telling you what’s happening and why it mattersPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionHas Trump dragged the US into a forever war in Iran? | Mohamad BazziRead moreThe maritime data and analysis firm Lloyd’s List Intelligence said the Houthi attacks put Saudi oil shipments from Yanbu at risk and presented a “double whammy” on top of the Hormuz disruptions, , adding that the Houthi threat “raises questions on the viability of this route”.Trump wrote on social media: “If [the Houthis] do this again, the US will hold Iran responsible, in that the Houthis are a Surrogate and/or Proxy of Iran, and major military punishment will be inflicted upon Iran and, of course, the Houthis, themselves.”The Houthis’ Saba news agency said the rebels had struck two tankers, the Encelia and the Layla, causing fires on both. There were no reports of casualties in their first reported attack on a vessel since they announced the blockade of Saudi-linked shipping.The Bab el-Mandeb is a vital shipping chokepoint connecting the Red Sea to the Gulf of Aden. About 12% of the world’s trade, including a fourth of global container traffic, passes through there, moving between Europe and Asia via the Suez canal at the northern end of the Red Sea.Trump has been escalating his rhetoric against Iran in recent days, hinting the US is prepared to bomb an underground tunnel complex at the “Pickaxe mountain” site where Israel alleges Tehran has hidden centrifuges used in uranium enrichment.As Trump has struggled to get a grip on the spiralling crisis, during which Iran has occasionally launched lethal strikes against US bases and Washington’s Gulf allies, he appears to have been sucked into an “escalation trap”, threatening ever more violent retaliation.On Wednesday, Trump said the US would destroy one bridge or power plant every time Iran shot at a ship in the strait.The US secretary of state, Marco Rubio, said Trump was seeking a “head for an eye” after the recent deaths of several US service members in an Iranian strike on the Muwaffaq Salti airbase in Jordan.However, speaking on the outskirts of the Asean conference in Manila, Rubio said Iran was “begging for a deal”, and insisted “they need to come to their senses” and that they will “pay a very heavy price for the things they are doing” – all claims US officials have made before.With Trump’s poll ratings in freefall, and his war against Iran deeply unpopular at home, the president and his administration are running short of time before the autumn midterm elections to find a resolution to the conflict on Washington’s terms.Underlining Trump’s growing political problems, the US House of Representatives narrowlypassed a resolutionon Thursday to halt US military action in Iran, the second such warning to the president.Explore more on these topicsUS-Israel war on IranHouthisIranDonald TrumpYemenMiddle East and north AfricaTrump administrationnewsShareReuse this content

title: Drought declared in parts of Wales as July on course to be driest in nearly 200 years | Wales | The Guardian
description: Government agency confirms status in north Wales and Upper Severn after series of heatwaves and lack of rainfall
Author: Bethan McKernan
Headline: Government agency confirms status in north Wales and Upper Severn after series of heatwaves and lack of rainfall
Content: A wildfire on Halkyn Mountain in Flintshire this week. The areas in drought have received less than 2% of the expected rainfall for July so far.Photograph: DGDImages/Alamy Live NewsView image in fullscreenA wildfire on Halkyn Mountain in Flintshire this week. The areas in drought have received less than 2% of the expected rainfall for July so far.Photograph: DGDImages/Alamy Live NewsWalesDrought declared in parts of Wales as July on course to be driest in nearly 200 yearsGovernment agency announces status in north Wales and Upper Severn after series of heatwaves and lack of rainfallBethan McKernanandSandra LavilleThu 23 Jul 2026 11.46 EDTLast modified on Thu 23 Jul 2026 18.59 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleParts ofWalesare now in drought and all other areas of the country are experiencing prolonged dry conditions, with this month on course to be the driest on record for nearly 200 years.Natural Resources Wales (NRW) said on Thursday that record-low river flows, back-to-back heatwaves and widespread wildfires have prompted the declaration of drought status in north Wales and the Upper Severn.As of Thursday, several fires were still burning across the country after more than a week, leading the Welsh government toactivate “crisis” plansamid concerns over a lack of firefighting helicopters.‘Unprecedented’ changes in UK climate are normalising extremes, report saysRead moreThe declaration of drought in Wales comes as anew scientific analysisfinds that extreme heat, supercharged by the human-caused climate crisis, is sucking Europe dry in summer and intensifying the drought hitting the continent.Deadly wildfires haveraged through Spain and France, and rivers are running low across Europe. On Monday, the National Drought Group – the members of which include the Met Office, the National Farmers’ Union, water companies and expert bodies –declaredparts of southern, central and eastern England to be in “prolonged dry weather”.The two areas now in drought in Wales have received less than 2% of the expected rainfall for the month so far, and the rivers Clwyd, Alwen, Alyn, Clywedog, Dyfi and Conwy in north Wales, as well as the Wye, have recorded new monthly minimum flows or are approaching record lows.The hot and dry weather over recent weeks means July is on course to be the driest on record in Wales for nearly 200 years, causing problems for fish, wildlife and crops.NRW announced last week that north and south-east Wales were moving to “prolonged dry weather” status, the stage before drought, triggering extra checks on waterways and protected wildlife sites as well as the release of reservoir water into struggling rivers.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |Every weekdaySign up toFirst EditionOur morning email breaks down the key stories of the day, telling you what’s happening and why it mattersPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionHow can I water my garden in a hosepipe ban and will there be drought this summer?Read moreRhian Thomas, the sustainable water and nature manager at NRW, said the hot and dry spell was “having serious impacts on the environment across parts of Wales”, adding: “We have seen rivers fall to exceptionally low levels, groundwater continuing to decline, and increasing reports of environmental impacts affecting wildlife, fisheries, farming and sensitive habitats. The recent wildfires across north Wales and south-east Wales are one of the clearest and most visible signs of how dry our landscapes have become.”Drought has no official threshold criteria, but is measured by NRW as “a combination of indicators showing a significant shortage of rainfall and water availability, resulting in impacts on rivers, groundwater, ecosystems, agriculture and land management”.While Welsh Water has reported that reservoir levels remain healthy overall, the company has implemented a hosepipe ban in parts of west Wales. People across England and Wales are beingurgedto be mindful of water usage. About 23 million people will have beenbanned from using hosepipesby this weekend.View image in fullscreenPeople play on a cricket pitch surrounded by parched grass in Victoria Park, east London, on Wednesday.Photograph: Ben Whitley/PAIn England, eight areas are in prolonged dry weather, the National Drought Group said this week. England has received only 3% of its long-term average July rainfall, with the south and east having nothing at all so far – the longest spell without rain since the mid-1990s.Some rivers are at lower levels than during the heatwave last summer. In the south-west the Dart and Tor flow is lower than this time last year. In central and south-eastern England the Coln in Gloucestershire and the Soar in Leicestershire both have lower flows than this time last year.Farmers have had to begin their harvests early, with warnings that cereal yields will be lower than expected.Drought was declared across much of England, Wales and eastern Scotland last summer – conditions that did not officially end until March, after a wet autumn and winter replenished rivers and reservoirs.TheMet Office predictsthat climate change will make UK summers 1C-6C (1.8F-10.8F) hotter and up to 60% drier by 2070, compared with 1990.Hannah Cloke, professor of meteorology and climate science at the University of Reading, said: “Unless the UK collectively gets its act together, and builds greater resilience into its water system at every point, we face a more uncertain future in which the tap could run dry. There is a better future, where we use water better where it falls and don’t waste as much. But we need a fundamental shift in understanding from almost everyone to get there.”Explore more on these topicsWalesDroughtWaterExtreme weatherClimate crisisUK weathernewsShareReuse this content

title: The king emerges from Tardis as Commonwealth Games face battle for time and space | Commonwealth Games 2026 | The Guardian
description: The troubled Games looked doomed until Glasgow stepped in but this opening ceremony had plenty of feelgood factor
Author: Sean Ingle
Headline: The troubled Games looked doomed until Glasgow stepped in but this opening ceremony had plenty of feelgood factor
Content: King Charles and Queen Camilla are greeted by Sir Chris Hoy after stepping out of the Tardis.Photograph: Andrew Milligan/PAView image in fullscreenKing Charles and Queen Camilla are greeted by Sir Chris Hoy after stepping out of the Tardis.Photograph: Andrew Milligan/PACommonwealth Games 2026The king emerges from Tardis as Commonwealth Games face battle for time and spaceThe troubled Games looked doomed until Glasgow stepped in but this opening ceremony had plenty of feelgood factorSean Ingleat the OVO HydroThu 23 Jul 2026 18.17 EDTLast modified on Thu 23 Jul 2026 19.01 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleTheCommonwealth Gamesin Glasgow opened on Thursday evening to the surreal sight of King Charles and Queen Camilla stepping out of a Tardis – and a mechanical Loch Ness monster roaring the first few bars of Flower of Scotland.Final hurrah? Glasgow’s cut-price Commonwealth Games fight for survivalRead moreThe Tardis was a nod to the fact that police boxes were designed by the Scottish architect Gilbert MacKenzie Trench.
