But the Doctor Who reference proved to be doubly apt given the Games faced being exterminated until Glasgow stepped in after Victoria pulled out due to financial pressures.And with the prime minister, Andy Burnham, watching on in his most significant public engagement since taking office on Monday, this was an opening ceremony that was also notably political.At one point a spinning clock turned into a kaleidoscope, with organisers wanting to reflect the change that overcomes people when we are freed from the shackles of work. On another, there was a visit to Glasgow’s famous shipyards, while the screen displayed the message “People make Glasgow”.The last time Glasgow hosted the Games in 2014, Rod Stewart had them swinging in the aisles at Hampden Park and a nervous Susan Boyle forgot the words to Mull of Kintyre. But this, necessarily, was a more stripped-back affair.It had to be given thatthe budgets for these Gamesare so much smaller. There are also only 10 sports compared with 19 in Birmingham four years ago, and 3,000 athletes compared with nearly 5,000 back then. As a result, the Commonwealth Games is having to cut its cloth accordingly.View image in fullscreenKT Tunstall gets the crowd going at the opening ceremony.Photograph: Andrew Milligan/PAUnderstandably, it meant that the opening ceremony was largely a belts-and-braces affair. There were no dazzling pyrotechnics. The biggest musical star was KT Tunstall. And more than half of it involved celebrating the athletes as they paraded in front of a nearly packed Hydro Arena.But the crowd still loved it. One of the biggest cheers of the night came when the Scottish team, led by bowls star Alex “Tattie” Marshall, came out in their kilts. They waved their phones as they sang along to I’m Gonna Be (500 miles).And they were off their feet as Callum Beattie sang Yes Sir, I Can Boogie as sparkly unicorns danced across the stage in homage to Glasgow nightlife.View image in fullscreenA mechanical Loch Ness monster roars out the first few bars of Flower of Scotland.Photograph: Simon Stacpoole/AAPIt was not particularly flashy. But it was authentic. Perhaps there was a subtle message here for Burnham, who was seen making small talk with the Queen and nodding along to the musical interludes. You are who you are.And while some have questioned whether the Commonwealth Games has a future, the King made an impassioned case as he declared the 23rd edition open.“The Commonwealth is a unique and extraordinary family of nations,” he said. “As we gather, we celebrate the dedication and talent of every athlete and team. Your perseverance inspires us, and we eagerly anticipate your achievements in the coming days.”The king also used the baton relay, which has carried messages from the 74 Commonwealth nations toScotland, to make another political point by stressing the need to tackle pollution in the oceans.“For these Games, the relay has carried another message – to help remove the plastic pollution that harms our oceans and waterways, and can impact on human health,” he said. “Addressing plastic pollution complements the immense global efforts required to protect 30% of the world’s ocean by 2030.View image in fullscreenFlags are waved by fans as the Scotland team arrive on stage during the Commonwealth Games opening ceremony.Photograph: Manish Swarup/AP“Caring for the ocean’s natural capital ensures our communities and businesses can continue to thrive,” he added. “And vital global food resources can be enhanced through the establishment of marine reserves to restore threatened marine ecosystems and diminished fish stocks.”The night ended with KT Tunstall having the crowd eating out of her hands with a pounding rendition of No Scotland No Party, which segued into Suddenly I See and Scotland is a place called Home.That had the athletes on their feet and waving their flags – and leaving the Hydro with the feelgood factor. The big question now is whether it will sustain itself over the next 10 days of sporting competition. A lot is riding on it – not only for the athletes but the future of these Games themselves.Explore more on these topicsCommonwealth Games 2026Commonwealth GamesKing Charles IIIMonarchyScotlandfeaturesShareReuse this content

title: Elon Musk says he got ‘carried away’ with Trump – but still holds on to contentious political views | Elon Musk | The Guardian
description: In interview, billionaire says instead of running ‘Doge’ he should’ve ‘worked on my companies’ and shares extreme predictions
Author: Richard Luscombe
Headline: In interview, billionaire says instead of running ‘Doge’ he should’ve ‘worked on my companies’ and shares extreme predictions
Content: 1:01'I got a little too involved in politics,' says Elon Musk – videoElon MuskElon Musk says he got ‘carried away’ with Trump – but still holds on to contentious political viewsIn interview, billionaire says instead of running ‘Doge’ he should’ve ‘worked on my companies’ and shares extreme predictionsRichard LuscombeThu 23 Jul 2026 11.27 EDTLast modified on Thu 23 Jul 2026 15.51 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleElon Musk has admitted he became too consumed by politics during his volatile alliance with Donald Trump and his brief appointment as the president’s financial axman, running the so-called “department of government efficiency” (Doge).The world’s richest person, and briefly itsfirst trillionaireafter his SpaceX company floated on the New York stock exchange in June, made the confession in awide-ranging interviewwith the Economist published on Thursday. He shared his positions on subjects from foreign wars to AI, and made a number of eye-opening predictions for the future.“I got carried away, frankly,” Musk said of his relationship with the president, whom he helped get elected in 2024 with$200m in financial backing, then served during his controversial tenure as head of Doge, overseeing $150bn in“harmful” budget cutsand forcing tens of thousands of people out of federal jobs.The owner ofTesladid not expand on the statement, but appeared cognizant of the impact of his dalliance with the levers of power in Washington DC.In December, heoffered in a podcastwith Katie Miller, wife of Trump’s White House adviser Stephen Miller, that his wrecking-ball approach to the federal government had been only partially successful, and that given the opportunity he probably wouldn’t do it again.“I think instead of doing Doge, I would have basically worked on my companies,” he said at the time.Talking to the Economist, however, he appeared to relish the attention his political involvement had garnered, and the influence he believed he exerted. “People don’t realize that what I’m saying will come to pass,” he said, the outlet referring to Musk – at least in his own mind – as a modern-day Cassandra.The interview, a 90-minute conversation for the publication’s video platform The Insider, recorded at a Tesla factory in Texas, was Musk’s first since theSpaceXflotation, in which his personal wealth briefly topped $1.25tn before dropping back to a relatively penurious $750bn.He reflected on moving from “exhilaration to terror” in a single day, but was not referring to the SpaceX share price. Instead, he was talking about artificial intelligence, which he believes will ultimately render work by humans “optional”.AI systems and robots will eventually handle most digital and physical jobs, he said, and the merger of his own AI venture with SpaceX will allow him to develop systems to perform cognitive tasks such as software programming while Tesla manufactures humanoids.Musk, the Economist said, plans to power his AI venture with datacenters in space, which experts have warned will be“catastrophic” for Earth.Within five years, Musk said, AI intelligence systems may surpass the sum of all human intelligence. Within 10 years, robots in the workplace will help usher in an era of such “amazing abundance” that money will become meaningless.It was, however, Musk’s political views, and predictions, that were perhaps the most contentious parts of the interview. The UK, a country he has not visited for years, but whose politics, especially on immigration and race, have become arecent obsession, will become engulfed in a civil war within 20 years, he claimed.Asked if he was anti-Muslim, Musk attacked the questioner, a policy of diversion similar to that which has served Trump well, and which he has employed previously, notably calling the Guardian “irrelevant” when itdecided to withdrawfrom his “toxic” social media platform X.“I’m against rape and murder. I’m against the imposition of rules and laws that are contrary to what we’ve come to accept in the west,” he said. “It’s a crying shame that traditional media, like you, don’t recognize this.”He described the media’s characterization of Europe’s far right as “false, misleading and nonsense”, and argued that it was made up of “normal people” seeking safe cities, secure borders and sensible spending, the Economist said.In ananalysispiece accompanying the interview, the outlet acknowledged that Musk was a divisive figure. “His political views, disseminated to his 240m followers on X, strike many as plain-speaking and strike many more, including The Economist, as plainly bigoted,” it wrote.“But he is also one of a handful of men who are pioneering AI and who thereby have an outsize influence on its trajectory. When he speaks, he reflects the debates they are having. His datacentres in space could power AI’s future.“For all his political polemics, he has a record of being right about technology in fields such as electric cars, rockets and satellite communications that confounded other engineers and entrepreneurs.”Another Musk prediction: after AI destroys jobs, there may need to be a large-scale wealth redistribution to create “universal high income” for humans.He said he would be willing to pay “trillions in tax” then, conversely, argued that money will become irrelevant inside 10 years. In which eventuality, he said, governments should “issue people cheques”, because, he said, amid an infinite supply of goods and services produced by machines, deflation will be a bigger problem than inflation.He also shared thoughts about the Ukraine war, in which his Starlink satellites have been used to help that country’s military against Russia’s aggression. Despite this, Musk said any peace deal should include territorial gains for Russia.The interview concluded with an assessment by Musk of his achievements and ambitious current projects, in AI and in space, a field in which he has also made a number of bold predictions. He said in 2024 that humans couldset foot on Mars by 2028(currently Nasa is targeting 2028 for a return to the moon, which many analysts think is unlikely).The universe, Musk said, according to the Economist, could be a computer simulation created by aliens. “The things I’m doing are so preposterous that it’s hard to believe they’re real,” he said.Explore more on these topicsElon MuskUS politicsTrump administrationTeslaSpaceXAI (artificial intelligence)newsShareReuse this content

title: How powerful is your passport? Explore the global rankings | World news | The Guardian
description: A symbol of global inequalities and shifting geopolitical fortunes, some passports offer their holders far more freedom to travel than others
Author: Ana Lucía González Paz
Headline: A symbol of global inequalities and shifting geopolitical fortunes, some passports offer their holders far more freedom to travel than others
Content: World newsHow powerful is your passport? Explore the global rankingsA symbol of global inequalities and shifting geopolitical fortunes, some passports offer their holders far more freedom to travel than othersAna Lucía González Paz,Prina ShahandGarry BlightThu 23 Jul 2026 03.00 EDTLast modified on Thu 23 Jul 2026 04.42 EDTSharePassports can resemble abridged memoirs, their stamps telling romantic stories of wanderlust, freedom and belonging.But they are also a symbol of global inequalities and shifting geopolitical fortunes. Passports from some places allow their holders to travel abroad simply by booking a flight and packing a bag. Others, however, entail booking an appointment at the nearest consulate, presenting documents to prove identity and financial means, then waiting weeks for a decision.How does the status of your passport compare globally? How has its power changed over time?Search to find out …How has passport strength changed?In the last 10 years, more countries have gained access to more destinations and, at first glance, it would seem that the world is opening up.“People think that walls are increasing as the extreme right is coming [into government] in various places around the world,” says Armand Arton, who runsPassport Index, a website that tracks visa requirements. “But what the statistics are saying is that an increasing number of countries are signing visa-free agreements.”You can chart a passport’s strength by counting the number of countries, states and territories its holder can enter visa-free, or with a visa on arrival, an electronic travel authorisation (ETA), or a fast-track e-visa. This is known as a mobility score.Chart showing some of the passports that have gone up more than 60 places in the rankingSmall island nations’ passport strength rose as they earned visa-free status from developing countries. Arton attributes the rise to these countries’ often small populations, which are not seen as a national security threat, and to a sense among some governments of a moral obligation to offer visa-free travel to people on the frontlines of the climate crisis.China has moved up the rank by opening up and expanding bilateral agreements with regional neighbours. Since signing a bilateral visa-free agreement with Singapore in February 2024, there was a sharp increase in travel between the two countries.When countries sign a visa-free agreement with the Schengen area, which comprises 29 European countries, the standing of their passports can shoot up the ranking almost overnight. That is why Georgia, Ukraine and Kosovo are among the biggest climbers in the passport table.Vjosa Musliu is an associate professor at Vrije Universiteit Brusselwho has writtenabout the experience of travelling with a Kosovan passport, which has gained in mobility. She describes the joy of seeing her relatives and friends being able to travel.“These were upper-middle class people with decent jobs who never travelled because of the cost of getting a visa, which is sometimes close to £300,” she says. “[People] who wanted to see grandmas and grandpas and not celebrate birthdays on FaceTime.”But visa-free travelling does not mean you automatically get free entry, she adds. “Technically you can travel for free, but there is still subjective interrogation that takes place at borders.”The lowest-ranking passportsMost passports have gained mobility in the last decade, but some have fallen in the tiered ranking. Vanuatu fell 14 positions when the EU and the UK removed it from their visa-exempt list, citing concerns over Vanuatu’s investor citizenship scheme. The US dropped five places to ninth tier this year, mostly due to countries applying reciprocity in their visa systems in response to Washington’s tightening of its visa requirements.Then there are countries such as Afghanistan, Iraq, Syria, Pakistan and Somalia, for which the world is still mostly closed. Anyone with an Afghan passport can only travel visa-free to three countries, and will have to apply for a visa at a consulate before travelling to 113 countries.A line chart showing how Somalia, Pakistan, Iraq, Syria and Afghanistan have consistently stayed in the bottom of the rankingAfghan passport holders are also barred from entering the US, where citizens of 19 countries face a full travel ban, and another 20 face partial restrictions.Having a weak passport can sour even life’s greatest achievements. Omar Artan was to become the first referee from Somalia to officiate at a World Cup after being named on Fifa’s final list for the tournament, but he was denied entry to the US.It can also delay your plans.Visual artist Syed Hussain, who is from Pakistan, was granted a US residency in 2019 – but it took three attempts until he was finally granted the visa so that he could travel in 2024.View image in fullscreenArtist Syed Hussain turned his experience into artwork. This is a drawing of his US visa.“You get the chance to go somewhere, and they hold you back for five years, and they don’t give you the reason why, they just give you a yellow paper and refuse the visa,” Hussain said. “It’s a 10-minute interview in which you cannot clarify your things, or bring your portfolio.”How reciprocal are the measures?Passport Index has introduced a “welcoming score”, which counts the number of countries whose passport holders are eligible to enter a destination visa-free, or with a visa on arrival, an ETA or an e-visa. Although some e-visas still require documents to be submitted and reviewed, they are generally considered less burdensome than traditional visas.Setting up the welcoming scores against the mobility scores of countries paints a mixed picture. The US, Canada, Australia and New Zealand hold some of the world’s strongest passports, yet keep their own borders comparatively closed. At the other end of the spectrum are countries such as South Sudan, Nigeria, Ethiopia and the Democratic Republic of the Congo, whose citizens need visas to travel to most destinations but whose governments admit foreign visitors relatively easily.Chart showing reciprocityThe Schengen effectThe 29 European countries that belong to the Schengen agreement (plus four that apply the same rules), grant visa-free access to almost half of the countries in the world.But people who cannot travel visa-free must obtain a visa in advance from a consulate. This applies mostly to passport holders from south and south-east Asia, northern Africa, central Asia, the Middle East, and most of sub-Saharan Africa.Chart showing which regions can visit visa-free or require a visa to access the Schengen blocThe futureBeyond the Schengen area, passport mobility is likely to increase through new bilateral and regional agreements. Africa remains the least integrated region, with only 29% of travel within the continent taking place visa-free.The balance will also shift as more countries replace traditional visas with digital ETAs and e-visas, “driven by advances in border security, traveller screening, and digital immigration management”, says Arton. This might spell the end of the paper visa.But it also means, according to Arton, that travellers who previously enjoyed visa-free access will increasingly be required to obtain an electronic authorisation prior to departure. The EU, for example, is expected to introduce itsEtias travel authorisation systemby the end of the year for visitors who currently travel visa-free.Passport data fromPassport Indexas of June, 2026. Visa requirements can change and may include additional conditions. Always check the relevant embassy, consulate or official government website before travelling.

title: Kemi Badenoch ‘has to adapt’ her rhetoric to take on Andy Burnham | Kemi Badenoch | The Guardian
description: Conservative leader is becoming more confident and forging a message for her party, but faces a new opponent
Author: Peter Walker
Headline: Conservative leader is becoming more confident and forging a message for her party, but faces a new opponent
Content: Kemi Badenoch repeatedly questioned how Burnham would pay for his early flurry of policies in her speech in London on Thursday morning.Photograph: Stefan Rousseau/PAView image in fullscreenKemi Badenoch repeatedly questioned how Burnham would pay for his early flurry of policies in her speech in London on Thursday morning.Photograph: Stefan Rousseau/PAKemi BadenochAnalysisKemi Badenoch ‘has to adapt’ her rhetoric to take on Andy BurnhamPeter WalkerSenior political correspondentConservative leader is becoming more confident and forging a message for her party, but faces a new opponentThu 23 Jul 2026 14.14 EDTLast modified on Thu 23 Jul 2026 16.18 EDTShare“So, Andy Burnham seems like a nice guy.” Those were the slightly unexpected opening words of Kemi Badenoch’sfirst speechsince the change in prime minister. But make no mistake: she was there to bury her opponent.Even Badenoch-sceptics inside the Conservative party and farther afield agree the Tory leader is becoming more confident, and has been able to forge an increasingly coherent message for her party, one based on a promise of economic competence.She does, nonetheless, face a different challenge now Burnham has replaced Keir Starmer.Aside from being a fresh face with a likely polling bounce, the former Greater Manchester mayor is more of a media natural, with the ability to deflect Badenoch’s jibes using self-deprecating humour, such as aviral social media responselast month after the Tory leader dismissed him as a “pair of eyelashes and a black T-shirt”.“She has to adapt – it’s like with anything when your opponent changes,” one senior Conservative said. “As a personality, Burnham is much more likable [than Starmer], and comes with a fair degree of popularity. She needs to wait for weaknesses, and one immediately seems to be: where is the money coming from for all these giveaways?”And that was one of the main themes of Badenoch’s speech on Thursday morning, held in muggy sunshine on a rooftop terrace in central London.Labelling Burnham’s philosophy as “cap one price and send the bill somewhere else”, she repeatedly questioned how the new prime minister would pay for his early flurry of policies. The other central theme was ambition, Badenoch calling Burnham a prime minister “with small answers to big problems”.View image in fullscreenKemi Badenoch now has a schedule of regular press conferences and lengthy media Q&As, often without a particular policy to announce.Photograph: Stefan Rousseau/PASuch message discipline would be necessary in taking on the new operation in No 10, one senior Conservative MP said, especially given Badenoch’s occasional tendency to get caught up in culture-war debates.The MP said: “When she first became leader there was a bit of a worry that she would just talk endlessly about culture-war issues. But that hasn’t happened. The focus should be on the economy and jobs, which it currently is.”A manifestation of Badenoch’s increased confidence is a schedule of regular press conferences and lengthy media Q&As, often without a particular policy to announce. It is notably similar to the timetable maintained last summer by Nigel Farage – before his byelection battle and anxieties over questionsabout his financesscuppered this.And while she still maintains a largely take-no-prisoners approach, Badenoch now has rhetorical registers other than the sometimes caustic antagonism she used on Starmer and, in a speech which some Tories say began her turnaround, towards Rachel Reeves whenshe evisceratedthe then-chancellor’s budget with almost startling ferocity.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |Every daySign up toHeadlines UKGet the day’s headlines and highlights emailed direct to you every morningPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionSuch an approach would not necessarily work against politicians more relaxed in their own skin, said Luke Tryl, the UK director of the pollsters More in Common, who is a regular observer of its focus groups.“If Burnham is seen to just laugh it off it might be less effective than against brittleness,” Tryl said. “The reason it worked so well against Reeves, and her budget response got such rave reviews, was in part because Reeves looked so shellshocked. I suspect [the new chancellor] John Healey and Burnham won’t.”Bigger questions remain for Badenoch, not least why her own improved personal poll ratings have yet to translate into more support for her party, and whether her mission to reshape the Tories in her muscularly rightwing approach could put off some more moderate voters.But for now, much of the coming weeks will be devoted to pinning down how best to tackle a PM who is a notably more slippery target that his predecessor.“She has a summer to figure this out,” the senior Tory said. “But she has to be nimble – what works in July might not by September.”Explore more on these topicsKemi BadenochConservativesAndy BurnhamanalysisShareReuse this content

title: Charli xcx: Music, Fashion, Film review – slippery search into selfhood that hits just as hard as Brat | Music | The Guardian
description: How do you follow up the album that made you a star – and a caricature? In classic Charli style: with a pivot to guitar and a meta masterpiece about the instability of identity
Author: Laura Snapes
Headline: How do you follow up the album that made you a star – and a caricature? In classic Charli style: with a pivot to guitar and a meta masterpiece about the instability of identity
Content: Fearless … Charli xcx.View image in fullscreenFearless … Charli xcx.MusicReviewCharli xcx: Music, Fashion, Film review – slippery search into selfhood that hits just as hard as Brat(Atlantic)How do you follow up the album that made you a star – and a caricature? In classic Charli style: with a pivot to guitar and a meta masterpiece about the instability of identityLaura SnapesThu 23 Jul 2026 19.01 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleAfter a decade spent trailblazing a unique kind of pop freedom from the genre’s fringes, A-list success seemed like the thing that might finally trapCharli xcx. Her2024 club-rat opus Bratspawned memes, embarrassing political bandwagoning, Collins’ word of the year and possibly terminal damage to the bra industry. Its ludicrous cultural impact looked set to pickle her as a caricature of a Parliament-toting bad girl; the question of whether “Brat summer” could last – whether Charli could sustain the interest – was so rife she turned it into a mockumentary,The Moment. It made success look miserable: your creation turned cringe by execs wanting to milk their prize cow dry of every last acid-green drop.View image in fullscreenCharli xcx: Music, Fashion, Film.Photograph: Aidan ZamiriEven for a pop star renowned for reinvention, it put a higher price on her next move. Pivot and risk losing new fans and looking wilfully contrarian. When Charli released the sub-two-minute singleRock Music, many heard it as a middle finger. “I think the dancefloor is dead,” she drawls over guitars that buzzed like speakers weathering interference from a Nokia 5110, “so now we’re makingrock music.” There were boring thinkpieces about the dancefloor being very much alive,actually(as if Charli had ever ridden culture for clout then binned it off), and about whether she was trying to eject fair-weather new fans. But the key to this cheeky song is surprisingly earnest: “Jump off the stage / I hope they catch you today / But if they don’t it’s OK,” she sings naively, an affirmation to take creative leaps, and to come with Charli – or not, as you like – as she takes this one.Music, Fashion, Film’s prosaic title references Charli’srecent turn towards actingand fashion collaborations. But from the first singles, the bigger picture felt a little murky compared with Brat’s excavation of anxieties around female relationships. She quickly protested that she wasn’treeeeaallymaking rock music. The wistful SS26 dealt in slimy celebrity apologia; the addictive Wink Wink hat-tipped to 00s French indie sleazers the Teenagers as Charli pinky-promised that she was respectable now. But I’d bet these releases were intentionally diffuse, preventing Music, Fashion, Film from slipping out of Charli’s control before it was even out.Charli xcx: Camera – videoThat slipperiness turns out to be the essence of another gorgeously astute and disarmingly deep album about the instability of identity – not just as a vanguard songwriter striving for evolution, but how much we can ever know one another, or even ourselves. Rock Music and SS26 explore self-definition: the defiant cultural reset; the celebrity scoundrel who dodges criticism by exploiting certain sympathies (“my heritage could give me quite the USP”). On Card Declined’s blunt, fuzzed-out chug, she’s shopping for “a personality / A whole backstory” via luxury goods. There’s no moralising side-eye: Charli, always a laser-focused cultural observer, understands that self-commodification is just the way we live now, how it might offer an escape from dire times, or helpfully beckon the hellfires faster. Even leaning on guitars and stripping away Charli’s usual AutoTune toys with the idea of authenticity, though it’s far from genre cosplay. Alongside co-producers AG Cook and Easyfun, Charli remakes guitar music at intimate, pocket-sized scale – though with outsized hookiness.There are two telling songs about different best-friendships. Magic Metal Montana, a dreamy blur of Strokes guitars and emo half-time, is a love song to long-term producer AG Cook. “We don’t talk,” she sings tenderly, “we make music, we talk that way.” The bolshie, muscular Persona teases a friend who talks endlessly and calls Charli her bestie but never says anything real, a performance Charli understands: “We’re both under the influence of life imitating art, and we both know it,” she chants. Who do you trust with your true, faltering self?For Charli, ever fearless about looking unflattering, it’s her audience. Two shockingly brilliant standouts address her self-actualisation through art. The melancholy, jittering Camera confronts her anxieties about whether she’s stupid for wanting to “throw my life away” to pursue acting, but the way it softens into a heavenly, firefly-dappled rhapsody makes you understand exactly how liberating it is. Pop star-actors are always labelled dilettantes. Charli reframes the idea: how at any moment, a bolt from the blue might undo your idea of yourself, make you feel terrifyingly reborn.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |WeeklySign up toSleeve NotesGet music news, bold reviews and unexpected extras. Every genre, every era, every weekPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionBrat pack: Charli xcx’s 20 best songs – ranked!Read moreHer actual birth features on I’m Afraid, about how her insecurities may stem from her father not being present at hers. Over spare, bassy dissonance that echoes her fantastically dank John Cale collaboration House, she screams about her fears of failing as a wife (she married 1975 drummer George Daniel last summer) when she’s just a drunk, numb, desperate kid, two more ill-fitting identities. “Take the ring, take the ring,” she yells; then she sounds exhausted as she admits she has to say these “complicated”, unsayable things “so my insides don’t turn black”. That’s less of a case for artistic purification than it might seem: “All this pain makes me believe / I’m a better artist now / An excuse I tell myself / To act selfishly,” she admits. Does she contradict herself? Absolutely. Does that unsparing compulsion to lay it all out for us – the ego and self-hate; the defensiveness and chagrin – make Charli our most consistently bracing pop star? God, yes.This week Laura listened toHeavensouls – StraightRawhhhhhhhhhThis week and every week since I discovered it: the 20-year-old Houston musician’s albumWestside Trappedis a blurry, blown-out love letter to his Nigerian heritage. Among the frisky Fela-worthy moments comes this fantastically bitter, gnarled blues.Alexis Petridis is awayExplore more on these topicsMusicCharli xcxPop and rockreviewsShareReuse this content

title: ‘My life’s work was being delightful. That really hurt me’: breakout Americana star Brennan Wedl on freeing herself through song | Music | The Guardian
description: ​With her roots stretching from the Catholic church to riot grrrl, the US singer-songwriter explains how sobriety and self-analysis powered her classic new Waxahatchee-produced album
Author: Laura Snapes
Headline: ​With her roots stretching from the Catholic church to riot grrrl, the US singer-songwriter explains how sobriety and self-analysis powered her classic new Waxahatchee-produced album
Content: Timeless and confident … Brennan Wedl.Photograph: Daniel TopeteView image in fullscreenTimeless and confident … Brennan Wedl.Photograph: Daniel TopeteMusicInterview‘My life’s work was being delightful. That really hurt me’: breakout Americana star Brennan Wedl on freeing herself through songLaura Snapes​With her roots stretching from the Catholic church to riot grrrl, the US singer-songwriter explains how sobriety and self-analysis powered her classic new Waxahatchee-produced albumThu 23 Jul 2026 07.30 EDTLast modified on Thu 23 Jul 2026 14.01 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleThe songwriting on Brennan Wedl’s new album is so ironclad that I kept checking the credits to see if any of its songs were actually covers of peak Lucinda Williams tracks that I’d somehow missed. Nope: all originals by a 29-year-old songwriter whose radiant, hooky, tough Americana puts her toe-to-toe with genre godheads such as Williams circa Car Wheels on a Gravel Road, or Sheryl Crow had she grown up playing church basement punk shows; also, a vision of Courtney Love in cowboy boots.“I love Lucinda’s very earnest way of looking at daily life, and I hoped to achieve that on this record,” says Wedl, calling from her Nashville home, surrounded by vintage magazines and tchotchkes. Wedl is warm and dudely; she answers almost every question with an exuberant “toootally!”. She cites her favourite of her new songs, the absurdly immediate Just Another Daddy Who Did Me Wrong, a bittersweet number that questions her self-destructive appeal. “It’s a very harsh look at reality, but also about how our minds can create so much fantasy around reality. I’m working on not projecting too much on reality these days.”You could reasonably say Wedl doesn’t have too many reasons to fantasise Her new, self-titled record was produced by Waxahatchee (AKA Katie Crutchfield) and her longtime producer Brad Cook, who asked to make the album after Snail Mail’s Lindsey Jordan put them on to Wedl’s music.“I just couldn’t wrap my head around her voice,” says Crutchfield, who had never produced before. “It’s immediately iconic, so timeless and confident – for someone who was, at the time, so undiscovered.” When Crutchfield heard an early version of Two Dollar Pistol, a loving, windows-down nudge to someone frustrated by ambition, “I was like, this person’s writing huge melodies that can be played in arenas. This is a really different level of songwriting.”Wedl puts the fully formed nature of the album – her third solo release after more muted outings – down to never having known herself better. Combining newly written songs and others from her 20s, “it’s a lot about communicating between various versions of you, time-travel with music; conversations with the unknown, dialogue with large themes like the man, God, Jesus, the trope of ‘daddy’.”View image in fullscreenBrennan Wedl … ‘I dream of the day I’ll move back to Minnesota.’Photograph: Daniel TopeteLike many of the US’s best young alternative songwriters, Wedl comes from a religious background – but, unusually, she hasn’t rejected it wholly. She grew up in Missouri with nuns for aunts. “I’m really grateful to have seen a lot of different sides of the Catholic church,” she says. “I saw nuns devoted to service, to education. But I also saw the assault of young boys. My grandfather is a huge advocate for female priests and says we can’t keep brushing this under the rug – the church will die if they do not change their ways. So, coming from elders in my family who have really made Christianity their own, it inspired me to put a little pagan twist on it in my adult life.”Wedl discovered riot grrrl when she attended the She Rock She Rock summer camp at age 12, and found common ground between the church and punk in their impetus to come together “to build the new era of something”. Minnesota offered a great local soundtrack in the Jayhawks, the Replacements and Prince (members of the New Power Generation, his backing band, played in her grandparents’ church band), and Wedl played both with her uncle and her first band, Sex Education. (Not something they actually got much of in high school.) “As I get older and listen to these artists, it really makes me dream of the day that I’ll move back to Minnesota and be with family,” she says.She first left home to attend Berklee College of Music in Boston, where she studied songwriting and co-founded the playful queercore outfit Dazey and the Scouts. They lasted just over two years, then became a minor pandemic TikTok sensation when a kid dancing to a Morrissey-impersonating verse in their song Wet went viral. “TikTok has its dark sides, but the discovery factor does bring people together – being able to see yourself in some kind of art or music, no matter where you are, is magic,” says Wedl.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |WeeklySign up toSleeve NotesGet music news, bold reviews and unexpected extras. Every genre, every era, every weekPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionWaxahatchee on success and sobriety: ‘You don’t have to be tortured to make interesting art’Read moreMusic also helped Wedl see herself more clearly. Her album’s rockiest moment, Pretty Little Fantasy, attacks perceptions of her as “somebody’s daughter … prim and proper”. Growing up as the eldest and only girl, she says, “I wanted to be so helpful, and I think girls are taught that a lot. But then I was introduced to riot grrrl and Marina Abramović and disgraced women throughout history, and it took most of my 20s to come to the conclusion that I was still carrying the self-imposed life’s work of being delightful, and that doesn’t help anyone – actually, it really hurts me. I think ‘delightful’ is the mask I wore out of not dealing with my own shit – and so if I was a delight, no one would have to investigate further.”On stage, Wedl cultivated being “dead serious about having fun – the ambition to live the life I want”. It filtered into the rehearsal space, then her everyday confidence. Recently, she was reminiscing on an earlier version of her solo project. “There was an upright bass player and an electric bass player because I didn’t want to hurt someone’s feelings by saying they couldn’t play,” she says, laughing. “I’m sure that sounded horrible!” That was 10 years ago now. “Being the leader of a band is a constant crash course in people-pleasing, self-assuredness and respect, and I’m so grateful that I get to learn these huge life lessons through music.”She also credits “various 12-step programmes” with breaking these moulds. “Twenty-one has found me sober,” she sang on her last album, 2019’s Holy Water Branch. “A lot of my life boils down to: how do my daily habits make up who I am?” she says. “When I was drinking, I was really focused on the next time I was gonna do that, who was gonna be there – this mental obsession with the wrong things.” Quitting drinking didn’t erase her problems, but it made them clearer. She realised she had become someone who wasn’t writing, neglecting “this shortcut for when I couldn’t really explain what’s on my heart to others; I could explain it to my guitar and to my journal.”Writing, she says, “can be how we communicate with what we don’t see, what contracts we sign with ourselves. What we want to bring into this reality can be done with pen and paper a lot of the time. It’s really magical.”Waxahatchee on Brennan WedlView image in fullscreenWaxahatchee.Photograph: Sonja Horsman/The GuardianFrom first hearing Brennan to making the record to now, I kind of still can’t fully wrap my head around her existence. She’s unique. Three or four years ago, Lindsey Jordan [AKA Snail Mail] was at my house and we were going back and forth, playing each other stuff. She put on I Wanna Be Your TV, by Brennan, and didn’t give me any context, and I had to stop her and be like, what is this? Is it recent? Did somebody young make this or is this an old song I’ve never heard? She told me Brennan was from Nashville. I couldn’t stop listening to her – I listened to that song a million times.Shortly thereafter, she put out some singles with [west coast label] Kill Rock Stars, a handful of which we re-recorded. Eventually, we got in contact and I got to know her, then got to watch her play music in a studio, her technique, her approach. At every turn, she impresses me – she’s so talented and so humble but not in a grating, overly precious,shucks, I shouldn’t be herekinda way. She has the absolute perfect amount of confidence. She can really write a hook. The melodies are amazing. As I got to know her better, [I saw that] her lyric writing is really strong, too. It’s all influenced by my favourite stuff: it’s Kathleen Edwards, Lucinda Williams, Tom Petty – the people’s songwriters.Brennan Wedl is released via Anti- on 21 AugustExplore more on these topicsMusicCountryPop and rockIndieinterviewsShareReuse this content

title: ‘Unbearably sexy in his rodenty way’: Benedict Cumberbatch’s 20 best films – ranked! | Movies | The Guardian
description: To mark the two-time Oscar nominee’s recent 50th birthday, we rate his finest screen roles – from hyper-British patricians and seething serpents, to ghastly Yanks, tragic codebreakers and sci-fi scoundrels
Author: Peter Bradshaw
Headline: To mark the two-time Oscar nominee’s recent 50th birthday, we rate his finest screen roles – from hyper-British patricians and seething serpents, to ghastly Yanks, tragic codebreakers and sci-fi scoundrels
Content: Benedict Cumberbatch as the persecuted mathematician, Alan Turing, in 2014’s The Imitation Game.Photograph: Black Bear Pictures/AllstarView image in fullscreenBenedict Cumberbatch as the persecuted mathematician, Alan Turing, in 2014’s The Imitation Game.Photograph: Black Bear Pictures/AllstarRankedMovies‘Unbearably sexy in his rodenty way’: Benedict Cumberbatch’s 20 best films – ranked!To mark the two-time Oscar nominee’s recent 50th birthday, we rate his finest screen roles – from hyper-British patricians and seething serpents, to ghastly Yanks, tragic codebreakers and sci-fi scoundrelsPeter BradshawThu 23 Jul 2026 08.00 EDTLast modified on Thu 23 Jul 2026 10.57 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on Google20.The Roses (2025)Benedict Cumberbatch does his considerable best opposite the equally prestigious Olivia Colman in this souped-up marital-breakdown dramedy, essentially remade from the 1989 movie with Michael Douglas and Kathleen Turner. This time, though, it’s all about the Britons – indicating the supposed classiness of the affair. But the film is overwrought and unconvincing; it never convinces us that the couple were once passionately in love and now passionately in hate. The below-par script has the effect of bringing out an unrewarding kind of coldness in Cumberbatch, technically assured though he always is.19.The Thing With Feathers (2025)View image in fullscreenIt’s behind you … The Thing With Feathers.Photograph: TCD/Prod.DB/AlamyHere is an example of Cumberbatch’s presence signalling a film’s purposeful seriousness – and he does indeed give an honest, intelligent and well-intentioned performance in an otherwise self-conscious and contrived study of grief and emotional breakdown. He is the writer and illustrator whose wife has died, and who is visited by a gigantic hallucinatory crow (raspingly voiced by David Thewlis) symbolising the agony of loss. Cumberbatch’s performance, however, looks a little refined and dry for the material.18.August: Osage County (2013)An interesting, subdued appearance from Cumberbatch in this film version of Tracy Letts’s award-winning play about an explosive family reunion. It is an example of how his theatrical accomplishment, technique and distinctive appearance, when married up to material that doesn’t overstate things, can work well. In fact, it is the rest of the cast, headed up by Meryl Streep, who bring the fireworks; Cumberbatch, playing the shy, depressed cousin Little Charles, delivers a less-is-more premium.17. The Hobbit trilogy (2012-2014)Popcorn movies like this, as opposed to upmarket Masterpiece Theatre-type productions, allow Cumberbatch to have some fun – and here he is, playing in motion-capture as the terrifying dragon Smaug and the equally daunting Necromancer, in Peter Jackson’s LOTR prequel-triple. Within the confines of motion-capture, his Smaug – a little like Ian McKellen’s Gandalf – delivers authentic British vocal richness and theatrical hamminess, evil and yet lonely.16.The Other Boleyn Girl (2008)View image in fullscreenSaucy romping … The Other Boleyn Girl.Photograph: Bbc Films/AllstarNowadays, a Tudorbethan romp with Cumberbatch would have to cast him as Henry VIII or Thomas Cromwell at the very least. In Justin Chadwick’s enjoyably gossipy and saucy what-if period drama about Henry’s illicit relationship with Anne Boleyn’s sister Mary (played by Scarlett Johansson), Cumberbatch has to content himself with the more self-effacing role of Henry’s courtier and Mary’s husband, William Carey, who is a step away from the real action. But Cumberbatch’s theatrical credentials are important to the film’s integrity.15.The Courier (2020)Cumberbatch gives some serious moustache action in this espionage drama-thriller, based on a true story. It’s not as good as his appearance in Tinker Tailor Soldier Spy, but Cumberbatch is very good at conveying something that for most actors is just the pretext for comedy – a certain kind of simmeringly significant British uptightness. He plays Greville Wynne, a mousy 1960s businessman, always going to Moscow on boring business and who is asked by MI6 to make contact with a possible Soviet defector. And so, with his fusty homburg and moustache, he makes his appointment with destiny.14.Atonement (2007)View image in fullscreenHazy memories … Atonement.Photograph: Working Title/AllstarOne of Cumberbatch’s distinctive supporting roles is in this bold and starrily cast version of the Ian McEwan bestseller about a tragic lie that changes lives. Cumberbatch is Paul Marshall, a well-spoken young man who visits an aristocratic country house before the second world war, and whose sexual assault of a girl there forms part of a calamitous misunderstanding and untruth. Cumberbatch does sterling work, but this is one of the lesser roles that he tended to get before TV’s Sherlock catapulted him into the big league.13.Four Lions (2010)Cumberbatch can play comedy, although he rarely gets the chance. He displays those skills in this small but perfectly deadpan role, as the special branch negotiator who has to talk to the potential suicide bombers in Chris Morris’s acidic black comedy about jihadist extremism. The character’s stolid professionalism coexists with a certain kind of cluelessness almost comparable to that of the bumbling terrorists themselves.12.Black Mass (2015)View image in fullscreenCrime-adjacent … as Billy Bulger in Black Mass.Photograph: Courtesy of Warner Bros. Pictures/Warner Bros./AllstarNo self-respecting English actor shrinks from the challenge of an accent – and in Scott Cooper’s Black Mass, Cumberbatch does an entertainingly studied version of south Bostonian: posh, but with a hint of the mean streets. In this true-story mob drama, he is Massachusetts senator Billy Bulger, at home in the cigar-smoky gentleman’s clubs and bars exchanging worldly badinage with the great and the good. But his brother is gangster Whitey Bulger, played by Johnny Depp, and Billy finds himself under pressure to persuade Whitey to become an informant. Cumberbatch is very good at showing how infatuated Billy is with his own prestige.11.War Horse (2011)As befits the title, this movie demonstrated Cumberbatch’s horse-riding chops and the elegant figure he cuts in the saddle. As the blusteringly patriotic Maj Jamie Stewart, he is every inch a clenched member of the officer class, giving a fierce speech to his men before a deeply misjudged cavalry charge in which the British are cut down by German machine guns. Cumberbatch looks and sounds the part, and you can fully imagine that gimlet-eyed fanaticism on the faces of actual officers of the time. But it’s a slightly cartoony character in a slightly cartoony film.10.Starter for 10 (2006)View image in fullscreenFingers on buzzers … Starter for 10.Photograph: Icon Film/AllstarA very early movie role for Cumberbatch, in which – perhaps before the industry quite realised what a thoroughbred they were dealing with – he was cast in an almost slapstick role as the pop-eyed, geeky butt-of-the-joke. In this version of David Nicholls’ novel about students competing in the TV quizshow University Challenge, James McAvoy is the fresher at Bristol Uni, mad keen to be on the team. But he is unbearably patronised by Cumberbatch’s team captain – a pompous postgrad – and their confrontation ends in chaotic violence. It’s a very tasty, broad comic role for Cumberbatch – and it’s a shame he hasn’t developed that side of his career.9.1917 (2019)View image in fullscreenA fierce martinet … 1917.Photograph: François Duhamel/APCumberbatch’s second performance as a ramrod-straight commanding officer in the first world war. His contribution here is smaller than in Steven Spielberg’s War Horse, but it is a very powerful cameo and in some ways more realistic. He is Col Mackenzie, to whom two desperate young lance corporals have to deliver a vital message to call off an attack the cunning Germans are attempting to lure the British into making. His Mackenzie is a fierce martinet, careworn by slaughter and by the burden of command, and with a poignant hint of despair.8.Tinker Tailor Soldier Spy (2011)Cumberbatch had one of the trickiest, knottiest roles of his career in this John le Carré thriller, playing Peter Guillam – an intelligence officer low enough on the food chain to have escaped suspicion of double-dealing, but nonetheless little liked by his superiors. He has, however, a close relationship with Gary Oldman’s spy chief George Smiley and, by virtue of the semi-protected status this gives him, has a somewhat cool and insolent manner. Cumberbatch amusingly carries off a cuff-shooting style with a rather absurd mop of a haircut, and a dandruffy three-piece suit.7.Star Trek Into Darkness (2013)View image in fullscreenUnprecedented villainy … as Khan in Star Trek Into Darkness.Photograph: Paramount Pictures/AllstarCumberbatch deserves a massive supervillain role and perhaps the sheer obviousness of this possibility means that he hasn’t really had one. (He could be a Bond villain – but, then again, he may be more interesting as Bond himself.) At all events, he was a watchable turn as the blankly named John Harrison, AKA Khan Noonien Singh – the enigmatically cool foe of Kirk and Spock in JJ Abrams’ 2013 follow-up to his great reboot of the sci-fi pop classic. His role here is a little light on dialogue, and Abrams’ intention was probably more to let us simply luxuriate in that mysterious face: unbearably sexy in its rodenty way.6.Wreckers (2011)Writer-director Dictynna Hood’s little-known indie drama gives us one of Cumberbatch’s most interesting and fully realised performances, notable because there is less of the slant, quirk or mannerism that many other directors and writers would contrive for him. He plays David, a schoolteacher and decent guy married to Claire Foy’s Dawn, living in a quiet English village. Their home life is upended when his troubled brother Nick (Shaun Evans) returns from the army and reveals some uncomfortable family secrets. Cumberbatch’s David shows that he himself is not the cool customer we thought. A finely detailed and well-judged performance.5.Doctor Strange (2016)Cumberbatch was genius casting as Doctor Strange, arriving at what now, perhaps, looks like the end of the Marvel superhero golden age. Strange is a brilliant but arrogant neurosurgeon who achieves his superpowers – in time-honoured fashion – through an accident, but has to be schooled in a distinctively Zen kind of mastery by an enigmatic mentor (played by Tilda Swinton). With this, and 2022 follow-up Doctor Strange in the Multiverse of Madness, Marvel gave Cumberbatch a massive global cineplex audience for his unique charisma, theatricality and unconventional handsomeness.4.The Wonderful Story of Henry Sugar (2023)View image in fullscreenMagical stuff … The Wonderful Story of Henry Sugar.Photograph: Courtesy of NetflixPerhaps it is odd that Cumberbatch has only been in two Wes Anderson films: this Oscar-winning short for Netflix, and The Phoenician Scheme two years later. The elegance and British idiosyncrasy of Cumberbatch would make him an obvious candidate. Henry Sugar gives Cumberbatch another side to his underappreciated comedy style: the deadpan pose, which is here an almost Keatonian stoneface. His Sugar is a wealthy, greedy man with an inscrutable manner, who discovers in himself magic powers (which are, however, not quite superpowers in the Marvel sense) that he is able to exploit for gain in casinos all over the world.3.12 Years a Slave (2013)View image in fullscreenComplicit and self-pitying … 12 Years a Slave.Photograph: Jaap Buitendijk/New Regency Pictures/AllstarCumberbatch played the most insidiously culpable role in Steve McQueen’s Oscar-winning slavery drama – the true story of Solomon Northup (Chiwetel Ejiofor), a black man abducted in 1841 from freedom in the US north into slavery in the south. Northup has two masters: the apparently thoughtful and liberal William Ford, played by Cumberbatch, who then sells him to the sadistic and odious Edwin Epps, played by Michael Fassbender. Ford is supposedly the good guy, and yet he is all the more despicable for being so blandly complicit in the situation, and for his weakness in selling Solomon to a man he knows is evil just to clear his personal debt. His chief emotion is a kind of sorrowing and worldly self-pity.2.The Imitation Game (2014)Here is the one movie that really gave Cumberbatch a chance to develop the Sherlockian persona that had made him an international star. He plays Alan Turing, the genius mathematician and wartime codebreaker who was later persecuted as a gay man in the 1950s, in the middle of a seedy moral panic led by Churchill’s home secretary, David Maxwell Fyfe. Cumberbatch is excellent at showing the young Turing’s brilliance, his abruptness and tactlessness (which may now be attributed to autism spectrum disorder), and also his sensitivity and loneliness. There is a rich, romantic melancholy in the fake heterosexual relationship that Turing confects with his fellow codebreaker and friend Joan Clarke, played with energy and charm by Keira Knightley. Sherlock was fictional, but the equally exceptional Turing was real, and achieved more than Sherlock ever could.1.The Power of the Dog (2021)View image in fullscreenOrnery and sociopathic … The Power of the Dog.Photograph: Kirsty Griffin/APIronically, Cumberbatch’s greatest film to date is a western – and this most English of actors plays the nastiest US roughneck imaginable. He is the boorish and charmless Phil Burbank, who runs a ranch in remote Montana with his brother George (Jesse Plemons). Phil is more than just ornery; he is a sociopath and a bully, obsessed with mocking his brother’s pretensions to culture and refinement, but profoundly dependent on him emotionally. And so, when George marries sensitive widow and single mother Rose Gordon (Kirsten Dunst), Phil starts harassing and abusing her – there is a bizarre scene where he strums derisively along on a five-string banjo while she is trying to play the piano. In his dysfunctional, childlike way, he is clearly unable to stop thinking about the fact that his brother and this woman are enjoying sexual relations. Phil himself would appear to be a virgin. Or is he? He also begins to take an oppressively strange interest in Rose’s teenage son, offering to teach him to ride and take him out into the remote hills to school him in the rancher ways of manly self-reliance. It is a huge role for Cumberbatch, and his wild-eyed magnetic and sinister aura floods the screen.Explore more on these topicsFilmRankedBenedict CumberbatchComedy filmsRomance filmsThrillersFilm adaptationsComedyShareReuse this content

title: My first relationship just ended. How can I expect to find someone else when it took 15 years to find her? | Australian lifestyle | The Guardian
description: To stay open-hearted and optimistic, you need hope, writes advice columnist Eleanor Gordon-Smith. That’s not the same as expectation
Author: Eleanor Gordon-Smith
Headline: To stay open-hearted and optimistic, you need hope, writes advice columnist Eleanor Gordon-Smith. That’s not the same as expectation
Content: ‘My self-esteem and confidence wasn’t exactly soaring before I met her, but now I feel even less so,’ writes a reader.Painting: The Wounded Man by Gustave Courbet.Illustration: Gustave Courbet (1819–1877)/Wikimedia CommonsView image in fullscreen‘My self-esteem and confidence wasn’t exactly soaring before I met her, but now I feel even less so,’ writes a reader.Painting: The Wounded Man by Gustave Courbet.Illustration: Gustave Courbet (1819–1877)/Wikimedia CommonsLeading questionsAustralian lifestyleMy first relationship just ended. How can I expect to find someone else when it took 15 years to find her?To stay open-hearted and optimistic, you need hope, writes advice columnistEleanor Gordon-Smith.That’s not the same as expectationRead moreLeading questionsEleanor Gordon-SmithThu 23 Jul 2026 11.00 EDTLast modified on Thu 23 Jul 2026 20.11 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleI’m nearly 30 and my first serious relationship just ended a month ago. It lasted eight months, she was my first girlfriend and first kiss, etc. She broke up with me in a very kind and respectful way, and I have been … OK, I guess.The thing is, everyone has been saying to me, “But you must feel so much more confident now.” I don’t. My self-esteem and confidence wasn’t exactly soaring before I met her, but now I feel even less so. It just feels like most people are less tolerant or accepting of my shortcomings.If this wonderful, understanding person couldn’t see me as worth it, or enough or whatever, how can I expect to find someone elsewhen it took 15 years to find her?Eleanor says:A person declining or ending a relationship with you is not a scorecard on your value. I know it sure feels like it.But it’s really important to separate the pain of being rejected from the pain of being proven “not worth it”, because although they feel nearly identical, only one has actually happened.After high school, a friend I was very close to drifted away. Should I seek closure from her? | Leading questionsRead morePeople end relationships because of the relationship itself: they felt you were not a fitwith them.The same trait that makes you not fit with one person can make you a perfect fit with someone else. And in the search for love, that’s what we’re really after: not approval, not having lots of people like us, nice though that would be. We want to find a relationship that fits.The fact this lovely person felt you didn’t quite fit together isn’t evidence of your objectivevalue, any more than learning that your neighbour doesn’t like porridge would convince you that porridge is bad.The bad news is, we can’t predict if or when we’ll find that fit. You asked: “How can Iexpectto find someone else when it took 15 years to find her?” and I can’t give you that. I can’t give you proof that you can expect to find someone else.But do you need it? To stay open-hearted and optimistic, you need the hope, not the expectation, you’ll find someone.It might feel daunting to do the things that would help you find someone new – putting yourself out there; giving new connections the benefit of the doubt – when you’re not even sure they’ll work. But nobody’s sure they’ll work. Hope can be the motivation where expectation can’t be. You don’t need to know that it’ll work out. You can be led by the possibility that it might.In fact, that’s what everyone has to do in the search for love, no matter how many relationships they’ve had. Don’t think of yourself as unlucky in love, such that you alone have to subsist on hope when everyone else gets to dine on sumptuous certainty. A very long romantic history doesn’t get you across the line toknowingyou’ll find a relationship, or even that the one you’re in will last. Everyone, no matter how historically well-liked or beautiful or charismatic, faces ineliminable uncertainty that can only be bridged by choosing to pay attention to the possibility that lovemightwork out instead of the possibility that it won’t.Remember, too, that self-esteem and esteem from others have to be separable if either is to last. You said you feel like “most people” are less accepting of your “shortcomings”. You might be right, but double-check to make sure it’s notyouwho’s withholding the acceptance you want. Often we feel things are happening relationally when in fact – or as well – they’re happening within us.Other people’s love and acceptance is worth wanting – everybody wants to feel cherished. But try hard to separate uncertainty about whether you’ll find that with another person from whether you can feel it for yourself.Relationshipsare about finding a fit; self-esteem is about recognising worth. Neither one can replace the other.Ask Eleanor a questionShare your questionTell us hereYour responses, which can be anonymous, are secure as the form is encrypted and only the Guardian has access to your contributions. We will only use the data you provide us for the purpose of the feature and we will delete any personal data when we no longer require it for this purpose. For alternative ways to get in touch securely please see ourtips guide.NameWhat is your question for Eleanor?Email addressYour contact details are helpful so we can contact you for more information. They will only be seen by the Guardian.By submitting your response, you are agreeing to share your details with us for this feature.SubmitShow moreExplore more on these topicsAustralian lifestyleLeading questionsRelationshipsDatingfeaturesShareReuse this content

title: Iraq’s impossible predicament: why the country is trapped between Iran and the US | Iraq | The Guardian
description: The long read: With the outbreak of this year’s war, Iraq’s balancing act between the two antagonists has collapsed, exposing a perilously fragile state
Author: Ghaith Abdul-Ahad
Headline: The long read: With the outbreak of this year’s war, Iraq’s balancing act between the two antagonists has collapsed, exposing a perilously fragile state
Content: An oil tanker burns after being hit by an Iranian strike at Khor Al Zubair port near Basra, Iraq, on 11 March.Photograph: APView image in fullscreenAn oil tanker burns after being hit by an Iranian strike at Khor Al Zubair port near Basra, Iraq, on 11 March.Photograph: APThe long readIraqIraq’s impossible predicament: why the country is trapped between Iran and the USWith the outbreak of this year’s war, Iraq’s balancing act between the two antagonists has collapsed, exposing a perilously fragile stateThe summer issue of the Long Read magazine is out now.Click hereto orderGhaith Abdul-AhadThu 23 Jul 2026 00.00 EDTLast modified on Thu 23 Jul 2026 05.35 EDTShareOne week after the United States and Israel killed Ayatollah Ali Khamenei and ignited a regional war, drones started flying over Basra in southernIraq. They were targeting Iraq’s oil industry. They hit an oilfield operated by BP, as well as a cargo terminal at Basra airport and a large compound that houses foreign companies that service the oil sector. Another attack on 6 March hit the same compound, striking offices and warehouses used by Halliburton and KBR, two US companies that have been in Iraq since the 2003 invasion. The attack sparked a massive fire that lit up the night sky.In the wider war, it was not a particularly notable day – but in Iraq the strikes laid bare just how weak the state had become and “how easily sovereignty could be violated”, as one senior Iraqi security official put it to me. For 40 years, the US, Iran and Israel existed in an uneasy equilibrium of neither peace nor war. As this has come to a sudden, dramatic end, Iraq finds itself caught in the middle.More attacks on Iraqi energy infrastructure were to come. On 11 March, an Iranian drone boat attacked two oil tankers off the port of Basra, setting them ablaze and killing at least one crew member. In the following days and weeks, a succession of drone strikes hit more oilfields operated by foreign companies. Hundreds of energy sector workers from American, European and Chinese firms left, many travelling overland because the country’s airspace was closed. Thousands of Iraqi workers were sent home.No one claimed responsibility for these attacks (apart from the strikes on the oil tankers, which Iran acknowledged). The central government in Baghdad and the local authorities in Basra were remarkably quiet, offering only empty official statements about sovereignty and the promise of investigations. The truth is that most of these attacks were not coming from Iran. They were undertaken by Iraqis acting on Iraqi soil – the work of factions of the country’s powerful, Iran-backed Shia militias. “This is an act of treason,” a visibly shaken senior official at the oil ministry in Basra told me.Speaking confidentially, multiple officials told me the oil attacks had been carried out by the Islamic Resistance in Iraq (IRI), an umbrella group of Shia paramilitary organisations that were originally trained and equipped by Iran to fight the Americans after the 2003 invasion. IRI factions are also members of the Popular Mobilisation Units (PMU) – a collection of Iran-backed, mainly Shia militias in Iraq that were brought together in 2014 to fight the Islamic State. The PMU is, in theory, subordinate to the Iraqi government but in reality follows its own agenda, with investments in construction and agriculture giving it a source of economic power.While Iraq, which is majority Shia, may seemingly have a single, unitary government run from Baghdad, in reality multiple groups with varying degrees of independence – formed in the aftermath of the 2003 invasion – exert huge influence. Today, there are two types of paramilitary militia in the country. Those that use their weapons to protect their economic interests – the state contracts and businesses that they have cultivated over the years. And those that are primarily driven by ideology: deeply committed to the Iranian project of defeating Israel and expelling the United States from the Middle East.In 2026, this unstable balance has erupted into violence not seen for years. On the one hand, the US/Israeli war on Iran has spurred the hardline ideological paramilitaries to Tehran’s aid, attacking not just Iraq’s oil infrastructure but US military bases and the US embassy in Baghdad. On the other hand, a new, US-backed Iraqi prime minister, Ali al-Zaidi, has upped the ante by giving all of the country’s militia groups a deadline of 30 September to disarm and incorporate their fighting divisions into the Iraqi state. This is to satisfy longstanding American ambitions to reduce Iran’s influence in Iraq.While al-Zaidi has had some success, the hardcore groups have steadfastly refused to give up their weapons. As long as there is an American presence in Iraq, with Israeli jets flying overhead, the IRI will continue to fight.Speak to Iraqi government officials and you hear nothing but anger and exhaustion about the attacks on their oil infrastructure. “The installations that were attacked were on Iraqi soil, paid for by Iraq, as was all the destroyed equipment,” one told me. It was not the US companies that would be harmed – they would charge Iraq for the destroyed assets – but the Iraqi economy, the oil sector and the reputation of Iraq as a safe place to conduct business, he added. He also noted that the attackers had highly accurate intelligence, probably leaked from within, about where to strike and which building or warehouse to destroy.View image in fullscreenPeople walk near farmland by the Zubair oil field near Basra, Iraq.Photograph: Essam Al-Sudani/ReutersHe and other oil-sector officials in Basra told me that the aim of the attacks was to disrupt oil production, raise global prices and ramp up pressure on the US administration to stop its war on Iran. Within weeks of the war’s start, Basra’s output had fallen by two-thirds, with exports essentially halted. “Maybe they want to drive these western companies out to replace them with companies from countries more aligned to them,” the official said.What is clear is that consequences of the war in Iran, which escalated dramatically this week after the collapse of the ceasefire, have been profound in Iraq – exposing the weakness of a state that is perilously divided between the war’s two main antagonists.In one of Baghdad’s upmarket restaurants, a government adviser reclined on a large sofa. Until recently, he said, the Iraqi government had managed to keep most of the country’s militia factions under control. As the war in Gaza raged, and even during the 12-day war between Israel and Iran in 2025, they were kept at bay through dialogue and negotiation. But the killing of Ali Khamenei changed everything. “You can’t ask the factions not to participate any more,” he said. “This is an existential struggle and a religious duty.”The adviser could see why Iran and its supportive factions in Iraq were doing what they were doing. Iraqi airspace and American bases in Iraq were being used to pummel Iran with airstrikes. Iranian officials had told him that 40,000 Starlink devices, used to bypass the Islamic republic’s internet clampdown, had been smuggled into Iran from Iraq during the recent anti-regime demonstrations. “We should be grateful they didn’t use their full force and strike Iraq in retaliation, because they could easily have burned half the country,” he said. “And what do we have to defend ourselves against them?”In another part of Baghdad, I met with a high-ranking security official who had worked closely with the Americans for many years. He had little time to empathise with Iranian anxieties, but echoed the analysis of Iraq’s military ineptness.“The war has proven the weakness of Iraq. I don’t mean only militarily. Our country was violated by everyone, our airspace used by both sides.” The state could not even protect “its own soldiers and officers when we came under attack by the militias”, he said. “Everyone in the military command knows exactly who [the attackers] are. Maybe we don’t know the name of the person who pilots the drone, but we know who gave the orders, the chain of command and where they were launched from.”He nodded towards a police car parked nearby, the policemen standing around it, and referred to all the cameras installed on street corners. “Some rockets and drones were launched from the centre of Baghdad. Who can travel with rocket launchers through the city? How can you pass all the checkpoints, or drive into the countryside and fire them as they show in videos, without being detected?”By way of explanation, the conversation turned to corruption. There are honourable commanders in the Iraqi security forces, he conceded, but many of the highest-ranking posts are awarded to officers only after they make a payment to their superiors. “If you pay US$5m to take command of a force,” he said, “the priority becomes protecting that investment by avoiding confrontation, and that is a disaster.” The reason it is an investment is because a high-ranking security post is a way of making money – through fraud, embezzlement and accepting bribes. He placed his hands on the table, to emphasise his point. “When officers look and see their commander running their own schemes to siphon money, that affects the whole security forces.”Economics partly explains the division between the older militias, which have largely accepted the prime minister’s decree that they be incorporated into the Iraqi state, and the newer, hardline factions under the IRI banner. In late May, the highly influential Shia cleric Moqtada al-Sadr called for his militia to be placed under the command of the state, and two other factions followed suit. But the move was met with derision and disdain by the hardline paramilitaries, who declared that their weapons were sacred and would not be surrendered until the end times. In effect, they were saying that they were fighting for an ideological cause, while the older militias were merely looking out for their own economic interests.View image in fullscreenMoqtada al-Sadr.Photograph: Qassem Al-Kaabi/AFP/Getty ImagesOn 7 April, Iran and the US.The following day, the IRI announced that it would also be suspending operations for the duration of the ceasefire. (Fighting continued, albeit at a low level.) On that same day, Iraq and Saudi technical teams were having an online meeting to explore the reopening of the Iraqi-Saudi pipeline, which has been closed since the invasion of Kuwait in 1990. They were looking for a new way for Iraqi oil to bypass the pinch point in the strait of Hormuz.As the oil industry came to a near halt, long queues formed outside petrol stations, bringing to mind the worst days of chaos that followed the 2003 war. In a statement, the ministry of oil blamed the shortage on the shutdown of some refineries after foreign workers left the country and on its inability to export unrefined, heavy fuel oil.For a country so dependent on oil sales, which underwrite the salaries of 4 million public-sector employees, the decision by Iran-aligned militias to attack their own economy might have looked suicidal. “Not necessarily,” a civil-rights activist in the city of Basra told me. “Many people believe that oil companies are exploiting the country and they find the attacks justified.” Basra is one of the most polluted cities in the world, where people breathe the toxins from the burning flares of oil wells and drink brackish water. At the same time, its citizens are surrounded by signs of oil wealth – car dealerships, fancy sushi restaurants, expensive real estate – that are available only to a few. It is not surprising that many shrug their shoulders when they hear of attacks on oil companies’ assets.How did postwar Iraq fail to make use of its immense oil wealth to deliver a better life for its people? On the eve of the US invasion in 2003, the country’s oil infrastructure was in near ruin after decades of war and UN sanctions. The machinery was antiquated and production was kept alive only by veteran engineers. In the aftermath of the invasion, looting and sabotage nearly collapsed the industry altogether. Lacking the finances and capacity to rebuild the sector on its own, Iraq turned to international firms, signing contracts with major oil companies including BP, ExxonMobil and Shell. The deals inked were not the profit-sharing agreements that the foreign companies wanted – Iraq still takes huge pride in having nationalised its oil industry in 1972 – but less lucrative, flat per-barrel service fees. Baghdad had a stated ambition to pump about 12m barrels a day by 2017, a level that would rival Saudi Arabia. That target was never remotely met, peaking closer to 4.5-5m barrels a day in the late 2010s and hovering there.Gradually, the postwar euphoria around Iraq’s oil potential cooled, and within a few years companies began to abandon their projects. The thin margins had made Iraq one of the least profitable places the firms operated, but the retreat was mainly driven by political instability, contractual disputes and massive delays in government payments. The delays occur because of multiple layers of bureaucracy, deployed ostensibly to prevent corruption but in practice functioning as bottlenecks where bribes are extracted to smooth the process. Multinationals that are bound by anti-corruption laws generally refuse to pay such bribes, and their fees are held up as a result.Ministry officials and business insiders in Basra described to me a contracting system that – as elsewhere in Iraq – is dominated by political parties, clans and sometimes armed groups that line their pockets by exploiting agreements with contractors and subcontractors.In late May, the deputy oil minister responsible for the country’s refineries, Adnan al-Jumaili, was detained on corruption charges – part of the new prime minister’s anti-corruption drive. Al-Jumaili was shown on television in a yellow jumpsuit, surrounded by dozens of machine guns and boxes of ammunition that were reportedly found at his home. Investigators said they also recovered $10m (£7.5m) in cash, 3bn dinars (roughly £1.7m) and 1.5kg of gold. As the investigation expanded, security forces detained 47 people, including 13 MPs and senior officials, and seized hundreds of millions of dollars in cash and gold. Among those arrested in the same sweep was a second deputy oil minister, who had already been sanctioned by the US for allegedly diverting Iraqi oil to benefit Iran.It is no surprise that, for many Iraqis, the oil industry is less a symbol of the country’s pride than all that is wrong with it.This July, the funeral procession of the Iranian supreme leader, Ali Khamenei, took place in Iran – though it also crossed the border with Iraq, drawing 3.8 million people on to the streets of Najaf alone, according to official figures. Unlike in the choreographed ceremonies in Iran, massive throngs in Iraq pushed and shoved to get close to the coffin in the fierce summer heat. The white turbans of mullahs came undone as they jostled with the crowds; arms stretched forward, people wept, and those who could not get close enough threw their shawls and scarves at the casket to be blessed by it. Away from the crowds, the coffins, including a smaller one for Khamenei’s granddaughter, who was killed alongside him, were received inside the shrine of Imam Ali, a holy site in Shia Islam. Iraq’s leaders, among them the new prime minister, Ali al-Zaidi, stood in solemn silence alongside the Iranian president.View image in fullscreenThe coffin of the late Iranian supreme lleader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei is carried by mourners to the Imam Ali Shrine in Najaf, Iraq, on 8 July.Photograph: Anmar Khalil/APSix days later, as if to perfectly illustrate the double-edged nature of Iraqi politics, that same prime minister sat next to Donald Trump in the Oval Office, beaming as he listened to the American president declare that the two men had “tremendous chemistry” and that Iraq was “very well represented”. Al-Zaidi pledged, again, to disarm the militias and fight corruption, and invited US companies to invest in Iraq.For many years, a well-connected government insider told me, the Americans sat down with Iraqi prime ministers and repeated the same demands about disarming the Iran-aligned militias and ending corruption. “This has been going on since God knows how long,” he said. “They sat with Haider al-Abadi, with Adel Abdul-Mahdi, with Mustafa al-Kadhimi, with al-Sudani, with al-Maliki, with every prime minister. And during all those years, Iraq received a great deal of American support, but did nothing whatsoever to change its relationship with Iran. On the contrary, Iranian influence only deepened and expanded with time.”Why the seeming change this year, with al-Zaidi telling the Iranian-backed militias that they need to give up their arms?
