American pressure coincided with the economic crisis. The closure of the strait of Hormuz and the attacks on its oil installations have brought Iraq to the brink of bankruptcy. Washington has also made use of the huge leverage it has over the Iraqi economy. In an arrangement dating back two decades, Iraq’s oil revenues are held at the Federal Reserve Bank of New York. Physical shipments of dollars are flown from the US to Baghdad so the government can pay salaries and run the state.In April, the US took the extraordinary measure of stopping these dollar shipments. The Iraqi government got the message: al-Zaidi ordered the pro-Iranian militias to give up their guns in June, and the dollar shipments resumed in July.One spring evening in Basra, the corniche along the Shatt al-Arab river was packed with people walking up and down, enjoying the early-evening breeze: families with children holding balloons; young men in fitted dishdashas or jeans; women, some in black abayas, others in Gulf-style robes and headscarves, a few in trousers with their waist-length hair flowing. As music pulsed through the air, a line of photographers did brisk business as people queued to be pictured in front of the fountain installed in the river.View image in fullscreenAli al-Zaidi meets Donald Trump at the White House on 14 July.Photograph: Andrew Thomas/CNP/ShutterstockOn the other side of the street, gazing down benevolently, was a massive portrait of the city’s governor, Asaad Al Eidani, hung from a five-storey building. After taking office in 2017, Al Eidani initiated major public works: paving roads, extending the sewage network and securing Basra extended hours of electricity supply. (His detractors accuse him of benefiting from public contracts and awarding major projects to his inner circle, claims he vehemently denies.) He also built relationships with the neighbouring Gulf states, easing trade and travel to Kuwait and Saudi Arabia, hosting a Gulf football tournament that drew thousands of regional visitors to Basra, turning the city towards its Arab neighbours and their lucrative markets while maintaining its cultural and political ties with neighbouring Iran.Along the corniche stands the empty shell of the Kuwaiti consulate. It wasstormed in Aprilby locals at the instigation of militias, in response to a suspected Kuwaiti airstrike that killed several Iraqis; this was itself retaliation, Iraqi officials said, for an earlier attack launched by militias from Iraqi soil. The abandoned structure stands as a reminder of the dashed hopes of Iraq maintaining close relations with both Iran and the Gulf states.Walking through Basra, I found myself thinking about how implausible it was to imagine that Iraq’s pro-Iran fighters would give up their weapons; that Iran, at a moment when it needs to marshal every asset it has to fight the US and Israel, would abandon its allies; and that the corrupt politicians and businesspeople who have spent two decades siphoning Iraq dry would relinquish their wealth at the behest of a new, inexperienced prime minister.As I walked, I met one old man dressed in a white dishdasha with a knitted skull cap on his head. Iran, he said, was the last chess piece protecting Islam and defending Arab nationalism. It’s our duty as Iraqis to support it, he added. When I asked him if he was worried that with no oil sales, the Iraqi economy might collapse, he saidla(no): America will not abandon its project in Iraq. It will make sure our economy won’t collapse. And there, in a few words, was Iraq’s impossible predicament – dependent for life on two sworn enemies.View image in fullscreenThe Long Read magazine summer editionAI in the classroom, vending machines in the canteen, homeless people in the library, no one in the pub. Surprising stories from our strange new world in the new edition of the Long Read magazine.Buy your copy here.Listen to our podcasts here and sign up to the long read weekly email here.

title: One day, your life and future may depend on a jury. The integrity of that legal process is in peril right now | Imran Khan | The Guardian
description: When a barrister is cited for contempt because of his argument to a jury, as happened in a Palestine Action trial, the risks affect us all, says Imran Khan KC
Author: Imran Khan
Headline: When a barrister is cited for contempt because of his argument to a jury, as happened in a Palestine Action trial, the risks affect us all, says Imran Khan KC
Content: ‘Public confidence in the criminal justice system depends upon proceedings being conducted fairly.’Photograph: George Clerk/Getty Images/iStockphotoView image in fullscreen‘Public confidence in the criminal justice system depends upon proceedings being conducted fairly.’Photograph: George Clerk/Getty Images/iStockphotoOpinionTrial by juryOne day, your life and future may depend on a jury. The integrity of that legal process is in peril right nowImran KhanWhen a barrister is cited for contempt because of his argument to a jury, as happened in a Palestine Action trial, the risks affect us allPalestine Action barrister’s prosecution criticised as ‘chilling’Thu 23 Jul 2026 07.00 EDTLast modified on Thu 23 Jul 2026 08.12 EDTShareWithin days of Mr Justice Johnson’s decision to seekcontempt proceedings against Rajiv Menon KCbecause of that barrister’s closing speech to the jury in a Palestine Action criminal damage trial, I happened to be making a closing speech to a jury. Neither the case nor the personnel of my case are relevant to what happened next, but it is telling that counsel for the prosecution appeared to invoke Mr Justice Johnson’s imprimatur to rebuke me for stepping over the boundary as to what was acceptable to say to the jury.Thankfully, nothing of substance came of that. Not so for Menon. On 22 June 2026, Mr Justice Johnson handed down his approved judgment in Menon’s case such that he now faces a possible two-year prison sentence for defending his client in what was, it seems to me, in accordance with his professional duties and obligations.As a highly experienced and successful member of the English bar, Menon was giving his closing speech in a case that involved pro-Palestine activists who broke into an Elbit Systems UK arms factory. The trial judge alleged that Menon deliberately ignored directions and misled the jury by discussing jury equity and the war in Gaza.The judgment is significant not only for those directly affected by practising in the criminal courts but society as a whole because it starkly demonstrates how words spoken by those defending their clients – the traditional fortress of forensic freedom – can attract the machinery of contempt. The seriousness of that needs to be understood. The prospect of contempt proceedings is in danger of imposing a chilling effect on the willingness of defence advocates to test, for example, institutional misconduct themes robustly before a jury.As I experienced first-hand, the risk will discourage advocates from deploying precisely those tools that the adversarial system contemplates. Defence counsel in cases involving allegations of police misconduct, for example, must be able to put credibility challenges with vigour, to name dishonesty where the evidence supports it, and to invite jurors to engage empathetically with the defendant’s experience. If such language can attract a contempt referral, advocates will face an unwelcome calculation: whether to advance the strongest case open to them or to temper their rhetoric in order to insulate themselves from future jeopardy.The right to a fair trial, protected by article 6 of the European convention on human rights and embedded in the common law long before that instrument was enacted, encompasses the right to effective legal representation. Effectiveness, in a jury trial, requires more than the formal presence of counsel; it demands that counsel be free to deploy every legitimate forensic tool available to challenge the prosecution case. Public confidence in the criminal justice system depends upon the perception – as much as the reality – that proceedings are conducted fairly and that no party enters the courtroom at a structural disadvantage.A climate in which defence advocates self-censor out of fear of contempt undermines that perception. If it becomes publicly known that counsel in cases involving police misconduct temper their submissions to avoid personal exposure, reasonable observers will question whether the adversarial process retains its integrity. That erosion of confidence is corrosive: the legitimacy of jury verdicts – acquittal and conviction alike – rests in part upon the assurance that they were returned after the defence case was pressed as fully and fearlessly as the evidence permitted.View image in fullscreenRajiv Menon KC.Photograph: Garden Court ChambersThe quality of jury deliberation is also imperilled. Jurors are lay decision-makers. They depend upon counsel to distil complex factual and legal questions into accessible, persuasive narrative. A closing speech that is moderated by the advocate’s anxiety about personal sanction is likely to be less effective. The result is not necessarily that the jury reaches the wrong verdict, but that it does so with a thinner and less complete presentation of the defence case than the evidence would have supported.The risk of wrongful conviction must be a key factor here. It is an uncomfortable but empirically grounded observation that a disproportionate number of wrongful convictions in England and Wales have involved some form of police misconduct, whether in the gathering of evidence, the presentation of testimony or the suppression of material. Cases of that kind depend heavily upon vigorous adversarial challenge in the closing speech. If that challenge is muted, the risk that a factually innocent defendant is convicted is materially increased. The chilling effect of Menon’s case thus carries a direct, if unquantifiable, contribution to the wrongful conviction risk that the system as a whole must bear.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |WeeklySign up toMatters of OpinionGuardian columnists and writers on what they’ve been debating, thinking about, reading, and morePreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionYou may not sympathise with the Elbit four’s methods. But you should be outraged by their treatment under the law | Geoffrey RobertsonRead moreAs for barristers themselves, if senior advocates perceive that hard cases carry a personal professional hazard not faced in more conventional litigation, the rational response is to avoid that kind of work. Defendants in such cases will find themselves represented by advocates who are either less experienced or less willing to advance their case with full forensic force. What price, then, the quality of justice?But what is most fundamentally at stake is the constitutional value of fearless advocacy itself. The principle that counsel must be free to advance a client’s case without fear of personal retribution is not a professional privilege; it is a constitutional safeguard. It reflects the understanding, embedded in centuries of common law, that the state – which commands the resources of the police, the Crown Prosecution Service and the wider apparatus of prosecution – should not also be able to deter challenges to its own witnesses through the threat of proceedings against the advocate raising them.When the contempt is used or threatened in the context of advocacy, that constitutional equilibrium is disturbed.Society has a collective interest in maintaining it – not for the benefit of the bar, but for the benefit of every person who may one day stand accused, and for the integrity of the process by which guilt or innocence is determined in their name. That vigilance should start here.Imran Khan KC is a practising solicitorDo you have an opinion on the issues raised in this article? If you would like to submit a response of up to 300 words by email to be considered for publication in ourletterssection, pleaseclick here.Explore more on these topicsTrial by juryOpinionContempt of courtUK criminal justiceBarristerscommentShareReuse this content

title: Why would Trump agree to the Saudi nuclear deal? | Kenneth Roth | The Guardian
description: The agreement offers the Saudis the same options Trump has sworn Iran will never have
Author: Kenneth Roth
Headline: The agreement offers the Saudis the same options Trump has sworn Iran will never have
Content: ‘Why would Trump agree to such a deal? Money seems to have played a big part.’Photograph: Ken Cedeno/AFP/Getty ImagesView image in fullscreen‘Why would Trump agree to such a deal? Money seems to have played a big part.’Photograph: Ken Cedeno/AFP/Getty ImagesOpinionTrump administrationWhy would Trump agree to the Saudi nuclear deal?Kenneth RothThe agreement offers the Saudis the same options Trump has sworn Iran will never haveThu 23 Jul 2026 05.00 EDTLast modified on Thu 23 Jul 2026 11.55 EDTShareThe stark contrast between Donald Trump’snuclear dealwith Saudi Arabia and his nucleardemandsfor Iran would be comical if not so tragic. Trump has handed the Saudis the same nuclear options he swore Iran would never have.To make matters worse, Trump seems to have offered the Saudis this sweetheart deal to make amends for hisdisastrouswar of choice with Iran. Meanwhile, he continues to bomb Iran in a push toopenthe strait of Hormuz, which wasnotclosed until Trump launched his counterproductive war.As recently as this past April,Trump reiteratedin discussing Iran: “There will be no enrichment of uranium.” That goal had become more urgent because, once Trumpripped upthe nuclear accord negotiated by Barack Obama, Iran stepped up its enrichment tonearthe level needed for an atomic bomb. When Israel and the United States started bombing Iran in June 2025, Iran alsobarredindependent UN inspectors.So what does Trumpofferthe Saudis? The possibility, after a joint study, to enrich uranium (and reprocess spent atomic fuel) and to bar inspectors from certain sites that they might deem suspicious. The lack of inspectors would mean there is little to prevent the Saudi government from transforming a civilian nuclear program into a clandestine weapons operation, despite Saudi Arabia (likeIran) being asignatoryof the nuclear Non-Proliferation Treaty.US signs landmark nuclear deal with Saudi Arabia that could pave the way for domestic nuclear enrichmentRead moreThe Saudi crown prince hassaidthat his country would develop a nuclear weapon if Iran did. Notably, Trump’s deal is much less stringent than the 2009dealnegotiated by George W Bush and Barack Obama with the United Arab Emirates, Saudi Arabia’s neighboring ally-turned-competitor.Trump describes himself as having mastered the “art of the deal”, but his agreement with the Saudis is likely to torpedo any more restrictive deal with Iran. Iran is already being asked to forsake a nuclear weapon while Israelhasthem. Any claim that Israel can be trusted to handle its weaponry responsibly is belied by itsgenocidein Gaza and its decimation of much of thatoccupied territoryas well as southernLebanon. Now Iran must consider that its Saudi adversary could also develop a bomb.Why would Trump agree to such a deal? Money seems to have played a big part. The deal could providetens of billions of dollarsto the US nuclear industry if it were to sell reactors to the Saudis. Suddenly the principle that Trump said was worth fighting for can be jettisoned for the right price.I suspect part of the reason is Trump’s effort to revive an alliance that his war has jeopardized. Trump has long had asoft spotfor the Saudi crown prince, who possesses the kind of monarchial power that Trumpcovets. Like other Gulf Arab states, Saudi Arabia had looked to the United States as a security partner, evenwantingto formalize the arrangement with a treaty.But Trump’s war has made the partnership risky. Rather than the United States protecting its regional allies, the war provided an excuse for Iran to attack them, targeting the US militarybaseson their soil and sometimes their petroleum and otherinfrastructure.About the only good thing that can be said about Trump’s giveaway to the Saudis is that it reflects a continuedlesseningof the genocidal Israel’s influence in Washington DC. An earlier version of a Saudi nuclear deal waspremisedon the Saudi government opening diplomatic relations with Israel. That requirement has now been abandoned. Riyadh has said that recognition is off the table without “a credible and irreversible path to a Palestinian state”. Netanyahu is doing all he can topreventthat.As the nuclear rationale for war wears thin, Trump’s secretary of state, Marco Rubio, has begun tostressthe importance of Iran allowing the strait of Hormuz to reopen. Trump has gone so far as tothreatenwar crimes – bombing Iran’s bridges and power plants – for every Iranian attack in the strait, even though war crimes by one sideneverjustify war crimes by the other.But the straitwasopenuntil Trump’s war encouraged the Iranians to retaliate asymmetrically by closing it. Now, Trump’smemorandum of understandingwith Iran – another brilliant stroke by the master negotiator – implicitlyallowsIran to charge fees after 60 days.Has Trump dragged the US into a forever war in Iran? | Mohamad BazziRead moreIn other words, the main remaining justification for war has become a matter that was never an issue before Trump’s war, while Trump has effectively given away with his Saudi deal a main rationale for starting the war.There of course is another factor behind the continued bombing. Trump seems to be seeking a face-saving concession from Iran before his Republicans face a midtermdebacle. But that is hardly a reason for more bloodshed and destruction.Revelation of the Saudi deal comes as the Trump administration is trying to persuade Congress to fund a deeply unpopular war that it had nosayin launching. The defense secretary, Pete Hegseth, says it has cost$37.5bnso far. That is about what it would cost for, say, a year’srental assistancefor 2.3 million low-income people, or to enable about 7 million people to maintain theirhealth insurance.And to make matters worse, Hegseth wants a total of$67.1bnto keep the war going. Because Trump cannot articulate a persuasive rationale for his pointless war, Congress should say no. This war never should have started and now clearly should not continue.Kenneth Roth is a Guardian US columnist, a senior fellow at Yale University, and former executive director of Human Rights Watch. He is the author ofRighting Wrongs: Three Decades on the Front Lines Battling Abusive GovernmentsExplore more on these topicsTrump administrationOpinionSaudi ArabiaIranDonald TrumpNuclear weaponsUS-Israel war on IranMiddle East and north AfricacommentShareReuse this content

title: Nobody in Europe knows or cares what Kemi Badenoch thinks. That’s a big mistake | Simon Nixon | The Guardian
description: The opposition leader wants to pull the UK out of the ECHR, wrecking treaties from the Good Friday agreement to the Gibraltar deal, says economics commentator Simon Nixon
Author: Simon Nixon
Headline: The opposition leader wants to pull the UK out of the ECHR, wrecking treaties from the Good Friday agreement to the Gibraltar deal, says economics commentator Simon Nixon
Content: Conservative leader Kemi Badenoch speaking in Farnborough, Hampshire, 21 July 2026.Photograph: Andrew Matthews/PAView image in fullscreenConservative leader Kemi Badenoch speaking in Farnborough, Hampshire, 21 July 2026.Photograph: Andrew Matthews/PAOpinionEuropeNobody in Europe knows or cares what Kemi Badenoch thinks. That’s a big mistakeSimon NixonThe opposition leader wants to pull the UK out of the ECHR, wrecking treaties from the Good Friday agreement to the Gibraltar dealThu 23 Jul 2026 00.00 EDTLast modified on Thu 23 Jul 2026 03.15 EDTShareSitting in Brussels, or one of the EU’s 27 chancelleries, one could be forgiven for tuning out the latest psychodramas in Britain’s Conservative party. After all, the country has a new Labour prime minister who is a pro-European keen to build upon the“reset” begun by Keir Starmer. Who cares what a party in opposition thinks about anything when it is still reeling from its landslide 2024 defeat, and languishing third in the opinion polls as it haemorrhages support from its right flank? It is easy to dismiss Kemi Badenoch and her party as a sideshow.That would be a mistake. Following her latest pronouncements, Badenoch has effectively committed the Conservatives to a policy of conflict, and possibly even rupture, with the EU. Sherecentlysuggested in a newspaper article that anyone who opposes her policy of withdrawing from the European convention on human rights (ECHR) or ditching net zero commitments would be considered “not serious”, and thus excluded from consideration as a Conservative candidate at the next election. She then withdrew the party whip (aUK parliamentary euphemism for “kicked out”) from Gavin Barwell, a member of the House of Lords whocriticised Badenoch’s stance. Such a choice revives memories of Boris Johnson’s purge of pro-Europeans from the party.Westminster has mostly viewed this through a domestic lens: a Tory leader prepared to jettison centre-right supporters in the hope of preventing further rightwing defections to Reform UK. What has been missing is the European dimension. Were theConservativesto return to power – which polls suggest is possible in alliance with Reform UK – Badenoch’s ideological red lines will resurrect the ghost of “no deal” Brexit.That’s because the UK’s continued membership of the ECHR underpins every element of its post-Brexit relationship with the EU. Article 763 of the trade and cooperation agreement (2021), for instance, commits both sides to adhere to shared values, including the human rights treaties to which they are signatories, while article 771 makes clear that this commitment is an essential condition of the deal and 772 states that in the event of a breach of this undertaking, the other party has the right to suspend or terminate the agreement.The EU insisted on those references above all toprotect peace in Northern Ireland, notes Catherine Barnard, professor of EU law and a fellow of the UK in a Changing Europe thinktank. The ECHR is embedded in the Good Friday agreement, in which Britain committed to enshrining the convention rights in Northern Irish law. Meanwhile, the Windsor framework, the dealhammered out by Rishi Sunakto ease post-Brexit trade across the Irish sea while keeping the land border open,specifically commits Britainto “no diminution of rights, safeguards or equality of opportunity” as set out in the Good Friday agreement.What’s more, ECHR membership underpins many other areas of cooperation, including data sharing and criminal extradition arrangements. Even theGibraltar agreement, signed only on 15 July, requires both sides to remain signatories. A British government that left the convention would not be making a narrow adjustment to its human rights regime; it would be pulling on a thread that runs through the entire fabric of the post-Brexit relationship.Conservatives claim that the danger is overblown, that the EU would never let a dispute over the ECHR detonate an important trading relationship. But that smacks of the complacency apparent throughout the Brexit process. David Wolfson, the shadow attorney general,argued last yearthat quitting the ECHR would not breach the Good Friday agreement because it only required convention rights to be accessible in domestic courts. Other legal experts, not least in Northern Ireland,disagree, noting that the reason the ECHR is in the Good Friday agreement is to ensure that those rights are protected by a body independent of the British state. The EU’svictory over Hungaryin the European court of justice this year in relation to an anti-LGBTQ+ law showed the weight it attaches to fundamental rights. It would hardly be less vigilant if it judged that the vital interests of a member state were threatened.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |WeeklySign up toThis is EuropeThe most pressing stories and debates for Europeans – from identity to economics to the environmentPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionWhat the EU wants from Andy BurnhamRead moreNor is it just Badenoch’s ECHR loyalty test that is problematic. The trade and cooperation agreement’s article 772 also states that “an act or omission that materially defeats the object and purpose of the Paris agreement shall always be considered a serious and substantial failure” to fulfil the “essential obligations” of the agreement. Depending on where the Conservative leader’s anti-net zero policy lands, that could spell serious trouble for the trading relationship. Its provisions to level the playing field hinge on shared climate goals.Europeans may roll their eyes and take Badenoch’s grandstanding as further evidence that British politics remains, to use her word, “unserious”. But this, too, would be complacent. As the global order fragments and Europeans find themselves squeezed by an aggressive Russia, coercive US and mercantilist China, the UK-EU relationship has become vital to European security. It is not enough for Brussels to repeat that it is for Britain to decide what relationship it wants. Both sides have a responsibility to solidify it ahead of the next UK election.Indeed,Andy Burnhammay need all the help he can get. His goodwill has yet to materialise into clear European policy, and Starmer’s reset reached the limit of what can be achieved within Labour’s red lines of no return to the single market, the customs union, or free movement. To keep the relationship moving forward, a new project that deepens institutional and political links and does not cross either side’s current red lines is needed. A defence pact – building on existing coalitions of the willing and including Ukraine – is the obvious candidate. To slay Badenoch’s no-deal ghost, Brussels must play its part in proving the relationship’s value.Simon Nixon is a journalist and economics commentatorExplore more on these topicsEuropeOpinionAndy BurnhamKemi BadenochHuman rightsLabourConservativescommentShareReuse this content

title: Take Centrica’s gas storage warning seriously – Britain still needs secure supplies | Nils Pratley | The Guardian
description: One task awaiting new energy secretary Miatta Fahnbulleh is devising a plan to avoid a crisis by the end of the decade
Author: Nils Pratley
Headline: One task awaiting new energy secretary Miatta Fahnbulleh is devising a plan to avoid a crisis by the end of the decade
Content: The Rough gas storage platform, off the Yorkshire coast. Centrica has warned it will close the facility unless it receives long-term support.Photograph: PA Images/AlamyView image in fullscreenThe Rough gas storage platform, off the Yorkshire coast. Centrica has warned it will close the facility unless it receives long-term support.Photograph: PA Images/AlamyNils Pratley on financeGasTake Centrica’s gas storage warning seriously – Britain still needs secure suppliesNils PratleyOne task awaiting new energy secretary Miatta Fahnbulleh is devising a plan to avoid a crisis by the end of the decadeThu 23 Jul 2026 13.44 EDTLast modified on Thu 23 Jul 2026 15.30 EDTShareWelcome to the energy secretary job,Miatta Fahnbulleh. You have a politically tricky decision to make on whetherto approve productionat the Jackdaw gasfield and the Rosebank oilfield. But here’s another one that can’t wait much longer: what is the broader plan to ensure Britain avoidsa gas supply crisis in 2030?By way of reminder, one of the more alarming reports published by the energy department under Ed Miliband was the one that warned of“an emerging risk” to gas suppliesat the turn of the decade if an important piece of kit – an import terminal, say, or the critical 725-mile Langeled pipeline from Norway – were to be out of the action during a severe cold snap.“In the unlikely event of the loss of the single largest piece of gas infrastructure, gas supply falls short of demand for all pathways in 2030-31,” saidthe reportfrom the government’s in-house adviser, the National Energy System Operator (Neso). Unlikely maybe – but the risk can’t be wished away, which was the point of the report.Now here comes Centrica,owner of British Gas, warning that it will close its Rough gas storage facility off the coast of Yorkshire unless it can agree a long-term financial support deal with the government to back a £2bn expansion. Rough is Britain’s largest such facility and, although Centrica has been issuing dire warnings for a while, it clearly means it this time. It has set a date for closure next April, in the absence of a deal. The company isn’t even injecting gas into Rough for this winter.“I think this [Rough] is essential for the UK energy security,” said Centrica’s chief executive, Chris O’Shea, on Thursday. “I think it would not be good if this was to be allowed to close, and that’s why it’s really a decision for government now. What we hear a lot is that this is a commercial decision forCentrica. That is patently not true.”On his commercial point, O’Shea is clearly correct. The economics of storage rely on buying low and selling high, but winter/summer spreads are often narrower these days. Disruption caused by the closure of the strait of Hormuz worked in Centrica’s favour at Rough as it made a £57m top-line profit at the facility in the half-year, but last year it lost almost as much. That is not a basis on which to sanction a £2bn investment without some form of regulated return.Whether Rough is the right storage project for the country is another matter.Gasfolk refer to it as “a big balloon with a small straw”, meaning it can hold large volumes but extraction can be slow at the moment they’re urgently needed. Maybe that feature could be re-engineered away if the £2bn investment happens. Or maybe a different storage solution would be preferable – more on-shore salt caverns, for example, which comprise most of the rest of Britain’s storage capacity. Or the economics favours other ideas in the Neso report: expanding interconnector capacity to the continent, or building more liquefied natural gas import terminals (Britain only has three). Or a combination of the above.The future of North Sea gas production is not incidental to this debate either. The basin may be in long-term decline, but the rate of decline matters (and Jackdaw alone would be 6% of domestic production). If the gas system operator knows the stuff is coming, that is storage by proxy.The point is that a joined-up strategy is needed. Gas still accounted for 35% of the UK’s total energy demand in 2024,according to government statistics. Even if it dwindles to next to nothing for the purposes of electricity generation by 2030 with the rollout of renewables, demand for gas for domestic heating will be slower to shift, as there are 24m households with a gas connection.The short-term question for Fahnbulleh, then, is whether to pay Centrica to fill Rough for this winter. The argument in favour is that storage on the continent, which Britain usually taps during the cold months, is projected to be low after the Hormuz disruption. There is a risk of price spikes. A fuller Rough might dampen them.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |Every weekdaySign up toBusiness TodayGet set for the working day – we'll point you to all the business news and analysis you need every morningPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionThe longer-term issue is the overall strategy to decide where the gas will come from in 2030. The storage question is central, and so is the policy on North Sea production. There is a strong sense that both have been dodged until now.The government’s interim response toits own “gas system in transition” reviewis due within weeks. Real choices have to follow because none of these projects – more connectors, more terminals, or more storage – will be quick to build if they are needed. In the meantime, that “emerging risk” of a supply crunch has, presumably, already emerged a bit further.Explore more on these topicsGasNils Pratley on financeEnergy industryCommoditiesCentricaMiatta FahnbullehEnergy storageEnergycommentShareReuse this content

title: I am used to hot days now. But the warm wind has really panicked me | Adrian Chiles | The Guardian
description: I first noticed it a couple of weeks ago on the streets of Cardiff – and it felt alarmingly wrong, writes Adrian Chiles
Author: Adrian Chiles
Headline: I first noticed it a couple of weeks ago on the streets of Cardiff – and it felt alarmingly wrong, writes Adrian Chiles
Content: Warm winds … an unpleasant blast. (Posed by model)Photograph: Liudmila Chernetska/Getty ImagesView image in fullscreenWarm winds … an unpleasant blast. (Posed by model)Photograph: Liudmila Chernetska/Getty ImagesOpinionClimate crisisI am used to hot days now. But the warm wind has really panicked meAdrian ChilesI first noticed it a couple of weeks ago on the streets of Cardiff – and it felt alarmingly wrongThu 23 Jul 2026 06.00 EDTLast modified on Thu 23 Jul 2026 12.29 EDTShareWhen the wind is warm, you know something is up. Naturally, I have been well aware of the awful reality of the climate crisis, but it has taken the warmth of the wind to really panic me. Not the endless 30C+ days, the desiccated garden, the tap slowing to a drip. No, it is the warm wind blowing on me. I can just about manage in punishing heat, even though it is particularly suffocating when the air is still. But I thought I had a tacit agreement with the British climate that at least when there was a breeze it would cool me down a bit. When a breeze warms me up, here in the UK anyway, it feels nothing less than un-British, and I wonder if we really are getting into the end of days.I first noticed it a couple of weeks ago on the streets of Cardiff. I turned a corner on St Mary’s Street and there it was, a strong gust of properly warm wind, right in my face and down into my lungs. Reeling, gasping a little, I looked around for the idling bus that had surely generated this unpleasant blast of air. But this wind hadn’t emanated from a bus’s rear, for there was no bus in sight. It was the weather and it felt alarmingly wrong.I have spent much time in southWalesand the weather has thrown a lot at me over the years. It’s one of the many things I love about it here: rain hammering down on a caravan roof; rain coming at you horizontally; wind to lift you off your feet; tempests whipping the sea into a frenzy; fog so thick that you can’t see the sea even when it is lapping around your ankles. And punctuating these various weather events are frequent spells of sunshine lighting up the colours so brightly that your eyeballs ache. But a wind that is warm? Never.The last time I felt wind this warm it was coming off the Gulf of Mexico into Galveston. In Texas, it was tolerable. It came with the territory. But blowing hotly into Cardiff off the Bristol Channel? Nah, sorry. All wrong. We’re doomed.Adrian Chiles is a broadcaster, writer and Guardian columnistDo you have an opinion on the issues raised in this article? If you would like to submit a response of up to 300 words by email to be considered for publication in ourletterssection, pleaseclick hereExplore more on these topicsClimate crisisOpinionUK weatherWalescommentShareReuse this content

title: Like Trump, I went through a home-renovation obsession. The results are often disappointing | Jackie Delamatre | The Guardian
description: We are now a nation of homogenized open concepts and curb appeal. It’s an approach that leads to anxiety and isolation
Author: Guardian staff reporter
Headline: We are now a nation of homogenized open concepts and curb appeal. It’s an approach that leads to anxiety and isolation
Content: ‘Why do any of us put ourselves through this torturous process?’Photograph: Andrew Leyden/Zuma Press Wire/ShutterstockView image in fullscreen‘Why do any of us put ourselves through this torturous process?’Photograph: Andrew Leyden/Zuma Press Wire/ShutterstockOpinionDonald TrumpLike Trump, I went through a home-renovation obsession. The results are often disappointingJackie DelamatreWe are now a nation of homogenized open concepts and curb appeal. It’s an approach that leads to anxiety and isolationThu 23 Jul 2026 10.00 EDTLast modified on Thu 23 Jul 2026 11.44 EDTShareRecently, Jonathan Swan – one of the authors of a new tell-all about Donald Trump’s second term –told the hosts of The Viewthat if you had a pie chart of Trump’s mind, “a pretty substantial slice is devoted to interior decorating”. He also revealed that Trump is engaged in “design wars” with Melania and moved furniture she had selected into his spaces. The commander-in-chief apparently has even been caught supergluing gilded accents to the Oval Office mantel himself. Though Trump claims taxpayers won’t be on the hook for the costs of his renovations, the New York Times reported that the White House’s 18 major remodeling projects – from the botched Lincoln Memorial reflecting pool to the paved-over Rose Garden to the looming “Arc de Trump” – will probablycome with a price tagof $1.2bn.Of course, Trump’s renovation compulsion didn’t start this year. The president celebrated winning his first term, in 2017, by ripping out the Oval Office’s royal blue carpet with the presidential seal andreplacing itwith a Mar-a-Lago peach version, accented with – what else? – gold. But in his second term, he’s gotten bolder, both politically and aesthetically – and more addicted. By the autumn of his first year back in office, he went ahead andtore downthe East Wing entirely.DC’s ‘renovated’ pool reflects the Trump administration’s dangerous hubris | Arwa MahdawiRead moreAnd, OK, I get it. In January, my husband and I bought a money pit of a house. In this economy, we counted ourselves lucky. But, within the first 24 hours of ownership, we had a fire, a leak, and the boiler burst, filling the basement with several inches of rusty water. There went the aesthetic tweaks we’d planned, not to mention the furniture. The problem was that my Instagram algorithm had already picked up on my new life stage. Soon, I was plagued by an irrational, unyielding fear of ugly rooms. I wanted nothing but to designmaxx. I began to give up an inordinate amount of my brainpower to rules like: “One throw per sofa, draped not folded. Folded is a store display. Draped is a home.” I actually battled it out with my tween over the “rule of three”. In desperation, one day, encouraged by aZillow studyabout which hues raise property values, I painted a room sage green only to realize all my furniture clashed. (I believe they call it “scope creep”.) If, at that moment, I’d been supplied with an Ikea hack for transforming discount chair cushions into a padded room, I would have felt I’d alighted upon the perfect decorating scheme.In other words, this stuff could make anyone crazy. In onesurvey, 12% of new couples (together five years or less) considered separating or divorcing during the remodeling process. Inanother, more than two-thirds of millennials reported moderate to high stress amid remodeling, with 40% saying it strained their relationships. Yet so many of us keep coming back for more. What began with the Puritans and their stonewalls, their European notions of private property, has spawned an entire channel devoted to renos. On HGTV, the Property Brothers alone have 10 spin-offs. When Edith Wharton tried to sell her interior design guide, The Decoration of Homes, in 1897, it was rejected by publishers. No one, they told her, would be interested. But the interior design market hit over$185bn globally in 2025– and is projected to reach $298bn by 2033. We are now a nation of open concepts and curb appeal and pops of color, of mid-century and farmhouse sinks and shiplap and Homegoods, homes that look “good”, yes, but often strikingly the same.In arecent essayfor the New Yorker, Jia Tolentino describes a similar homogenizing phenomenon with faces, surgically or AI-enhanced, “increasingly, more technically beautiful but more spiritually unattractive”. Trump’s design choices may not be your standard Joanna Gaines style, but, in Washington DC, the decorating analogue for Mar-a-Lago face is massive, gilded anything. All this uniformity is enough to make you long for Jerry Seinfeld’s cluttered cereal shelves or the Friends’ violet walls or even Archie Bunker’s threadbare wing chair.Why do any of us put ourselves through this torturous process? Is it all for resale value? To keep up with the Joneses? Or for the positiveemotional effectsof built environment factors like natural light, wood furniture and high ceilings? Most perplexing of all: why is Trump, in the middle of a war, plunging approval polls and an inflation crisis, devoting mental space to the future of a home he doesn’t even own? Is it a classic response to feeling out of control, or perhaps an attempt at denying reality? (“Like the Administration,” writes Tolentino, “Mar-a-Lago Face locates power in the spectacle of dismissing reality altogether.”) Or is Trump just the most extreme version of our narcissistic instinct to put our stamp on spaces, to claim ownership, like a boy with a Sharpie tagging everything around him?‘Make your homes weird,’ urges an interior designer. Me? I’ve a stuffed magpie and three pewter goats | Emma BeddingtonRead moreUnfortunately for our president, one thing I’ve learned is that on the other side of a reno does not always lie satisfaction. Only 52% of couples reported feeling happier in their newly renovated homes. In the HBO show DTF St Louis, the female lead’sraison d’êtreis a redo of her son’s bedroom decor. Spoiler alert: she gets it but loses a lot more. And Annetta Grant, now an associate professor at Bucknell University, confirmed this kind of disappointment in her 2023study, with a nod to HGTV’s impact. Americans, she learned, have stopped designing their houses for themselves. Because of home-improvement media (read: everything from Better Homes and Gardens to Pinterest), increasingly, the changes homeowners are making are for resale value, or as she puts it, “a market-reflected gaze” – and that means they decorate in a more and more homogenized style. It’s an approach that leads to anxiety and isolation, as homeowners worry about being judged by visitors at the same time that they don’t feel their homes reflect their own preferences.Recently, I started to lust after the kind of homes where no one is thinking about the proper way to size a rug – homes you can relax in, homes covered in Legos and board game pieces. I blocked social media on my phone in an attempt to limit the kinds of Instagram visits that convince me I need a custom monogrammed cross stitch kitchen towel. Instead, I started reading Middlemarch. In it, George Eliot writes that a “betrothed bride” often “dictates” changes to her future home before marriage only to discover: “The mistakes that we male and female mortals make when we have our own way might fairly raise some wonder that we are so fond of it.” We may think what we want is what we want, but it’s often not what we need at all.Trump, for his part, may think what he needs is a new ballroom complete with bunker or just one more gilded vase, when what he really requires is a gut reno of his relationship with himself – and the American public.Jackie Delamatre is a freelance writer based in Providence, Rhode IslandExplore more on these topicsDonald TrumpOpinionHomescommentShareReuse this content

title: In a fractious, divided world, I think I know why Pride is the hottest show in town | Emma Brockes | The Guardian
description: The 2014 film was only a modest success – but now the musical’s message of gay rights and solidarity feels both outlandish and full of hope, says columnist Emma Brockes
Author: Emma Brockes
Headline: The 2014 film was only a modest success – but now the musical’s message of gay rights and solidarity feels both outlandish and full of hope, says columnist Emma Brockes
Content: The cast of Pride at the National Theatre, London.Photograph: Manuel HarlanView image in fullscreenThe cast of Pride at the National Theatre, London.Photograph: Manuel HarlanOpinionPrideIn a fractious, divided world, I think I know why Pride is the hottest show in townEmma BrockesThe 2014 film was only a modest success – but now the musical’s message of gay rights and solidarity feels both outlandish and full of hopeThu 23 Jul 2026 05.00 EDTLast modified on Thu 23 Jul 2026 05.04 EDTShareHands down the hottest ticket in London this summer isthe musical Prideat the National Theatre, which is booked solid until the end of its run in September and will presumably transfer to the West End after that. There are lots of gay shows doing well at the moment: I sawOh, Mary!for the second time this week when the show’s creator, Cole Escola, came over from New York to resume the title role, andOperation Mincemeatkeeps going and going. But both those shows are tonally rather than politically gay. Pride, which is adapted from the 2014 film and had modest commercial success when it came out, is a different kettle of fish and I’m curious as to why it’s flown now.I should say up front I haven’t seen the show (can’t get a ticket!). But this week, as I watched my timeline continue to fill up with effusive praise and lots of crying about the musical, Iwent back to the film. It’s a strange time to watch it, in a news cycle in which issues around sexuality in public life and old views about gay rights have surfaced to yank us back to earlier times. Thedeath of the US senatorLindsey Graham triggered widespread speculation about what, exactly, Donald Trump held over him and gave rise to a lot of gay jokes pulled out of the deep freeze.Pride is set during the 1984 miners’ strike, whenan unlikely alliance sprang upbetween a group of lesbians and gay men in London and striking miners in a Welsh town. Stephen Beresford, who wrote the film and the stage production, tells the story of LGSM – Lesbians and Gays Support the Miners – a group set up by a young activist from County Antrim, Mark Ashton. I imagine there were hopes that the film would do a Billy Elliot, but neither British nor American audiences overly responded. Although the film was critically applauded, it didn’t break through.Anyway, I don’t want to be too much of a buzzkill , because the whole point aboutPrideis that it doesn’t feel like homework and I would urge everyone who can’t get a ticket to the show to go back to the movie, which stars Andrew Scott (who must by law be in everything), a really good Dominic West, and Jessica Gunning, who was by far the best thing in Baby Reindeer. Ashton is played by a brilliant Ben Schnetzer and, compared with the entire output of, say, Russell T Davies, Beresford seems actually interested in the women in the story. I’m sorry, but I have to quote some key lines: “Give it to the lesbians, they love a banner”; “Police harassment, dear, I could set it to music”; “Don’t be daft, love. We’ve come all the way from Powys.”That last line is uttered by Imelda Staunton, who will make you laugh and break your heart, and the main takeaway is solidarity, which viewed through a 2026 lens is perhaps the most outlandish thing about it. In this world of feverishly defended political differences between people on the same side, Pride tells a different story, in which vastly different groups organise around a common cause. Paddy Considine as the Welsh miner Dai Donovan was the first person to accept help from the “perverts”, as they were referred to in the popular press in those days, and says it best: “It’s more than money, it’s friendship.”Perhaps that’s what’s done it, this time around. The Dorfman theatre at the National only holds 450 seats, which has given the show a way to build an audience without the pressure of filling a bigger theatre. It’s true, too, that gays will go to see a show we love several times, which definitely helps. But in this era in which all we hear is that we’re living in political end times, my sense is that this show offers something unusual for everyone. It is both shocking and moving to be reminded of how bad things once were, and what could still be achieved.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |WeeklySign up toMatters of OpinionGuardian columnists and writers on what they’ve been debating, thinking about, reading, and morePreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionEmma Brockes is a Guardian columnist. She is the author of She Left Me The Gun: My Mother’s Life Before MeExplore more on these topicsPrideOpinionNational TheatreThe miners’ strike 1984-85commentShareReuse this content

title: The Guardian view on Britain’s uninhabitable homes: as temperatures rise, a new approach is needed  | Editorial | The Guardian
description: Editorial: Sadiq Khan’s heat-ready plan for London is a sensible strategy for adaptation that should provide a roadmap for other cities
Author: Editorial
Headline: Editorial: Sadiq Khan’s heat-ready plan for London is a sensible strategy for adaptation that should provide a roadmap for other cities
Content: ‘With no national strategy for dealing with oppressive heat, many homeowners have installed air conditioning.’Photograph: David Taljat/Getty ImagesView image in fullscreen‘With no national strategy for dealing with oppressive heat, many homeowners have installed air conditioning.’Photograph: David Taljat/Getty ImagesOpinionHousingThe Guardian view on Britain’s uninhabitable homes: as temperatures rise, a new approach is neededEditorialSadiq Khan’s heat-ready plan for London is a sensible strategy for adaptation that should provide a roadmap for other citiesThu 23 Jul 2026 13.21 EDTLast modified on Thu 23 Jul 2026 21.30 EDTShare“Our ability to trust that our homes will keep us safe from the outside is under threat,” the geographer Sam Johnson-Schlee writes in Hot House,his recent book on how heat has transformed domestic life. This summer, high temperatures have become inescapable. People have coped with makeshift solutions: keeping curtains drawn, placing foil over windows or – for those who can afford one – plugging in an air-conditioning unit. Planning experts warn that, with only about half of councils requiring new buildings to include cooling strategies, many homes will become “death traps” as temperatures rise.Britain’s homesweren’t builtto cope with this. The country’s historically mild climate allowed developers to build houses without insulation, while industrialisation supplied plentiful coal to burn when temperatures dropped. Many homes are consequently drafty in winter and sweltering in summer. Unlike those in Mediterranean climates, British homes lack shutters and shaded communal spaces. WhileIldefons Cerdà, Barcelona’s masterplanner, was designing housing blocks with cool courtyards and chamfered edges to allow breezes to flow, Britain’s Victorian builders were throwing up rows of terrace houses – manywithout cross-ventilation– and giving little thought to the movement of the sun.View image in fullscreenSir Sadiq Khan.Photograph: Ben Whitley/PAA new approach is overdue. Insulation would help to keep properties cool, but schemes to encourage it have languished. The promisinggreen homes grant, launched in 2020 as part of Boris Johnson’s pledge to “build back greener”, was the victim of a political culture that prized soundbites over meaningful policy. The scheme gave upfront vouchers to homeowners and landlords to upgrade their insulation, but collapsed after just six months, with the public accounts committee saying that it had been “set up to fail” owing to an impossible timetable and rushed design.With no national strategy for dealing with oppressive heat, many homeowners have installed air conditioning. There are nowtwice the numberof air-conditioned homes in Britain that there were three years ago. While this technology has a role to play, it should form part of a broader cooling strategy alongside less energy-intensive techniques. Adopted en masse, air conditioning raises the urban temperature by expelling hot air, making life even warmer for people without it.A recent plan from the mayor of London, Sir Sadiq Khan, sketches a welcome alternative. The capital is more affected by extreme temperatures than anywhere in Britain due to itsdistinct heat island effect. London’s heat-ready plan puts forward a collective response based on sensible measures that should serve as a roadmap for other cities, such as retrofitting homes with shutters, installing air conditioning inschoolsand other public buildings, planting more trees for shade, and providing more swimming spots where people can take a dip.There is much to admire here. But Sir Sadiq’s plan collides with a political reality that poses severe challenges to adaptation. In a fiscal climate hostile to public investment, it will demand many billions of public money to deliver, particularly since such changes, its authors note, “do not always translate into clear financial savings or returns”. The new prime minister, Andy Burnham, has made much of his vision for a politics that is more attuned to place, but has so far remained quiet about the climate crisis. Its terrifying impact on the places where we live should make this threat an urgent priority.Do you have an opinion on the issues raised in this article? If you would like to submit a response of up to 300 words by email to be considered for publication in ourletterssection, pleaseclick here.Explore more on these topicsHousingOpinionExtreme heatLondonClimate crisisUK weathercommentShareReuse this content

title: The Guardian view on Europe’s demographic crisis: the young must not be left to deal with the consequences | Editorial | The Guardian
description: Editorial: A new Brussels study highlights the pace at which EU countries are ageing. A more informed public debate is overdue
Author: Editorial
Headline: Editorial: A new Brussels study highlights the pace at which EU countries are ageing. A more informed public debate is overdue
Content: ‘It cannot be left to younger generations to pick up the pieces of a broken social model.’Photograph: ANP/ShutterstockView image in fullscreen‘It cannot be left to younger generations to pick up the pieces of a broken social model.’Photograph: ANP/ShutterstockOpinionAgeingThe Guardian view on Europe’s demographic crisis: the young must not be left to deal with the consequencesEditorialA new Brussels study highlights the pace at which EU countries are ageing. A more informed public debate is overdueThu 23 Jul 2026 13.22 EDTLast modified on Thu 23 Jul 2026 21.30 EDTShareEurope is ageing at pace as birthrates fall and people live longer. A newreportby the European Commission, published this month, notes that 2012 marked the first year in which fewer people were born in EU countries than people died in them. By the middle of this century, nearly one in three EU residents will be 65 or older. The numbers of those over 80 will almost triple from 6% to 15%. The old continent is getting visibly greyer.The transition to a “longevity society”, as the report’s authors describe it, is something to be celebrated. Many people are enjoying healthier, richer lives for longer, andredefiningthe meaning and possibilities of old age. But the statistics and direction of travel also point to immense social challenges, with implications for the way we work, intergenerational fairness and the future funding of care. Sadly, amid fraught and at times toxic debates about immigration and the cost of welfare, Europe’s politics is struggling to meet the moment.A relativedeclinein the size of the working-age population underlines the crucial role that non-EU migrants will continue to play in easing demographic pressures. The zero-net-migration future dreamed of by far-right parties would leave the EU’s population an estimated 32% smaller by 2100, dramatically increasing the fiscal burden on those in work. Yet mainstream politicians avoid highlighting this inconvenient truth, and devoting the necessary resources to fostering integration, while competing with the far right to create an ever more hostileenvironmentfor asylum seekers.On welfare, the framing of the debate is increasingly dominated by the idea that Europe’s postwar social model – conceived in an era of population growth – is no longer affordable. That is a politically dangerous calculation. Since the 2008 crash, resentment at austerity programmes introduced across Europe has beeninstrumentalin the rise of the authoritarian populism which imperils the EU’s democratic values and social fabric. In deeply unequal societies, themessagethat the less well-off will simply have to work harder and longer for less will further fuel the politics of the far right.The argument for more cuts is also economically flawed. Future growth and productivity in demographically challenged communities will depend on making it easier for more people to participate in a flexible and accessible labour market. From the reform of social care to offering affordable childcare and sustained help for the long-term unemployed, that will require renewed investment to unlock dormant potential. The related issue ofrural depopulationand decline will also need to be addressed, as the young increasingly gravitate to cities in search of opportunities that can no longer be found where they grew up.The factors underlying what is a global fertilitycrisisare complex. But this is a problem that is here to stay. In Europe, public policies designed to increase the birthrate have signallyfailedto shift the dial, and the European Commission’s study indicates that the pace of demographic change is quicker than anticipated. Nevertheless, beyond controversies over changes to retirement ages, the long-term consequences and implications continue to fly under the radar.A bigger, more high-profile debate is overdue. It cannot be left to younger generations to pick up the pieces of a broken social model which should have been renewed and redesigned while we had the chance.Do you have an opinion on the issues raised in this article? title: Iran crisis is not solely a product of Trump’s rule | US-Israel war on Iran | The Guardian
description: Letters: Focusing on Donald Trump’s defects risks making war appear as an aberration rather than as a recurring feature of American power, writes David Eaton. Plus letters from Blaine Stothard and Harvey Gilbert
Author: Guardian staff reporter
Headline: Letters: Focusing on Donald Trump’s defects risks making war appear as an aberration rather than as a recurring feature of American power, writes David Eaton. Plus letters from Blaine Stothard and Harvey Gilbert
Content: Donald Trump and Pete Hegseth salute at a dignified transfer for US army service members killed in the Iran war, at Dover air force base in Delaware, US, on 22 July.Photograph: Matt Rourke/APView image in fullscreenDonald Trump and Pete Hegseth salute at a dignified transfer for US army service members killed in the Iran war, at Dover air force base in Delaware, US, on 22 July.Photograph: Matt Rourke/APUS-Israel war on IranLettersIran crisis is not solely a product of Trump’s ruleFocusing on Donald Trump’s defects risks making war appear as an aberration rather than as a recurring feature of American power, writesDavid Eaton. Plus letters fromBlaine StothardandHarvey GilbertThu 23 Jul 2026 11.26 EDTLast modified on Thu 23 Jul 2026 12.21 EDTShareSimon Tisdall’s article (Trump, not Iran, is the world’s greatest danger. He’s a one-man weapon of mass destruction, 18 July) is a forceful polemic, but it risks turning a structural problem into a personal one. If Donald Trump is uniquely reckless, that deserves criticism. Yet the article implies that America’s present predicament is chiefly the consequence of his character, rather than asking why successive administrations, Republican and Democrat alike, have repeatedly resorted to military intervention in the Middle East. To focus on Trump’s defects risks making war itself appear as an aberration rather than as a recurring feature of American power.The same applies to the strait of Hormuz. Its strategic importance does not begin or end with one president. It reflects a global economic order that depends on secure energy supplies, trade routes and the projection of military power to guarantee them. That broader context is largely absent.The article also assumes that a more competent president, acting with Congress, allies and international law on side, would have avoided today’s crisis. History offers little comfort. We have been here before: the Iraq, Afghanistan and Libya conflicts were carried out under far more conventional presidencies.Trump may be the spectacle, but he is not the problem. The article focuses on the drama rather than the system that keeps producing it.David EatonSunderlandSimon Tisdall is right to identify the dangers to the world presented by the Trump administration and the president himself. Tisdall enumerates the recent damage, death and destruction that the Trump administration has presided over, seeing current excesses and abuses of power as dangers to the rest of us, as indeed they are. But the current excesses, overt and covert, can be seen as a continuation of American history and practice that not only has led to the 20th century being seen as the American century, but also as the century of US wars.Since the end of the first world war, we have seen the US initiate the war for oil, the war against communism, the war against drugs, the war against terror and the war against Islam. All continue to have negative impacts on the rest of the world, and perpetuate American foreign policy arrogance and belligerence. All these wars failed to achieve their supposed aims. They have also provided cover for other tyrannical governments to act in similar, if less universal, fashion. I’m not sure we should see Trump as an aberration – more as a more shameless manifestation of what has always been there.Blaine StothardLondonI agree wholeheartedly with Simon Tisdall, but he didn’t answer his own question: who will stop Donald Trump? The polls showhis weakness, but the upcoming elections may not because the Democrats are so weak and Trump will inevitably confuse the results to his own benefit. The answer to the question must lie in the Republican party itself. Surely there are enough right-thinking Republicans to turn votes against him and force some control over his behaviour. It is beginning to happen.This may be the way to begin to bring him to heel, but it creates further issues before any real resolution. The immediate problem for the American people and the world is twofold. First, as the “wounded bear”, Trump will continue to do catastrophic damage before November’s elections. And second,Marco Rubioto take over? God save us all.Harvey GilbertMilton KeynesHave an opinion on anything you’ve read in the Guardian today? Pleaseemailus your letter and it will be considered for publication in ourletterssection.Explore more on these topicsUS-Israel war on IranDonald TrumpIranMiddle East and north AfricaUS foreign policylettersShareReuse this content

title: Regulation cannot eliminate surrogacy risks  | Surrogacy | The Guardian
description: Letter: Lexi Ellingsworth of Stop Surrogacy Now UK responds to news that the rise in the use of surrogates abroad is leaving more babies at risk of becoming stateless
Author: Guardian staff reporter
Headline: Letter: Lexi Ellingsworth of Stop Surrogacy Now UK responds to news that the rise in the use of surrogates abroad is leaving more babies at risk of becoming stateless
Content: ‘Every surrogacy arrangement begins with the deliberate separation of a newborn baby from their mother.’Photograph: GettyView image in fullscreen‘Every surrogacy arrangement begins with the deliberate separation of a newborn baby from their mother.’Photograph: GettySurrogacyLettersRegulation cannot eliminate surrogacy risksLexi Ellingsworthof Stop Surrogacy Now UKresponds to news that the rise in the use of surrogates abroad is leaving more babies at risk of becoming statelessThu 23 Jul 2026 11.25 EDTLast modified on Thu 23 Jul 2026 12.21 EDTShareYour article (Rise in overseas surrogates ‘increases risk of stateless babies, 19 July)suggests that the solution lies in better regulation to make surrogacy safe and ethical. But regulation cannot remove the central ethical problem: every surrogacy arrangement begins with the deliberate separation of a newborn baby from their mother, who they have naturally bonded with throughout the pregnancy.Regulation is also not able to eliminate the physical risks borne exclusively by surrogate mothers. Astudyin Canada has suggested that surrogate mothers aremore likely to experience severe complicationsincluding postpartum haemorrhage and pre-eclampsia than women who conceived naturally or with IVF.Regulation cannot eliminate the risks and realities of surrogacy, and the evidence referenced in the article should give decision-makers pause. Countries and US states that have introduced detailed regulatory frameworks continue to reportconcernsabout theexploitationof economically vulnerable women, inadequate informed consent and difficulties with oversight and enforcement. Laws may change the way that surrogacy is organised, but they have not consistently prevented abuses or addressed the inequalities that drive many women to engage with surrogacy.Rather than assuming that better regulation will make surrogacy ethical, we should ask whether a practice that depends on separating a baby from their birth mother, while exposing women to significant medical risks and creating opportunities for exploitation, can ever truly be made safe.Lexi EllingsworthFounder, StopSurrogacyNow UKHave an opinion on anything you’ve read in the Guardian today? Pleaseemailus your letter and it will be considered for publication in ourletterssection.Explore more on these topicsSurrogacyPregnancyParents and parentingFamilyWomenHealth & wellbeingChildrenlettersShareReuse this content

title: Aid cuts will harm lives and livelihoods in Somalia  | Foreign policy | The Guardian
description: Letter: Humanitarian support keeps families buying food, shops trading and clinics open when crisis hits, says Abdullahi Nur Osman
Author: Guardian staff reporter
Headline: Letter: Humanitarian support keeps families buying food, shops trading and clinics open when crisis hits, says Abdullahi Nur Osman
Content: Somali school pupils displaced by drought on the outskirts of Kismayo, southern Somalia.Photograph: Simon Maina/AFP/Getty ImagesView image in fullscreenSomali school pupils displaced by drought on the outskirts of Kismayo, southern Somalia.Photograph: Simon Maina/AFP/Getty ImagesForeign policyLettersAid cuts will harm lives and livelihoods in SomaliaHumanitarian support keeps families buying food, shops trading and clinics open when crisis hits, saysAbdullahi Nur OsmanThu 23 Jul 2026 11.25 EDTLast modified on Thu 23 Jul 2026 12.21 EDTShareForeign Office figures showing that the UK will almost halve bilateral aid to Somalia by 2029 (Report, 16 July) come as families across the country brace for yet another climate emergency.Forecasters are warning that rains in Somalia in October could bring catastrophic flooding, displacing thousands. TheWorld Meteorological Organizationhas flagged a heightened risk of extreme rainfall, which will strike communities still recovering from a severe drought that has left 6 million people facing high levels of acute food insecurity.For Somali families, this means facing yet another major disruption to their lives and livelihoods, and having less humanitarian support available to those affected.Britain has long supportedSomaliathrough some of its hardest years, and its ambition to use trade, investment and private sector partnership to support Africa’s development is welcome.However, the decision to channel available funds into multilateral funders like the World Bank cannot happen at the expense of aid provision. Humanitarian support keeps families buying food, shops trading and clinics open when crisis hits. Without it, each crisis will leave less for businesses and investors to build on.Aid and investment must go hand in hand. One helps countries weather the emergency ahead; the other helps build the strength to face the next. Britain cannot expect investment to succeed if it withdraws aid too soon.Abdullahi Nur OsmanChief executive, Hormuud Salaam FoundationHave an opinion on anything you’ve read in the Guardian today? Pleaseemailus your letter and it will be considered for publication in ourletterssection.Explore more on these topicsForeign policyAidSomaliaAfricaForeign, Commonwealth and Development OfficeLabourlettersShareReuse this content

title: No matter your age, you can always enjoy a rave | Clubbing | The Guardian
description: Letter: Brian F Nattrass went to a festival to celebrate his 80th birthday, started dancing and suddenly felt ageless. Plus a letter from John Parry
Author: Guardian staff reporter
Headline: Letter: Brian F Nattrass went to a festival to celebrate his 80th birthday, started dancing and suddenly felt ageless. Plus a letter from John Parry
Content: ‘I was initially afraid that the dance style I learned 60 years ago in the age of rock would look really out of it. But nope.’Photograph: GettyView image in fullscreen‘I was initially afraid that the dance style I learned 60 years ago in the age of rock would look really out of it. But nope.’Photograph: GettyClubbingLettersNo matter your age, you can always enjoy a raveBrian F Nattrasswent to a festival to celebrate his 80th birthday, started dancing and suddenly felt ageless. Plus a letter fromJohn ParryThu 23 Jul 2026 11.24 EDTLast modified on Thu 23 Jul 2026 11.26 EDTShareThanks for an inspiring and timely article (The rave secret: why middle-aged women are larging it – and seeing big health benefits, 20 July). My 37-year-old daughter recently invited me to a festival to celebrate my 80th birthday. There were raves each night for three nights in a row. I learned to dance in the late 50s and 60s, during the era of Elvis Presley and the Beatles. I used to absolutely love dancing and used to party in clubs well into my late 30s. But reading your article, I realised that I hadn’t danced in over 40 years!When my daughter invited me to a festival, naturally I had all the self-doubts and concerns you mentioned in your article, including “internalised ageism”. But I love and trust my daughter. She said: “Dad, you’ll do great!” So I went, and I did – dancing with total strangers until 4-5am every night for three nights.It was so incredibly fun. Feeling my body move to the beat was so tangible and felt so right, and it was so energising. I was initially afraid that the dance style I learned 60 years ago in the age of rock would look really out of it. But nope. Things on the dancefloor haven’t changed that much. And once I started dancing and felt the beat of the music, I completely forgot my age of 80 years; I became ageless.Dancing awakened a sense of physical vibrancy that I had forgotten. I definitely intend to rave on. It feels so good!Brian F NattrassGibsons, British Columbia, CanadaAs a 63-year-old man who still goes dancing, there were no surprises in this article for me. Around 10 times a year I go clubbing or to festivals with dance stages/tents. The shared feelings of euphoria with polite and kind people from every generation far outweigh the tiredness the day after. I’m thoroughly convinced of the extended health benefits of raving for six-ish hours (in my case with ongoing rheumatoid arthritis). And leaving the club (or wherever) as dawn breaks just makes me feel completely alive. If you like dancing, go dancing. It’s lovely.John ParryTorremolinos, SpainExplore more on these topicsClubbingAgeingMiddle ageWomenMental healthPsychologyThe BeatleslettersShareReuse this content

title: Falkland Islands bowler criticises ‘disappointing’ Argentina World Cup banner | Commonwealth Games | The Guardian
description: The Falkland Islands bowler Cecil Alexander called Argentina’s World Cup banner ‘disappointing’ after his opening match at the Commonwealth Games
Author: Yara El-Shaboury
Headline: The Falkland Islands bowler Cecil Alexander called Argentina’s World Cup banner ‘disappointing’ after his opening match at the Commonwealth Games
Content: The Falkland Islands team, including Cecil Alexander (third left), at the Commonwealth Games opening ceremony in Glasgow.Photograph: Aaron Chown/PAView image in fullscreenThe Falkland Islands team, including Cecil Alexander (third left), at the Commonwealth Games opening ceremony in Glasgow.Photograph: Aaron Chown/PACommonwealth GamesFalkland Islands bowler criticises ‘disappointing’ Argentina World Cup bannerCecil Alexander is competing at Commonwealth GamesJohnson-Thompson and Asher-Smith pull out of athleticsYara El-Shabouryat SEC CentreThu 23 Jul 2026 13.30 EDTLast modified on Thu 23 Jul 2026 19.19 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleThe Falkland Islands bowler Cecil Alexander called Argentina’s World Cup banner “disappointing” after his opening men’s singles match at theCommonwealth Games.Alexander, 45, made the comments after a straight sets 10-3, 7-4 defeat by the former world No 1 Izzat Shameer Dzulkeple of Malaysia.Final hurrah? Glasgow’s cut-price Commonwealth Games fight for survivalRead moreJust over a week ago at the men’s World Cup, Argentina’s players celebrated their semi-final defeat of Englandby holding up a bannerthat read: “Las Malvinas son Argentinas”, referring to their country’s claim over the Falkland Islands. The banner reignited tensions over the South Atlantic territory, known as the Falkland Islands in Britain and Islas Malvinas in Argentina. The dispute led to the 74-day 1982 Falklands war, where 649 Argentinians and 255 Britons were killed.“It’s disappointing when politics gets mixed with sport,” Alexander told the Guardian regarding Argentina’s banner. “We are here to compete and represent our country. We keep politics out of it.”Alexander, who moved to theFalkland Islandsfrom South Africa eight years ago, said representing the British overseas territory had been a rewarding experience. “I am fairly new to the Falkland Islands but I am enjoying [living there],” he said.Argentina’s celebrations prompted the now former prime minister of the United Kingdom, Keir Starmer, to back calls for Fifa to investigate whether any rules were breached when the banner was displayed.View image in fullscreenArgentina footballers hold up a banner referring to their country’s claim over the Falkland Islands after their World Cup semi-final win over England.Photograph: Rebecca Blackwell/APStarmer’s spokesperson had said: “The World Cup might not be ours, but the Falkland Islands definitely are. Our position is unchanged. Self-determination rests with the islanders and our commitment to the Falklands will never waver.“More broadly, potential action is a matter for Fifa, but it’s been a fantastic World Cup and we’ve said throughout that politics should stay out of football.”Bowls is one of the original six sports on the Commonwealth Games programme and it has been played at every Games since 1930, with the exception of the 1966 Games in Kingston, Jamaica, where no bowls facilities were available. In a Games first, bowls and para bowls are being contested indoors in Glasgow.“We’ve been to other indoor tournaments but it’s the first at the Commonwealth Games,” said Alexander. “A little bit hot. You would think the outdoors would be hotter. But it’s good.”Star athletes add to list of withdrawalsKatarina Johnson-ThompsonandDina Asher-Smithbecame the latest high-profile withdrawals from the Games, dealing a blow to the event on the day of the opening ceremony.The pair are among nine athletes to pull out of Team England’s athletics squad, alongside the Olympic medallists Matthew Hudson-Smith, Daryll Neita and Amber Anning, as well as Zak Seddon, Anna Purchase, Elliot Giles and Jona Efoloko.View image in fullscreenKatarina Johnson-Thompson will not compete in the Commonwealth Games after pulling out.Photograph: Mast Irham/EPAJohnson-Thompson had been aiming for a third successive Commonwealth heptathlon title after winning gold on the Gold Coast in 2018and Birmingham in 2022. Team England did not give a reason for her withdrawal, although it is expected that several athletes are prioritising next month’s European Championships in Birmingham amid a congested schedule.The three-time Olympic medallist Asher-Smith had been due to compete in the women’s 4x100m relay, while Hudson-Smith, Neita and Anning were all expected to strengthen England’s medal prospects.In response to the high-profile absentees the chief executive of the Games, Phil Batty, said he was focused on the quality of talent competing in Glasgow, while also looking at athletes for whom the event will be a springboard to future success.“It’s really important to make sure we have those athletes that are at the pinnacle of their career and you can see that across the sporting programme,” he said. “It’s equally exciting to see – just like what happened in 2014 – [for] which athletes this starts their breakthrough moment.”Niamh Emerson replaces Johnson-Thompson after previously winning Commonwealth bronze behind her in 2018, while Renee Regis, Ebuka Nwokeji, Tara Simpson-Sullivan and Tom Keen also come into the team. The withdrawals follow the Olympic champion Keely Hodgkinson’s decision to skip the Games. The athletics programme begins on Monday.Explore more on these topicsCommonwealth GamesCommonwealth Games 2026Falkland IslandsKatarina Johnson-ThompsonDina Asher-SmithArgentinanewsShareReuse this content

title: Commonwealth Games: 10 things to look out for at Glasgow 2026 | Commonwealth Games 2026 | The Guardian
description: With the Games about to get under way, here are 10 of the most intriguing subplots to the event
Author: Yara El-Shaboury
Headline: With the Games about to get under way, here are 10 of the most intriguing subplots to the event
Content: The Commonwealth Games in Glasgow will feature (from left) Ellie Black, Adam Peaty and Josh Kerr.Composite: Getty Images, ReutersView image in fullscreenThe Commonwealth Games in Glasgow will feature (from left) Ellie Black, Adam Peaty and Josh Kerr.Composite: Getty Images, ReutersCommonwealth Games 2026Commonwealth Games: 10 things to look out for at Glasgow 2026With the Games about to get under way, here are 10 of the most intriguing subplots to the eventYara El-Shabouryin GlasgowThu 23 Jul 2026 03.00 EDTLast modified on Thu 23 Jul 2026 13.07 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on Google1Peaty is back where it all beganTwelve years after winning his first major international title in the 100m breaststroke at Glasgow 2014, Adam Peaty, now 31, heads back to Scotland hoping to remind everyonewhy he is one of Britain’s greatest swimmers. Peaty has won three Olympic gold medals and dominated the event for much of the past decade, but his aura of invincibility has faded since losing his unbeaten streak in 2022 and dealing with injuries and time away from the sport. Glasgow offers an opportunity to complete the circle.2Return of Commonwealth MileFor the first time in 60 years, the mile will replace the traditional 1500m on the programme, reviving an event synonymous with the Games’ early history.
