Branded the Commonwealth Mile, the race’s return was championed by the World Athletics president, Sebastian Coe, who said it was a “quintessential Commonwealth athletics event”. The mile featured at every Games from 1930 until metric distances took over after 1966, but remains the only non‑metric event recognised officially for world records and rankings. Scotland’s Josh Kerr,the new world record-holder, will be favourite for the men’s event and with women contesting the Commonwealth Mile for the first time organisers hope one of athletics’ most romantic distances will once again become one of the Games’ showpiece events.3Can low-cost model save Games?Behind every event sits a much bigger question: can this become the blueprint for the Games’ future? Rather than chasing scale, the Games have embraced simplicity, using only existing venues, reducing travel between sites and dramatically cutting costsafter Victoria’s withdrawal. Commonwealth Sport describes the event as “a bridge to a new era”, arguing that a smaller, financially responsible Games will encourage more cities to bid in future after declining interest from hosts and broadcasters. Sustainability is central to that vision, with reduced carbon emissions, less waste and a place‑based model designed to leave a lasting legacy without burdening taxpayers. Failure of these Games would only intensify the questions over whether they still have a place in an increasingly crowded sporting calendar.View image in fullscreenGlasgow prepares for the Commonwealth Games.Photograph: Jeremy Sutton-Hibbert/The Guardian4Biggest Para programmeWhile the overall Games have shrunk, the Para programme has never been bigger. Glasgow 2026 will stage a record 47 Para medal events in six sports, making it the most extensive Para programme inCommonwealth Gameshistory. Para athletics, swimming, track cycling, bowls, powerlifting and wheelchair 3x3 basketball feature, with the expanded programme demonstrating that cutting costs does not have to come at the expense of representation. Unlike at the Olympics, athletes from dozens of nations will compete alongside their non‑disabled counterparts at the same time rather than after in a standalone event.View image in fullscreenMembers of the men’s and women’s Australian bowls and para bowls team pose for a photo outside their team headquarters in Glasgow.Photograph: Dean Lewins/AAP5Jackson on the comeback trailJamaica’s Shericka Jackson arrives as one of the world’s most decorated sprinters, with five Olympic medals and four world titles, but her appearance comes at a different stage of her career. The 32-year-old is continuing her comeback after the injury problems that disrupted her buildup to Paris 2024 and has made it clear her priority is staying healthy, opting out of the individual 100m and 200m, instead competing only in the women’s 4x100m relay. Having returned to form by reclaiming the Jamaican 100m title and helping her country to win relay gold at the WorldAthleticsRelays, Glasgow offers another opportunity for her to sharpen up.View image in fullscreenShericka Jackson is aiming to get back to her best.Photograph: Kaz Photography/Getty Images6New broadcast eraFor the first time in more than 70 years, the Games will not be shown live on the BBC. Since 1954, the broadcaster has been synonymous with the event, but Glasgow 2026 marks the beginning of a new era after TNT Sportsoutbid the corporation for the television rights in the UK. TNT will show more than 600 hours of live coverage across its platforms, while Channel 5 has secured a daily highlights package after the BBC declined to sublicense the rights. The move will be a significant test of whether the Games can thrive behind a paywall.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |WeeklySign up toSport in FocusOur picture editors select their favourite sporting images from the past week, from the spectacular to the powerful, and with a little bit of fun thrown inPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotion7Bowls gets top billingUsually overshadowed by athletics and swimming, bowls will take centre stage as the first sport to begin in Glasgow. Competition starts on a newly introduced Day Zero, hours before the opening ceremony, after an extra day was added in response to demand from Commonwealth nations. A new indoor format promises four matches taking place simultaneously, music inside the venue and a faster-paced competition aimed at attracting new audiences. Scotland’s Alex “Tattie” Marshall and Paul Foster headline a strong home team, while England boast the world champions including Nick Brett.8Gymnastics’ star-studded returnArtistic gymnastics may feature for only five days, but it boasts one of the strongest fields of the Games. The reigning Olympic champion, Rhys McClenaghan, headlines the competition as the Northern Irishman hopes to reclaim the pommel horse title he won in 2018 after taking silver in Birmingham four years ago. He returns from injury alongside a host of established names, including Australia’s Georgia Godwin and Canada’s four-time Olympian Ellie Black, both also making welcome comebacks after injury. Scotland’s young squad, led by Reuben Ward, will hope home support can inspire an upset.View image in fullscreenRhys McClenaghan punches the air after taking gold in the men’s pommel horse final at Paris 2024.Photograph: Ed Alcock/The Guardian9India’s changing challengeNo nation has more to lose from Glasgow’s slimmed‑down programme than India. The country has long relied on wrestling, badminton, table tennis and hockey to fuel its rise up the medal table and 30 of India’s 61 medals from Birmingham 2022 came in events that will not feature this summer, leaving a 125-strong squad with far fewer opportunities than the 215 athletes who travelled four years ago. India will host the centenary Games in Ahmedabad in 2030 and organisers hope that event will strengthen the country’s bid to stage the 2036 Olympic Games. A successful showing inGlasgowwould provide further momentum, giving India’s athletes the chance to build confidence before back-to-back home Games.10Next generation hope to impressUnlike the Olympics, there is more room for breakthrough stars to shine at the Commonwealth Games and Glasgow looks no different. The 20-year-old distance runner Innes FitzGerald arrives as one of England’s brightest prospects after finishing second at the UK Athletics Championships, while the Welsh sprinter Charlotte Henrich has enjoyed a remarkable first senior season, twice breaking the national 400m record. In the pool, the 19-year-old Eva Okaro and 17-year-old Amelie Blocksidge headline England’s next generation, while Jersey’s European junior champion, Filip Nowacki, has already been tipped as the next great British breaststroker.Explore more on these topicsCommonwealth Games 2026Adam PeatySwimmingAthleticsGlasgowScotlandGymnasticsfeaturesShareReuse this content

title: Tour de France worries emerge for Tadej Pogacar after illness sweeps through team | Tour de France 2026 | The Guardian
description: Richard Carapaz won stage 18 of the Tour, but uncertainty surrounds the health of race leader Tadej Pogacar’s UAE Emirates XRG team
Author: Jeremy Whittle
Headline: Richard Carapaz won stage 18 of the Tour, but uncertainty surrounds the health of race leader Tadej Pogacar’s UAE Emirates XRG team
Content: Tadej Pogacar (second left) rides with his UAE Team Emirates colleagues on Thursday’s stage.Photograph: Yoan Valat/EPAView image in fullscreenTadej Pogacar (second left) rides with his UAE Team Emirates colleagues on Thursday’s stage.Photograph: Yoan Valat/EPATour de France 2026Tour de France worries emerge for Tadej Pogacar after illness sweeps through teamRichard Carapaz roars clear of breakaway to win stage 18McNulty abandons, Wellens and Vermeersch struggleJeremy Whittlein Orcières-MerletteThu 23 Jul 2026 12.56 EDTLast modified on Thu 23 Jul 2026 19.31 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleRichard Carapaz won stage 18 of theTour de Franceafter a breakaway group of six riders duelled their way to the winter sports station at Orcières-Merlette, but uncertainty surrounds the health of race leader Tadej Pogacar’s UAE Team Emirates XRG, which has been swept by a virus since Monday’s rest day.After Adam Yates’s stage-long calvary on the road to Voiron, Pogacar’s team was further weakened by the abandonment of his American colleague, Brandon McNulty, who quit the Tour with 21km of Thursday’s stage remaining.Tour de France director rejects accusations of bias towards French ridersRead moreTwo more of Pogacar’s teammates, Tim Wellens and Florian Vermeersch, also appeared to struggle at times during the stage, which included five categorised climbs, including the final seven-kilometre ascent to the finish.Speaking at the stage start in Voiron, Wellens suggested other riders within the team were unwell and revealed that he had a sore throat. Seen through the lens of Friday and Saturday’s mountain-top finishes on Alpe d’Huez, Pogacar, despite his 4m 32s lead over second-placed Remco Evenepoel, has reason to be worried.“I’m sad that Brandon could not finish. We will see in the next two days but Adam is getting better, and tomorrow, we will go with the flow,” Pogacar said. “I think we can manage. It’s the third week, all the teams are suffering. There are bodies everywhere on a stage like today. We will keep fighting and I believe we can do it.”The full extent of any illness within UAE Team Emirates XRG was further speculated on by Pogacar’s decision to swap his usual yellow shorts for black. McNulty’s decision to stop may have been for other reasons too, though. He had also been nursing injuries sustained when he was hit by a car during his reconnaissance of Tuesday’s time trial.View image in fullscreenThere are two more tough mountain stages in the Alps before Sunday’s final run into Paris.Photograph: Stéphanie Lecocq/ReutersThe former Giro d’Italia champion Carapaz,who won a mountain stage at nearby Superdévoluyin 2024, narrowly avoided colliding with an errant fan earlier in the stage. The Ecuadorian recovered from that mid-race scare to win with a typically gutsy lone attack.On a stage in which the main favourites called a truce, those who had tried and failed to succeed from breakaways earlier in the race made one last-ditch bid for glory. That included the Geraint Thomas-directed Netcompany Ineos, which has yet to win a stage this year despite numerous attempts.Time is now running out and Thomas has described this Tour as “tough going” for the British team. So it proved once more, with Egan Bernal and Thymen Arensman trying but failing to join the decisive move. The group that finally formed included Carapaz, but also Matteo Jorgenson, teammate to the now absent Jonas Vingegaard at Visma Lease-a-bike, and Mauro Schmid, already a stage winner for Jayco AlUla in Belfort.View image in fullscreenRichard Carapaz pours water on himself after winning stage 18.Photograph: Jeff Pachoud/ReutersCarapaz has proven a very reliable performer in the Tour’s mid-mountain stages, however, and once again he had enough power to distance his rivals in the final kilometres. The EF Education-EasyPost rider finished 45 seconds clear of both Schmid and Jorgenson. All eyes are now on this weekend’s double-headed suffer-fest on the daunting Alpe d’Huez, with a worried Pogacar looking, for the first time this year, almost vulnerable.Explore more on these topicsTour de France 2026Tour de FranceCyclingTadej PogacarnewsShareReuse this content

title: Chelsea paid Aston Villa £117m for Morgan Rogers. Is he the right fit? | Chelsea | The Guardian
description: The England attacker thrives when he can gallop into space but Xabi Alonso’s best team built attacks slowly
Author: Guardian staff reporter
Headline: The England attacker thrives when he can gallop into space but Xabi Alonso’s best team built attacks slowly
Content: Morgan Rogers has become the most expensive British footballer in history.Photograph: Darren Walsh/Chelsea FC/Getty ImagesView image in fullscreenMorgan Rogers has become the most expensive British footballer in history.Photograph: Darren Walsh/Chelsea FC/Getty ImagesGuardian Sport NetworkChelseaChelsea paid Aston Villa £117m for Morgan Rogers. Is he the right fit?The England attacker thrives when he can gallop into space but Xabi Alonso’s best team built attacks slowlyByOpta AnalystRyan BensonThu 23 Jul 2026 14.00 EDTLast modified on Thu 23 Jul 2026 15.32 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleIt quickly became apparent this summer that Aston Villa would not be holding on to Morgan Rogers. Having helped the club finish fourth in the Premier League andwin the Europa League, the 23-year-old was in demand.Arsenal looked like his likeliest next destination, with the champions eager to strengthen the left side of their attack. The charm offensive came from many angles, with Martin Ødegaard, Mikel Arteta and the club’s England players trying to sell the idea to Rogers. But, with Arsenal reluctant to meet Villa’s asking price, Chelsea intervened and made Rogers the most expensive British player ever, surpassing the £116m thatManchester City paid Nottingham Forest for Elliot Andersonearlier this summer.Xabi Alonso is new to Chelsea, so it is difficult to know exactly where Rogers will play. The versatile forward, who started out at West Brom before moving to Manchester City, Middlesbrough and then Villa, will probably be used in a variety of roles – often starting from the left but having opportunities to take up various attacking positions.View image in fullscreenAlonso predominantly used a 3-4-3 formation at Bayer Leverkusen and a 4-2-3-1 at Real Madrid. IfChelseaplay with three at the back, Rogers will be the left-sided No 10 in the team; if they use a four-man defence, he will start slightly wider but with the freedom to come inside – or he might even occupy the central role.Morgan Rogers ‘excited to join biggest club in London’ after making £117m Chelsea moveRead moreIf Alonso goes with the style he used at Leverkusen, Rogers might not be the best fit. During Alonso’s two full seasons in the Bundesliga, the team averaged 62.1% (2023-24) and 59.6% (2024-25) possession. During their title-winning campaign in 2023-24, they went unbeaten all season while dominating the ball and moving up the pitch slowly.They were set up to patiently build with lots of passes, win back possession quickly if they lost it and keep the ball as close to the opposition’s goal as possible. They made 367 high turnovers, which was 54 more than any other side. This system works best with attacking players who are tidy and intricate in tight spaces. However, Rogers is at his most effective when he can use his pace and gallop into space.His dribble completion rate last season was 34.2%, which was nothing to shout about. And his completion rate for passes under pressure was 73.4% – again not exceptional. Of the 161 forwards and midfielders who attempted at least 300 passes under pressure last season, Rogers ranked 143rd. The statistics suggest he is not particularly effective in close-quarters engagements.View image in fullscreenWith space to attack, Rogers is a threat. He made 223 progressive carries last season, the 10th highest amongPremier Leagueforwards, wingers and attacking midfielders. Only Cody Gakpo (41) and Matheus Cunha (35) had more carries that ended in shots than Rogers (30). He also created 16 chances for teammates after a carry: only 11 players created more. Rogers’ combined 46 chances created and shots taken following carries put him behind just Jérémy Doku (58), Gakpo (57) and Bukayo Saka (56). Alonso’s passing style may not bring the best from Rogers.There are reasons for optimism though. Rogers is frequently on the move and that makes him difficult to track. Only four players made more off-ball runs in the league last season; his 160 off-ball runs ending in the box was the eighth highest in the division.View image in fullscreenA mural of Jude Bellingham and Rogers in Birmingham.Photograph: Christopher Thomond/The GuardianHe also has the potential to form a good relationship with João Pedro. The Brazilian will probably be the focal point of the team and he is not a conventional No 9. He likes to drop deep, link with teammates and be a creator almost as much as a finisher. With Pedro dropping back, there will be room for Rogers to exploit beyond him: he made 258 runs in behind last season, the 12th highest in the league.Why did Manchester United pay Chelsea £48m for Andrey Santos?Read moreRogers’ strong relationship with Ollie Watkins bodes well for Chelsea. He created 20 open-play chances for Watkins last season, which was the second most from one player to another in the league. Wherever he has played, Rogers has developed an understanding with his teammates. He already has a close friendship with Cole Palmer after their time together in the Manchester City academy.Rogers’ other great attribute is his ball-striking ability. Since he made his Premier League debut in February 2024, Rogers has scored 21 non-penalty goals from 14.7 non-penalty expected goals. His overperformance of +6.3 is the 10th highest in the league.Rogers has a great strike on him and it could be a gamechanger for Chelsea, particularly when they play teams employing a low block. His five league goals from outside the box since his Villa debut is bettered by only six players. Few players are as accurate when shooting from distance. It was his shot against Norway that led toJude Bellingham’s winning goal in the World Cup quarter-final.View image in fullscreenChelsea have paid an incredible amount of money for Rogers and there are reasons to wonder if the move will work tactically. But Rogers has a habit of making an impact regardless of the context. He’s not a right winger but he still made his presence known forEnglandagainst Argentina in the World Cup semi-final, setting up Anthony Gordon’s opener from the right.Is he a £117m player? Perhaps not. Will he succeed playing the same way he played at Villa? Perhaps not. But it’s hard to imagine him not making a significant impact at Stamford Bridge.This is an article byOpta AnalystExplore more on these topicsChelseaGuardian Sport NetworkTransfer windowAston VillaPremier LeagueEnglandXabi AlonsofeaturesShareReuse this content

title: Rugby brain injury case reaches crisis point as judge retires to consider 95% strikeouts | Concussion in sport | The Guardian
description: The judge criticised solicitor Richard Boardman’s handling of the case as he retired to consider throwing out the landmark legal action over rugby brain injuries
Author: Andy Bull
Headline: The judge criticised solicitor Richard Boardman’s handling of the case as he retired to consider throwing out the landmark legal action over rugby brain injuries
Content: The governing bodies in rugby union and rugby league are pressing to have the claims thrown out.Photograph: Kai Schwoerer/Getty ImagesView image in fullscreenThe governing bodies in rugby union and rugby league are pressing to have the claims thrown out.Photograph: Kai Schwoerer/Getty ImagesConcussion in sportRugby brain injury case reaches crisis point as judge retires to consider 95% strikeoutsSenior Master Cook says case in ‘uncharted territory’Judge blames solicitor Richard Boardman for situationAndy Bullat the Royal Courts of JusticeThu 23 Jul 2026 13.36 EDTLast modified on Thu 23 Jul 2026 15.31 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleThe landmark legal action over brain damage in rugby is on the brink of collapse as the managing judge weighs whether to strike out 95% of the first batch of 561 claimants on the grounds they have failed to comply with a court order to disclose all available documents relating to their neurological testing.After two days of hearings at the Royal Courts of Justice, Senior Master Cook said the case had entered “uncharted territory” because he was unable to find any legal precedent for what to do next before retiring to make his judgment.It’s unclear how long he will take. Cook said that, given that absence of precedent,he needed to operate in “a careful and considered manner”. The court is due to break for summer next Friday, 31 July.The defendants – World Rugby, the Rugby Football Union and the Welsh Rugby Union in one case, and Rugby Football League and British Amateur Rugby League Association in the other – all pressed to have these cases struck out on the grounds that there had been “wholesale and widespread non-compliance” with the court order to disclose all the documents.Cook agreed with this at the high court on Thursday, but was unequivocal that it was the solicitor until this week in charge of the case, Richard Boardman of Rylands Garth, who was at fault rather than the claimants themselves. “The only complaint in this case is against the way Mr Boardman has handled the material,” Cook said. “No claimant in this case can be said in any way, shape or form to be responsible for the deficiencies in the way this case has been presented.”Boardman has notified the court of his intention to “come off the record” in the case after the claimants unanimously agreed to terminate their dealings with Rylands Garth.They are in the process of appointing new solicitors, but until they have done so Boardman remains notionally in charge despite being absent from court for the last day and a half of hearings. The firm Leigh Day has been consulting on the case since February, and is considering whether to take it on.Cook said he felt this change “raises more questions than it answers”. There are still second tranches of stayed cases in both league and union, which could conceivably proceed to trial even if this first tranche is struck out. But as the barrister representing the Rugby Football League, William Audland KC, said in court: “There may be no one that is prepared to pick up this mess.” Even if they were, it would probably necessitate more delays and expenses in a case that has been running for six years and has cost millions.Yet Cook was mindful of the severity of strikeouts, which would deny the claimants the opportunity for justice. He described them as passengers in a vehicle being driven by somebody else. “The point is simply this: you have three buses full of passengers, all going to the same destination. The first bus, the driver takes them off the edge of a cliff, with the result they do not get to their destination. The other two buses get to the destination. Let’s say they are successful, buses two and three. What of those on board bus one?”Cook told the court the case had only reached this crisis point because Boardman had given misleading evidence about whether or not he had successfully complied with the court order. The defence have been provided with thousands of documents relating to the testing, and the claimants argue that these ought to be sufficient. But there remain significant gaps, despite Boardman’s insistence in court that there were not.“Mr Boardman should have said he had complied as far as he was capable instead of making these bland statements that the order had been complied with to the letter,” said Cook.“But that’s the evidence he has chosen to put here, with not a bit of mea culpa.”Explore more on these topicsConcussion in sportRugby unionRugby leaguenewsShareReuse this content

title: London Spirit begin new Hundred era with Tinie Tempah and double defeat | The Hundred | The Guardian
description: The Hundred put on a show at Lord’s but the hosts’ men’s and women’s teams both lost to Manchester Super Giants
Author: Simon Burnton
Headline: The Hundred put on a show at Lord’s but the hosts’ men’s and women’s teams both lost to Manchester Super Giants
Content: Sonny Baker shows his delight after taking the prized wicket of Liam Livingstone.Photograph: Alex Broadway/ECB/Getty ImagesView image in fullscreenSonny Baker shows his delight after taking the prized wicket of Liam Livingstone.Photograph: Alex Broadway/ECB/Getty ImagesThe HundredLondon Spirit begin new Hundred era with Tinie Tempah and double defeatThe Hundred put on a show at Lord’s but the hosts’ men’s and women’s teams both lost to Manchester Super GiantsSimon Burntonat Lord'sThu 23 Jul 2026 17.07 EDTLast modified on Thu 23 Jul 2026 18.18 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleLondon Spirit launched their new era with double defeat at Lord’s, and though they may have new owners, new coaches and new players it seems they’re still getting the same results. For the fifth time in their six-year history the men started their season with defeat, losing by seven runs a game of the Hundred that Liam Livingstone came very close to turning into The One.The Spirit captain took responsibility for 20 of the last 25 balls of Manchester Super Giants’ innings, shouldering what should have been the hardest part of the bowling burden, and finished with two wickets for just 14. Then, with his team flailing in pursuit of 139, he scored 69 off 40 to keep them in the contest until the moment he swung at its antepenultimate delivery and was bowled by Sonny Baker.The Hundred returns with the hits and hope that fans feel the love againRead moreThey had been helped by a run of drops and misfields by the Super Giants, most notably when Livingstone heaved Noor Ahmad to deep midwicket where Tim Seifert – whose 67 off 45 ultimately decided the match – let the ball bounce off his hands and into the boundary padding for six. But on the pitch that four days earlier allowed England and India to score 747 runs between them in a ODI batters in both games struggled, and even Seifert soared for only part of his innings. His team’s knock was a curiosity, with 26 runs and one wicket coming in the first 25 balls and six wickets falling for just 22 in the final 26, while in between they rampaged to 90 at nearly two runs a ball.Of the crowd of 23,847 some will have come for the women’s game, some for the men, and others presumably for early-noughties chart-topping rapper Tinie Tempah. That said, it seems extraordinarily unlikely that anyone will have shelled out the £55 required for an adult ticket to watch 20 minutes of twixt-game entertainment.From pony racing to spear-throwing, from being requisitioned by the RAF toJack Leach scoring 92as a Test opener, Lord’s has witnessed a few unlikely sights over the years and that of Tempah, jets of fire screaming skywards behind him, was another to add to the list. But despite at one stage uttering the dreaded words “Lord’s, this is a brand new one” he produced a masterclass in scepticism-obliteration.View image in fullscreenTinie Tempah performing, with fire screaming skywards behind him, was an unlikely sight at Lord’s.Photograph: Adam Davy/PAHelpfully, he spent very little of it on his heavily branded stage, favouring wading into the crowd to hug mildly bemused paunchy white blokes. “He was,” Eoin Morgan later enthused on Sky, “magnificent.” This might not have been the future Tempah envisaged when Pass Out was conquering the charts 16 years ago but it was one he embraced with winning enthusiasm.Quick GuideSouthampton to host first men's Ashes Test next yearShowSouthampton’s Utilita Bowl will host a men’s Ashes Test for the first time next year with the men’s and ⁠women’s series against Australia again running concurrently, the England and Wales Cricket Board (ECB) announced.Trent Bridge will host the first men’s Test, starting on 18 June, while the ⁠first match of the ⁠multi-format women’s series, the ​sole test match, begins at Headingley on 24 June.Southampton will host the fourth men’s Test starting on 21 July. England last played a Test match at the venue, ⁠also known as the Rose Bowl, in 2020 when Pakistan were the opposition during the Covid pandemic. The World Test Championship final was also played at the ground in 2021 when New Zealand beat ⁠India.A packed summer of cricket in England will also include 10 ODIs for the England men’s team, the final of ​the World Test Championship at London’s Oval and England ‌men’s Mixed Disability team hosting Australia.The ‌men’s and women’s Ashes were also held at the same time in England in 2023, boosting crowds for the women’s ‌fixtures.ReutersMen’s Ashes series:1st Test - June 18-22 - Trent Bridge2nd Test - June 30-July 4 - Lord’s3rd Test - July 8-12 - Edgbaston4th Test - July 21-25 - Southampton5th Test - July 29-August 2 - London Oval.Women’s Ashes series:Test - June 24-27 - Headingley.T20I: July 3 - Trent BridgeT20I: July 6 - London OvalT20I: July 9 - Old TraffordODI: July 13 - Southampton.ODI: July 16 - Lord’sODI: July 20 - EdgbastonWas this helpful?Thank you for your feedback.It was not so much Pass Out as fail and out for London Spirit’s women, even if they started beautifully, with Grace Harris hitting the opening delivery for four. But that was very much as good as it got: by the end of the powerplay they were 23 for four and having to reset their ambitions to mere survival.In the end they did not manage that, bowled out after 91 balls having scored just 80. No team in the history of the women’s Hundred has successfully defended a score below 106 and Spirit never looked likely to become the first, Super Giants cantering to a total that was neither of those things in just 59 balls to win by seven wickets, with Paige Scholfield carrying her side over the finish line with a four and a six off successive Josie Groves deliveries and in the process becoming the game’s top scorer with just 22.Explore more on these topicsThe HundredCricketfeaturesShareReuse this content

title: From Popp to Diani: 10 women’s transfers you may have missed during the men’s World Cup | Transfer window | The Guardian
description: Sam Kerr’s and Alexia Putellas’s moves were hard to miss but these transfers may have escaped your attention
Author: Suzanne Wrack
Headline: Sam Kerr’s and Alexia Putellas’s moves were hard to miss but these transfers may have escaped your attention
Content: Kirsty Hanson, Kadidiatou Diani, Alexandra Popp.Composite: Guardian Design, Getty ImagesView image in fullscreenKirsty Hanson, Kadidiatou Diani, Alexandra Popp.Composite: Guardian Design, Getty ImagesTransfer windowFrom Popp to Diani: 10 women’s transfers you may have missed during the men’s World CupSam Kerr’s and Alexia Putellas’s moves were hard to miss but these transfers may have escaped your attentionSuzanne WrackThu 23 Jul 2026 15.00 EDTLast modified on Thu 23 Jul 2026 15.47 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleThere were a host of big women’s football transfers which were hard to miss across June and July, despite the dominance of the men’s World Cup. Among them wereBeth Mead’s move from Arsenal to Manchester City,Mary EarpsandGeorgia Stanwayreturning to the Women’s Super League with London City Lionesses and Arsenal,Sam Kerr leaving Chelsea to return to Gotham FCand, most prominently,Alexia Putellas’s game-changing switch from Barcelona to London City.But here are 10 transfers you might have missed while attention was focused on the action in the US, Canada and Mexico.Alexandra Popp to Borussia DortmundThe three-time Champions League winner Alex Popp may be reaching the twilight of her playing career but her move to Borussia Dortmund, signing a three-year contract at the tier-three club, after 14 years with Wolfsburg shows she is not quite ready to hang up her boots. The 35-year-old has been prolific for club and country, scoring 112 goals in 219 games for Wolfsburg and 67 goals in 145 games for Germany prior to her international retirement in 2024. A Borussia Dortmund fan whose “heart beats for this club”, Popp couldn’t pass up the opportunity to assist the development of the women’s team, which was launched in 2020.Kirsty Hanson to TottenhamThe 28-year-old was a top target for Spurs after a phenomenal fourth season at Aston Villa. Last year Hanson said she was working on adding more goals to her game – come the end of last season she was the third-highest top scorer in the WSL, finishing on 12 in 22 games, which was 43% of Villa’s total tally and only one goal behind second-place Alessia Russo. She heads to a focused Tottenham who are giving their manager, Martin Ho, the tools to push closer to, or even break into, the top four.Ally Sentnor to Angel CitySentnor, who was the first overall pick in the 2024 NWSL draft, was the subject of a blockbuster $850,000 (£635,000) trade between Kansas City Current and Angel City on 19 June. She then made her debut for her new side on 3 July, starting a 2-0 win against Orlando Pride. The USA women’s national team player has scored seven goals and provided three assists in 23 international games and, at just 22 years old, has a lot of football ahead of her.View image in fullscreenAlly Sentnor (right) on the ball for Angel City in a 2-0 win against against Chicago Stars last weekend.Photograph: Shaina Benhiyoun/SPP/ShutterstockFelicia Schröder to Real MadridMany clubs were circling but in the end Felicia Schröder completed a big-money move from Häcken to Real Madrid. Multiple Swedish outlets reported the deal for the 19-year-old forward was worth over 15m Swedish kronor (£1.15m). She attracted attention from across the world as she racked up 91 goals in 128 games for Häcken and starred in Uefa’s inaugural women’s Europa Cup competition, finishing as top scorer with eight goals, four of which came in the two-legged final against Hammarby.Manaka Matsukubo to ChelseaThe versatile 21-year-old, who can play as a forward or attacking midfielder, joined Chelsea from North Carolina Courage for an undisclosed fee but, as one of the most exciting young talents in the women’s game, it’s unlikely she came cheap. The Japan international scored 11 goals, became the youngest NWSL player to score a hat-trick, and was named NWSL midfielder of the year in 2025. She was up to five goals in nine games in the 2026 season before Chelsea swooped earlier this month.View image in fullscreenManaka Matsukubo at her Chelsea unveiling.Photograph: Harriet Lander/Chelsea FC/Getty ImagesJohanna Rytting Kaneryd to OL LyonnesThe 29-year-old won three league titles, two FA Cups and two League Cups with Chelsea, including a domestic treble in . Rytting Kaneryd is one of several attacking players to leave Chelsea this summer with Sam Kerr, Guro Reiten and Catarina Macario also having departed as they revamp their front line in an effort to reclaim their WSL crown after Manchester City ended their six-year title-winning run.Noémie Mouchon to EvertonThe loss of the forward Kelly Gago, who has signed for West Ham, has been tempered a little by the arrival of the French striker Noémie Mouchon from Leicester. Mouchon did not have the easiest time at Leicester after arriving from Reims with expectations high. The 23-year-old sustained an anterior cruciate ligament injury one month into her first season in the WSL and was ruled out for the remainder of the campaign. She made 16 appearances and scored two goals in the WSL last season but made only two starts. Everton will be hoping she can find the form that helped her to nine goals in 22 games for Reims in the 2023-24 season.Denise O’Sullivan to Gotham (on loan)The influential Republic of Ireland midfielder returns to the NWSL with Gotham after six months with Liverpool. The 32-year-old spent 10 seasons in the US before her January move to Liverpool, with Houston Dash and North Carolina Courage (albeit with a number of loan spells in Australia and England). She won the NWSL Shield in 2017 and again in 2018 and 2019 when Courage also secured the NWSL Championship, with O’Sullivan named the side’s MVP in both years. She has 22 goals in 132 games for Ireland.View image in fullscreenDenise O’Sullivan (right) showing off her close control against Aston Villa last season.Photograph: Nick Taylor/Liverpool FC/Getty ImagesBethany England to Crystal PalaceBethany England’s Tottenham departure was mourned by fans, with the former Chelsea forward having become the team’s record goalscorer in the WSL with 32 in three-and-a-half seasons, helping them to a first FA Cup final in 2024. The 32-year-old has signed a two-year contract with Crystal Palace, who earned promotion back into the top flight with a second-place finish in WSL2. After four WSL titles, two FA Cups and two League Cups with Chelsea, England brings a wealth of experience to Palace.Kadidiatou Diani to London City LionessesThe arrival of Alexia Putellas and Mary Earps has dominated the London City Lionesses transfer talk, while the acquisition of the France forward Kadidiatou Diani from Lyonnes for more than £500,000 has flown somewhat under the radar. The 31-year-old scored 41 goals in 93 games in three seasons for the Champions League giants following her stunning 92 goals in 168 games for Paris Saint-Germain. Michelle Kang, who owns both London City and Lyonnes, will be hoping she brings that edge up front to the ambitious WSL side. They finished sixth last season, their debut campaign in the top flight, nine points behind Tottenham, but they want more and are spending big to make progress.Explore more on these topicsTransfer windowWomen's footballfeaturesShareReuse this content

title: Teacher and daughter die after trying to help boy, seven, on Essex beach | Essex | The Guardian
description: Shalina Rahman, 42, and daughter Sameeha, 15, were on holiday in West Mersea and boy is believed to be family member
Author: Rachel Hall
Headline: Shalina Rahman, 42, and daughter Sameeha, 15, were on holiday in West Mersea and boy is believed to be family member
Content: Left, Sameeha Rahman, 15, who died along with her mother, Shalina, in West MerseaView image in fullscreenLeft, Sameeha Rahman, 15, who died along with her mother, Shalina, in West MerseaEssexTeacher and daughter die after trying to help boy, seven, on Essex beachShalina Rahman, 42, and daughter Sameeha, 15, were on holiday in West Mersea and boy is believed to be family memberRachel HallThu 23 Jul 2026 12.15 EDTFirst published on Thu 23 Jul 2026 05.45 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleThe teenage girl and woman who died in the waters off an Essex beach were trying to help a seven-year-old boy who had got into difficulty, police believe.Family members identified the pair as Shalina Rahman, a 42-year-old British-Bangladeshi teacher who is married to a Bangladeshi politician, and her 15-year-old daughter, Sameeha. The boy, who police say was a relative, is on life support in a hospital in London.Shalina Rahman’s husband is Mansur Rahman, the leader of Jubo Dal UK, the overseas youth wing of the Bangladesh Nationalist party in Britain. Her father is Mujibur Rahman, the former vice-president of the party in Britain.The family are understood to have been on a summer holiday trip in West Mersea, where they may have got into difficulty after a heavy incoming tide caused the sand beneath their feet to give way, sweeping them into the water.Essex police were called by the ambulance service at 5.35pm on Wednesday after reports of the incident near Seaview Avenue in West Mersea.DCI Al Pitcher confirmed that a mother and daughter had died, saying: “My thoughts, and those of my team, are with the friends and family of the woman and girl who died yesterday. We can now confirm they were a mother and daughter from London and we are supporting their family in what is an unimaginable time.“Since yesterday we have been working to establish the circumstances which led up to this incident. At this stage we believe they were trying to help a seven-year-old boy who had got into difficulties.“The boy was taken to hospital, where he remains in a critical condition, but the woman and teenage girl, sadly, died. All the people involved in this incident are related to each other.“As part of our investigation we are working through speaking to dozens of witnesses and taking their accounts, and we are building a picture of what happened.”View image in fullscreenWest Mersea beach.Photograph: Jamie Lashmar/PAMishba Uddin, a family friend, said he believed the mother and teenage girl “were not very good swimmers”. He said that those involved came from east London, with one family based in Barking.Matiur Rahman, the brother-in-law of Shalina Rahman, told the BBC: “We all feel very bad, my four brothers, my sister … My whole family – we are speechless. We are very shocked.”Rahman was told in a phone call from his brother about the tragedy. “They went in Colchester, [to the] seaside, and they were near water and playing with them, then suddenly a big wave came,” he said, adding that about 10 family members were on the day out.Carl Powling, of Colchester council, said his daughter was one of three teenage waitresses at a local cafe who had tried to help. “Members of the public witnessed it, called the emergency services, helped starting to pull people out, then they went into the Seaview cafe to ask for assistance,” he said.A woman, who did not give her name, told PA Media that the incident had happened at high tide, which caught the small group on a sandbank.“It comes in very quickly and you have to be careful,” she said, adding that there was “quite a commotion” and “just total panic”. She said helicopters were deployed and the air was filled with the sound of sirens.“That was quite alarming, for a small place like this, I think that’s why so many people are shocked,” she said. “The beach was packed with people, they were coming down to see what was going on.”The woman said the emergency services “worked diligently” to deal with an “absolutely tragic” incident.There have been at least 29 accidental deaths in open water across the UK since the middle of May, according to theNational Water Safety Forum.The area in Essex is known for its strong currents, though the local MP, Bernard Jenkin, told the BBC that “we don’t think of West Mersea beach as a dangerous beach”.Anyone who was in the area at the time and has information or footage that may assist the investigation is asked to contact the police on 101 and quote incident 1090.Explore more on these topicsEssexnewsShareReuse this content

title: Salman Rushdie testifies at terrorism trial of man convicted of stabbing him | Salman Rushdie | The Guardian
description: Author testified for second time about near-killing four years ago during event at New York amphitheater
Author: Guardian staff reporter
Headline: Author testified for second time about near-killing four years ago during event at New York amphitheater
Content: In a courtroom sketch, Salman Rushdie testifies in federal court in Buffalo, New York, on 23 July 2026.Photograph: Alexandra Newbould/APView image in fullscreenIn a courtroom sketch, Salman Rushdie testifies in federal court in Buffalo, New York, on 23 July 2026.Photograph: Alexandra Newbould/APSalman RushdieSalman Rushdie testifies at terrorism trial of man convicted of stabbing himAuthor testified for second time about near-killing four years ago during event at New York amphitheaterAssociated PressThu 23 Jul 2026 17.33 EDTLast modified on Thu 23 Jul 2026 18.06 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleFour years after being stabbed on an amphitheater stage,Salman Rushdieon Thursday sat across a courtroom from his convicted attacker and told jurors for a second time about the ambush that nearly killed him.The prize-winning writer recalled feeling what he thought was a punch under his chin, then feeling blood coming from his neck.Soon, he said, “I was on the stage, lying down, with an enormous pool of blood all around me,” Rushdie testified. “It was an enormous and expansive lake of blood.”A Buffalo federal court jury watched Rushdie fixedly as he testified – and at one point removed his glasses to show his now-sightless right eye – at Hadi Matar’s terrorism trial. Matar has pleaded not guilty.Matar was already convicted of a state-level attempted murder charge in the 2022 attack at a cultural institution where Rushdie was poised to speak about threats to writers and artists. Federal prosecutors say Matar was acting on such a threat: an Iranian leader’s 1989 call for Rushdie’s death because of his novel The Satanic Verses.Salman Rushdie attacker faces US terrorism trialRead more“I can’t say what his ambitions or his aim was, but the wounds were dispersed across my body,” Rushdie testified.Rushdie, 79, was composed and straightforward during just more than an hour on the witness stand. He outlined the attack – which blinded him in the right eye, damaged his liver, paralyzed his left hand for a time and briefly put him on a ventilator – and his lengthy recuperation and enduring injuries.“The knife was a millimeter from my brain,” said Rushdie, whose attacker had climbed on stage, approached from behind him and then slashed the author 15 times as the Chautauqua Institution audience gasped. Spectators and another speaker, Henry Reese, scrambled to aid Rushdie and restrain Matar, who was arrested by law enforcement officers assigned to the event. Rushdie had described the stabbing and recovery previously during Matar’s earlier trial, in a memoir and in interviews with the Associated Press and other outlets.As Rushdie testified, Matar, 28, looked down at the defense table, sometimes rested his hand on his chin or turned to speak to his lawyer, looking toward the author only as Rushdie walked out a door. The writer glanced at Matar fleetingly, if at all.Matar’s defense has argued that prosecutors can’t prove his intent or mindset at the time of the attack – a key element in their claim that it was an act of terrorism motivated by allegiance to Iran’s government and the Iranian-backed Hezbollah militant group. Matar, currently serving a 25-year state prison sentence, faces a potential life sentence if convicted of federal charges that include engaging in an act of transnational terrorism.When defense lawyer Nathaniel Barone asked whether Rushdie believed the defendant had ties to terrorism, the author replied: “I had no knowledge, but I had my suspicions.”Iran’s late Ayatollah Ruhollah Khomeini issued the 1989 fatwa, or religious edict, calling for Rushdie to be killed over The Satanic Verses, which includes a dream sequence about Islam’s prophet Muhammad that some Muslims considered blasphemous. The novel sparked often-violent protests against Rushdie, who was born into a Muslim family, and the fatwa drove him into hiding for years. He gradually emerged after the Iranian government distanced itself from the order in 1998, though the fatwa was never officially repealed.“For two decades or more, I led a public life without a hint of trouble,” Rushdie testified on Thursday.Barone repeatedly asked Rushdie whether he had anticipated blowback from the book.“Probably, but I don’t believe the book to be on trial here. I believe someone else is on trial,” Rushdie said. At Barone’s request, the judge struck the comment from the court transcript.Prosecutors say Matar was animated partly by comments that a former Hezbollah leader, the late Hassan Nasrallah, made about Rushdie and the fatwa in 2006. The US-born Matar also holds citizenship in Lebanon, where Hezbollah is based, and Matar’s mother has said a 2018 trip there left him moody and withdrawn.Matar started researching Rushdie in 2020 and eventually wrote to himself, “We need to kill him ASAP,” said assistant US attorney Timothy Lynch in an opening statement on Wednesday. Matar cast the killing as part of what he considered a jihad, or holy war, Lynch said.Matar’s New Jersey bedroom and electronics held photos and other material showing his regard for Hezbollah, and he carried a fake driver’s license with a name similar to that of a Hezbollah figure, said Lynch. The US government designates Hezbollah as a terrorist organization.Matar’s lawyer has said the government is asking jurors “to connect the dots” in a case without hard evidence about what he was thinking on the date of the attack. In a seeming effort to scrutinize how much the fatwa was on anyone’s mind by 12 August 2022, the defense also has asked some witnesses about the relatively light security around Rushdie that day. A state trooper and a sheriff’s deputy were assigned to the event.Matar declined to testify at his state court trial but may do so at the federal trial, Barone said outside court on Thursday.Rushdie was born in India, raised in Britain and is now a US citizen. He won the Booker prize in 1981 for his novel Midnight’s Children. His most recent work is The Eleventh Hour, a book of short stories and novellas published last year.Iran’s government denied involvement in the 2022 stabbing, but its foreign ministry’s then-spokesperson suggested Rushdie invited trouble by insulting Islam. Hezbollah’s Nasrallah did not comment on the attack.Explore more on these topicsSalman RushdieUS justice systemUS crimeNew YorkLaw (US)newsShareReuse this content

title: Owner of Viking ship used in The Odyssey says studio has not paid bill for damage  | Sweden | The Guardian
description: Swedish nonprofit alleges cost to repair replica was $6,000 but Universal – which made $264m on film’s opening weekend – has not stumped up
Author: Miranda Bryant
Headline: Swedish nonprofit alleges cost to repair replica was $6,000 but Universal – which made $264m on film’s opening weekend – has not stumped up
Content: The Glad av Gillberga was used as one of the escort vessels in the flotilla, which are thought to have been similar to Nordic Viking ships.Photograph: GCMC/Peter OlaussonView image in fullscreenThe Glad av Gillberga was used as one of the escort vessels in the flotilla, which are thought to have been similar to Nordic Viking ships.Photograph: GCMC/Peter OlaussonSwedenOwner of Viking ship used in The Odyssey says studio has not paid bill for damageSwedish nonprofit alleges cost to repair replica was $6,000 but Universal – which made $264m on film’s opening weekend – has not stumped upMiranda BryantNordic correspondentThu 23 Jul 2026 11.28 EDTLast modified on Thu 23 Jul 2026 21.30 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleThe Swedish owners of a Viking longship replica that features in the Hollywood blockbusterThe Odysseysay they feel “forgotten” after film-makers allegedly returned the vessel damaged and failed to foot the bill for repairs.In nearly three decades at sea, the warship Glad av Gillberga – based on a wreck from around 1040 found in Denmark’s Roskilde fjord – has successfully sailed the North Sea, the Baltic and even voyaged to Canada.But after six months in the hands of Universal, which according to its owners promised to cover the cost of any repairs, it allegedly sustained considerable damage including to the stern and the mast.Vikingaleden, a nonprofit Viking centre in Värmland, westernSweden, said that when it invoiced Universal for the materials used to repair their ship (they did not charge for their volunteer labour), no payment arrived.View image in fullscreenMatt Damon stars as Odysseus in The Odyssey.Photograph: Melinda Sue Gordon/Universal PicturesA year later, while the Christopher Nolan epic starring Matt Damon, Tom Holland, Anne Hathaway and Zendayastorms global box offices, taking $264m (£196m) in its opening weekend alone, it says it is still waiting for its $6,000 payment.Peter Olausson, chair of the Vikingaleden association, said that although he felt proud that the ship was featured in the film, that feeling had been tinged with injustice after the film giant’s alleged failure to cover the cost of damages.Some of the damage allegedly caused to the boat’s woodwork.Photograph: SVT“We are only charging for the material cost, not the labour,” he said. “Of course Universal can afford this $6,000. We feel forgotten.”The ship is now back in action but Olausson accused Universal of going back on their word. “The agreement was that we would fix it and get financially reimbursed.”Olausson said that to Universal, the outstanding sum would be comparatively small, but for the 200-member association that runs the Viking “village” and museum, it is a significant amount.Paint damage allegedly caused to the boat.Photograph: SVTThecritically acclaimed film, which reportedly cost $250m to make, is based on Homer’s ancient Greek epic poem about the king of Ithaca, Odysseus, on a 10-year sea journey home from the Trojan war.The Glad av Gillberga was used as one of the escort vessels in the flotilla, which are thought to have been similar to Nordic Viking ships.The main ship, Odysseus’s boat, was the 35-metre-long Norwegian replica Draken Harald Hårfagre.Its Swedish captain, Björn Ahlander, said life on the ships filming for six months around Greek and Italian islands was not easy for the 30-person crew. He told the broadcasterSVT: “We rebuilt the ship into an ancient Greek ship. We removed the tents and all the modern things and had an open deck that we lived on during this time. It was quite tough.”A Universal spokesperson said: “Our records show that all invoices relating to the Glad av Gillberga were paid in full, including repairs. The studio and the production have contacted the association to clear up any misunderstanding.”Explore more on these topicsSwedenThe OdysseyEuropeChristopher NolannewsShareReuse this content

title: Southern California suburb alarmed as ‘exceptionally rare’ cancer sickens multiple children | California | The Guardian
description: Residents in Ladera Ranch in Orange county voice concerns after six children diagnosed with Ewing sarcoma
Author: Dani Anguiano
Headline: Residents in Ladera Ranch in Orange county voice concerns after six children diagnosed with Ewing sarcoma
Content: Ladera Ranch in southern California on 17 May 2016.Photograph: Jeff Gritchen/Orange County Register/Getty ImagesView image in fullscreenLadera Ranch in southern California on 17 May 2016.Photograph: Jeff Gritchen/Orange County Register/Getty ImagesCaliforniaSouthern California suburb alarmed as ‘exceptionally rare’ cancer sickens multiple childrenResidents in Ladera Ranch in Orange county voice concerns after six children diagnosed with Ewing sarcomaSign up for the Breaking News US newsletter emailDani AnguianoThu 23 Jul 2026 15.34 EDTLast modified on Thu 23 Jul 2026 16.24 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleConcerns are growing in southernCaliforniaover a cluster of “exceptionally rare”cancercases that has sickened multiple children in a suburban community.Families living in the Orange county neighborhood of Ladera Ranch, home to about 26,000 people, have reported that six children in the community were diagnosed with Ewing sarcoma, Katrina Foley, the vice-chair of the county board of supervisors, said in astatement.Ewing sarcoma is a rare cancer that primarily affects bone and soft tissue and is largely seen in young adults and children.California toddler bitten by rattlesnake ‘hiding under’ Amazon packageRead moreAt a community meeting this week, hundreds of residents turned up to voice their concerns, including several who spoke about additional cancer cases in adults and pets. While the AmericanCancerSociety reports there are “no known lifestyle-related or environmental causes” of Ewing sarcoma, many residents believe the cancer cases are linked to the use of herbicides and pesticides.“We’re very concerned about pesticides that are being sprayed all around the community,” Liesl Heizt toldABC7. “There’s so many times that I walk around and they’re spraying, and I ask what they’re spraying and they’re not telling us.”During the meeting, Brian Dalton spoke about his daughter Lily who died earlier this year after being diagnosed with the same rare cancer as the other cases,KTLA 5reported.“I don’t want another mother holding her child’s hand through endless treatments, praying for one more birthday, planning a funeral instead of a future,” Dalton said.In a letter sent last week, Foley urged the state to expedite a review of the cases and give residents “timely, transparent updates”.“Ladera Ranch families deserve answers, transparency and urgency from the state,” Foley said. “We do not yet know what caused these cancers, but that uncertainty demands a comprehensive review of every credible factor, including possible environmental exposures, along with direct communication and the public release of the findings.”skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |Around 2-3 times a daySign up toBreaking News USGet the most important news as it breaksPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionLast week, Bill Essayli, an assistant US attorney, asked the Environmental Protection Agency to investigate the cluster to determine if there is an environmental cause behind the cases. At least six children have been diagnosed with the rare bone and soft-tissue cancer, he wrote.“The Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC) and National Cancer Institute recognize that unusual patterns of cancer and community concerns regarding potential environmental exposures should be evaluated through a systematic, science-based process,” Essayli said.Explore more on these topicsCaliforniaCancerChildren's healthWest CoastnewsShareReuse this content

title: Tesla stock suffers double-digit drop on carmaker’s worst day in years | Tesla | The Guardian
description: Elon Musk’s EV company lost a quarter of its value after revealing weak profits and high spending on AI and robotics
Author: Nick Robins-Early
Headline: Elon Musk’s EV company lost a quarter of its value after revealing weak profits and high spending on AI and robotics
Content: Tesla has lost about 27% of its market value throughout the year.Photograph: Bloomberg/Getty ImagesView image in fullscreenTesla has lost about 27% of its market value throughout the year.Photograph: Bloomberg/Getty ImagesTeslaTesla stock suffers double-digit drop on carmaker’s worst day in yearsElon Musk’s EV company lost a quarter of its value after revealing weak profits and high spending on AI and roboticsNick Robins-Earlyand agenciesThu 23 Jul 2026 18.35 EDTLast modified on Thu 23 Jul 2026 18.39 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleTesla’s stock fell about 13.5% on Thursday in one of the biggest single-day market losses in the company’s history.Elon Musk’s automaker has struggled throughout the year, losing about 27% of its market value as huge capital expenditures and weaker-than-expected profits worry investors.US stocks as a whole tumbled on Thursday withNasdaqdown more than 2% after touching its lowest level since early May as the latest earnings updates from large technology companies ⁠revived concerns about heavyAI⁠spending, while soaring oil prices amped ​up inflation worries and pushed up bond yields.The S&P 500 and the Dow exchanges fell more than 1% after second-quarter results fromAlphabetand Tesla, the first of the so-called “Magnificent Seven” megacap companies to report this season, failed to impress ⁠investors.Elon Musk says he got ‘carried away’ with Trump – but still holds on to contentious political viewsRead moreTesla’s market losses comethe day after it released its second-quarter earnings, which revealed the company made 31 cents per share as opposed to the 51 cents per share that Wall Street analysts expected, a proxy measure for the company’s profitability. Though it did beat revenue predictions, a sharp selloff in Tesla stock has followed.Musk faced numerous questions from investors on Wednesday’s earnings call about the timeline and logistics of the two projects he has most heavily touted – Tesla’s driverless Robotaxi car service and Optimus humanoid robot. Musk has for years promoted both products as world-changing sources of near-infinite potential revenue, but the rollout has been slow and Musk has repeatedly failed to meet his own targets. Tesla’s Robotaxis are available only on a limited basis in the US, and Optimus is not available to consumers.Musk attempted to assuage investor concerns on Wednesday’s call by once again proclaiming that Optimus robots would be Tesla’s biggest product ever, but cautioned that there were numerous hurdles the company faced in creating the product. He similarly claimed that Robotaxi’s slow rollout was the result of an abundance of concern for safety and worry that deadly accidents would result in negative media attention and a regulatory crackdown.Tesla’s pivot to robotics, autonomous vehicles and AI has required immense amount of investments with little immediate return. The company spent $5.8bn in the second quarter of this year, resulting in negative free cashflow of $1.1bn for the first time in more than two years. Although its electric vehicle sales surged on the strength of European buyers and rising fuel costs, Tesla’s spending on its new products has shifted investor focus away from its auto revenue.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |WeeklySign up toTechScapeA weekly dive in to how technology is shaping our livesPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionTesla’s loss in stock value makes it the poorest-performing of the magnificent seven companies, which also include Alphabet, Amazon, Meta, Nvidia, Apple and Microsoft. The group suffered declines across the board on Thursday, with Bloomberg reporting thattheir losses wiped out $767bnfrom the market.Shares of Google’s parent, Alphabet, fell 6.5% after reporting higher spending plans and its first ​ever cash burn. Wall Street’s fear gauge, the CBOE Volatility Index, was up three points, at 19.64, after hitting its highest level in nearly a month.Explore more on these topicsTeslaQuarterly resultsElon MuskDow JonesAlphabetAmazonMetanewsShareReuse this content

title: UK households free to install plug-in balcony solar panels from end of August | Energy bills | The Guardian
description: Law change means renters and flat owners may be able to cut bills as energy costs soar amid Iran war
Author: Jillian Ambrose
Headline: Law change means renters and flat owners may be able to cut bills as energy costs soar amid Iran war
Content: DIY solar panels are popular in Germany, where they are known asBalkonkraftwerke (balcony power plants).Photograph: Ronald Rampsch/AlamyView image in fullscreenDIY solar panels are popular in Germany, where they are known asBalkonkraftwerke (balcony power plants).Photograph: Ronald Rampsch/AlamyEnergy billsUK households free to install plug-in balcony solar panels from end of AugustLaw change means renters and flat owners may be able to cut bills as energy costs soar amid Iran warJillian AmbroseEnergy correspondentThu 23 Jul 2026 09.00 EDTLast modified on Thu 23 Jul 2026 21.30 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleBritish households will be able to buy plug-in solar panels later this summer after the government changed the law so the UK’s biggest retailers could bring them to market for the first time.People across the country will be able to join the millions across Europe generating their own electricity using “balcony solar panels” from 27 August, according to legislation passed last week.These plug-in panels have in recent years become familiar sights on balconies in Spain and in Germany, wherethey are known asBalkonkraftwerk(balcony power plant).Unlike traditional rooftop solar panels, which require professional installation,balcony solar installationscan be plugged into a three-prong wall socket, generating cheap electricity from a balcony, terrace, shed roof or any other outdoor space.The legal change means that, from the end of this summer, retailers including Currys, B&Q and Asda will be able to sell DIY solar setups to people living in flats or rented homes without rooftop access, so they can generate their own solar power from their balconies.They were among some of the UK’s biggest retail brands – including Screwfix, Wickes and Amazon – thatmet the government earlier this summerto discuss guidelines for the sale of balcony solar panels, which are expected to retail for at least £400 and could shave up to £110 a year off energy bills.The government’s move to make plug-in solar lawful is expected to bring “cheap, clean power to households that have not been able to access these benefits before”, said Chris Hewett, the chief executive of SolarEnergyUK.“Our industry is working closely with government to ensure that all customers can have confidence in the safety and reliability of these new technologies,” he added.When panels are plugged in, the clean energy generated when the sun is shining flows directly into the property’s electrical circuit through a standard three-prong plug to power electrical appliances without needing to buy as much from the grid.Plug-in systems usually have a capacity of 800 watts, or one-fifth of the size of a typical residential rooftop solar system, so while they wouldn’t be able to power a home alone, they could help to reduce bills by up to £70-110 a year. This means it would take four to seven years to cover the initial outlay of buying a plug-in system.View image in fullscreenRecord numbers of people in Britain installed solar panels on rooftops last year.Photograph: Pat  Tuson/AlamyThe DIY solar systems are expected to come to market at a time when households haveturned in record numbers to green home energy upgradesto help keep costs down as the war in Iran causes global energy prices to soar.A record 269,000 solar installations were completed in the UK last year, over a third more than the previous year and the equivalent of a new rooftop solar installation every two minutes.Rob Hallifax, a co-founder of Windfall Energy, said the new legislation could also pave the way forplug-in batteriesin British homes. The government is reportedly considering plans to bulk buy the devices and distribute them to low-income homes, according to the i Paper.The proposals could help households to save money on their bills by storing electricity when there is an abundance of energy available and drawing on the power when supplies are tighter. If paired with a plug-in solar system, households could store their own renewable electricity during the day and use it to run their appliances in the evenings.Hallifax said the move towards plug-in solutions was a “massive win for consumer energy independence, and a huge leap forward for anyone who has previously been locked out of the energy transition”.“For renters, flat owners and anyone on a budget, this opens up clean energy in a way we haven’t seen before,” said Hallifax. He added that households should still set “realistic expectations” for their new solar setup.“Coming into force in late August means generation will naturally decline as we head into autumn and winter,” he said. “You still need access to a sunlit balcony, terrace or suitable outdoor area. And you may need permission from a landlord or freeholder to put panels in place.”Explore more on these topicsEnergy billsSolar powerRenewable energyEnergy efficiencyEnergyEnergy industryConsumer affairsnewsShareReuse this content

title: ‘People are scared their neighbours will call the police on them’: how the hosepipe ban got caught up in the culture wars | Water | The Guardian
description: While many people in the UK recognise the need to preserve water, others are furious and planning to flout the ban in their area. The target of their anger? Failing water companies and frighteningly thirsty datacentres
Author: Sammy Gecsoyler
Headline: While many people in the UK recognise the need to preserve water, others are furious and planning to flout the ban in their area. The target of their anger? Failing water companies and frighteningly thirsty datacentres
Content: Gardeners bear the brunt of the ban – but are they really to blame?Illustration: Sean O'Brien/The GuardianView image in fullscreenGardeners bear the brunt of the ban – but are they really to blame?Illustration: Sean O'Brien/The GuardianWater‘People are scared their neighbours will call the police on them’: how the hosepipe ban got caught up in the culture warsWhile many people in the UK recognise the need to preserve water, others are furious and planning to flout the ban in their area. The target of their anger? Failing water companies and frighteningly thirsty datacentresSammy GecsoylerThu 23 Jul 2026 00.00 EDTLast modified on Thu 23 Jul 2026 03.17 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on Google‘Plants are like your babies,” says Harry. And accordingly, he argues, most people would do anything to keep them alive, including break the law. This is what he plans to do this week – defying a hosepipe ban in place across large parts of England, to ensure his shrubs maintain a steady pulse.He may not be alone. On Tuesday,Thames Waterbecame the latest company to ban hosepipes – a restriction that will affect more than 10 million people. Since mid-July, Welsh Water, Anglian Water, Affinity Water (serving Bedfordshire, Berkshire, Buckinghamshire, Hertfordshire and Surrey), Southern Water and Cambridge Water have all announced hosepipe bans. And earlier this week, South East Water extended its existing ban to cover an additional 1.5 million.Harry, 24, has spoken to friends and family in his leafy corner of Essex who are equally fed up with the ban that has been in place on and off since the beginning of July. “Unless they turn the taps off, I’m still going to use it,” he says. “It’s another thing that makes people say they want to leave the UK, these silly little rules.”Others are using the ban to stoke up division. Suella Braverman MP says it’s yet another “act of lunacy in Labour’s two-tier Britain”. The Reform UK politician was responding to aTelegraph front pagedeclaring that benefit claimants are exempt from the hosepipe ban. The Conservative party chair, Kevin Hollinrake, also chimed in, calling it a “kick in the hosepipe”.Despite the furore the exemption has caused, the truth is that it does not apply to all those who claim benefits: households that qualify to be on a water company’s priority services register would need to be benefits claimants who have either three or more children, or someone with a medical condition that requires the use of significant amounts of water – dialysis, for example. Disabled people and blue badge holders are also exempt.Regardless of this much more nuanced reality, the hosepipe ban has become the subject of a new culture war, an attempt to pit the haves (in this scenario, poor and disabled people, who are allowed more water) against the have-nots (able-bodied people with an earned income).View image in fullscreenSt James’s park in London earlier this week.Photograph: Amer Ghazzal/ShutterstockWhy has the ban been put in place, anyway? The answer, as with so many things, is the climate crisis. In 2025, the UK recorded itshottest year ever. According to the Met Office, 2026 is already showing hotter and drier conditions than last year – with three heatwaves to date. Daniel Johns, the managing director at the non-profit Water Resources East, says river levels have reached “all-time lows”. In May, a House of Lords report saidEngland faces a 5bn-litre-a-day water shortfallby 2055. Water companies, says Johns, have been left with “no choice” but to impose hosepipe bans because the “hot weather has led households to use more water than ever before, depleting rivers and reservoirs at concerning rates” – which, if left unchecked, could lead to even more severe water shortages.But Harry says his ire isn’t directed towards vulnerable people who can still use their hosepipes. Instead, he blames water companies – which, according to water regulator Ofwat, lose almost 3bn litres of drinking water a day in England and Wales through leaking pipes. That’s five times more than even a nationwide hosepipe ban could save,says Greenpeace UK.Another thing guzzling our water supplies is the rise of AI, which Harry also hit out against. There are 450-plus AI datacentres in the UK, with more planned, which require enormous amounts of water to cool servers. On Tuesday, the water industry warned that these plans were “fatally flawed” as the policies make “not a single mention of water”. Water UK said that more than two-thirds of existing datacentres are in the south and south-east of England, where many of the hosepipe bans are in place. The Government Digital Sustainability Alliance, a public-private group led by the Department for Environment, Food and Rural Affairs, has already warned thatAI is predicted to lead to an increase in global water usagefrom 1.1bn to 6.6bn cubic metres by 2027 – in other words, equivalent to more than half of the UK’s total water usage. “Is it really gardeners at fault?” Harry says.View image in fullscreenIllustration: Sean O’Brien/The GuardianHarry plans to water his plants using a hose, but others have shared how they will more discreetly flout the ban – one TikTok user appears tohide a hosepipe inside a watering can, so a powerful stream emerges from its spout. Some are connecting their hosepipes to buckets being constantly refilled by a running tap to technically remain compliant, despite the large amounts of water still being used.I speak to Harry in a leafy suburb of Harlow, which is filled with beautiful homes and thriving, well-kept front gardens. Most of the people I meet are planning to comply with the ban, including an elderly woman, whose hands are shaking while she tends a hanging plant with a watering can. She says the ban has “massively impacted her” and she doesn’t want her “beautiful garden” to be ruined.Isabella, 24, who works in a garden centre, understands why the ban is in place and will comply with it, switching the hose for a watering can and now having to wake up extra early before work. “It takes me 40 minutes to water my plants, whereas it normally takes me 15 minutes,” she says. Some of her customers, who have an exemption from the ban, have told her they “feel like they’re being watched by their neighbours”, especially when tending their front gardens. They are “scared that neighbours will call the police on them so they feel too intimidated to use their hoses”, she says. However, she does understand why her customers “find it frustrating” to restrict their hosepipe use. “It’s just another thing on top of the issues they’re already facing,” she says.View image in fullscreenThe ‘climate changed oak tree’ in Kew Gardens, London – one of 400 that died during the 2022 drought, painted red to highlight the climate crisis.Photograph: Posed by model; Brook Mitchell/AFP/Getty ImagesFor disabled people, the feeling of being watched and scrutinised is familiar. Maddy Alexander-Grout, a 42-year-old disability advocate and ADHD coach from Southampton, informs people on social media about the benefits and support they might not be aware they are entitled to. She has been dubbed a “sickfluencer” by the tabloids, a label she refutes. “I give disabled people information that they don’t always have access to, because the government doesn’t want people to know about it,” she says.Anger over the hosepipe ban is the latest culture war furore to target disabled people, despite the fact that the WaterSure tariff – the scheme at the heart of this new controversy – was introduced back in 1999. It comes after arightwing campaignthat has taken aim at Motability, a longstanding scheme that leases 300,000 cars a year to people with mobility problems. A social media and tabloid campaign spread the idea that it was offering disabled people “free” BMWs and Mercedes.In reality, the scheme allows people with serious disabilities to get a car by using a portion of their benefits to pay for a three-year lease, after which the vehicle is returned, with newer cars preferred because they can be resold for a higher price afterwards, saving the taxpayer money.Following the campaign – which had support from Reform MP Robert Jenrick, whocalled the scheme “farcical”, and Conservative leader Kemi Badenoch, whosaid it was a “scam”– Motability was the main target for significant welfare cuts in last year’s autumn budget. Earlier this month,the Treasury saidthese cuts would “save taxpayers” £1bn by 2030. Rachel Reeves, the then chancellor, denied the campaign was behind the decision. “Disabled people are getting the brunt of everything,” says Grout.double quotation markThe water companies don’t seem to be doing anything to fix these problems. People have had enoughShe says there are very valid reasons that disabled people need access to a hosepipe, especially in the scorching heat. Grout has a number of disabilities, including postural orthostatic tachycardia syndrome (POTS), which can affect all unconscious bodily functions, including heart rate, blood flow, breathing and digestion; Hashimoto’s disease, an autoimmune condition that can cause extreme fatigue and sensitivity to temperature; and Ehlers-Danlos syndrome, which can cause joint hypermobility. This means she sometimes needs to use a wheelchair.Grout needs access to a hosepipe to cool down when outdoors because of her Hashimoto’s disease, and to clean her wheelchair, especially when it’s been in a “dusty area and it’s too dirty to bring into the house”. She says other disabled people might need to clean mobility equipment to “wash off bodily fluids” and, for those with respiratory conditions, to clean dusty outside spaces that may cause wheezing.Although she’s concerned about the rising anger over the ban, she says disabled people should also be sensible and do their bit, where possible, to reduce their water usage. “It’s about common sense, being considerate of others and not rubbing it in people’s faces,” she says. Many disabled people, she adds, “would give up their benefits and a hosepipe to have even a few days where their health wasn’t a problem”.View image in fullscreen‘Exceptionally frustrating’ … a burst water main causes flooding on Tooley Street, London, on Tuesday.Photograph: Jill Mead/The GuardianLaura Greasley is hardly a rule-breaker. She does as much as she can to conserve water, collecting grey water (lightly used water from, say, a bath, shower or washing machine) when possible, and using water butts because “we take individual responsibility really seriously”. However, a pipe outside her home has been leaking for a year. She can hear it when cars drive by, splashing through the wasted water.Still, her countryside property in the Norfolk Broads is now under a hosepipe ban. After such a long spell of dry weather, her water butt is empty, so instead she has beentaking her fillfrom the untended leak outside her home. She says she “would be happy to support a hosepipe ban under normal circumstances”, but this time she is doing it begrudgingly. “It’s exceptionally frustrating to have leaks outside your house, wasting litres of water, and seeing that it doesn’t appear to be prioritised.”Her garden is “suffering” as a result of the ban, and her family is “having to work a lot harder” to keep everything alive. “People are extremely frustrated that they’re being asked to sacrifice the gardens they put a lot of time and investment into when the water companies don’t seem to be doing anything to fix these problems. The system is really being run into the ground and people have had enough.”Vanessa Speight, a water systems professor at the University of Sheffield, says it’s time to replace the ageing, decaying pipes that carry the nation’s water. Of the pipes in our infrastructure that have been examined, the average age is 80. “This means there are some that are a lot older than 80,” she says. Some are weaker than others, particularly those built in the aftermath of the second world war, “when metal was difficult to find”.View image in fullscreenSwithland reservoir in Leicestershire, pictured yesterday.
