It is at less than 44% capacity.Photograph: Joe Giddens/PAWater companies have to justify their spending to Ofwat during the price review process, where the regulator can determine the highest price firms can charge customers. As part of this process, the economic level of leakage is calculated (where the cost of reducing leaks is weighed up against the cost of lost water). The conclusion, Speight says, tends to be that “pipes are very expensive and water is fairly cheap to produce, meaning it’s never going to be economical to replace the pipes”. Privatisation is a factor, too, she adds, leaving the UK in a “difficult situation, where everything about the water systems is monetised or turned into an economic value, but you can’t put a value on water when you run out”.And running out is a real concern. While the UK’s climate isbecoming wetteroverall, with winters being defined by punishing rainfall and devastating flooding, our summers are becoming hotter and dryer. The government has been warned thatrainwater harvesting, along with other household measures such as taking shorter showers, is needed to avoid severe water shortages in England.“The distribution of rain is what’s changing, and that’s really hurting the UK, because the reservoir storage that would be required to cope is not there,” says Speight. “We can’t live like a wet country any more; we have to live like a drier country.”Still, she understands why the hosepipe ban has hit a nerve. “It feels more frustrating as people become aware of water issues and frustrated with the water companies for totally unrelated scenes like wastewater overflows,” she says. Overhauling our water infrastructure will come with a lot of disruption, though. “Unfortunately, it means construction, roadways being disturbed and traffic being diverted. All the things people hate in the short term – but it’s necessary,” she says. And the good news is that if our pipes were fixed, Speight says the need for hosepipe bans “would likely be very small”.View image in fullscreenThe Canary Island date palm, seen here in Spain, could become a common sight in UK gardens.Photograph: Manuel Fil Ordieres Garcia/Getty Images/iStockphotoGardeners are locked in a battle with our changing climate. Some plants that have long been staples in English gardens are now struggling to survive. Tim Parry, a gardener in the Isle of Wight, points to hydrangeas as an example: “They are a very thirsty plant.” He suggests we gravitate towards plants that can tolerate drought, such asPhoenixcanariensis(Canary Island date palm) andTrachycarpus fortunei(Chusan palm), both of which require less watering in the long term.Parry recommends being smart about which plants to water. “Some are a lot hardier than you think, particularly your lawn. It might look dry, brown and horrible right now, but it will come back.”Nevertheless, it can have a profound impact on people when a plant doesn’t make it. “Plants aren’t just something you stick in your garden,” Parry says, “they have sentimental value. It could be a rose grown in your pot with your grandfather’s ashes, or a tree that you planted in memory of somebody. They’re so important to people.” And more beautiful and meaningful plants could die off as the UK’s climate changes beyond recognition.“This time round, I feel it’s come on all of a sudden,” says Parry of the drought-like conditions that have scorched gardens. “We’re not talking August. This started happening in late June. I fear for what the future holds.”Do you have an opinion on the issues raised in this article? If you would like to submit a response of up to 300 words by email to be considered for publication in ourletterssection, pleaseclick here.Explore more on these topicsWaterWater industryPlantsGardening adviceDatacentresThames WaterfeaturesShareReuse this content

title: Besieged by lobbyists, will UK ministers protect our ecosystems? No, they’ll chicken out | George Monbiot | The Guardian
description: Industrial poultry farming is killing our rivers. But rather than stop it, the government has been facilitating it. Will Andy Burnham end this ecocide, asks Guardian columnist George Monbiot
Author: George Monbiot
Headline: Industrial poultry farming is killing our rivers. But rather than stop it, the government has been facilitating it. Will Andy Burnham end this ecocide, asks Guardian columnist George Monbiot
Content: Illustration: Sébastien Thibault/The GuardianView image in fullscreenIllustration: Sébastien Thibault/The GuardianOpinionFarmingBesieged by lobbyists, will UK ministers protect our ecosystems? No, they’ll chicken outGeorge MonbiotIndustrial poultry farming is killing our rivers. But rather than stop it, the government has been facilitating it. Will Andy Burnham end this ecocide?Thu 23 Jul 2026 01.00 EDTLast modified on Thu 23 Jul 2026 09.25 EDTShareThere’s a slightly mean question I often ask British people with an interest in the living world: can you name the environment secretary? I don’t intend to show them up: it’s not them I’m testing. I watch the brows knit, a finger go up, the mouth open and close. “It’s, you know, the one who … no, they went, didn’t they? That other one …” It’s been like this for seven years.Why? Because throughout this period, we’ve not had environment secretaries. We’ve had portals through which corporate lobbyists may pass. The last incumbent to represent the wider public interest (and I can scarcely bring myself to type these words) was Michael Gove. In his other roles, he’s widely remembered with loathing and disgust. Out of office, he continues topoison public life, as editor of the Spectator. But in this role, to general astonishment, he placed the public good above special interests.Standard operating procedure, by contrast, is exemplified by the final acts of the outgoing secretary, whose name, to put you out of your misery, is Emma Reynolds. To give just one example – which could be seen as an embodiment of everything that has gone wrong here – she spent her last weeks in the post trying to remove the brakes on industrial chicken farming.In front of aparliamentary committeethis month, she railed against the planning constraints holding back yet more giant chicken factories (let’s not grace them with the name of farms). If only these obstacles were demolished, she mused, “there is great investment out there waiting to be made … I want to bring down these barriers as much as you do”. She said she had spoken to other departments to ensure permission for new chicken factories was easier to obtain.Neither she nor the MPs questioning her gave any hint of why these buildings have run into trouble with the planning system. In fact, there was no mention of any of the problems the factories cause: the MPs’ only apparent interest was in what the industry wanted. What’s the point of MPs if they simply channel corporate demands? But these factories arekilling some of our most beautiful rivers. They generate far more dung than the surrounding fieldscan absorb. The surplus washes downhill into the nearest stream.The only decision that counts is the planning decision. As soon as permission for a chicken unit is granted, the local tributary, and possibly the main stem of the river it drains into, is as good as dead. It’s not even as if these factories support jobs and growth: by killing the rivers, they help todestroy local economies.I thought we’d won this argument. But argument, it seems, is no match for economic power. Reynolds’s loyalties appeared to lie not with the ecosystems she was meant to protect, but with the lobbyists threatening them. Perhaps this shouldn’t be surprising: before she joined Keir Starmer’s government, she worked as alobbyist, representing the financial sector.Disturbingly, it was the new environment secretary, Angela Eagle, while working as Reynolds’s deputy, who sought to drive through the “planning reforms” the poultry industry was demanding, telling chicken lobbyists“planning should enable ambition, not stifle it”. Will she now take Burnham’s“circuit breaker”promise seriously, and junk this appeasement of one of our most destructive industries?At a recent farming festival, Reynolds claimed that boosting the poultry sector wouldenhance our food security. I don’t know how many times we need to point this out, but isn’t it bleeding obvious that it has the opposite effect? The feed sustaining chickens in these factories must be either imported or grown on land that could otherwise directly feed people. There could be more soya in chicken than in tofu:one reportestimates that it takes 109g of soya to produce 100g of chicken breast. Yet this is just one of several components of their diet.The chicken business in the UK is also controlled by a handful of giant companies: another security red light. If food security were the objective, the government would encourage us to eat fewer chickens and other animals, and more plants. But in parliament, Reynolds alsoproudly announcedthat she was disregarding the targets set by the government’s Climate Change Committee to reduce livestock numbers. I find myself asking, over and again: “Are theytryingto harm this country?”To advance their dismal agenda, Reynolds and Eagle set up something called the Farming and Food Partnership Board. Its aim is to encourage“closer collaboration between farming, industry and government”. How could it possibly be closer? The environment department, Defra, is a wholly owned subsidiary of the farming industry.The only membersof this new board are government ministers and corporate representatives and lobbyists. Among itsobjectivesis a “poultry sector growth plan”, to address the issue of “planning barriers”.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |WeeklySign up toMatters of OpinionGuardian columnists and writers on what they’ve been debating, thinking about, reading, and morePreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionThis chickening out is just one of many examples of how, by working for special interests, the environment department has harmed the ecosystems and the people it is supposed to protect. Another is the government’s“delivery plan”to restore nature, released by Reynolds just before she left office. It’s not a plan, and it won’t deliver: just a catalogue ofvague hopes and blandishments. It could be summarised as “do nothing, for fear of offending landowners”. Far from meeting its promise to protect and restore 30% of the land in England by 2030, Reynolds’s department has presided overcontinued decline. That’s her legacy: less nature, less resilience, less security.Much worse is planned: Starmer’s government proposed a“regulating for growth bill”, whose text we haven’t yet seen, but which appears to threaten even greater assaults on nature protection. It may contain one of his government’s most alarming proposals: creating a system that could be described aspop-up freeports, in which companies all over the country would be able to operate intemporary “sandboxes”, where the usual rules don’t apply. We should see this as a key test ofBurnham’s promise to end neoliberalism: will he drop this nonsense?I’ll keep asking the question, regardless of who occupies this office: do you know the name of the environment secretary? The answer is likely to depend on one factor: whether they are ready to break the grip of corporate power, and defend us and the rest of the living world. Whether, in other words, they want to do their job.George Monbiot is a Guardian columnistExplore more on these topicsFarmingOpinionRiversPollutionLobbyingFoodMeat industrycommentShareReuse this content

title: Legal logging and soaring saplings: the ‘green islands’ bucking the deforestation trend in central America | Environment | The Guardian
description: In Guatemala’s Maya biosphere reserve local people are making a living from trees at the same time as increasing forest cover, showing nature is best protected by those who depend on it
Author: Mark Hillsdon
Headline: In Guatemala’s Maya biosphere reserve local people are making a living from trees at the same time as increasing forest cover, showing nature is best protected by those who depend on it
Content: A worker at one of the Carmelita cooperative’s sawmills in Petén, northern Guatemala.Photograph: Hum Images/Universal Images Group/Getty ImagesView image in fullscreenA worker at one of the Carmelita cooperative’s sawmills in Petén, northern Guatemala.Photograph: Hum Images/Universal Images Group/Getty ImagesThe age of extinctionEnvironmentLegal logging and soaring saplings: the ‘green islands’ bucking the deforestation trend in central AmericaIn Guatemala’s Maya biosphere reserve local people are making a living from trees at the same time as increasing forest cover, showing nature is best protected by those who depend on itThe age of extinction is supported byAbout this contentMark HillsdonThu 23 Jul 2026 06.00 EDTLast modified on Thu 23 Jul 2026 12.59 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleThe low rumbling grows louder until a huge harvester crashes into the open, dragging a 15-metre trunk of mahogany in its wake. The tractor manoeuvres before releasing its load and shovelling it like a matchstick on to a growing pile of lumber at the edge of the clearing. Then, with a crunch of gears and a belch of diesel, it turns and lurches back into the forest.This is sustainable forestry at its most brutal, yet in a few weeks’ time silence will return to this pocket of tropical forest in Guatemala’sMaya biosphere reserve(MBR). Nature will be left to reclaim the land and chainsaws won’t return for at least four years, as part of a strict management plan that is helping to buck the trend of rampant deforestation.The 2.1m-hectare (5.2m-acre) MBR, in the northern province of Petén, was established by the Guatemalan government in 1990 andrecognised by Unesco. It now anchors the largest natural forest in Central America, says José Román Carrera, a forestry director at the Rainforest Alliance, who was part of the initial discussions to establish the reserve.View image in fullscreenTropical forest within the Maya biosphere reserve.Photograph: Johan Ordóñez/AFP/Getty ImagesThe MBRis divided into three zones: a buffer (23%) protects the forest from encroachment around its southern edge, while the rest is divided between a core (39%), which is the base for national parks and conservation areas, and a multiple-use zone (38%), where people are allowed to make a living from the forest – as long as it is done sustainably.The multiple-use zone includes 12 community forestry concessions (CFCs), each with 25-year contracts, collectively spread across 400,000 hectares. The first concession was established in 1997 following the end of the country’s long civil war and over the last 20 years they have been central to ending decades of deforestation in Central America’s most illegally logged jungle.Map of Maya biosphere reserveWhile the reserve as a whole has lost nearly 13% of its natural cover in 24 years, the community concessions have maintained near-zero deforestation, serving as “green islands” for the region, according to the National Council of Protected Areas (Conap).A few miles along the forest road from the growing piles of valuable timber, a temporary camp of huts has been set up. Some hammocks are strung between trees and food is being prepared in an open kitchen.This is the hub of the logging operations and for the next three weeks will be home to 30 men from the Carmelita cooperative that manages the concession.View image in fullscreenThe Carmelita cooperative’s ability to process trees has boosted local employment.Photograph: Hum/Universal Images/Getty ImagesEvery year, they take just two trees from each of the 1,000 hectares earmarked for harvesting on a rotational basis within the 43,000-hectare concession.Every tree is mapped, logged and numbered and there are strict criteria about which can be felled, depending on size and abundance, explains Hedy Godinez Pulido, a forestry engineer. “The young trees define how many old trees you can cut,” he says. Carefully selecting the trees helps to preserve the forest, as well as opening up pockets of forest floor for seeds to germinate and grow.View image in fullscreenForester Bayron Chub shows how a tree was carefully felled to avoid damaging other trees and meet FSC guidelines.Photograph: Mark HillsdonBefore the sustainable plan was in place, says forestry technician Bayron Chub, it was “chaos”. Private companies would select trees at random by hitting them with a hammer. “If it sounded damaged, they left it,” Chub says. “They cut the easy way … and left huge wasted stumps in the ground. There was no planning.”As part of their contract, the concession must work to strictForestry Stewardship Council (FSC)standards, a stamp that means the timber can be exported. FSC staff inspect every stump, assessing how it was cut and the angle the tree fell; any miscalculations and the community will be fined.None of this comes as a surprise to those who have always lived on the land. “Our fathers taught us to live with the forest without causing damage,” says Chub.The community of La Pasadita, further south, has seen how strictly the rules are applied. In 2009, explains Walbi Cardona, the out-going president of the village association, Conap suspended the community’s contract after discovering illegal logging within the 18,000-hectare concession. Winning their licence back has proved a struggle. “Nobody believed in them [the former concession managers] because they did bad things,” he says.In the past five years, they have worked with the Rainforest Alliance through itsProsperous and Resilient Landscape programmeto create a plan for restorative production, in an attempt to regain Conap’s trust. The project is based around a community nursery – the largest within the MBR – where more than 120,000 seedlings from 23 different species of trees have been nurtured, creating dozens of jobs.The trees are used to restore degraded pastureland, bringing back tree cover and removing the threat of wildfires, which regularly swept across the dry fields.View image in fullscreenCedar and mahogany saplings surrounded by a temporary planting of maize and beans.Photograph: Mark HillsdonThe former concession is regreening, as tall, spindly cedar and mahogany saplings poke up above an understory of maize and beans, which will be grown for just two years, providing food and improving the soil.“We’re very grateful … for the restoration that has taken place in our community,” says Cardona. “There has been a lot of participation from women and youth, generating social benefits and bringing income back to their homes, which did not happen before. This has been a massive project for us.”The mahogany trees in La Pasadita’s nursery grow much more quickly than those in the forest, says Cardona. “The secret of the success is the nursery,” he says – they come from good seeds, have been well planted and have space to grow. They alsoflourish in direct sunlight, without competition from other vegetation.Old spice and ramón (a nut tree) have also been planted, along with cantemo, a type of acacia, which is a favourite nesting site for thescarlet macaw, a move that will hopefully encourage the rare birds back to the area.View image in fullscreenTwo Rainforest Alliance workers inspect a young tree on the Maya reserve.Photograph: Mark HillsdonDrones are used to monitor the landscape and, according to data from Conap, there has been a net increase in forest coverage of 105 hectares. “Restored forest is coming back at the expense of pasture,” says Carrera, proudly.Conap is now looking at how it can use the success of the project to guide a national policy of productive restoration, a move that could finally see the community win back its concession.Uaxactún, in the far north of Petén province, is another concession where, according to Conap, forest cover has grown by 113 hectares. This is one of the largest concessions in the MBR, covering more than 83,000 hectares, with a village spread out either side of a long grassy area that previously doubled as a landing strip for planes collecting the sap from chicle trees, which was once used in chewing gum.View image in fullscreenWomen from Uaxactún select xate palm leaves, used in flower arranging, for sale.Photograph: Johan Ordóñez/AFP/Getty ImagesAlongside strict forestry management, Uaxactún has used lucrative non-timber forest products such asxate palmto boost its income. This, too, is harvested to strict guidelines, with most of the fronds sold to the US for religious ceremonies. It is a value-added product that can employ 40 people at certain times of the year, explains Francisco Aldana, vice-president of the village. And since they took over managing sales, a trade that was once worth a few hundred dollars a year now brings in $60,000 (£45,000) annually.In the village’s timber workshop, Rafael Fajardo and Duglas Barillas are working on fittings for the 10 cabins they are building as part of the community’s big tourism push. Their scholarships in carpentry were paid for by money made from sustainably managing the forest. By creating jobs this way, the concession hopes to stem the migration of young people from the countryside.Can Bolivia’s historic big cat release help change jaguar conservation in the country?Read moreThe community is also working with the US environmental non-profit The Nature Conservancy (TNC) on itstourism offering, which includes tours of its nearby Maya ruins, trekking to the ancient city of El Mirador and mountain biking.Back at theCarmelita cooperative, the rich scent of sap from freshly cut timber mingles with the smell of varnish from the community’s woodworking shop. The group’s president, Jesús Barrera, is preparing the vast marshalling yard for the next influx of timber.View image in fullscreenJesús Barrera: ‘We’ve invested in training for local people. That’s a big change.’Photograph: Mark HillsdonIn the past, he says, they simply sold the trees as rough lumber, as they had no equipment or skilled labour to process them. With four sawmills and a team of local carpenters, the emphasis has switched to value-added products. “Workers used to come from outside, now 95% are local people because we have invested in training. That’s a big change,” says Barrera.Alongside planks cut to size, the workshops produce furniture – there is growing interest from European buyers – necks for guitars and even kits to build wooden houses.The work has brought jobs and money to pay for services such as health and education that are not provided by the government. Reports show that household incomes in the concessions are up tothree times higherthan the national poverty level.View image in fullscreenA mahogany sapling: La Pasadita’s tree nursery has been highly successful.Photograph: Leonardo Diaz Romero/AlamyAt first, sustainable management was unpopular, says Carrera, and people wanted freedom to do what they wanted. But they worked through the challenges, he says, with the cooperative projected to make around $3m this year by sustainably targeting a controlled number of trees.Initially, he adds, 80% of the concession was given over to conservation and just 20% to production. Now these figures have been reversed but still the forest continues to grow and flourish.It goes to show, he says, that “the forest is best protected by those whose livelihoods depend on it”.Find moreage of extinction coverage here, and follow the biodiversity reportersRebecca Ratcliffe,Phoebe WestonandPatrick Greenfieldin the Guardian app for more nature coverageExplore more on these topicsEnvironmentThe age of extinctionDeforestationLogging and land-clearingGuatemalaTrees and forestsAmericasConservationfeaturesShareReuse this content

title: Ex-BBC journalist awarded £20,000 after MI5 and PSNI unlawfully accessed phone data | Police | The Guardian
description: Tribunal rules surveillance aimed at identifying Vincent Kearney’s sources was disproportionate and unlawful
Author: Rory Carroll
Headline: Tribunal rules surveillance aimed at identifying Vincent Kearney’s sources was disproportionate and unlawful
Content: Vincent Kearney was subjected to unlawful covert intelligence gathering when he was the BBC’s Northern Ireland home affairs correspondent.Photograph: Liam McBurney/PAView image in fullscreenVincent Kearney was subjected to unlawful covert intelligence gathering when he was the BBC’s Northern Ireland home affairs correspondent.Photograph: Liam McBurney/PAPoliceEx-BBC journalist awarded £20,000 after MI5 and PSNI unlawfully accessed phone dataTribunal rules surveillance aimed at identifying Vincent Kearney’s sources was disproportionate and unlawfulRory CarrollIreland correspondentThu 23 Jul 2026 11.56 EDTFirst published on Thu 23 Jul 2026 11.46 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleA former BBC journalist has been awarded £20,000 in damages after MI5 and the Police Service ofNorthern Irelandunlawfully obtained data from his mobile phone.A specialist tribunal on Thursday ordered the agencies to pay the compensation to Vincent Kearney for attempts to identify his sources while he was the BBC’s Northern Ireland home affairs correspondent.The investigatory powers tribunal (IPT), a special court that investigates complaints against the UK’s spy agencies, said the surveillance had been disproportionate, not in accordance with the law and ordered each agency to pay Kearney £10,000.“The heightened protection provided to journalists is not for the benefit of the journalist personally, but for the public interest in the ability of his readers or audience to receive information from him,” three judges, Lady Carmichael, Mr Justice Chamberlain and Stephen Shaw KC, said in the ruling.Lawyers for Kearney and the BBC said there were seven separate police orMI5operations that unlawfully obtained Kearney’s confidential communications data between 2006 and 2018.The PSNI ran four operations and created a “profile” of Kearney that included car registrations and the names of his wife and mother-in-law.The monitoring enabled the security agencies to see numbers in contact with Kearney and the time of calls and texts, though not their content. Other data revealed subscriber names and addresses of caller numbers. In one operation,the tribunal heard,the PSNI obtained information relating to 1,580 calls or texts.Kearney, now the northern editor of the Irish broadcaster RTÉ, said the ruling was a victory for journalism and an affirmation of the duty and legal rights of journalists to protect sources.“The fact that the court decided to award damages reflects the seriousness of the case,” he said. “The judges make clear that the impact of the illegal activities on me, and on the trust that potential sources can place in the confidentiality of their communications with me, was an important factor in their decision to award damages.”A BBC spokesperson said the case raised issues of grave concern not just to the corporation but wider society. They said: “Journalistic freedoms are a basic part of how democracies work. Their importance is recognised in law and matters to all of us.“What happened in this instance was wrong and must never be repeated. It caused harm to a journalist, jeopardised trust and threatened the safeguards on which public-interest journalism depends and has a right to expect.”Kearney took legal action after information emerged in the case of journalists Barry McCaffrey and Trevor Birney. In alandmark judgmentin 2024, the IPT found the PSNI and the Metropolitan police had unlawfully carried out covert surveillance on the two Belfast-based journalists.The pair were wrongfully arrested in 2018 after they producedNo Stone Unturned, a documentary that alleged collusion between police and loyalist paramilitaries in a Troubles massacre. Details disclosed in that case suggested that public bodies, including police forces, had spied on Kearney.Jon Boutcher, the PSNI chief constable, accepted the ruling and apologised to Kearney. He said “significant changes to practice and policy” had taken place.Last year, an independent review ​commissioned by Boutcher found the PSNI ​used journalists’ ‌phone numbers ​to check ​for information leaks by officers or staff but that improper surveillance was not “widespread or systemic”.Explore more on these topicsPoliceNorthern IrelandMI5BBCUK security and counter-terrorismTelevision industrynewsShareReuse this content

title: Jailed double killer says new sexual offences trial a tactic to stop his release | UK news | The Guardian
description: Allan Grimson, convicted in 2001 of murdering two men, was in line for a parole hearing before new investigation
Author: Steven Morris
Headline: Allan Grimson, convicted in 2001 of murdering two men, was in line for a parole hearing before new investigation
Content: Allan Grimson spoke to police about sexual contact with four young males while under investigation for the murders, but was told no further action would be taken.Photograph: PAView image in fullscreenAllan Grimson spoke to police about sexual contact with four young males while under investigation for the murders, but was told no further action would be taken.Photograph: PAUK newsJailed double killer says new sexual offences trial a tactic to stop his releaseAllan Grimson, convicted in 2001 of murdering two men, was in line for a parole hearing before new investigationSteven MorrisThu 23 Jul 2026 11.45 EDTLast modified on Thu 23 Jul 2026 21.30 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleA double killer who has been in jail for a quarter of a century has claimed he is standing trial over new charges of sexual offences as a tactic to stop him from being freed on parole.The former Royal Navy petty officer Allan Grimsonwas jailed in 2001for sexually assaulting and battering to death two young men in the late 1990s.At the time, he also spoke to police about sexual contact with four other young males but was told no further action would be taken over them. Six years ago, the Parole Board recommended Grimson be moved to an open prison as a first step towards being freed on licence.But his barrister, John Femi-Ola KC, said in court on Thursday that Grimson’s chance of parole had been deliberately “stymied” when police began reinvestigating his contacts with the four men and the Crown Prosecution Service gave the go-ahead for him to be charged.Femi-Ola told a jury at Winchester crown court hearing the allegations, that in 2020 the Parole Board recommended that Grimson be transferred to an open prison for an “extensive period” of testing to see if he was suitable for release.The barrister described this as a “huge step” for Grimson, but said the authorities stepped in, approaching the four young males.Femi-Ola said that when police contacted one of the alleged victims, he was hesitant about making a fresh complaint and asked what it would achieve. The officer replied: “If successful it would mean his parole is denied.”View image in fullscreenNicholas Wright.Photograph: PAView image in fullscreenSion Jenkins.Photograph: PAFemi-Ola also said a journalist writing a book had “marched” another of the men to a police station for him to talk to officers about Grimson.The barrister said the book would be for sale and told the jury: “In this country, justice is not for sale at any cost.”Femi-Ola argued there were inconsistencies in the accounts given by the men now and what they said to police more than 20 years ago.Grimson, now 66, was jailed in 2001 for battering Nicholas Wright, 18, and Sion Jenkins, 20, to death at his flat above a parade of shops in Portsmouth, Hampshire, in 1997 and 1998 respectively. Both victims had served in the navy.The prosecution has said Grimson was an instructor at a navy firefighting school, a posting that brought him into contact with many young men over whom he exercised significant authority.Grimson denies charges of rape, indecent assault and taking indecent pictures of a child.The trial continues.Explore more on these topicsUK newsEnglandnewsShareReuse this content

title: Palestine Action barrister’s prosecution criticised as ‘chilling’ | Contempt of court | The Guardian
description: CBA chair says contempt charge over closing speech by Rajiv Menon KC has left lawyers fearful of doing their job
Author: Haroon Siddique
Headline: CBA chair says contempt charge over closing speech by Rajiv Menon KC has left lawyers fearful of doing their job
Content: Menon is accused of breaching a judge’s directions in the trial of six people over a protest in 2024.Photograph: Garden Court ChambersMenon is accused of breaching a judge’s directions in the trial of six people over a protest in 2024.Photograph: Garden Court ChambersContempt of courtPalestine Action barrister’s prosecution criticised as ‘chilling’CBA chair says contempt charge over closing speech by Rajiv Menon KC has left lawyers fearful of doing their jobHaroon SiddiqueLegal affairs correspondentThu 23 Jul 2026 04.52 EDTFirst published on Thu 23 Jul 2026 03.11 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleThe prosecution of a leading human rights barrister for contempt of court over his closing speech during a trial of Palestine Action activists has left lawyers fearful of doing their job, the chair of the Criminal Bar Association (CBA) has said.Rajiv Menon KC, who was acting for the defence, is due to stand trial next week, accused of breaching the judge’s directions in the trial of six people for a direct action protest at an arms factory of the Israeli subsidiary Elbit Systems UK in Filton, near Bristol, in 2024.On Wednesday, senior legal figures criticised the prosecution of Menon, believed to be the first barrister to be prosecuted over remarks made during a closing speech.One day, your life and future may depend on a jury. The integrity of that legal process is in peril right now | Imran KhanRead moreRiel Karmy-Jones KC, the chair of the CBA, told the online meeting: “We have heard of barristers changing their speeches or being told to change them – including in cases where no direction has been given by a judge. And that really can’t be ignored, because people at the criminal bar have become uncertain and scared of what they can and can’t say; scared that somehow they might be – at the end of a trial where they have faithfully represented their client themselves – facing a charge of criminal contempt.“It is starting tofeel like it’s about the politics of the subject matter of the trial, rather than the core principles of how a barrister represents his client.”Last month, the trial judge in the Palestine Action case, Mr Justice Johnson, referred the question of whether Menon should face contempt of court proceedings to another judge, Mr Justice Nicklin, who subsequently ruled that he should. It came after a previous attempt to refer Menon was overturned on appeal on procedural grounds.The Labour peer and former Liberty director Shami Chakrabarti said criticising judges was a delicate and dangerous thing to do given the threats they could face but that in Menon’s case they had made “a terrible, capricious mistake” and acted in “a very chilling manner”.In a written message, her fellow Labour peer Helena Kennedy KC, one of the UK’s most renowned criminal lawyers, described Menon’s prosecution as “another assault upon our justice system … [which] ought to be resisted”, while the leading human rights lawyer Gareth Pierce called the prosecution “terrifying”.Support for Menon was also conveyed by families of victims of the Hillsborough disaster, whom he represented.Nicklin’s court order initiating contempt proceedings said Menon was alleged to have deliberately breached Johnson’s rulings and directions “knowing that his conduct would create, or was capable of creating, a real risk of impeding the administration of justice.“It is further alleged that, in the same speech to the jury, the respondent made statements which were capable of suggesting to the jury that the court was not impartial, in that he referred to the defendants being prevented from giving evidence about Elbit Systems, and later referred to Elbit Systems being ‘protected’ and ‘wined and dined in the corridors of power’.“Those statements were capable of creating the impression that the defendants had been treated unfairly and that Elbit Systems had been improperly protected in the proceedings.”Explore more on these topicsContempt of courtTrial by juryBarristersUK criminal justicenewsShareReuse this content

title: Police admit investigation errors in run-up to Manchester synagogue attack | Police | The Guardian
description: GMP chief constable says crucial data on phones seized from Heaton Park attacker was not examined
Author: Mark Brown
Headline: GMP chief constable says crucial data on phones seized from Heaton Park attacker was not examined
Content: Jihad al-Shamie’s phones were seized by officers before the attack on Heaton Park synagogue but crucial data was not examined.Photograph: FacebookView image in fullscreenJihad al-Shamie’s phones were seized by officers before the attack on Heaton Park synagogue but crucial data was not examined.Photograph: FacebookPolicePolice admit investigation errors in run-up to Manchester synagogue attackGMP chief constable says crucial data on phones seized from Heaton Park  attacker was not examinedMark BrownNorth of England correspondentThu 23 Jul 2026 08.38 EDTFirst published on Thu 23 Jul 2026 06.30 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleGreater Manchester police have apologised for a “missed opportunity” to act before an attack on a synagogue.The force’s chief constable, Sir Stephen Watson, said the phones of the Heaton Park synagogue attacker, Jihad al-Shamie, were seized by officers but crucial data was not examined.“There can, however, be little doubt that the information contained within these devices would have inspired further investigative action,” Watson said. “It is clear that our not having reviewed the data available to us prior to the attack constitutes a missed opportunity.“I am disappointed at the prospect of adding to the distress of all of those affected by the terrible events last October. For this, I am deeply sorry.”Watson’s apology came as a friend of Shamie, Mohammad Bashir, was on Thursday jailed for life with a minimum term of 17 years for a terrorist act that the pair had planned two months before the synagogue attack.View image in fullscreenMohammad Bashir, who was jailed for life on Thursday for a terrorist act planned two months before the synagogue attack.Photograph: Greater Manchester Police/PABashir, 31, and Shamie, 35, travelled by car to the UK Defence Academy in Shrivenham, Swindon, on a hostile reconnaissance mission,Manchestercrown court heard.That took place in August last year. In October, Shamie was shot dead by police after he attacked the synagogue on Yom Kippur, the holiest day in the Jewish calendar. He was wearing a fake suicide belt.Two Jewish men, Adrian Daulby, 53, and Melvin Cravitz, 66, died in the deadliest antisemitic attack in Britain in recent memory.Watson said that now legal proceedings against Bashir were completed, he could share more information about police dealings with Shamie.He said Shamie was arrested by police in February and September last year.Watson said: “In conducting an internal review, it became apparent that GMP [Greater Manchester police] had seized al-Shamie’s mobile devices, at the point of each arrest. The significance of the data contents of these devices was substantial in respect of evidencing an extremist mindset.“Regrettably, I can confirm that the significance of the information stored on al-Shamie’s devices was not realised as they had not been examined before the attack took place on 2 October 2025.”He said the Independent Office forPoliceConduct was reviewing GMP’s past investigations into Shamie.Bashir pleaded guilty last month to a charge of preparing terrorist acts. He drove his friend Shamie on a 10-hour round trip to MoD Shrivenham, home of the UK’s Defence Academy, which provides professional defence and security education to the British armed forces, the wider UK government, UK industry and overseas personnel.Normally, up to 4,000 staff, contractors, students and visitors are on site at any given time, the court heard.Prosecutors said there was no evidence to suggest Bashir had any involvement in the Heaton Park incident but that the planned attack on the defence facility was similar in nature and would have caused multiple fatalities.Raphi Bloom, who sits on the managing board of the Jewish Representative Council of Greater Manchester, had been heading to Heaton Park on the day of the attack before receiving a call telling him to stay away.Bloom said he welcomed the apology and the candour of the chief constable and would let the watchdog investigation take its course.“We have great respect for Sir Stephen and his officers,” he said. “They have been outstanding in the way they have protected the community, made us feel safe and made us feel reassured.”Jailing Bashir, Judge Cheema-Grubb described him as a “fully committed jihadist” at the time of the reconnaissance trip. The pair, she said, were “equally engaged” in planning an attack on a British military target “in which you and he expected to kill and be killed”.She rejected his claim to a probation officer that he no longer held extremist views and regretted his actions. The judge said: “I consider you remain dangerous. You have given no grounds that your attitude has really changed at all.”The court heard that after the synagogue attack, WhatsApp messages and voice-note exchanges between the pair were uncovered when police analysed the two phones seized from Shamie earlier in 2025.CCTV footage of Bashir and Shamie at the Al-Sunnah mosque in Cheetham Hill formed a key part of the prosecution case against them. Both wrongly thought they had hidden from cameras and that their conversations would not be heard as they discussed their plans.After the sentencing, Manchester-born Bashir, who also holds a Pakistani passport, smiled to family members in the upper public gallery of the courtroom as he walked from the dock.Explore more on these topicsPoliceGreater ManchesterUK security and counter-terrorismCounter-terrorism policyManchesterAntisemitismnewsShareReuse this content

title: Mayor shot dead in Mexico months after surviving assassination attempt | Mexico | The Guardian
description: Valentín Lavín Romero is the 14th mayor to be murdered since Claudia Sheinbaum became president in 2024
Author: Thomas Graham
Headline: Valentín Lavín Romero is the 14th mayor to be murdered since Claudia Sheinbaum became president in 2024
Content: Valentín Lavín Romero, of the Green party, was killed in the town hall in Temoac, near Mexico City.Photograph: Valentín Lavín Romero/FacebookView image in fullscreenValentín Lavín Romero, of the Green party, was killed in the town hall in Temoac, near Mexico City.Photograph: Valentín Lavín Romero/FacebookMexicoMayor shot dead in Mexico months after surviving assassination attemptValentín Lavín Romero is the 14th mayor to be murdered since Claudia Sheinbaum became president in 2024Thomas GrahamThu 23 Jul 2026 12.32 EDTLast modified on Thu 23 Jul 2026 21.30 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleA mayor was shot dead on Wednesday in the Mexican state of Morelos after having survived a previous assassination attempt earlier this year. He is the 14th mayor to be murdered sinceClaudia Sheinbaumbecame president in October 2024.Valentín Lavín Romero, who was from the Green party, an ally of the ruling leftwing Morena party, was wounded at the end of January when hitmen shot him at a petrol station on a motorway.This time the hitmen entered the town hall of Temoac, nearMexicoCity, in the middle of the day before shooting Lavín Romero dead and escaping on a motorbike.Authorities are yet to identify a motive or suspects, but the Morelos prosecutor’s office vowed to “exhaust all lines of investigation”.Almost 100 mayors have been murdered in Mexico since 2006, when the then president, Felipe Calderón, deployed the military to take on Mexico’s organised crime groups, ultimately causing drug-related violence to rise.The municipal level of government is theleast protected layerof the state and where criminal groups seek deals with authorities to strengthen their hold over the local territory and its businesses.Kidnappings, threats and ‘protection fees’: how can Mexico confront rise in deadly extortion?Read moreLocal media reported Lavín Romero himself allegedly had ties to local crime groups. His mother-in-law, a former treasurer for the municipal government, was arrested in September 2025 for alleged ties to a criminal group known as Los Aparicio and involvement in kidnappings.In March, the People’s Front in Defence of Land and Water, an activist group, demanded authorities investigate Lavín Romero for his alleged involvement in the murders of two activists, Samir Flores and Sandra Rosa Camacho Flores.In May – three months after the first attempt on his life –a banner was leftin the centre of Temoac, seemingly by a criminal group, accusing Lavín Romero of protecting its rivals and demanding he fall in line with their interests.His assassination comes as Mexico’s federal government takes steps to unpick the corruption networks between municipal authorities and criminal groups as part of Operation Swarm, under which 85 officials have been arrested across three states, including Morelos.According to official data, the homicide rate in Mexico has fallen by roughly half over the past year, althoughsecurity analysts saythe true reduction is likely smaller, while more than 130,000 people are registered as missing.Explore more on these topicsMexicoOrganised crimeClaudia SheinbaumAmericasGun crimenewsShareReuse this content

title: Dubai offers £600 rewards to residents who lure friends to visit as tourist numbers plummet | Dubai | The Guardian
description: Residents eligible for dining and hotel packages if they can persuade family and friends to city spurned by visitors amid Iran war
Author: Natasha May
Headline: Residents eligible for dining and hotel packages if they can persuade family and friends to city spurned by visitors amid Iran war
Content: Dubai downtown showing world's tallest building, Burj Al Khalifa.Photograph: Abdel Hadi Ramahi/ReutersView image in fullscreenDubai downtown showing world's tallest building, Burj Al Khalifa.Photograph: Abdel Hadi Ramahi/ReutersDubaiDubai offers £600 rewards to residents who lure friends to visit as tourist numbers plummetResidents eligible for dining and hotel packages if they can persuade family and friends to city spurned by visitors amid Iran warMiddle East crisis – live updatesNatasha MayThu 23 Jul 2026 07.28 EDTLast modified on Thu 23 Jul 2026 21.30 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleDubai is offering to pay residents who attract family and friends to visit the emirate in an effort to revive tourist numbers that have plummeted since the city was drawn into theUS-Israel war on Iran.The Dubai Invite scheme, an initiative of the department of economy and tourism, allows residents of theUnited Arab Emiratesto nominate friends and relatives and then to receive benefits worth more than 3,000 UAE dirham (about £610) if they visit.Visit Dubai’s website says residents will receive a “benefits package” including hotel stays, dining deals and attraction tickets.Dubai property sales have fallen ‘off a cliff’ since start of Middle East warRead moreEligibility terms state that each resident is eligible for a maximum of three benefit packages for visitors arriving before 31 October, but benefits can be redeemed until December.The visitors must be non-UAE residents, arrive on a valid tourist visa and can only be nominated once.The scheme comes as the UAE’s tourism sector experiences a significant downturn, with international passenger numbers falling sharply after theGulf states became drawn into the US-Israel war on Iran.In February, Iran retaliated by firing missiles and drones which hit the world-famous Fairmont hotel and Dubai’s international airport,shattering the imagethe city had sold influencers and the ultra-rich of a gilded playground, safe from the volatility of the wider region.In 2025 Dubai welcomednearly 20 million tourists, a record. However, since the conflict thenumber of airlines operating at Dubai airports has halvedas many planes were turned around due to missiles and tourists avoided routes over the Middle East.There are reports of massive hotel vacancies and businesses sufferingdeclines in revenue of more than 50%.Some of Dubai’smost famous hotels have shut entirely,many bringing forward plans for renovations and refurbishments such as the sail-shaped Burj Al Arab, which will close for 18 months for a major restoration.Other hotels and businesses are leaning into the government’s new scheme, offering discounts of 45% and free nights.Local media on Thursday reportedthat within the first 48 hours, the scheme had attracted more than 10,000 applications.Explore more on these topicsDubaiUS-Israel war on IranMiddle East and north AfricaUnited Arab EmiratesnewsShareReuse this content

title: Minnesota man sentenced to life for killing Democratic lawmaker Melissa Hortman | Minnesota | The Guardian
description: Vance Boelter receives two life terms after admitting politically motivated killings and shootings
Author: Guardian staff reporter
Headline: Vance Boelter receives two life terms after admitting politically motivated killings and shootings
Content: The caskets of Melissa Hortman and her husband, Mark, lie in state at the Minnesota capitol in St Paul on 27 June 2025.Photograph: Tim Evans/ReutersView image in fullscreenThe caskets of Melissa Hortman and her husband, Mark, lie in state at the Minnesota capitol in St Paul on 27 June 2025.Photograph: Tim Evans/ReutersMinnesotaMinnesota man sentenced to life for killing Democratic lawmaker Melissa HortmanVance Boelter receives two life terms after admitting politically motivated killings and shootingsSign up for the Breaking News US newsletter emailAssociated PressThu 23 Jul 2026 13.32 EDTLast modified on Thu 23 Jul 2026 18.29 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleTheMinnesotaman who pleaded guilty to killing a top Democratic lawmaker and her husband was sentenced to spend the rest of his life in prison on Thursday, a little over a year after the political attacks.Vance Boelter, 59, was sentenced to two consecutive life terms plus 40 years in prison after pleading guilty in June to the six counts related to the killings of the former house speaker Melissa Hortman and her husband, Mark, and the shootings of state senator John Hoffman and his wife, Yvette.Fellow lawmakers and survivors of the shooting spoke earlier in the hearing, describing the lasting fear and physical harm that Boelter caused.Boelter pleaded guilty to avoid the death penalty; instead, he agreed to the maximum sentence he could be issued short of the death penalty. Mark Hortman’s father and siblings voiced their anger that he would not face the death penalty.Wearing orange prison garb and shackled at the ankles, Boelter sat extremely still, never looking at the survivors and family members as they read their victim impact statements.Victims and survivors described the impact of the attacks.In court on Thursday, Hoffman said he and his family were constantly reminded of the extraordinary violence of that night, even in the mundane details of day-to-day life. He said they did not receive mail at home any more, they installed solid steel doors, and that they had changed how they do everyday tasks because of their injuries.“The defendant looked me in the eyes and pulled the trigger. The defendant looked at my wife and chose to do the same,” Hoffman said. “My wife and I now live with injuries. We live with our bodies remembering the gunfire.”Yvette Hoffman said she had been too traumatized to work since the attack in their home, and that she sees a therapist weekly.“I fought him, your Honor, with everything I had. And I finally got him out of our home. I will never forget looking down and watching my blood slowly stream down that white washing machine as I felt myself sinking to the floor,” she said, barely holding back tears as she spoke.Boelter admitted in court in June that he spent months identifying and stalking his targets, before driving to their homes in the middle of the night, disguised as a police officer, with the intention of killing them. The killings sparked the largest police search in state history.Mark Hortman’s parents and siblings spoke of their anger on Thursday, both at the man who killed him and at the justice system.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |Around 2-3 times a daySign up toBreaking News USGet the most important news as it breaksPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotion“It will not offer me peace of mind that he will have room and board provided for the rest of his life, paid for by my tax dollars,” said Lisa Hortman Bean, Mark’s sister.Boelter went to Hortman’s home, wearing a tactical uniform and mask, about 3.30am on 14 June 2025. He rang the doorbell while shouting he was a police officer doing a welfare check, according to court documents.Mark Hortman answered the door. Boelter gave him a fake name and badge number before requesting he summon Melissa Hortman to the door as well. He shot Mark Hortman multiple times before pushing into the house and shooting the lawmaker as she fled upstairs. Melissa and Mark Hortman were killed.Boelter had already visited the Hoffmans’ home that night, shooting and critically injuring the senator and his wife, according to police and court documents. Court documents said Boelter also went to two other lawmakers’ houses that night. In one instance, no one answered the door, and in the other, Boelter was spooked when a police officer approached him in his car, believing he was another officer.Explore more on these topicsMinnesotaUS crimenewsShareReuse this content

title: How the Bab al-Mandab blockade threat helped push oil back above $100 | Oil | The Guardian
description: Escalating Houthi warnings to ships carrying Saudi crude through the strait lead to fears of further price surge
Author: Jillian Ambrose
Headline: Escalating Houthi warnings to ships carrying Saudi crude through the strait lead to fears of further price surge
Content: A satellite view of the Bab-al-Mandab strait.Photograph: worldview.earthdata.nasa.gov/Nasa WorldviewView image in fullscreenA satellite view of the Bab-al-Mandab strait.Photograph: worldview.earthdata.nasa.gov/Nasa WorldviewOilExplainerHow the Bab al-Mandab blockade threat helped push oil back above $100Escalating Houthi warnings to ships carrying Saudi crude through the strait lead to fears of further price surgeJillian AmbroseEnergy correspondentThu 23 Jul 2026 12.04 EDTLast modified on Thu 23 Jul 2026 21.30 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleA fresh front in the Middle East crisis opened this week, bringing a return of soaring global energy markets and stoking fears that the oil price could surge to $120 (£90) a barrel.In a matter of days the price of Brent crude has jumped by more than 13%,breaching the $100 a barrel markon Thursday after Yemen’s Houthi militias took aim at a new target: Saudi oil exports via the Bab al-Mandab strait.The development comes as the tentative recovery of shipping through the strait of Hormuz, which had helped oil prices to return to pre-crisis prices of as low as $71 at the start of the month, grinds to a halt amidthe breakdown of the US-Iran ceasefire.Any disruption at Bab al-Mandab, the narrow waterway between the Arabian peninsula and the Horn of Africa, would threaten one of the few remaining routes for Gulf oil exports to circumvent the Hormuz blockade, said Jorge León, head of geopolitical analysis at Rystad Energy.“If a ceasefire does not materialise and Hormuz remains largely closed while the Houthi threat to Red Sea shipping intensifies, the risk of a significant rebound in oil prices would be substantial,” he said.What has happened?The latest escalation began with theHouthissending out an email to shipping companies on Monday warning them not to load and unload at Saudi ports or else face being targeted “in any location” within its reach.The Tehran-allied group, which controls Yemen’s capital and its north-western territory along the Bab al-Mandab,reiterated its threatthe following day, claiming that at least six ships had changed course as a result.map showing the strait of Hormuz and the Bab al-Mandab straitThe trade route through the southern entrance to the Red Sea carried about 4.1m barrels of crude oil and refined petroleum products each day last year, or roughly 5% of the global total compared with the20% of global oil supplies that once transited the strait of Hormuz.But in the months since the US-Israeli surprise strikes on Iran at the end of February, volumes through Bab al-Mandab have surged as the channel has become a vital alternative export route for Saudi Arabia’s vast crude volumes.Riyadh began to reroute oil via a pipeline from its Abqaiq processing plant, near the Gulf, to the Yanbu export terminal on its west coast, effectively channelling about 75% of its usual exports to the global market via the Red Sea.Rystad Energy vessel-tracking data indicates that approximately 2.5m barrels a day of these Yanbu volumes are currently moving south through Bab al-Mandab and on to customers in distant markets such as India and China.Traffic through the strait collapsed during the Gaza war when Houthistrikes on vessels near the waterway, launched in support of Palestinians in Gaza, caused many of the world’s largest shipping companies to use alternative routes. They were understood to be considering a return to the trade route this summer, almost three years after the initial disruption began, but the escalating Gulf crisis has laid waste to such plans.What does this mean for oil prices?Since peace talks between the US and Iran broke down andTrump declared the ceasefire “over”, the Brent crude benchmark has climbed by more than a quarter to over $100 a barrel, putting the market on track for its largest monthly gain since March – the first month of the Hormuz blockade.Washington is still insisting the strait is open even though transits have slowed to a trickle amid strikes by both sides on each other’s maritime traffic through the channel.Graph showing daily transits through the strait of HormuzMeanwhile a senior Houthi official said this week that its policing of Saudi crude exports via Bab al-Mandab would cause oil prices to “skyrocket to $200 a barrel in a dreadful shock” for the market.In a video statement this week, a Houthi military spokesperson described it as an “equation of ‘an eye for an eye’” after accusing Riyadh of breaching a four-year ceasefire earlier this month.In response, Saudi Arabia’s military has warned that all Houthi threats against transiting vessels would “be dealt with swiftly and firmly, as such threats are a blatant violation of international law and fall under acts of maritime piracy”. Still, the Houthi threats have taken a toll on the market.At least five Saudi-linked oil tankers bound for Bab al-Mandab turned back as Houthi blockade threats picked up, according to maritime intelligence data from Windward, of which four were carrying Saudi-origin cargo. In addition, an oil tanker destined for China is understood to have reversed course after leaving the Red Sea port of Yanbu to transit the strait.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |Every weekdaySign up toBusiness TodayGet set for the working day – we'll point you to all the business news and analysis you need every morningPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionThe danger posed by not turning back is high. On Thursday the Houthissaid they had hit two ​Saudi tankers, with a Riyadh news agency later confirming that one was ablaze.Yet changing course comes at a steep financial cost. Full avoidance of this waterway, through which a quarter of global container trade passes on its way to and from the Suez canal, requires rerouting around Africa via the Cape of Good Hope. That journey roughly doubles the voyage length and adds an estimated $2m-$2.5m in cost for each transit.Map showing Bab al-Mandab strait and the route around the Cape of Good HopeThis helps explain why some owners keep running the risk, according to a Windward report on Bab al-Mandab traffic on Tuesday. It recorded three Saudi-linked oil tankers continuing towards the strait despite the Houthi warnings, while another three nearby turned off their transmitters in response to the threat.What about road fuels and gas?The pressure on crude oil supplies in the global market is only part of the problem, according to the head of the International Energy Agency.Fatih Birol warned this week that many refineries had reduced their production of fuel products, including transport fuels such as diesel, to avoid the surging cost of crude. Although the lower oil demand from refineries has helped to keep a lid on oil market prices, it has created even greater stress on global fuel supplies.Even as Gulf crude exports increased during the US-Iran ceasefire, the production of road fuels has remained weak, Birol pointed out: “Refinery activity and product supplies have not picked up as much as crude deliveries, meaning that markets for refined oil products, including diesel and gasoline, are considerably tighter than those for crude.”Goldman Sachs has said global supplies of diesel have been under pressure regardless of the resumption of US-Iran hostilities, owing to the market’s high exposure to refinery outages in the Middle East and especially in Russia, where Ukrainian drone attacks have reduced refining capacity by 80%.Graph showing price of oil rising above 100 dollars“Just three weeks ago we were celebrating the biggest ever monthly drop in the price of diesel,” Simon Williams, head of policy at the UK motoring group RAC, said this week. Now, he said, fuel prices are “shooting up like a rocket”. The average price of diesel on British forecourts has climbed by almost 8p, or 5%, to 172.14p a litre in the past fortnight, while petrol has risen by 5p in two and a half weeks to 155.57p, a 3% increase.There are worrying implications for global gas supplies too. While an increase in gas exports from the US and Canada helped to offset about 70% of the lost Gulf supply via Hormuz, availability is expected to remain tight in the run-up to winter as European buyers attempt to refill depleted gas storage facilities, Birol said.Clémence Dubois, head of global campaigns at the green group 350.org, said: “One of the world’s critical choke points remains closed as attempts to reopen it have failed. Now, we see a second critical choke point on the knife’s edge. That should terrify anyone who still thinks fossil fuel dependence is a safe bet ...“It shows just how fragile and dangerous our dependence on oil and gas really is. Those shocks travel straight into food, fuel and transport costs, hitting hardest in countries with the least fiscal room to absorb them, while fossil fuel companies reap insane profits.”Explore more on these topicsOilStrait of HormuzShipping industryCommoditiesUS-Israel war on IranMiddle East and north AfricaHouthisexplainersShareReuse this content

title: Former Barclays CEO Jes Staley faces US congressional panel over Epstein ties | Jeffrey Epstein | The Guardian
description: Staley is latest high-profile figure to testify before House amid investigation into Epstein, convicted sex offender
Author: Anna Betts
Headline: Staley is latest high-profile figure to testify before House amid investigation into Epstein, convicted sex offender
Content: Jes Staley walks near the court in London on 7 March 2025, on the day the Bank of England governor, Andrew Bailey, gave evidence on Jeffrey Epstein.Photograph: Toby Melville/ReutersView image in fullscreenJes Staley walks near the court in London on 7 March 2025, on the day the Bank of England governor, Andrew Bailey, gave evidence on Jeffrey Epstein.Photograph: Toby Melville/ReutersJeffrey EpsteinFormer Barclays CEO Jes Staley faces US congressional panel over Epstein tiesStaley is latest high-profile figure to testify before House amid investigation into Epstein, convicted sex offenderAnna BettsandKalyeena MakortoffThu 23 Jul 2026 08.36 EDTLast modified on Thu 23 Jul 2026 16.12 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleFormerBarclaysbossJes Staleyappeared before by US lawmakers on Thursday in a closed-door interview about his past ties to the convicted child sex offenderJeffrey Epstein.Staley, who spent more than three decades at JPMorgan Chase before joining Barclays in 2015, is the latest in a series of high-profile figures to appear in front of the House committee on oversight and reform as part of its investigation into Epstein, whodied in prisonin August 2019 while awaiting trial over child sex-trafficking charges.A transcript of Staley’s interview is expected to be released at a later date. The panel has released transcripts or videos of interviews with previous witnesses, includingBilland Hillary ClintonandBill Gates. It is also expected to release a transcript of its recent exchange with Goldman Sachs’ former top lawyerKathyrn Ruemmler.Staley’s ties with the well-connected billionaire spelled the end of his career, with the American banker havingresignedas the chief executive of Barclays in November 2021 after UK regulators launched an investigation into Staley’s ties to Epstein and Barclays said the pair “did not have a close relationship”.However, the investigation by Britain’s Financial Conduct Authority, triggered by a cache of 1,200 emails from Staley’s former employer JP Morgan, concluded that the pair were“indeed close”and had a relationship that “went beyond one that was professional in nature”. The former Barclays chief was eventually banned from taking any job in the UK’s financial sector for playing down those ties.US senator accuses Barclays of ‘failure’ to investigate ex-CEO’s ties to EpsteinRead moreBarclays said in a recent statement that the FCA concluded the bank was also “misled by Jes Staley”. The bank added that its own internal investigation was “based on the information that was available to us at the time”.Staley unsuccessfully tried to challenge the regulator’s ruling, resulting in a court hearing that publicly detailed Epstein’s role in Staley’s life.During that hearing, Staleyadmittedto having sex with a member of Epstein’s staff in New York but agreed with a lawyer during cross-examination that he would describe the intercourse as “consensual”.Judges ultimately sided with the FCA, upholding its ban and scuppering efforts to restore Staley’s reputation. He also lost out on £18m ($24m) worth of pay and bonuses fromBarclaysas a result of the ruling.After losing his legal challenge, Staley said he was “disappointed” by the outcome but was “never dishonest”. He previously said of Epstein in 2020, “obviously I thought I knew him well, and I didn’t” and “with hindsight of what we all know now, I deeply regret having had any relationship withJeffrey Epstein.”Staley originally met Epstein in 2000 after he became head of JP Morgan’s private bank, where Epstein was a client. Heremainedin touch with Epstein for several years after the financier pleaded guilty to state charges in Florida of solicitation of prostitution and of solicitation of prostitution with a minor, even visiting Epstein while he was serving his sentence and out on work release.Staley has said that their relationship tapered off after he left JP Morgan, with the two men meeting for a final time in 2015, when Staley took his own yacht, the Bequia, to visit Epstein’s private island.In March 2023, JPMorgan Chasesued Staley, arguing that he should be held liable for any financial penalties the bank faces from lawsuits accusing the bank of helping to facilitate Epstein’s sex trafficking. Later that year, the two sides reached asettlement, of which the terms are confidential.But over the last year, documents released by the US Department of Justice have renewed scrutiny of Staley’s ties to Epstein. They included files, reported by the Guardian, showing that justice department prosecutorshad reviewedallegations of rape and serious bodily harm against a woman by Staley. There is no evidence that prosecutors decided to pursue the allegations. Staley has denied any wrongdoing and has never been charged with a crime related to the allegations.Staley’s US lawyers were contacted for comment.In remarks on Thursday afternoon, Representative Robert Garciasaidthat during the committee’s interview with Staley, the former Barclays boss claimed to lawmakers that he “had no real friendship with Epstein”, which Garcia dismissed and called “just ridiculous”.Explore more on these topicsJeffrey EpsteinJes StaleyHouse of RepresentativesUS CongressBarclaysBankingnewsShareReuse this content

title: EU fines Google €890m for competition breaches over search and apps | Google | The Guardian
description: Firm told to treat third-party services that appear in its results in ‘fair and non-discriminatory manner’
Author: Dan Milmo
Headline: Firm told to treat third-party services that appear in its results in ‘fair and non-discriminatory manner’
Content: Google has been fined €460m for the search-related breach and €430m for the app store violation.Photograph: Steve Marcus/ReutersView image in fullscreenGoogle has been fined €460m for the search-related breach and €430m for the app store violation.Photograph: Steve Marcus/ReutersGoogleEU fines Google €890m for competition breaches over search and appsFirm told to treat third-party services that appear in its results in ‘fair and non-discriminatory manner’Dan MilmoandLisa O’CarrollThu 23 Jul 2026 06.00 EDTLast modified on Thu 23 Jul 2026 14.32 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleGoogle has been fined a total of €890m (£760m) by the EU for breaches of online competition laws by its search and app store services.TheEuropean Commission, the EU’s executive arm, said Google had broken the Digital Markets Act by giving priority to its own services, such as shopping and hotel deals, in search results over those of its rivals.It also infringed the DMA by preventing app developers from steering consumers towards cheaper offers, including for subscriptions, on websites or alternative app stores.Google has been fined €460m for the search-related breach and €430m for the app store violation. The commission has ordered the company to treat third-party services that appear in its search results in a “fair and non-discriminatory manner” and allow app developers to make offers outside Google’s app store.It noted that Google had already started testing changes to how it displayed search results featuring its own services. It said those changes represent “substantial progress towards compliance”.Consumers would be direct beneficiaries of the decision by the EU, a senior official said. “Research results will be in different inEurope. They will have to adapt their search engine going forward,” they said.Max von Thun, director of the Open Markets Institute Europe thinktank, said the fines were the “bare minimum” for a company that made revenues of just over $400bn last year.“Having finally established Google’s non-compliance, the commission must now move quickly to force Google to end its anti-competitive practices once and for all. Europe’s startups and innovators cannot wait much longer,” he said.The decision to impose the fine risks the ire of Donald Trump, only hours before a series of temporary global tariffs against about 60 countries expires.A senior official for the EU said they had no knowledge of how Trump was likely to react, insisting that the bloc had the “sovereign right” to regulate US tech companies in its own jurisdiction and that the timing of the fine was not connected to tariffs.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |Every weekdaySign up toBusiness TodayGet set for the working day – we'll point you to all the business news and analysis you need every morningPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionLast yearApple and Mark Zuckerberg’s Meta were finedunder the DMA. Apple was told to pay €500m for anti-competitive practices at its app store and Meta was told to pay €200m in a ruling on its ad-free“consent or pay”proposal for facebook and Instagram.Google can appeal against the decision and ask for interim measures, including a request to suspend the measure. The search company’s president of global affairs, Kent Walker, described the fine as “product degradation driven by a small group of self-serving complainants” that will have a negative impact on European businesses and consumers.He argued that the DMA forces Google “to strip away real-time search features Europeans love – like instant pricing and direct availability for hotels, flights, and restaurants – and dismantle safety protections on Google Play”.Explore more on these topicsGoogleEuropean UnionTechnology sectorEuropeEuropean CommissionnewsShareReuse this content

title: Carmakers lobbied UK to revoke ban on petrol and diesel cars after 2035  | Electric, hybrid and low-emission cars | The Guardian
description: BMW, Ford, Nissan and Toyota, plus parts maker Bosch, wrote joint letter to ministers in April
Author: Jasper Jolly
Headline: BMW, Ford, Nissan and Toyota, plus parts maker Bosch, wrote joint letter to ministers in April
Content: A Nissan Leaf electric vehicle is assembled. A government insider said the 2035 ban on petrol and diesel vehicles is not up for negotiation.Photograph: Bloomberg/GettyView image in fullscreenA Nissan Leaf electric vehicle is assembled. A government insider said the 2035 ban on petrol and diesel vehicles is not up for negotiation.Photograph: Bloomberg/GettyElectric, hybrid and low-emission carsCarmakers lobbied UK to revoke ban on petrol and diesel cars after 2035BMW, Ford, Nissan and Toyota, plus parts maker Bosch, wrote joint letter to ministers in AprilJasper JollyThu 23 Jul 2026 03.00 EDTLast modified on Thu 23 Jul 2026 21.30 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleMajor carmakers privately lobbied the UK government to revoke a ban on new petrol and diesel cars after 2035, according to documents that reveal for the first time the efforts to overturn a key decarbonisation policy.BMW, Ford, Nissan andToyota, plus the parts maker Bosch, wrote a joint letter to ministers in April calling for an “open technology approach” including petrol and diesel cars after 2035, when the government had said new cars must produce zero carbon emissions.The letter was obtained byThe Fast Charge newslettervia a freedom of information request and shared with the Guardian.The companies wrote that the government should allow “highly efficient ICE [internal combustion engines], hybrids, plug-in hybrids, range extenders and combustion engines when utilising green steel and sustainable fuels” beyond 2035. All those technologiesproduce carbon dioxide by burning fuel, as well as other harmful pollutants.The 2035 ban on sales of new petrol and diesel cars is a cornerstone policy in cutting the UK’s carbon emissions. The switch to electric vehicles is the single biggest contributor to cutting UK carbon pollution in the next decade, according to the government’sClimate Change Committee.A government spokesperson said the 2035 ban was not up for negotiation. Yet successive governments have weakened battery EV policies.Labour has already introduced “flexibilities” into electric vehicle targets that run up to 2030. The government is nowconsidering further changes to the rules, known as the zero emission vehicle (ZEV) mandate, after strong lobbying by carmakers.The suggestion of revoking the 2035 ban was heavily criticised by campaigners and Polestar, an EV brand.Matt Galvin, managing director of Polestar UK, said: “Reversing the transition to pure electric vehicles in the middle of a climate emergency would be a historic policy failure. The technology exists, consumers are embracing it and the economic case is becoming stronger every year. There is simply no justification for prolonging our dependence on petrol and diesel.“Rather than reopening the door to new petrol and diesel sales, policymakers should be accelerating the transition by removing the barriers that still prevent millions of drivers from switching.”The manufacturers have sway with the government because they employ 30,000 people in the UK. BMW owns the Mini factory in Oxford and Rolls-Royce in West Sussex,Nissanhas the largest UK car factory in Sunderland, and Ford has factories in Dagenham near London and Merseyside.The companies said in the letter that a “multipath strategy” should match the EU, whichweakened its own electric car targets in Decemberto enforce 90% electric car sales after 2035, down from 100% before. The letter requested a meeting with the then ministers for Wales, Northern Ireland and Scotland to discuss the mandate, suggesting that carmakers were trying to build up broad support for diluting the targets.The companies believed that the extra emissions could be offset by using lower-carbon steel and “e-fuels” – petrol made using green electricity. However, experts say e-fuels are not viable on a large scale because ofmassive energy wastage.Critics of European car industry lobbying say the continued sale of internal combustion engine cars could backfire, withfierce competition from Chinese electric carmakersin particular.Colin Walker, head of transport at the Energy and Climate Intelligence Unit, a thinktank, said carmakers battling against the transition “looks like a case of King Canute trying to hold back the tide”.“For the UK car industry a failure to make the transition quickly enough risks factories and communities being left behind, ultimately putting jobs in peril,” he said. “There is simply no technology that can rival electrification when it comes to reducing emissions and offering significantly lower running costs.”skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |Every weekdaySign up toBusiness TodayGet set for the working day – we'll point you to all the business news and analysis you need every morningPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionA Department for Transport spokesperson said: “We remain committed to phasing out all new non-zero-emission car and van sales by 2035.“The UK EV market is strong and growing, with sales up 35% on June last year, and we’re backing manufacturers through our £2bn electric car grant, which is boosting sales and helping more drivers into EVs.“We’ve committed to review the mandate by 2027.”BMW,Bosch, Nissan and Toyota all said the targets should be matched to customer demand, and that they were committed to decarbonisation.BMW said it has “concerns about the trajectory of the ZEV mandate as the sales requirements for 2026 and beyond do not reflect current levels of consumer demand”. A Bosch spokesperson said: “Supporting all technologies that can reduce CO2 emissions provides the best opportunity for the sector to achieve decarbonisation.”A Nissan spokesperson said it remains committed to a “fully electric future” but asked for a more gradual transition to battery sales.A Toyota spokesperson said the government should “remain open-minded” because demand did not match the mandate.
