She brought humanity to the second world war murder mystery The Night of the Generals, a British-French production shot in Paris. As the daughter of a Nazi officer suspected of sadistically killing a sex worker, her character has a relationship with another officer, played by Tom Courtenay.View image in fullscreenJoanna Pettet, right, in the The Best House in London, with Carol Friday and David Hemmings (1968).Photograph: Sipa/ShutterstockAfter Casino Royale, Pettet switched back to drama as the wife of a crime boss, played by Stanley Baker, who tries to dissuade him from carrying out a railway heist in Robbery, based on the real-life1963 Great Train Robberyfrom a Glasgow-to-London Royal Mail train.During this time, she had a year-long romance withVictor Lownes, who headed the Playboy empire in London, and planned to stay in Britain. However, the 1968 western Blue took her to the US, where she had started her career, to play a GP’s daughter nursing a wounded outlaw, acted byTerence Stamp.Pettet had a relationship with Stamp before marrying the American actorAlex Cordin 1968. Four months later, she gave birth to a son, Damien. In later years, she said that Stamp was the father and called her marriage a “sham”. She and Cord divorced in 1989.Blue was widely panned, as was her final British film, The Best House in London (1969), the writer Denis Norden’s parody of the Victorian sex industry, which featured Pettet as a feminist crusader rescuing “fallen women”.Leaving Britain and feature films behind, over the following two decades Pettet settled into guest roles on American television until she retired from the screen before turning 50.Born in Chelsea, London, she was the daughter of Cecily (nee Tremaine) and Harold Salmon, known as Sammy, who had been a wartime RAF pilot until being court-martialled for siphoning off service petrol for his car. He then became a civilian pilot with RAF Transport Command, taking planes from North American aircraft factories across the Atlantic.She met her father on only one occasion, when she was a few months old and he was on leave, before in 1943 he went missing, presumed dead, in a bomber en route from Newfoundland, Canada, to Reykjavik, Iceland. Her mother then emigrated to Canada to live in Montreal, Quebec, later marrying Charles Pettet.Joanna attended Westmount high school, then in 1960 appeared at McGill University in a musical titled Got It Made, co-written by her boyfriend, Allan Shiach, who under the name Allan Scott later scripted films such as Don’t Look Now and The Witches. She added her $60 earnings from performing in it to money from her mother’s savings to travel to New York at the age of 18 and study drama at the Neighborhood Playhouse School of the Theatre.View image in fullscreenJoanna Pettet in the TV thriller A Midsummer Nightmare (1975).Photograph: ITV/ShutterstockPettet acted in three Broadway plays, culminating in the Jean Kerr comedy Poor Richard (Helen Hayes theatre, 1964-65). Her performance as the secretary to an English poet in Greenwich Village, played byAlan Bates, won her a Theatre World award, and the New Yorker critic John McCarten described her as “the most radiant ingenue I’ve seen around in a long time”.Later, Pettet revealed that she and Bates had had an eight-month affair, occasionally revived over the years, until she nursed him in London before his death from cancer in 2003. Bates left her £95,000 in his will.She made her film debut in The Group (1966) as one of eight Ivy League college graduates setting out on life, withLarry Hagmanplaying her drunken playwright husband. Despite mixed reviews, the movie proved to be her breakthrough.Later, on her return to the US after leaving Britain, Pettet constantly popped up in popular television series such as Mannix (1971), McCloud (1973), Police Woman (1975), The Love Boat (1978-82), Knight Rider (1984) and Murder, She Wrote (1987), as well as a dozen TV movies.She also had a run in the soap opera Knots Landing in 1983 as a detective investigating the murder of Ciji Dunne (Lisa Hartman), but regretted that other long-running roles were not forthcoming. “I’d like a series,” she said. “I’d like to know that there is something I have to do every single day.”After her last screen appearance, in the 1991 film Terror in Paradise, she settled in Arizona. Her son died of a drug overdose in 1995.Joanna Jane Pettet, actor, born 16 November 1942; died 7 July 2026Explore more on these topicsCasino RoyaleTelevisionobituariesShareReuse this content

title: Raymond Briggs’s library goes on sale at his local Oxfam shop | Books | The Guardian
description: Books belonging to the beloved creator of The Snowman and When the Wind Blows were donated by his estate after his death
Author: Ella Creamer
Headline: Books belonging to the beloved creator of The Snowman and When the Wind Blows were donated by his estate after his death
Content: ‘A wonderful glimpse of the visual and intellectual world of one of Britain’s most distinctive illustrators’ … Raymond Briggs in 2008.Photograph: Anthony Devlin/PAView image in fullscreen‘A wonderful glimpse of the visual and intellectual world of one of Britain’s most distinctive illustrators’ … Raymond Briggs in 2008.Photograph: Anthony Devlin/PABooksRaymond Briggs’s library goes on sale at his local Oxfam shopBooks belonging to the beloved creator of The Snowman and When the Wind Blows were donated by his estate after his deathElla CreamerThu 23 Jul 2026 12.52 EDTLast modified on Thu 23 Jul 2026 15.23 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleThe book collection of the creator of The Snowman and Fungus the Bogeyman has gone on sale at Oxfam’s Hove bookshop in East Sussex andonline.More than 1,000 books from the collection of Raymond Briggs, the beloved British author and cartoonist, were donated by his estate after hisdeath aged 88 in 2022.Briggs’s personal library spans comics, illustration, novels and social history, including works by Peanuts creator Charles Schulz and Winnie-the-Pooh illustrator EH Shepard as well as a signed first edition of Graham Rawle’s Woman’s World, the 2005 book composed of fragments from women’s magazines.Other illustrators featured in Briggs’s library include Shirley Hughes, Aubrey Beardsley, Edward Gorey and Posy Simmonds. There are about 30 comic annuals from the 50s, offering “a glimpse into the visual world that shaped Briggs’s style”, according toOxfam.View image in fullscreenThe Raymond Briggs collection in Hove’s Oxfam bookshop.Photograph: Oxfam“What’s fascinating is the range,” said Oxfam Hove bookshop volunteer Martin Hill. “You begin to see connections between the books Briggs created and the books and artists he chose to have around him.”“There are great illustrators and cartoonists, but then you find books about ageing, bathrooms, domestic life, old annuals and wartime milkmen,” he added. “It is serious, funny, ordinary and sometimes rather dark – which feels very familiar to anyone who knows Briggs’s work.”The collection includes pamphlets on nuclear disarmament, a subject explored in Briggs’s 1982 graphic novel When the Wind Blows. A 1974 book by Monty Python’s Terry Jones and Michael Palin, Bert Fegg’s Nasty Book for Boys and Girls, is also listed, along with a set of John Constable’s reproduced sketches and a first edition copy of Riddley Walker, the post-apocalyptic novel by Russell Hoban.Born in Wimbledon in 1934, Briggs later spent more than 50 years living beneath the South Downs near Hove. The area inspired the 1978 picture book The Snowman, which features Brighton’s Royal Pavilion. Briggs taught illustration at Brighton School of Art from the early 60s to 1986.View image in fullscreenHe became known for picture books that subverted conventions of children’s storytelling, in the likes of 1977’s Fungus the Bogeyman and 1973’s Father Christmas, which imagines a cantankerous Santa Claus.“I liked to find fantastical creatures and treat them realistically,” said Briggs in an interviewwith the Guardianin 2019, on the publication of his illustrated memoir Time for Lights Out. “I don’t see any difference between doing children’s and adult books. My attitude is exactly the same. I just do the book as I want it to be.”The animated adaptation of The Snowman first hit screens in 1982, and has been broadcast almost every Christmas since.After Briggs’s death, children’s authorMichael Rosen saidthat “we have been lucky to have read and watched such great work from a great artist with a great heart”.The Oxfam collection “offers a wonderful glimpse of the visual and intellectual world of one of Britain’s most distinctive illustrators”, said Hill. Prices for the books on theonline storerange from £5.99 to £400 for the first edition of Rawle’s Woman’s World.Explore more on these topicsBooksRaymond BriggsChildren and teenagersOxfamnewsShareReuse this content

title: Children’s literature festival criticised after requests that comments on diversity be deleted | Books | The Guardian
description: John McLay, one of the founders of the festival in Bath, contacted writers who had noted a lack of diversity in the programme to request they remove comments posted to social media
Author: Guardian staff reporter
Headline: John McLay, one of the founders of the festival in Bath, contacted writers who had noted a lack of diversity in the programme to request they remove comments posted to social media
Content: Photograph: ViewStock/Getty Images/View Stock RFView image in fullscreenPhotograph: ViewStock/Getty Images/View Stock RFBooksChildren’s literature festival criticised after requests that comments on diversity be deletedJohn McLay, one of the founders of the festival in Bath, contacted writers who had noted a lack of diversity in the programme to request they remove comments posted to social mediaLiv FaceyThu 23 Jul 2026 08.58 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleBath children’s literature festivalis facing criticism after its founder emailed authors asking them to delete social media posts about the festival’s lack of diversity in its 2026 programme.Authors said that their comments criticising the festival for its lack of representation under a post on its Instagram page were deleted, with the original post later removed and comments limited on other posts.Several authors said they received emails from one of the founders, John McLay, on Monday evening asking them to delete their comments because they hadn’t “investigated the full line-up before hitting the keyboard”.John McLay, a children’s literary scout, founded the festival 19 years ago alongside his wife Gill, an agent at Bath Literary Agency.Gill McLay defended the decision to contact authors directly, saying the festival wanted a post with incorrect information, which claimed there were no writers of colour in the programme, to be removed.The email cited seven authors of colour in the programme, one of whom is UK-Cypriot, as evidence that the complaints were inaccurate. The programme features almost 100 events in total, and bills itself as the UK’s largest children’s literature festival.The festivalalso contacted one author’s agent to ask them to retract their comments.“Maybe it wasn’t the right way to respond to it, and we have since apologised for that,” said Gill McLay. “It certainly wasn’t our intent to silence anybody at all. It was purely to just try and ensure that it was the right information.”Children’s author Sophie Anderson, who received the email, told the Guardian: “To have someone so powerful in the industry sending these quite threatening and bullying emails is just horrible.”Alexandra Sheppard, an author whose video about the situation has amassed more than 20,000 views, said that “the fear of being blacklisted for critiquing powerful organisations feels very real”. She is now organising an open letter to be sent to the festival.In anInstagramstatement, the festival said it is “very proud of this year’s line-up”, but had “listened to the comments made”. While it “chose authors and illustrators from the selection put forward by publishers”, it said it will “work harder to ensure we have greater representation”, adding that it has “already brought new people into the management of the festival to help us with this”.Authors argue the issue reflects a broader pattern, with similar concerns raised about other events including the London children’s literature festival.“In 2026, we need to do better,” said author Iqbal Hussain. “We are not living in Regency Bath, nor 1950s London.”The row comes amid wider concerns about representation in children’s books. RecentDepartment for Education figuresshow that 38.7% of school-age pupils in England are from minority ethnic backgrounds, but theeighth Reflecting Realities survey, run by the Centre for Literacy in Primary Education,found that only 24% of books had main characters from racial minorities, down from 30% in 2022.“I’ve seen countless conversations, panels, articles and social media posts about how publishing can better reflect the young readers it serves,” said Sheppard. “If an organisation like BCLF still lacks meaningful inclusion, the reason is simple: they don’t want it.”Campaigners warn that limited representation can further alienate young readers who struggle to see their lives reflected in books.Gill McLay told the Guardian that the festival is working to improve, citing steps such as introducing a steering committee. “All we’re asking of people today is that they give us the opportunity to do it better,” she added.Explore more on these topicsBooksFestivalsSocial mediaDigital mediaChildren's books: 8-12 yearsChildren's books: 7 and underTeen booksnewsShareReuse this content

title: Ken Turner, the 100-year-old artist who flattened a Tesla with a tank: ‘I’m not a revolutionary, I’m a simple rebel’ | Art | The Guardian
description: The painter, sculptor and performance artist has devoted his life to making art available to the masses. Now with a new exhibition, the centenarian discusses inequality, inspiration, and why he won’t be stopping any time soon
Author: Eddy Frankel
Headline: The painter, sculptor and performance artist has devoted his life to making art available to the masses. Now with a new exhibition, the centenarian discusses inequality, inspiration, and why he won’t be stopping any time soon
Content: Drawn from life … Ken Turner.Photograph: Patrick ShanahanView image in fullscreenDrawn from life … Ken Turner.Photograph: Patrick ShanahanArtKen Turner, the 100-year-old artist who flattened a Tesla with a tank: ‘I’m not a revolutionary, I’m a simple rebel’The painter, sculptor and performance artist has devoted his life to making art available to the masses. Now with a new exhibition, the centenarian discusses inequality, inspiration, and why he won’t be stopping any time soonEddy FrankelThu 23 Jul 2026 03.00 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on Google‘Life is short, art long,” says Ken Turner, reading from a book of aphorisms by ancient Greek physician Hippocrates. And he’s half right, really, because at 100 years old – and very possibly the world’s oldest performance artist – art might have been long, but life hasn’t been all that short for him, either. And he has filled those years to the brim with radicalism, experimentalism and a singular dedication to pushing ideas as far as he can make them go.In the 1960s, Turner moved from painting towards a socially conscious form of public art, creating giant inflatable sculptures that visitors could inhabit and interact with decades before immersive art became a thing. He was taught by Fernand Léger, he was mates with John Berger, he made frames for Frank Auerbach and Leon Kossoff. Last year, he drove a tank over a Tesla in a performance meant as a damning indictment of corporate greed and rising fascism. His has been a life of rebellion and revolution, of obstinately following his own artistic path no matter what obstacles he encounters, no matter how much easier it would have been to walk down a more well-trodden one.View image in fullscreenCrushing it … A still from My Ride Over a Tesla.Photograph: Huw WahlHe’s written two books: Crashing Culture and the brilliantly named A Life Being Ken Turner. Those two titles sum him up perfectly; he smashes art conventions to bits, all while being uniquely, resolutely and utterly himself. And he’s still at it: still performing, still painting, still making. Ahead of an exhibition of new work at Wharfside Arts Hub in Penzance, he sits in his kitchen talking about his life and work, dressed in a blue work shirt and cap. A Michael Eavis-esque moustache-less beard makes him look every inch the Cornwall creative that he is. We’re joined by his son, the film-maker Huw Wahl, whose tender, grainy filmic portrait of his father plays on a loop in the show. “I think his painting is getting better and better, in a way, because he’s becoming more and more playful,” says Wahl. “But Ken’s always been childish, in the best sense.”“I was always interested in imaginative ideas, and the idea of play as an education in itself,” Turner says. “The more you go through education into university, the more you forget to play, and playing is incredibly important.”double quotation markThere’s no difference between performance and painting for me. I perform when I’m painting and I’m painting when I performTurner has a lot to say, a lot of stories to tell, as you’d expect after 80 years of art-making. Dates and names may escape him now, but the anecdotes tumble out in an endless stream. He struggles to talk about the emotions and reasoning behind most of the work, but perhaps even at 100 years old he’s still processing it all. His art isn’t a fait accompli. He doesn’t seem comfortable looking backwards when there’s so much still to look forward to.Turner was drafted into the mines at the outbreak of the second world war, and eventually into the Royal Engineers. He emerged desperate to make art, but deeply suspicious of the art establishment and turned down a place at London’s Slade School of Fine Art because he didn’t like the colour of the walls. “If they can’t get the colour of the walls right, how can they teach people?” he says. Later, he refused to do his final exam at Regent Street Polytechnic because they expected him to paint in an impressionist style, and turned down invites to show in the art galleries of the West End. “I didn’t like the dealers, I thought they smelled foul, they looked crooked, and they were monsters in their own right.” At every stage, Turner has pushed back against expectations: “I’m not a revolutionary, I’m a simple rebel, really.”View image in fullscreenBlank canvas … Turner in a still from My Ride Over a Tesla.Photograph: © Huw WahlInstead, Turner worked as a framer before taking a position as a teacher at Barnet College and eventually Central Saint Martins. In the late 1960s, an encounter with a public project by radical theatre director Joan Littlewood inspired him to form Action Space, an art collective hellbent on taking art out of the galleries and putting it where the public could actually reach it. They described themselves as “an association of artists working in the community, through education and play, developing art forms relevant to a wider public”. They created vast inflatable sculptures you could walk through and perform in, they used light, sound, movement and drama, they put on events for children. This was socially conscious, community-engaged art well ahead of its time.“Well, that’s the social conscience I have,” Turner says when asked about making art for a wider public. “I almost cry about the inequality that’s happening.” Action Space was about finding ways of connecting with people outside traditional, conservative art contexts. But rejecting the established gallery model to work in disadvantaged urban areas was also a continuation of his rebellious streak. “It was something intuitive, it was in my bones to reject institutional thinking.” Action Space still exists today, in another guise, as a charity working with learning-disabled artists, including Turner prize-winner Nnena Kalu.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |WeeklySign up toThe GuideGet our weekly pop culture email, free in your inbox every FridayPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotiondouble quotation markI still need to sell paintings, but I don’t want to go into the West End to sell them. I just intensely dislike criticsIt was also around this time that Turner discovered his love of performance art, a passion that peaked last year when he drove that 15-tonne tank over a Tesla in a project coordinated by guerrilla art provocateurs Led By Donkeys. “There’s no difference between performance and painting for me,” he states. “I perform when I’m painting, and I’m painting when I perform.” And age is no barrier to that – he’ll be performing at the opening of the new exhibition alongside his daughter.Turner moved to Cornwall in the 1990s and rediscovered his love of playing around with brushes and oils, rekindling his passion for painting. It’s still his main form of creative output, though that comes with its own pressures: “I’m still at that point where I need to sell paintings, but I don’t want to go into the West End to sell them. That’s the art industry. I just intensely dislike the critics, and you’re not an art critic, are you?”He looks disappointed at my response. That old suspicion of the art establishment rearing its head again. “Well, have a look at the paintings, and then decide who should buy them,” he says. So I do. They’re big, grandiose, semi-abstract things, often chaotic, sometimes violent, deeply emotional and overwrought. They deal with the destruction of Gaza, the impact of the climate emergency; they don’t make for easy viewing. “I think museums should buy them,” Turner says. “They instruct people, they’re about the sadness of the planet, the development of cruelty and inequality that’s happening.”He cares deeply about the world, and he genuinely thinks art can help change things for the better. Is it that belief in art’s power that pushes him forward? “Even though I’m 100, I’ll get to about 110 and I’ll still go on painting, because it’s signposted: ‘This is the way, this is where you should go.’ It’s a voice inside.”It’s also a necessity. “If I didn’t [make art], I’d die. It gives me life. If I don’t paint, I get sick, I really get sick in my mind, and that’s no good.” Even at 100, you get the feeling that as long as he’s got art in his life, Ken Turner could go on for ever.Ken Turner at 100: A Century of Crashing Cultureis at Wharfside Arts Hub, Penzance, to 4August.Explore more on these topicsArtExhibitionsfeaturesShareReuse this content

title: Before you throw out all the synthetic fabrics in your closet, read this | Well actually | The Guardian
description: Experts weigh in on wellness culture’s obsession with natural fibers and the growing trend of people shunning plastic-based clothing
Author: Guardian staff reporter
Headline: Experts weigh in on wellness culture’s obsession with natural fibers and the growing trend of people shunning plastic-based clothing
Content: Over the past few years, a heightened awareness of fabric content has spread among shoppers.Composite: Rita Liu/The Guardian/Getty ImagesView image in fullscreenOver the past few years, a heightened awareness of fabric content has spread among shoppers.Composite: Rita Liu/The Guardian/Getty ImagesWell actuallyBefore you throw out all the synthetic fabrics in your closet, read thisExperts weigh in on wellness culture’s obsession with natural fibers and the growing trend of people shunning plastic-based clothingKate CrayThu 23 Jul 2026 12.00 EDTLast modified on Thu 23 Jul 2026 13.03 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleA few months ago, Lane Koch made a decision: she would purge her family’s closets of all plastic-based fabrics and replace them with natural fibers, such as cotton and linen.Koch, a longtime Republican political strategist who was a senior staffer on Robert F Kennedy Jr’s presidential campaign, has long avoided processed foods and rid her kitchen of plastic cookware about a decade ago. But, for all that she cared about what she put in her body, she told me she had paid little attention to what she putonher body.Then she watchedThe Plastic Detox, a Netflix documentary following six couples experiencing infertility, who reduce the amount of plastic in their lives as they try to get pregnant. By the end of the film, three of the pairs have a baby.Koch isn’t trying to conceive, but the idea resonated. “If plastic exposure has that big of an effect on people’s fertility, then certainly it must be affecting our health as well in different areas,” she thought.That concern was on her mind when, a few days later, her three-year-old woke up sick – and happened to be wearing polyester. “I had this lightbulb moment,” Koch said, “that I need to get her out of these pyjamas.”Scientists told me that any dangers they’d expect from plastic-based clothing would accumulate over many years, not overnight. But Koch was convinced. She said she doubted any study had exactly reproduced the conditions her child experienced – and anyway, she questioned the incentives behind much scientific research.After that, she cleared 90% of her closet. Her kids had fewer synthetic items, but she tossed those too. She spent hundreds of dollars, mostly at secondhand stores, replacing their bedding with 100% cotton sheets and buying new socks, underwear and PJs. We “microdose poison in a thousand different respects every single day”, she told me. Now she was done with synthetic fabrics.How fabric content became an obsessionOver the past few years, a heightened awareness of fabric content has spread among shoppers. “When did you start caring about fabric content?” apopular Reddit postreads. “I can’t stop reading labels now.” Searches related to natural fibers began to spike on Googlein 2024and have risen since. Elena Karpova, a professor of consumer apparel-retail studies at the University of North Carolina Greensboro, told me that, in a recent unpublished study, one of her graduate students found that fabric content was a major factor influencing gen Z shoppers. Brands have picked up on the hype, and now mainstream stores like Gap and J Crew let online shoppers filter by material.For many, this concern is aesthetic. As fast fashion has proliferated and retailers have cut costs, clothing quality hasdeteriorated.Consumers havenoticed the declineand are searching for a way around it. Opting for natural fibers is one solution, though it’s not a cure-all. Clothing quality is defined by lots of factors, including construction. But construction is complicated to evaluate. Part of the appeal of “natural fibers only”, according to Hanna Lee, an assistant professor at the Wilson College of Textiles at North Carolina State University, is that it’s simple for anyone to check and easy for brands to advertise.As the hunger for natural fiber clothing has grown, it’s found a new group of supporters in people like Koch, whose aims aren’t so much sartorial as they are health-focused – even political. Now, wellness-obsessed men, concerned about their virility, are switching frompolyester to cotton underwear. The conservative women’s magazineEvie recommendedthat readers dress in “natural fiber staples” rather than synthetic fabrics, which, it claimed, “mess with your hormones”. The sentiment dovetails with the rise of Maha; a recent Maha report on children’s health even mentioned thedangers of microplastics in clothing.These fears have taken off on social media, wherehalf of adults under 50say they get their health information.CountlessTikTokersareassertingthatour clothesaremaking us sick– exactly the type of sensational content that algorithms thrive on. The phenomenon has become a microcosm of modern wellness culture: a complicated scientific issue flattened into exaggerated claims that erode trust and drive people to buy more stuff.The real health impacts of plastic-based clothingWorries about plastic-based clothing do have some basis. As many people know, plastic production is deleterious to our environment and health, said Phil Landrigan, a biology and public health professor at Boston College who co-authoredan international report on the topic. But the lion’s share of that harm doesn’t come from people wearing the clothes; plastic-caused sicknesses and deaths are primarily a mixture of workplace accidents and diseases resulting from emissions and pollution, he said.The potential dangers of wearing synthetic materials are less immediate and harder to quantify. Here, scientists are less concerned about plastic in the clothing than any chemicals used to treat it. Those chemicals, which can include phthalates, bisphenols, Pfas and brominated flame retardants, are toxic, and have been linked to health outcomes likecancer,heart attacks,infertilityandimmune suppression.The hidden plastics in our clothes – and how to avoid themRead moreBut a link isn’t the same as a cause, and for the chemicals to hurt us, our skin would have to absorb them. That’s possible, but it’s generally less risky than other modes of exposure. Sheela Sathyanarayana, a professor of pediatrics at Seattle children’s hospital who researches endocrine-disrupting chemicals in plastics, told me in an email that she was more worried about plastic in food and cookware, as well as personal care products, such as creams and perfumes.For some, the primary route of exposure to these chemicals might be a water source, said Audrey Gaskins, a professor at Emory University studying how the environment affects fertility. So much varies based on a person’s lifestyle and environment, Gaskins said, but “dermal absorption is probably not the average person’s primary exposure to these chemicals”.Certain circumstances can make skin more absorbent. Mohamed Abdallah, an environmental chemistry professor at the University of Birmingham, told me he and his colleaguesconducted a studythat found people were at greater risk when they were sweating. Still, he wasn’t able to put a number on the magnitude of that risk. He said quantifying the overall danger of wearing synthetics would be impossible, since many different factors affect how much the skin absorbs. “What’s the chemical we’re talking about?” Abdallah asked. “How much of it has been added to this fabric? How long has this piece of clothing been worn? What type of exercise has been going on, and for how long?”These factors build up over a lifetime and intermingle with other exposures. “It’s not a case of acute toxicity,” Abdallah said. No one will do one gym session in a pair of plastic leggings and “fall ill immediately”, he said, which makes it challenging to measure.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |WeeklySign up toWell ActuallyPractical advice, expert insights and answers to your questions about how to live a good lifePreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionThread carefully: your gym clothes could be leaching toxic chemicalsRead moreNone of the scientists I spoke to avoided synthetic fabrics entirely, but many said that they limited them. During our interview, Landrigan wore mostly natural fibers, but also a fleece vest, made from nylon flock; he believes that, given the harms of plastic production, there’s a strong case for avoiding synthetics. Abdallah told me that he’d embraced cotton in the gym, sweat spots and all; Sathyanarayana had also switched up her workout gear and underwear.While all agreed it didn’t hurt to wear natural fibers and that it was probably the lowest-risk route, no one seemed too concerned about the synthetics remaining in their closets.When I asked Sathyanarayana about claims that our clothes are making us sick and at the root of modern infertility, she was clear. “There is no evidence to support this right now,” she wrote. “There is evidence to support that these chemicals are harmful, but we are likely getting most of them from other sources.”Our growing distrust of the‘unnatural’Although the research comes with plenty of asterisks, Gaskins gets “why people have connected the dots”. Colleen Derkatch, a professor at Toronto Metropolitan University and the author of Why Wellness Sells, told me that, in her work studying wellness culture, one point has come up again and again: “People are really worried that everyday life is steeped in hazardous chemicals.”The advice to buy natural is compelling, because it acknowledges that fear and offers an answer. The solution it peddles also happens to be particularly powerful, Alan Levinovitz, a professor at James Madison University and the author of Natural, told me, drawing on a long history of worshipping the natural and rejecting the unnatural.Natural remedies might not be better – so why do we still prefer them?Read moreAt first glance, buying natural fibers and tossing out rayon and nylon may seem harmless – potentially even beneficial. And, on its own, it largely is (aside from the waste and any superfluous shopping that may follow).But the underlying motivations may signal something more serious. Believing your clothes are making you sick might reflect a larger belief that “the systems that are in place to protect us have failed”, Levinovitz explained. That fear “opens us up to all kinds of vulnerabilities”, he said – a possible gateway to public figures saying: “They tricked you about the clothes. Guess what else they’re tricking you about?”According to Derkatch and Levinovitz, wearing natural fibers can be a way to reassert agency in a confusing and often dangerous-feeling world. Alli Walker, a singer and songwriter based in Nashville, told me that for years, she struggled with conditions like irritable bowel syndrome, mast cell activation syndrome and polyendocrine metabolic ovarian syndrome (PMOS, formerly known as PCOS), and doctors never seemed able to address her symptoms. Then she visited a functional medicine doctor, who ordered blood tests, put her on supplements and recommended that she look into all the toxins she could have been exposed to.That edict put Walker’s health back in her own hands. After the appointment, Walker said she “spent day and night researching everything: what is the healthiest version of every single thing that I touch in my life?” She purged her closet of synthetics (minus the costumes she has to wear when she performs) and bought a few new natural fiber basics, though she limited the shopping.“Once you get into one side of this ‘non-toxic life’, there is no turning back,” she told me. “It’s almost like a hobby for me to get healthier and healthier.”‘Overconsumption isolates us’: how to start shopping lessRead moreShe doesn’t identify as a part of Maha – she said she hadn’t heard of it – and isn’t sure about the most intense claims about synthetics. Her reasoning for wearing natural fibers instead of plastics is simple: “If there’s another option, why not take it?” Down the line, she is hoping to replace her synthetic rugs with wool, and her vinyl flooring with wood.For Koch, the project of optimizing her and her family’s health is also ongoing. In the long term, she wants a house-wide water filtration system, but for now she’s planning on getting a snake plant for air purification. She values wellness so much, she said, in part because she had her youngest child in her late 30s and is nearly 20 years older than some of the other kids’ parents. She doesn’t want age to hold her back from being an active parent or to stop her daughter from having a rich childhood.When it comes to her health, Koch knows there’s a lot she can’t control: not the pesticides her neighbors use, nor pollution in the water supply; she’s also lost trust that any “three-letter agency” would ever keep her safe.“But I can control the things I put on my body,” she said. What she wears might not have a “life or death” impact, she admitted. But if it offers even a slim chance at greater health? For her, that’s “worth it”.Kate Cray is a freelance writer, reporter and editor based in Brooklyn, New York, who writes about how we live now, broadly defined.Explore more on these topicsWell actuallyPlasticsPfasfeaturesShareReuse this content

title: You be the judge: should my girlfriend stop insisting we queue for hours at concerts? | Life and style | The Guardian
description: Fay likes to get a good spot and watch the support acts, but Odysseus would rather rock up for the main band. Who is hitting a false note? You decide
Author: Georgina Lawton
Headline: Fay likes to get a good spot and watch the support acts, but Odysseus would rather rock up for the main band. Who is hitting a false note? You decide
Content: Illustration: Igor Bastidas/The GuardianView image in fullscreenIllustration: Igor Bastidas/The GuardianYou be the judgeLife and styleYou be the judge: should my girlfriend stop insisting we queue for hours at concerts?Fay likes to get a good spot and watch the support acts, but Odysseus would rather rock up for the main band. Who is hitting a false note? You decideFind out how to get a disagreement settled or become a jurorInterviews byGeorgina LawtonThu 23 Jul 2026 03.00 EDTLast modified on Thu 23 Jul 2026 10.48 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleThe prosecution: Odysseusdouble quotation markFay likes to be on the front row but I think it’s overratedFay and I love going to concerts – we probably go to five a year together. Fay likes to get there as early as possible, ideally before the venue opens so we can queue up. When the doors open, she wants to rush in and get the best spot, preferably in the front row.I just want to arrive for the main act. If there are three or four support acts, I don’t think it’s necessary to watch all of them. If we have to get there before the main act, I’d like to arrive right before the main support. That way we can enjoy the concert and still feel fresh, not having waited for hours beforehand. If we do that, we usually still get a good spot.I enjoy heavy metal concerts with smaller crowds, and no one gets there early as there’s no need. People push and mosh out. Usually, the mainstream bands, which I think are lower quality, have bigger audiences. We went to see Coldplay in Austria last year and we had to queue in the sun. Inside, we waited three hours for them to come on. By then I was exhausted.double quotation markFay is short, so she wants to get a good spot so she can see. But I’ve said she can go on my shouldersFay likes to be at the front, but I think it’s overrated. You can’t go and grab food or go to the toilet for fear of losing your spot. Plus, it’s hard to get in and out of the crowd.At a gig at Wembley, we didn’t go to the toilet because Fay was by the barrier at the front. She deliberately doesn’t drink water so she can watch the show without having to go, but getting dehydrated is dangerous.Fay is short and wants to get a good spot so she can see, but I’ve said she can go on my shoulders. She says getting to a concert late means we have to push through people to get a good position. We did this once and I managed to get us a space that was only 20 people from the front. Another time, we got in late and Fay still got to the front. Fay says she doesn’t like pushing past people and filling in the spaces of a crowd, but they are there to be filled.I’m happy to get to a gig slightly early, so that Fay can see, but some people queue from the morning. I definitely don’t want to queue for hours before it’s even started.You be the judge – send us your domestic disputesRead moreThe defence: Faydouble quotation markI enjoy the support acts. They are part of the experienceWhat Odysseus calls low-quality bands are popular bands. He says good-quality bands are the ones that no one knows, which are easier to get into and don’t have crowds, but I don’t agree. Coldplay are my favourite, but he also enjoys them.We have never been stuck in a crowd or unable to go to the toilet. We take turns to go to the toilet and get back to our spot easily enough.double quotation markOdysseus likes to arrive later and then try to squeeze past people, but it annoys themOdysseus is 1.88 metres tall (6ft 2in), while I’m only 1.59 metres (5ft 2in). He can see everything regardless of where he is in the crowd. I’m the one affected by not being able to see. Odysseus will offer to put me on his shoulders but I wouldn’t be comfortable sitting there for more than a few minutes, so that doesn’t help me if the show is three hours long. We both love music and concerts, so who cares if we are a bit inconvenienced by adding three hours of queueing to our day? We get rewarded by being on the first or second row and can see the whole act.Odysseus likes to arrive later and then try to squeeze past people, but it annoys them. He almost fought with a woman once to get me to the barrier. I appreciated it, but she’d probably queued for ages to get that spot. He will try to fix being late by pushing past people, but it makes me uncomfortable. He says: “It’s OK, it’s a concert, we can do that,” but it’s embarrassing. People need gaps to move freely and breathe.We went to see Ne-yo and Akon recently and paid £500 for VIP tickets, but it was super-crowded and I couldn’t see anything. I’d rather just buy normal tickets and get there early. As for being dehydrated, I always set alarms to drink water as I forget, even if I’m not at a concert, so it’s no different from how I am in my everyday life.I like everything about going to concerts. The waiting and warm-up acts are part of the experience, it’s not all about the main act. I want to keep arriving before the doors open. These events bring us so much joy what difference does a few more hours make if it means we secure a good spot?skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |WeeklySign up toInside SaturdayThe only way to get a look behind the scenes of the Saturday magazine. Sign up to get the inside story from our top writers as well as all the must-read articles and columns, delivered to your inbox every weekend.Preview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionThe jury of Guardian readersAt 6ft 2in, Odysseus doesn’t understand the pain of being too short to see properly at concerts. He should be more sympathetic to Fay and get there early.Sasha, 39The case for the prosecution crumbles with Fay’s claim that “Odysseus almost fought with a woman once to get me to the barrier … she’d probably queued for ages to get that spot.” Dude, that is totally against the spirit and etiquette of attending a live music performance.Ali, 42I sympathise with Odysseus, but if Fay can’t see and wants to watch the support acts, he needs to suck it up and be there for her. Who knows, he may learn to enjoy the full experience as Fay does.Sid, 63Fay needs to be at the front so she can see, but if she goes with Odysseus, it means fellow shorties behind him can’t see! Does Fay have other friends who could be her gig buddy sometimes? Pushing to the front, as a tall person, is not going to make you popular!Donna, 34With the price of concert tickets these days, they’re lucky they get to so many gigs. Maybe that’s why Fay is so keen to experience the full show? If Odysseus has no interest in the support, why doesn’t Fay head in earlier for the warm-up acts, then Odysseus can follow later for the main show and find her at the front.Clara, 33Now you be the judgeIn our online poll, tell us:is Fay making a poor show of things?The poll closes on Wednesday 29 July at 9am BSTLast week’s resultsWe asked ifTolu should stop buying so many flowers59%of you said yes –Tolu is guilty41%of you said no –Tolu is not guiltyExplore more on these topicsLife and styleYou be the judgefeaturesShareReuse this content

title: The best cleansers in the UK for every skin type and budget – tested | Skincare | The Guardian
description: They’re the foundation of a good routine, but which face wash does our beauty expert rate for oily, dry, or normal skin?
Author: Mollie Davies
Headline: They’re the foundation of a good routine, but which face wash does our beauty expert rate for oily, dry, or normal skin?
Content: View image in fullscreenThe FilterSkincareReviewThe best cleansers in the UK for every skin type and budget – testedThey’re the foundation of a good routine, but which face wash does our beauty expert rate for oily, dry, or normal skin?How to build the perfect skincare routineMollie DaviesThu 23 Jul 2026 05.41 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleThe Guardian’s journalism is independent. We will earn a commission if you buy something through an affiliate link.Learn more.Choosing a cleanser can feel like Goldilocks’ task of searching for the perfect bowl of porridge – finding one that’sjust rightis rarely straightforward. Add in that our skin is constantly changing, due to hormones, the seasons and environmental stressors, and it’s easy to see why finding the ideal formula can be challenging.The Guardian’s journalism is independent. We will earn a commission if you buy something through an affiliate link.Learn more.Cleansers are often overlooked, but the right one is the basis of agood skincare routine. To understand what we should be looking for, I spoke with Dr Ahmed El Muntasar, GP and founder ofthe Aesthetics Doctor, who explained that the ideal formula depends entirely on skin type. “If someone is acne-prone or oily, I would usually recommend something more gel-based, because it helps break through excess oil more effectively,” he says. “Ingredients such as salicylic acid and niacinamide can also be helpful for those skin types.”For very dry or sensitive skin types, he advises cream, milk or balm cleansers, with gentle formulations that are less likely to leave the skin feeling stripped. “The goal is always to cleanse properly without disrupting the skin barrier or causing irritation,” El Muntasar says.To bring you the best cleanser for every skin type, budget and concern, I spent six months putting the most popular cleansers through their paces. You’ll find my 12 favourites below.At a glanceBest overalland best budget cleanser:Cetaphil gentle exfoliating salicylic acid cleanser£9.94 for 236ml at AmazonBest cleanserif you can afford it:Sarah Chapman Skinesis Ultimate Cleanse£45 for 100ml at Sarah ChapmanBest cleanser for redness:Dr Hauschka cleansing cream£12.84 for 50ml at AmazonBest spa-like cleanser:Elemis Pro-Collagen hydrating cleansing mousse£48 for 125ml at ElemisBestcleanser for a clean feel:Chanel La Mousse Camellia cleanser£51 for 150ml at John LewisBest balancing cleanser:Rhode Pineapple Refresh PGA daily cleanser£30 for 150ml at SephoraWhy you should trust meAn interest in skincare has seen me reviewing beauty products for nearly a decade. Over the past few years, my own complexion has seen many changes, with my skin type seemingly fluctuating from one week to the next. During that time, I’ve tested more cleansers than I can count, tackling everything from redness and breakouts to dehydration and fine lines.How I testedView image in fullscreenLather up … every cleanser was assessed for how well it removed makeup, dirt and SPF without stripping the skin.Photograph: Mollie Davies/The GuardianI tested 25 cleansers over several months, using each one for at least a week, morning and evening, to see how well it removed makeup, daily grime andSPF. I paid close attention to how each product felt during use and immediately afterwards, and whether it lived up to any claims. To ensure fair comparison, the rest of my skincare routine remained consistent throughout testing, allowing any changes to be attributed to the cleanser alone. I also considered packaging, ingredients, texture and feel during application, and overall cost.The best face washes and cleansers in 2026View image in fullscreenPhotograph: Mollie Davies/The GuardianBest overall and best budget cleanser:Cetaphil gentle exfoliating salicylic acid cleanserCetaphilGentle exfoliating salicylic acid cleanser, 236mlfrom£9.94£14 at Lookfantastic£9.94 at AmazonWhat we loveAffordable and widely available; gentle yet effectiveWhat we don’t loveCould be too strong for dry or blemish-prone skinView image in fullscreenPhotograph: Mollie Davies/The Guardian£14 for 236ml at Lookfantastic£9.94 for 236ml at AmazonCetaphil offers hard-working skincare at a budget-friendly price. My hormonal skin is prone to bumps and excess oil, and this cleanser performed exceptionally well, leaving my skin feeling clean and balanced.Why we love itFormulated with a triple acid blend of salicylic acid, mandelic acid and gluconolactone, this foaming gel helps promote natural cell turnover, smoothing rough, bumpy skin. Despite the acids, I had no problems on my sometimes reactive skin.It can be used on the face and body, which made it ideal for using in the shower when I had breakouts on my arms and shoulders. It’s fragrance-free, not too drying, and asolid choice for a deep cleanse.It’s a shame that …there wasn’t a little more foam, as I enjoy the visual cue of a thorough cleanse, although in reality this isn’t an indicator of performance. While it’s suitable for most skin types, those with very dry, dehydrated or blemish-prone skin may find the formula a touch too strong for regular use.Type:gelMost suitable for:all skin types, particularly oily skin, sensitive skin and those with keratosis pilarisPrice per ml:from about 4pKey ingredients:salicylic acid, mandelic acid, niacinamide, vitamin B5 and gluconolactoneCertified cruelty-free?NoVegan?YesCetaphilGentle exfoliating salicylic acid cleanser, 236mlfrom£9.94£14 at Lookfantastic£9.94 at AmazonWhat we loveAffordable and widely available; gentle yet effectiveWhat we don’t loveCould be too strong for dry or blemish-prone skinBest cleanser if you can afford it:Sarah Chapman Skinesis Ultimate CleanseSarah ChapmanSkinesis Ultimate Cleanse, 100mlfrom£45£45 at Sarah Chapman£60 at AmazonWhat we loveWorks for any skin type; lasts a while; great to useWhat we don’t loveIt’s priceyView image in fullscreenPhotograph: Mollie Davies/The Guardian£45 for 100ml at Sarah Chapman£60 for 100ml at AmazonI’ve used Sarah Chapman’s Skinesis Ultimate Cleanse before, so when I set out to test the best cleansers on the market, it was an easy choice to revisit. Anyone who’s had a facial at the Skinesis clinic will recognise the indulgent and results-driven products.Why we love itThis balm is packed with omega fatty acids and anti-inflammatory ingredients such as baobab and borage oils, which worked wonders at soothing my flare-ups. It also contains a gentle dose of vitamin A to smooth texture and vitamin E with hydration-boosting properties, leaving skin with that classic Skinesis glow. A single pump was enough to cleanse my face and neck, melting away makeup and SPF in one fell swoop.It has a noticeably spa-like scent, strong but not overwhelming, and transforms from balm to a milky oil when mixed with water. With ageing concerns starting to show, particularly around my eyes, I also appreciated the inclusion of ingredients such as the peptide dermaxyl and Q10, which support collagen production and skin firmness. With consistent use, my skin looked fresher and more polished.This cleanser is a great option for sensitive skin, thanks to its relatively gentle actives, although those prone to congestion may prefer to alternate it with a lighter formula.It’s a shame that …it’s pricey, making it a less realistic option for everyday use. However, if your budget stretches to it, it’s an excellent all-rounder.Type:soufflé balm to oilMost suitable for:dry skinPrice per ml:from about 45pKey ingredients:vitamins A and E, dermaxyl, baobab and borage oilsCertified cruelty-free?NoVegan?NoSarah ChapmanSkinesis Ultimate Cleanse, 100mlfrom£45£45 at Sarah Chapman£60 at AmazonWhat we loveWorks for any skin type; lasts a while; great to useWhat we don’t loveIt’s priceyBest cleanser for redness:Dr Hauschka cleansing creamDr HauschkaCleansing cream, 50mlfrom£12.84£17 at Lookfantastic£12.84 at AmazonWhat we loveCalming and softening; can double as a mask; affordableWhat we don’t loveTexture takes getting used to; packaging isn’t greatView image in fullscreenPhotograph: Mollie Davies/The Guardian£17 for 50ml at Lookfantastic£12.84 for 50ml at AmazonWhile particularly suited to sensitive, redness-prone skin, Dr Hauschka’s Cleansing Cream is gentle enough for most complexions. After a week of use, my skin felt noticeably smoother, softer, and more hydrated.Why we love itThe cleanser is marketed as a cream, yet it has a thicker, more mask-like texture – which, once or twice, I left on for a few minutes to sink in before washing off. I found a pea-sized amount enough to thoroughly remove all traces of makeup, which is impressive for a cleanser with a non-oily texture.The fine sweet almond meal provides gentle exfoliation, helping to lift excess oil from around the T-zone with gentle, circular motions, while soothing botanical additions, including St John’s wort, chamomile and calendula, worked well to calm and nourish my skin. This cleanser is a reliable option even during times of heightened sensitivity or reactions.It’s a shame that …the packaging made it difficult to squeeze out the last remnants of product. You’ll have to use your muscles!Type:creamMost suitable for:sensitive skin and rednessPrice per ml:from about 26pKey ingredients:sweet almond meal, anthyllis, calendula and chamomileCertified cruelty-free?YesVegan?YesDr HauschkaCleansing cream, 50mlfrom£12.84£17 at Lookfantastic£12.84 at AmazonWhat we loveCalming and softening; can double as a mask; affordableWhat we don’t loveTexture takes getting used to; packaging isn’t greatBest spa-like cleanser:Elemis Pro-Collagen hydrating cleansing mousseElemisPro-Collagen hydrating cleansing mousse, 125mlfrom£48£48 at Elemis£48 at Space NKWhat we loveBeautiful texture; hydrating; lasts a long timeWhat we don’t loveThe fragrance isn’t for everyone, and it’s priceyView image in fullscreenPhotograph: Mollie Davies/The Guardian£48 for 125ml at Elemis£48 for 125ml at Space NKI’ve long been partial to an Elemis product, although I have found some of its fragranced cleansing balms a little too rich for my skin, occasionally leading to breakouts. So I opted to test a different cleanser from the range, and was pleasantly surprised.Why we love itThe mousse has a soufflé-like texture that feels rich on the skin, and while it carries that signature Elemis scent, it’s noticeably lighter and therefore more suitable for daily use. The brand claims it is “clinically proven to increase skin hydration by 125% after just one use”, and while I can’t quantify that, I did notice my skin felt better hydrated after a week of use, especially around my mouth and eyes.Formulated with a natural prebiotic derived from inulin, the cleanser helps support the skin barrier and lock in moisture. It left my skin feeling soft and comfortable, and was especially soothing on a dry patch on my cheeks triggered by a potent retinol. I was particularly careful not to go too close to my eyes, as the essential oils can irritate.A little goes a long way, and while it removes makeup and impurities well, I found it worked best when removed with a mitt or flannel.It’s a shame that …the signature blend of nine essential oils may feel a little overpowering for some, both in terms of fragrance and feel on the skin.Type:mousseMost suitable for:dry and dehydrated skinPrice per ml:about 38pKey ingredients:earth marine water, padina pavonica ferment, inulin and a blend of essential oilsCertified cruelty-free?NoVegan?NoElemisPro-Collagen hydrating cleansing mousse, 125mlfrom£48£48 at Elemis£48 at Space NKWhat we loveBeautiful texture; hydrating; lasts a long timeWhat we don’t loveThe fragrance isn’t for everyone, and it’s priceyBest cleanser for a clean feel:Chanel La Mousse Camellia cleanserChanelLa Mousse Camellia cleanser, 150mlfrom£51£51 at John Lewis£51 at SephoraWhat we loveProvides an exceptional clean; lathers wellWhat we don’t lovePricey, and can be a bit drying if overusedView image in fullscreenPhotograph: Mollie Davies/The Guardian£51 for 150ml at John Lewis£51 for 150ml at SephoraChanel’s La Mousse Camellia cleanser was a delightful surprise, and, unexpectedly, more affordable per ml than some other cleansers here. Results aside, I loved that it brought a touch of luxury to my bathroom shelf.Why we love itI chose this cleanser for its promise of an effective cleanse without drying out skin. The cream transforms into a rich foam on contact with water, and I could genuinely feel it working as I massaged it into my skin.The cleanser is infused with camellia ceramides to hydrate and preserve the skin’s barrier, and works a treat when it comes to removing daily grime.In fact, it performed impressively – effortlessly removing a full day of sweat, SPF and makeup. After use, my skin felt refreshed, clean and balanced.It’s a shame that …while it works incredibly hard to offer a deep and effective cleanse, it may prove too harsh for breakout- and irritation-prone skin.Type:foaming creamMost suitable for:dehydrated, dull and dry skin, and those with rednessPrice per ml:34pKey ingredients:camellia ceramides and camellia waterCertified cruelty-free?NoVegan?NoChanelLa Mousse Camellia cleanser, 150mlfrom£51£51 at John Lewis£51 at SephoraWhat we loveProvides an exceptional clean; lathers wellWhat we don’t lovePricey, and can be a bit drying if overusedBest balancing cleanser:Rhode Pineapple Refresh PGA daily cleanserRhodePineapple Refresh PGA daily cleanser, 150mlfrom£30£30 at Sephora£30 at RhodeWhat we loveLeft skin feeling clean and hydratedWhat we don’t loveSkin can break out when you’re first using itView image in fullscreenPhotograph: Mollie Davies/The Guardian£30 for 150ml at Sephora£30 for 150ml at RhodeI’m usually sceptical of celeb brands that launch a range of skincare, with claims that can seem overreaching, but Hailey Bieber’s Rhode Pineapple Refresh PGA daily cleanser is a standout example.Why we love itEnzyme cleansers often have a nibbling sensation that I find a little offputting, making me worry that my skin is reacting badly. However, despite containing pineapple enzyme, this gentle balm-to-lather formula felt more comforting than aggressive, with just a mild tingling.Its balmy texture, combined with plumping polyglutamic acid (PGA) and low-molecular-weight hyaluronic acid, left my skin feeling not just clean, but soft, hydrated and supple too.It’s a shame that …I did notice a few breakouts along my chin and jawline during the first few days of use, which may simply have been my skin adjusting to unfamiliar ingredients. That said, it may not be the best choice for acne- or breakout-prone skin.Type:balm to latherMost suitable for:most skin types, particularly dry skinPrice per ml:20pKey ingredients:pineapple enzyme, glycerine, saccharide isomerate and polyglutamic acidCertified cruelty-free?YesVegan?YesRhodePineapple Refresh PGA daily cleanser, 150mlfrom£30£30 at Sephora£30 at RhodeWhat we loveLeft skin feeling clean and hydratedWhat we don’t loveSkin can break out when you’re first using itThe best of the restBeauty Pie Super Healthy Skin hot oil double cleansing balmBeauty PieSuper Healthy Skin hot oil double cleansing balm, 100mlfrom£34£34 at Beauty Pie£34 at LibertyWhat we loveLess overpowering than competitors; warms up in useWhat we don’t loveGloopy texture; pricey without subscriptionView image in fullscreenPhotograph: Mollie Davies/The Guardian£34 for 100ml at Beauty Pie£34 for 100ml at LibertyBest for:an oil cleanserThis Beauty Pie Super Healthy Skin balm has a pleasant, subtle scent and a beautifully smooth texture that glides on to the skin without feeling sticky. Warming a grape-sized amount between the palms before massaging into the face, then removing with a warm cleansing cloth, made for a satisfying, spa-like ritual.It left my skin feeling thoroughly clean and soft, with a noticeable reduction in the appearance of pores, particularly around my chin, and I enjoyed the pleasant warming sensation as I massaged it in.It didn’t make the final cut because …you have to sign up to be a Beauty Pie member to get it for £18, otherwise it’s £34. Plus, it’s a little gloopy (not sticky, but gloopy), and can be harder to break down than other balm cleansers.Type:balm;most suitable for:dry skin and dullness;price per ml: 34p;key ingredients:aloe juice, niacin-rich rice bran oil and baobab sariché oil;certified cruelty-free?Yes;vegan?NoBeauty PieSuper Healthy Skin hot oil double cleansing balm, 100mlfrom£34£34 at Beauty Pie£34 at LibertyWhat we loveLess overpowering than competitors; warms up in useWhat we don’t loveGloopy texture; pricey without subscriptionCharlotte Tilbury Magic Revival cleanserCharlotte TilburyMagic Revival cleanser, 120mlfrom£28£28 at John Lewis£28 at Space NKWhat we loveHelps to restore skin barrier; isn’t strippingWhat we don’t loveCan be a bit irritating where skin is thinView image in fullscreenPhotograph: Mollie Davies/The Guardian£28 for 120ml at John Lewis£28 for 120ml at Space NKBest for:abrightening cleanserSuitable for most skin types (but can be a bit harsh on reactive skin due to citric acid and fragrance), Charlotte Tilbury’s Magic Revival cleanser is best used with warm water and removed with a cloth. After cleansing twice, I saw it remove a full face of makeup, leaving my skin feeling super clean but not stripped of moisture.It’s described as a priming cleanser, and is designed to support clearer, smoother and more radiant-looking skin. The skin-lipid-barrier-supporting ingredients of shea butter, rosehip oil and camellia oil ensured that it felt nourishing on my skin, leaving a healthy, natural glow.It didn’t make the final cut because …it made my skin feel nice after use, but I did experience some irritation around the eye area at the beginning.Type:cream to foam to milk;most suitable for:most skin types apart from reactive skin;price per ml: about 23p;key ingredients:hyaluronic acid and peptide complex;certified cruelty-free?No;vegan?NoCharlotte TilburyMagic Revival cleanser, 120mlfrom£28£28 at John Lewis£28 at Space NKWhat we loveHelps to restore skin barrier; isn’t strippingWhat we don’t loveCan be a bit irritating where skin is thinByoma Foaming Rice deep cleanserByomaFoaming Rice deep cleanser, 175mlfrom£13.86£13.99 at Cult Beauty£13.86 at AmazonWhat we loveGreat for sensitive skin; easy to removeWhat we don’t lovePump is prone to breaking; scent is strangeView image in fullscreenPhotograph: Mollie Davies/The Guardian£13.99 for 175ml at Cult Beauty£13.86 for 175ml at AmazonBest for:a deep cleanse that won’t irritateI love Byoma’s skincare, particularly for the affordable price point and effectiveness. Each use of the Foaming Rice cleanser left my skin feeling clean, hydrated and comfortable, and after a week of use my complexion looked clearer with no breakouts.It worked particularly well on my sensitive skin, with no stinging or burning, even around the eye area, and it was easily removed with just water – no flannel needed. Although it’s worth noting that it could maybe irritate some very sensitive or acne-prone skin, as it contains myristic acid, lauric acid and potassium hydroxide. Overall, though, this is a strong performer for a great price.It didn’t make the final cut because …I wasn’t a fan of its smell, which is likely down to its natural, fragrance-free formulation. However, despite its slightly earthy, fermented notes, it isn’t overpowering and will become familiar with continued use. Also, the pump broke during testing.Type:cream to foam;most suitable for:most skin types except very sensitive or acne-prone skin;price per ml: from about 8p;key ingredients:tri-ceramides, rice extract and marine algae;certified cruelty-free?Yes;vegan?YesByomaFoaming Rice deep cleanser, 175mlfrom£13.86£13.99 at Cult Beauty£13.86 at AmazonWhat we loveGreat for sensitive skin; easy to removeWhat we don’t lovePump is prone to breaking; scent is strangeJan Marini Bioglycolic face cleanserJan MariniBioglycolic face cleanser, 237mlfrom£25.79£25.79 at Face the Future£25.79 at CosmetifyWhat we loveGood for those wanting to resurface skin; texture is lovelyWhat we don’t loveDoesn’t remove makeup that well; stingsView image in fullscreenPhotograph: Mollie Davies/The Guardian£25.79 for 237ml at Face the Future£25.79 for 237ml at CosmetifyBest for:a resurfacing cleanserThis cleanser has a lovely, creamy gel texture that glides on to the skin. The scent isn’t strong, although I did find it slightly reminiscent of baby skincare products. After use, my skin felt a little tight, but also smooth, fresh and clean. It also removed a small amount of makeup that was still left from the night before, although I didn’t find it as good at removing a full face of makeup. My skin didn’t seem to need as much of this one as other cleansers of this consistency, so a small amount goes further. It does a great job of washing away impurities and softening bumps.It didn’t make the final cut because …I did experience a little stinging in certain areas while massaging it into the skin, especially where I had spots or existing redness. It wouldn’t be ideal as a first cleanse, as it wasn’t the best at removing makeup.Type:cream;most suitable for:resilient skin;price per ml: about 11p;key ingredients:sorbitol and glycolic acid;certified cruelty-free?No;vegan?YesJan MariniBioglycolic face cleanser, 237mlfrom£25.79£25.79 at Face the Future£25.79 at CosmetifyWhat we loveGood for those wanting to resurface skin; texture is lovelyWhat we don’t loveDoesn’t remove makeup that well; stingsAllies of Skin Molecular Silk Amino hydrating cleanserAllies of SkinMolecular Silk Amino hydrating cleanser, 100mlfrom£38£38 at Space NK£38 at Cult BeautyWhat we loveThe cooling texture is great on sunburnt or flushed skinWhat we don’t loveTakes perseverance and can be irritatingView image in fullscreenPhotograph: Mollie Davies/The Guardian£38 for 100ml at Space NK£38 for 100ml at Cult BeautyBest for:a nourishing cleanserI enjoyed using the Allies of Skin Molecular Silk Amino hydrating cleanser each morning and evening: it applies beautifully with a cooling gel texture that transforms into a light foam on contact. It felt refreshing from initial use and gave an immediate sense of clean without stripping.Over time, I noticed my skin looked brighter, clearer, and more even. The scent is pleasant and subtle, which added to the overall experience. While I did initially experience a few breakouts, they quickly cleared, and my skin was left noticeably more balanced.It didn’t make the final cut because …it requires a “breaking in” period. It can trigger short breakouts for some skin types, but I found that once the initial adjustment passed, my skin looked clear and brighter.Type:gel to foam;most suitable for:dry skin;price per ml: 38p;key ingredients:hyaluronic acid, organic safflower and moringa oil, and ethylated L-ascorbic acid;certified cruelty-free?Yes;vegan?NoAllies of SkinMolecular Silk Amino hydrating cleanser, 100mlfrom£38£38 at Space NK£38 at Cult BeautyWhat we loveThe cooling texture is great on sunburnt or flushed skinWhat we don’t loveTakes perseverance and can be irritatingDialogue Leo Radiance cleansing balm and maskDialogueLeo Radiance cleansing balm & mask, 90ml£48£48 at DialogueWhat we loveContains the sweet spot of vitamin C; makes cleansing funWhat we don’t loveCan only buy direct; pricey; needs to be kept uprightView image in fullscreen£48 for 90ml at DialogueBest for:a brightening, cleansing maskDialogue launched in 2026, and its concept immediately caught my attention. Each product takes its name from a zodiac sign, and comes with a corresponding affirmation and a five- to seven-minute meditation, available via YouTube and Spotify to listen to while cleansing. I’m not sure whether the stars aligned in terms of astrology (although I am a Leo), but I appreciated the reminder to make my self-care routine a bit more of a ritual-like experience.The cleanser itself is gentle yet effective, making light work of removing mascara and SPF without leaving any residue. Containing astaxanthin, a potent antioxidant known for being more stable than vitamin C, it helps support the skin barrier and protect against environmental stressors.It didn’t make the final cut because …it sits at the pricier end of the scale, which may put off those wanting to try before committing fully. Mini versions would be a welcome addition (and great for travel). That said, if you’re able to stretch to it, it won’t disappoint.Type:balm;most suitable for:all skin types, especially those needing a deep cleanse;price per ml: about 53p;key ingredients:astaxanthin, safflower, cherry kernel, passion fruit and apricot kernel oils and milk thistle seed esters;certified cruelty-free?No;vegan?YesDialogueLeo Radiance cleansing balm & mask, 90ml£48£48 at DialogueWhat we loveContains the sweet spot of vitamin C; makes cleansing funWhat we don’t loveCan only buy direct; pricey; needs to be kept uprightWhat you need to knowView image in fullscreenWash and glow … cleansing twice a day suits most people, but the right routine depends on your skin type.Photograph: PeopleImages/Getty/iStockphotoHow often do I need to use cleanser?“For most people, cleansing twice a day is usually appropriate, once in the morning and once in the evening. That said, it does depend on your skin type,” says aesthetics doctor El Muntasar.He suggests that those with naturally very dry or sensitive skin could skip the morning cleanse and simply rinse with water, as this helps preserve some of the skin’s natural oils. However, if you have very oily skin, exercise frequently, or wear heavy SPF and makeup daily, then you may benefit from a more thorough cleanse.What is the correct way to use a cleanser?El Muntasar recommends “starting with lukewarm water rather than hot water, because very hot water can damage the skin barrier and increase dryness or sensitivity.“Massage the cleanser into damp skin for about 30 to 60 seconds, paying particular attention to areas that tend to be oilier or more congested, such as around the nose, chin or beard area if someone has facial hair. After cleansing, the skin should feel clean and comfortable, not tight or squeaky. That overly stripped feeling is usually a sign the cleanser is too harsh for your skin.”Do I need to double cleanse?According to El Muntasar, double cleansing can be helpful, particularly for people who wear makeup daily, use a lot of SPF or have oilier skin.“The first cleanse is designed to break down oil-based products such as makeup, sunscreen, excess sebum and environmental debris,” he says. “That is where balm cleansers, oil cleansers or micellar waters can work well. The second cleanse is then used to properly clean the skin itself using a gentler water-based cleanser. Not everybody needs to double cleanse, but for many people, especially in the evening, it can make a noticeable difference to how effectively the skin is being cleansed.”What should I be careful about?“One of the biggest red flags is a cleanser that leaves the skin feeling overly tight or ‘squeaky clean’, because that often means the skin barrier is being stripped,” says El Muntasar. “I would also be cautious of cleansers with a lot of fragrance, essential oils or abrasive exfoliating particles, particularly for sensitive skin types. If your skin becomes very red, irritated or uncomfortable after cleansing, that’s usually a sign the product is too harsh or not suitable for your skin.”For more:The best eye creams for banishing bags, puffiness and fine linesThe best LED face masks, testedThe best vitamin C serums for every budgetMollie Davies is a beauty, fashion and lifestyle journalist, with nearly a decade’sexperience in testing everything from the most flattering denim for long legs, to lipsticks that stay in place after several glasses of wine. When she’s not testing the best cleansers or perfecting a French manicure, you’ll find her in a bubble bath, listening to a historypodcast, or cuddling her basset houndExplore more on these topicsSkincareThe FilterBeautyfeaturesShareReuse this content

title: Cool your boots: icy cuppa substitutes for summer | Food | The Guardian
description: Iced tea doesn’t have to be the super sweet, sugar-packed mainstream brands – there are plenty of offerings ready to convert even the staunchest tea purist
Author: Guardian staff reporter
Headline: Iced tea doesn’t have to be the super sweet, sugar-packed mainstream brands – there are plenty of offerings ready to convert even the staunchest tea purist
Content: Legend has it that English tea merchant Richard Blechynden was responsible for popularising iced tea in the American south.Photograph: creacart/Getty ImagesView image in fullscreenLegend has it that English tea merchant Richard Blechynden was responsible for popularising iced tea in the American south.Photograph: creacart/Getty ImagesFoodCool your boots: icy cuppa substitutes for summerIced tea doesn’t have to be the super sweet, sugar-packed mainstream brands – there are plenty of offerings ready to convert even the staunchest tea puristMina HollandThu 23 Jul 2026 08.00 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleThe Guardian’s journalism is independent. We will earn a commission if you buy something through an affiliate link.Learn more.As a very prescriptive tea drinker – piping hot“Stephen Fry” everyday Twinings, thank you, brewed strong with a dash of milk and no sugar under any circumstances– iced tea has always been lost on me. But there’s nothing like an unprecedented heatwave to encourage this sleepless mother to review her position on something as vital as tea.The Guardian’s journalism is independent. We will earn a commission if you buy something through an affiliate link.Learn more.Many of us associate iced tea with the American south, but legend has it that it was actually an English tea merchant who popularised it there. Richard Blechynden met countless visitors who were unwilling to drink hot tea in the summer heat of 1904’sLouisiana Purchase Exposition, so instead he served it over ice. With the American penchant for iced drinks being already well-documented (Mark Twainwrote that if there was one thing that could be called “American”, it was “the national devotion to ice-water”), it took off.Iced tea has evolved in myriad ways, but the southern style, for which black tea is heavily sweetened and often flavoured before serving on ice, reigns supreme in the mainstream. Until recently, I had tried onlyLipton’s iterations, which tick precisely zero boxes for me, mostly because they use only the faintest trace of actual tea (0.12% of black tea extract, to be precise), combined with fruit concentrate (peach, lemon) and abundant sugars and sweeteners. IMHO, something as sweet as that is refreshing only if it’s fizzy.I realised during my testing, however, that I could get on board with the still, sweet iced teas that dominate the market if they had a bit more, well,teaabout them. As well as sugar and peach concentrate, Harry Brompton’s Peach IcedTea(see today’s pick) contains 96% brewed Kenyan black tea, and it really delivers: I can really feel the grip of the tannins. The ChariTea range, also in my pick, uses a similarly high proportion of tea, as well as agave syrup in place of refined sugar and organic fruit juices for flavourings – the Red one, made with rooibos, passionfruit and elderberry juice, is everything I want on a hot day.The handful of sparkling teas I tried are pushing the definition of iced tea a little.Twining’s Refreshdescribes itself as a “juicy raspberry lemonade with hibiscus” (unsurprisingly, I couldn’t taste the 1% white tea infusion), andHolos’ organic Jasmine Hopsnumber is really more a kombucha.Anyone who is overheated and in search of a cuppa substitute, in fact, might best be advised to make their own iced tea, perhaps using Brew Tea Co’s kit in today’s pick, which I brewed hot and served over ice with no milk (obvs) and a little dash of maple syrup. Don’t tell me a girl can’t change.Four iced teas for a hot summer’s dayM&S Peach Iced Tea No Added Sugar£2.10 (750ml) Ocado. White tea infused with real peach juice – still too sweet for my palate but, unlikeSnappleand Lipton’s, it has no added sugars or sweeteners, so take the win.ChariTea Red Iced Tea£1.95 (330ml) Waitrose. An original and moreish blend of South African rooibos and organic passionfruit; a percentage of proceeds goes to social projects in the tea-growing region.Harry Brompton’s Peach Iced Tea£1.25 (500ml; on offer) Sainsbury’s. Satisfyingly tannic and very refreshing. Serve with lemon wedges and a good fan.Brew Tea Co Peach & Orange Iced Tea£12.50. Ceylon tea meets dried peach and citrus rind in a giant bag designed for home infusions, so great for determining strength and sweetness yourself.Explore more on these topicsFoodTeaAmerican food and drinkfeaturesShareReuse this content

title: Rachel Roddy’s ratatouille and potato gratin recipe | Stew | The Guardian
description: The classic Provencal stew, but with potato for ballast and topped with a cheesy herb crumb
Author: Rachel Roddy
Headline: The classic Provencal stew, but with potato for ballast and topped with a cheesy herb crumb
Content: Rachel Roddy’s ratatouille potato gratin.Photograph: Rachel Roddy/The GuardianView image in fullscreenRachel Roddy’s ratatouille potato gratin.Photograph: Rachel Roddy/The GuardianA kitchen in RomeStewRachel Roddy’s ratatouille and potato gratin recipeThe classic Provencal stew, but with potato for ballast and topped with a cheesy herb crumbRachel RoddyThu 23 Jul 2026 01.00 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleWe swapped the heatwave and melting pavements in Rome for a similar situation in London, which meant catching a train to Dorset felt something of a relief. Not that it wasn’t hot there too, but it was offset by the sea, a garden and a dark, cool house. The weather also meant that the always good July produce was superb and abundant: all the berries, cherries, peas, runner and french beans; but also potatoes, aubergines, courgettes, tomatoes and herbs – all of which go into this week’s recipe.My template for ratatouille is a mix of Jane Grigson’s recipe in herVegetable Bookand Robert Carrier’s version in Great Dishes of the World, plus the advice from a French friend to cook the aubergine separately, either by deep-frying (by far the tastiest option), baking, or air frying. The benefit of this step is not just flavour; it ensures the aubergine holds its shape better, meaning distinct chunks in the final stew.What I like about Grigson’s recipe is the initial lively simmer (with a lid on), which kickstarts the cooking, followed by an uncovered simmer, then a final livelier simmer so any wateriness disappears. You will have a good idea of how sweet or acidic the tomatoes are from tasting them raw, but taste again towards the end of cooking and, if necessary, add a pinch of sugar or jot of red-wine vinegar.Ratatouille is a Provençal word, which, according to the digital dictionary Orthodidacte, is linked to two terms:tatouiller,which means “to handle repeatedly” or “grope,” as well as “to stir in water’; andratouiller, which means ‘to stir in water,” “mix for a long time,” or “stir up the mud at the bottom of the water”.
