I am at a loss as to how “grope” can be applied here, but the rest of the terms can be applied in some way to the making of ratatouille, especially the stirring of the mud at the bottom of the water, which is a reminder to swirl the spoon deeply so the vegetables don’t clump and stick to the bottom of the pan.Potatoes are delicious when simmered along with tomatoes until soft, but their addition does turn it into another dish, which is why I have called this ratatouille and potato gratin – the gratin being a topping of soft breadcrumbs, grated cheese, minced herbs and olive oil, which, under the grill, turns into a crusty top.If you are eating straight away, let the dish sit for about 10 minutes, so the juices that have run free are reabsorbed, before serving with sausages, grilled cheese, slices of roast chicken or simply a green salad. While the top will lose some of its crusty edge, the dish is just as good and even more velvety at room temperature.Ratatouille and potato gratinServes41 large aubergine,cut into 2cm chunksOlive oilSalt1 large onion,peeled and sliced1garlic clove,peeled2 courgettes, cut into chunks1 red or yellow pepper,cut into chunks1all purpose large potato,peeled and cut into chunks3big tomatoes,peeled, or 1 x 400g tin tomatoesSugar and red-winevinegar(optional)For the topping100g of soft breadcrumbs2 tbspgrated parmesan2 tbspminced herbs(parsley, thyme, marjoram, say)Either deep-fry the aubergine chunks until golden, or spread them on a baking tray, rub them with oil and salt and bake for 25 minutes, until soft and golden, or use an air fryer. Set aside.In a heavy-based pan, warm four to six tablespoons of olive oil and the onion and garlic. After about five minutes, add the courgettes, peppers and a pinch of salt, and stir. Five minutes later, add the potato and tomato, cover and simmer for 20 minutes.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |WeeklySign up toFeastRecipes from all our star cooks, seasonal eating ideas and restaurant reviews. Get our best food writing every weekPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionCheck the amount of liquid and add a little water so that the vegetables are surrounded, then reduce to a simmer for 40-45 minutes, stirring every now and then.Ten minutes before the end of cooking, raise the heat a little so wateriness bubbles away for a dense stew. Then, in the last minutes of cooking, stir in the aubergine and taste for salt, adding a jot of sugar or red-wine vinegar, if you wish. Leave to cool a little.Tip the stew into a roughly 30cm x 20cm rectangular dish. Combine the topping ingredients with some olive oil, then spread the crumbs over the top of the ratatouille and put under the grill for a few minutes, until the top is crusty.Explore more on these topicsStewA kitchen in RomeItalian food and drinkFoodFrench food and drinkVegetablesrecipesShareReuse this content

title: Risk of Parkinson’s disease may increase with prolonged exposure to road traffic noise | Parkinson's disease | The Guardian
description: Pioneering study linking noise pollution to Parkinson’s finds 3% increase in risk for every 11.5dB rise in noise levels
Author: Guardian staff reporter
Headline: Pioneering study linking noise pollution to Parkinson’s finds 3% increase in risk for every 11.5dB rise in noise levels
Content: Research suggests a quiet or less exposed part of the home could mitigate the association between road traffic noise and risk of Parkinson’s disease.Photograph: georgeclerk/Getty ImagesView image in fullscreenResearch suggests a quiet or less exposed part of the home could mitigate the association between road traffic noise and risk of Parkinson’s disease.Photograph: georgeclerk/Getty ImagesParkinson's diseaseRisk of Parkinson’s disease may increase with prolonged exposure to road traffic noisePioneering study linking noise pollution to Parkinson’s finds 3% increase in risk for every 11.5dB rise in noise levelsUshika KiddThu 23 Jul 2026 09.24 EDTFirst published on Thu 23 Jul 2026 08.56 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleLong-term exposure to road traffic noise is associated with a higher risk of Parkinson’s disease, the biggest study of its kind has found.Parkinson’s disease is a progressive disorder in which parts of the brain become damaged over time, affecting movement and balance.Existing research has found potential biological pathways that link noise exposure to Parkinson’s disease, largely due to stress responses and disrupted sleep. The researchers modelled noise exposure at the most and least exposed exterior of the residence of each participant and calculated the difference in noise levels.AI-enhanced blood test may detect Parkinson’s years before onsetRead moreThe magnitude of the effect was modest but consistent; at the most exposed facade, for every 11.5dB rise in noise level, the risk of Parkinson’s disease rose by 3% over the study period.Having a quiet part of the home may mitigate the association between exposure to road traffic noise and higher risk of Parkinson’s disease, according to the findings.Thestudy, published in Jama Neurology, included 3.1 million Danish participants aged 40 and over, and followed them for 18 years. It was established using nationwide health register data, making it the largest study on road traffic noise and Parkinson’s disease.Previous researchlinked the rise in neurological disorders, including Parkinson’s disease, with exposure to environmental toxins. Environmental risk factors such as air pollution, microplastics and pesticides have become the main focus of prevention strategies.The study is one of the first to make the link to noise pollution. Chronic noise exposure exceeding World Health Organization thresholds affects nearly one-third of Europeans. Thomas Münzel, one of the authors of the study, said environmental noise is “still a young entrant in the Parkinson’s disease risk-factor literature”.Münzel said the study’s findings should move noise from a speculative to a credible risk factor for Parkinson’s disease, prompting further research into the topic.View image in fullscreenA traffic jam passing through a residential area.Photograph: Graeme Robertson/The GuardianThe researchers took air pollution into account but lacked information on people’s lifestyles. They argue, however, that lifestyle factors would not have changed the findings, because they saw a consistent association with Parkinson’s regardless of people’s education and income.Chronically disturbed sleep from noise pollution is thought to cause increased levels of the stress hormone cortisol. Anna Hansell, a professor of environmental epidemiology at the University of Leicester, who was not involved in the study, said noise was “more likely to be a general stressor” rather than a specific environmental risk factor for Parkinson’s disease, and the nature of the study design “doesn’t ‘prove’ causation”.Dr Peter Chan, a senior researcher in environmental epidemiology at Nuffield Department of PopulationHealth, who was not involved in the study, said: “The study of [difference in noise levels] is interesting as it highlights a dimension of noise issue that noise at home is not uniform and people can mitigate by moving to different rooms.” However, he added the findings were not unexpected.He said: “We know that many potential risk factors of Parkinson’s disease are also linked to low socioeconomic status, and it is very hard to disentangle whether the increased risk with noise is totally due to noise itself or something else.”John Stewart, the chair of the UK Noise Association who was not involved in the study, said: “This research joins a growing list of studies which show a connection between traffic noise and people’s physical and mental health. It shows the link is strongest where the noise is loudest. In practice, this means main roads.“That suggests governments and local authorities should focus their efforts on cutting main road noise.”Explore more on these topicsParkinson's diseaseHealthMental healthResearchMedical researchDenmarkRoad transportnewsShareReuse this content

title: ‘Why do I feel crappy?’: women are learning about their luteal phases - and Maha is weaponizing it | Women's health | The Guardian
description: People have found language for moodiest part of their menstrual cycles, and the right has seized on it
Author: Alaina Demopoulos
Headline: People have found language for moodiest part of their menstrual cycles, and the right has seized on it
Content: The sudden increase in people talking about the luteal phase online is ‘about [them] wanting to take control and learn more about their bodies’, says Dr Karen Tang.Composite: Rita Liu/The Guardian/Getty Images/NIHView image in fullscreenThe sudden increase in people talking about the luteal phase online is ‘about [them] wanting to take control and learn more about their bodies’, says Dr Karen Tang.Composite: Rita Liu/The Guardian/Getty Images/NIHWomen's health‘Why do I feel crappy?’: women are learning about their luteal phases - and Maha is weaponizing itPeople have found language for moodiest part of their menstrual cycles, and the right has seized on itAlaina DemopoulosThu 23 Jul 2026 08.00 EDTLast modified on Thu 23 Jul 2026 11.30 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleEmily Rae Detrick, a health coach, wants women to be more in touch with their menstrual cycle in its entirety. This, she said on an Octoberepisodeof Culture Apothecary with Alex Clark, a podcast popular among “Make America Healthy Again” (Maha) adherents, is “a way that you honor God and the way we were created”. It also serves her self-assignedmissionto help “make America fertile again”.In the episode and on social media, Detrick speaks at length about the luteal phase, the 14-ish days between ovulation andmenstruationwhen women are more likely to experience irritability, bloating and food cravings. If a woman does not get pregnant after ovulating, or so Detrick’s line of thinking goes, her body becomes upset. “During the luteal phase, the reason why your stress threshold is so much lower is because this is the time your body would be incubating a potential baby,” she said. No baby, and you might turn into a frazzled, nagging shrew.This interpretation might seem retrograde, conjuring ancient medical theories of female hysteria or wandering wombs. (Plato wrote that a childless woman ultimately goes mad because of her angry, empty uterus.) But for some women, the luteal phase – a real scientific term – has become a kind of catchall used to explain feminine malaise.‘A medical miracle’: is period blood ‘the most overlooked opportunity’ in women’s health?Read moreThousands of posts on TikTok are tagged “#lutealphase”, with women sharing memes or humorous videos about their temperament changes during this period. “I won’t tell anyone I’m in my luteal phase but there will be signs,” one womancaptioneda closeup of her blistering acne. In another videoskit, a woman washes dishes at home. Her husband comes up to give her a light, affectionate butt slap. She shouts, recoils and sprays him with soap. Blame it on her luteal.The clip has almost 100,000 likes. “I don’t even want to be PERCEIVED during my luteal phase,” reads one comment. A man chimed in: “I don’t think my wife ever gets out of her luteal phase.”Google searches for “luteal phase” have risen 300% in the past five years, probably due to these kinds of videos. Other popular searches include “luteal phase workout” (low-impact cardio, pilates and yoga are good for stress management, according to infographics); “luteal phase meal” (sweet potatonachossatisfy cravings, according to one influencer); and “luteal phase mood” (rancid, according to everyone).double quotation markExplaining what happens in the luteal phase provides a way for people to understand why they feel the way they doDr Karen TangThis content traffics in hyperbole – and even pronatalism in the hands of influencers such as Detrick and Clark. But growing interest in the luteal phase does gnaw at an ugly truth. Americans have long received inadequate sexual health education, especially when it comes to women’s health, and it’s getting worse. Abortion misinformation floodedTikTokafter the supreme court’s reversal of Roe v Wade, and “don’t say gay” laws in states such as Florida and Texas ban discussions of sexual orientation and gender identity in schools. It makes sense that women might find empowerment in talking about their luteal phases.“It’s about wanting to take control and learn more about their bodies,” said Dr Karen Tang, a Pennsylvania gynaecologist. “People are saying: ‘I had no idea that other people had these similar patterns.’”“Women’s bodies are understudied, underfunded, under-researched,” added Mariah Wellman, an assistant professor at Michigan State who studies influencers and wellness culture. “By no fault of their own, women are lacking information and not getting it from their healthcare providers.”A better understanding of cyclesAccording to Tang, the luteal phase describes what happens on a physiological, biological level: progesterone rises and estrogen lowers, and these hormonal swings result in physical and emotional symptoms until menstruation happens. Premenstrual syndrome, or PMS, refers to the symptoms that flare up – moodiness, bloating, breast tenderness, hunger and sluggishness, for example – during someone’s luteal phase.“Explaining what happens in the luteal phase provides a way for people to understandwhythey feel the way they do, so it’s not as confusing or mysterious why their physical and mental health seems to change so drastically,” Tang said.Tang says she has noticed an uptick in women coming into her practice who are potentially experiencing premenstrual dysphoric disorder (PMDD), which they first heard about on social media – sometimes through luteal phase posts. An intense hormonal sensitivity, PMDDaffectsabout 6% of people who menstruate, according to the American Psychological Association. It wreaks havoc – onestudyfound that 72% of individuals with PMDD reported “lifetime active suicidal ideation” – and often goes undiagnosed.“It’s wonderful to see those patients go from being absolutely miserable almost a quarter of their life, where [PMDD] is affecting their relationships,” she said. “When they’re able to get treatment, they’re themselves again.”double quotation markThe wellness industry has a big incentive to commercialize the luteal phaseMariah WellmanHaley Martinez, 29, is an energy-healing and breath-work coach living in Clinton, New Jersey. She used to bodybuild but quit the sport because it was too hard on her body. “I had missing cycles on and off for years,” she said. “I just felt disconnected from that part of me as a woman.” Martinez says her doctor “dismissed” her concerns, so she began studying holistic health. When she started to get her periods back after quitting bodybuilding, she felt heightened emotions, which she later learned occurred during her luteal phase. She wondered why her doctors or health teachers had never mentioned it.“There was a relief having an explanation, but there’s also an anger at not being taught this at all,” Martinez said.Luteal phase is a genuine medical term. This gives credence to influencers to talk about it, even if they are not licensed medical professionals. “They seem more credible and educated,” Wellman said.But Tang warns against their tendency to make generalizations. “There’s a lot of verbiage and absolutes, as if it were astrology, where a Taurus or a Virgo is prone to this sort of behavior,” she said. For instance, TikTok usersjokeabout their sense of self totally derailing once the luteal phase sets in; every month, without fail, theybelievethey have turned into “the ugliest person alive” or a“forest shrew”. Detrick, the guest on Clark’s podcast, claimed that working out is inevitably harder: “You’re like, ‘Am I weak? What is wrong with me?’” Clark agreed: “Ialwaysfeel that way.”“They have latched on to these little kernels of truth about the phases of the cycle and turned it into almost a prescription,” Tang said. “None of this is so absolutely universal that you have to live your life based on these principles.”Selling Maha ideologyMost women who post online are not politicizing their luteal phases. Talk of women’s cycles among Maha evangelists, however, easily lends itself to conservative viewpoints about pronatalism, traditional gender roles andrestricting access to reproductive healthcare such as birth control.Hormonal birth control methods such as the pill prevent ovulation from taking place, which means that there is no luteal phase. This might sound like a positive change for people who deal with intense discomfort in this part of their cycle, and many take birth control at least partlyfor this exact reason. (Along with preventing pregnancy, hormonal birth control can ease cramps and symptoms of PMDD, and help treat hormonal acne.)That does not sit well with Clark, a self-described “cuteservative”, who has said she wants toget women off birth controland experience their “natural” hormonal cycle – even if that means they suffer a couple of weeks every month. In anepisodeof Clark’s podcast earlier this month, Kylah Smook, a content creator who runs retreats for women getting off birth control, said that ovulation was a “vital sign” that indicates whether a woman is in good health: “If you’re not ovulating [due to birth control], it’s your body telling you: ‘Hey, we’re not feeling good. Like, I don’t feel safe to make a baby.’”The rise of pronatalism: why Musk, Vance and the right want women to have more babiesRead moreIt istruethat ovulation can be an indicator of general health, and irregular cycles might indicate an issue, but millions of people do not ovulate while safely using birth control. Smook and Clark’s skepticism evokes acommon threadofpronatalism: getting people to question and quit birth control would probably lead to more babies being born and women becoming mothers, signaling a return to conservatives’ beloved nuclear family structure. “Me and the man who loves me through my luteal phase,” Clarkcaptioned an Instagram photoof her fiance, Maha blogger Vance Voetberg, implying that the perfect couple won’t let a mood swing scare them if it’s in service of procreation.In this way, telling women to embrace the luteal phase helps sell Maha ideology. It’s also being used by the adjacent wellness industry to market products such as “PMS bars”, aromatherapy oils and many, many teas. “The wellness industry has a big incentive to commercialize the luteal phase as something you can purchase your way out of,” said Wellman.And while familiarity with the luteal phase has been helpful to women, it also can be easily caricatured.Sharon Cancio is a full-time YouTuber who lives in Florida and makes what she calls “big sister content” – the sometimes awkward conversations you might have with a sibling about bodies, health and sex. Before learning about the luteal phase about five years ago, Cancio assumed she was depressed. “Why did I feel so crappy and depleted most of the time?” she said. “Is it my boyfriend who’s ruining my life?’” (It wasn’t; they’re still together.)Knowing about the luteal phase has not magically fixed Cancino’s mood issues, but it has helped her deal with them. “I hate to admit that I do get very mean,” she said. “Now knowing that it’s a thing my body actually goes through, and it’s not just me suddenly hating everyone, I can prepare for it when it comes around.”double quotation markIt’s another way women can be torn down by men for something that is natural and that they have no control overMariah WellmanWomen’s partners often gird themselves, too.Boyfriends and husbands frequently make appearances in luteal phase TikToks, often painting themselves as longsuffering and subject to the wild whims of a woman. In one video, a husbandreadsoff a list of things his wife said to him during her luteal phase. Samples: “Shut up,” “Can you not breathe on me,” “Don’t even, that’s just gonna piss me off.” (“Sounds like clear communication to me,” one presumably female user quipped.)In anotherskit, a group of men muse on ways they could annoy their wives during the luteal phase, including loudly playing video games while they’re trying to read, watching football all day and then complaining about being tired, and leaving dirty utensils in the sink.“Women are the butt of the joke,” Wellman said. “It’s another way women can be torn down by men for something that is natural and that they have no control over. And then they are being told by other women – wellness influencers – that they can be ‘fixed’ if they buy this product.”Women who pathologize their luteal phase struggles may find comfort in social media sisterhood. That does not belie the fact that women’s health can easily be co-opted to poke fun, sell products or advance political agendas. Ultimately, it is up to individuals to come to terms with how they want to engage with this content.Martinez, the former bodybuilder, finds humor in the unpredictability that comes with hormonal shifts. “I’ll joke with my boyfriend if I’m feeling especially sensitive about something and I can recognize it’s because of my luteal phase,” she said. “But I always want to say: be careful not to dismiss yourself, because our feelings are always valid to some degree.”Explore more on these topicsWomen's healthWomen (Society)Women (Life and style)TikTokHealthReproductionBiologyfeaturesShareReuse this content

title: Why Scotland’s cinematic Moray coast made it a perfect location to shoot The Odyssey | Scotland holidays | The Guardian
description: ‘Legends cling like barnacles to these shores,’ says our local writer, who reveals how the lore of these craggy cliffs and beaches resonates with the Homeric classic
Author: Guardian staff reporter
Headline: ‘Legends cling like barnacles to these shores,’ says our local writer, who reveals how the lore of these craggy cliffs and beaches resonates with the Homeric classic
Content: Matt Damon as Odysseus, on location in Scotland.Photograph: Melinda Sue Gordon/Universal PicturesView image in fullscreenMatt Damon as Odysseus, on location in Scotland.Photograph: Melinda Sue Gordon/Universal PicturesScotland holidaysWhy Scotland’s cinematic Moray coast made it a perfect location to shoot The Odyssey‘Legends cling like barnacles to these shores,’ says our local writer, who reveals how the lore of these craggy cliffs and beaches resonates with the Homeric classicEstella ShardlowThu 23 Jul 2026 02.00 EDTLast modified on Thu 23 Jul 2026 08.11 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleThe incoming tide has made a labyrinth of the beach. As silvery rivulets spread across Culbin Sands to meet the mudflats, my walk becomes increasingly circuitous: weaving inland towards the pine forest, backtracking closer to the waves, skirting tidal pools and scaling dunes constantly resculpted by the North Sea breeze.In this shapeshifting stretch of north-east Scotland’s Moray coast, there’s not another soul in sight, nothing to anchor me to the modern day. It’s easy, then, to imagine a longship bringing weary warriors ashore, or a band of man-eating giants marauding from the woodland. Which is exactly what happens here – on screen – in Christopher Nolan’s adaptation ofThe Odyssey.At first glance, Scotland is not an obvious backdrop for a nearly 3,000-year-old Greek epic. While the poem describes the Ithacan king Odysseus and his men being cast adrift on mysterious, far-flung shores, its imagined geography is the Mediterranean (unsurprisingly, Sicily, Morocco and the Peloponnese are the movie’s other locations). Few people who live in Moray, including me, anticipated Hollywood actors Matt Damon, Zendaya and Tom Holland descending on our local beaches and sleepy fishing villages last summer. The Moray coast isn’t even well known domestically, overshadowed by the likes of the Isle of Skye and the North Coast 500 driving route in the Highlands.double quotation markIn this shapeshifting stretch of north-east Scotland’s coast, there’s not another soul in sight, nothing to anchor me to the modern dayThe more I think about it, though, the more Nolan’s decision makes sense. Unfurling due east of Inverness in a series of vast sand dunes, shingle bars, pine-edged marshes and sandstone skerries, this coastline is both cinematic and unusually varied. The absence of tourist crowds and coastal development must be a boon to film-makers.Yet the resonances between the world ofThe Odysseyand these Scottish seafaring communities run deeper than scenery. Legends cling like barnacles to Moray’s shores. Superstitions intermingle with real histories of sieges and tragedies. Take the “Atlantis of the North” at Culbin Sands, the RSPB nature reserve and forest where Matt Damon’s Odysseus clashes with the giant-like Laestrygonians. A thriving agricultural estate and village stood here until 1694, when a freak sandstorm buried the settlement in a single night. Locals blamed either a witch’s curse or their reckless laird’s bargain with the devil.View image in fullscreenCulbin Sands, where Matt Damon’s Odysseus clashes with the giant-like Laestrygonians.Photograph: Jan Holm/Alamy“The lore you hear along this coast is worthy of a Homeric epic,” says Moray-based author and classicist VJ Randle, whose novel The Athenian Murders is inspired by Greek mythology. She leads me along a section of the 50-mileMoray Coast Trail. Through an early morning Stygian sea-mist, we set off from the fishing village of Hopeman, the sandy footpath weaving between tangles of gorse and heather, before we clamber over the rock pools of Clashach Cove to a shadowy opening between the golden sandstone cliffs.“This cave always reminds me of Calypso’s home,” Randle says. “Although locally, it’s said that a scarier sort of sea-spirit lives here.” She’s talking about theGrin Iron Wife– a witch with pale-green skin, rusted-red teeth and hair of seaweed who drags children and the elderly down to her underwater lair.double quotation markLocal storyteller Michelle is helping to revive fading traditions. Her walking tours introduce figures with striking parallels to classical mythologySuch tales were once shared byseanchaidhean– oral historians who preserved heroic battles, local legends and clan genealogy. They were to Scottish Gaelic culture what the wandering bards were to ancient Greece.It’s a fading tradition that local storytellers like Michelle van den Hout ofOtherworldly Talesare helping to revive. Her walking tours introduce figures with striking parallels to classical mythology: theScottishcreator goddess and giantessCailleach, a titan if ever there was one, islands and mountains formed from the rocks that fell from her creel basket; or the siren-likeCeasg, luring sailors who listened to her song to a watery grave.View image in fullscreenBurghead harbour is where The Odyssey’s film crew docked a longship alongside the usual fishing vessels.Photograph: Helen Hotson/AlamySuch tales were never simply about frightening people, van den Hout explains: “For isolated coastal communities, storytelling was a way to pass down lessons about survival, respecting nature and how to treat strangers. Rather than warning a child with, ‘Careful, that place has a riptide,’ it was more effective to say that a witch lives there.”Threats coming from the North Sea could sometimes be made of flesh and blood, of course. In the town of Burghead, I stand on the grassy promontory overlooking granite cottages huddled around a working harbour. This is where The Odyssey’sfilm crew docked a 35-metre (115ft) longship last year alongside the usual fishing vessels. My attention turns to the raised earthworks beneath my feet: the remains of one of the largest Pictish forts ever built, burnt down in the 10th century, most likely during a Viking raid.double quotation markThe Moray coast’s fishwives were no idle, passive Penelopes. While the menfolk were at sea, they trudged miles to sell their catchAlong the neighbouring beach of Roseisle, dozens of barnacle-covered concrete blocks and pillboxes are wedged in the sand, half buried, like the ruins of a forgotten civilisation – and resembling the Trojan horse in the movie. But these relics are not ancient; they’re part of a vast defensive frontier from the second world war, when Moray became a secret training ground for the D-day landings. This legacy continues beneath the waves, where several sunken tanks from the wartime exercises can be explored on scuba diving excursions.Heading further east, the cliffs steepen and craggy outcrops have long stirred up stories of metamorphosis. The soaring, 50ft sea-arch of Bow Fiddle Rock, for instance, is a giant that’s fallen beneath the waves, only the edge of his fiddle still visible, and the cries of nesting seabirds are its mournful music.A few miles further on, the ruins of Findlater Castle cling to a narrow promontory. Befitting its turbulent past, from Viking occupation to being besieged by troops loyal to Mary, Queen of Scots, this is where Nolan has the sorceress Circe bewitch Odysseus’ men into swine.View image in fullscreenFindlater Castle, where Christopher Nolan has the sorceresss Circe bewitch Odysseus’ men into swine.Photograph: John Bracegirdle/AlamyThere’s magic on my visit, too, only it erupts from the ocean below. The setting sun is a bronze shield, the sea uncharacteristically calm, when a dozen dorsal fins pierce the surface. Bottlenose dolphins. They’re a regular sight along these shores, with the Moray Firth home to Europe’s most northerly resident pod. Minke whales, humpbacks and orcas sometimes pass through, too, making this one of the UK’s best cetacean-spotting locations.A motor launch scuds across the bay, heading for shore apace as dusk falls. My thoughts turn to homecomings. That, after all, is the essence of The Odyssey, more than monsters or battles. The hero’s yearning to return. His wife Penelope anxiously waiting. Countless women through the centuries have surely done the same from this very clifftop, staring out to sea for a sign of a familiar sail.But a visit toBuckie and District Fishing Heritage Centrethe following day reveals that the Moray coast’s fishwives were no idle, passive Penelopes. While the menfolk were at sea, they baited thousands of hooks by hand, gutted and packed herring, and trudged 13 miles inland to sell their catch.One faded, sepia photograph in the display is unforgettable: it shows the female fishers, barefoot and skirts hitched, carrying their husbands on their backs through the surf, ensuring the men’s boots stayed dry before they boarded their boats. These are, perhaps, the true heroes of these Scottish shores: not a cunning soldier-king, as Homer would have it, but the stalwart, unsung “fisher lassies”, sharing a sea-swell of stories and ballads as they worked.Where to stayThe Odyssey’scast and crew made their base atThe Seafield Armsin Cullen (doubles from £150). “The production team turned our meeting rooms into a working base for editing the film,” says owner Charles Milne, “while our grand ballroom became a private cinema where Christopher Nolan and the crew watched the day’s footage each evening.” Its refurbished self-catering accommodation Bow Cottage, where Matt Damon stayed, has been decked out with ancient Greek-inspired touches and movie memorabilia for visiting fans (sleeps seven from £300 a night, minimum three-night stay.) While you’re in Cullen, browse the shelves atBeinn Booksfor collections of Scottish folklore or perhaps a translation of Homer’s original.How to get thereNational rail services run to Inverness and Aberdeen, with onward train and bus connections to larger coastal towns. Hiring a car is the easiest way to reach its more remote beaches, castles and walking trails.Explore more on these topicsScotland holidaysFilm-inspired holidaysThe OdysseyUnited Kingdom holidaysBeach holidaysfeaturesShareReuse this content

title: ‘I would rather be poor’: man says money cannot compensate for partner’s death after failed cancer diagnosis  | NHS | The Guardian
description: Darren Rosheski won NHS payout over partner Helen Dutton’s death from rectal cancer after GP misjudged her symptoms
Author: Denis Campbell
Headline: Darren Rosheski won NHS payout over partner Helen Dutton’s death from rectal cancer after GP misjudged her symptoms
Content: Darren Rosheski and partner Helen Dutton, who died in August 2021, after a GP misdiagnosed her rectal cancer as IBS.Photograph: HandoutView image in fullscreenDarren Rosheski and partner Helen Dutton, who died in August 2021, after a GP misdiagnosed her rectal cancer as IBS.Photograph: HandoutNHS‘I would rather be poor’: man says money cannot compensate for partner’s death after failed cancer diagnosisExclusive: Darren Rosheski won NHS payout over partner Helen Dutton’s death from rectal cancer after GP misjudged her symptomsDenis CampbellHealth policy editorThu 23 Jul 2026 00.00 EDTLast modified on Thu 23 Jul 2026 03.23 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on Google“I remember the doctor, after he’d examined Helen and found a large lump in her colon, saying: ‘It’s very sinister – we need to bring you in to be investigated.’”Darren Rosheski is recalling the moment a consultant doctor at Tameside hospital broke the news to his partner, Helen Dutton, that her symptoms – which also included back pain, passing blood and altered bowel habits – were so worrying that they needed further tests.“I lost her because nobody listened to her. It came down to the GP not listening to her. They should have investigated her when she first saw the GP. Passing blood is a serious thing, isn’t it?”The family doctor who Dutton saw then, back in late 2016, judged that her symptoms were signs of irritable bowel syndrome and haemorrhoids. The GP did not recommend any further action.It proved a serious misjudgment. Dutton, a 35-year-old finance manager and mother of one, already had bowel cancer. However, it went undetected until she went back to her GP surgery two years later, saw a different doctor and was referred to Tameside hospital in Greater Manchester. In January 2019 she had a physical examination, CT and MRI scans and finally a colonoscopy, which confirmed the presence of a cancer that had already begun to spread.Three weeks later doctors at Manchester’s Christie cancer hospital told Dutton she had advanced rectal cancer, a type of bowel cancer, that was already at stage 4. She had all three main types of cancer treatment: chemotherapy, radiotherapy and surgery. In June 2020, she was given the all-clear. But her cancer returned just two months later and she died aged 40 a year later, in August 2021.View image in fullscreenNHS in England has paid out almost £190m over the last five years to settle 870 claims involving misdiagnosis or delayed diagnosis of cancer.Photograph: Geoffrey Swaine/ShutterstockThe GP’s failure to refer Dutton for tests when she first presented with her symptoms proved disastrous for her chances of survival – and also very costly for theNHS. Rosheski, a tradesman, hired Irwin Mitchell solicitors to sue the NHS over the errors in his partner’s care. He reached an out-of-court settlement for an undisclosed sum in January. He and their daughter were financially dependent on Dutton. The NHS did not admit liability.“I would rather be poor and have Helen still around. A family has been destroyed by a doctor’s negligence. I don’t care about the compensation. I just wish she was here now,” he said. Both he and their daughter have suffered serious mental health problems since Dutton’s death five years ago. His diagnosed PTSD and complex grief mean he has only worked for a month since then.Rosheski told his story as new figures today reveal that the NHS inEnglandhas paid out almost £190m over the last five years to settle 870 claims involving misdiagnosis or delayed diagnosis of cancer. Total costs, including legal fees, have risen from £32m in 2020-21 to £41.3m in 2024-25.“These figures are really alarming because they reveal that mistakes by NHS clinicians, in either misdiagnosing cancer or taking too long to diagnose it, are having devastating consequences for people’s health and lives”, said Olivia Boschat, a solicitor at lawyers Bolt Burdon Kemp.View image in fullscreenA framed photograph of Rosheski and Helen with their daughter.Photograph: Joel Goodman/The Guardian“It is terribly sad to learn that 870 people have experienced such poor cancer care from the NHS that they or their relatives felt that they had to sue over mistakes that were highly damaging, and in some cases fatal.“Behind these figures lie heartbreak, tragedy and, in some cases, lives cut short – lives that could have been saved but, because of errors and negligence, were ended prematurely,” added Boschat, a medical negligence specialist.The difficulties patients can face accessing what is meant to be urgent testing for cancer on the NHS are often a key factor in such lawsuits, Boschat said. “Some delays in diagnosis are unavoidable because doctors need time to rule out other possible diagnoses, others happen because symptoms are overlooked, necessary tests are not carried out quickly enough, or results are missed or not acted on promptly.“The consequences can be devastating. Delays may allow the cancer to spread, meaning more aggressive treatment is needed, and in the worst cases it may become untreatable, with life-changing or fatal consequences.”Bolt Burdon Kemp obtained the figures using a freedom of information request to NHS Resolution, the agency which handles medical negligence lawsuits against the health service.They show that the NHS settled 171 such cases last year, fewer than in each of the previous three years. Despite that, though, the average cost of settling actions has jumped over the last five years from £199,896 in 2020-21 to £241,705 in 2024-25 – a 20% rise.Boschat believes that increase reflects the fact that delayed diagnosis of cancer is increasingly leading to greater harm for the patients involved, such as having to have more aggressive treatment or their ability to have children being impaired, especially among younger people.The NHS has not met a target to start urgent cancer treatment within 31 days of a patient and doctor agreeing a treatment plan since December 2020. It has not met a target to start treatment within two months of a hospital receiving a suspected urgent cancer referral since December 2015.View image in fullscreen‘I don’t care about the compensation’: Rosheski said his family had been ‘destroyed by a doctor’s negligence’.Photograph: Joel Goodman/The GuardianGuy Forster, the president of the Association of Personal Injury Lawyers, said: “Some surviving victims need additional care, therapies and specialist equipment if the delay in diagnosis means they now have mobility or continence issues, for example.“Some will be out of work for a long time. For those who have lost loved ones as a result of delays, lives will be turned upside down, partners and their children may be without the practical, emotional and financial support on which they are reliant.”The Department of Health and Social Care did not respond directly to the rising costs of payouts for botched diagnosis of cancer, but admitted that mistakes happen.“Early and accurate diagnosis is key to effective cancer treatment. This hasn’t happened in enough cases, and this government is determined to address this,” a spokesperson said.“But we are making progress. In the last year, 82,000 more people received a timely diagnosis for suspected cancer or were given the all-clear compared to the year before.“ThroughJess’s rulewe are supporting GPs in taking a ‘fresh eyes’ approach to cancer detection, and our national cancer plan will give family doctors digital tools to spot and flag concerning symptoms or test results earlier,” they added.Explore more on these topicsNHSEnglandBowel cancerCancerGPsDoctorsnewsShareReuse this content

title: Thursday news quiz: Elections denied, a dog surprise, and a very viral raccoon | Life and style | The Guardian
description: Test yourself on topical news trivia, pop culture and general knowledge every Thursday. How will you fare?
Author: Martin Belam
Headline: Test yourself on topical news trivia, pop culture and general knowledge every Thursday. How will you fare?
Content: This raccoon has some questions for you.Photograph: GlobalP/Getty Images/iStockphotoView image in fullscreenThis raccoon has some questions for you.Photograph: GlobalP/Getty Images/iStockphotoThursday quizLife and styleThursday news quiz: Elections denied, a dog surprise, and a very viral raccoonTest yourself on topical news trivia, pop culture and general knowledge every Thursday. How will you fare?Martin BelamThu 23 Jul 2026 05.34 EDTLast modified on Thu 23 Jul 2026 11.43 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleWhile the keys to the country keep changing hands, the Thursday news quiz remains reassuringly constant, steadfastly refusing to hand over power, or grow up in any way. Fifteen questions on topical news, general knowledge and popular culture await, this week with a hefty dose of novelty animal stories and Liz Truss again, because she won’t let it be. Anyway, there are no prizes, but let us know how you got on in the comments. Allons-y!The Thursday news quiz, No 2571.80-year-old authoritarian leader Daniel Ortega (pictured in mural form) has said his country will hold no more elections. Which country is that?El SalvadorHondurasNicaraguaBorduriaReveal2.In a move that definitely doesn't look like a grift, the New York sculptor Sergio Furnari has been left furious after spending his own money to build a godawful lifesize statue of Charlie Kirk, only to find that no one wants to buy it. How much does he claim it has cost him?$1,000$10,000$100,000$1,000,000Reveal3.Which Australian politician startled television viewers by suddenly bellowing at their dog (not pictured, that's a dog surprised to be appearing in the quiz) mid-interview?Barnaby JoycePauline HansonPenny WongBob KatterReveal4.The Russian warship Neustrashimy (pictured) carried out a rare live-fire exercise on the day Andy Burnham became UK PM. Where?In the North Sea, just off SkegnessIn the Atlantic Ocean, off the west coast of ShetlandIn the Irish Sea, to the east of the Isle of ManIn the Channel, just off PlymouthReveal5.Talking of Andy Burnham, he risked the wrath of voters by revealing what, which he said would 'offend a lot of people' …He always wears odd socksHe puts the milk first in teaHe doesn't care whether the cream or the jam goes first on a sconeHe intends for every boomer to be sent a telegram directly from King Charles III ordering them to stop posting AI slop on FacebookReveal6.A Michelin-starred restaurant owner in South Korea (not pictured) faces jail over garnishing a dessert with what?Crushed opium poppies to add a highImported dried ants to add acidityMicrodoses of pufferfish poison to add a tingleHuman hair to add that 'return it to the kitchen, please' vibeReveal7.It is David Essex's birthday today. Happy birthday, David! Rock on! Who produced his first few solo albums, and was also famous for their musical adaptation of a HG Wells story that Essex appeared in?Jeff WayneMike BattRick WakemanKate BushReveal8.This week's guest avian is a very tame robin that wanted some of the Thursday quiz's snacks at Birdworld near Farnham. It wants to know the name of the beloved bald eagle in Big Bear Valley that went missing from her viral nestcam, but has now been found alive but injured. It is …SylviaJackieFrancineLaviniaReveal9.Scores on the doors with Swindon's Diana Dors! Who scored the winning goal in the Fifa World Cup final between Spain and Argentina on Sunday?Mikel MerinoDani OlmoFerran TorresAny Argentinian playerReveal10.On this day with Brian May! And his badger! 23 July is the anniversary of the first publicly available live transatlantic television signal. The Thursday quiz imagines Queen's legendary guitarist wants you to tell him – and his badger – what enabled it …Telstar 1Echo 1Sputnik 1Gary Barlow's massive son standing on a big ladder holding an aerial really high in the airReveal11.Zendaya (pictured, but not doing the thing) is facing a backlash in some quarters for doing what at the premiere of Christopher Nolan's Homer's The Odyssey?Spoiling the ending of the 3,000-year-old poemWearing ancient looted Iranian jewelleryWearing a dress made of genuine vintage film stockComplaining that the movie is too longReveal12.Who is fronting the new ‘What has the BBC ever done for me?’ advert, which is nearly as long as The Odyssey movie?Nish KumarRomesh RanganathanRichard AyoadeRon from SparksReveal13.What is the name of the Seattle raccoon (not pictured, that one is in Germany) that has gone viral because it is assumed he has a rare genetic condition called short spine syndrome, giving him his distinctive look?TomathanBilliamSvenjaminJimothyReveal14.Who is having to offer full refunds after fans were evacuated from a show in Milan because of a violent hailstorm?Bad BunnyAwful AarvarkMischievous MeerkatLousy LorisChaotic ChaffinchFaulty FrogPoxy PonyDastardly DachshundReveal15.And finally, the Thursday news quiz regrets to report that Liz Truss has been at it again – speaking in public at CPAC UK despite being UK prime minister for all of six weeks. Where was the event held?Near the NEC, in BirminghamNear the O2, in LondonNear Co-op Live, in ManchesterFour Seasons Total LandscapingRevealIf you really do think there has been an egregious error in one of the questions or answers – and can show your working and are absolutely 100% positive you aren’t attempting to factcheck a joke – you can complain about it in the comments below. Why not watchAlways (Erasure cover)by Robyn instead?Always (Erasure cover) by RobynExplore more on these topicsLife and styleThursday quizQuiz and trivia gamesquizzesShareReuse this content

title: Country diary: The eyebright paradox: a sunny healer and tiny parasitic thief | Wild flowers | The Guardian
description: The Marches, Shropshire: Eyebrights are ancient herbs used to cure eye problems, but they also penetrate grass roots to siphon off water and nutrients
Author: Paul Evans
Headline: The Marches, Shropshire: Eyebrights are ancient herbs used to cure eye problems, but they also penetrate grass roots to siphon off water and nutrients
Content: Common eyebright in Old Racecourse Common, Oswestry.Photograph: Maria Nunzia @VarveraView image in fullscreenCommon eyebright in Old Racecourse Common, Oswestry.Photograph: Maria Nunzia @VarveraCountry diaryWild flowersCountry diary: The eyebright paradox: a sunny healer and tiny parasitic thiefThe Marches, Shropshire: Eyebrights are ancient herbs used to cure eye problems, but they also penetrate grass roots to siphon off water and nutrientsPaul EvansThu 23 Jul 2026 00.30 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleAsight for sore eyes.Eyebrightsflower in the short turf of Old Racecourse Common as sunlight burns a hole through rowan trees to cast sunset colours on the grass. The eyebrights are annuals with tiny flowers, 4mm-11mm long with a violet vibe, a white corolla with purple marks and a central flash of yellow.There are about 20 British eyebright species, some of which are endemic. Their genetic complexity makes them an arcane group of plants and a taxonomic puzzle to all but their most dedicated acolytes. “Life’s too short to key out [make a precise identification of] eyebrights,” one botanist told me.For the sake of argument, this one isEuphrasia officinalis agg,an aggregate of species, microspecies and hybrids, one of which isEnemorosa, the common eyebright. They belong toOrobanchaceae, the family that also includes lousewort, toothwort and yellow rattle. Like these, the eyebrights are parasitic, or at least hemi-parasitic, capable of independent living but also sending out roots to penetrate the roots of grasses to siphon water and nutrients from them, like wiring into streetlights to nick the ’leccy. This reduces grass dominance and changes the composition of the turf.Euphrasiacomes from the Greek word for cheerfulness, obvious when you get down to look into their faces, even through the smudgy lenses of my specs. According to the medievalDoctrine of Signatures, objects in nature have trademark characteristics that are key to their influence on human conditions, so gazing at these flowers looking back with such a bright, sunny disposition can help us see and feel better. Following on from intuitions and symbolism, eyebright is an ancient medicinal herb with properties such as aucubin and iridoid glycosides, lignans and flavonoids used in tinctures and compresses to cure eye problems. They also promote wellbeing and were burned as an incense to aid divination.So, is the future bright for eyebrights? A lack of grazing and encroaching bracken, recently cut here, is a common problem for short-turf habitats. Conventional conservation of distinctive species has to be balanced with the conservation of processes that create the extraordinary evolution of eyebright diversity for these shapers of place and their richly storied lives.Under the Changing Skies: The Best of the Guardian’s Country Diary, 2018-2024, is available now atguardianbookshop.comExplore more on these topicsWild flowersCountry diaryRural affairsPlantsSummerfeaturesShareReuse this content

title: Orcas seen ramming prey so hard it explodes may be playing game | Marine life | The Guardian
description: Scientists believe sunfish-smashing behaviour could be form of entertainment or to help younger whales feed
Author: Hannah Devlin
Headline: Scientists believe sunfish-smashing behaviour could be form of entertainment or to help younger whales feed
Content: Orcas have been known to exhibit play behaviour such as throwing kelp at each other and engaging in tactical games.Photograph: James RD Scott/Getty ImagesView image in fullscreenOrcas have been known to exhibit play behaviour such as throwing kelp at each other and engaging in tactical games.Photograph: James RD Scott/Getty ImagesMarine lifeOrcas seen ramming prey so hard it explodes may be playing gameScientists believe sunfish-smashing behaviour could be form of entertainment or to help younger whales feedHannah DevlinScience correspondentThu 23 Jul 2026 00.00 EDTLast modified on Thu 23 Jul 2026 14.02 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleOrcas have been observed ramming into giant fish so hard that the prey explode, possibly as a form of gory entertainment.On several occasions, orcas were observed to hold sunfish in their jaws while a second whale smashed into the target at high speed, causing it to break into thousands of pieces. Since the fish had already been killed, scientists suggested the behaviour may represent a form of play.Dr Kathryn Ayres, a scientist at non-profit organisation Beneath The Waves, said: “We think this may help younger orcas feed more easily or it could also just be for fun. Orcas are known for playing with their food.”Ayres captured the first fish-ramming event on a GoPro camera in July 2024 and can be heard shrieking in shock on the audio.Orca explodes sharp-tail sunfish with ramming attack - loop“When you see a multi-ton apex predator charge a massive fish at that speed right next to your boat, the sheer acoustic thud and the resulting explosion of tissue are overwhelming,” said Erick Higuera, a marine biologist and wildlife cinematographer at Mexican non-profit Conexiones Terramar and co-author of the research.Sunfish are among the world’s largest fish, growing to more than three metres long and weighing up to 2,000kg. They are commonly hunted and eaten by orcas.In Ayres’ footage, a female orca is seen gripping the tail of a sunfish carcass while an adult male accelerates towards it. Just before impact, the female releases the dead fish and there is an audible crunch as the male strikes the target, leaving behind a plume of white fragments. A juvenile whale is subsequently seen eating the small pieces of flesh, while the adult orcas feed on the remains of the main carcass.In a second instance, captured by a tourism operator in September 2025, an adult female orca was spotted holding out a dead sunfish, while a second orca executed a high-speed ramming manoeuvre, breaking it into thousands of pieces. The events were recorded in the Gulf of California, off the coast of Mexico, but it is unclear whether the same orcas were involved.One hypothesis is that ramming is used to break the tough, rubbery sunfish into bite-size pieces for juveniles, whose jaws are smaller. But orcas are also known for social fads and boisterous games that do not have an obvious practical purpose. In the 1980s, a pod of orcas started carrying dead salmon on their head like hats,a trend that was recently revived. A subpopulation off the coast of Spain and Portugal has attracted attention forunusual interactions with boats.“They throw kelp at each other, surf in boat wakes and engage in intricate tactical games,” said Higuera. “Play is a core pillar of orca society.”skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |WeeklySign up toDown to EarthThe planet's most important stories. Get all the week's environment news - the good, the bad and the essentialPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionWhatever the purpose of the behaviour, it demonstrates an impressive level of cognitive sophistication, requiring the planning and coordination of several steps, according to the findings reported in the journalFrontiers in Ethology.Eco-tour operators increasingly use underwater drones and cameras, which are capturing new behaviours that are of interest to scientists. But there are also concerns about thedisruptive impact of wildlife tourism, with Mexico issuing new regulations on swimming with orcas last year.“If we can maintain a respectful distance and protect these animals, continuing this collaboration with tour operators will allow us to safely document mysteries of ocean life that would otherwise remain completely hidden from science,” said Higuera.Explore more on these topicsMarine lifeWhalesAnimalsWildlifenewsShareReuse this content

title: Tell us: do you help clean up your local area? | Environment | The Guardian

Whether it be maintaining parkland, beaches, waterways or roadsides, we want to hear from the volunteers who are getting involved in the maintenance and preservation of their local areas.Do you take part in weekly litter picking outings? Are you part of a group taking on the responsibility of keeping our beaches or rivers clean?Whether you’re part of a volunteer group or go out on solo clean-ups, we’d like to hear from you.Share your experienceYou can tell us your experience using this form.Please share your story if you are 18 or over, anonymously if you wish.
