title: Two Russian men jailed in Angola for terrorism and spying | Angola | The Guardian
description: The men were convicted over alleged plan to stage coup and organise deadly demonstrations over fuel prices last year
Author: Rachel Savage
Headline: The men were convicted over alleged plan to stage coup and organise deadly demonstrations over fuel prices last year
Content: Lev Lakshtanov, left, and Igor Ratchin, second left, who have been sentenced to eight and 11 years respectively, at a hearing in August 2025.Photograph: Ministerio do Interior/Governo de AngolaView image in fullscreenLev Lakshtanov, left, and Igor Ratchin, second left, who have been sentenced to eight and 11 years respectively, at a hearing in August 2025.Photograph: Ministerio do Interior/Governo de AngolaAngolaTwo Russian men jailed in Angola for terrorism and spyingThe men were convicted over alleged plan to stage coup and organise deadly demonstrations over fuel prices last yearRachel Savagein Johannesburg and agenciesThu 23 Jul 2026 10.24 EDTFirst published on Thu 23 Jul 2026 08.08 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleTwo Russian men have been found guilty by an Angolan court of terrorism and spying after being accused of planning a coup and helping to organise deadly demonstrations in the southern African country last year.Lev Lakshtanov and Igor Ratchin were sentenced to eight and 11 years in jail respectively. They had denied the charges, claiming they were inAngolafor cultural and business activities.From 28-30 July 2025, a minibus taxi strike over fuel price hikes descended into riots and looting, in which 22 people were killed and more than 1,600 arrested according to official figures.Angola’s security forces were accused of firing live ammunition at protesters; the government said its “progressive” use of forcewas justified. Human Rights Watch had previously said Angola employed “excessive force” when it used rubber bullets and teargasto break up a peaceful protestagainst rising fuel prices on 12 July 2025.Angolan prosecutors said Lakshtanov and Ratchin wanted to influence next year’s presidential elections, including by funding Angola’s largest opposition party, the National Union for the Total Independence of Angola (UNITA), or by forcing a leadership change within the Popular Movement for the Liberation of Angola (MPLA), which has ruled the country since independence from Portugal in 1975.Prosecutors also claimed the men were linked toAfricaPolitology, a Russian disinformation and propaganda network that emerged from the Wagner mercenary group.Africa Politology was taken over by Russia’s Foreign Intelligence Service (SVR) after the death of the Wagner chief Yevgeny Prigozhin in 2023, according to an investigation by media outletsincluding Forbidden Stories. Wagner was rebranded as Africa Corps and absorbed by Russia’s military.View image in fullscreenWood is burned on the street near where people vandalised stores during the first day of the taxi strike in Luanda, Angola, in July 2025.Photograph: Ampe Rogerio/EPAAngola, whose economy is highly dependent on oil exports, has historically been close with Russia. During the cold war years of Angola’s 1975-2002 civil war, the Soviet Union and Cuba supported the MPLA, while the US and apartheid South Africa backed its opponents, including UNITA. However, Angola’s president, João Lourenço, hasimproved relations with the USand Europe since taking power in 2017.In the last decade, Russia has increased its presence in Africa. The Wagner group and then Africa Corps have provided securityfor the Central African Republic’s president, Faustin-Archange Touadéra, since 2017.In 2021, Wagner forces arrived in Mali to support the military junta. Africa Corps also backs the military regimes in neighbouring Niger and Burkina Faso. Since 2023, Russian troops have also been deployed in Equatorial Guinea, Libya and Sudan, according to a US Congressreport.Madagascar’s president, Michael Randrianirina, has alsoboosted ties with Russiasince taking power in a coup in October 2025. There are Russians in his presidential guard and a weekly programme about Madagascar-Russia relations was launched earlier this month on national television.Two Angolans were also tried alongside the Russians. The sports journalist Carlos Tomé received a two-year suspended prison sentence. Francisco de Oliveira, the secretary-general of UNITA’s youth wing, was acquitted.Prosecutors have appealed against those sentences, according to the Portuguese news agencyLusa. Lakshtanov and Ratchin have also appealed against their sentences, but will remain in detention while the appeal is processed, according to Lusa.Russia’s embassy in Angola did not immediately respond to a request for comment.Agence France-Press contributed to this reportExplore more on these topicsAngolaHuman rightsAfricaRussianewsShareReuse this content

title: MoD cancels army training in Kenya over powers to prosecute British soldiers | British army | The Guardian
description: September exercise called off after Kenya raises concerns over funding and jurisdiction for criminal offences
Author: Hannah Al-Othman
Headline: September exercise called off after Kenya raises concerns over funding and jurisdiction for criminal offences
Content: Soldiers of the British army training unit in Kenya (BATUK) with troops from the Kenya Defence Forces (KDF) on a training exercise on the Laikipia plateau.Photograph: Tony Karumba/AFP/GettyView image in fullscreenSoldiers of the British army training unit in Kenya (BATUK) with troops from the Kenya Defence Forces (KDF) on a training exercise on the Laikipia plateau.Photograph: Tony Karumba/AFP/GettyBritish armyMoD cancels army training in Kenya over powers to prosecute British soldiersSeptember exercise called off after Kenya raises concerns over funding and jurisdiction for criminal offencesHannah Al-OthmanNorth of England correspondentThu 23 Jul 2026 10.06 EDTFirst published on Thu 23 Jul 2026 09.11 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleTheBritish armyhas been forced to cancel training for its troops in Kenya after the Kenyan government refused to ratify a bilateral defence agreement.While the UK government wanted to renew the existing agreement, Kenyan lawmakers had sought changes to give local authorities greater powers to hold British soldiers accountable for any offences committed in the country.According to the Kenyan news outlet the Daily Nation, the dispute centres on which casesKenya will retain primary jurisdictionover, while the amount of money Britain pays Kenya to host the training camp has also been a sticking point.The chair of the Kenyan national assembly’s defence, intelligence and foreign relations committee, Nelson Koech, said the two governments were “actively engaging to address these outstanding issues”.As a result of the Kenyan government’s decision, a planned exercise involving the 1st Battalion the Duke of Lancaster’s Regiment, which was due to begin in September, has been cancelled.AMinistry of Defencespokesperson said: “The UK highly values its relationship with Kenya, which is underpinned by mutual benefit, respect and cooperation. The UK-Kenya Defence Partnership is one of the great strengths of our relationship. Our joint training and operations with the Kenyan Defence Forces are keeping both Kenyan and British people safe.“It is with regret that the necessary licences required to allow training to take place in Laikipia later this year have not been forthcoming. As a result, the planned training will now be conducted at an alternative location outside Kenya.”Kenya also benefits from training of its own armed forces at BATUK, the British army base near Nanyuki, and the presence of British troops is valuable for the economy.The MoD spokesperson added: “We recognise the disappointment this decision will cause for those who would have benefited from the economic activity generated by the exercise.”They said the UK government remained “committed to our defence partnership with Kenya”, adding that the UK would “continue to engage closely with the Kenyan government to support the resumption of training in-country at the earliest appropriate opportunity”.However, there has been sustained pressure from Kenyan people opposed to the British army’s continued presence. Last year, a report to the Kenyan parliament into the base alleged human rights violations, environmental destruction and sexual abuse by British soldiers.British troops accused of human rights violations and sexual abuse in KenyaRead moreThe most high-profile case linked to the base isthe deathof Agnes Wanjiru, in which a British soldier has been charged with murder. Kenya has retained jurisdiction in the case and an extradition hearing is due to take place in November. Robert Purkiss denies the single charge of murder.Legal proceedings had stalled since Wanjiru’s death in 2012, but advanced under John Healey’s tenure as defence secretary. As shadow defence secretary, he had called on the previous government to do more to progress the case.Koech said the committee had called for “stronger accountability measures, including jurisdiction over serious offences, enhanced protection for local communities, and implementation of recommendations arising from the BATUK inquiry”.Explore more on these topicsBritish armyMilitaryKenyaAfricaMinistry of DefencenewsShareReuse this content

title: Mayor shot dead in Mexico months after surviving assassination attempt | Mexico | The Guardian
description: Valentín Lavín Romero is the 14th mayor to be murdered since Claudia Sheinbaum became president in 2024
Author: Thomas Graham
Headline: Valentín Lavín Romero is the 14th mayor to be murdered since Claudia Sheinbaum became president in 2024
Content: Valentín Lavín Romero, of the Green party, was killed in the town hall in Temoac, near Mexico City.Photograph: Valentín Lavín Romero/FacebookView image in fullscreenValentín Lavín Romero, of the Green party, was killed in the town hall in Temoac, near Mexico City.Photograph: Valentín Lavín Romero/FacebookMexicoMayor shot dead in Mexico months after surviving assassination attemptValentín Lavín Romero is the 14th mayor to be murdered since Claudia Sheinbaum became president in 2024Thomas GrahamThu 23 Jul 2026 12.32 EDTLast modified on Thu 23 Jul 2026 21.30 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleA mayor was shot dead on Wednesday in the Mexican state of Morelos after having survived a previous assassination attempt earlier this year. He is the 14th mayor to be murdered sinceClaudia Sheinbaumbecame president in October 2024.Valentín Lavín Romero, who was from the Green party, an ally of the ruling leftwing Morena party, was wounded at the end of January when hitmen shot him at a petrol station on a motorway.This time the hitmen entered the town hall of Temoac, nearMexicoCity, in the middle of the day before shooting Lavín Romero dead and escaping on a motorbike.Authorities are yet to identify a motive or suspects, but the Morelos prosecutor’s office vowed to “exhaust all lines of investigation”.Almost 100 mayors have been murdered in Mexico since 2006, when the then president, Felipe Calderón, deployed the military to take on Mexico’s organised crime groups, ultimately causing drug-related violence to rise.The municipal level of government is theleast protected layerof the state and where criminal groups seek deals with authorities to strengthen their hold over the local territory and its businesses.Kidnappings, threats and ‘protection fees’: how can Mexico confront rise in deadly extortion?Read moreLocal media reported Lavín Romero himself allegedly had ties to local crime groups. His mother-in-law, a former treasurer for the municipal government, was arrested in September 2025 for alleged ties to a criminal group known as Los Aparicio and involvement in kidnappings.In March, the People’s Front in Defence of Land and Water, an activist group, demanded authorities investigate Lavín Romero for his alleged involvement in the murders of two activists, Samir Flores and Sandra Rosa Camacho Flores.In May – three months after the first attempt on his life –a banner was leftin the centre of Temoac, seemingly by a criminal group, accusing Lavín Romero of protecting its rivals and demanding he fall in line with their interests.His assassination comes as Mexico’s federal government takes steps to unpick the corruption networks between municipal authorities and criminal groups as part of Operation Swarm, under which 85 officials have been arrested across three states, including Morelos.According to official data, the homicide rate in Mexico has fallen by roughly half over the past year, althoughsecurity analysts saythe true reduction is likely smaller, while more than 130,000 people are registered as missing.Explore more on these topicsMexicoOrganised crimeClaudia SheinbaumAmericasGun crimenewsShareReuse this content

title: Total solar eclipse books out hotels two years in advance in New Zealand city | New Zealand | The Guardian
description: Dunedin will be the focal point, having last experienced such an eclipse in 1163 – before any humans reached New Zealand
Author: Eva Corlett
Headline: Dunedin will be the focal point, having last experienced such an eclipse in 1163 – before any humans reached New Zealand
Content: In 2028, Dunedin will see its first total solar eclipse since 1163, and New Zealand’s first since 19065.Photograph: Héctor Vivas/Getty ImagesView image in fullscreenIn 2028, Dunedin will see its first total solar eclipse since 1163, and New Zealand’s first since 19065.Photograph: Héctor Vivas/Getty ImagesNew ZealandTotal solar eclipse books out hotels two years in advance in New Zealand cityDunedin will be the focal point, having last experienced such an eclipse in 1163 – before any humans reached New ZealandEva Corlettin WellingtonThu 23 Jul 2026 00.59 EDTLast modified on Thu 23 Jul 2026 21.30 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleHotels are booking out two years in advance in theNew Zealandcity of Dunedin as it prepares for the region’s first total solar eclipse in more than 850 years.At 4.17pm on 22 July 2028, the moon will pass in front of the sun, casting a roughly 100km-wide shadowy band across parts of southern New Zealand. Everywhere within that band – from tourist hotspot Queenstown in the west to Dunedin city in the east – will be plunged into daytime darkness for 2 minutes and 51 seconds.Despite there being two years still to go, hotels are already fielding booking inquiries, including the Dunedin Leisure Lodge, which by Thursday was fully booked for its “solar eclipse package” that includes complimentary eclipse glasses.A moment that changed me: I saw my first total solar eclipse – and its beauty shook me to my coreRead moreDr Ian Griffin, an astronomer and director of Tūhura Otago Museum, said: “The last one visible from Dunedin was in 1163, which was before humans reached New Zealand.“So we will be the first people in Dunedin ever to see a solar eclipse from this part of the world, which is quite exciting.”It will also be the first total solar eclipse visible anywhere in New Zealand since 1965.“That three minutes of darkness, if it’s clear, will be absolutely memorable for anybody who sees it,” Griffin said.On Wednesday, Dunedin’s museum started the clock for the two-year countdown, in what is tipped to be a major tourism event for the city. Roughly 35,000 visitors are expected to descend on the city, which has a population of 130,000.View image in fullscreenView at dawn along St Clair Beach, Dunedin. A total eclipse will be seen for the first time by humans across a band of southern New Zealand.Photograph: David C Tomlinson/Getty ImagesGriffin, meanwhile, has been preparing for the eclipse since 2013.“I did buy my house all those years ago based on the fact that you could see the eclipse from it,” he said.The eclipse will happen in the middle of New Zealand’s winter, just after the country celebrates Matariki – the Māori new year. Matariki is theMāoriname for a cluster of stars known elsewhere as the Pleiades. The constellation is visible from New Zealand for 11 months of the year, but disappears from the skies for a month in winter, reappearing around the time of the winter solstice.Dunedin is planning a night-sky festival beginning with Matariki and going through to the eclipse.“It’s actually unique anywhere around the world,” Griffin said. “We’ve got the cultural holiday celebrating the night sky, and then will be following it about 10 days later with a fantastic eclipse.”The Dunedin mayor, Sophie Barker, said anticipation for the eclipse was being felt across the city. “This remarkable event is now firmly on the horizon.”Explore more on these topicsNew ZealandSolar eclipsesSpaceAsia PacificnewsShareReuse this content

title: Donald Trump hits Australian exports to US with new higher trade tariff over claims of ‘forced labour’ | Australia news | The Guardian
description: Australia’s trade minister, Don Farrell, says tariff regime is unjustified and inconsistent with free trade agreement with US
Author: Josh Butler
Headline: Australia’s trade minister, Don Farrell, says tariff regime is unjustified and inconsistent with free trade agreement with US
Content: Donald Trump has imposed a new 12.5% tariff on Australian exports to the US.Photograph: Darren England/AAPView image in fullscreenDonald Trump has imposed a new 12.5% tariff on Australian exports to the US.Photograph: Darren England/AAPAustralia newsDonald Trump hits Australian exports to US with new higher trade tariff over claims of ‘forced labour’Australia’s trade minister, Don Farrell, says tariff regime is unjustified and inconsistent with free trade agreement with USFollow our Australia news live blog for latest updatesGet ourbreaking news email,free appordaily news podcastJosh ButlerThu 23 Jul 2026 19.46 EDTLast modified on Thu 23 Jul 2026 20.27 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleDonald Trump has hit Australian exports with new trade tariffs, claiming the country is not doing enough to combat forced labour, but the Albanese government has demanded he remove them immediately.TheUnited States announced on Friday morning(AEST) that many Australian exports now attract a new 12.5% tariff as part of a broader regime affecting dozens of countries. However the Albanese government says Australia’s three largest export categories – including beef and gold – will avoid the tariff hit.In a statement, the US trade department said Australia was on a list of 54 economies it deemed as having “failed to impose and effectively enforce a prohibition on the importation of goods produced with forced labor”. Other countries facing a 12.5% tariff include Brazil, China, Egypt, Israel, Japan, New Zealand, Norway, Russia and Singapore.Sign up for the Breaking News Australia emailEconomies on a separate list – including the UK, India, Indonesia, Canada and Mexico – will be subject to a lower 10% tariff after the US claimed they had only failed to “enforce” forced labour prohibitions.Friday’s news followed through onan earlier threat from the Trump administrationto level such tariffs, which came after the US supreme court earlier this year struck downTrump’s 10% global tariffs.Trump had responded to that court ruling by immediately announcing temporary trade sanctions, which were due to expire today.Australian government sources had previously told Guardian Australia they believed the forced labour-related tariffs had been levelled as a replacement for the tariffs struck down by the courts.US congresswoman Linda Sánchez, a California Democrat, has denounced the pretext for the new tariffs as a sham.“President Trump isn’t serious about combating forced labor. He’s trying to make an end-run around the Supreme Court and Congress to reimpose his illegal tariffs,” Sánchez wrote on social media.The American trade action document claims “the United States is the only country in the world to adopt, and effectively enforce, a ban on imports made with forced labor”.“President Trump is asking all trading partners to join the United States in eliminating forced labor from global supply chains.”In total the tariffs apply “to the top 60 U.S. trade partners covering 99.4% of U.S. imports”, according to a trade department statement.The Australian trade minister, Don Farrell, immediately called for the US to change course.“These tariffs are unjustified, inconsistent with our free-trade agreement and should be removed,” Farrell said.“Australia’s measures to combat forced labour and modern slavery are among the strongest in the world and we are recognised globally, including in the US, for our leadership.”skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |When neededSign up toBreaking News AustraliaGet the most important news as it breaksPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionIn a subsequent press conference, Farrell said Australian exports to the US had increased since Trump’s so-called “liberation day” tariffs, and that while he would strongly protest for the removal of the trade barriers, many of Australia’s biggest exports to Australia would not be affected.“It’s beef, it’s gold and it’s copper… I don’t see any reason to think that’s not going to continue,” he said.The deputy prime minister,Richard Marles, told ABC radio the US actions “make no sense”.“Forced labour is definitely something which needs to be combated around the world, so we support that objective,” he said. “But we have laws, which are amongst the strongest in the world, and we’ve been making that point to the Trump administration.”Trump to impose fresh wave of tariffs on more than 80 countriesRead moreThe US trade action document that sets out the new tariffs includes exemptions for a range of goods including beef; agricultural products such as vegetables and fruits; aircraft parts; certain coal, oil, mineral and chemical products; some metal and steel products; timber products; and machinery.The document says it will broadly apply to goods entering from 12.01am US eastern time on 24 July (2pm Aest).The action, as it pertains to Australia specifically, states that after an investigation, “the Trade Representative has determined to impose 12.5 percent tariffs on products of Australia” except for a limited number of exemptions.Explore more on these topicsAustralia newsAustralian politicsAustralian economyLabor partyTariffsTrump tariffsRichard MarlesnewsShareReuse this content

title: Pauline Hanson blames ‘fake news’ media for her bad press. But serious leaders have to own their actions | Pauline Hanson | The Guardian
description: In a fortnight of rolling blunders, the One Nation leader digs deep into the Trump playbook of blame-shifting
Author: Josh Butler
Headline: In a fortnight of rolling blunders, the One Nation leader digs deep into the Trump playbook of blame-shifting
Content: The One Nation leader, Pauline Hanson, this week claimed there was ‘a concerted effort by some in the media to constantly take my comments out of context’.Photograph: Mick Tsikas/AAPView image in fullscreenThe One Nation leader, Pauline Hanson, this week claimed there was ‘a concerted effort by some in the media to constantly take my comments out of context’.Photograph: Mick Tsikas/AAPPauline HansonAnalysisPauline Hanson blames ‘fake news’ media for her bad press. But serious leaders have to own their actionsJosh ButlerIn a fortnight of rolling blunders, the One Nation leader digs deep into the Trump playbook of blame-shiftingFollow our Australia news live blog for latest updatesGet ourbreaking news email,free appordaily news podcastThu 23 Jul 2026 11.00 EDTLast modified on Thu 23 Jul 2026 17.12 EDTShareThere’s apparently only one thing to blame for Pauline Hanson’s string of bad headlines in recent weeks, from her decision to pal around with the far-right convicted criminal Tommy Robinson to herdescription of domestic violenceon Wednesday as a “two-way street”.According to theOne Nationleader, it’s of course not Hanson herself who is to blame.No – it is the media’s fault.On Thursday she posted audio of the domestic violence exchange and claimed “there is a concerted effort by some in the media to constantly take my comments out of context”.The One Nation leader criticised “journalistic integrity” and drew links tojob cuts at the Nine newspapers.“I’m growing less tolerant of many journalists within the MSM [mainstream media] and so too are the public,” she wrote.Not content with aping Donald Trump’s policies andslogan, this week shows Hanson and One Nation are now leaning further than ever into his fallback method of blaming journalists when the consequences of their own remarks or actions spawn an entirely predictable controversy.Pauline Hanson shares Seven pitch for Spotlight special that says program would highlight ‘uncontrolled immigration’Read moreThis is hardly new; Hanson herself has “banned” Guardian Australia, along with our fellow travellers at the ABC and others, from her public appearances. Media alerts, press releases and even returned phone calls used to come our way, but don’t any more. In a trend evident since Hanson first burst on to the national political scene in 1996, One Nation has blamed the media for reporting on its slip-ups, instead of turning that gaze inward.It works for Hanson politically to pick fights with the “mainstream media”, positioning her as an anti-establishment outsider fighting against the system.But Hanson, on current figures, could be the official opposition leader after the next election; that, undeniably, demands the same level of scrutiny that the media gives to Labor or Liberal leaders.This predictable cycle, of controversy followed by blame-shifting, has come into sharp focus in the past fortnight amid the rolling blunders emanating from Hanson’s European holiday.Hansonraising the end of the White Australia policywhen Robinson asked “how have you ended up with Pakistanis, Somalis, all of these African problems?” That was simply “fake news”, Hansonsaid, the fault of the “leftwing media and their false, clickbait stories”.Backlash over her decision to give Robinson a cosy interview, then accompany him on a UK road trip? Seven’s Spotlight was the one who pushed for it in the first place, according to Hanson, Barnaby Joyce and One Nation chief of staff James Ashby (Seven sources said the podcast was instigated by One Nation and that Hanson wasn’t forced to do anything).For good measure, as scrutiny grew on the Spotlight special – including her gushing “I love it” when asked about the concept of mass deportation – Hansonpublished what she said was the “pitch” document from Sevenproposing the documentary, insinuating she was the victim of a media stitch-up.In between, Malcolm Roberts accused media of“selective cherry picking and dishonest distortions”and seeking to“drag up nothing stories”when his own past comments – aboutany manner of conspiracy theories, from Covid to chemtrails, and his support of Vladimir Putin – resurfaced.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |WeekdaysSign up toAfternoon UpdateOur Australian afternoon update breaks down the key stories of the day, telling you what’s happening and why it mattersPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionBut at each point it is unclear exactly what One Nation’s complaints were – other than that they didn’t like the headlines. Few specific rebuttals were given, other than tone. The list of controversies are hardly confected outrages; they are obvious questions about things Hanson or her colleagues have said and done in public.At various stages, Hanson has accused journalists of taking her out of context, reposting her own comments “so you don’t get it twisted by the media or the Labor Party”. But in the media articles she complained about, those comments had already been reproduced at length and in context.On Wednesday afternoon and Thursday morning, as criticism grew over her domestic violence comments, she posted first a written excerpt and then a full audio clip of the interview, claiming media had “decided to use only a few quotes”.But, in fact, the quotes she sought to highlight had already been published at length by the Guardian, the ABC, the Nine newspapers, News.com.au and more.Pauline Hanson says domestic violence prevents police from dealing with ‘real crime’ in unearthed interviewRead moreHanson claimed it was “untrue” that she had downplayed domestic violence. Ashby on Sky accused media of taking a “five-second clip” rather than a “30-second clip”.But it is a fact that experts including the Women’s and Girls’ Emergency Centre have called out Hanson’s rhetoric, with its chief executive, Nicole Yade, branding the comments “vile and shameful”.Hanson has spoken of being the prime minister one day and of dragging One Nation from the fringes to become a mainstream party. But the perks of popularity also come with responsibilities – including being responsible for your actions and the words that come out of your own mouth.Explore more on these topicsPauline HansonOne NationAustralian politicsAustralian mediaanalysisShareReuse this content

title: Personal and banking details among customer data stolen in Origin Energy hack | Hacking | The Guardian
description: Hackers access Australian customers’ names, addresses, dates of birth, phone numbers and some bank account details, company says
Author: Luca Ittimani
Headline: Hackers access Australian customers’ names, addresses, dates of birth, phone numbers and some bank account details, company says
Content: Origin Energy has confirmed customers’ names, addresses, dates of birth, phone numbers and some bank account details are among the personal data stolen by hackers.Photograph: Jason Reed/ReutersView image in fullscreenOrigin Energy has confirmed customers’ names, addresses, dates of birth, phone numbers and some bank account details are among the personal data stolen by hackers.Photograph: Jason Reed/ReutersHackingPersonal and banking details among customer data stolen in Origin Energy hackHackers access Australian customers’ names, addresses, dates of birth, phone numbers and some bank account details, company saysFollow our Australia news live blog for latest updatesGet ourbreaking news email,free appordaily news podcastLuca IttimaniThu 23 Jul 2026 05.55 EDTFirst published on Thu 23 Jul 2026 03.18 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleOriginEnergycustomers’ addresses, phone numbers and partial bank account data have been accessed in a hack, the company has confirmed.The firm has 4.8m customer accounts in Australia, providing electricity, fossil gas, LPG and internet services to homes and businesses.Origin has yet to confirm which, and how many, customers had been affected. In a statement to the ASX on Thursday, it said it would tell customers once it confirmed whether they had been affected.A person claiming to be the hacker has reportedly contacted media outlets with unverified claims that 2 million customers’ details were accessed.Sign up for the Breaking News Australia emailOrigin said data may include customers’ names, addresses, dates of birth, phone numbers and Origin account information, as well as the last four digits of a credit card, or the last three digits of a bank account.The company said incomplete credit card or bank account information could not be used to make purchases or access accounts.Origin first revealed the hack in a statement yesterday, saying it believed credit card or bank details had not been accessed. The company has not detailed how the hack occurred.Origin’s chief executive, Frank Calabria, said the company was securing its systems and ensuring there was no further unauthorised access, working with independent cyber experts and authorities.“I’m sorry this has happened,” Calabria said.“Customers trust Origin with their information, and I apologise for the impact this may cause.”Experts have warned customers could be vulnerable if the data is leaked and used for identity theft, or by scammers tricking people into believing they are representatives of real businesses.Rumpa Dasgupta, a lecturer in cybersecurity at La Trobe University, said personalised records could be misused for physical robberies.“In the wrong hands, this information could be exploited not only for highly targeted phishing campaigns but also to support physical crimes such as burglary by identifying vulnerable properties,” Dasgupta said.Origin said the Australian Cyber Security Centre, the Australian federal police and the Office of the Australian Information Commissioner (OIAC) were all investigating.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |When neededSign up toBreaking News AustraliaGet the most important news as it breaksPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionThe National Office of Cyber Security is leading the government’s response.The AFP declined to comment on whether it had identified or communicated with Origin’s hackers.The Australian reported it was first contacted by a person claiming to have hacked Origin on Tuesday, after which the newspaper alerted Origin, which made a statement on Wednesday.An Origin spokesperson said the company had moved immediately to update the ASX as soon as it was aware of a potential incident.The OIAC reportedit received 1,205 data breach notifications in 2025, of which 716 were related to malicious or criminal activity.Among the hacks was a leak of 5 million Qantascustomers’ informationin October, which prompted warnings scammers could cold call leaked phone numbers.The federal privacy commissioner last week found Qantas did not make any omissions or failings in breach of the Privacy Act, in relation to the hack. The Australian federal police are investigating.Explore more on these topicsHackingData and computer securityEnergynewsShareReuse this content

title: Ousted Ukrainian defence minister refuses any other role in Zelenskyy’s government – as it happened | Ukraine | The Guardian
description: Mykhailo Fedorov says only in his former post could he ‘combat procurement corruption and complete the army’s transformation’
Author: Jakub Krupa
Headline: Mykhailo Fedorov says only in his former post could he ‘combat procurement corruption and complete the army’s transformation’
Content: 11.10 EDTOusted defence minister Fedorov rejects suggestions of any other job in Zelenskyy's governmentMeanwhile,Ukraine’s Volodymyr Zelenskyy remains under pressure to restore ousted defence minister, Mykhailo Fedorov, after he told reporters he would not accept any other role in the government– despite alternatives put forward by the president (14:01).Former Ukrainian defence minister Mykhailo Fedorov summarizes his work in office during a press conference in Kyiv last week.Photograph: Global Images Ukraine/Getty Images“No other role carries the actual authority to ‌combat procurement corruption, complete the ​army’s transformation,plan and execute asymmetric operations ⁠against the enemy,” ​Fedorov, whose dismissal ​last ​week rare ​wartime ‌street ​protests, ​said in a statement sent to reporters and quoted by Reuters.Fedorov’s supporters vowed to continue rare wartime street protestsuntil he gets restored to his original position as defence minister.1:32Why Ukrainians are protesting against Zelenskyy's dismissal of defence minister – videoShareUpdated at11.23 EDT

title: Owner of Viking ship used in The Odyssey says studio has not paid bill for damage  | Sweden | The Guardian
description: Swedish nonprofit alleges cost to repair replica was $6,000 but Universal – which made $264m on film’s opening weekend – has not stumped up
Author: Miranda Bryant
Headline: Swedish nonprofit alleges cost to repair replica was $6,000 but Universal – which made $264m on film’s opening weekend – has not stumped up
Content: The Glad av Gillberga was used as one of the escort vessels in the flotilla, which are thought to have been similar to Nordic Viking ships.Photograph: GCMC/Peter OlaussonView image in fullscreenThe Glad av Gillberga was used as one of the escort vessels in the flotilla, which are thought to have been similar to Nordic Viking ships.Photograph: GCMC/Peter OlaussonSwedenOwner of Viking ship used in The Odyssey says studio has not paid bill for damageSwedish nonprofit alleges cost to repair replica was $6,000 but Universal – which made $264m on film’s opening weekend – has not stumped upMiranda BryantNordic correspondentThu 23 Jul 2026 11.28 EDTLast modified on Thu 23 Jul 2026 21.30 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleThe Swedish owners of a Viking longship replica that features in the Hollywood blockbusterThe Odysseysay they feel “forgotten” after film-makers allegedly returned the vessel damaged and failed to foot the bill for repairs.In nearly three decades at sea, the warship Glad av Gillberga – based on a wreck from around 1040 found in Denmark’s Roskilde fjord – has successfully sailed the North Sea, the Baltic and even voyaged to Canada.But after six months in the hands of Universal, which according to its owners promised to cover the cost of any repairs, it allegedly sustained considerable damage including to the stern and the mast.Vikingaleden, a nonprofit Viking centre in Värmland, westernSweden, said that when it invoiced Universal for the materials used to repair their ship (they did not charge for their volunteer labour), no payment arrived.View image in fullscreenMatt Damon stars as Odysseus in The Odyssey.Photograph: Melinda Sue Gordon/Universal PicturesA year later, while the Christopher Nolan epic starring Matt Damon, Tom Holland, Anne Hathaway and Zendayastorms global box offices, taking $264m (£196m) in its opening weekend alone, it says it is still waiting for its $6,000 payment.Peter Olausson, chair of the Vikingaleden association, said that although he felt proud that the ship was featured in the film, that feeling had been tinged with injustice after the film giant’s alleged failure to cover the cost of damages.Some of the damage allegedly caused to the boat’s woodwork.Photograph: SVT“We are only charging for the material cost, not the labour,” he said. “Of course Universal can afford this $6,000. We feel forgotten.”The ship is now back in action but Olausson accused Universal of going back on their word. “The agreement was that we would fix it and get financially reimbursed.”Olausson said that to Universal, the outstanding sum would be comparatively small, but for the 200-member association that runs the Viking “village” and museum, it is a significant amount.Paint damage allegedly caused to the boat.Photograph: SVTThecritically acclaimed film, which reportedly cost $250m to make, is based on Homer’s ancient Greek epic poem about the king of Ithaca, Odysseus, on a 10-year sea journey home from the Trojan war.The Glad av Gillberga was used as one of the escort vessels in the flotilla, which are thought to have been similar to Nordic Viking ships.The main ship, Odysseus’s boat, was the 35-metre-long Norwegian replica Draken Harald Hårfagre.Its Swedish captain, Björn Ahlander, said life on the ships filming for six months around Greek and Italian islands was not easy for the 30-person crew. He told the broadcasterSVT: “We rebuilt the ship into an ancient Greek ship. We removed the tents and all the modern things and had an open deck that we lived on during this time. It was quite tough.”A Universal spokesperson said: “Our records show that all invoices relating to the Glad av Gillberga were paid in full, including repairs. The studio and the production have contacted the association to clear up any misunderstanding.”Explore more on these topicsSwedenThe OdysseyEuropeChristopher NolannewsShareReuse this content

title: Murdoch and Musk in the crosshairs at Venice film festival | Venice film festival | The Guardian
description: Danny Boyle’s drama about the acquisition of the Sun and a four-hour Elon Musk documentary will premiere alongside new films from Martin McDonagh and Werner Herzog – but only one female director has a movie in competition
Author: Philip Oltermann
Headline: Danny Boyle’s drama about the acquisition of the Sun and a four-hour Elon Musk documentary will premiere alongside new films from Martin McDonagh and Werner Herzog – but only one female director has a movie in competition
Content: Paper tiger … Jack O'Connell in Ink by Danny Boyle, the opening film of this year’s Venice film festival.Photograph: StudiocanalView image in fullscreenPaper tiger … Jack O'Connell in Ink by Danny Boyle, the opening film of this year’s Venice film festival.Photograph: StudiocanalVenice film festivalMurdoch and Musk in the crosshairs at Venice film festivalDanny Boyle’s drama about the acquisition of the Sun and a four-hour Elon Musk documentary will premiere alongside new films from Martin McDonagh and Werner Herzog – but only one female director has a movie in competitionPhilip OltermannEuropean culture editorThu 23 Jul 2026 10.58 EDTLast modified on Thu 23 Jul 2026 21.30 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleTheVenice film festivalpromises to take a swing at media moguls of the print and digital era this year, with Trainspotting director Danny Boyle’s new film about Rupert Murdoch’s acquisition of the Sun and a new documentary about Elon Musk included in the lineup of the films for the festival’s 83rd edition.In Boyle’s Ink, which will open the festival on the Lido on 2 September, Australian actor Guy Pearce plays compatriot newspaper tycoon Murdoch, whose purchase of the Sun in 1969 sensationalised the media landscape and transformed public life in Britain. The script is written by Olivier award-winning playwright and screenwriter James Graham (Dear England, Brexit: The Uncivil War).Oscar-winning veteran film-makerAlex Gibneyprofiles the wealthiest person in the world, rightwing agitator and owner of social network X in his four-hour documentary Musk, which will be screened out of competition in Venice.In recent years, Venice has sought to profile itself as the glitziest and starriest of the big three European film festivals when compared with artistically challenging Cannes and politically charged Berlin.This year’s lineup, presented by long-running artistic director Alberto Barbera this week, will still draw major stars to Venice’s waterways.View image in fullscreenSam Rockwell (left) and John Malkovich in Wild Horse Nine.Photograph: Diego Araya Corvalán/Searchlight Pictures/APBritish-Irish director Martin McDonagh will showcase his new film Wild Horse Nine, in which John Malkovich and Sam Rockwell are two unlikely CIA agents in action in Chile on the eve of the coup led by Augusto Pinochet. Steve Buscemi, Tom Waits and Parker Posey also star in the awaited return of the director of In Bruges, Three Billboards Outside Ebbing, Missouri and The Banshees of Inisherin.Robert Pattinson will grace the Lido as the lead in A24’s Primetime, a psychological thriller inspired by the US reality TV show To Catch a Predator, directed by Lance Oppenheim.Real-life spouses Penélope Cruz and Javier Bardem star in French playwright-director Florian Zeller’s Bunker, in which a publisher-architect couple move to London and are invited by a millionaire to design a building that could withstand a nuclear bomb.Real-life sisters Kate and Rooney Mara star in Bucking Fastard, the first dramatic feature film in seven years by German cult director Werner Herzog. The film was originally announced as a biopic of Freda and Greta Chaplin, twins from York who spoke and moved in unison and gained notoriety after a brush with the law. It seems to have since veered off into a direction more familiar from the maker of Aguirre, the Wrath of God and Fitzcarraldo, centred around a mission to dig a tunnel through a mountain range “in search of an imaginary land”.Barbera said that Liam and Noel Gallagher had confirmed their attendance at the festival, for the premiere of Oasis reunion documentary Don’t Look Back in Anger, while Russell Crowe is expected to attend a screening of What Love Builds, a feature-length documentary about his band Indoor Garden Party.But there is some more serious fare on the Lido this year. Russian-born film-maker Ilya Khrzhanovsky will show the latest film to emerge out of hisDar project, likened to a Stalinist Truman Show and inspired by a Soviet research institute that contributed to the creation of the first atomic bomb.Ukrainian director Sergei Loznitsa will bring his documentary Imperium, about a film that was commissioned by the Italian Communist party to glorify the triumph of the Soviet Union but ended up revealing its contradictions.Laura Poitras’ latest documentary They’re Here, made with Rachel Mueller, dives into ICE’s intervention in Minneapolis and the killing of Renée Good, with some previously undisclosed footage.Tragicomedies such as Caro Diario and The Son’s Room have madeNanni Morettione of Italy’s most revered film-makers, but for the last two decades he has preferred to show his new works at Cannes rather than Venice. Showing in competition this year, Succederà Questa Notte (It Will Happen Tonight) will be Moretti’s first film in Venice since 1989.After Tunisian director Kaouther Ben Hania made a mark on last year’s festival with The Voice of Hind Rajab, there is a dramatic drop in female directors in this year’s competition. After six female directors competed for the Golden Lion in 2025, Danish-Egyptian director May El-Toukhy’s drama Woman Unknown is the only female-directed film among the 20 titles in the main competition.Venice 2026 official selection: the full listInkDanny BoyleCompanyCasey AffleckA Bit of LightAli AsgariRitorno a Buenos AiresMarco BechisUn Bon Petit SoldatStéphane BrizéIl Fuoco Che Ti title: Risk of Parkinson’s disease may increase with prolonged exposure to road traffic noise | Parkinson's disease | The Guardian
description: Pioneering study linking noise pollution to Parkinson’s finds 3% increase in risk for every 11.5dB rise in noise levels
Author: Guardian staff reporter
Headline: Pioneering study linking noise pollution to Parkinson’s finds 3% increase in risk for every 11.5dB rise in noise levels
Content: Research suggests a quiet or less exposed part of the home could mitigate the association between road traffic noise and risk of Parkinson’s disease.Photograph: georgeclerk/Getty ImagesView image in fullscreenResearch suggests a quiet or less exposed part of the home could mitigate the association between road traffic noise and risk of Parkinson’s disease.Photograph: georgeclerk/Getty ImagesParkinson's diseaseRisk of Parkinson’s disease may increase with prolonged exposure to road traffic noisePioneering study linking noise pollution to Parkinson’s finds 3% increase in risk for every 11.5dB rise in noise levelsUshika KiddThu 23 Jul 2026 09.24 EDTFirst published on Thu 23 Jul 2026 08.56 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleLong-term exposure to road traffic noise is associated with a higher risk of Parkinson’s disease, the biggest study of its kind has found.Parkinson’s disease is a progressive disorder in which parts of the brain become damaged over time, affecting movement and balance.Existing research has found potential biological pathways that link noise exposure to Parkinson’s disease, largely due to stress responses and disrupted sleep. The researchers modelled noise exposure at the most and least exposed exterior of the residence of each participant and calculated the difference in noise levels.AI-enhanced blood test may detect Parkinson’s years before onsetRead moreThe magnitude of the effect was modest but consistent; at the most exposed facade, for every 11.5dB rise in noise level, the risk of Parkinson’s disease rose by 3% over the study period.Having a quiet part of the home may mitigate the association between exposure to road traffic noise and higher risk of Parkinson’s disease, according to the findings.Thestudy, published in Jama Neurology, included 3.1 million Danish participants aged 40 and over, and followed them for 18 years. It was established using nationwide health register data, making it the largest study on road traffic noise and Parkinson’s disease.Previous researchlinked the rise in neurological disorders, including Parkinson’s disease, with exposure to environmental toxins. Environmental risk factors such as air pollution, microplastics and pesticides have become the main focus of prevention strategies.The study is one of the first to make the link to noise pollution. Chronic noise exposure exceeding World Health Organization thresholds affects nearly one-third of Europeans. Thomas Münzel, one of the authors of the study, said environmental noise is “still a young entrant in the Parkinson’s disease risk-factor literature”.Münzel said the study’s findings should move noise from a speculative to a credible risk factor for Parkinson’s disease, prompting further research into the topic.View image in fullscreenA traffic jam passing through a residential area.Photograph: Graeme Robertson/The GuardianThe researchers took air pollution into account but lacked information on people’s lifestyles. They argue, however, that lifestyle factors would not have changed the findings, because they saw a consistent association with Parkinson’s regardless of people’s education and income.Chronically disturbed sleep from noise pollution is thought to cause increased levels of the stress hormone cortisol. Anna Hansell, a professor of environmental epidemiology at the University of Leicester, who was not involved in the study, said noise was “more likely to be a general stressor” rather than a specific environmental risk factor for Parkinson’s disease, and the nature of the study design “doesn’t ‘prove’ causation”.Dr Peter Chan, a senior researcher in environmental epidemiology at Nuffield Department of PopulationHealth, who was not involved in the study, said: “The study of [difference in noise levels] is interesting as it highlights a dimension of noise issue that noise at home is not uniform and people can mitigate by moving to different rooms.” However, he added the findings were not unexpected.He said: “We know that many potential risk factors of Parkinson’s disease are also linked to low socioeconomic status, and it is very hard to disentangle whether the increased risk with noise is totally due to noise itself or something else.”John Stewart, the chair of the UK Noise Association who was not involved in the study, said: “This research joins a growing list of studies which show a connection between traffic noise and people’s physical and mental health. It shows the link is strongest where the noise is loudest. In practice, this means main roads.“That suggests governments and local authorities should focus their efforts on cutting main road noise.”Explore more on these topicsParkinson's diseaseHealthMental healthResearchMedical researchDenmarkRoad transportnewsShareReuse this content

title: UK ‘ready to defend itself’ after Iranian threat over US bases | US-Israel war on Iran | The Guardian
description: IRGC claims base in south-west England was used by US to launch bombing missions, making it ‘legitimate target’
Author: Hayden Vernon
Headline: IRGC claims base in south-west England was used by US to launch bombing missions, making it ‘legitimate target’
Content: An American B-1 bomber at RAF Fairfield in Gloucestershire, south-west England. The UK Ministry of Defence has not commented on whether the US used the base for operations against Iran this week.Photograph: Andrew Fox/The GuardianView image in fullscreenAn American B-1 bomber at RAF Fairfield in Gloucestershire, south-west England. The UK Ministry of Defence has not commented on whether the US used the base for operations against Iran this week.Photograph: Andrew Fox/The GuardianUS-Israel war on IranUK ‘ready to defend itself’ after Iranian threat over US basesIRGC claims base in south-west England was used by US to launch bombing missions, making it ‘legitimate target’Hayden VernonThu 23 Jul 2026 18.41 EDTLast modified on Thu 23 Jul 2026 22.32 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleThe government has said the UK is “ready to defend itself” after Iran’s military warned that any bases being used by the US were “legitimate targets”.The UK has allowed the US to launch “defensive” operations from British bases hosting American planes since the start of its war on Iran but has refused to help in offensive operations.That policy has not been altered by the new prime minister, Andy Burnham, who was notified last week that a decision had been made to extend the agreement with the US.Iran’s Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps said on Thursday that “any base used for aggression against Iranian territory constitutes a legitimate target for our forces”.In a statement published on the IRGC-linked Tasnim news agency, the group said American bombing missions had been launched from RAF Fairford inGloucestershiretwo days earlier.The statement came after the US carried out a 12th consecutive night of strikes against targets in Iran. The Ministry of Defence has not commented on whether Fairford was used by the US for operations against Iran this week.The IRGC also accused the “British monarchy regime” of causing being the “primary cause of hardships in our region with a black record of partitioning Islamic nations, widespread massacres, imposing despotic regimes, and organising the occupation of Palestine”.It warned the government “not to weigh down its record any further”.A spokesperson for the British government said: “Our armed forces are ready to keep ⁠the United Kingdom safe from any kind of attacks, whether it’s on our soil ⁠or from abroad. The UK stands ready 24/7 to defend itself.“This includes through ‌operating a layered approach to air and missile defence, ‌provided by Royal Navy, British Army andRoyal Air Forceassets equipped with a range of advanced capabilities, working closely with our Nato allies.“We are committed to defending our people, our interests and our allies, acting in accordance with international law and not getting drawn into the wider conflict.”On 1 March a dronestruck the RAF base at Akrotiriin Cyprus, hitting a hangar, and prompting a partial evacuation of the facility. On 19 March, Iranfired two ballistic missiles at the UK’s Diego Garcia basein the Chagos Islands. One reportedly failed mid-flight, while the other was shot down by a US airship.Iran has launched strikes against countries in the Gulf that houseUS militarybases, including Bahrain, the United Arab Emirates and Kuwait, killing American service personnel in some of the attacks. Donald Trump said Iran would “pay a big price” for killing US troops.On Thursday afternoon, the US presidentsaid he was considering ordering a “massive attack” on Iran. “I am close to making a decision,” he told Axios. “We’re all set for it.” He said he believed Iran’s leaders “want to negotiate”, but aren’t yet ready to make a deal.In another sign of the conflict potentially escalating, Yemen’s Iran-backed Houthi rebelssaid they attacked two Saudi oil tankers in the Red Seaon Thursday, as oiltopped $100 a barrel.Explore more on these topicsUS-Israel war on IranUS militaryRoyal Air ForceMilitaryIranDefence policyMiddle East and north AfricanewsShareReuse this content

title: Donald Trump says decision on ‘massive attack’ against Iran is close | US-Israel war on Iran | The Guardian
description: President’s remarks follow Houthi strikes on Saudi tankers in the Red Sea and oil prices rising past $100 a barrel again
Author: Peter Beaumont
Headline: President’s remarks follow Houthi strikes on Saudi tankers in the Red Sea and oil prices rising past $100 a barrel again
Content: Donald Trump said: ‘I am considering a massive attack. Bigger than ever before.’Photograph: Julia Demaree Nikhinson/APView image in fullscreenDonald Trump said: ‘I am considering a massive attack. Bigger than ever before.’Photograph: Julia Demaree Nikhinson/APUS-Israel war on IranDonald Trump says decision on ‘massive attack’ against Iran is closePresident’s remarks follow Houthi strikes on Saudi tankers in the Red Sea and oil prices rising past $100 a barrel againPeter BeaumontSenior international correspondentThu 23 Jul 2026 12.58 EDTLast modified on Thu 23 Jul 2026 22.42 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleDonald Trump has said he is close to deciding whether to launch “a massive attack” against Iran, on a scale larger than strikes already witnessed during the months-long conflict with Tehran.Speaking to the Axios news site, the US president said: “I am considering a massive attack. Bigger than ever before. I am close to making a decision. We are all set for it.”Trump made his remarks on Thursday asoil prices once again surged past the symbolic $100for a barrel of Brent crude, the international benchmark, and amid indications the price could go higher still.He acknowledged that such a decision would have consequences but said he had not yet made a final determination. Israel “would join in two minutes if I ask them to”, he added, before saying: “We don’t need anybody.”Hours later the US carried out a 13th consecutive night of strikes onIranlasting more ⁠than two ​hours. Iranian state media reported explosions along the strait of Hormuz in Qeshm and Bandar Abbas, as well as to the north-west of the country and in Tehran.The strikes however appeared to broadly match the scope and intensity of those the US has carried throughout the most recent escalation of violence.Trump’s comments on Thursday followed the earlier threat of “major military punishment” against Iran and the Houthis, after the Iran-backedYemeni militia’s attacks on two Saudi Arabian oil tankersin the Red Sea.Underlining the sense of a region increasingly approaching an abyss, the UN secretary general, António Guterres, told the security council that the Middle East was being pushed to the “edge of the unimaginable” amid mounting fears over the trajectory of the conflict.“The situation is getting out of control. It is teetering on the edge of the unimaginable. The region is being pulled into an ever-widening circle of confrontation. One crisis feeds another. One escalation triggers the next,” Guterres told a security council meeting.“As this dynamic spreads, the political objectives are becoming increasingly obscured by the confrontation itself.”The UK announced it was temporarilywithdrawing diplomatic staff from Iranon Wednesday, and the German military announced on Thursday that it would withdraw two vessels from the Red Sea.And as Israeli cities opened bomb shelters, the US state department warned Americans living in the region that flights may be cancelled.In a separate development, Trump clarified on Thursday that an announced agreement thatthe US would help Saudi Arabia with its civilian nuclear programmewould require the kingdom to normalise relations with Israel through the Abraham Accords, a move Saudi Arabia has long resisted until there is demonstrable movement on Palestinian rights and statehood.It emerged later that Trump had not spoken toSaudi Arabiaabout the new condition.The Houthi attacks threatened the safety of a second major maritime route connecting the Suez canal to the Red Sea and Gulf of Aden. They also came amid reports that Iran had sent military advisers and drone parts to theHouthis10 days ago.Quoting four sources, including two Iranians, Reuters reported on Thursday that Iran had transferred ​Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps personnel and military-related equipment on a flight from Tehran toYemenon 13 July, as Tehran was calling for the Houthis to blockade the Bab el-Mandeb strait.Ships had begun turning back from sailing through the narrow strait this week after the Houthis announced a blockade of Saudi ports, most notably the Red Sea port of Yanbu, which Riyadh has been using to ship oil to Asia during the conflict that has all but halted shipping through the strait of Hormuz.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |Every weekdaySign up toFirst ThingOur US morning briefing breaks down the key stories of the day, telling you what’s happening and why it mattersPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionHas Trump dragged the US into a forever war in Iran? | Mohamad BazziRead moreThe maritime data and analysis firm Lloyd’s List Intelligence said the Houthi attacks put Saudi oil shipments from Yanbu at risk and presented a “double whammy” on top of the Hormuz disruptions, , adding that the Houthi threat “raises questions on the viability of this route”.Trump wrote on social media: “If [the Houthis] do this again, the US will hold Iran responsible, in that the Houthis are a Surrogate and/or Proxy of Iran, and major military punishment will be inflicted upon Iran and, of course, the Houthis, themselves.”The Houthis’ Saba news agency said the rebels had struck two tankers, the Encelia and the Layla, causing fires on both. There were no reports of casualties in their first reported attack on a vessel since they announced the blockade of Saudi-linked shipping.The Bab el-Mandeb is a vital shipping chokepoint connecting the Red Sea to the Gulf of Aden. About 12% of the world’s trade, including a fourth of global container traffic, passes through there, moving between Europe and Asia via the Suez canal at the northern end of the Red Sea.Trump has been escalating his rhetoric against Iran in recent days, hinting the US is prepared to bomb an underground tunnel complex at the “Pickaxe mountain” site where Israel alleges Tehran has hidden centrifuges used in uranium enrichment.As Trump has struggled to get a grip on the spiralling crisis, during which Iran has occasionally launched lethal strikes against US bases and Washington’s Gulf allies, he appears to have been sucked into an “escalation trap”, threatening ever more violent retaliation.On Wednesday, Trump said the US would destroy one bridge or power plant every time Iran shot at a ship in the strait.The US secretary of state, Marco Rubio, said Trump was seeking a “head for an eye” after the recent deaths of several US service members in an Iranian strike on the Muwaffaq Salti airbase in Jordan.However, speaking on the outskirts of the Asean conference in Manila, Rubio said Iran was “begging for a deal”, and insisted “they need to come to their senses” and that they will “pay a very heavy price for the things they are doing” – all claims US officials have made before.With Trump’s poll ratings in freefall, and his war against Iran deeply unpopular at home, the president and his administration are running short of time before the autumn midterm elections to find a resolution to the conflict on Washington’s terms.Underlining Trump’s growing political problems, the US House of Representatives narrowlypassed a resolutionon Thursday to halt US military action in Iran, the second such warning to the president.Explore more on these topicsUS-Israel war on IranHouthisIranDonald TrumpYemenMiddle East and north AfricaTrump administrationnewsShareReuse this content

title: Israeli security minister joins Jewish extremists as they storm al-Aqsa mosque compound | Palestine | The Guardian
description: Far-right minister Itamar Ben-Gvir was among thousands who gathered at Jerusalem’s most sensitive holy site
Author: Emma Graham-Harrison
Headline: Far-right minister Itamar Ben-Gvir was among thousands who gathered at Jerusalem’s most sensitive holy site
Content: Ben-Gvir in front of al-Aqsa mosque in the latest of his inflammatory visits to the compound.Photograph: Itamar Ben Gvir/Telegram/ReutersView image in fullscreenBen-Gvir in front of al-Aqsa mosque in the latest of his inflammatory visits to the compound.Photograph: Itamar Ben Gvir/Telegram/ReutersPalestineIsraeli security minister joins Jewish extremists as they storm al-Aqsa mosque compoundFar-right minister Itamar Ben-Gvir was among thousands who gathered at Jerusalem’s most sensitive holy siteEmma Graham-HarrisonThu 23 Jul 2026 10.33 EDTLast modified on Thu 23 Jul 2026 21.30 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleIsrael’s far-right minister of national security, Itamar Ben-Gvir, joined thousands of Jewish extremists who stormed Jerusalem’s most sensitive holy site on Thursday.Escorted by armed police, they prayed, waved the Israeli flag and sang the national anthem in a provocative display of political power.Under a fragile, decades-old agreement known as the status quo, non-Muslim prayer is banned at the site, known to Muslims as al-Haram al-Sharif compound and to Jews as the Temple Mount.Ben-Gvir has eroded the agreement in recent years through a series ofinflammatory visits to the compound, and unilateral changes to rules for Jewish visitors, including allowing them to bring prayers and printed religious material to the site.He installed an ideological ally as the head of the Jerusalem police force in January, smoothing the path to greater changes.In a statement, the Palestinian ministry of religious endowments and affairs said: “These provocative practices, emanating from the highest political levels of the occupying government, seek to ignite a religious war throughout the entire region.”Israelis prayed on Thursday in parts of the compound close to al-Aqsa mosque and the seventh-century Dome of the Rock shrine.Likud lawmaker Amit Halevi joined a group that raised Israeli flags and sang the national anthem, Haaretz reported, citing Israeli “Temple Mount” activists pushing for Jewish worship at the site.In a social media post, Ben-Gvir wrote “we see enormous progress here”, and Jews praying there “feel like the owners”.He has previously said he wanted to raise the Israeli flag at the compound and build a synagogue there.Thursday is the Jewish holy day of Tisha B’Av, marking the destruction at the same site of the 10th-century BC first temple and the second temple, which was destroyed by the Romans in AD70.Map of Old City showing al-Aqsa mosqueChanges in the status quo agreement have historically driven conflict in Jerusalem and Palestine, with repercussions across the world. A visit in 2000 by the then Israeli opposition leader,Ariel Sharon,ignited the second Palestinian intifada, which lasted five years, and Hamas gave the name “al-Aqsa flood” to its attack on Israel in October 2023 that killed 1,200 Israelis and triggered the Gaza war, claiming it was provoked by Israeli violations at the Jerusalem mosque.The prayers outside al-Aqsa and the Dome of the Rock are a “dangerous provocation” by extremists seeking to ignite a religious war, the Israeli activist group Peace Now said in a post on X. “This directly violates the fragile status quo, which bars non-Muslim prayer at the holy site.”‘Al-Aqsa is a detonator’: six-decade agreement on prayer at Jerusalem holy site collapsesRead moreThe Jordanian foreign ministry strongly condemned the storming and called on the international community “to take a firm stance obliging Israel, as the occupying power, to halt its ongoing violations against the Islamic and Christian sanctities in Jerusalem”.The Islamic Waqf Department, which officially manages the compound, said 3,228 Israelis entered the compound during the morning period, Al Jazeera reported. Thousands more are expected in the afternoonEarlier this year, the prime minister, Benjamin Netanyahu,publicly backed Jewish prayer at the site, claiming changes pushed by Ben-Gvir were coordinated with him.Although his government has effectively torn up a key part of the fragile status quo, Netanyahu also insisted that Israel had not violated the agreement.Overnight, thousands of people marched through areas of old Jerusalem near the compound, and some attacked Palestinians and vandalised a Muslim cemetery near the Lions’ Gate entrance, Haaretz reported. Police arrested 16 Jewish suspects over the violence.Explore more on these topicsPalestineIsraelMiddle East and north AfricaBenjamin NetanyahunewsShareReuse this content

title: Oil passes $100 a barrel again and shares slide as Middle East conflict escalates | Oil | The Guardian
description: Sharp rise in oil price reflects fears Houthi militias could strangle Saudi exports as US-Iran fighting intensifies
Author: Jillian Ambrose
Headline: Sharp rise in oil price reflects fears Houthi militias could strangle Saudi exports as US-Iran fighting intensifies
Content: A tugboat guiding a ship. Houthis say they targeted two Saudi oil tankers in the Red Sea.Photograph: AFP/Getty ImagesView image in fullscreenA tugboat guiding a ship. Houthis say they targeted two Saudi oil tankers in the Red Sea.Photograph: AFP/Getty ImagesOilOil passes $100 a barrel again and shares slide as Middle East conflict escalatesSharp rise in oil price reflects fears Houthi militias could strangle Saudi exports as US-Iran fighting intensifiesHow the Bab al-Mandab blockade threat pushed oil back above $100Jillian Ambrose,Joanna PartridgeandRichard PartingtonThu 23 Jul 2026 09.37 EDTLast modified on Thu 23 Jul 2026 21.30 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleThe oil price broke above $100 a barrel for the first time in two months as a fresh escalation of the Middle East conflict threatens to compound disruption to global oil supplies.The benchmark oil price rose sharply on Thursday, from $95 the day before, amid fears that Yemen’s Houthi militia could strangle Saudi oil exports through the Red Sea while US-Iran tensions over oil flows through thestrait of Hormuzintensify.The disruption to two critical oil trade arteries has reignited fears that oil could climb to highs of $120 a barrel in a blow to households and the global economy after years of energy cost inflation.Share prices also fell on both sides of the Atlantic, reflecting the volatile situation in the Middle East and fears of a bubble in AI stocks – sending New York’s tech-heavy Nasdaq index down by more than 2%. Shares in Tesla crashed 12% after it reportedlower-than-expected profitsand amid wider worries about AI spending.The Middle East conflict widened as the Iranian-alignedHouthisclaimed responsibility for attacks on two Saudi Arabian oil tankers, the ‌Encelia and Layla, using a number of ballistic and cruise missiles as well as drones.The Houthi militias launched the attacks, which have left one vessel ablaze, after accusing the crews of violating a naval blockade imposed by the group in the Red Sea.The escalation marks a new front in the Gulf oil crisis, almost five months after Tehran’s effective block on the strait of Hormuz triggered what many feared would bring the greatest energy supply disruption in the history of the market.In the first weeks after the US-Israeli attacks onIran, oil prices surged past $100 a barrel and market observers predicted prices of up to $150 a barrel as oil flows through Hormuz came to a halt.The oil pricepeaked at $126 a barrelin April during the conflict, but fell back below $100 in late May and then to lows of $71 at the start of July amid hopes of a ceasefire in the region. The price began to climb again after thememorandum of understandingbetween the US and Iran fell apart and fresh hostilities broke out in the Gulf.The market has avoided runaway oil prices due to “cushioning factors” that have helped to cool the market, according to Fatih Birol, head of the International Energy Agency. But he warned this week that there was no room for complacency amid the escalation in hostilities.Fears about the mounting economic impact from the Iran war caused government borrowing costs for the world’s biggest economies to rise on Thursday as investors weighed up the risk that a surge in the oil price could rekindle global inflationary pressures.Investors dumped US, German and Japanese government bonds to push up the interest on government borrowing – or yields – across the globe. In the UK, the yield on 10-year government borrowing rose by about 0.1 percentage points to trade above 5.1% for the first since May.The latest market upheaval has come amid investor jitters in Britain over the tax and spending plans of the new prime minister, Andy Burnham, which some City traders have said could add to pressure on government borrowing costs.Chris Beauchamp, chief market analyst at the online trading platform IG, said: “Government bond yields continue to climb, spelling major trouble for developed economies and risking a repeat of the March/April 2025 market panic.“Volatility is surging and equities are moving further into the red as a return to full-blown conflict now looms, potentially drawing in Israel and more countries in the region.”Explore more on these topicsOilUS-Israel war on IranHouthisIranCommoditiesMiddle East and north AfricanewsShareReuse this content

title: ‘Cockroach’ protests: India’s Modi promises to punish officials over exam leaks | India | The Guardian
description: PM says ‘those who try to harm the future of our youth will not be spared’ as exam leaks scandal spirals into widespread anger
Author: Amrit Dhillon
Headline: PM says ‘those who try to harm the future of our youth will not be spared’ as exam leaks scandal spirals into widespread anger
Content: A Cockroach Janta party (CJP) supporter shouts at Jantar Mantar in Delhi on Thursday during the ongoing protest to demand the resignation of India's education minister, Dharmendra Pradhan.Photograph: Sajjad Hussain/AFP/Getty ImagesView image in fullscreenA Cockroach Janta party (CJP) supporter shouts at Jantar Mantar in Delhi on Thursday during the ongoing protest to demand the resignation of India's education minister, Dharmendra Pradhan.Photograph: Sajjad Hussain/AFP/Getty ImagesIndia‘Cockroach’ protests: India’s Modi promises to punish officials over exam leaksPM says ‘those who try to harm the future of our youth will not be spared’ as scandal spirals into widespread angerAmrit Dhillonin DelhiThu 23 Jul 2026 06.31 EDTFirst published on Thu 23 Jul 2026 01.49 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleNarendra Modi has vowed to set up “fast-track courts” to speed up the punishment of officials accused of leaking examination papers, as the Indian prime minister seeks to quell ayouth-led “cockroach”protest movement that has rocked Delhi and posed one of the biggest challenges to his government.Opposition parties shut down Indian parliament in solidarity with ‘cockroach’ protestsRead moreIn his first public remarks on the movement, Modi posted on X: “Nothing is more important than the welfare and future of our youth … Those who try to harm the future of our youth will not be spared.”The offer of faster justice than usual from India’s clogged judicial system, which can take decades to deliver a verdict, was swiftly rejected by the Cockroach Janta party (CJP), the satirical name the youth protest movement coined after India’s top judge called unemployed youths “cockroaches”.View image in fullscreenDemonstrators carrying the Indian national flag at a march in Patna.Photograph: Sachin Kumar/AFP/Getty ImagesSaurav Das, a CJP spokesperson, said: “It’s a Band-aid. That’s not giving us accountability. There have been zero convictions so far all these years, so this measure is of no use.” A core demand of the movement is the resignation of the education minister, Dharmendra Pradhan, as well as reform of the education system.An estimated 50,000 people protested in Delhi on Monday, after police denied them permission to march on parliament. Officers later mounted baton charges and fired teargas. By Wednesday, more than 1,000 people remained camped at the CJP’s protest site in centralDelhidespite the gruelling heat and humidity. Many had travelled long distances to be there.Renuka Chowdhury, an opposition MP with the Congress party, told the news channel India Today that no one could fathom why Modi would not sack Pradhan. “Honestly, is this one minister more valuable or more important than restoring harmony and order to the country?” he said.Modi’s statement follows signs the government may be softening its stance. Ministers have insisted they are willing to talk to the CJP but have refused to discuss Pradhan’s resignation.View image in fullscreenVolunteers distribute food received to protesters.Photograph: Vipin/APThe protest site at Jantar Mantar has drawn crowds of supporters eager to show their solidarity. Unable to fit inside the enclosed area, many have spilled on to the surrounding roads. Some organise donations of food and water sent by wellwishers, while others distribute meals – including to the police on duty – fan those wilting from the heat or collect litter.Young people across India have also rallied behind the CJP’s demands, with three consecutive days of demonstrations in Mumbai and solidarity protests in Chennai, Indore, Hyderabad and Patiala.At a press conference on Wednesday, the opposition leader Rahul Gandhi escalated his criticism, demanding that in addition to Pradhan resigning, Modi should apologise for failing to provide a fair education system.Gandhi said there had been 152 exam paper leaks across the country over the past decade without a single conviction.“Somebody, or a group of people, are destroying our education system, India’s most valuable asset, destroying the lives of our students. What does the government do? Zero convictions,” he said.The health minister, JP Nadda, accused Gandhi of exploiting the protests for political gain.View image in fullscreenDemonstrators hold anti-Modi placards during a protest.Photograph: Ashish Vaishnav/Sopa Images/Shutterstock“Your concern is not to improve the education system, enhance the quality of education or ensure justice for students. Instead, you are trying to use students’ issues and their demands for political mileage. You are trying to derive political benefit from them, and that is unfortunate,” he said.CJP leaders were deeply disappointed after Monday’s meeting with Nadda – the first in over 60 days – ended without a breakthrough. Its founder, Abhijeet Dipke, has since said that if ministers want to speak to them, they will have to come to the protest site and do so in public.Many in India are asking why Pradhan will not resign. Anish Gawande, an opposition politician, said Modi viewed backing down as a sign of weakness, particularly now that he no longer commands the parliamentary majority he enjoyed during his first two terms.“He is facing global headwinds that prevent him from executing any major reforms in agriculture, energy, the economy. The unemployment rate is climbing to levels not seen in three decades,” he said. “A show of weakness now will give an entry point for the opposition to move in and build a similar momentum around other issues […] Before you know it, things have spiralled out of control.”Explore more on these topicsIndiaNarendra ModiSouth and central AsiaDelhiProtestnewsShareReuse this content

title: Regional mayors to take key roles in UK policy decisions as part of No 10 North plans | Politics | The Guardian
description: Burnham says ‘no one in government loses when power is shared’ as he revives Brown-era national economic council
Author: Pippa Crerar
Headline: Burnham says ‘no one in government loses when power is shared’ as he revives Brown-era national economic council
