Content: Then mayor of Greater Manchester Andy Burnham (third from left) and other regional mayors outside No 10 in July 2024.Photograph: ZUMA Press, Inc./AlamyView image in fullscreenThen mayor of Greater Manchester Andy Burnham (third from left) and other regional mayors outside No 10 in July 2024.Photograph: ZUMA Press, Inc./AlamyPoliticsRegional mayors to take key roles in UK policy decisions as part of No 10 North plansBurnham says ‘no one in government loses when power is shared’ as he revives Brown-era national economic councilIt’s not just a No 10 in the north, it’s an engine for growth throughout the whole UK | Andy BurnhamPippa CrerarPolitical editorThu 23 Jul 2026 17.30 EDTLast modified on Thu 23 Jul 2026 18.59 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleRegional mayors are to be given a leading role in the UK’s economic policy,Andy Burnhamhas said, as he called the opening of No 10 North “the biggest rebalancing of power our country has seen”.The new prime minister has revived the national economic council [NEC], first set up byGordon Brownto put the government on a war footing during the 2008 financial crash, arguing that economic growth requires the same level of urgency, focus and collective action.In anarticle for the Guardian, he wrote: “When I entered Downing Street, I told staff that the era of fighting devolution is over. No one in government loses when power is shared.”Burnham will chair the inaugural meeting of the council on Friday, bringing together senior ministers and regional mayors to put devolution at the heart of his government’s mission.Burnham wrote: “Even though Whitehall departments have set up outposts, decision-making power has remained in London … This is what No 10 North is seeking to correct and, if we succeed, it could bring the much-needed sea change in the way Britain is run.“It is about driving growth from the most powerful office in the land and doing so in close alignment with the Treasury. It is about taking power down and placing it in the hands of people who can use it best.”View image in fullscreenHeron House, in central Manchester, will provide an interim HQ for Andy Burnham’s No 10 North.Photograph: Peter Byrne/PAAs he becomes the first British leader to run the government on a weekly basis fromManchester, Burnham will face calls to prove that the operation is not tokenistic.Earlier this week, he created a powerful new prime minister’s department taking in parts of the Cabinet Office, Treasury and housing ministry to become the “dominant driver of the UK’s economy” – an apparent tension with the No 10 North plan.In one of his first acts,Burnham set up the Office for the Prime Minister and the Cabinet, to be led by his ally and enforcerLouise Haigh, which will oversee No 10 North in Manchester, the No 10 operation in London, and parts of the Cabinet Office.Haigh, along with the education secretary, Lucy Powell, and the communities secretary, Angela Rayner – who both haveGreater Manchesterseats – are planning to spend some of their time working from No 10 North, while teams of civil servants are expected to be relocated there.With regional mayors invited to sit round the table, Downing Street argues that devolution will be cemented at the centre of economic decision-making, maintaining the prime minister’s pledge that decisions will be taken with local leaders, not just for them.View image in fullscreenAngela Rayner has returned to her previous role as the housing and communities secretary in Burnham’s cabinet.Photograph: Tolga Akmen/EPAOperating from Heron House in Manchester’s city centre, the home of GCHQ in the north-west, No 10 North is intended to bring together the levers of local economic growth and devolution under one roof for the first time, working from a single plan rather than competing ones.Burnham is expected to work from No 10 North each week so that decisions about the country’s economic future will no longer exclusively be made in Westminster, with other ministers also working regularly from the building.Dave Penman, the general secretary of the FDA union which represents senior civil servants, has welcomed the changes to the structure of government but cautioned that relocation was a big decision for staff.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |Every weekdaySign up toFirst EditionOur morning email breaks down the key stories of the day, telling you what’s happening and why it mattersPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionBusiness rates cut for pubs, clubs and music venues in England only ‘a small move’, say criticsRead moreAt least seven Greater Manchester Combined Authority employees, including Burnham’s deputy chief of staff, Caroline Simpson, and longstanding aide Kevin Lee, are already moving into the temporary building, while a brownfield site is developed. The Heron House office will be led by Sam Lister, a senior civil servant who has worked across several departments.The NEC’s attenders will change at each meeting, depending on the issues under consideration, bringing together business leaders, trade unions and other partners where their expertise can help drive decision-making.Under Gordon Brown, the body met twice a week and acted as the focus of his government’s effort to stave off recession, the global crisis in the financial markets and volatile prices, although there were concerns at the time that it could clip the wings of the Treasury.The new committee was quickly described as an economic version of the Cobra emergency committee. It included key figures such as the then chancellor Alistair Darling and former business secretary Peter Mandelson, and was instrumental in coordinating emergency banking sector bailouts.11:01Gary Lineker calls for Burnham to introduce wealth tax - The LatestIn his article, Burnham suggested that the Manchester department would be crucial to his aim to “drive reindustrialisation and growth across every postcode of the UK”.He went on: “It will remove barriers to regeneration, from council house building to public transport infrastructure. Crucially, No 10 North will bring the whole of Whitehall together behind places rather than standing in their way.“Our long-term ambition is clear: to achieve equivalent living standards across all the regions and nations of the UK.”Explore more on these topicsPoliticsAndy BurnhamManchesterGordon BrownLabourDevolutionLouise HaighnewsShareReuse this content

title: Kemi Badenoch ‘has to adapt’ her rhetoric to take on Andy Burnham | Kemi Badenoch | The Guardian
description: Conservative leader is becoming more confident and forging a message for her party, but faces a new opponent
Author: Peter Walker
Headline: Conservative leader is becoming more confident and forging a message for her party, but faces a new opponent
Content: Kemi Badenoch repeatedly questioned how Burnham would pay for his early flurry of policies in her speech in London on Thursday morning.Photograph: Stefan Rousseau/PAView image in fullscreenKemi Badenoch repeatedly questioned how Burnham would pay for his early flurry of policies in her speech in London on Thursday morning.Photograph: Stefan Rousseau/PAKemi BadenochAnalysisKemi Badenoch ‘has to adapt’ her rhetoric to take on Andy BurnhamPeter WalkerSenior political correspondentConservative leader is becoming more confident and forging a message for her party, but faces a new opponentThu 23 Jul 2026 14.14 EDTLast modified on Thu 23 Jul 2026 16.18 EDTShare“So, Andy Burnham seems like a nice guy.” Those were the slightly unexpected opening words of Kemi Badenoch’sfirst speechsince the change in prime minister. But make no mistake: she was there to bury her opponent.Even Badenoch-sceptics inside the Conservative party and farther afield agree the Tory leader is becoming more confident, and has been able to forge an increasingly coherent message for her party, one based on a promise of economic competence.She does, nonetheless, face a different challenge now Burnham has replaced Keir Starmer.Aside from being a fresh face with a likely polling bounce, the former Greater Manchester mayor is more of a media natural, with the ability to deflect Badenoch’s jibes using self-deprecating humour, such as aviral social media responselast month after the Tory leader dismissed him as a “pair of eyelashes and a black T-shirt”.“She has to adapt – it’s like with anything when your opponent changes,” one senior Conservative said. “As a personality, Burnham is much more likable [than Starmer], and comes with a fair degree of popularity. She needs to wait for weaknesses, and one immediately seems to be: where is the money coming from for all these giveaways?”And that was one of the main themes of Badenoch’s speech on Thursday morning, held in muggy sunshine on a rooftop terrace in central London.Labelling Burnham’s philosophy as “cap one price and send the bill somewhere else”, she repeatedly questioned how the new prime minister would pay for his early flurry of policies. The other central theme was ambition, Badenoch calling Burnham a prime minister “with small answers to big problems”.View image in fullscreenKemi Badenoch now has a schedule of regular press conferences and lengthy media Q&As, often without a particular policy to announce.Photograph: Stefan Rousseau/PASuch message discipline would be necessary in taking on the new operation in No 10, one senior Conservative MP said, especially given Badenoch’s occasional tendency to get caught up in culture-war debates.The MP said: “When she first became leader there was a bit of a worry that she would just talk endlessly about culture-war issues. But that hasn’t happened. The focus should be on the economy and jobs, which it currently is.”A manifestation of Badenoch’s increased confidence is a schedule of regular press conferences and lengthy media Q&As, often without a particular policy to announce. It is notably similar to the timetable maintained last summer by Nigel Farage – before his byelection battle and anxieties over questionsabout his financesscuppered this.And while she still maintains a largely take-no-prisoners approach, Badenoch now has rhetorical registers other than the sometimes caustic antagonism she used on Starmer and, in a speech which some Tories say began her turnaround, towards Rachel Reeves whenshe evisceratedthe then-chancellor’s budget with almost startling ferocity.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |Every daySign up toHeadlines UKGet the day’s headlines and highlights emailed direct to you every morningPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionSuch an approach would not necessarily work against politicians more relaxed in their own skin, said Luke Tryl, the UK director of the pollsters More in Common, who is a regular observer of its focus groups.“If Burnham is seen to just laugh it off it might be less effective than against brittleness,” Tryl said. “The reason it worked so well against Reeves, and her budget response got such rave reviews, was in part because Reeves looked so shellshocked. I suspect [the new chancellor] John Healey and Burnham won’t.”Bigger questions remain for Badenoch, not least why her own improved personal poll ratings have yet to translate into more support for her party, and whether her mission to reshape the Tories in her muscularly rightwing approach could put off some more moderate voters.But for now, much of the coming weeks will be devoted to pinning down how best to tackle a PM who is a notably more slippery target that his predecessor.“She has a summer to figure this out,” the senior Tory said. “But she has to be nimble – what works in July might not by September.”Explore more on these topicsKemi BadenochConservativesAndy BurnhamanalysisShareReuse this content

title: Lobbying firm pressed for details about payments to journalists for favourable stories | Lobbying | The Guardian
description: MPs and others condemn behaviour of CT Group, which offered cash for positive coverage of clients
Author: Michael Savage
Headline: MPs and others condemn behaviour of CT Group, which offered cash for positive coverage of clients
Content: Screengrab from covert filming as part of the Guardian’s investigation.Photograph: The GuardianView image in fullscreenScreengrab from covert filming as part of the Guardian’s investigation.Photograph: The GuardianLobbyingLobbying firm pressed for details about payments to journalists for favourable storiesMPs and others condemn behaviour of CT Group, which offered cash for positive coverage of clientsMichael SavageMedia editorThu 23 Jul 2026 13.52 EDTFirst published on Thu 23 Jul 2026 12.13 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleA political lobbying firmsecretly paying journalists for articlesfavourable to its clients is facing calls to reveal full details of the payments, after the practice was denounced as a dangerous “attack on democracy”.MPs, senior lobbyists and transparency campaigners all condemned the behaviour of CT Group, the firm co-founded by the campaign strategistLynton Crosby, an election guru previously hired by David Cameron and Boris Johnson.There were calls for an overhaul of lobbying rules and demands for CT Group to reveal the full extent of the practice, exposed after a four-month Guardian investigation.Lobbying giant filmed offering reporter payment for flattering client coverage in national pressRead more“Lobbyists paying journalists to produce apparently independent articles is an attack on the ethics of journalism, public relations and, indeed, on democracy,” said Alastair McCapra, the chief executive of the Chartered Institute of Public Relations. CT Group is not a member of the body.“Anyone can practise public relations in this country without belonging to a professional body or answering to a code of conduct. The majority do that ethically but these reported practices undermine our profession and erode the credibility on which responsible lobbying and public relations depend.”Lobbyists for CT Group told an undercover reporter they had paid journalists to place apparently independent stories in newspapers. They also offered hundreds of pounds in cash incentives to do the same for a property industry client and said that in return for the payment, CT Group would have increased editorial control.Jemimah Steinfeld, the chief executive of Index on Censorship, said: “To spin what is lobbying as journalism is shortsighted and frankly dangerous.” She called on CT Group to launch an internal investigation.Steve Goodrich, the head of research and investigations at Transparency International UK, said: “When lobbyists secretly pay journalists to write favourable coverage for their clients, it undermines the integrity of the press. This investigation raises serious questions about the conduct of CT Group, and about how widespread these hidden arrangements are across the lobbying industry.”Luke Taylor, a Liberal Democrat member of parliament’s public administration and constitutional affairs committee, said there should now be “full transparency on when money has changed hands when it comes to reporting”.5:46Lobbyists meet undercover journalist to commission newspaper piece – video“Across the board we are seeing worrying signs of our democracy being in peril,” he said. “A truly free press is a fundamental pillar of a healthy democracy and so we must do everything in our power to maintain it.”Sam Carling, a Labour MP on the committee, said: “If secret payments or other ethical failings are taking place widely, then the government ought to consider giving the Office of the Registrar of Consultant Lobbyists regulatory powers to hold lobbyists to account.”Rupa Huq, a Labour member of the Commons media select committee, said: “We need more transparency in lobbying which almost feels like the wild west, such is the lack of regulation.” She added: “I hope the new Andy Burnham government can grip this issue.”The Guardian began investigating the arrangements after CT Group approached a freelance journalist and offered to pay him to pitch a piece to a British national newspaper about problems caused for the housebuilding industry by the Building Safety Regulator, set up in the wake of the Grenfell Tower fire thatkilled 72 people.CT Group is a lobbying company with a £40m-a-year turnover whose recent clients include the tobacco firm Philip Morris and the owner of the football club Manchester City.Besieged by lobbyists, will UK ministers protect our ecosystems? No, they’ll chicken out | George MonbiotRead moreIn a statement, CT Group said: “We do not comment publicly on work we do for clients and we therefore cannot respond to the specific allegations you have made.”It said the events had been “deliberately mischaracterised” and that it had not engaged the undercover journalist to do anything and did not pay him anything. It said the journalist was looking for work and “chased us relentlessly over a period of more than six months”.The investigation has also raised concerns about the vulnerability of freelance journalism, which can often feature irregular and low-paying work.“If reporters are being paid to place stories in papers by private companies this breaches the essential buffer zone between lobbying and journalism,” said Martin Bright, a senior lecturer in journalism at the University of Essex.“The market is very tough for freelance journalists in the present media climate. They should never be exploited in this way.”Explore more on these topicsLobbyingNewspapersNewspapers & magazinesLynton CrosbynewsShareReuse this content

title: Southport killer Axel Rudakubana moved to Broadmoor psychiatric hospital | Southport attack | The Guardian
description: Exclusive: Clinical assessments concluded Rudakubana could not be safely managed in prison, sources say
Author: Rajeev Syal
Headline: Exclusive: Clinical assessments concluded Rudakubana could not be safely managed in prison, sources say
Content: Rudakubana was moved from Belmarsh prison to Broadmoor high-security hospital (above) this week.Photograph: Neil Hall/ShutterstockView image in fullscreenRudakubana was moved from Belmarsh prison to Broadmoor high-security hospital (above) this week.Photograph: Neil Hall/ShutterstockSouthport attackSouthport killer Axel Rudakubana moved to Broadmoor psychiatric hospitalExclusive: Clinical assessments concluded Rudakubana could not be safely managed in prison, sources sayRajeev SyalHome affairs editorThu 23 Jul 2026 13.51 EDTLast modified on Thu 23 Jul 2026 18.59 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleThe Southport killer Axel Rudakubana has been moved from prison to a psychiatric hospital after clinical assessment, the Guardian can disclose.He was transferred to Broadmoor high-security hospital from the category A prison Belmarsh this week after it was concluded that he could not be managed safely in a prison environment, sources confirmed.People in psychiatric units such as Broadmoor are treated as patients rather than prisoners. A Ministry of Justice spokesperson said he would remain locked up and is unlikely to ever be released.The move is likely to anger many of the families of his victims, who will argue he was assessed as fit to stand trial in January 2025.It means the cost of his incarceration will rise. Costs for patients in hospital secure units are more than £300,000 each a year, compared with about £60,000 for prisoners, Whitehall sources said.Rudakubana, 19, from Banks, Lancashire, was jailed for life in January 2025, with a minimum sentence of 52 years, for the “sadistic” murders of Elsie Dot Stancombe, seven, Bebe King, six, and nine-year-old Alice da Silva Aguiar.View image in fullscreenLeft to right: Elsie Dot Stancombe, seven, Bebe King, six, and Alice da Silva Aguiar, nine.Composite: Merseyside policeHe attacked eight other children, the class instructor Leanne Lucas, and businessman John Hayes, who had tried to disarm him at a Taylor Swift-themed dance class on 29 July 2024 in Southport. He was also found guilty of producing the biological toxin ricin and of possessing an al-Qaida training manual, an offence under the Terrorism Act.Alder Hey children’s NHS foundation trust confirmed Rudakubana had been under its care as a mental health patient between 2019 and 2023, when he “stopped engaging”. He was referred to them after taking a knife to school and later saying he had done so “to use it”.He was also referred to the UK’s counter-extremism programme, Prevent, because he had been researching school massacres on a computer at his next school, the Acorns.There were two other referrals to Prevent, but none were progressed as Rudakubana had no clear ideology behind his obsession with violence.In May 2025, Rudakubana attacked a prison officer with boiling water, sparking an internal inquiry at Belmarsh.View image in fullscreenAn artist’s sketch of Axel Rudakubana appearing via video link during a court appearance.Photograph: Julia Quenzler/ReutersResponding to the move to Broadmoor, Mark Fairhurst, the national chair of the POA union, which represents prison, correctional, and secure psychiatric workers, said his members believed he was “playing the system” so he could move to a more comfortable environment.He said: “[Rudakubana] is not mentally unwell. He is playing the system and is nothing more than evil. He should spend his entire sentence in solitary confinement staring at four walls.“It is appalling to think that he will now enjoy all the freedoms a secure hospital is forced to give him and even more unjustified is the taxpayer actually paying him benefits because he is now classed as mentally unwell.“It is POA members who will now have to carry out the wishes of those who think he can be cured. This is a sickening development and my thoughts are with the families of the victims and all those who have been affected by his atrocious crime.”Broadmoor, in Crowthorne, Berkshire, is the oldest of England’s three high security psychiatric hospitals. It has been used to hold some of the UK’s most notorious murderers, including Peter Sutcliffe, the Yorkshire Ripper.Patients remain locked up, with conditions heavily regulated by strict physical security including locked wards, monitored by staff and 24-hour CCTV. They receive mandatory therapeutic and rehabilitative programmes. If offenders no longer require treatment in hospital, the 1983 Mental Health Act provides for a swift return to prison.If an offender presents an increased risk and cannot be managed safely in a prison environment because of a mental disorder, a request to be moved to a secure psychiatric unit can be made by clinical professionals.Sources said the act and the clinical assessments meant ministers had no choice but to accept the request to transfer the prisoner in this instance.A Ministry of Justice spokesperson said: “The families of those murdered and injured in Southport will be in this country’s heart for ever and our thoughts remain with them.“The offender who carried out these vile crimes will rightly spend the entirety of his 52-year custodial sentence locked up, and is unlikely to ever be released.“High secure hospitals are not places of freedom. He will remain under constant supervision in a high-security psychiatric unit, and be returned to prison as soon as possible.“The transfer of an offender follows a robust independent clinical assessment.”Explore more on these topicsSouthport attackPrisons and probationUK criminal justiceEnglandnewsShareReuse this content

title: Business rates cut for pubs, clubs and music venues in England only ‘a small move’, say critics | Business rates | The Guardian
description: Hospitality businesses call on Andy Burnham to cut VAT and extend help to hotels and restaurants
Author: Pippa Crerar
Headline: Hospitality businesses call on Andy Burnham to cut VAT and extend help to hotels and restaurants
Content: Andy Burnham visits the Hare Inn in Harlow, Essex, on Thursday.Photograph: Richard Pohle/ReutersView image in fullscreenAndy Burnham visits the Hare Inn in Harlow, Essex, on Thursday.Photograph: Richard Pohle/ReutersBusiness ratesBusiness rates cut for pubs, clubs and music venues in England only ‘a small move’, say criticsHospitality businesses call on Andy Burnham to cut VAT and extend help to hotels and restaurantsPippa CrerarandRob DaviesThu 23 Jul 2026 13.02 EDTFirst published on Thu 23 Jul 2026 01.37 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GooglePubs, clubs and live music venues in England will receive a 20% discount on their business rates bills as part of a £100m aid package butAndy Burnhamhas immediately faced calls to be more ambitious.The prime minister said the measure, aimed at supporting the high street and easing cost of living pressures, would be fully funded, including through a review of relief for businesses deemed not to make a positive contribution to communities, such as vape shops.Other cost of living measures announced this week –capping bus fares at £2andcutting VAT on electricity bills– have prompted questions abouthow they will be funded, leaving the new administration under pressure to set out the detail of its plans.Burnham stirs up political row with review of release plans for violent criminalsRead moreBurnham, who took office on Monday, has promised to give people “more breathing space” on living costs through a series of policy interventions, as he attempts to demonstrate to an often sceptical public that the government can deliver meaningful change.Speaking on Thursday, the Conservative leader, Kemi Badenoch, said Burnham’s early policy announcement had left her thinking: “Is that it?”And while hospitality leaders welcomed the tax cut, some questioned its scale and raised concerns that businesses such as hotels and restaurants had been left out.The business rates cut will benefit almost 32,000 pubs, clubs and live music venues, according to the government, with the typical pub expected to save an estimated £1,100 in the next financial year. The support will be targeted so that the largest live music venues are excluded.The measure builds on the15% business rates relief for pubs and live music venuesannounced by Rachel Reeves this year, as Covid-era relief ended and new revaluations took effect, with bills frozen in real terms for a further two years.“For too long, governments have stood by while cherished venues have disappeared from our local high streets. So today I am changing that,” the prime minister said. “This government will back the businesses that people want to see in their communities. I said I would protect pubs and local high streets – the beating heart of our communities – and that’s what we will do.”View image in fullscreenThe new chancellor, John Healey, left, with Burnham in the Hare Inn in Harlow, Essex. Healey said the government remained committed to overhauling the business rates system.Photograph: Richard Pohle/ReutersThenew chancellor, John Healey, said thegovernment remained committed to overhauling the wider business rates system, including relief for small businesses, at the budget.Some hospitality leaders urged Burnham to go further, particularly given that he has previouslybacked the industry’s callfor a reduction in VAT to 10%. That policy would have been worth £10bn to the hard-pressed sector, although tax experts have warned that large, profitable businesseswould enjoy most of the benefit.Tim Martin, the outspoken founder of the JD Wetherspoon pub chain, said business rates relief was “a small move in the right direction” but called on Burnham to make the cut in VAT.Nick Evans, a co-owner of the Old Crown Coaching Inn in Faringdon, Oxfordshire, said business rates relief would do little to help the pub.“It’ll save £3,000 a year, something like that. When you’ve got a £1.8m turnover business that shows less than 2% gross profit, chucking me £3,000 doesn’t help me at all,” he said. “Prices are going up, energy is going up, so what can you do? He said he was going to cut VAT to 10%.”skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |Every weekdaySign up toFirst EditionOur morning email breaks down the key stories of the day, telling you what’s happening and why it mattersPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionKate Nicholls, the chair of the UKHospitality trade body, welcomed the government’s recognition of the sector’s difficulties but pointed out that restaurants and hotels were excluded. “We now need to see a solution for the rest of the sector,” she said.Ministers also plan to crack down on businesses that sell through online marketplaces but do not comply with their tax obligations, putting them at an unfair advantage over those that do, and are consulting on measures to make those marketplaces more responsible for this.Tina McKenzie, the policy chair of the Federation of Small Businesses, welcomed the proposal but said it must be a “down payment” on further action to help small businesses more broadly at the next budget.“Failure is not an option,” she said. “We are encouraged at the signal from the prime minister today, instructing his government to plan for a significant increase in small business rates relief at the heart of the next budget. This would deliver on promises made campaigning for the role, and fix the damage caused by business rates decisions that sent bills up and are holding back [small and medium-sized enterprise] growth and jobs in every postcode.”On Tuesday, in hisfirst policy intervention, Burnham said the government would cut VAT on electricity bills, reducing them by an average of £45 a year from October. This would be funded, officials said, by his decision to scrap the digital ID scheme.However, less than an hour after the measure was announced, Darren Jones, who was sacked as chief secretary to the prime minister in Burnham’s reshuffle, raised doubts over how it would be paid for, saying the digital ID project – which was expected to save £1.8bn over three years – was unfunded.Then on Wednesday, the prime minister said he would reduce the bus fare cap next year from £3 a journey to £2, funded mainly by changing international climate donations into repayable loans.The switch allows the government to borrow more against those loans without violating its borrowing rules. But some experts say it could leave people in the poorest parts of the worldmore vulnerableto the effects of the climate crisis.Explore more on these topicsBusiness ratesAndy BurnhamTax and spendingLabourEconomic policyPubsEnglandnewsShareReuse this content

title: Oil prices near $100 a barrel after US attacks Iran and Houthis hit tankers in Red Sea – as it happened | Business | The Guardian
description: Live, rolling coverage of business, economics and financial markets as traders fear attacks from Yemen and Iran creating ‘two-chokepoint problem’
Author: Jasper Jolly
Headline: Live, rolling coverage of business, economics and financial markets as traders fear attacks from Yemen and Iran creating ‘two-chokepoint problem’
Content: 07.25 EDTHigher oil prices hit drivers at the petrol pump - closing summaryHigher oil pricesbecause of the resumption of US attacks on Iran and the spread of the conflict to the Red Sea areforcing up the cost to driversat petrol pumps, according to the latest UK figures.The average price of petrol in the UK has risen to 155.57p per litre, up from 150.59p on 6 July, according to theRAC, a motoring services company. Diesel is up to 172.14p, after having fallen as low as 164p when it appeared that the US and Iran might be reaching a truce.Renewed US strikes on Iranfor 12 days in a row, and new attacks on Saudi oil tankers by Yemen’s Houthis, have put paid to any hopes of peace in the near term. Brent crude prices rose by $4.50 as high as $98.88 on Thursday – leaving them just shy of the $100 mark not seen for nearly two months.Simon Williams, head of policy at theRAC, said:double quotation markFuel prices are shooting up like a rocket on the back of oil being above $90 for the last days. The average price of diesel has gone up almost 8p, or 5%, to 172.14p a litre in the last fortnight, while petrol has risen by 5p in two and a half weeks to 155.57p, a 3% increase.All the cuts of the last few months are sadly being reversed, with the price of unleaded now heading back up towards 160p and diesel to a shocking 180p. If petrol was to climb to 160p, it would surpass its Iran war high of 159.53p, seen on 28 May. Unless the renewed conflict is brought to an abrupt end soon, it’s looking like UK drivers are going to suffer some stinging summertime pump prices.Petrol prices are up by 17% compared with before the US-Israeli attacks on Iran, according to the RAC.Photograph: RACIn other business news from today:Google fined €890m by EU for search and app store competition violationsFord and China’s Geely to share US carmaker’s Spanish factoryEasyJet profits hit by higher oil pricesCarmakers privately lobbied UK government to revoke 2035 banBritish Gas owner Centrica said it is cutting 1,300 jobsAndy Burnham to cut business rates for pubs, clubs and live music by 20%Thanks for following today, and please do join us tomorrow for more live coverage of business, economics and financial markets. JJShareUpdated at07.27 EDT

title: US senator accuses Barclays of ‘failure’ to investigate ex-CEO’s ties to Epstein | Barclays | The Guardian
description: Exclusive: Elizabeth Warren claims bank did not ‘meaningfully investigate’ Jes Staley’s links with sex offender
Author: Kalyeena Makortoff
Headline: Exclusive: Elizabeth Warren claims bank did not ‘meaningfully investigate’ Jes Staley’s links with sex offender
Content: Jes Staley was forced to step down as CEO of Barclays over his links with Jeffrey Epstein.Photograph: Bloomberg/GettyView image in fullscreenJes Staley was forced to step down as CEO of Barclays over his links with Jeffrey Epstein.Photograph: Bloomberg/GettyBarclaysUS senator accuses Barclays of ‘failure’ to investigate ex-CEO’s ties to EpsteinExclusive: Elizabeth Warren claims bank did not ‘meaningfully investigate’ Jes Staley’s links with sex offenderKalyeena MakortoffBanking correspondentThu 23 Jul 2026 06.22 EDTFirst published on Thu 23 Jul 2026 00.00 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleThe leading US senatorElizabeth Warrenhas called for urgent answers from Barclays over what she claimed was its “apparent failure to meaningfully investigate” ties between the bank’s former boss, Jes Staley, and the late sex offender Jeffrey Epstein.In a letter sent privately to Barclays’s chair, Nigel Higgins, and seen by the Guardian, Warren – the most senior Democrat on the US senate banking committee – said it was “deeply unclear howBarclays, supposedly investigating Staley’s connection to Epstein, failed to uncover this decades-long relationship”.The letter, sent also on behalf of the Congress members Ro Khanna and Raja Krishnamoorthi, gave Barclays two weeks to answer a series of questions about how they handled information on the links between its former chief executive and Epstein, who died while awaiting trial on child sex trafficking charges in 2019.The questions included whether the bank conducted any reviews into “deficiencies” in its executive hiring process, which had “allowed the board to hire a CEO who held extensive professional and personal ties to a convicted sex offender”.The US lawmakers raised a string of concerns about information arising from a UKcourt hearing last year, in which Staley unsuccessfullytried to overturn the regulator’s decisionto ban him from the British banking industry, four years after he was forced to step down as boss of Barclays.View image in fullscreenElizabeth Warren, a Democrat, tells Barclays it ‘simply took him [Jes Staley] at his word’.Photograph: Allison Robbert/APThose concerns included Higgins’s admission that he had not asked Staley about hislast contact with Epsteinbefore Higgins’s declaration to the Financial Conduct Authority that the last contact had been “well before” the CEO joined Barclays.“It appears that neither you nor any other member of the board conducted any deeper due diligence to verify Staley’s claims and simply took him at his word,” the letter said, referring to exchanges between the executive and board members during Staley’s tenure. “It is unclear what specific actions, if any, Barclays took to investigate Staley and Epstein’s personal ties.”The US lawmakers said the episode sparked questions about how the bank was managed. “Barclays’s apparent failure to meaningfully investigate or address Staley’s relationship with Epstein raises significant governance questions regarding the bank’s ability to hold senior executives accountable for wrongdoing,” the letter said.The signatories also warned that it was a “privilege” to hold local banking licences and operate in the US, where the bank had “extensive” operations and held about $200bn (£150bn) in assets. That privilege “is contingent on the ongoing character and fitness of management and the ability of the firm to conduct its operations in a safe and sound manner”.The letter was sent hours before Staley was due to be grilled in a closed-doors hearing by the House Oversight Committee, on which the letter’s co-signatories, Khanna and Krishnamoorthi, both sit.Staley originally met Epstein in 2000 after he became head of JP Morgan’s private bank, where Epstein was a client. He later took over as chief executive of Barclays in 2015, but was forced to step down in 2021, after City regulators launched an investigation into the nature of the relationship between the two men.Staley was eventually banned from taking any job in the UK’s financial sector for playing down those ties, which Barclays vouched for in its letter to the Financial Conduct Authority in 2019.In the former Barclays boss’s failed attempt last year to overturn his banking ban, the court battle revealed intimate details of the men’s friendship. Staley also lost out on £18m worth of pay and bonuses from Barclays as a result of the ruling.A spokesperson for Barclays said: “The UK regulator concluded that Barclays was misled byJes Staley. We conducted an investigation based on the information that was available to us at the time.“Since then, new information has emerged that was not available to Barclays that demonstrates Mr Staley misled Barclays and many others as to the nature of the relationship with Epstein. That information relates to activities that predated his employment at Barclays.”Explore more on these topicsBarclaysJes StaleyElizabeth WarrenBankingJeffrey EpsteinUS politicsnewsShareReuse this content

title: Tour de France worries emerge for Tadej Pogacar after illness sweeps through team | Tour de France 2026 | The Guardian
description: Richard Carapaz won stage 18 of the Tour, but uncertainty surrounds the health of race leader Tadej Pogacar’s UAE Emirates XRG team
Author: Jeremy Whittle
Headline: Richard Carapaz won stage 18 of the Tour, but uncertainty surrounds the health of race leader Tadej Pogacar’s UAE Emirates XRG team
Content: Tadej Pogacar (second left) rides with his UAE Team Emirates colleagues on Thursday’s stage.Photograph: Yoan Valat/EPAView image in fullscreenTadej Pogacar (second left) rides with his UAE Team Emirates colleagues on Thursday’s stage.Photograph: Yoan Valat/EPATour de France 2026Tour de France worries emerge for Tadej Pogacar after illness sweeps through teamRichard Carapaz roars clear of breakaway to win stage 18McNulty abandons, Wellens and Vermeersch struggleJeremy Whittlein Orcières-MerletteThu 23 Jul 2026 12.56 EDTLast modified on Thu 23 Jul 2026 19.31 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleRichard Carapaz won stage 18 of theTour de Franceafter a breakaway group of six riders duelled their way to the winter sports station at Orcières-Merlette, but uncertainty surrounds the health of race leader Tadej Pogacar’s UAE Team Emirates XRG, which has been swept by a virus since Monday’s rest day.After Adam Yates’s stage-long calvary on the road to Voiron, Pogacar’s team was further weakened by the abandonment of his American colleague, Brandon McNulty, who quit the Tour with 21km of Thursday’s stage remaining.Tour de France director rejects accusations of bias towards French ridersRead moreTwo more of Pogacar’s teammates, Tim Wellens and Florian Vermeersch, also appeared to struggle at times during the stage, which included five categorised climbs, including the final seven-kilometre ascent to the finish.Speaking at the stage start in Voiron, Wellens suggested other riders within the team were unwell and revealed that he had a sore throat. Seen through the lens of Friday and Saturday’s mountain-top finishes on Alpe d’Huez, Pogacar, despite his 4m 32s lead over second-placed Remco Evenepoel, has reason to be worried.“I’m sad that Brandon could not finish. We will see in the next two days but Adam is getting better, and tomorrow, we will go with the flow,” Pogacar said. “I think we can manage. It’s the third week, all the teams are suffering. There are bodies everywhere on a stage like today. We will keep fighting and I believe we can do it.”The full extent of any illness within UAE Team Emirates XRG was further speculated on by Pogacar’s decision to swap his usual yellow shorts for black. McNulty’s decision to stop may have been for other reasons too, though. He had also been nursing injuries sustained when he was hit by a car during his reconnaissance of Tuesday’s time trial.View image in fullscreenThere are two more tough mountain stages in the Alps before Sunday’s final run into Paris.Photograph: Stéphanie Lecocq/ReutersThe former Giro d’Italia champion Carapaz,who won a mountain stage at nearby Superdévoluyin 2024, narrowly avoided colliding with an errant fan earlier in the stage. The Ecuadorian recovered from that mid-race scare to win with a typically gutsy lone attack.On a stage in which the main favourites called a truce, those who had tried and failed to succeed from breakaways earlier in the race made one last-ditch bid for glory. That included the Geraint Thomas-directed Netcompany Ineos, which has yet to win a stage this year despite numerous attempts.Time is now running out and Thomas has described this Tour as “tough going” for the British team. So it proved once more, with Egan Bernal and Thymen Arensman trying but failing to join the decisive move. The group that finally formed included Carapaz, but also Matteo Jorgenson, teammate to the now absent Jonas Vingegaard at Visma Lease-a-bike, and Mauro Schmid, already a stage winner for Jayco AlUla in Belfort.View image in fullscreenRichard Carapaz pours water on himself after winning stage 18.Photograph: Jeff Pachoud/ReutersCarapaz has proven a very reliable performer in the Tour’s mid-mountain stages, however, and once again he had enough power to distance his rivals in the final kilometres. The EF Education-EasyPost rider finished 45 seconds clear of both Schmid and Jorgenson. All eyes are now on this weekend’s double-headed suffer-fest on the daunting Alpe d’Huez, with a worried Pogacar looking, for the first time this year, almost vulnerable.Explore more on these topicsTour de France 2026Tour de FranceCyclingTadej PogacarnewsShareReuse this content

title: Cooper Lutkenhaus: US track phenom on family ties, LA 2028 and balancing stardom with high school | Athletics | The Guardian
description: The 17-year-old dreamed of playing for the Dallas Cowboys. Now he is America’s next big track star, with another chance for gold at this week’s US championships
Author: Rob Draper
Headline: The 17-year-old dreamed of playing for the Dallas Cowboys. Now he is America’s next big track star, with another chance for gold at this week’s US championships
Content: Cooper Lutkenhaus became the youngest world champion in indoor track and field history earlier this year.Photograph: Dan Mullan/Getty ImagesView image in fullscreenCooper Lutkenhaus became the youngest world champion in indoor track and field history earlier this year.Photograph: Dan Mullan/Getty ImagesAthleticsInterviewCooper Lutkenhaus: US track phenom on family ties, LA 2028 and balancing stardom with high schoolRob DraperThe 17-year-old dreamed of playing for the Dallas Cowboys. Now he is America’s next big track star, with another chance for gold at this week’s US championshipsThu 23 Jul 2026 05.00 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleGeorge Lutkenhaus and his family were rushing to the airport. There was a plane to catch and traffic was bad. Inside the car was a tense silence. In the back, a teenager, minding his own business, AirPods in, seemingly oblivious. Wife Tricia turned to George. “Are you OK?” she said.“No, I am not OK!” he replied. “What just happened there was not normal! And he’s just sitting there in the back … ”Hewas Cooper Lutkenhaus, nonchalant, oblivious to the fuss and 16 years old at the time. This was last summer, in the hours after the USA Track and Field Championships, where Cooper had just finished second in the 800m. That in itself was an astonishing feat; it earned him a spot on the US men’s senior team for the WorldAthleticsChampionships in Tokyo.Yet what was off-the-scale in terms of the jaw-dropping performances was the time: 1min 42.27secs. It was more than a second faster than the previous under-18 record holder – and Lutkenhaus was still under 17. George, a former college runner and now the athletic director at the Texas high school Cooper attends, knew exactly what it meant, as did mom, the school’s principal who was a track star herself. In short, it was decidedly not normal.US high school student Cooper Lutkenhaus wins 800m to become youngest ever indoor world championRead moreOn Friday Cooper will attempt to repeat the trick and go one better at Icahn Stadium in New York City, the site of the 2026 US championships. One of his key goals this year was to win the national 800m title. The final is on Sunday.For a boy who hasn’t yet finished high school, his achievements are almost unprecedented. It’s not typically possible to reach male athletic peak until at least your late teens. Boris Becker winning Wimbledon at 17 is perhaps comparable.Quincy Wilson, a 16-year-old 4x400m Olympic gold medalist in Paris, was similar but understandably has yet to build on that achievement after that breakthrough.The Lutkenhaus family are adjusting to their new normal of having a now-17-year-old superstar around the house. In March, Cooperwon the 800mat the world indoor championships in Poland, becoming the youngest world champion in indoor track and field history. There have been Nike contracts to negotiate, dope tests to navigate, the pleasant surprise of being feted in Europe at Oslo and Stockholm this summer.And nowadays visiting journalists from all around the world may get in touch when traveling through Dallas. I reached out to the family during the World Cup and, with true Texan hospitality, they immediately invited me to the Lonesome Spur roadside cafe just around the corner from their home in Justin, north of Dallas, to join breakfast following Cooper and older brother Andrew’s morning run. Andrew himself has run 1:48.01 for 800m and is on a scholarship at the University of Tulsa.Younger sibling advantage is a well-established trope in sport – the younger child excels precisely because they have to keep up with elders – a point made in conversation to Andrew. “He tends to forget it,” says Andrew with a sideways glance and grin at his little brother, who is busy ordering the Cowboy omelette, hash browns and toast. There is another older brother, George Jr, who swam for Adams State University but he isn’t home on this day to keep all the youngsters in line.There is a juxtaposition between this ordinary family scene, the closeness of the brothers evident in the rivalry and the superstardom that potentially awaits. Some of life remains ordinary. Cooper still trains with his Northwest High School classmates in the morning before school, enjoys studying American history and is contemplating majoring in kinesiology, the study of movement in college. But Cooper doesn’t run track for Northwest and won’t compete in college, because he abandoned his amateur status by taking his Nike contract.View image in fullscreenCooper Lutkenhaus competes at the Prefontaine Classic earlier this year.Photograph: Ali Gradischer/Getty ImagesThere were some clues as to what was coming. The parental gene pool was promising. And he ran 1:53 for 800m in the eighth grade. But until that point he was following a broad sports program, not specializing in track and field and was more intent on a football career. “We did everything the same,” Andrew explains, talking about the multi-sport approach his parents took with their kids. “But he started football in fifth grade and I didn’t start until the seventh grade.”Cooper, it turns out, was a determined 10-year-old. “The only reason I got to play football early was because I begged. I had to convince my dad over and over every day: ‘Please let me play, please let me play.’” Track was not a dream at the point. “I thought I was going to play football.” A Dallas Cowboys quarterback? “That’s exactly what I wanted to do. But after I ran 1:47 at 15 I thought: ‘Maybe not. Maybe I might be better at this. I have to hang up the helmet.’”The jump last year from high schooler to elite athlete was almost an end of innocence. “Hewas fine,” says George. “I probably aged 10 years. We had a plan for him running 1:45 [last year]. Andrew was going to graduate college the same time that Cooper graduated high school. Andrew had a fifth year [at college] so he said to Cooper: ‘Choose your college and I’ll go there and be a graduate assistant and help coach you.’ So they had it all planned out. The moment he hit 1:42, I was like: ‘Uh oh!’ There’s a big difference between 1:45 and 1:42.”‘He’s phenomenal’: American teen fast becoming athletics’ next big thingRead moreCooper interjects: “He [George] is the one that took me to the USA [champs], so you can blame him! I was ready to be done. I’d run 1:45, broken the national record twice. I’m happy. There was nothing more we could accomplish. My IT band [the connective tissue between hip and knee] flared up and he was like, ‘If he heals up let’s just see.’ I’d never done back-to-back 800m [heats at the Championships] before so I was super excited [making the final] but it was like, ‘One more race, then I’m done and it’s over!’ After the final [finishing second] I was: ‘Hold on a minute? There’s more racing!’ The first time I saw him [George] I was like ‘Are we going to Tokyo?’ And he was: ‘Absolutely!’”For George, the elation also brought a world of worry. “When he ran 1:42, I was excited for about 30 seconds and then I realized: ‘He’s won a medal so he’s now in the testing pool,’” George says, referring to mandatory dope testing. All elite athletes have to make themselves available for one specified hour a day for drug testing, a necessary duty but one which any parent of any 16-year-old boy would approach with trepidation, given that missing the stipulated window can lead to a two-year doping ban.“I had no idea what the testing pool was,” Cooper says. “I never knew whereabouts was a thing until one day I was sent a message saying: ‘Fill this out.’ It’s not the worst thing. But in high school, I’m going to hang out at someone’s house and it’s: ‘Hold on guys – what’s your address? How long we going to be here?’”Though the Tokyo world championships proved a race too far – he waseliminatedin a tactical first-round heat in 1:47.68 – his return to the world stage in March with indoor gold in Toruń confirmed his superstar status in the sport. Coming to Europe this summer and beating Olympic champion Emmanuel Wanyonyi and 2023 world champion Marco Arop and improving his time to 1:42.08 cemented those credentials.View image in fullscreenCooper Lutkenhaus has spent the past few months competing in Europe.Photograph: Jonathan Nackstrand/AFP/Getty ImagesCome the 2028 Los Angeles Olympics, a maelstrom of patriotic pressure and mainstream exposure will probably blow through the quiet world of Justin, Texas. Mary Decker-Slaney experienced something similar at LA 1984 and it crushed her; Carl Lewis rode the same wave and thrived, as did Michael Johnson at Atlanta 1996. But with the USA hosting their first Summer Games in 32 years, there is little doubt that, if trajectories continue, the Lutkenhaus clan are about to assume a profile way beyond the celebrity status they now enjoy at small stadiums in northern European nations that revere track.“I haven’t really thought about the LA Games at all,” Cooper says. “That’s not what I’m focused on. Obviously it’s in the back of my mind – it has to be – but I’m focused on my next workout. You can have a big goal in your mind but you have to have the small goals leading up to it.”George adds: “The big thing for him is to focus on staying healthy and stringing together good workouts. He’s really good about understanding that when it comes to pressure, the only real pressure you have is the pressure you place on yourself. [With] the outside noise, we’ll get online and look at stuff and we’re like: ‘Can you believe what this guy said?!’ And we just laugh at it. Some people try to avoid it. I don’t think you can avoid it. You have to approach it the right way.”Cooper nods. “You can’t let random people who ran two minutes flat in high school tell you what you need to be doing.”You really can’t. Not when you have run 1:42 at 17 and beat the Olympic champion. And not when you have high school to graduate next year, the prom to look forward to, hangouts with friends to enjoy and homework to do. The remarkably normal Lutkenhaus family may become a global phenomenon in the next 18 months. For now, it’s all about the homely Icahn Stadium in Manhattan this week. Watch this space.Explore more on these topicsAthleticsUS sportsLA Olympic Games 2028interviewsShareReuse this content

title: Trump hosts champion Dodgers at White House as some players stay away | Los Angeles Dodgers | The Guardian
description: Mookie Betts and Kike Hernández were among those who did not join team’s visit, the prospect of which left many loyal fans in Los Angeles feeling conflicted
Author: Guardian sport
Headline: Mookie Betts and Kike Hernández were among those who did not join team’s visit, the prospect of which left many loyal fans in Los Angeles feeling conflicted
Content: Donald Trump holds up a World Series championship ring gifted to him by Dodgers owner Mark Walter.Photograph: Shawn Thew/EPAView image in fullscreenDonald Trump holds up a World Series championship ring gifted to him by Dodgers owner Mark Walter.Photograph: Shawn Thew/EPALos Angeles DodgersTrump hosts champion Dodgers at White House as some players stay awayBetts and Hernández opt against joining team for eventBack-to-back World Series winners gift ring to TrumpMany loyal fans had conflicted feelings over visitGuardian sportThu 23 Jul 2026 18.59 EDTLast modified on Thu 23 Jul 2026 19.00 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleDonald Trump hosted the World Series championLos Angeles Dodgersat the White House on Thursday for the team’s second trip to the White House in two years.The team gave the president a custom jersey and a World Series championship ring during a ceremony at the Rose Garden. Four-time MVP Shohei Ohtani, star first baseman Freddie Freeman, World Series MVP Yoshinobu Yamamoto and manager Dave Roberts were among those in attendance, while several players – most notably star shortstop Mookie Betts and utility man Kike Hernández – opted not to attend.As Dodgers head to White House, loyal fans are conflicted: ‘Trump is tearing this city apart’Read more“Special winners, unbelievable,” Trump said. “I would not be surprised to see them back here again.”In remarks that also included lengthy tangents on former Yankees owner George Steinbrenner and the renovation of the Rose Garden, Trump complimented several Dodgers players while also confusing the roles of some others. He made a lengthy comparison between Ohtani and legendary slugger Babe Ruth, and praised Clayton Kershaw, the Dodgers ace who pitched an inning of last year’s epicWorld Series Game 3 winover the Blue Jays in 18 innings.“I watched him beat a lot of teams. They didn’t do too well against him. He is still pitching well, I bet,” Trump said of Kershaw, who retired after last year’s World Series.Trump said he spoke with Roberts before the event and expressed surprise at the Dodgers’ status this season as the best team in baseball, but noted he’s “been a little busy with Iran and some other things”.The team gave Trump what appeared to be a replica of their championship ring.“Wow. Do I have to report this?” he said as he put the ring in his jacket. “I don’t want to report this.”The Dodgers’ trip to the White House left many of their fans with conflicted feelings, given recent ICE raids and protest crackdowns in the city of Los Angeles as ordered by the Trump administration.Betts, who did not participate in the Boston Red Sox’s trip during Trump’s first term but was thereduring the Dodgers’ visitlast year, told reporters he preferred to spend the day with his family. Hernández, an outspoken critic of the ICE raids in Los Angeles, said he had a conflicting injury rehab assignment but wouldn’t have participated if he was available to join. Pitcher Edgardo Henriquez also reportedly did not attend.“My personal line, my company line, is I hope we get this invitation every year because that’s the goal, to win a championship,” Roberts said earlier this year after the team accepted the invitation. “I’m not a politician, and I’m doing something that teams have done for decades. And so that’s where I stand, really. I’m a baseball coach. That’s what I do.”Teams who have won a major championship have traditionally been invited to the White House to celebrate their victory with the president. During Trump’s first term, several teams were not invited or made clear they would not attend, including champions from the NBA and the WNBA.This year, the White House has hosted the Olympic gold-winning US men’s hockey team and the NHL’s Florida Panthers to celebrate championships. The Oklahoma City Thunder, the 2025 NBA champions, opted not to visit; the owner of the 2026 champion New York Knicks has saidTrump invited the teambut there has been no decision made on whether they will attend.Explore more on these topicsLos Angeles DodgersMLBBaseballUS sportsnewsShareReuse this content

title: ‘Eyes forward now’: George Russell upbeat after Mercedes solve engine problem | Formula One | The Guardian
description: ‘I’m glad we found it. Spa and Silverstone probably exposed the issue,’ George Russell said of the electrical fault that had damaged his world title hopes
Author: Giles Richards
Headline: ‘I’m glad we found it. Spa and Silverstone probably exposed the issue,’ George Russell said of the electrical fault that had damaged his world title hopes
Content: George Russell spun off the track and out of the race at the Belgian Grand Prix.Photograph: Geert Vanden Wijngaert/APView image in fullscreenGeorge Russell spun off the track and out of the race at the Belgian Grand Prix.Photograph: Geert Vanden Wijngaert/APFormula One‘Eyes forward now’: George Russell upbeat after Mercedes solve engine problemElectrical fault had damaged Briton’s world title hopes‘I’m glad we found it. Spa and Silverstone exposed issue’Giles Richardsat the HungaroringThu 23 Jul 2026 12.32 EDTLast modified on Thu 23 Jul 2026 14.01 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleGeorge Russell believes his Mercedes team have identified the problem with his engine that damaged his world championship hopes over recent races and left the driver fuming when it played a key role in him being knocked out of Sunday’s round in Belgium.Russell has been struggling with issues with his electrical energy deployment for three races now, since the Austrian GP, particularly at Silverstone and Spa which are energy-dependent circuits. In Belgium he was furious after he lacked electrical energy when exiting turn one on the opening lap. It left him vulnerable to the chasing pack, notably the Ferraris which swept past and he entered Les Combes corner alongside Lewis Hamilton, who clipped the Mercedes,sending Russell out of the race.F1’s algorithms spark driver fury as number neuters thrills of Spa-Francorchamps | Giles RichardsRead moreHe was angry with the team at the deployment problem having left him alongside Hamilton. His Mercedes teammate Kimi Antonelli went on to win the race and extend his lead over Russell in the title race to 50 points.Mercedes admitted there was a problemwith the car’s energy management, but also that at the time they were unable to identify what was causing the erratic performance which is governed by the electronic control systems as part of the engine’s hybrid battery unit. However before this weekend’s Hungarian Grand Prix, Russell confirmed they have identified and solved the problem.“I’m feeling good and glad there are some positive answers that have come back from the battery and it’s eyes forward now,” he said. “These new power units are just exceptionally complex, and there was something deeply embedded in the calibration of the code that was almost impossible to see unless … you knew exactly what to look for. So I’m glad we have found it. Spa and Silverstone probably exposed the issue just because of the nature of the track.”Russell had lambasted his team across the radio after the incident at Spa and stood by the strength of his reaction as part of the process of ensuring the team addressed the problems.“My words could have been slightly different,” he said. “I felt quite vocal about this issue pre-Spa and during Spa, and ultimately we have achieved what we wanted to in the sense of finding there was an issue. If you sit there and do not express any views, maybe you find an issue, maybe you don’t, you don’t move forward.”The British driver is not alone in his frustration with how the algorithms that dictate the deployment of electrical energy are having an effect on the new formula. McLaren’s Oscar Piastri was also struggling with a lack of energy during qualifying which left him two-tenths down on teammate Lando Norris. His team also ascribed the deficit not to driving style but to variables in the computer-controlled deployment systems they too could not identify.Meanwhile, the possibility of a return for the Malaysian Grand Prix to the calendar this year has also been mooted in the buildup in Budapest. It is understood the meeting is being considered to slot in between the races in Baku and Singapore on 4 October. Bahrain had been in the running to take the spot but the renewed hostilities in the Middle East have made it highly unlikely.F1 is also considering options of alternatives to step in should the final two races of the season in Qatar and Abu Dhabi be cancelled, with Portimão in Portugal the likely candidate to take place two weeks after the previous round in Las Vegas. However the sport will not make a decision to call off the two meetings in the Middle East until as late as possible.Explore more on these topicsFormula OneFormula One 2026Motor sportnewsShareReuse this content

title: Norway considers filing Fifa ethics complaint over Trump role in World Cup red card saga | World Cup 2026 | The Guardian
description: The president of the Norwegian federation decried the process to overturn Folarin Balogun’s red card as being ‘influenced by external forces’ and ‘an error’
Author: Guardian staff reporter
Headline: The president of the Norwegian federation decried the process to overturn Folarin Balogun’s red card as being ‘influenced by external forces’ and ‘an error’
Content: Lise Klaveness with the Fifa president Gianni Infantino at Norway’s World Cup quarter-final.Photograph: Eva Marie Uzcategui/FIFA/Getty ImagesView image in fullscreenLise Klaveness with the Fifa president Gianni Infantino at Norway’s World Cup quarter-final.Photograph: Eva Marie Uzcategui/FIFA/Getty ImagesWorld Cup 2026Norway considers filing Fifa ethics complaint over Trump role in World Cup red card sagaNorwegian federation president: Fifa on a ‘slippery slope’Trump called Infantino for review of Balogun’s redAssociated PressThu 23 Jul 2026 09.40 EDTLast modified on Thu 23 Jul 2026 21.30 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleDonald Trump’s role in clearing Folarin Balogun from a mandatory one-game ban at theWorld Cupis set to provoke a formal complaint to Fifa ethics investigators.TheNorwaysoccer federation said Thursday its board of directors will consider filing the complaint at its next meeting, likely on 6 August.Don’t like the result? Don’t worry, sport no longer ends on the field of play | Jonathan LiewRead more“We need our leader in Fifa to say that this was an error,” Norwegian federation president Lise Klavenesstold the Timesin an interview published Thursday.After Trump called Fifa president Gianni Infantino, a one-game ban for Balogun’s red-card tackle against Bosnia and Herzegovina was deferred so he could play against Belgium with a quarterfinals place at stake.Belgiumbeat the US 4-1after a chaotic 36 hours at the World Cup. The Belgium players were seemingly fired up by one of the all-time controversies in tournament history.Trump called the referee’s red card for Balogun after video review a “horrible” call andtook creditfor getting Fifa to review what should routinely have been a one-game ban.“We all know that this verdict was influenced from external forces and did not have the proper process,” Klaveness told the Times of the Fifa ruling that was unprecedented in the past 60 years at World Cups. “When you bend a rule like this, you’re on a slippery slope that will put the whole game at risk,” said the former Women’s World Cup player for Norway, who is an elected member of European soccer body Uefa’s executive committee.Klaveness is a lawyer and judge who has positioned the Norwegian federation as a conscience in world soccer since her election in 2022. “It is a worry for the game when you compromise fundamental rules of the game,” she said Thursday about not enforcing Balogun’s ban.Could the World Cup be back in the US as soon as 2038? AbsolutelyRead moreNorway previously joined a London-based NGO’s complaint to the Fifa ethics committee alleging Infantino breached a statutory duty of political neutrality, including by creating a peace prize to award to Trump at the World Cup draw in December.The close personal and political ties between Trump and Infantino include the US president believing the soccer official could be a good pick as next secretary general of the United Nations, the New York Post reported this week.The human rights NGO, FairSquare, also hasfiled an ethics complaintabout the Balogun incident to the International Olympic Committee, where Infantino is an elected member, about breaching its rules on political neutrality.Fifa and the IOC rarely acknowledge or provide updates on potential cases before their ethics bodies. The independence of Fifa’s process to handle complaints alleging unethical conduct has been questioned since the lead investigator and judge both were replaced in 2017, in the second year of Infantino’s presidency. Fifa has yet to publish documents explaining how its disciplinary judges reached verdicts about Balogun or other World Cup players.Explore more on these topicsWorld Cup 2026Football politicsWorld CupNorwayUSAnewsShareReuse this content

title: Don’t like the result? Don’t worry, sport no longer ends on the field of play | Sport | The Guardian
description: From the wrong headset in an F2 garage to Fifa overturning a red card, we are in a landscape where fairness is the enemy
Author: Jonathan Liew
Headline: From the wrong headset in an F2 garage to Fifa overturning a red card, we are in a landscape where fairness is the enemy
Content: Folarin Balogun was shown a red card in the World Cup – but Fifa overturned the decision.Photograph: Phil Noble/ReutersView image in fullscreenFolarin Balogun was shown a red card in the World Cup – but Fifa overturned the decision.Photograph: Phil Noble/ReutersSportDon’t like the result? Don’t worry, sport no longer ends on the field of playJonathan LiewFrom the wrong headset in an F2 garage to Fifa overturning a red card, we are in a landscape where fairness is the enemyThu 23 Jul 2026 03.00 EDTLast modified on Thu 23 Jul 2026 06.20 EDTShareAfunny and very small thing happened at the Formula Two race at Spa on Sunday. Noel León, of Campos Racing, carved his way through the field to finish seventh, earning himself six points in the drivers’ championship, and promptly left the circuit to go and do whatever it is Noel León likes to do after a race. Take a nap, hit the Belgian brews, congratulate himself on his palindromic name – whatever. Anyway, while he was doing that, more than three hours after the chequered flag, the FIA governing body called a meeting and decided to disqualify him.The reason: a serious breach of appendix 5 and article 21.5 of the F2 Sporting Regulations – and assuming you don’t have those to hand, it’s the rule governing the use of headsets. During the race, a guest of León had been spotted in the Campos garage wearing what was deemed the wrong type of headset. According to the aforementioned appendix 5, only listening devices may be used by non-team personnel. This headset had a microphone, albeit upturned and unused.A tournament that crossed borders: crunching the numbers on the diaspora World CupRead moreNever mind that the guest had at no point attempted to communicate with the driver. Never mind the fact that León had no knowledge of any offence, no means of detecting or preventing it, and derived no advantage whatsoever from the presence of an extra unused microphone in the pit lane. How would you even cheat in this scenario, anyway? What would you even say? “Hey! Drive faster!”We could of course devote an entire column to the pedantic weirdness of the FIA rulebook, its unshakeable fondness for petty penalties and meaningless bureaucracy. Watch any F1 race and invariably within a few laps the screen will flash up with the ominous words “FIA Stewards”, followed by some piddling infraction demanding immediate, race‑moulding punishment. (“8.5-second time penalty for Car 34 – Eating A Twix At The Wheel”).But for the most part, at least the FIA dispenses its justice in a hurry. And in any case, the point here is a larger one. To an increasing and alarming extent, sport no longer ends on the field of play. The result is no longer the result. What happens out there is often simply a placeholder, a basis for negotiation, litigation and re-litigation: not the end of an argument but merely the start of a new one.And this is a process that takes many forms. Debate, discourse, postmortem, pub debrief: this has always been part of the essential fabric of sport, and you would scarcely wish it otherwise. Complaining about a sporting outcome is one thing. Challenging it is another thing entirely, and a phenomenon that goes well beyond Daddy Trump and his recent invocationsat the World Cupandthe Open golf.In 1990, the recently established court of arbitration for sport in Lausanne received a grand total of seven requests. As recently as the 2000s, it would hear no more than a few dozen cases a year. Now that number is close to 1,000, ranging from the minor to the seismic, from whether the Polish winger Konrad Michalak is owed unpaid wages by his former Egyptian club Zamalek, to the definition of a woman, to – at some point this year, with any luck – decidingwho won the 2026 Africa Cup of Nations.As you probably remember, the game ended in chaos. Senegal walked off in protest at refereeing decisions before returning to the pitch and winning 1-0; the Confederation of African Football later decided that they had effectively forfeited the game and awarded the cup to Morocco instead. And, of course, once you start the process of redistributing trophies and medals two months after a final whistle, why on earth would you expect Senegal to end it there? Lawyer up, gentlemen: to the death!View image in fullscreenBryson DeChambeau was docked two shots for a rules violation at the Open and is alleged to have said he would call Donald Trump for help over the matter.Photograph: Greig Cowie/ShutterstockCloser to home, the Premier League’s legal battle with Manchester City has now stretched into its fourth year, with no end in sight and thus no real clarity on exactly how seriously we should take all those league titles they won under three coaches. City, as ever, fiercely deny any wrongdoing. And yet whatever the findings of the tribunal, this is basically an insane thing to happen to a sporting competition: close to half a decade in which its very history and legacy, an entire era of English football, is being argued very slowly, in a room, in secret.Even a cursory glance at Cas’s inbox for the next few months will reveal that football is obviously the main culprit here: too big and too rich, too toxic and too tribal, too loosely and incompetently governed. At virtually every level of the game, from non‑league all the way to the World Cup, football has long been administrated along the principle that you take the money first, and work out the details later. Perhaps this is why the Lausanne dockets are replete with agent disputes, transfer disputes, competition disputes, quibbles over financial regulations and who gets to levy them.And so when Donald Trump successfully lobbied Fifa to overturn a red card awarded to a player he had almost certainly never heard of before June 2026, it was not some shocking rupture but an act entirely on trend, for football as well as Trump himself. Don’t like the result? There is an entire apparatus constructed to indulge you: lawyers desperate for your business, administrators willing to bend an ear, armies of post-truth clones desperate to do your bidding on social media.Perhaps all this was inevitable in a world quietly supplanting the idea of rules with the idea of brute power. Rules – in theory at least – imply fairness, the concept that we are all equal and to be treated the same. But what if – on the most fundamental level – you do not believe that we are all equal? What if your very purpose in life is to ensure that some (ie you) are treated better than others? What happens in a landscape where fairness is not an ideal, but the enemy?And no, nobody is equating appendix 5 and article 21.5 of the FIA’s F2 Sporting Regulations with the disintegration of the rules-based world order. You might do that. I’m not going to do that. All the same, there is a kind of continuum here, one that leads all the way from the trivial to the tectonic: a portal of pedantry, a spectrum of sophistry, a continuum of choosiness. Once you start messing with sport after it has finished, even in the tiniest of ways, you can be sure the big stuff is only just around the corner.Explore more on these topicsSportWorld Cup 2026Africa Cup of Nations 2025Africa Cup of NationsUSASenegal football teamMorocco football teamcommentShareReuse this content

title: ‘Don’t become the protagonist’: Andoni Iraola on the secret of Spain’s success and his role at Liverpool | Liverpool | The Guardian
description: Liverpool’s new head coach is settling into the pre-season tour in Chicago as he begins to reshape the Reds’ identity
Author: Andy Hunter
Headline: Liverpool’s new head coach is settling into the pre-season tour in Chicago as he begins to reshape the Reds’ identity
Content: Andoni Iraola trains in Chicago with the message: ‘I think things work when we are not just selfish, no? When you prioritise the situation of the one who has less.’Photograph: Nikki Dyer/Liverpool FC/Getty ImagesView image in fullscreenAndoni Iraola trains in Chicago with the message: ‘I think things work when we are not just selfish, no? When you prioritise the situation of the one who has less.’Photograph: Nikki Dyer/Liverpool FC/Getty ImagesLiverpoolInterview‘Don’t become the protagonist’: Andoni Iraola on the secret of Spain’s success and his role at LiverpoolAndy Hunterin ChicagoLiverpool’s new head coach is settling into the pre-season tour in Chicago as he begins to reshape the Reds’ identityThu 23 Jul 2026 07.00 EDTLast modified on Thu 23 Jul 2026 14.21 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleOver coffee in Liverpool’s plush hotel in Chicago,Andoni Iraolapresents his theory for the phenomenal success of Basque and Spanish coaches. It is a theory that will shape his entire approach to life as the Liverpool head coach and his attempts to bring the connection, the joy and the belief back to Anfield. It centres on the power of the collective.The Champions League, the Premier League, the Europa League, the FA Cup, the League Cup, the European Championship and, as of Sunday, the World Cup: all currently in the hands of Basque and Spanish coaches, even if Pep Guardiola has since taken his leave from Manchester City. Next season, four men from the small Basque region of Gipuzkoa will manage four of the Premier League’s main contenders, Iraola included, alongside Mikel Arteta, Xabi Alonso and Unai Emery. There is, Iraola believes, a simple explanation for the rise of the guys from Gipuzkoa and for Spanish dominance across a global stage.Liverpool primed for business: Bezos, Bhatia and the next steps at Anfield | Andy HunterRead more“They ask me why a lot of Basque managers especially have arrived to this level and why Spanish teams are winning a lot of things,” Iraola explains. “I always say that a sense of collectiveness is very important. Spain has never had the best athletes. If you analyse in swimming, boxing, track and field, Spain don’t normally win medals. But in collective sports they are very good. In football, basketball, handball, hockey, normally they have good teams. We always put the team first. An example is someone like Rodri. He didn’t score or make an assist but at the end they won the World Cup and he won best player of the tournament. Team-first was always integrated in how we were raised.”Iraola, warm and friendly despite the jetlag at the start of Liverpool’s pre-season tour of the US, then gives an immediate example of how he embodies those values. He is asked about a letter he sent toLiverpoolin January 2014 when, as captain of boyhood club Athletic Club, he rejected the opportunity to sign for Brendan Rodgers’s team. By all accounts courteous and apologetic, as well as highly unusual, the letter provides a telling insight into Iraola’s character. It is an open goal of a question to a new head coach wanting to win over a new fanbase. But Iraola declines to shoot.Following a lengthy pause, he says: “I don’t know if I want to tell this story so early in my career here. I would like to win some games first. It is a nice story but if you remember me in some months, then you can ask me again. I don’t like to do this from the beginning. I just don’t want to. It will look like I am selling myself. We need some time, and to win some games.”Iraola’s outlook is refreshing – “Don’t become the protagonist,” he says of his role – and one that Liverpool can easily identify with. Continuing with the theme of Spain’s collectiveness, he adds: “I think there has to be something in society. It cannot only just be about football. I think things work when we are not just selfish, no? When, in the end, you prioritise the situation of the one who has less. That happens also in football. The ones that don’t play, the ones who come from the academy, the ones who earn less.
