You can compare it with society.”View image in fullscreenAndoni Iraola will hope to inspire Jeremie Frimpong this season after his disappointing debut campaign at Anfield.Photograph: Nick Taylor/Liverpool FC/Getty ImagesBut, and it is a “but” that needs saying given what Iraola hopes to achieve, there were few signs of togetherness at Liverpool last season asArne Slot’s reign unravelledamid acrimony in the dressing room and in the crowd. The power of the individual took over, and the power of the team appeared secondary. Mohamed Salah’s frequent attempts to undermine his former, title-winning coach was not the only example of that last season.“I cannot change the personality of the players,” says Iraola, who will manage for the first time as Liverpool head coach on Saturday against Sunderland in Nashville. “Some players are very different to me but they are great players. I don’t want to change everyone and what they think is the best way. But I think we need to think collectively in this game. As a player you are always selfish. You always want to play, you always want to get your stats, you always want to play all the minutes, but I think there has to be an understanding that this is not going to always happen and still I have to be helpful with my teammates and with the team.double quotation markI always say we are assistants to the players. They are the main partAndoni Iraola“The first thing we have to do is try to play well, but it’s not just only about playing well. Supporters have to identify with what they see on the pitch. Sometimes it’s not about just putting one ball in the top corner. A lot of the time, the ones watching the game, OK, they cannot do that but they can run, they can fight for the balls, they can press, they can fight until the last line for every situation and when they see this from the players of their team it’s easier to get behind that team. I will encourage my team to give them these things.”Iraola never came close to signing for Liverpool 12 years ago despite the best efforts of Michael Edwards,until recently the chief executive of footballfor the club’s owners, Fenway Sports Group. “I never really wanted to leave Athletic Club,” their former right-back admits. “For me, Athletic Club was the place to be. The only way of leaving was to go to the MLS [with New York City FC] and try something completely different. I didn’t want to become a problem for the club.”The 44-year-old, however, does see parallels between his boyhood club and his new one. He explains: “This is one of the biggest clubs in the world and, in some way, the comparison to Athletic Club probably isn’t fair, but in a lot of ways I see a lot of similar things. This sense of belonging, of being a little bit different. This connection, also, with players from the academy. The atmosphere of Anfield.”At hisofficial unveiling in Liverpoollast week, Iraola said he intends to immerse himself in the city and “take some pictures” of the tourist spots. The truth is he has already done so. He visited the city a few times during his playing days with Athletic and for a very simple reason. “It had the best flight connections,” he laughs. “The best connections were Manchester and Brussels and sometimes we only had two or three days so the main thing was to go somewhere quick. I remember the first time I went to Liverpool it was already the Christmas market so it was probably around the November international break. We had no kids at the time so we are talking about a lot of years ago but it was a lovely time with my wife.”skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |Every weekdaySign up toFootball DailyKick off your evenings with the Guardian's take on the world of footballPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionThere has been no holiday this summer for Iraola and his family, now extended by two children. “I went home for one week in the half-term for the kids at the end of May,” he says. “That was once the season had ended. I have mostly been in Bournemouth but I also had some trips to Liverpool to sort the schools and the housing. This is the stuff I wanted to do before I started working officially.”Iraola describes the process of leaving Bournemouth after three impressive years as “very, very clean” and claims he only started considering his next step once last season had ended with the club in Europe for the first time. The process of becoming Slot’s replacement “was actually quite fast”. He says: “Honestly. It was true that I was talking with some clubs [Milan, Bayer Leverkusen and Crystal Palace all made approaches]. I think it was the first or second week after the season ended. But it happened fast. They contacted me, and it didn’t take a lot [to say yes to Liverpool this time].”View image in fullscreenAndoni Iraola answers questions in the US before Liverpool’s opening pre-season game against Sunderland on Saturday.Photograph: Anadolu/Getty ImagesIt hasn’t taken long for Liverpool fans to create a song in honour of their new head coach. Sung to the tune of Christy Moore’sViva la Quinta Brigada, written about Irish volunteers who fought against Franco’s fascist forces in the Spanish civil war, the song went viral within days of Iraola’s appointment in June. He is genuinely astonished when told, almost spluttering over a second cup of coffee.“A new one?” Iraola asks incredulously. “For me it is a strange situation because I always understand that football is about the players. At the beginning when I arrived in England, I remember the first few games I didn’t go with the players to clap the supporters after games. Tommy and Shaun [Elphick and Cooper, Bournemouth assistants who have followed Iraola to Liverpool] said I should join in. In Spain, normally you leave it to the players; they are the ones who have played the game and we are the ones who stayed behind. In England, managers are the ones who go to the fans, so I have done it every time.Quick GuideLiverpool and Newcastle plan Keegan tributesShowNewcastle and Liverpool are in discussions over a tribute to Kevin Keegan before the clubs meet for their opening fixture of the new Premier League season at St James' Park on 23 August.Keegan rose to prominence at Liverpool, later becoming a folk hero on the pitch and as manager on Tyneside. The former England striker's death at the age of 75 was announced on Monday, and Newcastle have confirmed that both clubs are talking about a shared tribute.'The opening Premier League match of 2026-27 sees Newcastle United hosting Liverpool – where Kevin made 323 appearances between 1971 and 1977,' a statement on the club website said. 'To ensure his memory is marked fittingly before the game, the club is in communication with Liverpool and will work closely with supporter groups ... and the Premier League, to shape tributes.'Newcastle will hold a minute's silence before their friendly at Gateshead on Saturday, while during their trip to Bristol City, Keegan’s image will be shown on the stadium’s screens in the seventh minute, a reference to the shirt number he wore during his distinguished career.In addition, the club will consult Keegan’s family 'at the appropriate time' as they consider options for a permanent memorial.PA MediaWas this helpful?Thank you for your feedback.“These are the cultural things you have to learn when you arrive in a new league. But I always say we are assistants to the players. They are the main part. Our main thing is to help them and they are the only ones who can give us success. We are not as important as they are, that is pretty clear.”The Kop may dispute that theory, but not Iraola’s take on the power of the collective if restored to full working order.Explore more on these topicsLiverpoolAndoni IraolainterviewsShareReuse this content

title: Blow for Manchester City with Rodri out for extended period due to back injury | Manchester City | The Guardian
description: Rodri could face an extended period of time out with a back injury, which may require surgery
Author: Jamie Jackson
Headline: Rodri could face an extended period of time out with a back injury, which may require surgery
Content: Rodri, who excelled for Spain at the World Cup, continues to be linked with a move to Real Madrid.Photograph: Ian MacNicol/Getty ImagesView image in fullscreenRodri, who excelled for Spain at the World Cup, continues to be linked with a move to Real Madrid.Photograph: Ian MacNicol/Getty ImagesManchester CityBlow for Manchester City with Rodri out for extended period due to back injurySpain’s World Cup-winning captain may require surgeryLeeds in advanced talks with City to sign James TraffordJamie JacksonThu 23 Jul 2026 05.10 EDTLast modified on Thu 23 Jul 2026 08.04 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleRodri could face an extended period of time out with a back injury, which may require surgery.The 30-year-old Manchester City midfielderreturned to his finest form at the World Cupfollowing two injury-plagued seasons. Having captained Spain to Sunday’s 1-0final win over Argentinain New Jersey, Rodri was awarded the Golden Ball as the tournament’s best player. Yet he may now require an operation on a back issue that is set to rule him out for the start of the season.‘A unique talent’: Phil Foden signs new four-year Manchester City contractRead moreIt would be the latest setback following two injury-hit seasons which began with the rupturing of his anterior cruciate ligament in the2–2 draw with Arsenal at the Etihad Stadium in September 2024. It ruled him out for most of the rest of that campaign before he was injured again at the Club World Cup last July. Hamstring and groin injuries followed last season, meaning Rodri started only 17 Premier League matches.The back problem comes with the Spaniard’s future at City uncertain, his contract expiring next June. While he has a desire to play in his home country again at some point and is thought to favour a transfer to Real Madrid, they are yet to make a concrete move to sign him. Enzo Maresca, City’s new manager, hopes to keep him.After the World Cup victory, Rodri spoke of his difficulties with injury. “For me, it’s been a very tough time,” he said. “I just want the new generations to see my example as an opportunity. When you go down, you can rise again. This is my philosophy for my entire life. Of course, sometimes things go well, things go wrong, but always the positiveness.”Meanwhile, Leeds are in advanced discussions to sign James Trafford for a fee in the region of £50m. The 23-year-old goalkeeper rejoined City from Burnley last summer in a £27m deal believing he would be the first choice only to see that position taken by Gianluigi Donnarummafollowing the Italian’s arrival from Paris Saint-Germain.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |Every weekdaySign up toFootball DailyKick off your evenings with the Guardian's take on the world of footballPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionTrafford started City’sCarabao Cup final victory over Arsenalin MarchFA Cup final victory over Chelseain May.Explore more on these topicsManchester CitynewsShareReuse this content

title: Chelsea paid Aston Villa £117m for Morgan Rogers. Is he the right fit? | Chelsea | The Guardian
description: The England attacker thrives when he can gallop into space but Xabi Alonso’s best team built attacks slowly
Author: Guardian staff reporter
Headline: The England attacker thrives when he can gallop into space but Xabi Alonso’s best team built attacks slowly
Content: Morgan Rogers has become the most expensive British footballer in history.Photograph: Darren Walsh/Chelsea FC/Getty ImagesView image in fullscreenMorgan Rogers has become the most expensive British footballer in history.Photograph: Darren Walsh/Chelsea FC/Getty ImagesGuardian Sport NetworkChelseaChelsea paid Aston Villa £117m for Morgan Rogers. Is he the right fit?The England attacker thrives when he can gallop into space but Xabi Alonso’s best team built attacks slowlyByOpta AnalystRyan BensonThu 23 Jul 2026 14.00 EDTLast modified on Thu 23 Jul 2026 15.32 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleIt quickly became apparent this summer that Aston Villa would not be holding on to Morgan Rogers. Having helped the club finish fourth in the Premier League andwin the Europa League, the 23-year-old was in demand.Arsenal looked like his likeliest next destination, with the champions eager to strengthen the left side of their attack. The charm offensive came from many angles, with Martin Ødegaard, Mikel Arteta and the club’s England players trying to sell the idea to Rogers. But, with Arsenal reluctant to meet Villa’s asking price, Chelsea intervened and made Rogers the most expensive British player ever, surpassing the £116m thatManchester City paid Nottingham Forest for Elliot Andersonearlier this summer.Xabi Alonso is new to Chelsea, so it is difficult to know exactly where Rogers will play. The versatile forward, who started out at West Brom before moving to Manchester City, Middlesbrough and then Villa, will probably be used in a variety of roles – often starting from the left but having opportunities to take up various attacking positions.View image in fullscreenAlonso predominantly used a 3-4-3 formation at Bayer Leverkusen and a 4-2-3-1 at Real Madrid. IfChelseaplay with three at the back, Rogers will be the left-sided No 10 in the team; if they use a four-man defence, he will start slightly wider but with the freedom to come inside – or he might even occupy the central role.Morgan Rogers ‘excited to join biggest club in London’ after making £117m Chelsea moveRead moreIf Alonso goes with the style he used at Leverkusen, Rogers might not be the best fit. During Alonso’s two full seasons in the Bundesliga, the team averaged 62.1% (2023-24) and 59.6% (2024-25) possession. During their title-winning campaign in 2023-24, they went unbeaten all season while dominating the ball and moving up the pitch slowly.They were set up to patiently build with lots of passes, win back possession quickly if they lost it and keep the ball as close to the opposition’s goal as possible. They made 367 high turnovers, which was 54 more than any other side. This system works best with attacking players who are tidy and intricate in tight spaces. However, Rogers is at his most effective when he can use his pace and gallop into space.His dribble completion rate last season was 34.2%, which was nothing to shout about. And his completion rate for passes under pressure was 73.4% – again not exceptional. Of the 161 forwards and midfielders who attempted at least 300 passes under pressure last season, Rogers ranked 143rd. The statistics suggest he is not particularly effective in close-quarters engagements.View image in fullscreenWith space to attack, Rogers is a threat. He made 223 progressive carries last season, the 10th highest amongPremier Leagueforwards, wingers and attacking midfielders. Only Cody Gakpo (41) and Matheus Cunha (35) had more carries that ended in shots than Rogers (30). He also created 16 chances for teammates after a carry: only 11 players created more. Rogers’ combined 46 chances created and shots taken following carries put him behind just Jérémy Doku (58), Gakpo (57) and Bukayo Saka (56). Alonso’s passing style may not bring the best from Rogers.There are reasons for optimism though. Rogers is frequently on the move and that makes him difficult to track. Only four players made more off-ball runs in the league last season; his 160 off-ball runs ending in the box was the eighth highest in the division.View image in fullscreenA mural of Jude Bellingham and Rogers in Birmingham.Photograph: Christopher Thomond/The GuardianHe also has the potential to form a good relationship with João Pedro. The Brazilian will probably be the focal point of the team and he is not a conventional No 9. He likes to drop deep, link with teammates and be a creator almost as much as a finisher. With Pedro dropping back, there will be room for Rogers to exploit beyond him: he made 258 runs in behind last season, the 12th highest in the league.Why did Manchester United pay Chelsea £48m for Andrey Santos?Read moreRogers’ strong relationship with Ollie Watkins bodes well for Chelsea. He created 20 open-play chances for Watkins last season, which was the second most from one player to another in the league. Wherever he has played, Rogers has developed an understanding with his teammates. He already has a close friendship with Cole Palmer after their time together in the Manchester City academy.Rogers’ other great attribute is his ball-striking ability. Since he made his Premier League debut in February 2024, Rogers has scored 21 non-penalty goals from 14.7 non-penalty expected goals. His overperformance of +6.3 is the 10th highest in the league.Rogers has a great strike on him and it could be a gamechanger for Chelsea, particularly when they play teams employing a low block. His five league goals from outside the box since his Villa debut is bettered by only six players. Few players are as accurate when shooting from distance. It was his shot against Norway that led toJude Bellingham’s winning goal in the World Cup quarter-final.View image in fullscreenChelsea have paid an incredible amount of money for Rogers and there are reasons to wonder if the move will work tactically. But Rogers has a habit of making an impact regardless of the context. He’s not a right winger but he still made his presence known forEnglandagainst Argentina in the World Cup semi-final, setting up Anthony Gordon’s opener from the right.Is he a £117m player? Perhaps not. Will he succeed playing the same way he played at Villa? Perhaps not. But it’s hard to imagine him not making a significant impact at Stamford Bridge.This is an article byOpta AnalystExplore more on these topicsChelseaGuardian Sport NetworkTransfer windowAston VillaPremier LeagueEnglandXabi AlonsofeaturesShareReuse this content

title: From Popp to Diani: 10 women’s transfers you may have missed during the men’s World Cup | Transfer window | The Guardian
description: Sam Kerr’s and Alexia Putellas’s moves were hard to miss but these transfers may have escaped your attention
Author: Suzanne Wrack
Headline: Sam Kerr’s and Alexia Putellas’s moves were hard to miss but these transfers may have escaped your attention
Content: Kirsty Hanson, Kadidiatou Diani, Alexandra Popp.Composite: Guardian Design, Getty ImagesView image in fullscreenKirsty Hanson, Kadidiatou Diani, Alexandra Popp.Composite: Guardian Design, Getty ImagesTransfer windowFrom Popp to Diani: 10 women’s transfers you may have missed during the men’s World CupSam Kerr’s and Alexia Putellas’s moves were hard to miss but these transfers may have escaped your attentionSuzanne WrackThu 23 Jul 2026 15.00 EDTLast modified on Thu 23 Jul 2026 15.47 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleThere were a host of big women’s football transfers which were hard to miss across June and July, despite the dominance of the men’s World Cup. Among them wereBeth Mead’s move from Arsenal to Manchester City,Mary EarpsandGeorgia Stanwayreturning to the Women’s Super League with London City Lionesses and Arsenal,Sam Kerr leaving Chelsea to return to Gotham FCand, most prominently,Alexia Putellas’s game-changing switch from Barcelona to London City.But here are 10 transfers you might have missed while attention was focused on the action in the US, Canada and Mexico.Alexandra Popp to Borussia DortmundThe three-time Champions League winner Alex Popp may be reaching the twilight of her playing career but her move to Borussia Dortmund, signing a three-year contract at the tier-three club, after 14 years with Wolfsburg shows she is not quite ready to hang up her boots. The 35-year-old has been prolific for club and country, scoring 112 goals in 219 games for Wolfsburg and 67 goals in 145 games for Germany prior to her international retirement in 2024. A Borussia Dortmund fan whose “heart beats for this club”, Popp couldn’t pass up the opportunity to assist the development of the women’s team, which was launched in 2020.Kirsty Hanson to TottenhamThe 28-year-old was a top target for Spurs after a phenomenal fourth season at Aston Villa. Last year Hanson said she was working on adding more goals to her game – come the end of last season she was the third-highest top scorer in the WSL, finishing on 12 in 22 games, which was 43% of Villa’s total tally and only one goal behind second-place Alessia Russo. She heads to a focused Tottenham who are giving their manager, Martin Ho, the tools to push closer to, or even break into, the top four.Ally Sentnor to Angel CitySentnor, who was the first overall pick in the 2024 NWSL draft, was the subject of a blockbuster $850,000 (£635,000) trade between Kansas City Current and Angel City on 19 June. She then made her debut for her new side on 3 July, starting a 2-0 win against Orlando Pride. The USA women’s national team player has scored seven goals and provided three assists in 23 international games and, at just 22 years old, has a lot of football ahead of her.View image in fullscreenAlly Sentnor (right) on the ball for Angel City in a 2-0 win against against Chicago Stars last weekend.Photograph: Shaina Benhiyoun/SPP/ShutterstockFelicia Schröder to Real MadridMany clubs were circling but in the end Felicia Schröder completed a big-money move from Häcken to Real Madrid. Multiple Swedish outlets reported the deal for the 19-year-old forward was worth over 15m Swedish kronor (£1.15m). She attracted attention from across the world as she racked up 91 goals in 128 games for Häcken and starred in Uefa’s inaugural women’s Europa Cup competition, finishing as top scorer with eight goals, four of which came in the two-legged final against Hammarby.Manaka Matsukubo to ChelseaThe versatile 21-year-old, who can play as a forward or attacking midfielder, joined Chelsea from North Carolina Courage for an undisclosed fee but, as one of the most exciting young talents in the women’s game, it’s unlikely she came cheap. The Japan international scored 11 goals, became the youngest NWSL player to score a hat-trick, and was named NWSL midfielder of the year in 2025. She was up to five goals in nine games in the 2026 season before Chelsea swooped earlier this month.View image in fullscreenManaka Matsukubo at her Chelsea unveiling.Photograph: Harriet Lander/Chelsea FC/Getty ImagesJohanna Rytting Kaneryd to OL LyonnesThe 29-year-old won three league titles, two FA Cups and two League Cups with Chelsea, including a domestic treble in . Rytting Kaneryd is one of several attacking players to leave Chelsea this summer with Sam Kerr, Guro Reiten and Catarina Macario also having departed as they revamp their front line in an effort to reclaim their WSL crown after Manchester City ended their six-year title-winning run.Noémie Mouchon to EvertonThe loss of the forward Kelly Gago, who has signed for West Ham, has been tempered a little by the arrival of the French striker Noémie Mouchon from Leicester. Mouchon did not have the easiest time at Leicester after arriving from Reims with expectations high. The 23-year-old sustained an anterior cruciate ligament injury one month into her first season in the WSL and was ruled out for the remainder of the campaign. She made 16 appearances and scored two goals in the WSL last season but made only two starts. Everton will be hoping she can find the form that helped her to nine goals in 22 games for Reims in the 2023-24 season.Denise O’Sullivan to Gotham (on loan)The influential Republic of Ireland midfielder returns to the NWSL with Gotham after six months with Liverpool. The 32-year-old spent 10 seasons in the US before her January move to Liverpool, with Houston Dash and North Carolina Courage (albeit with a number of loan spells in Australia and England). She won the NWSL Shield in 2017 and again in 2018 and 2019 when Courage also secured the NWSL Championship, with O’Sullivan named the side’s MVP in both years. She has 22 goals in 132 games for Ireland.View image in fullscreenDenise O’Sullivan (right) showing off her close control against Aston Villa last season.Photograph: Nick Taylor/Liverpool FC/Getty ImagesBethany England to Crystal PalaceBethany England’s Tottenham departure was mourned by fans, with the former Chelsea forward having become the team’s record goalscorer in the WSL with 32 in three-and-a-half seasons, helping them to a first FA Cup final in 2024. The 32-year-old has signed a two-year contract with Crystal Palace, who earned promotion back into the top flight with a second-place finish in WSL2. After four WSL titles, two FA Cups and two League Cups with Chelsea, England brings a wealth of experience to Palace.Kadidiatou Diani to London City LionessesThe arrival of Alexia Putellas and Mary Earps has dominated the London City Lionesses transfer talk, while the acquisition of the France forward Kadidiatou Diani from Lyonnes for more than £500,000 has flown somewhat under the radar. The 31-year-old scored 41 goals in 93 games in three seasons for the Champions League giants following her stunning 92 goals in 168 games for Paris Saint-Germain. Michelle Kang, who owns both London City and Lyonnes, will be hoping she brings that edge up front to the ambitious WSL side. They finished sixth last season, their debut campaign in the top flight, nine points behind Tottenham, but they want more and are spending big to make progress.Explore more on these topicsTransfer windowWomen's footballfeaturesShareReuse this content

title: Elliot Anderson lauds City as ‘kings of Manchester’ after £116m Forest transfer | Manchester City | The Guardian
description: ‘They’re winners, relentless, a team I want to be a part of,’ said the midfielder, who is taking a three-week break after playing for England at the World Cup
Author: Jamie Jackson
Headline: ‘They’re winners, relentless, a team I want to be a part of,’ said the midfielder, who is taking a three-week break after playing for England at the World Cup
Content: Elliot Anderson is taking a break after the World Cup and has targeted September’s Manchester derby as a game he is relishing.Photograph: Allstar Picture Library Ltd/Richard Sellers/Apl/SportsphotoView image in fullscreenElliot Anderson is taking a break after the World Cup and has targeted September’s Manchester derby as a game he is relishing.Photograph: Allstar Picture Library Ltd/Richard Sellers/Apl/SportsphotoManchester CityElliot Anderson lauds City as ‘kings of Manchester’ after £116m Forest transfer‘They’re winners, relentless, a team I want to be part of’Midfielder hails De Bruyne as a childhood inspirationJamie JacksonThu 23 Jul 2026 12.00 EDTLast modified on Thu 23 Jul 2026 14.01 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleElliot Anderson has calledManchester Citythe “kings of Manchester” after completing his £116m move to the club from Nottingham Forest.The 23-year-old was a key part of England’s third-place finish at the World Cup, having been an ever-present for Forest in the Premier League last season. BeforeMorgan Rogers’ £117m transfer to Chelsea from Aston Villa on Tuesday, Anderson’s fee was the record for a British player.Blow for Manchester City with Rodri out for extended period due to back injuryRead moreThe midfielder is taking a three-week break after his exploits in North America but is clearly relishing the prospect of playing for City, picking out the first Manchester derby of the new season on 13 September as a standout fixture. He may well be in line for a hot reception from the Old Trafford faithful given his claims about City’s superior status.“It’s one of the biggest derbies in the world and for as long as I’ve known Manchester, City have been the kings of Manchester,” Anderson said in an interview on City’s website. “It’ll be a great fixture and I can’t wait to be involved in something like that.“They’re winners, they’re relentless, and that’s the sort of team I want to be a part of – champions and people who win trophies,” Anderson said of his reason for signing for City.“Over the last 10, 12 years, they’ve dominated, so that’s why I wanted to join. It’s happened quite quick, but I think I always believed I could get to this point and this is where I want to be. Now that I’m here, I’ll keep pushing and try to take this club forward.” Anderson’s rise has been rapid, with his England debut coming as recently as last September, in a2-0 victory over Andorra.The Tyneside-born player, who began his career at Newcastle before joining Forest for £35m in July 2024, believes there is much more to come. “I’m only 23, so I’ve got plenty of time to get to my best levels and I definitely think I’m not there yet,” he said.The deal was agreed three weeks ago but finalised this week after the player’s return from the World Cup. Anderson has signed until 2031 and is City’s first major signing sinceEnzo Maresca succeeded Pep Guardiola as manager. He is looking forward to working with the Italian. “I’ve watched his teams over the past couple of years and spoke to a few of the lads from Chelsea and they all rate him very highly,” Anderson said.“I like to be intense when I play and use lots of energy around the pitch and get the fans going. So, I think that’ll be good.”Kevin De Bruyne, who departed City two summers ago, is an idol for Anderson. “Growing up through the academy at Newcastle, I’ve always wanted to play like him,” Anderson said. “I’ve watched him all the time and to be sat here in the team that he was doing it for is so special.”Anderson led the Premier League last season in most touches (3,300), most duels won (297) and winning possession (306). “I think I bring a bit of everything. I bring quality, physicality, energy,” he said. “I’d like to think I’d get them [the City supporters] excited, get them off their feet and bring energy to the team.”Anderson spent half a season on loan at Bristol Rovers in 2022 and became known as the “Geordie Maradona” among supporters. “They started that and it sort of stuck everywhere I’ve been,” he said. “It makes me happy and it gives me real confidence.”Reflecting on the World Cup, Anderson added: “It was disappointing, but I loved it personally. We had the whole country behind us and we’ve done so well but just fell short. From a personal point of view, I thought I did pretty well.”Explore more on these topicsManchester CitynewsShareReuse this content

title: Aston Villa seal loan signing of Alejandro Garnacho from Chelsea | Transfer window | The Guardian
description: The Argentinian winger was surplus to requirements at Chelsea as loan follows deal taking Morgan Rogers to Stamford Bridge
Author: John Brewin
Headline: The Argentinian winger was surplus to requirements at Chelsea as loan follows deal taking Morgan Rogers to Stamford Bridge
Content: Alejandro Garnacho signs on loan at Aston Villa.Photograph: Neville Williams/Aston Villa FC/Getty ImagesView image in fullscreenAlejandro Garnacho signs on loan at Aston Villa.Photograph: Neville Williams/Aston Villa FC/Getty ImagesTransfer windowAston Villa seal loan signing of Alejandro Garnacho from ChelseaArgentinian surplus to requirements at ChelseaDeal follows Morgan Rogers’ switch to Stamford BridgeJohn BrewinThu 23 Jul 2026 11.02 EDTLast modified on Thu 23 Jul 2026 11.11 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleAston Villa have confirmedthe loan signing of the Chelsea winger Alejandro Garnacho, as a summer of change continues at Villa Park.The deal for Garnacho’s season-long loan includes a conditional obligation to buy a player who joined Chelsea last August from Manchester Unitedin a deal worth £40m. Villa will be expected to pay a similar fee for Garnacho, who struggled to impress at Stamford Bridge despite featuring in 43 games in all competitions and scoring eight goals.Arsenal sign Greece forward Christos Tzolis from Club Brugge for £34mRead moreGarnacho has been allowed to depart Stamford Bridge after being informed he was not guaranteed regular playing time under the club’s new manager, Xabi Alonso. He did not report for duty whenChelseaplayers returned for pre-season, as pre-arranged.It was agreed he would stay away from the training ground while any departure was finalised. Named in Argentina’s initial 55-man selection, he missed out on this summer’s World Cup.“We are delighted with Alejandro. He is so talented, young and he ⁠showed us his wish to help our ​project. We are ‌really happy,” ‌Unai Emery said.Villa’s third signing of the summer, to follow the arrivals of Johan Manzambi and João Gomes, comes via a familiar trade route.The £117m deal that took Morgan Rogers to Stamford Bridgeearlier this week continued a run of transfers between the two clubs, incorporating Carney Chukwuemeka’s and Omari Kellyman’s sale to Chelsea in 2022 and 2024, Ian Maatsen heading in the reverse direction in 2024 and Axel Disasi’s brief 2025 loan to Villa.Another former Chelsea charge, Jadon Sancho, spent last season on loan at Villa from United and, despite becoming a free agent, he was not signed. Youri Tielemans has also departed Villa for Manchester United this month after his £35m release clause was triggered as the Europa League champions rebuild as a Champions League club, though one under Uefa sanction.Earlier this summer Villa received a partially suspended £19.4m fine by Uefa for a “significant breach” of squad-cost rule for 2025. Depending on the club significantly decreasing their squad-cost ratio in 2026, £12.9m is suspended, with the club restricted on the registration of new players on their squad list for the coming Champions League season.Explore more on these topicsTransfer windowChelseaAston VillaPremier LeaguenewsShareReuse this content

title: Police call on English football clubs to ease security burden on taxpayers | Soccer | The Guardian
description: The Home Office says the increase is largely down to incidents of sexism and violence against women being included for the first time in the 2025-26 season
Author: Paul MacInnes
Headline: The Home Office says the increase is largely down to incidents of sexism and violence against women being included for the first time in the 2025-26 season
Content: The Home Office says the increase is explained by incidents of sexism and violence against women being included last season.Photograph: Jason Cairnduff/Action Images/ReutersView image in fullscreenThe Home Office says the increase is explained by incidents of sexism and violence against women being included last season.Photograph: Jason Cairnduff/Action Images/ReutersSoccerPolice call on English football clubs to ease security burden on taxpayersHome Office reveals police incidents for 2025-26 seasonFour arrests for every 100,000 people attending gamesPaul MacInnesThu 23 Jul 2026 11.30 EDTLast modified on Thu 23 Jul 2026 14.39 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleThe number of football matches in England and Wales featuring incidents reported to police reached record levels last season, even as the number of arrests continued to return to pre-Covid figures.According to an annual report by the Home Office, released on Thursday, there was a 4% increase in the number of matches with reports of “football-related anti-social behaviour, violence and disorder” during the 2025-26 season. Reports were made at 1,644 games, up from 1,580 the previous campaign.Meanwhile, the Home Office calculated there were four arrests (or other actions such as voluntary police interviews) for every 100,000 people attending a men’s football match in 2025-26, with the FA Cup the most likely competition for arrests to be made. Across the women’s game, however, that number falls to four in total.Quick GuideHow do I sign up for sport breaking news alerts?ShowIn the Guardian app, tap the Profile settings button at the top right, then select Notifications. Turn on sport notifications.If you already have the Guardian app, make sure you’re on the most recent version.If you don't have the Guardian app, download it from theiOS App Storeon iPhone or theGoogle Play storeon Android by searching for 'The Guardian'.Was this helpful?Thank you for your feedback.The total number of arrests last season stood at 1,695, down from 1,744 in 2024-25 and a peak of 2,198 duringthe pandemic-affected campaign of 2021-22. Contained within these figures was an increase in the number of arrests and other actions related to public disorder, the most common type of offence.This year’s figures in part reflect a change in the data being collected. Last season, for example, the crime of “unauthorised entry” was added to the Football Spectators Act 1989. There were 45 arrests made under the new law in 2025-26, meaning total arrests and actions would otherwise have declined by 4%.New categories of “sexism and misogyny” and “violence against women and girls” were also added to the list of behaviours that can be reported by supporters, either to police or to bodies such as the FA or Kick It Out. The Home Office said 22 matches were reported for instances of sexist behaviour and 37 for violence against women. “The overall increase was therefore largely driven by the introduction of these new categories”, a Home Office statement said.Of the incidents reported, the most common type was “hate crime”, which was reported at 491 matches. The second most common was reports of missiles being thrown (359 games) and then the use of pyrotechnics (316 matches).In other data released by the Home Office, the number of football banning orders – a sanction that prevents someone convicted of a football-related crime from attending matches – issued was at its highest level since 2012. Currently there are 2,460 FBOs in place. This is up by 568 on the previous season, but a smaller rise than the 685 recorded in 2024-25.Chief constable Mark Roberts, the lead for football policing in England and Wales, said the latest Home Office report showed the need to revisit the funding of football policing, with 46% of arrests and actions taking place outside of stadiums.“Football-related crime doesn’t simply stop when fans exit the stadium, but under the current system multibillion-pound football clubs do not have to pay a penny towards the policing outside their grounds,” Roberts said. “This means that taxpayers are left footing the £70m bill each season, which simply cannot continue.”A Premier League spokesperson said: “The Premier League and its clubs work closely with police forces at a national and local level to ensure safe and welcoming environments for all fans. We are investing more than £90m a season in stewarding, security, crime prevention in communities and a series of technology-led pilots in partnership with a number of police forces and the UKFPU (United Kingdom Football Policing Unit).skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |Every weekdaySign up toFootball DailyKick off your evenings with the Guardian's take on the world of footballPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotion“This partnership approach will help the police to safely reduce the demand for policing around football matches, as the number of arrests continue to fall significantly over the last decade.”A Premier League spokesperson said: “The Premier League and its clubs work closely with police forces at a national and local level to ensure safe and welcoming environments for all fans. We are investing more than £90m a season in stewarding, security, crime prevention in communities and a series of technology-led pilots in partnership with a number of police forces and the UKFPU.“This partnership approach will help the police to safely reduce the demand for policing around football matches, as the number of arrests continue to fall significantly over the last decade.”Explore more on these topicsFootballFootball violencePolicenewsShareReuse this content

title: Arsenal sign Greece forward Christos Tzolis from Club Brugge for £34m | Arsenal | The Guardian
description: Arsenal have signed the Greece forward Christos Tzolis from Club Brugge ⁠on a long-term contract
Author: Guardian staff reporter
Headline: Arsenal have signed the Greece forward Christos Tzolis from Club Brugge ⁠on a long-term contract
Content: Arsenal unveil new signing Christos Tzolis.Photograph: Stuart MacFarlane/Arsenal FC/Getty ImagesView image in fullscreenArsenal unveil new signing Christos Tzolis.Photograph: Stuart MacFarlane/Arsenal FC/Getty ImagesArsenalArsenal sign Greece forward Christos Tzolis from Club Brugge for £34mTzolis joins Hincapié and Meslier among arrivals‘⁠It’s absolutely amazing to sign for such a big ​club’ReutersThu 23 Jul 2026 09.00 EDTLast modified on Thu 23 Jul 2026 10.31 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleArsenal have completed the £34m signing of Christos Tzolis from Club Brugge. The Greece international, 24, has signed a five-year contract with the Premier League champions and arrives as a replacement for Leandro Trossard following the Belgian’sswitch to Besiktas.Tzolis’s move to the Emirates Stadium – Arsenal’s third permanent transfer of the summer – arrives 48 hours after the north Londoners missed out on Morgan Rogers who signed a £117m British-record fee with Chelsea, describing his new employers asthe “biggest club in London”.Trossard, Ramos, Dumfries: 10 transfers you may have missed during the World CupRead moreTzolis scored 43 times in 108 appearances for Club Brugge, and lined up in the Belgians’3-0 Champions League defeatby Mikel Arteta’s side in December. “I need some time to realise it, I think,” Tzolis said. “⁠It’s absolutely amazing to sign for such a big ​club, the champions of ‌England. I’m very ‌proud to be part of this team for ‌the next season.”Arsenal’s sporting director Andrea Berta added: “We’re delighted to have completed the signing of Christos. [He] is an extremely versatile attacking player who naturally operates on the left but is comfortable across the front line. He’s an excellent finisher with both feet, thrives in tight spaces, and possesses incredible technical ability.“Christos has produced outstanding numbers in terms of goals and assists over the last three seasons, and he’s a player who will raise the technical level of our squad, while bringing positive energy, enthusiasm and a strong mentality to our team. We’re confident Christos will make a significant contribution to our club, and we hope our supporters will enjoy watching him play in an Arsenal shirt. We look forward to sharing this exciting new chapter together.”Tzolis played 14 Premier League games for Norwich during the 2021-22 campaign but his only two goals at the club came in the League Cup. Loan periods with Twente and Fortuna Düsseldorf followed before he made the move to Brugge in 2024.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |Every weekdaySign up toFootball DailyKick off your evenings with the Guardian's take on the world of footballPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionThe Greek follows Piero Hincapié – who turned his temporary deal with Arsenal into a full-time switch – and goalkeeper Illan Meslier, a free signing from Leeds, as the Gunners’ third signing. The announcement arrives as a welcome boost for Arteta after defender William Saliba was this weekruled out for an “extensive period”with the back problem the curtailed his World Cup. The France international is set to miss the start of Arsenal’s Premier League defence.Explore more on these topicsArsenalTransfer windowClub BruggenewsShareReuse this content

title: Maldini confirms crisis-hit Italy have held talks with Guardiola and Ancelotti | Italy | The Guardian
description: With the four-time world champions failing to qualify for the last three World Cups, Paolo Maldini, the Italian football federation’s new technical director, has revealed Pep Guardiola and Carlo Ancelotti are among the targets for the top job
Author: Guardian staff reporter
Headline: With the four-time world champions failing to qualify for the last three World Cups, Paolo Maldini, the Italian football federation’s new technical director, has revealed Pep Guardiola and Carlo Ancelotti are among the targets for the top job
Content: Carlo Ancelotti (left) and Pep Guardiola are targets for Italy.Photograph: Nick Potts/PAView image in fullscreenCarlo Ancelotti (left) and Pep Guardiola are targets for Italy.Photograph: Nick Potts/PAItalyMaldini confirms crisis-hit Italy have held talks with Guardiola and AncelottiItaly have failed to qualify for last three World CupsGuardiola is out of work while Ancelotti is still with BrazilAssociated PressThu 23 Jul 2026 10.41 EDTLast modified on Thu 23 Jul 2026 10.56 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleThe Italian football federation (FIGC)has talked to Pep Guardiola, the former Manchester City coach, and Carlo Ancelotti, the Brazil manager, about taking over the crisis-hit national team.It hopes to announce the new Italy coach within the week, with theAzzurristill reelingafter failing for a third straight time to qualify for the World Cup.Norway considers filing Fifa ethics complaint over Trump role in World Cup red card sagaRead morePaolo Maldini, the formerItalyand Milan defender, was named as the federation’s new technical director at the start of the month and was present at a meeting with the Serie A clubs on Thursday, along with the new FIGC president, Giovanni Malagò, and the special advisor Leonardo.“Honestly, today we can’t give news about what’s happening. You’ve identified one of our targets,” Maldini said when asked about Guardiola in a news conference after the meeting. “We can’t hide that we also talked withCarlo Ancelottibefore talking with Pep. Honestly, it seemed right to start with the best in the world, to see their general availability.”Guardiola left City at the end of last season, bringing to a close a trophy-laden, 10-year spell in which he established the club as a force in Europe and changed the face of English football. The 55-year-old has won numerous major titles across his coaching career including at Barcelona and Bayern Munich.Maldini and Leonardo spent three days in Barcelona recently to speak with Guardiola about taking the Italy job. “The best thing would be to announce the new coach this week, but even more to wait for the person we really want,” Maldini said. “So there’s urgency, but not so much inside us.”Ancelotti signed a contract extension with Brazil in May through to the 2030 World Cup. But the five-time world champions had one of their worst ever performances on football’s biggest stage as they were eliminated in the last 16 thanks toa 2-1 loss against Norway.The 67-year-old has won league titles in Italy, England, France, Spain and Germany. He is also the only coach to have won the Champions League five times.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |Every weekdaySign up toFootball DailyKick off your evenings with the Guardian's take on the world of footballPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionItaly, four-time world champions, missed out on a third straight World Cupafter a playoff loss to Bosnia and Herzegovinain March, prompting the resignations of the FIGC president, Gabriele Gravina, and the head coach, Gennaro Gattuso. Gattuso had taken over from the fired Luciano Spalletti the previous June with the team already flailing following a defeat against Norway in their opening qualifier.The former Italy coaches Roberto Mancini – who led theAzzurrito victory in the European Championship in 2021 – and Antonio Conte are also among the favourites to take charge of the national team. Andrea Pirlo, who helped Italy to win theWorld Cupin 2006 as a player, is also reportedly in contention.Explore more on these topicsItalyWorld CupPep GuardiolaCarlo AncelottinewsShareReuse this content

title: Gina Rinehart offers bonuses for Australian swimming medals as funding gap exposed | Commonwealth Games 2026 | The Guardian
description: Swimmers to earn $30,000 for a world record and $20,000 for a gold medal, while Commonwealth Games Australia provides $5,000 to each team member
Author: Guardian staff reporter
Headline: Swimmers to earn $30,000 for a world record and $20,000 for a gold medal, while Commonwealth Games Australia provides $5,000 to each team member
Content: Gina Rinehart has offered Australian swimmers cash bonuses for breaking a world record or winning a medal at the 2026 Commonwealth Games in Glasgow.Photograph: Dean Lewins/AAPView image in fullscreenGina Rinehart has offered Australian swimmers cash bonuses for breaking a world record or winning a medal at the 2026 Commonwealth Games in Glasgow.Photograph: Dean Lewins/AAPCommonwealth Games 2026Gina Rinehart offers bonuses for Australian swimming medals as funding gap exposedSwimmers to earn $30,000 per world record, $20,000 for a gold medalCommonwealth Games Australia gives each team member $5,000Australian Associated PressThu 23 Jul 2026 19.56 EDTLast modified on Thu 23 Jul 2026 21.04 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleFreestyle king Kyle Chalmers has welcomed potential windfalls for Australia’sCommonwealth Gamesswimmers after Gina Rinehart offered up cash bonuses for medals and world records.Amid a spate of high-profile athlete withdrawals from Glasgow 2026, Dolphins squad members have been given a significant incentive to take part. Billionaire Rinehart has confirmed she will deliver the same bonuses that were on offer in the pool at the 2024 Paris Olympics.Cameron Myers leads Commonwealth Games charge as Australia enters middle-distance golden eraRead moreIndividual swimmers will pocket $20,000 for a gold medal, $15,000 for silver and $10,000 for bronze and $30,000 for a world record. Relay team members will also be rewarded with $7,500 per person for a world record, $5,000 for a gold medal, $3,750 for silver and $2,500 for bronze.Australians are well placed to cash in, with Canadian superstar Summer McIntosh skipping the Glasgow Games as she and other rivals focus on the ensuing Pan Pacific championships.The swimming bonanza comes as many athletes in other sports lament a lack of financial support, despite Commonwealth Games Australia (CGA) providing a $5,000 grant to every member of the Glasgow team.“I’m super grateful to have the support of CGA to get us here, but to be able to have some medal incentive, it gives us a lot more purpose and reason,” sprint star Chalmers said ahead of the opening ceremony. “Otherwise you get a medal and the uniform ... so [I’m] very grateful to have supporters.“CGA’s made this possible, and the Commonwealth Games internationally being able to put it on, but to have some incentive for all of our hard work is crucial, especially for us older athletes.”View image in fullscreenKyle Chalmers is among Australia’s medal hopes in the pool at the 2026 Commonwealth Games.Photograph: Quinn Rooney/Getty ImagesThe 28-year-old added Australia’s swimmers are “very lucky” with the level of support they receive compared to some other sports. But the seven-time Commonwealth Games gold medallist insists it remains “very challenging” for many swimmers to juggle arduous training schedules with work and study.“It’s a choice you make and I guess you kind of understand the financial position of the sport,” Chalmers said. “But fingers crossed there can be change for the next generation to kind of motivate them to continue to make the sacrifices and choose our sport, just to keep those talented athletes in there.”skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |Every daySign up toAustralia SportGet a daily roundup of the latest sports news, features and comment from our Australian sports deskPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionRinehart’s bonus scheme was announced bySwimmingQueensland, of which the mining magnate is patron, while Australia’s chef de mission Petria Thomas distanced CGA from it.“That’s a separate arrangement and it’s not something that Commonwealth Games Australia is involved in whatsoever,” former swimmer Thomas said. “Good luck to the swimmers, I guess. They have someone backing that performance, but that’s a separate matter for them.”The bonuses come on top of annual payments Rinehart makes to many of Australia’s top-tier swimmers. Athletes who win multiple medals will receive a payment for every medal they win.In a statement, Rinehart said the bonuses are a way of Australia showing appreciation. “There is something very uplifting about seeing an Australian athlete stand on the podium while our flag is raised,” Rinehart said.Explore more on these topicsCommonwealth Games 2026Commonwealth GamesGina RinehartSwimmingAustralia sportnewsShareReuse this content

title: Rugby brain injury case reaches crisis point as judge retires to consider 95% strikeouts | Concussion in sport | The Guardian
description: The judge criticised solicitor Richard Boardman’s handling of the case as he retired to consider throwing out the landmark legal action over rugby brain injuries
Author: Andy Bull
Headline: The judge criticised solicitor Richard Boardman’s handling of the case as he retired to consider throwing out the landmark legal action over rugby brain injuries
Content: The governing bodies in rugby union and rugby league are pressing to have the claims thrown out.Photograph: Kai Schwoerer/Getty ImagesView image in fullscreenThe governing bodies in rugby union and rugby league are pressing to have the claims thrown out.Photograph: Kai Schwoerer/Getty ImagesConcussion in sportRugby brain injury case reaches crisis point as judge retires to consider 95% strikeoutsSenior Master Cook says case in ‘uncharted territory’Judge blames solicitor Richard Boardman for situationAndy Bullat the Royal Courts of JusticeThu 23 Jul 2026 13.36 EDTLast modified on Thu 23 Jul 2026 15.31 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleThe landmark legal action over brain damage in rugby is on the brink of collapse as the managing judge weighs whether to strike out 95% of the first batch of 561 claimants on the grounds they have failed to comply with a court order to disclose all available documents relating to their neurological testing.After two days of hearings at the Royal Courts of Justice, Senior Master Cook said the case had entered “uncharted territory” because he was unable to find any legal precedent for what to do next before retiring to make his judgment.It’s unclear how long he will take. Cook said that, given that absence of precedent,he needed to operate in “a careful and considered manner”. The court is due to break for summer next Friday, 31 July.The defendants – World Rugby, the Rugby Football Union and the Welsh Rugby Union in one case, and Rugby Football League and British Amateur Rugby League Association in the other – all pressed to have these cases struck out on the grounds that there had been “wholesale and widespread non-compliance” with the court order to disclose all the documents.Cook agreed with this at the high court on Thursday, but was unequivocal that it was the solicitor until this week in charge of the case, Richard Boardman of Rylands Garth, who was at fault rather than the claimants themselves. “The only complaint in this case is against the way Mr Boardman has handled the material,” Cook said. “No claimant in this case can be said in any way, shape or form to be responsible for the deficiencies in the way this case has been presented.”Boardman has notified the court of his intention to “come off the record” in the case after the claimants unanimously agreed to terminate their dealings with Rylands Garth.They are in the process of appointing new solicitors, but until they have done so Boardman remains notionally in charge despite being absent from court for the last day and a half of hearings. The firm Leigh Day has been consulting on the case since February, and is considering whether to take it on.Cook said he felt this change “raises more questions than it answers”. There are still second tranches of stayed cases in both league and union, which could conceivably proceed to trial even if this first tranche is struck out. But as the barrister representing the Rugby Football League, William Audland KC, said in court: “There may be no one that is prepared to pick up this mess.” Even if they were, it would probably necessitate more delays and expenses in a case that has been running for six years and has cost millions.Yet Cook was mindful of the severity of strikeouts, which would deny the claimants the opportunity for justice. He described them as passengers in a vehicle being driven by somebody else. “The point is simply this: you have three buses full of passengers, all going to the same destination. The first bus, the driver takes them off the edge of a cliff, with the result they do not get to their destination. The other two buses get to the destination. Let’s say they are successful, buses two and three. What of those on board bus one?”Cook told the court the case had only reached this crisis point because Boardman had given misleading evidence about whether or not he had successfully complied with the court order. The defence have been provided with thousands of documents relating to the testing, and the claimants argue that these ought to be sufficient. But there remain significant gaps, despite Boardman’s insistence in court that there were not.“Mr Boardman should have said he had complied as far as he was capable instead of making these bland statements that the order had been complied with to the letter,” said Cook.“But that’s the evidence he has chosen to put here, with not a bit of mea culpa.”Explore more on these topicsConcussion in sportRugby unionRugby leaguenewsShareReuse this content

title: Falkland Islands bowler criticises ‘disappointing’ Argentina World Cup banner | Commonwealth Games | The Guardian
description: The Falkland Islands bowler Cecil Alexander called Argentina’s World Cup banner ‘disappointing’ after his opening match at the Commonwealth Games
Author: Yara El-Shaboury
Headline: The Falkland Islands bowler Cecil Alexander called Argentina’s World Cup banner ‘disappointing’ after his opening match at the Commonwealth Games
Content: The Falkland Islands team, including Cecil Alexander (third left), at the Commonwealth Games opening ceremony in Glasgow.Photograph: Aaron Chown/PAView image in fullscreenThe Falkland Islands team, including Cecil Alexander (third left), at the Commonwealth Games opening ceremony in Glasgow.Photograph: Aaron Chown/PACommonwealth GamesFalkland Islands bowler criticises ‘disappointing’ Argentina World Cup bannerCecil Alexander is competing at Commonwealth GamesJohnson-Thompson and Asher-Smith pull out of athleticsYara El-Shabouryat SEC CentreThu 23 Jul 2026 13.30 EDTLast modified on Thu 23 Jul 2026 19.19 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleThe Falkland Islands bowler Cecil Alexander called Argentina’s World Cup banner “disappointing” after his opening men’s singles match at theCommonwealth Games.Alexander, 45, made the comments after a straight sets 10-3, 7-4 defeat by the former world No 1 Izzat Shameer Dzulkeple of Malaysia.Final hurrah? Glasgow’s cut-price Commonwealth Games fight for survivalRead moreJust over a week ago at the men’s World Cup, Argentina’s players celebrated their semi-final defeat of Englandby holding up a bannerthat read: “Las Malvinas son Argentinas”, referring to their country’s claim over the Falkland Islands. The banner reignited tensions over the South Atlantic territory, known as the Falkland Islands in Britain and Islas Malvinas in Argentina. The dispute led to the 74-day 1982 Falklands war, where 649 Argentinians and 255 Britons were killed.“It’s disappointing when politics gets mixed with sport,” Alexander told the Guardian regarding Argentina’s banner. “We are here to compete and represent our country. We keep politics out of it.”Alexander, who moved to theFalkland Islandsfrom South Africa eight years ago, said representing the British overseas territory had been a rewarding experience. “I am fairly new to the Falkland Islands but I am enjoying [living there],” he said.Argentina’s celebrations prompted the now former prime minister of the United Kingdom, Keir Starmer, to back calls for Fifa to investigate whether any rules were breached when the banner was displayed.View image in fullscreenArgentina footballers hold up a banner referring to their country’s claim over the Falkland Islands after their World Cup semi-final win over England.Photograph: Rebecca Blackwell/APStarmer’s spokesperson had said: “The World Cup might not be ours, but the Falkland Islands definitely are. Our position is unchanged. Self-determination rests with the islanders and our commitment to the Falklands will never waver.“More broadly, potential action is a matter for Fifa, but it’s been a fantastic World Cup and we’ve said throughout that politics should stay out of football.”Bowls is one of the original six sports on the Commonwealth Games programme and it has been played at every Games since 1930, with the exception of the 1966 Games in Kingston, Jamaica, where no bowls facilities were available. In a Games first, bowls and para bowls are being contested indoors in Glasgow.“We’ve been to other indoor tournaments but it’s the first at the Commonwealth Games,” said Alexander. “A little bit hot. You would think the outdoors would be hotter. But it’s good.”Star athletes add to list of withdrawalsKatarina Johnson-ThompsonandDina Asher-Smithbecame the latest high-profile withdrawals from the Games, dealing a blow to the event on the day of the opening ceremony.The pair are among nine athletes to pull out of Team England’s athletics squad, alongside the Olympic medallists Matthew Hudson-Smith, Daryll Neita and Amber Anning, as well as Zak Seddon, Anna Purchase, Elliot Giles and Jona Efoloko.View image in fullscreenKatarina Johnson-Thompson will not compete in the Commonwealth Games after pulling out.Photograph: Mast Irham/EPAJohnson-Thompson had been aiming for a third successive Commonwealth heptathlon title after winning gold on the Gold Coast in 2018and Birmingham in 2022. Team England did not give a reason for her withdrawal, although it is expected that several athletes are prioritising next month’s European Championships in Birmingham amid a congested schedule.The three-time Olympic medallist Asher-Smith had been due to compete in the women’s 4x100m relay, while Hudson-Smith, Neita and Anning were all expected to strengthen England’s medal prospects.In response to the high-profile absentees the chief executive of the Games, Phil Batty, said he was focused on the quality of talent competing in Glasgow, while also looking at athletes for whom the event will be a springboard to future success.“It’s really important to make sure we have those athletes that are at the pinnacle of their career and you can see that across the sporting programme,” he said. “It’s equally exciting to see – just like what happened in 2014 – [for] which athletes this starts their breakthrough moment.”Niamh Emerson replaces Johnson-Thompson after previously winning Commonwealth bronze behind her in 2018, while Renee Regis, Ebuka Nwokeji, Tara Simpson-Sullivan and Tom Keen also come into the team. The withdrawals follow the Olympic champion Keely Hodgkinson’s decision to skip the Games. The athletics programme begins on Monday.Explore more on these topicsCommonwealth GamesCommonwealth Games 2026Falkland IslandsKatarina Johnson-ThompsonDina Asher-SmithArgentinanewsShareReuse this content

title: Tour de France director rejects accusations of bias towards French riders | Tour de France | The Guardian
description: Christian Prudhomme also defended the night-time drug tests, adding that International Testing Agency (ITA) practices did not favour any riders
Author: Jeremy Whittle
Headline: Christian Prudhomme also defended the night-time drug tests, adding that International Testing Agency (ITA) practices did not favour any riders
Content: Matej Mohoric appeared to imply that the French rider Paul Seixas (pictured) had been given preferential treatment by the ITA.Photograph: Gonzalo Fuentes/ReutersView image in fullscreenMatej Mohoric appeared to imply that the French rider Paul Seixas (pictured) had been given preferential treatment by the ITA.Photograph: Gonzalo Fuentes/ReutersTour de FranceTour de France director rejects accusations of bias towards French ridersChristian Prudhomme also defends night-time drug testsPrudhomme says ITA practices do not favour any ridersJeremy Whittlein Orcières MerletteThu 23 Jul 2026 11.55 EDTLast modified on Thu 23 Jul 2026 14.45 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleChristian Prudhomme, the director of the Tour de France, has rejected accusations of bias towards French riders and launched a staunch defence of thenight-time drug teststhat were carried out during this year’s race.Speaking at the start of stage 18 in Voiron, Prudhomme insisted that the International Testing Agency’s (ITA) testing practices did not favour any riders.“We fought to have an independent agency, and now we have one,” he told the Guardian. “I have no desire whatsoever to interfere with their work. Not only am I not informed, I must not be informed.”Tour de France 2026: Richard Carapaz roars clear of breakaway to win stage 18 – liveRead morePrudhomme’s comments come in the wake of the Slovenian professional Matej Mohoric, who rides for the Bahrain Victorious team, appearing to imply that Paul Seixas, the 19-year-old French favourite, had been given preferential treatment by the ITA, sayingin an interview on Tuesdaythat “it would be good to guarantee equal treatment for everyone.”“After all,” Mohoric said, “we know that this French rider [Seixas], who is well placed in the general classification, didn’t undergo that [night-time] test.”The ITA stressed its independence from French anti-doping authorities on Wednesday and Prudhomme underlined the significance for such autonomy. “The UCI has ensured that there is now an independent agency (ITA),” Prudhomme said. “I find out about testing at exactly the same time as you do – or often even after you do. I’m not informed beforehand, and nor should I be.”Both the current race leader, Tadej Pogacar, and rival Jonas Vingegaard, who has abandoned the Tour, were tested at 5am and 2am respectively last Sunday morning. The tests have been described by the UCI as an “exceptional measure, reserved for limited and justified circumstances”.The ITA, Prudhomme said, was fulfilling a long-wanted role in cycling. “In 2008 the Tour was actually organised without the UCI, under the authority of the FrenchCyclingFederation, precisely so that independent anti-doping controls could be carried out.”In a statement issued on Wednesday afternoon, the UCI said that the ITA’s activities demonstrate that “the fight against doping is an absolute priority”.The statement continued: “The UCI acknowledges that night-time testing can disrupt riders’ rest and recovery.” But it added that “these controls serve an overriding interest: the fight against doping”.“The ITA is doing the job it’s paid to do,” Prudhomme said. “I understand the riders’ emotions. My only question is whether this would happen in any other sport. But as far as we’re concerned, we wanted an independent agency. That’s exactly what we have and that’s what we asked for.“Obviously these kinds of checks can’t take place every single night. In this case they happened between two mountain stages. But that’s their role. That’s what the ITA are there for.”Prudhomme also rejected the notion that the unusual nature of these tests fuelled suspicion towards Pogacar, who is closing in on a record-equalling fifth Tour win. Instead he pointed to cycling as now being a leader in anti-doping control across all sports.“Pogacar is a rider who demonstrated exceptional talent from a very young age,” he said. “At just 20 years old, he finished third in the Vuelta a España and won stages in the race, which is an extraordinary achievement.“What I ask is this: would these kinds of controls be carried out in every other major sport – including the world’s most popular one [football]? I’m not so sure.”Explore more on these topicsTour de FranceTour de France 2026CyclingnewsShareReuse this content

title: ‘Like pulling a tractor trailer’: what it’s like to play on an awful MLB team | MLB | The Guardian
description: Failure is woven into baseball. Over six months, the challenge is not just avoiding defeat but maintaining standards, accountability and belief when losses pile up
Author: Jacob Uitti
Headline: Failure is woven into baseball. Over six months, the challenge is not just avoiding defeat but maintaining standards, accountability and belief when losses pile up
Content: It’s been a rough season for the cellar-dwelling New York Mets.Photograph: Jess Rapfogel/Getty ImagesView image in fullscreenIt’s been a rough season for the cellar-dwelling New York Mets.Photograph: Jess Rapfogel/Getty ImagesMLB‘Like pulling a tractor trailer’: what it’s like to play on an awful MLB teamA 162-game season guarantees failure as much as success. Inside a losing clubhouse, staying together and believing can become the hardest part of the jobJacob UittiThu 23 Jul 2026 05.00 EDTLast modified on Thu 23 Jul 2026 05.01 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleIf you ask professional athletes, they’ll tell you. Losing is no fun. But in Major LeagueBaseball, losing big can be especially tedious.AnMLBseasons boasts a whopping 162 games (twice as many as the NBA and NHL) and even the best hitters fail two-thirds of the time at the plate. If a pitcher wins half his starts, he’s an All-Star. Because of that, says decorated starter Jamie Moyer, a losing campaign takes on a particular consequence.“It’s like pulling a tractor trailer with no tires behind you all season,” Moyer tells the Guardian. “It’s just heavy!”Moyer would know. The former All-Star and World Series champion saw it all over his 25-year career. He was on the team with the best regular-season record in baseball history (the 2001, 116-game-winning Seattle Mariners) and he was on some dogs (the 64-98 2012 Colorado Rockies). Winning covers over a team’s ills. But losing only exacerbates them, he says.“When you’re not playing well, having a tough season, whether it’s individually or as a team, things tend to rear their ugly heads a little differently,” Moyer offers.But while casual baseball fans may think to change a losing season from bad to better takes a well-timed home run or a ninth-inning comeback, Moyer, who retired in 2013 as the oldest player in MLB history ever to record a win, says it’s about subtler things.For example: maybe a player notices his teammate isn’t running to first base fast enough on a ground ball. When a lack of effort like that is shown, the player might call the other guy out on it. “Maybe it’s happening far too often,” Moyer says. “Somebody might grab somebody and take them down into the tunnel. I’ve seen it happen a lot of different ways.”Moyer says he’s seen guys come into the dugout after making a lazy effort to first and another teammate yells, “Hey,blankety-blank-blank-blank!Are you hurt? Then why aren’t you running hard out there?”Anything from a brief conversation to fisticuffs may ensue afterwards. “It goes on from there based on the answers,” Moyer notes. “I’ve been on teams where a guy didn’t hustle to first base and the manager, as soon as he got back into the dugout, would walk over to him and say, ‘Hey, I’m going to replace you right now, have a seat.’”In baseball, a number of factors determine the outcomes of games – almost too many. Talent, chemistry, preparedness, resilience. Then there are umpires, the weather, and whether or not the team made it to the hotel the night before to get enough rest on a road trip. For Moyer, success (and sanity) comes down to controlling what you can control. For him, that meant looking in the mirror.“There are times in my career when I felt it,” says Moyer, who finished his time in the majors with a record of 269-209. “We’ve lost four games in a row and now it’s my turn in the rotation. I’m having that self-talk, like, ‘Hey, pal, it’s on you tomorrow. If you want to contribute to your team,wow, what a great time to do it.’”Most players will say baseball is more a mental game than a physical one. It’s often what’s between the ears that gets a player through a long year. For former All-Star catcher Alex Avila, to sustain a long campaign, it’s essential to avoid wallowing when things get tough.“It’s easy to fall in a trap of feeling sorry for yourself, feeling sorry for the team when things are going poorly,” the formerDetroit Tigersbackstop says.But Avila has a cure – a mantra to remember when the future looks bleak.“It’s always important for major league players to remember – when you’re in the big leagues, you’re the best of the best,” Avila says. “When you’re at that level, you’ve neverthatfar away from turning it around.”A pro’s pro, the catcher knows that whether or not a team achieves the overarching goal of making the playoffs, there are alwayslittlewins to shoot for.“There were some years that by the middle or end of the season we knew we weren’t incontention,” Avila says. “And that’s a difficult pill to swallow. But there’s still quite a bit to play for throughout the rest of the year.”Every teamwantsto win the World Series, but it doesn’t simply happen by wishing. “How do you get there?” muses Avila, the son of a former MLB GM. “You can’t just snap your fingers. You have to be able to work toward those goals. Stepping stones.”As a catcher, Avila knew it was his job to play well on offense and defense. It was also his job to help others play well. Sometimes that meant dealing with personalities, sometimes that meant calling impromptu team meetings when a dark cloud hung over the team.“There were times we struggled with the Tigers even during some of our best years,” Avila says. “We’d have player-only team meetings. Not something formal or announced. It’s just that we had a tough game and now six or seven of us are in the clubhouse figuring out what we need to do, how we can change it.”More than any other sport, the baseball season is a slog. Moyer calls it a “marathon”. This season, the Mariners andthe (expensive) New York Metshave endured painful losing stretches that have affected their standings. Other teams, though, including the Tigers and the Boston Red Sox, have seen bad springs turn into fruitful summers.Playing for the Phillies in the late 2000s, Moyer says he learned a lot from his manager, Charlie Manuel, about dealing with the peaks and valleys. He picked up tips on how to navigate poor stints – and even how to stave them off before they arrived. Again, it was about smaller interactions.“He would pull players aside or talk to you early in the afternoon at the ballpark,” Moyer remembers. “Like, ‘Hey, how you doing? Hey, did you ever think about this or about that?’”When Moyer got to Philly in 2006, the team just missed the postseason. In 2007, they lost to a buzzsaw Colorado team. But all the work and all that time sitting with the bitter taste of loss helped the team win it all in 2008.“Failure,” says Moyer, “propelled us into ’08.”But that mental edge is key not just in good times, but in lean ones, as well.“Pre-game, during a bad year,” Moyer says, “I’m not thinking about losing. Whether you believe it or not, I have to believe I’m going to be successful. I have to believe the four days of work between starts has gotten me to the point where I’m ready to make my next one.”Part of that work comes off the field. Dealing with planes, hotels, workouts. Enduring thatmarathonseason can drain a person. But Moyer tries to put it all in perspective. “I liken it to somebody working in a mill,” Moyer says. “You can’t compare playing baseball to putting out a fire or operating on a human being. I didn’t look at it as drudgery.”That veteran mentality can keep a team’s season afloat even in choppy waters. Sometimes there is no controlling a bad season – fate may yield a result no one intends. But that can’t stop a roster from trying to right the ship.“Young guys would seek out my advice,” Avila says. “A season is long – 162 games is a lot. You never really know how to navigate it until you actually have to go through it. It’s a grind. But it’s agreatgrind.”Explore more on these topicsMLBBaseballUS sportsPhiladelphia PhilliesDetroit TigersfeaturesShareReuse this content

title: EU fines Google €890m for competition breaches over search and apps | Google | The Guardian
description: Firm told to treat third-party services that appear in its results in ‘fair and non-discriminatory manner’
Author: Dan Milmo
Headline: Firm told to treat third-party services that appear in its results in ‘fair and non-discriminatory manner’
Content: Google has been fined €460m for the search-related breach and €430m for the app store violation.Photograph: Steve Marcus/ReutersView image in fullscreenGoogle has been fined €460m for the search-related breach and €430m for the app store violation.Photograph: Steve Marcus/ReutersGoogleEU fines Google €890m for competition breaches over search and appsFirm told to treat third-party services that appear in its results in ‘fair and non-discriminatory manner’Dan MilmoandLisa O’CarrollThu 23 Jul 2026 06.00 EDTLast modified on Thu 23 Jul 2026 14.32 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleGoogle has been fined a total of €890m (£760m) by the EU for breaches of online competition laws by its search and app store services.TheEuropean Commission, the EU’s executive arm, said Google had broken the Digital Markets Act by giving priority to its own services, such as shopping and hotel deals, in search results over those of its rivals.It also infringed the DMA by preventing app developers from steering consumers towards cheaper offers, including for subscriptions, on websites or alternative app stores.Google has been fined €460m for the search-related breach and €430m for the app store violation. The commission has ordered the company to treat third-party services that appear in its search results in a “fair and non-discriminatory manner” and allow app developers to make offers outside Google’s app store.It noted that Google had already started testing changes to how it displayed search results featuring its own services. It said those changes represent “substantial progress towards compliance”.Consumers would be direct beneficiaries of the decision by the EU, a senior official said. “Research results will be in different inEurope. They will have to adapt their search engine going forward,” they said.Max von Thun, director of the Open Markets Institute Europe thinktank, said the fines were the “bare minimum” for a company that made revenues of just over $400bn last year.“Having finally established Google’s non-compliance, the commission must now move quickly to force Google to end its anti-competitive practices once and for all. Europe’s startups and innovators cannot wait much longer,” he said.The decision to impose the fine risks the ire of Donald Trump, only hours before a series of temporary global tariffs against about 60 countries expires.A senior official for the EU said they had no knowledge of how Trump was likely to react, insisting that the bloc had the “sovereign right” to regulate US tech companies in its own jurisdiction and that the timing of the fine was not connected to tariffs.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |Every weekdaySign up toBusiness TodayGet set for the working day – we'll point you to all the business news and analysis you need every morningPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionLast yearApple and Mark Zuckerberg’s Meta were finedunder the DMA.
