Apple was told to pay €500m for anti-competitive practices at its app store and Meta was told to pay €200m in a ruling on its ad-free“consent or pay”proposal for facebook and Instagram.Google can appeal against the decision and ask for interim measures, including a request to suspend the measure. The search company’s president of global affairs, Kent Walker, described the fine as “product degradation driven by a small group of self-serving complainants” that will have a negative impact on European businesses and consumers.He argued that the DMA forces Google “to strip away real-time search features Europeans love – like instant pricing and direct availability for hotels, flights, and restaurants – and dismantle safety protections on Google Play”.Explore more on these topicsGoogleEuropean UnionTechnology sectorEuropeEuropean CommissionnewsShareReuse this content

title: Personal and banking details among customer data stolen in Origin Energy hack | Hacking | The Guardian
description: Hackers access Australian customers’ names, addresses, dates of birth, phone numbers and some bank account details, company says
Author: Luca Ittimani
Headline: Hackers access Australian customers’ names, addresses, dates of birth, phone numbers and some bank account details, company says
Content: Origin Energy has confirmed customers’ names, addresses, dates of birth, phone numbers and some bank account details are among the personal data stolen by hackers.Photograph: Jason Reed/ReutersView image in fullscreenOrigin Energy has confirmed customers’ names, addresses, dates of birth, phone numbers and some bank account details are among the personal data stolen by hackers.Photograph: Jason Reed/ReutersHackingPersonal and banking details among customer data stolen in Origin Energy hackHackers access Australian customers’ names, addresses, dates of birth, phone numbers and some bank account details, company saysFollow our Australia news live blog for latest updatesGet ourbreaking news email,free appordaily news podcastLuca IttimaniThu 23 Jul 2026 05.55 EDTFirst published on Thu 23 Jul 2026 03.18 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleOriginEnergycustomers’ addresses, phone numbers and partial bank account data have been accessed in a hack, the company has confirmed.The firm has 4.8m customer accounts in Australia, providing electricity, fossil gas, LPG and internet services to homes and businesses.Origin has yet to confirm which, and how many, customers had been affected. In a statement to the ASX on Thursday, it said it would tell customers once it confirmed whether they had been affected.A person claiming to be the hacker has reportedly contacted media outlets with unverified claims that 2 million customers’ details were accessed.Sign up for the Breaking News Australia emailOrigin said data may include customers’ names, addresses, dates of birth, phone numbers and Origin account information, as well as the last four digits of a credit card, or the last three digits of a bank account.The company said incomplete credit card or bank account information could not be used to make purchases or access accounts.Origin first revealed the hack in a statement yesterday, saying it believed credit card or bank details had not been accessed. The company has not detailed how the hack occurred.Origin’s chief executive, Frank Calabria, said the company was securing its systems and ensuring there was no further unauthorised access, working with independent cyber experts and authorities.“I’m sorry this has happened,” Calabria said.“Customers trust Origin with their information, and I apologise for the impact this may cause.”Experts have warned customers could be vulnerable if the data is leaked and used for identity theft, or by scammers tricking people into believing they are representatives of real businesses.Rumpa Dasgupta, a lecturer in cybersecurity at La Trobe University, said personalised records could be misused for physical robberies.“In the wrong hands, this information could be exploited not only for highly targeted phishing campaigns but also to support physical crimes such as burglary by identifying vulnerable properties,” Dasgupta said.Origin said the Australian Cyber Security Centre, the Australian federal police and the Office of the Australian Information Commissioner (OIAC) were all investigating.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |When neededSign up toBreaking News AustraliaGet the most important news as it breaksPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionThe National Office of Cyber Security is leading the government’s response.The AFP declined to comment on whether it had identified or communicated with Origin’s hackers.The Australian reported it was first contacted by a person claiming to have hacked Origin on Tuesday, after which the newspaper alerted Origin, which made a statement on Wednesday.An Origin spokesperson said the company had moved immediately to update the ASX as soon as it was aware of a potential incident.The OIAC reportedit received 1,205 data breach notifications in 2025, of which 716 were related to malicious or criminal activity.Among the hacks was a leak of 5 million Qantascustomers’ informationin October, which prompted warnings scammers could cold call leaked phone numbers.The federal privacy commissioner last week found Qantas did not make any omissions or failings in breach of the Privacy Act, in relation to the hack. The Australian federal police are investigating.Explore more on these topicsHackingData and computer securityEnergynewsShareReuse this content

title: Elon Musk says he got ‘carried away’ with Trump – but still holds on to contentious political views | Elon Musk | The Guardian
description: In interview, billionaire says instead of running ‘Doge’ he should’ve ‘worked on my companies’ and shares extreme predictions
Author: Richard Luscombe
Headline: In interview, billionaire says instead of running ‘Doge’ he should’ve ‘worked on my companies’ and shares extreme predictions
Content: 1:01'I got a little too involved in politics,' says Elon Musk – videoElon MuskElon Musk says he got ‘carried away’ with Trump – but still holds on to contentious political viewsIn interview, billionaire says instead of running ‘Doge’ he should’ve ‘worked on my companies’ and shares extreme predictionsRichard LuscombeThu 23 Jul 2026 11.27 EDTLast modified on Thu 23 Jul 2026 15.51 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleElon Musk has admitted he became too consumed by politics during his volatile alliance with Donald Trump and his brief appointment as the president’s financial axman, running the so-called “department of government efficiency” (Doge).The world’s richest person, and briefly itsfirst trillionaireafter his SpaceX company floated on the New York stock exchange in June, made the confession in awide-ranging interviewwith the Economist published on Thursday. He shared his positions on subjects from foreign wars to AI, and made a number of eye-opening predictions for the future.“I got carried away, frankly,” Musk said of his relationship with the president, whom he helped get elected in 2024 with$200m in financial backing, then served during his controversial tenure as head of Doge, overseeing $150bn in“harmful” budget cutsand forcing tens of thousands of people out of federal jobs.The owner ofTesladid not expand on the statement, but appeared cognizant of the impact of his dalliance with the levers of power in Washington DC.In December, heoffered in a podcastwith Katie Miller, wife of Trump’s White House adviser Stephen Miller, that his wrecking-ball approach to the federal government had been only partially successful, and that given the opportunity he probably wouldn’t do it again.“I think instead of doing Doge, I would have basically worked on my companies,” he said at the time.Talking to the Economist, however, he appeared to relish the attention his political involvement had garnered, and the influence he believed he exerted. “People don’t realize that what I’m saying will come to pass,” he said, the outlet referring to Musk – at least in his own mind – as a modern-day Cassandra.The interview, a 90-minute conversation for the publication’s video platform The Insider, recorded at a Tesla factory in Texas, was Musk’s first since theSpaceXflotation, in which his personal wealth briefly topped $1.25tn before dropping back to a relatively penurious $750bn.He reflected on moving from “exhilaration to terror” in a single day, but was not referring to the SpaceX share price. Instead, he was talking about artificial intelligence, which he believes will ultimately render work by humans “optional”.AI systems and robots will eventually handle most digital and physical jobs, he said, and the merger of his own AI venture with SpaceX will allow him to develop systems to perform cognitive tasks such as software programming while Tesla manufactures humanoids.Musk, the Economist said, plans to power his AI venture with datacenters in space, which experts have warned will be“catastrophic” for Earth.Within five years, Musk said, AI intelligence systems may surpass the sum of all human intelligence. Within 10 years, robots in the workplace will help usher in an era of such “amazing abundance” that money will become meaningless.It was, however, Musk’s political views, and predictions, that were perhaps the most contentious parts of the interview. The UK, a country he has not visited for years, but whose politics, especially on immigration and race, have become arecent obsession, will become engulfed in a civil war within 20 years, he claimed.Asked if he was anti-Muslim, Musk attacked the questioner, a policy of diversion similar to that which has served Trump well, and which he has employed previously, notably calling the Guardian “irrelevant” when itdecided to withdrawfrom his “toxic” social media platform X.“I’m against rape and murder. I’m against the imposition of rules and laws that are contrary to what we’ve come to accept in the west,” he said. “It’s a crying shame that traditional media, like you, don’t recognize this.”He described the media’s characterization of Europe’s far right as “false, misleading and nonsense”, and argued that it was made up of “normal people” seeking safe cities, secure borders and sensible spending, the Economist said.In ananalysispiece accompanying the interview, the outlet acknowledged that Musk was a divisive figure. “His political views, disseminated to his 240m followers on X, strike many as plain-speaking and strike many more, including The Economist, as plainly bigoted,” it wrote.“But he is also one of a handful of men who are pioneering AI and who thereby have an outsize influence on its trajectory. When he speaks, he reflects the debates they are having. His datacentres in space could power AI’s future.“For all his political polemics, he has a record of being right about technology in fields such as electric cars, rockets and satellite communications that confounded other engineers and entrepreneurs.”Another Musk prediction: after AI destroys jobs, there may need to be a large-scale wealth redistribution to create “universal high income” for humans.He said he would be willing to pay “trillions in tax” then, conversely, argued that money will become irrelevant inside 10 years. In which eventuality, he said, governments should “issue people cheques”, because, he said, amid an infinite supply of goods and services produced by machines, deflation will be a bigger problem than inflation.He also shared thoughts about the Ukraine war, in which his Starlink satellites have been used to help that country’s military against Russia’s aggression. Despite this, Musk said any peace deal should include territorial gains for Russia.The interview concluded with an assessment by Musk of his achievements and ambitious current projects, in AI and in space, a field in which he has also made a number of bold predictions. He said in 2024 that humans couldset foot on Mars by 2028(currently Nasa is targeting 2028 for a return to the moon, which many analysts think is unlikely).The universe, Musk said, according to the Economist, could be a computer simulation created by aliens. “The things I’m doing are so preposterous that it’s hard to believe they’re real,” he said.Explore more on these topicsElon MuskUS politicsTrump administrationTeslaSpaceXAI (artificial intelligence)newsShareReuse this content

title: The roaring 20s: how the current decade revolutionised cinema | Movies | The Guardian
description: Pre-Covid, the movie landscape was dramatically different: YouTubers weren’t dominating the box office, video game spin-offs rarely made an impact and Winnie-the-Pooh wasn’t yet the star of a slasher movie. Why such a flurry of innovation – and what will be next?
Author: Stuart Heritage
Headline: Pre-Covid, the movie landscape was dramatically different: YouTubers weren’t dominating the box office, video game spin-offs rarely made an impact and Winnie-the-Pooh wasn’t yet the star of a slasher movie. Why such a flurry of innovation – and what will be next?
Content: Horror adaptation … David Howard Thornton as Steamboat Willie in Screamboat.Photograph: SKLAE/Signature EntertainmentView image in fullscreenHorror adaptation … David Howard Thornton as Steamboat Willie in Screamboat.Photograph: SKLAE/Signature EntertainmentMoviesThe roaring 20s: how the current decade revolutionised cinemaPre-Covid, the movie landscape was dramatically different: YouTubers weren’t dominating the box office, video game spin-offs rarely made an impact and Winnie-the-Pooh wasn’t yet the star of a slasher movie. Why such a flurry of innovation – and what will be next?Stuart HeritageThu 23 Jul 2026 11.00 EDTLast modified on Thu 23 Jul 2026 12.21 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleCinema has always been a medium of research and development. At any given point, the industry is searching for yet another breakthrough that can drive it into the future. Synchronised sound in the 1920s. Technicolor in the 1930s. The invention of the modern blockbuster in the 1970s.Conversely, the consensus is that, if the 2020s has a defining trend, then it is one of decline. The theatrical experience is under attack from all angles: streaming,YouTube, a once-in-a-century health crisis that shut cinemas down entirely. And yet, if you look closer, this has actually been one of the most innovative decades that cinema has ever seen. Whether down to technology or desperation, the film industry has spent these last few years figuring out new ways to make films that simply could not have existed even at the turn of the decade.Currently, the most notable of these is the way that cinema has become a conversation with YouTube. Back in 2005, when Jawed Karim uploaded the first video (the 19-second Me at the Zoo) to the platform, YouTube felt like a novelty. It was cheap and cheerful, a collection of cat videos and people falling over. How things have changed. Not only has it become the largest streaming platform in the world, but the first generation of adults who grew up with YouTube has come of age.View image in fullscreenCreepypasta comes good … Chiwetel Ejiofor in Backrooms.Photograph: A24/APAs we speak, the seventh and eighth biggest films of 2026 have been directed by people who learned their trade making content for YouTube. Obsession (worldwide gross: $442m) was directed by Curry Barker, a 26-year-old who dropped out of film school to make YouTube videos and TikToks. Meanwhile, Backrooms (worldwide gross: $384m) is even more explicitly internet-inspired. Directed by the then 20-year-old Kane Parsons, the film is based on a series of shorts he began uploading to YouTube in 2022, which in turn were inspired by somecreepypasta(web-based horror content) that has been floating around online since 2019.A decade ago, studios would have got this disastrously wrong. Ask them to adapt a YouTube video and they would have hired Michael Bay and Rihanna to makeDavid After Dentist: The Motion Picture; it would have baffled anyone who didn’t watch YouTube – and appalled anyone who did. This is the first time that the industry has trusted natives to make the films they want to make. Clearly, given the enormous financial returns, it will not be the last. Steven Spielberg is now producingAlex Kister’s The Mandela Catalogue, based on a terrifying analogue horror series that has run on YouTube since 2021.This would also be a good time to mention the rise of the copyright expiry movie. In 1998, the Copyright Term Extension Act was passed in the US, which extended protection to works to 95 years after they were first published. (In the UK, the rules are different; copyright extends to 70 years after the creator’s death.) This effectively meant that two decades went by without anything going out of copyright. When things finally caught up in 2019, freeing up anything made in 1923, there was a goldrush.View image in fullscreenRuining your childhood … Winnie-the-Pooh: Blood and Honey 2.Photograph: Publicity imageNow, let it be said that it wasn’t a particularlygoodgoldrush. First out of the blocks was 2023’sWinnie-the-Pooh: Blood and Honey, a low-rent slasher movie whose only original thought was to have Winnie-the-Pooh in it. Then cameThe Mouse TrapandScreamboat(both based on Steamboat Willie, the first element of the Mickey Mouse characterto have lost copyright protection), along with Popeye the Slayer Man andBambi: The Reckoning. Without exception, these films are cheap, exploitative and burn out their premise long before the end credits – but more are to come.Next year, if you can wait that long, will see the release of something calledPoohniverse: Monsters Assemble, which will star – deep breath – Winnie-the-Pooh, Tigger, Bambi, Peter Pan, Tinker Bell, Pinocchio, Sleeping Beauty and the Cheshire Cat. It is also worth pointing out that King Kong enters the public domain in 2029, which will be brilliant news for anyone who has been longing to see a low-rent horror movie starring an unusually large gorilla.Now, here is where I may lose you, because I am about to name Sonic the Hedgehog as the most important movie of the decade. Please bear with me. One of the most pervasive trends of this decade has been the way that, for good or ill, audiences have hijacked movies to their own ends. The biggest example is obviouslyBarbenheimer, in which Barbie and Oppenheimer were released on the same day, kickstarting a phenomenon where people bought tickets to see one straight after the other. Nobody in the film industry expected this, but the publicity from this grassroots celebration drove receipts of both films through the roof.View image in fullscreenDoomed by irony … Morbius.Photograph: Landmark Media/AlamyThe flipside to this isMorbius, a terrible film that inspired no end of ironic online mockery – but what was hilarious was that Sony mistook the irony for sincere support, andrereleased the film on 1,000 screensto cash in. It goes without saying that nobody watched it. Also, think of all the extra teenagers who bought tickets for Minions: The Rise of Gru, purely so they could watch dressed in suits and babble nonsense to participate in theGentleminions trend, or the people who went to see A Minecraft Movie exclusively to scream and chuck stuff about whenJack Black said the words “chicken jockey”.No film, arguably, has benefited from fan reaction quite like Sonic the Hedgehog. The first we saw of it was anightmarish trailerfeaturing an unrecognisable main character. Sonic had bizarrely human proportions, as well as tiny eyes and teeth that looked as if they had been stolen from someone’s mouth while they slept. It was awful, and the internet wasted no time in saying that it was awful. In times gone by, studios would have simply taken the hit and released the film as quietly as possible, writing it off as another godawful video game adaptation destined to die on impact. But this time, producers listened. The trailer was released on Tuesday. By Thursday, the director had tweeted that the character would be completely redesigned. Six months after that,New Sonic was unveiled, and fans were delighted.View image in fullscreen‘Teeth stolen from someone’s sleeping mouth’ … a still from the first Sonic the Hedgehog trailer.Photograph: Paramount PicturesWith a character that people could actually stand to look at, Sonic the Hedgehog went on to become the sixth biggest film of 2020. Nobody saw this coming. Until Sonic, video game adaptations were universally written off as the lowest form of cinema. Think of thedismal live action Super Mario Bros movie, or Mark Wahlberg’sbaffling Max Payne, or Uwe Boll’s irredeemablePostal. About 46 English language video game adaptations were released between 1993 and 2019, and they made a combined total of $5.4bn. But A Minecraft Movie made close to a $1bn on its own. The animated Super Mario Movie released in 2023 ended the year second only to Barbie. At time of writing,The Super Mario Galaxy Movie is the biggest film of 2026so far worldwide. By demonstrating to other film-makers how to give people what they actually want to see, Sonic has turned the video game adaptation into a licence to print money.As you will agree, this makesSonic the Hedgehogby far the most important film of the decade. And the future will only bear this out further, once Mr Beast signs on to direct Sonic the Hedgehog 5 and someone makes a rubbish horror film about a blue hedgehog, as soon as the rights lapse in 2087.Explore more on these topicsFilmHorror filmsSonic the HedgehogBackroomsYouTubeGamesfeaturesShareReuse this content

title: There Are No Ghosts at the Grand – stripping back the physical and metaphorical layers of a haunted hotel | Games | The Guardian
description: Renovate rooms, investigate the supernatural and watch people burst into song in this surreal British ghost story
Author: Jordan Erica Webber
Headline: Renovate rooms, investigate the supernatural and watch people burst into song in this surreal British ghost story
Content: A very British sensibility … screenshot from There Are No Ghosts at the Grand.Illustration: Friday SundaeView image in fullscreenA very British sensibility … screenshot from There Are No Ghosts at the Grand.Illustration: Friday SundaeSummer games previewsGamesThere Are No Ghosts at the Grand – stripping back the physical and metaphorical layers of a haunted hotelRenovate rooms, investigate the supernatural and watch people burst into song in this surreal British ghost storyJordan Erica WebberThu 23 Jul 2026 09.00 EDTLast modified on Thu 23 Jul 2026 09.01 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleAyoung American man arrives in a dilapidated British seaside town to take on the renovations of an equally run-down hotel, inherited from a mysteriously absent father he never knew. His power tools talk to him in a Scottish accent, the local people speak in baffling slang, and occasionally people burst into song. Oh, and there are ghosts.There Are No Ghosts at the Grand is a novel mix of genres – no bad thing in these times of sequels and remakes – but, according to the creative director of independent developersFriday Sundae, Anil Glendinning, it grew organically from a single goal: to tell a very British ghost story.Based in Bristol, Anil and his partner and co-founder Rachel Glendinning have ready access to the UK’s abundance of stately homes. “We spent a lot of time wandering around National Trust properties,” says Anil. “Peeking around some of the back rooms that are guarded furiously by red ribbon tape and pensioners who tell you not to go back there. What we discovered was rooms that were half in disrepair and half returning to a state that they may have looked [like] back in Edwardian or Victorian ages. And we thought: wouldn’t that be such a huge amount of fun, to do that in a video game?”View image in fullscreenSecret spaces … There Are No Ghosts at the Grand.Illustration: Friday SundaeHence the main interaction: using a range of power tools to strip away mouldering wallpaper, rearrange furniture, vacuum up detritus, and apply a fresh coat of paint. (And, at night, to fight ghosts.) As you work, other characters chat about your choices, the room, its history. Many are based on real people, even the animated paintbrush in your toolkit, inspired by people Anil knew growing up in Scotland: “On the surface, our Robert C McBrushy character is an ode to Microsoft’s Clippy. But actually, he is a father figure, but one that spans a spectrum of different fathers, from one who is thoughtful and gives you wonderful advice, to one who loses his temper and berates you.”Several of the game’s quirkier features evolved in similar ways. Notably, the musical numbers were another British twist: “There’s a very blurry line between comedy and horror, particularly within UK movies and culture. We have always written our own music for the game, and during one sequence we thought we would play with the idea of the character actually singing it. It felt quite natural, being quite surreal, and having the players question if what they were seeing was real.”The game features voice talent including musical theatre performer Alex Brightman (of Beetlejuice the Musical fame) and Marcia Richards (from London band the Skints), but there is an option to skip the musical numbers. Drawing from his experience working on motion-controlled games for the Microsoft Kinect, Anil is keen to offer customisations to suit a range of players, such as an autocomplete slider for those unwilling or unable to paint every centimetre of a wall.Where they won’t make accommodations is, again, in the Britishness of the game, with no glossary for any slang. The actors collaborated in the creation and expression of their characters, leading to a natural style that might not always translate, even to other English-speakers, as with the (unannounced) British-Jamaican singer who voices Colin: “We allowed them to improvise and speak naturally in their own mix of patois and British English and find this really interesting natural code-switching, depending on which character they’re speaking to. So it feels really authentic.”The aim is for the characters you meet and the town they inhabit to feel like the real Britain that the developers know, in all its diversity. If, like the American protagonist, you feel a bit out of your depth; well, that’s kind of the point.There Are No Ghosts at the Grand is in development for PC and Xbox Series X/S. Release date late 2026.Explore more on these topicsGamesSummer games previewsRole playing gamesPuzzle gamesfeaturesShareReuse this content

title: Like Trump, I went through a home-renovation obsession. The results are often disappointing | Jackie Delamatre | The Guardian
description: We are now a nation of homogenized open concepts and curb appeal. It’s an approach that leads to anxiety and isolation
Author: Guardian staff reporter
Headline: We are now a nation of homogenized open concepts and curb appeal. It’s an approach that leads to anxiety and isolation
Content: ‘Why do any of us put ourselves through this torturous process?’Photograph: Andrew Leyden/Zuma Press Wire/ShutterstockView image in fullscreen‘Why do any of us put ourselves through this torturous process?’Photograph: Andrew Leyden/Zuma Press Wire/ShutterstockOpinionDonald TrumpLike Trump, I went through a home-renovation obsession. The results are often disappointingJackie DelamatreWe are now a nation of homogenized open concepts and curb appeal. It’s an approach that leads to anxiety and isolationThu 23 Jul 2026 10.00 EDTLast modified on Thu 23 Jul 2026 11.44 EDTShareRecently, Jonathan Swan – one of the authors of a new tell-all about Donald Trump’s second term –told the hosts of The Viewthat if you had a pie chart of Trump’s mind, “a pretty substantial slice is devoted to interior decorating”. He also revealed that Trump is engaged in “design wars” with Melania and moved furniture she had selected into his spaces. The commander-in-chief apparently has even been caught supergluing gilded accents to the Oval Office mantel himself. Though Trump claims taxpayers won’t be on the hook for the costs of his renovations, the New York Times reported that the White House’s 18 major remodeling projects – from the botched Lincoln Memorial reflecting pool to the paved-over Rose Garden to the looming “Arc de Trump” – will probablycome with a price tagof $1.2bn.Of course, Trump’s renovation compulsion didn’t start this year. The president celebrated winning his first term, in 2017, by ripping out the Oval Office’s royal blue carpet with the presidential seal andreplacing itwith a Mar-a-Lago peach version, accented with – what else? – gold. But in his second term, he’s gotten bolder, both politically and aesthetically – and more addicted. By the autumn of his first year back in office, he went ahead andtore downthe East Wing entirely.DC’s ‘renovated’ pool reflects the Trump administration’s dangerous hubris | Arwa MahdawiRead moreAnd, OK, I get it. In January, my husband and I bought a money pit of a house. In this economy, we counted ourselves lucky. But, within the first 24 hours of ownership, we had a fire, a leak, and the boiler burst, filling the basement with several inches of rusty water. There went the aesthetic tweaks we’d planned, not to mention the furniture. The problem was that my Instagram algorithm had already picked up on my new life stage. Soon, I was plagued by an irrational, unyielding fear of ugly rooms. I wanted nothing but to designmaxx. I began to give up an inordinate amount of my brainpower to rules like: “One throw per sofa, draped not folded. Folded is a store display. Draped is a home.” I actually battled it out with my tween over the “rule of three”. In desperation, one day, encouraged by aZillow studyabout which hues raise property values, I painted a room sage green only to realize all my furniture clashed. (I believe they call it “scope creep”.) If, at that moment, I’d been supplied with an Ikea hack for transforming discount chair cushions into a padded room, I would have felt I’d alighted upon the perfect decorating scheme.In other words, this stuff could make anyone crazy. In onesurvey, 12% of new couples (together five years or less) considered separating or divorcing during the remodeling process. Inanother, more than two-thirds of millennials reported moderate to high stress amid remodeling, with 40% saying it strained their relationships. Yet so many of us keep coming back for more. What began with the Puritans and their stonewalls, their European notions of private property, has spawned an entire channel devoted to renos. On HGTV, the Property Brothers alone have 10 spin-offs. When Edith Wharton tried to sell her interior design guide, The Decoration of Homes, in 1897, it was rejected by publishers. No one, they told her, would be interested. But the interior design market hit over$185bn globally in 2025– and is projected to reach $298bn by 2033. We are now a nation of open concepts and curb appeal and pops of color, of mid-century and farmhouse sinks and shiplap and Homegoods, homes that look “good”, yes, but often strikingly the same.In arecent essayfor the New Yorker, Jia Tolentino describes a similar homogenizing phenomenon with faces, surgically or AI-enhanced, “increasingly, more technically beautiful but more spiritually unattractive”. Trump’s design choices may not be your standard Joanna Gaines style, but, in Washington DC, the decorating analogue for Mar-a-Lago face is massive, gilded anything. All this uniformity is enough to make you long for Jerry Seinfeld’s cluttered cereal shelves or the Friends’ violet walls or even Archie Bunker’s threadbare wing chair.Why do any of us put ourselves through this torturous process? Is it all for resale value? To keep up with the Joneses? Or for the positiveemotional effectsof built environment factors like natural light, wood furniture and high ceilings? Most perplexing of all: why is Trump, in the middle of a war, plunging approval polls and an inflation crisis, devoting mental space to the future of a home he doesn’t even own? Is it a classic response to feeling out of control, or perhaps an attempt at denying reality? (“Like the Administration,” writes Tolentino, “Mar-a-Lago Face locates power in the spectacle of dismissing reality altogether.”) Or is Trump just the most extreme version of our narcissistic instinct to put our stamp on spaces, to claim ownership, like a boy with a Sharpie tagging everything around him?‘Make your homes weird,’ urges an interior designer. Me? I’ve a stuffed magpie and three pewter goats | Emma BeddingtonRead moreUnfortunately for our president, one thing I’ve learned is that on the other side of a reno does not always lie satisfaction. Only 52% of couples reported feeling happier in their newly renovated homes. In the HBO show DTF St Louis, the female lead’sraison d’êtreis a redo of her son’s bedroom decor. Spoiler alert: she gets it but loses a lot more. And Annetta Grant, now an associate professor at Bucknell University, confirmed this kind of disappointment in her 2023study, with a nod to HGTV’s impact. Americans, she learned, have stopped designing their houses for themselves. Because of home-improvement media (read: everything from Better Homes and Gardens to Pinterest), increasingly, the changes homeowners are making are for resale value, or as she puts it, “a market-reflected gaze” – and that means they decorate in a more and more homogenized style. It’s an approach that leads to anxiety and isolation, as homeowners worry about being judged by visitors at the same time that they don’t feel their homes reflect their own preferences.Recently, I started to lust after the kind of homes where no one is thinking about the proper way to size a rug – homes you can relax in, homes covered in Legos and board game pieces. I blocked social media on my phone in an attempt to limit the kinds of Instagram visits that convince me I need a custom monogrammed cross stitch kitchen towel. Instead, I started reading Middlemarch. In it, George Eliot writes that a “betrothed bride” often “dictates” changes to her future home before marriage only to discover: “The mistakes that we male and female mortals make when we have our own way might fairly raise some wonder that we are so fond of it.” We may think what we want is what we want, but it’s often not what we need at all.Trump, for his part, may think what he needs is a new ballroom complete with bunker or just one more gilded vase, when what he really requires is a gut reno of his relationship with himself – and the American public.Jackie Delamatre is a freelance writer based in Providence, Rhode IslandExplore more on these topicsDonald TrumpOpinionHomescommentShareReuse this content

title: Brett Kavanaugh has become Trump’s chief enabler on the supreme court | Sidney Blumenthal | The Guardian
description: The justice’s opinions have offered the president workarounds to enact his agenda despite clashes with the court
Author: Sidney Blumenthal
Headline: The justice’s opinions have offered the president workarounds to enact his agenda despite clashes with the court
Content: ‘Kavanaugh acts as though he is in an ex-officio capacity to the administration’s legal staff writing option memos.’Photograph: Chip Somodevilla/Getty ImagesView image in fullscreen‘Kavanaugh acts as though he is in an ex-officio capacity to the administration’s legal staff writing option memos.’Photograph: Chip Somodevilla/Getty ImagesOpinionBrett KavanaughBrett Kavanaugh has become Trump’s chief enabler on the supreme courtSidney BlumenthalThe justice’s opinions have offered the president workarounds to enact his agenda despite clashes with the courtThu 23 Jul 2026 08.00 EDTLast modified on Thu 23 Jul 2026 12.30 EDTShareOf the conservative justices on the supreme court, Clarence Thomas and Samuel Alito have in the current sessionvoted most consistently, at 89 and 88%, to support Donald Trump in his cases, according to the Wall Street Journal. Yet perhaps the most influential justice to advance key parts of Trump’s radical agenda has a slightly lower rating at 76%, and performed not so much by billowing lofty abstractions of originalism and textualism, but instead by contriving an assortment of glib schemes at justification.Mayhem in the city streets, violence, even killings, renewed rounds of Trump’s threats and shakedowns around the world through damaging tariffs, and chaos within the Congress around birthright citizenship, all of which helped fracture the divided Republican leadership on the eve of the midterm elections, have followed the opinions issued byBrett Kavanaugh. By the end of this session, he has clearly emerged as the chief enabler to operationalize Trump’s most salient political issues.Kavanaugh issued an unusual sole concurringopinionon 8 September 2025 in Noem v Vasquez Perdomo, staying a lower court’s temporary restraining order that had restricted ICE from targeting individuals based primarily on race, language or occupation. Kavanaughstatedthat stopping people based on ethnic appearance, speaking Spanish and laboring at certain jobs was a “relevant factor” and “common sense” to establish “reasonable suspicion”. Justice Sonia Sotomayor, in a blistering dissent, accused the court of legitimizing “racial profiling on a massive scale”. She wrote prophetically: “We should not have to live in a country where the Government can seize anyone who looks Latino, speaks Spanish, and appears to work a low wage job.”Legal scholars were even more devastating in their analyses of Kavanaugh’s opinion. His “invocation” of “common sense” came “at the expense of facts and evidence”, Jennifer Chacón, professor and vice-dean at Stanford Law School,wrote. “Note the number of factual assertions … supported by nothing more than ‘common sense.’ I am not omitting his citations to evidence; there are no such citations.” Duncan Hosie, an academic fellow at Stanford Constitutional Law Center,describedKavanaugh’s claims as “factual revisionism” (ie, falsehoods), “a previously unrecognized form of precedent subversion that operates as a strategic tool to reshape legal doctrine while managing the Court’s dwindling sociological legitimacy”. “Almost every word of this is preposterous,”wroteSherrilyn Ifill, professor at Howard Law School, about Kavanaugh’s opinion. “What we have seen repeatedly are not ‘stops.’ They are grabs and kidnapping.”The supreme court’s takedown of American democracy is complete | Austin SaratRead moreSince Kavanaugh’s rationalization of what have become known as “Kavanaugh Stops”, within the first month more than 170 US citizens were detained, “dragged, tackled, beaten, tased and shot”, according to aProPublica report, whilethrough July of this year, 32 American children have been detained, 31 people have died at ICE detention camps, six people have been shot and killed, including two US citizens, Renee Good and Alex Pretti in Minneapolis, and in July 2026 two men who were not the actual targets ICE agents were seekingwere killed.The sharp criticism of Kavanaugh’s opinion apparently embarrassed him. In an unrelated case, Trump v Illinois, in December 2025, Kavanaugh inserted a non sequitur as an obscure footnote repudiating his previous language. Now hewrote: “Officers must not make interior immigration stops or arrests based on race or ethnicity.” But Stephen Miller has paid no attention to the discreet footnote. The “Kavanaugh Stops” have continued, lately in Texas and Maine with fatal consequences.The method Kavanaugh applied to ICE abuses he also applied to Trump’s tariffs in the principal dissent on 20 February in Learning Resources, Inc v Trump, in which a six-to-three majority struck down Trump’s claim that he had the absolute power to impose tariffs at will under the International Emergency Economic Powers Act (IEEPA). Trump angrilycalledthose who ruled against him “fools” and “lap dogs”, but said of Kavanaugh: “I’m so proud of him.”Kavanaugh’sopinionagain rested on factual revisionism, citing “history, and precedent” to justify Trump’s unilateral action, claiming it was “a traditional and common tool”, and appealing once more to “common sense”. Kavanaugh had in fact invented an imaginary historical pattern. No president before Trump had used IEEPA in its 49-year history to levy broad tariffs. Kavanaugh distorted the definition of a tariff, not as an import tax whose constitutional power to assert lies with the Congress, but rather a weapon of national security and foreign policy that belongs to the executive. Then Kavanaugh engaged in highly unusual and borderline improper speculation about other non-litigated economic and trade statutes that “might justify most (if not all) of the tariffs at issue in this case”,laying out a roadmap for Trump to skirt the court’s denial of his unconstitutional use of emergency powers.double quotation markBy the end of this session, [Kavanaugh] has clearly emerged as the chief enabler to operationalize Trump’s most salient political issuesTrumpannouncedwithin hours his workaround: a 10% global temporary import surcharge using section 122 of the Trade Act of 1974, which on 7 May theUS court of international tradestruck down as illegal. More recently, Trump has threatened further tariffs–a25% tariffon Brazil,100% tariffson Britain and European nations that enforce digital services taxes against US tech companies, and a sudden punitive50% tariffon most Canadian goods – all likely to be subject to new lawsuits if and when enforced.Then came Kavanaugh’s coup de grace of the session, his strange dissent in the birthright citizenship case on 30 June,Trump v Barbara, in which he stated that Trump’s executive order violated federal law but was not unconstitutional. His argument was a compound of “factual revisionism”, historical nonsense, sophistry, illogic and political consulting. His syllabus of errors was tightly scripted:double quotation markFirst, significant illegal immigration into the United States is a new circumstance that was largely unknown as of 1868 and that the Framers of the Fourteenth Amendment could not have fully anticipated. And the Framers likely would not have anticipated (and presumably would not have intended) the odd result of granting a substantial birthright citizenship benefit to (i) those foreign citizens who violate U. S. immigration law and illegally enter or overstay and then have children in the United States over (ii) those foreign citizens who follow U. S. immigration law and have children in their home countries while seeking to lawfully immigrate to the United States … If Congress amends §1401(a) or otherwise enacts a statute creating new exceptions along the lines of the Executive Order for children born to foreign citizens unlawfully or temporarily in the country, such a statute, as I see it, would pass constitutional muster.While there was no “significant illegal immigration” as currently understood before 1868, chiefly because there were no federal legal restrictions, the Trump administration has invoked the Alien Enemies Act of 1798 for its policy of mass deportation. And before the first major federal restriction laws – the Page Act of 1875 aimed at Chinese women and the more sweeping Chinese Exclusion Act of 1882 – the colonies and then the states handled “illegal immigration”. The idea of illegal immigration was quite familiar to the framers of the 14th amendment, who openly discussed Chinese and other immigrants in connection to birthright citizenship. “If the 14th Amendment framers wanted to exclude the babies of temporary visitors (people not domiciled), children of unauthorized migration, or people who ‘owed allegiance’ to another country as the dissenters assert, they would have said so. They didn’t,”wroteAnna O Law, constitutional scholar at Cuny Brooklyn College, and author of Migration and the Origins of American Citizenship.Kavanaugh’s sheer speculation about what “the Framers likely would not have anticipated (and presumably would not have intended)” is mind reading in a time machine about a nonexistent legal category projected forward. Based on a fantastical fiction, he has reverse-engineered the originalist method into parody. His reasoning, such as it is, is an example of the well-known formal fallacy of arguing backwards from consequences, or “consequentialist” illogic.Kavanaugh piles on to this rubbish heap “the odd result of granting a substantial birthright citizenship benefit”, based on how he conjectures the 14th amendment’s framers would have reacted to their own constitutional creation. He guesses they would have shared his opinion and were already thinking about sorting out classes and castes of immigrants, even though they were deliberate and definitive in establishing a universal right. That they carved out two narrow exceptions, for children of foreign diplomats and of Native American tribes not taxed, showed the care they took. They purposely included, as a matter of constitutional rights, those very categories of immigrants Kavanaugh bizarrely speculates they would have excluded.The supreme court has again undermined the power of Congress | Moira DoneganRead moreKavanaugh’s suggestion that Trump revive his executive order by a supporting act of Congress would not “pass constitutional muster” as he insists it would. It should be elementary that a legislative bill cannot void the 14th amendment as properly understood, even by a majority on the current court. But, predictably, within minutes, Trump hailed Kavanaugh’s idea. “We can easily make it up in legislation,” hesaid. “No long and unwieldy Constitutional Amendment is necessary!” The speaker of the House, Mike Johnson, instantlyechoedTrump: “If there’s some legislative fix, we’ll advance that immediately,” he chimed in. And a bill was proposed that has no chance of ever being enacted by the Congress, but that if it were would be struck down under the recent court ruling.With his grab-bag of gambits, Kavanaugh mostly manages to avoid the more elaborate pretensions of other conservative justices, their conceits of originalism and textualism. He gets straight to the heart of achieving the desired political result. The conclusions of his opinions, after a bit of legal artifice, read like the political memos that they are. He offers a certain kind of cleverness that relies on sleight of hand, locates the cynical loophole, invents references if any at all, but which implodes to its hollow core upon examination.Kavanaugh’s controversial nomination in 2018 centered around allegations of high school sexual misconduct that he denied. In the uproar, his apprenticeship as a staffer was largely overlooked. He worked as anassociate special counselfor Ken Starr,whom he convincedto launch an investigation into the suicide of Clinton White House deputy counsel Vince Foster, the subject of myriad rightwing conspiracy theories, after the previous special counsel had already issued a report attributing Foster’s death to his untreated clinical depression. That Starr report wound up affirming the original report, but in an effort overseen by Kavanaugh dragged out the bogus inquiry for three years of political exploitation.As one of the lawyers working for the George W Bush campaign in the Florida contest of 2000, along with the young John Roberts and Amy Coney Barrett, Kavanaughadvancedthe notion that because counties counted votes differently, the vote count as a whole should be halted. This became the basis for the arbitrary Bush v Gore decision, an apotheosis of partisanship and blatant corruption of the 14th amendment’s guarantee of equal protection under the law – a ruling that even its author, Justice Antonin Scalia, laterconfessedwas “a piece of shit”.Now, wearing the black robe, Kavanaugh charts roadmaps for Trump on how to work around adverse decisions and present ever more sly cases in the future. Kavanaugh acts as though he is in an ex-officio capacity to the administration’s legal staff writing option memos. The gamester has matured into the justice of the supreme court of counsel to Trump. That is something rarer to find, and far more powerful, than the next Roy Cohn.Sidney Blumenthal, former senior adviser to President Bill Clinton and Hillary Clinton, has published three books of a projected five-volume political life of Abraham Lincoln:A Self-Made Man,Wrestling With His AngelandAll the Powers of Earth. He is a Guardian US columnist and co-host of theCourt of History podcastExplore more on these topicsBrett KavanaughOpinionDonald TrumpUS supreme courtTrump administrationLaw (US)ICE (US Immigration and Customs Enforcement)Trump tariffscommentShareReuse this content

title: The real aim of Trump’s New York Times leak investigation is to punish reporters | James Risen | The Guardian
description: A decade ago, experts said I’d be the last reporter to face a subpoena in a leak investigation. How wrong they were
Author: James Risen
Headline: A decade ago, experts said I’d be the last reporter to face a subpoena in a leak investigation. How wrong they were
Content: ‘Whether the case proceeds or not, it is just Trump’s latest excuse to punish reporters and news organizations.’Photograph: Spencer Platt/Getty ImagesView image in fullscreen‘Whether the case proceeds or not, it is just Trump’s latest excuse to punish reporters and news organizations.’Photograph: Spencer Platt/Getty ImagesOpinionDonald TrumpThe real aim of Trump’s New York Times leak investigation is to punish reportersJames RisenA decade ago, experts said I’d be the last reporter to face a subpoena in a leak investigation. How wrong they wereThu 23 Jul 2026 06.00 EDTLast modified on Thu 23 Jul 2026 07.27 EDTShareIn 2015, after I won aseven-year battle with the federal governmentand refused its demands that I reveal my confidential sources for a story I wrote about a botched CIA operation, many media experts predicted that I would be the last journalist ever subpoenaed by the government in a leak investigation. They argued that new surveillance technology made it possible for the government to more readily identify sources inside the government without having to rely on the testimony of reporters. There was no longer any need for the government to go through the lengthy and bitter legal process of trying to haul reporters into court and threaten them with jail if they refused to reveal their sources.Fast forward more than a decade, and it is obvious that those predictions from the experts were wrong. The notion that leak investigations are primarily designed to find leakers now seems quaint.The New York Times reportedearlier this monththat the Trump administration has subpoenaed its reporters in a leak investigation related to a story about problems with the plane that Donald Trump received as a gift from Qatar.One thing about this case is already clear: Donald Trump’s priority isn’t hunting down the sources for such a routine account of White House problems. What he appears to care most about ispunishing reporters. Issuing subpoenas to reporters and threatening them with jail if they don’t reveal their sources is just one more weapon that Trump wants to use in his broad campaign to damage press freedom. That was underlined once again on Monday, after it was disclosed that the Trump administration has added a vicious legal tactic in this case by seeking not only to subpoena the phone records of the reporters involved in the story, but alsothe phone records of their family membersgoing back months.The most remarkable thing about this entire episode is the unremarkable nature of the New York Times story at the heart of the case. Trump flew to the recent Nato summit in Turkey on the Qatar-provided plane but left on the old Air Force One.The Times reportedthat there had been security problems with the Qatar plane thatled to the switch. That’s the kind of story that is published on a daily basis in Washington. The administration’s claims that the story revealed sensitive national security information are laughable; Trump himself said on Monday that the plane needed to be “maxed out”.This case is so specious that it should be quickly tossed out of court. But whether the case proceeds or not, it is just Trump’s latest excuse to punish reporters and news organizations. Prosecuting some government official whom no one has ever heard of wouldn’t satisfy him. (Alternatively, it is also possible that the source is someone in Trump’s inner circle, and Trump definitely wouldn’t want to prosecute someone who knows where all the bodies are buried in his administration.)It’s clear he wants to go after theNew York Times.Such an obvious abuse of power has always been the danger behind leak investigations – that an autocratic president and a captive justice department, enabled by a puppet Congress, could misuse the legal process solely to imprison reporters and punish news organizations.With New York Times subpoenas, Trump is brazenly escalating his attacks on the press | Margaret SullivanRead moreDraconian leak investigations like this one are a fairly recent phenomenon, and were a byproduct of the post-September 11 era. Before September 11 and the Iraq war, government leak investigations almost never led anywhere; they were usually quietly dropped by the government without any sources being prosecuted or journalists being subpoenaed. It wasn’t until the so-called Plame case, which began in 2003, that reporters began to face a real threat of being subpoenaed. The Plame case was a complicated episode involving stories in which the covert identity of a CIA officer was publicly disclosed; several reporters were subpoenaed to testify about who revealed the CIA officer’s identity. The case set a dangerous precedent by normalizing the aggressive use of leak investigations by the government, and they became common in the George W Bush, Obama and first Trump administrations. I was among the reporters embroiled in those legal fights.Today, Trump may find his attacks on press freedom and efforts at censorship satisfying, but they are beginning to undermine his agenda in unintended ways. While Trump has been waging a war with Iran, he has found it nearly impossible to win public support for the war – at least in part because he has refused to tell the press what is going on. The war has started and stopped repeatedly with virtually no public explanation from Trump; he has mostly just offered boastful and callous social media posts. In addition, the Pentagon’s decision to keep reporters away from the war in order to avoid inconvenient questions about combat operations has had the unintended effect of making it impossible for the press to offer a full account of the actions of the US military personnel involved in the war. That means there are no narratives about the war for the public to embrace. Instead, the public has been left to fill in the blanks without much real information. The result is that the Iran war – the first in modern times without any significant press coverage – is theleast popular war on record.Even as Trump’s assault on the press continues, I still believe that my seven-year fight against the government was worthwhile. I do think it made government officials in several administrations more reluctant to subpoena other reporters for more than a decade. It is only because a would-be autocrat is now in the White House that all bets are off.James Risen is a Pulitzer prize-winning journalistExplore more on these topicsDonald TrumpOpinionNew York TimesUS press and publishingNewspapersNewspapers & magazinescommentShareReuse this content

title: Why would Trump agree to the Saudi nuclear deal? | Kenneth Roth | The Guardian
description: The agreement offers the Saudis the same options Trump has sworn Iran will never have
Author: Kenneth Roth
Headline: The agreement offers the Saudis the same options Trump has sworn Iran will never have
Content: ‘Why would Trump agree to such a deal? Money seems to have played a big part.’Photograph: Ken Cedeno/AFP/Getty ImagesView image in fullscreen‘Why would Trump agree to such a deal? Money seems to have played a big part.’Photograph: Ken Cedeno/AFP/Getty ImagesOpinionTrump administrationWhy would Trump agree to the Saudi nuclear deal?Kenneth RothThe agreement offers the Saudis the same options Trump has sworn Iran will never haveThu 23 Jul 2026 05.00 EDTLast modified on Thu 23 Jul 2026 11.55 EDTShareThe stark contrast between Donald Trump’snuclear dealwith Saudi Arabia and his nucleardemandsfor Iran would be comical if not so tragic. Trump has handed the Saudis the same nuclear options he swore Iran would never have.To make matters worse, Trump seems to have offered the Saudis this sweetheart deal to make amends for hisdisastrouswar of choice with Iran. Meanwhile, he continues to bomb Iran in a push toopenthe strait of Hormuz, which wasnotclosed until Trump launched his counterproductive war.As recently as this past April,Trump reiteratedin discussing Iran: “There will be no enrichment of uranium.” That goal had become more urgent because, once Trumpripped upthe nuclear accord negotiated by Barack Obama, Iran stepped up its enrichment tonearthe level needed for an atomic bomb. When Israel and the United States started bombing Iran in June 2025, Iran alsobarredindependent UN inspectors.So what does Trumpofferthe Saudis? The possibility, after a joint study, to enrich uranium (and reprocess spent atomic fuel) and to bar inspectors from certain sites that they might deem suspicious. The lack of inspectors would mean there is little to prevent the Saudi government from transforming a civilian nuclear program into a clandestine weapons operation, despite Saudi Arabia (likeIran) being asignatoryof the nuclear Non-Proliferation Treaty.US signs landmark nuclear deal with Saudi Arabia that could pave the way for domestic nuclear enrichmentRead moreThe Saudi crown prince hassaidthat his country would develop a nuclear weapon if Iran did. Notably, Trump’s deal is much less stringent than the 2009dealnegotiated by George W Bush and Barack Obama with the United Arab Emirates, Saudi Arabia’s neighboring ally-turned-competitor.Trump describes himself as having mastered the “art of the deal”, but his agreement with the Saudis is likely to torpedo any more restrictive deal with Iran. Iran is already being asked to forsake a nuclear weapon while Israelhasthem. Any claim that Israel can be trusted to handle its weaponry responsibly is belied by itsgenocidein Gaza and its decimation of much of thatoccupied territoryas well as southernLebanon. Now Iran must consider that its Saudi adversary could also develop a bomb.Why would Trump agree to such a deal? Money seems to have played a big part. The deal could providetens of billions of dollarsto the US nuclear industry if it were to sell reactors to the Saudis. Suddenly the principle that Trump said was worth fighting for can be jettisoned for the right price.I suspect part of the reason is Trump’s effort to revive an alliance that his war has jeopardized. Trump has long had asoft spotfor the Saudi crown prince, who possesses the kind of monarchial power that Trumpcovets. Like other Gulf Arab states, Saudi Arabia had looked to the United States as a security partner, evenwantingto formalize the arrangement with a treaty.But Trump’s war has made the partnership risky. Rather than the United States protecting its regional allies, the war provided an excuse for Iran to attack them, targeting the US militarybaseson their soil and sometimes their petroleum and otherinfrastructure.About the only good thing that can be said about Trump’s giveaway to the Saudis is that it reflects a continuedlesseningof the genocidal Israel’s influence in Washington DC. An earlier version of a Saudi nuclear deal waspremisedon the Saudi government opening diplomatic relations with Israel. That requirement has now been abandoned. Riyadh has said that recognition is off the table without “a credible and irreversible path to a Palestinian state”. Netanyahu is doing all he can topreventthat.As the nuclear rationale for war wears thin, Trump’s secretary of state, Marco Rubio, has begun tostressthe importance of Iran allowing the strait of Hormuz to reopen. Trump has gone so far as tothreatenwar crimes – bombing Iran’s bridges and power plants – for every Iranian attack in the strait, even though war crimes by one sideneverjustify war crimes by the other.But the straitwasopenuntil Trump’s war encouraged the Iranians to retaliate asymmetrically by closing it. Now, Trump’smemorandum of understandingwith Iran – another brilliant stroke by the master negotiator – implicitlyallowsIran to charge fees after 60 days.Has Trump dragged the US into a forever war in Iran? | Mohamad BazziRead moreIn other words, the main remaining justification for war has become a matter that was never an issue before Trump’s war, while Trump has effectively given away with his Saudi deal a main rationale for starting the war.There of course is another factor behind the continued bombing. Trump seems to be seeking a face-saving concession from Iran before his Republicans face a midtermdebacle. But that is hardly a reason for more bloodshed and destruction.Revelation of the Saudi deal comes as the Trump administration is trying to persuade Congress to fund a deeply unpopular war that it had nosayin launching. The defense secretary, Pete Hegseth, says it has cost$37.5bnso far. That is about what it would cost for, say, a year’srental assistancefor 2.3 million low-income people, or to enable about 7 million people to maintain theirhealth insurance.And to make matters worse, Hegseth wants a total of$67.1bnto keep the war going. Because Trump cannot articulate a persuasive rationale for his pointless war, Congress should say no. This war never should have started and now clearly should not continue.Kenneth Roth is a Guardian US columnist, a senior fellow at Yale University, and former executive director of Human Rights Watch. He is the author ofRighting Wrongs: Three Decades on the Front Lines Battling Abusive GovernmentsExplore more on these topicsTrump administrationOpinionSaudi ArabiaIranDonald TrumpNuclear weaponsUS-Israel war on IranMiddle East and north AfricacommentShareReuse this content

title: It’s not just a No 10 in the north, it’s an engine for growth throughout the whole UK | Andy Burnham | The Guardian
description: Power has been too centralised in Britain and tackling that is key to progress and prosperity. This initiative is ambitious: we’re remaking the state, says UK prime minister Andy Burnham
Author: Andy Burnham
Headline: Power has been too centralised in Britain and tackling that is key to progress and prosperity. This initiative is ambitious: we’re remaking the state, says UK prime minister Andy Burnham
Content: Andy Burnham confirming the No 10 plan at his first cabinet meeting, London. 21 July 2026.Photograph: WPA/Getty ImagesView image in fullscreenAndy Burnham confirming the No 10 plan at his first cabinet meeting, London. 21 July 2026.Photograph: WPA/Getty ImagesOpinionAndy BurnhamIt’s not just a No 10 in the north, it’s an engine for growth throughout the whole UKAndy BurnhamPower has been too centralised in Britain and tackling that is key to progress and prosperity. This initiative is ambitious: we’re remaking the stateAndy Burnham is prime minister of the United KingdomThu 23 Jul 2026 17.30 EDTShareOn Monday, while the cameras were fixed on that famous black door in Downing Street, something important was happening 165 miles away. A small team of officials and advisers were walking into an office building in Manchester to set about the remaking of the British state.This was the beginning ofNo 10 North: the first major act of this government and what I hope will be seen as its most defining.Over the course of the past 40 years, Britain has taken a series of wrong turns. In the 1980s, political power was centralised, economic power was privatised and communities were deindustrialised. Many of the places most affected have not recovered since and are still in decline. They have been unable to turn things around because they simply do not have the necessary power to do so. This feeling of neglect has lain underneath the increasingly fractious politics that has bedevilled Britain in recent times.Solving this problem is crucial for the cohesion of our country. It is something with which it has wrestled for a long time but never got right. We have seen gimmicks, such aslevelling up. We have seen the shifting of bits of the British state around the map to no great effect.Why has none of it worked? It is partly because, even though Whitehall departments have set up outposts, decision-making power has remained in London. It is also because, in the very centre of government itself, Nos 10 and 11 have not been sufficiently aligned – particularly on growth.This is what No 10 North is seeking to correct and, if we succeed, it could bring the much-needed sea change in the way Britain is run. It is about driving growth from the most powerful office in the land and doing so in close alignment with the Treasury. It is about taking power down and placing it in the hands of people who can use it best.No 10 North will be responsible foraccelerating devolution. It will drive reindustrialisation and growth across every postcode of the UK. It will remove barriers to regeneration, from council housebuilding to public transport infrastructure. Crucially, No 10 North will bring the whole of Whitehall together behind places rather than standing in their way. Our long-term ambition is clear: to achieve equivalent living standards across all the regions and nations of the UK.Giving places their own power matters because, without it, they cannot do those things. Growth cannot be imposed from the top down, but instead has to be nurtured from the bottom up. Britain has spent decades trying unsuccessfully to do the former and now, through No 10 North, it is about to give the latter a proper go.People will have heard me talk about wanting the nation to face the same way and pull together. That example has to be set by government. Whitehall departments need to stop fighting turf wars and start pulling together behind the places that desperately need their support.I know change is not always easy and that this is the biggest rebalancing of power our country has seen. When I entered Downing Street, I told staff that the era of fighting devolution is over. No one in government loses when power is shared. When you lift up a place, you lift up Britain.I know the power of devolution is real. A decade ago, I left Westminster to go back home and try to change things for the better. And we did. We brought buses back under public control. We created new opportunities for young people. We built a city-region withtwice the economic growthof anywhere else in the UK. What we did in Greater Manchester, we can do across our nation.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |WeeklySign up toMatters of OpinionGuardian columnists and writers on what they’ve been debating, thinking about, reading, and morePreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionThe Treasury will check Andy Burnham’s ambitions – unless old orthodoxies are challenged | Larry ElliottRead moreTomorrow, from No 10 North, I will chair the first meeting of the National Economic Council, bringing together senior ministers and regional mayors. Through this forum, we will ensure every department in Whitehall gets behind the growth and devolution ambitions of every region.I want Britain to believe again. That means creating a country where everybody feels a part of what is happening and everybody feels heard. This government will demonstrate to everyone that they are part of the story.Later this year, we will bring forward a 10-year plan for Britain, laying out a path from where we are now to where I believe we all want the country to be. Our plan will set out how we bring the essentials in life back under strongerpublic controlto make them affordable again; how we reindustrialise every part of Britain; and how we build more council homes and solve the housing crisis.I know people cannot wait forever to see change. So we will do smaller things too to provide some breathing space. We have already done this with two tax cuts: one onVAT on electricity bills for householdsover winter and another onbusiness rates for pubs, social clubs and live music venues.The new government has shown in its first five days how it is prepared to take the small steps to make life easier now and the big ones to put Britain back on the right long-term path. Both are needed simultaneously if we are to carry people with us.I am under no illusions about how hard this is all going to be, but I have a clear plan and I know how to get things done. And this week has left me in no doubt that the civil service is ready to come on this journey with me too.Andy Burnham is the prime minister of the United KingdomDo you have an opinion on the issues raised in this article? If you would like to submit a response of up to 300 words by email to be considered for publication in ourletterssection, pleaseclick here.Explore more on these topicsAndy BurnhamOpinionDevolutionLabourcommentShareReuse this content

title: Could I endure a frigid Canberra winter? Not in my wildest, electric dreams | Brigid Delaney | The Guardian
description: The best part of my day was sliding into that boiling bed! But then I’d wake at 1am like a piece of lasagne that is burnt on one side and frozen on the other
Author: Brigid Delaney
Headline: The best part of my day was sliding into that boiling bed! But then I’d wake at 1am like a piece of lasagne that is burnt on one side and frozen on the other
Content: ‘Everyone I met in Canberra had developed elaborate winter rituals that [involved] layering, thermal underwear, gloves, possum-fur socks and hats, specialty curtains that absorbed the cold and ducted heating,’ writes Brigid Delaney.Photograph: Volodymyr Melnyk/AlamyView image in fullscreen‘Everyone I met in Canberra had developed elaborate winter rituals that [involved] layering, thermal underwear, gloves, possum-fur socks and hats, specialty curtains that absorbed the cold and ducted heating,’ writes Brigid Delaney.Photograph: Volodymyr Melnyk/AlamyOpinionWinterCould I endure a frigid Canberra winter? Not in my wildest, electric dreamsBrigid DelaneyThe best part of my day was sliding into that boiling bed! But then I’d wake at 1am like a piece of lasagne that is burnt on one side and frozen on the otherThu 23 Jul 2026 11.00 EDTLast modified on Thu 23 Jul 2026 20.09 EDTShareOne of my earliest memories as a child is my little hands plugging in the old electric blanket for my visiting grandmother and then being violently thrown across the room after it short-circuited.Being electrocuted was a strange and horrible sensation. I don’t even remember crying out as a nasty current ran through my body like I was a human extension cord. I was literally shocked (just as the name of the affliction promised), bewildered that such a thing could happen, staring at the soot on my hands, then up at my ashen grandmother.It strikes me as strange that decades later, in this time of such enormous technological breakthroughs, Australians in their cold, poorly designed houses still rely on a rubber bag filled with boiling water pressed against their chests, and/or a mesh undersheet with an electric current running through it as their main source of night-time heating.Final draught: simple ways to keep your home warm in the winter monthsRead moreThat some people combine the boiling water rubber bag and the electric current blanket and blissfully surrender their consciousness for eight hours is wild to me.Think of all the ways it could go wrong: the bag bursting on to your bare skin, scalding it, being electrocuted when the water hits the electric grid that you’re lying on top of and then the bed catching fire after the grid short-circuits.I’d rather be cold.When I first started working at Parliament House in 2022, a veteran journalist with a terrifying reputation pulled me to one side to give me some advice aboutCanberra.I braced myself, expecting something along the lines of “stay away from the meditation room after dusk” or “avoid [name redacted] at the Kingo”. But instead she said, “Flannel.”“What?”“Flannel. You need flannel.”In order to survive a brutal Canberra winter I needed to buy good flannel sheets and flannel pyjamas, she said.The cold in Canberra, everybody told me, was not like other cities. It was not like the wet cold of Melbourne or the mild cold of Sydney.This was a cold that had teeth, that made you feel alive. Cold so brittle and sharp it was not unforeseeable that your toes, fingers and ears may fall off if exposed.Everyone I met in Canberra had developed elaborate winter rituals that didn’t just involve flannel but layering, thermal underwear, gloves, possum-fur socks and hats, specialty curtains that absorbed the cold and ducted heating.In my two-star energy-rated apartment, waking up to condensation on the inside of the windows and feeling as though I had frozen in the night to a solid block of ice, flannel did not cut it. I relented and bought an electric blanket. I made someone else plug it in – just in case it was faulty – and when they weren’t flung across the room, I quickly became a convert.The start of the nights with the electric blanket were heaven, so good in fact that my bedtimes kept getting earlier and earlier. To emerge from the cold night into an even colder house, then slide into the level-three heat lodge of my bed, felt like being placed in a searing hot sandwich press.‘Just tape your mouth shut!’ Can I unlearn the breathing habits of a lifetime? | Brigid DelaneyRead moreIt was bliss. The best part of my day was sliding into that boiling bed!But then the inevitable would happen. Having turned the blanket on at level three then fallen asleep without having turned it down, I would partially wake at 1am like a piece of lasagne that is burnt on one side and frozen on the other. I would be still, somehow, in a deep sleep, horrifically hot, partially frozen, yet unable to turn the blanket off because the cord was too far away – so just lying there, having anelectric sleepthat buzzed with malevolent, dangerous energy.I would wake up groggy and unrefreshed.Now it’s winter again, and the electric blanket is remaining in its box. I have discovered that the journalist’s advice is correct. You can’t go wrong with flannel.Brigid Delaney is the author of five books, including The Seeker and the Sage. She can be found on Substack at the Chaos EraExplore more on these topicsWinterOpinionCanberraHousingcommentShareReuse this content

title: One day, your life and future may depend on a jury. The integrity of that legal process is in peril right now | Imran Khan | The Guardian
description: When a barrister is cited for contempt because of his argument to a jury, as happened in a Palestine Action trial, the risks affect us all, says Imran Khan KC
Author: Imran Khan
Headline: When a barrister is cited for contempt because of his argument to a jury, as happened in a Palestine Action trial, the risks affect us all, says Imran Khan KC
Content: ‘Public confidence in the criminal justice system depends upon proceedings being conducted fairly.’Photograph: George Clerk/Getty Images/iStockphotoView image in fullscreen‘Public confidence in the criminal justice system depends upon proceedings being conducted fairly.’Photograph: George Clerk/Getty Images/iStockphotoOpinionTrial by juryOne day, your life and future may depend on a jury. The integrity of that legal process is in peril right nowImran KhanWhen a barrister is cited for contempt because of his argument to a jury, as happened in a Palestine Action trial, the risks affect us allPalestine Action barrister’s prosecution criticised as ‘chilling’Thu 23 Jul 2026 07.00 EDTLast modified on Thu 23 Jul 2026 08.12 EDTShareWithin days of Mr Justice Johnson’s decision to seekcontempt proceedings against Rajiv Menon KCbecause of that barrister’s closing speech to the jury in a Palestine Action criminal damage trial, I happened to be making a closing speech to a jury. Neither the case nor the personnel of my case are relevant to what happened next, but it is telling that counsel for the prosecution appeared to invoke Mr Justice Johnson’s imprimatur to rebuke me for stepping over the boundary as to what was acceptable to say to the jury.Thankfully, nothing of substance came of that. Not so for Menon. On 22 June 2026, Mr Justice Johnson handed down his approved judgment in Menon’s case such that he now faces a possible two-year prison sentence for defending his client in what was, it seems to me, in accordance with his professional duties and obligations.As a highly experienced and successful member of the English bar, Menon was giving his closing speech in a case that involved pro-Palestine activists who broke into an Elbit Systems UK arms factory. The trial judge alleged that Menon deliberately ignored directions and misled the jury by discussing jury equity and the war in Gaza.The judgment is significant not only for those directly affected by practising in the criminal courts but society as a whole because it starkly demonstrates how words spoken by those defending their clients – the traditional fortress of forensic freedom – can attract the machinery of contempt. The seriousness of that needs to be understood. The prospect of contempt proceedings is in danger of imposing a chilling effect on the willingness of defence advocates to test, for example, institutional misconduct themes robustly before a jury.As I experienced first-hand, the risk will discourage advocates from deploying precisely those tools that the adversarial system contemplates. Defence counsel in cases involving allegations of police misconduct, for example, must be able to put credibility challenges with vigour, to name dishonesty where the evidence supports it, and to invite jurors to engage empathetically with the defendant’s experience. If such language can attract a contempt referral, advocates will face an unwelcome calculation: whether to advance the strongest case open to them or to temper their rhetoric in order to insulate themselves from future jeopardy.The right to a fair trial, protected by article 6 of the European convention on human rights and embedded in the common law long before that instrument was enacted, encompasses the right to effective legal representation. Effectiveness, in a jury trial, requires more than the formal presence of counsel; it demands that counsel be free to deploy every legitimate forensic tool available to challenge the prosecution case. Public confidence in the criminal justice system depends upon the perception – as much as the reality – that proceedings are conducted fairly and that no party enters the courtroom at a structural disadvantage.A climate in which defence advocates self-censor out of fear of contempt undermines that perception. If it becomes publicly known that counsel in cases involving police misconduct temper their submissions to avoid personal exposure, reasonable observers will question whether the adversarial process retains its integrity.
