That erosion of confidence is corrosive: the legitimacy of jury verdicts – acquittal and conviction alike – rests in part upon the assurance that they were returned after the defence case was pressed as fully and fearlessly as the evidence permitted.View image in fullscreenRajiv Menon KC.Photograph: Garden Court ChambersThe quality of jury deliberation is also imperilled. Jurors are lay decision-makers. They depend upon counsel to distil complex factual and legal questions into accessible, persuasive narrative. A closing speech that is moderated by the advocate’s anxiety about personal sanction is likely to be less effective. The result is not necessarily that the jury reaches the wrong verdict, but that it does so with a thinner and less complete presentation of the defence case than the evidence would have supported.The risk of wrongful conviction must be a key factor here. It is an uncomfortable but empirically grounded observation that a disproportionate number of wrongful convictions in England and Wales have involved some form of police misconduct, whether in the gathering of evidence, the presentation of testimony or the suppression of material. Cases of that kind depend heavily upon vigorous adversarial challenge in the closing speech. If that challenge is muted, the risk that a factually innocent defendant is convicted is materially increased. The chilling effect of Menon’s case thus carries a direct, if unquantifiable, contribution to the wrongful conviction risk that the system as a whole must bear.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |WeeklySign up toMatters of OpinionGuardian columnists and writers on what they’ve been debating, thinking about, reading, and morePreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionYou may not sympathise with the Elbit four’s methods. But you should be outraged by their treatment under the law | Geoffrey RobertsonRead moreAs for barristers themselves, if senior advocates perceive that hard cases carry a personal professional hazard not faced in more conventional litigation, the rational response is to avoid that kind of work. Defendants in such cases will find themselves represented by advocates who are either less experienced or less willing to advance their case with full forensic force. What price, then, the quality of justice?But what is most fundamentally at stake is the constitutional value of fearless advocacy itself. The principle that counsel must be free to advance a client’s case without fear of personal retribution is not a professional privilege; it is a constitutional safeguard. It reflects the understanding, embedded in centuries of common law, that the state – which commands the resources of the police, the Crown Prosecution Service and the wider apparatus of prosecution – should not also be able to deter challenges to its own witnesses through the threat of proceedings against the advocate raising them.When the contempt is used or threatened in the context of advocacy, that constitutional equilibrium is disturbed.Society has a collective interest in maintaining it – not for the benefit of the bar, but for the benefit of every person who may one day stand accused, and for the integrity of the process by which guilt or innocence is determined in their name. That vigilance should start here.Imran Khan KC is a practising solicitorDo you have an opinion on the issues raised in this article? If you would like to submit a response of up to 300 words by email to be considered for publication in ourletterssection, pleaseclick here.Explore more on these topicsTrial by juryOpinionContempt of courtUK criminal justiceBarristerscommentShareReuse this content

title: In a fractious, divided world, I think I know why Pride is the hottest show in town | Emma Brockes | The Guardian
description: The 2014 film was only a modest success – but now the musical’s message of gay rights and solidarity feels both outlandish and full of hope, says columnist Emma Brockes
Author: Emma Brockes
Headline: The 2014 film was only a modest success – but now the musical’s message of gay rights and solidarity feels both outlandish and full of hope, says columnist Emma Brockes
Content: The cast of Pride at the National Theatre, London.Photograph: Manuel HarlanView image in fullscreenThe cast of Pride at the National Theatre, London.Photograph: Manuel HarlanOpinionPrideIn a fractious, divided world, I think I know why Pride is the hottest show in townEmma BrockesThe 2014 film was only a modest success – but now the musical’s message of gay rights and solidarity feels both outlandish and full of hopeThu 23 Jul 2026 05.00 EDTLast modified on Thu 23 Jul 2026 05.04 EDTShareHands down the hottest ticket in London this summer isthe musical Prideat the National Theatre, which is booked solid until the end of its run in September and will presumably transfer to the West End after that. There are lots of gay shows doing well at the moment: I sawOh, Mary!for the second time this week when the show’s creator, Cole Escola, came over from New York to resume the title role, andOperation Mincemeatkeeps going and going. But both those shows are tonally rather than politically gay. Pride, which is adapted from the 2014 film and had modest commercial success when it came out, is a different kettle of fish and I’m curious as to why it’s flown now.I should say up front I haven’t seen the show (can’t get a ticket!). But this week, as I watched my timeline continue to fill up with effusive praise and lots of crying about the musical, Iwent back to the film. It’s a strange time to watch it, in a news cycle in which issues around sexuality in public life and old views about gay rights have surfaced to yank us back to earlier times. Thedeath of the US senatorLindsey Graham triggered widespread speculation about what, exactly, Donald Trump held over him and gave rise to a lot of gay jokes pulled out of the deep freeze.Pride is set during the 1984 miners’ strike, whenan unlikely alliance sprang upbetween a group of lesbians and gay men in London and striking miners in a Welsh town. Stephen Beresford, who wrote the film and the stage production, tells the story of LGSM – Lesbians and Gays Support the Miners – a group set up by a young activist from County Antrim, Mark Ashton. I imagine there were hopes that the film would do a Billy Elliot, but neither British nor American audiences overly responded. Although the film was critically applauded, it didn’t break through.Anyway, I don’t want to be too much of a buzzkill , because the whole point aboutPrideis that it doesn’t feel like homework and I would urge everyone who can’t get a ticket to the show to go back to the movie, which stars Andrew Scott (who must by law be in everything), a really good Dominic West, and Jessica Gunning, who was by far the best thing in Baby Reindeer. Ashton is played by a brilliant Ben Schnetzer and, compared with the entire output of, say, Russell T Davies, Beresford seems actually interested in the women in the story. I’m sorry, but I have to quote some key lines: “Give it to the lesbians, they love a banner”; “Police harassment, dear, I could set it to music”; “Don’t be daft, love. We’ve come all the way from Powys.”That last line is uttered by Imelda Staunton, who will make you laugh and break your heart, and the main takeaway is solidarity, which viewed through a 2026 lens is perhaps the most outlandish thing about it. In this world of feverishly defended political differences between people on the same side, Pride tells a different story, in which vastly different groups organise around a common cause. Paddy Considine as the Welsh miner Dai Donovan was the first person to accept help from the “perverts”, as they were referred to in the popular press in those days, and says it best: “It’s more than money, it’s friendship.”Perhaps that’s what’s done it, this time around. The Dorfman theatre at the National only holds 450 seats, which has given the show a way to build an audience without the pressure of filling a bigger theatre. It’s true, too, that gays will go to see a show we love several times, which definitely helps. But in this era in which all we hear is that we’re living in political end times, my sense is that this show offers something unusual for everyone. It is both shocking and moving to be reminded of how bad things once were, and what could still be achieved.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |WeeklySign up toMatters of OpinionGuardian columnists and writers on what they’ve been debating, thinking about, reading, and morePreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionEmma Brockes is a Guardian columnist. She is the author of She Left Me The Gun: My Mother’s Life Before MeExplore more on these topicsPrideOpinionNational TheatreThe miners’ strike 1984-85commentShareReuse this content

title: If you’ve heard that London is dystopian and dangerous – read this. It just isn’t  | Sadiq Khan | The Guardian
description: Britain’s capital has faced social media, press and political attacks designed to make residents and tourists feel unsafe. Ignore that: we have the facts, says London mayor Sadiq Khan
Author: Sadiq Khan
Headline: Britain’s capital has faced social media, press and political attacks designed to make residents and tourists feel unsafe. Ignore that: we have the facts, says London mayor Sadiq Khan
Content: People walk along Millennium Bridge in London, July 2026.Photograph: Jonathan Brady/PAView image in fullscreenPeople walk along Millennium Bridge in London, July 2026.Photograph: Jonathan Brady/PAOpinionLondonIf you’ve heard that London is dystopian and dangerous – read this. It just isn’tSadiq KhanBritain's capital has faced social media, press and political attacks designed to make residents and tourists feel unsafe. Ignore that: we have the factsThu 23 Jul 2026 03.00 EDTLast modified on Thu 23 Jul 2026 03.34 EDTShareOver the summer months, 5 million people will visitLondon, with its free museums and galleries, massive events featuring the biggest names in music and sport, and everything Britain’s capital city has to offer. And, as new figures released today show, they’ll be able to do so knowing London is one of the safest cities of its size in the world.On social media, a dark blizzard of disinformation – much of it coordinated and amplified by extreme groups and hostile foreign actors – has seen bots and trollsdepict London as a “lawless” citywhere crime goes unpunished and basic social decency has all but disappeared. But let’s deal in facts: data published by the Metropolitan police this morning shows that nothing could be further from the truth. Across the capital, there were 35,000 fewer “neighbourhood crimes” – a category which includes burglaries, thefts, robberies and vehicle crime offences – in the past 12 months than in the year before.This has not happened by accident. As mayor I haveprovided a record £1.26bn in fundingfor the capital’s police force, more than double the amount under my predecessor. That funding has enabled it to invest in cutting-edge technology to keep pace with criminals, including ebikes and drones to pursue phone snatchers down London’s busy streets, but it has also boosted neighbourhood policing in communities across our city.Now, we have more evidence that our approach is yielding real results. In Westminster – home to many communities and the centre of the capital’s tourism – where we’ve doubled the number of police officers and introduced a new “command cell” to lead a crackdown on phone theft and robbery, neighbourhood crime has fallen by 36%. In the West End, the beating heart of London’s visitor economy, phone thefts are down by half.Since last year, thousands of police officers across our city have beenarresting an extra 1,000 suspectsevery month on average. Their work has been tireless. But the figures also reflect the astonishing impact of the violence reduction unit (VRU), which I set up in 2019. When first elected mayor, I promised to be tough on crime and tough on the complex causes of crime. Through the VRU, we’ve done just that, providing interventions and positive opportunities to prevent more than half a million young people from getting caught up in a vicious cycle of violence and exploitation. And note this: since it was set up, hospital admissions of young people after knife assault, have fallen by almost 50%.To anyone who has spent time on social media – where narratives describing London as a dangerous city in declinehave increased by between 150% and 200%in the past two years – or who has seen the non-stop negative press, today’s results might come as a surprise. In truth, though, they’re consistent with the progress we’ve made on cutting crime right across the board. Last year, we saw the fewest murders of those aged under 25 this century, and the capital’s homicide ratehas fallen to the lowest level in recorded historyon a per capita basis. It’s now lower than that in Berlin, Brussels, Barcelona, Madrid, Milan, Paris – and every US state.Of course, one victim of crime is one too many: we can’t afford to be complacent. Instead, we must remain resolute in tackling all forms of criminality. That’s exactly what we’ll continue to do from City Hall, by providing the Met with the backing it needs and by working hard to tackle the underlying social and economic conditions that can cause crime to flourish.I understand why online disinformation might make potential tourists think twice about visiting London this summer. That’s why, today, I want to send a clear message to anyone considering coming to the capital: the real London – the London that millions of us know and love – is not the dark dystopia you see on distorted social media feeds. Diverse, dynamic and prosperous, the real London is one of the safest megacities in the word. These are the facts. And this summer, we’re ready to welcome you with open arms.Sadiq Khan is mayor of LondonDo you have an opinion on the issues raised in this article? If you would like to submit a response of up to 300 words by email to be considered for publication in ourletterssection, pleaseclick here.Explore more on these topicsLondonOpinionCrimeSadiq KhancommentShareReuse this content

title: Besieged by lobbyists, will UK ministers protect our ecosystems? No, they’ll chicken out | George Monbiot | The Guardian
description: Industrial poultry farming is killing our rivers. But rather than stop it, the government has been facilitating it. Will Andy Burnham end this ecocide, asks Guardian columnist George Monbiot
Author: George Monbiot
Headline: Industrial poultry farming is killing our rivers. But rather than stop it, the government has been facilitating it. Will Andy Burnham end this ecocide, asks Guardian columnist George Monbiot
Content: Illustration: Sébastien Thibault/The GuardianView image in fullscreenIllustration: Sébastien Thibault/The GuardianOpinionFarmingBesieged by lobbyists, will UK ministers protect our ecosystems? No, they’ll chicken outGeorge MonbiotIndustrial poultry farming is killing our rivers. But rather than stop it, the government has been facilitating it. Will Andy Burnham end this ecocide?Thu 23 Jul 2026 01.00 EDTLast modified on Thu 23 Jul 2026 09.25 EDTShareThere’s a slightly mean question I often ask British people with an interest in the living world: can you name the environment secretary? I don’t intend to show them up: it’s not them I’m testing. I watch the brows knit, a finger go up, the mouth open and close. “It’s, you know, the one who … no, they went, didn’t they? That other one …” It’s been like this for seven years.Why? Because throughout this period, we’ve not had environment secretaries. We’ve had portals through which corporate lobbyists may pass. The last incumbent to represent the wider public interest (and I can scarcely bring myself to type these words) was Michael Gove. In his other roles, he’s widely remembered with loathing and disgust. Out of office, he continues topoison public life, as editor of the Spectator. But in this role, to general astonishment, he placed the public good above special interests.Standard operating procedure, by contrast, is exemplified by the final acts of the outgoing secretary, whose name, to put you out of your misery, is Emma Reynolds. To give just one example – which could be seen as an embodiment of everything that has gone wrong here – she spent her last weeks in the post trying to remove the brakes on industrial chicken farming.In front of aparliamentary committeethis month, she railed against the planning constraints holding back yet more giant chicken factories (let’s not grace them with the name of farms). If only these obstacles were demolished, she mused, “there is great investment out there waiting to be made … I want to bring down these barriers as much as you do”. She said she had spoken to other departments to ensure permission for new chicken factories was easier to obtain.Neither she nor the MPs questioning her gave any hint of why these buildings have run into trouble with the planning system. In fact, there was no mention of any of the problems the factories cause: the MPs’ only apparent interest was in what the industry wanted. What’s the point of MPs if they simply channel corporate demands? But these factories arekilling some of our most beautiful rivers. They generate far more dung than the surrounding fieldscan absorb. The surplus washes downhill into the nearest stream.The only decision that counts is the planning decision. As soon as permission for a chicken unit is granted, the local tributary, and possibly the main stem of the river it drains into, is as good as dead. It’s not even as if these factories support jobs and growth: by killing the rivers, they help todestroy local economies.I thought we’d won this argument. But argument, it seems, is no match for economic power. Reynolds’s loyalties appeared to lie not with the ecosystems she was meant to protect, but with the lobbyists threatening them. Perhaps this shouldn’t be surprising: before she joined Keir Starmer’s government, she worked as alobbyist, representing the financial sector.Disturbingly, it was the new environment secretary, Angela Eagle, while working as Reynolds’s deputy, who sought to drive through the “planning reforms” the poultry industry was demanding, telling chicken lobbyists“planning should enable ambition, not stifle it”. Will she now take Burnham’s“circuit breaker”promise seriously, and junk this appeasement of one of our most destructive industries?At a recent farming festival, Reynolds claimed that boosting the poultry sector wouldenhance our food security. I don’t know how many times we need to point this out, but isn’t it bleeding obvious that it has the opposite effect? The feed sustaining chickens in these factories must be either imported or grown on land that could otherwise directly feed people. There could be more soya in chicken than in tofu:one reportestimates that it takes 109g of soya to produce 100g of chicken breast. Yet this is just one of several components of their diet.The chicken business in the UK is also controlled by a handful of giant companies: another security red light. If food security were the objective, the government would encourage us to eat fewer chickens and other animals, and more plants. But in parliament, Reynolds alsoproudly announcedthat she was disregarding the targets set by the government’s Climate Change Committee to reduce livestock numbers. I find myself asking, over and again: “Are theytryingto harm this country?”To advance their dismal agenda, Reynolds and Eagle set up something called the Farming and Food Partnership Board. Its aim is to encourage“closer collaboration between farming, industry and government”. How could it possibly be closer? The environment department, Defra, is a wholly owned subsidiary of the farming industry.The only membersof this new board are government ministers and corporate representatives and lobbyists. Among itsobjectivesis a “poultry sector growth plan”, to address the issue of “planning barriers”.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |WeeklySign up toMatters of OpinionGuardian columnists and writers on what they’ve been debating, thinking about, reading, and morePreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionThis chickening out is just one of many examples of how, by working for special interests, the environment department has harmed the ecosystems and the people it is supposed to protect. Another is the government’s“delivery plan”to restore nature, released by Reynolds just before she left office. It’s not a plan, and it won’t deliver: just a catalogue ofvague hopes and blandishments. It could be summarised as “do nothing, for fear of offending landowners”. Far from meeting its promise to protect and restore 30% of the land in England by 2030, Reynolds’s department has presided overcontinued decline. That’s her legacy: less nature, less resilience, less security.Much worse is planned: Starmer’s government proposed a“regulating for growth bill”, whose text we haven’t yet seen, but which appears to threaten even greater assaults on nature protection. It may contain one of his government’s most alarming proposals: creating a system that could be described aspop-up freeports, in which companies all over the country would be able to operate intemporary “sandboxes”, where the usual rules don’t apply. We should see this as a key test ofBurnham’s promise to end neoliberalism: will he drop this nonsense?I’ll keep asking the question, regardless of who occupies this office: do you know the name of the environment secretary? The answer is likely to depend on one factor: whether they are ready to break the grip of corporate power, and defend us and the rest of the living world. Whether, in other words, they want to do their job.George Monbiot is a Guardian columnistExplore more on these topicsFarmingOpinionRiversPollutionLobbyingFoodMeat industrycommentShareReuse this content

title: Nobody in Europe knows or cares what Kemi Badenoch thinks. That’s a big mistake | Simon Nixon | The Guardian
description: The opposition leader wants to pull the UK out of the ECHR, wrecking treaties from the Good Friday agreement to the Gibraltar deal, says economics commentator Simon Nixon
Author: Simon Nixon
Headline: The opposition leader wants to pull the UK out of the ECHR, wrecking treaties from the Good Friday agreement to the Gibraltar deal, says economics commentator Simon Nixon
Content: Conservative leader Kemi Badenoch speaking in Farnborough, Hampshire, 21 July 2026.Photograph: Andrew Matthews/PAView image in fullscreenConservative leader Kemi Badenoch speaking in Farnborough, Hampshire, 21 July 2026.Photograph: Andrew Matthews/PAOpinionEuropeNobody in Europe knows or cares what Kemi Badenoch thinks. That’s a big mistakeSimon NixonThe opposition leader wants to pull the UK out of the ECHR, wrecking treaties from the Good Friday agreement to the Gibraltar dealThu 23 Jul 2026 00.00 EDTLast modified on Thu 23 Jul 2026 03.15 EDTShareSitting in Brussels, or one of the EU’s 27 chancelleries, one could be forgiven for tuning out the latest psychodramas in Britain’s Conservative party. After all, the country has a new Labour prime minister who is a pro-European keen to build upon the“reset” begun by Keir Starmer. Who cares what a party in opposition thinks about anything when it is still reeling from its landslide 2024 defeat, and languishing third in the opinion polls as it haemorrhages support from its right flank? It is easy to dismiss Kemi Badenoch and her party as a sideshow.That would be a mistake. Following her latest pronouncements, Badenoch has effectively committed the Conservatives to a policy of conflict, and possibly even rupture, with the EU. Sherecentlysuggested in a newspaper article that anyone who opposes her policy of withdrawing from the European convention on human rights (ECHR) or ditching net zero commitments would be considered “not serious”, and thus excluded from consideration as a Conservative candidate at the next election. She then withdrew the party whip (aUK parliamentary euphemism for “kicked out”) from Gavin Barwell, a member of the House of Lords whocriticised Badenoch’s stance. Such a choice revives memories of Boris Johnson’s purge of pro-Europeans from the party.Westminster has mostly viewed this through a domestic lens: a Tory leader prepared to jettison centre-right supporters in the hope of preventing further rightwing defections to Reform UK. What has been missing is the European dimension. Were theConservativesto return to power – which polls suggest is possible in alliance with Reform UK – Badenoch’s ideological red lines will resurrect the ghost of “no deal” Brexit.That’s because the UK’s continued membership of the ECHR underpins every element of its post-Brexit relationship with the EU. Article 763 of the trade and cooperation agreement (2021), for instance, commits both sides to adhere to shared values, including the human rights treaties to which they are signatories, while article 771 makes clear that this commitment is an essential condition of the deal and 772 states that in the event of a breach of this undertaking, the other party has the right to suspend or terminate the agreement.The EU insisted on those references above all toprotect peace in Northern Ireland, notes Catherine Barnard, professor of EU law and a fellow of the UK in a Changing Europe thinktank. The ECHR is embedded in the Good Friday agreement, in which Britain committed to enshrining the convention rights in Northern Irish law. Meanwhile, the Windsor framework, the dealhammered out by Rishi Sunakto ease post-Brexit trade across the Irish sea while keeping the land border open,specifically commits Britainto “no diminution of rights, safeguards or equality of opportunity” as set out in the Good Friday agreement.What’s more, ECHR membership underpins many other areas of cooperation, including data sharing and criminal extradition arrangements. Even theGibraltar agreement, signed only on 15 July, requires both sides to remain signatories. A British government that left the convention would not be making a narrow adjustment to its human rights regime; it would be pulling on a thread that runs through the entire fabric of the post-Brexit relationship.Conservatives claim that the danger is overblown, that the EU would never let a dispute over the ECHR detonate an important trading relationship. But that smacks of the complacency apparent throughout the Brexit process. David Wolfson, the shadow attorney general,argued last yearthat quitting the ECHR would not breach the Good Friday agreement because it only required convention rights to be accessible in domestic courts. Other legal experts, not least in Northern Ireland,disagree, noting that the reason the ECHR is in the Good Friday agreement is to ensure that those rights are protected by a body independent of the British state. The EU’svictory over Hungaryin the European court of justice this year in relation to an anti-LGBTQ+ law showed the weight it attaches to fundamental rights. It would hardly be less vigilant if it judged that the vital interests of a member state were threatened.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |WeeklySign up toThis is EuropeThe most pressing stories and debates for Europeans – from identity to economics to the environmentPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionWhat the EU wants from Andy BurnhamRead moreNor is it just Badenoch’s ECHR loyalty test that is problematic. The trade and cooperation agreement’s article 772 also states that “an act or omission that materially defeats the object and purpose of the Paris agreement shall always be considered a serious and substantial failure” to fulfil the “essential obligations” of the agreement. Depending on where the Conservative leader’s anti-net zero policy lands, that could spell serious trouble for the trading relationship. Its provisions to level the playing field hinge on shared climate goals.Europeans may roll their eyes and take Badenoch’s grandstanding as further evidence that British politics remains, to use her word, “unserious”. But this, too, would be complacent. As the global order fragments and Europeans find themselves squeezed by an aggressive Russia, coercive US and mercantilist China, the UK-EU relationship has become vital to European security. It is not enough for Brussels to repeat that it is for Britain to decide what relationship it wants. Both sides have a responsibility to solidify it ahead of the next UK election.Indeed,Andy Burnhammay need all the help he can get. His goodwill has yet to materialise into clear European policy, and Starmer’s reset reached the limit of what can be achieved within Labour’s red lines of no return to the single market, the customs union, or free movement. To keep the relationship moving forward, a new project that deepens institutional and political links and does not cross either side’s current red lines is needed. A defence pact – building on existing coalitions of the willing and including Ukraine – is the obvious candidate. To slay Badenoch’s no-deal ghost, Brussels must play its part in proving the relationship’s value.Simon Nixon is a journalist and economics commentatorExplore more on these topicsEuropeOpinionAndy BurnhamKemi BadenochHuman rightsLabourConservativescommentShareReuse this content

title: ​’Ian Curtis didn’t have a chance’: the Durutti Column on departed friends, Factory Records and fun with Morrissey | Pop and rock | The Guardian
description: As they release their first album in 16 years, the Vini Reilly-fronted band answer your questions on sandpaper record sleeves, having Harry Styles as a fan and inventing a new style of guitar music
Author: Dave Simpson
Headline: As they release their first album in 16 years, the Vini Reilly-fronted band answer your questions on sandpaper record sleeves, having Harry Styles as a fan and inventing a new style of guitar music
Content: ‘Asked what kind of music we played, I piped up: “Avant garde jazz classical.” It was a joke, but it stuck’ … Vini Reilly, centre, with producer Keir Stewart, left, and drummer Bruce Mitchell.Photograph: Michael SteffView image in fullscreen‘Asked what kind of music we played, I piped up: “Avant garde jazz classical.” It was a joke, but it stuck’ … Vini Reilly, centre, with producer Keir Stewart, left, and drummer Bruce Mitchell.Photograph: Michael SteffFilm & Music reader interviewPop and rockInterview​’Ian Curtis didn’t have a chance’: the Durutti Column on departed friends, Factory Records and fun with MorrisseyAs told toDave SimpsonAs they release their first album in 16 years, the Vini Reilly-fronted band answer your questions on sandpaper record sleeves, having Harry Styles as a fan and inventing a new style of guitar musicThu 23 Jul 2026 11.16 EDTLast modified on Thu 23 Jul 2026 11.26 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleVini, you’ve created some of the most beautiful, ethereal music of the last 40 years. Is it true that in your youth you were in a violent Manchester street gang?Songy24Vini Reilly [guitars, vocals, keyboards]:Gangs then had a different meaning. It was just “their lot” and “our lot”. We’d sling insults at each other. Then you’d play football in the park and realise you were playing with the local thugs. They were actually very funny, and I could have fun with them because I was so little. At the time I’d been going through a recurring depressive illness and felt I didn’t fit in at home, so I left without any clean clothing. I wanted to end my life so I decided to go to Moss Side, where the gangsters would shoot me. Of course they didn’t. I’d had a very happy childhood until my father died when I was doing my O-levels, but I’ve come to the conclusion that the really bad things gave me more than they could ever take. How can you know happiness until you’ve known deep sadness?View image in fullscreen‘I wanted to create something which wasn’t a recognisable form’ … Reilly in April 2021.Photograph: Michael SteffBruce Mitchell [drums]:Vini does kick off, though, properly battles. We had to section him once, and six people had to hold him down.Legend has it that Joy Division helped cut and glue the sandpaper sheetson to the famous original cover ofThe Return of the Durutti Column. Were you involved?Gomgom71VR:I walked into Factory Records to find Joy Division gluing them all. It was mortifying, so I escaped as quickly as I could.BM:Vini denies this, but Hooky [Peter Hook] always says that Vini quickly stopped gluing them so he didn’t damage his nails. Joy Division had no cash, so they were paid to do it, which they did while watching porno movies. Alan Erasmus at Factory Records had got a load of thrown-away sleeves from an album of steam train noises: you can tell it’s an original edition because if you peel the sandpaper back you’ll find a steam train.Is there a story behind your Blood Orange collaboration, The Field?nobblehobbleKeir Stewart [producer]:Dev Hynes [Blood Orange] has been a fan for a long time, and he sampled [the 1998 track] Sing to Me and made a tune out of it. He asked permission for the sample, and when we met we had this two-hour babble about the Durutti Column and all sorts. Durutti have all these famous covert fans: Stewart Lee, Chris Morris, Max Richter, Richard Ayoade, John Frusciante from the Red Hot Chili Peppers, Chris Martin from Coldplay, AG Cook, Caroline Polachek, loads of artists on the hyperpop scene. It’s great that Vini’s been able to harvest this recognition in his lifetime, but he keeps it all outside his door. He wants to remain unpolluted.VR:I’ve never heard of most of these people, but it’s very nice.Which guitarists did you listen to growing up?TroykatooVR:One of my favourite guitarists was Al Casey, who played with Thomas “Fats” Waller: really sparse melodies and harmonic progressions. I had this notion that whether you went to a big orchestral performance at the Free Trade Hall or a folk club in Sale, you knew exactly what the audience would look like, because people identify with genre. So I wanted to create something that wasn’t a recognisable form. When we were trying to get into Canada, the customs guy asked what kind of music we played and I piped up: “Avant garde jazz classical.” It was a joke, but it stuck.View image in fullscreenDurutti Column play on the TV show The Tube.Photograph: ITV/ShutterstockHow do you feel about the recentFor Vini – A Tribute to the Durutti Columnshow in London?NickJMorganKS:Harry Styles told Radio 1 that the biggest influences on his new album are LCD Soundsystem and the Durutti Column. Then he was asked to curate the Meltdown festival, so we were asked if there was any way Vini could do a show. There wasn’t, so we got the musicians together for the tribute. I played guitar – I can’t play like Vini but put in a lot of practice! When Bruce came on to play drums, everyone was cheering. A promoter’s already asked if we could do a UK tour, so we’ll see.VR:It’s fantastic that they’re doing it, but I couldn’t bear to see it. If Bruce is involved, it’s always right. Whenever we played together we didn’t rehearse, but we had such chemistry he always knew when a song would end. It worked every time.Could you tell us your memories of Ian Curtis and the inspiration behind the song The Missing Boy?PhilshawknightVR:Ian and I were getting to know each other – we both worked for different parts of the civil service. We were planning to do some music together but never told anyone in case people like Mark E Smith called it nepotism. Then I was staying with Tony [Wilson, Factory boss] and Lindsay [Reade, Wilson’s then wife]. I answered the phone and it was Ian. He wasn’t making any sense. He fluctuated from being really positive to the opposite. He was angry that Tony had called him a poet. When I saw all the pills Ian had been prescribed for epilepsy, I knew he didn’t have a chance. Also his marriage was falling apart, he had a little girl … I wasn’t surprised when he killed himself: it’s all there in the songs, but nobody – not even Tony – ever asked him about them. There’s an anger to The Missing Boy because it’s like I’m gritting my teeth.BM:I was living round the corner. Vini said he needed someone to go in the studio with him to record a memorial for someone who had died. He played it for the engineer, then played once more, leaning over to play the grand piano while his guitar went through a space echo. That second take is on the record.Who is Paul inDetail for Paul?UncleheinrichagainVR:A friend I had for a long time who’d been at art school. Really intelligent, good guy. Then he got a job in advertising which went against his socialist principles. One day he just didn’t show up. I miss him. People always assume my songs are named after girlfriends, which they’re not at all. Jacqueline is Bruce’s wife, a fantastic artist. Quite a few are named after my nieces or cousins. Do they feel honoured? Oh no. My family are terribly insulted.What was the best and worst thing about Tony Wilson?dramaaghVR:There wasn’t a worst. The best is that he was a true altruist, and that never changed. Factory was conceived with the money his mother bequeathed to him after her death, and he was determined he was gonna do something out of respect for her. He made loads of recordings possible. A lot are very precious to me, because without him people would have never got to hear them.View image in fullscreenReilly plays Brussels.Photograph: David Corio/RedfernsWithout Mercyis absolutely beautiful – a modern classical classic. How do you rate it among your work?FarmyardManureVR:It was a nightmare to make, but I don’t regret it. Tony said: “Will you do an album forme?” How could I say no? He’d decided that Keats’s poem Without Mercy [La Belle Dame sans Merci] was the same formula as a pop song – boy meets girl, girl dumps boy – and wanted a musical interpretation. I don’t really like it [laughs], but I’m glad that Tony did, because it was a way of trying to thank him.Was the name the Durutti Column deliberately or accidentally misspelt from theSpanishcivilwar anarchist Buenaventura DurrutiandLa Colonna Durruti?somna123VR:The name was Tony’s idea. He felt that since anarchy was the basis of punk, we should go back to that idea. But he decided not to explain that to people, which was great for me because it meant I could waffle on talking absolute nonsense about it. It was probably misspelled by Factory as a mistake, but even in Spain some people think it looks better with one “r”.View image in fullscreenBruce Mitchell and Vini Reilly in Manchester in 2023.Photograph: Christopher Thomond/The Guardian‘I’ve played for 60 years. That’s long enough’: guitar hero Vini Reilly on PTSD, life on the streets and the little girl who saved himRead moreWhat was it like to work onViva Hate with Morrissey,and would you work with him again?deFaoiteKVR:Certainly, because he’s so ridiculously gifted. I remember him talking about the Moors murderers and how he could have been one of those [murdered] kids, which never left him. On the song Late Night, Maudlin Street you really felt that doomy power. But also we never stopped laughing.BM:I dropped Vini off at the studio half-expecting to pick him up again after half an hour, because all the musicians and engineers were eating at this long table, and right at the end on his own was Morrissey. But they got on so well that when I eventually picked him up, Vini said: “Can we take Mozza?” They were nattering in the back seat all the way. It was like listening to two old ladies on Coronation Street.Renascent is released via London Records on 31 JulyExplore more on these topicsPop and rockFilm & Music reader interviewIndieinterviewsShareReuse this content

title: The roaring 20s: how the current decade revolutionised cinema | Movies | The Guardian
description: Pre-Covid, the movie landscape was dramatically different: YouTubers weren’t dominating the box office, video game spin-offs rarely made an impact and Winnie-the-Pooh wasn’t yet the star of a slasher movie. Why such a flurry of innovation – and what will be next?
Author: Stuart Heritage
Headline: Pre-Covid, the movie landscape was dramatically different: YouTubers weren’t dominating the box office, video game spin-offs rarely made an impact and Winnie-the-Pooh wasn’t yet the star of a slasher movie. Why such a flurry of innovation – and what will be next?
Content: Horror adaptation … David Howard Thornton as Steamboat Willie in Screamboat.Photograph: SKLAE/Signature EntertainmentView image in fullscreenHorror adaptation … David Howard Thornton as Steamboat Willie in Screamboat.Photograph: SKLAE/Signature EntertainmentMoviesThe roaring 20s: how the current decade revolutionised cinemaPre-Covid, the movie landscape was dramatically different: YouTubers weren’t dominating the box office, video game spin-offs rarely made an impact and Winnie-the-Pooh wasn’t yet the star of a slasher movie. Why such a flurry of innovation – and what will be next?Stuart HeritageThu 23 Jul 2026 11.00 EDTLast modified on Thu 23 Jul 2026 12.21 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleCinema has always been a medium of research and development. At any given point, the industry is searching for yet another breakthrough that can drive it into the future. Synchronised sound in the 1920s. Technicolor in the 1930s. The invention of the modern blockbuster in the 1970s.Conversely, the consensus is that, if the 2020s has a defining trend, then it is one of decline. The theatrical experience is under attack from all angles: streaming,YouTube, a once-in-a-century health crisis that shut cinemas down entirely. And yet, if you look closer, this has actually been one of the most innovative decades that cinema has ever seen. Whether down to technology or desperation, the film industry has spent these last few years figuring out new ways to make films that simply could not have existed even at the turn of the decade.Currently, the most notable of these is the way that cinema has become a conversation with YouTube. Back in 2005, when Jawed Karim uploaded the first video (the 19-second Me at the Zoo) to the platform, YouTube felt like a novelty. It was cheap and cheerful, a collection of cat videos and people falling over. How things have changed. Not only has it become the largest streaming platform in the world, but the first generation of adults who grew up with YouTube has come of age.View image in fullscreenCreepypasta comes good … Chiwetel Ejiofor in Backrooms.Photograph: A24/APAs we speak, the seventh and eighth biggest films of 2026 have been directed by people who learned their trade making content for YouTube. Obsession (worldwide gross: $442m) was directed by Curry Barker, a 26-year-old who dropped out of film school to make YouTube videos and TikToks. Meanwhile, Backrooms (worldwide gross: $384m) is even more explicitly internet-inspired. Directed by the then 20-year-old Kane Parsons, the film is based on a series of shorts he began uploading to YouTube in 2022, which in turn were inspired by somecreepypasta(web-based horror content) that has been floating around online since 2019.A decade ago, studios would have got this disastrously wrong. Ask them to adapt a YouTube video and they would have hired Michael Bay and Rihanna to makeDavid After Dentist: The Motion Picture; it would have baffled anyone who didn’t watch YouTube – and appalled anyone who did. This is the first time that the industry has trusted natives to make the films they want to make. Clearly, given the enormous financial returns, it will not be the last. Steven Spielberg is now producingAlex Kister’s The Mandela Catalogue, based on a terrifying analogue horror series that has run on YouTube since 2021.This would also be a good time to mention the rise of the copyright expiry movie. In 1998, the Copyright Term Extension Act was passed in the US, which extended protection to works to 95 years after they were first published. (In the UK, the rules are different; copyright extends to 70 years after the creator’s death.) This effectively meant that two decades went by without anything going out of copyright. When things finally caught up in 2019, freeing up anything made in 1923, there was a goldrush.View image in fullscreenRuining your childhood … Winnie-the-Pooh: Blood and Honey 2.Photograph: Publicity imageNow, let it be said that it wasn’t a particularlygoodgoldrush. First out of the blocks was 2023’sWinnie-the-Pooh: Blood and Honey, a low-rent slasher movie whose only original thought was to have Winnie-the-Pooh in it. Then cameThe Mouse TrapandScreamboat(both based on Steamboat Willie, the first element of the Mickey Mouse characterto have lost copyright protection), along with Popeye the Slayer Man andBambi: The Reckoning. Without exception, these films are cheap, exploitative and burn out their premise long before the end credits – but more are to come.Next year, if you can wait that long, will see the release of something calledPoohniverse: Monsters Assemble, which will star – deep breath – Winnie-the-Pooh, Tigger, Bambi, Peter Pan, Tinker Bell, Pinocchio, Sleeping Beauty and the Cheshire Cat. It is also worth pointing out that King Kong enters the public domain in 2029, which will be brilliant news for anyone who has been longing to see a low-rent horror movie starring an unusually large gorilla.Now, here is where I may lose you, because I am about to name Sonic the Hedgehog as the most important movie of the decade. Please bear with me. One of the most pervasive trends of this decade has been the way that, for good or ill, audiences have hijacked movies to their own ends. The biggest example is obviouslyBarbenheimer, in which Barbie and Oppenheimer were released on the same day, kickstarting a phenomenon where people bought tickets to see one straight after the other. Nobody in the film industry expected this, but the publicity from this grassroots celebration drove receipts of both films through the roof.View image in fullscreenDoomed by irony … Morbius.Photograph: Landmark Media/AlamyThe flipside to this isMorbius, a terrible film that inspired no end of ironic online mockery – but what was hilarious was that Sony mistook the irony for sincere support, andrereleased the film on 1,000 screensto cash in. It goes without saying that nobody watched it. Also, think of all the extra teenagers who bought tickets for Minions: The Rise of Gru, purely so they could watch dressed in suits and babble nonsense to participate in theGentleminions trend, or the people who went to see A Minecraft Movie exclusively to scream and chuck stuff about whenJack Black said the words “chicken jockey”.No film, arguably, has benefited from fan reaction quite like Sonic the Hedgehog. The first we saw of it was anightmarish trailerfeaturing an unrecognisable main character. Sonic had bizarrely human proportions, as well as tiny eyes and teeth that looked as if they had been stolen from someone’s mouth while they slept. It was awful, and the internet wasted no time in saying that it was awful. In times gone by, studios would have simply taken the hit and released the film as quietly as possible, writing it off as another godawful video game adaptation destined to die on impact. But this time, producers listened. The trailer was released on Tuesday. By Thursday, the director had tweeted that the character would be completely redesigned. Six months after that,New Sonic was unveiled, and fans were delighted.View image in fullscreen‘Teeth stolen from someone’s sleeping mouth’ … a still from the first Sonic the Hedgehog trailer.Photograph: Paramount PicturesWith a character that people could actually stand to look at, Sonic the Hedgehog went on to become the sixth biggest film of 2020. Nobody saw this coming. Until Sonic, video game adaptations were universally written off as the lowest form of cinema. Think of thedismal live action Super Mario Bros movie, or Mark Wahlberg’sbaffling Max Payne, or Uwe Boll’s irredeemablePostal. About 46 English language video game adaptations were released between 1993 and 2019, and they made a combined total of $5.4bn. But A Minecraft Movie made close to a $1bn on its own. The animated Super Mario Movie released in 2023 ended the year second only to Barbie. At time of writing,The Super Mario Galaxy Movie is the biggest film of 2026so far worldwide. By demonstrating to other film-makers how to give people what they actually want to see, Sonic has turned the video game adaptation into a licence to print money.As you will agree, this makesSonic the Hedgehogby far the most important film of the decade. And the future will only bear this out further, once Mr Beast signs on to direct Sonic the Hedgehog 5 and someone makes a rubbish horror film about a blue hedgehog, as soon as the rights lapse in 2087.Explore more on these topicsFilmHorror filmsSonic the HedgehogBackroomsYouTubeGamesfeaturesShareReuse this content

title: ‘My life’s work was being delightful. That really hurt me’: breakout Americana star Brennan Wedl on freeing herself through song | Music | The Guardian
description: ​With her roots stretching from the Catholic church to riot grrrl, the US singer-songwriter explains how sobriety and self-analysis powered her classic new Waxahatchee-produced album
Author: Laura Snapes
Headline: ​With her roots stretching from the Catholic church to riot grrrl, the US singer-songwriter explains how sobriety and self-analysis powered her classic new Waxahatchee-produced album
Content: Timeless and confident … Brennan Wedl.Photograph: Daniel TopeteView image in fullscreenTimeless and confident … Brennan Wedl.Photograph: Daniel TopeteMusicInterview‘My life’s work was being delightful. That really hurt me’: breakout Americana star Brennan Wedl on freeing herself through songLaura Snapes​With her roots stretching from the Catholic church to riot grrrl, the US singer-songwriter explains how sobriety and self-analysis powered her classic new Waxahatchee-produced albumThu 23 Jul 2026 07.30 EDTLast modified on Thu 23 Jul 2026 14.01 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleThe songwriting on Brennan Wedl’s new album is so ironclad that I kept checking the credits to see if any of its songs were actually covers of peak Lucinda Williams tracks that I’d somehow missed. Nope: all originals by a 29-year-old songwriter whose radiant, hooky, tough Americana puts her toe-to-toe with genre godheads such as Williams circa Car Wheels on a Gravel Road, or Sheryl Crow had she grown up playing church basement punk shows; also, a vision of Courtney Love in cowboy boots.“I love Lucinda’s very earnest way of looking at daily life, and I hoped to achieve that on this record,” says Wedl, calling from her Nashville home, surrounded by vintage magazines and tchotchkes. Wedl is warm and dudely; she answers almost every question with an exuberant “toootally!”. She cites her favourite of her new songs, the absurdly immediate Just Another Daddy Who Did Me Wrong, a bittersweet number that questions her self-destructive appeal. “It’s a very harsh look at reality, but also about how our minds can create so much fantasy around reality. I’m working on not projecting too much on reality these days.”You could reasonably say Wedl doesn’t have too many reasons to fantasise Her new, self-titled record was produced by Waxahatchee (AKA Katie Crutchfield) and her longtime producer Brad Cook, who asked to make the album after Snail Mail’s Lindsey Jordan put them on to Wedl’s music.“I just couldn’t wrap my head around her voice,” says Crutchfield, who had never produced before. “It’s immediately iconic, so timeless and confident – for someone who was, at the time, so undiscovered.” When Crutchfield heard an early version of Two Dollar Pistol, a loving, windows-down nudge to someone frustrated by ambition, “I was like, this person’s writing huge melodies that can be played in arenas. This is a really different level of songwriting.”Wedl puts the fully formed nature of the album – her third solo release after more muted outings – down to never having known herself better. Combining newly written songs and others from her 20s, “it’s a lot about communicating between various versions of you, time-travel with music; conversations with the unknown, dialogue with large themes like the man, God, Jesus, the trope of ‘daddy’.”View image in fullscreenBrennan Wedl … ‘I dream of the day I’ll move back to Minnesota.’Photograph: Daniel TopeteLike many of the US’s best young alternative songwriters, Wedl comes from a religious background – but, unusually, she hasn’t rejected it wholly. She grew up in Missouri with nuns for aunts. “I’m really grateful to have seen a lot of different sides of the Catholic church,” she says. “I saw nuns devoted to service, to education. But I also saw the assault of young boys. My grandfather is a huge advocate for female priests and says we can’t keep brushing this under the rug – the church will die if they do not change their ways. So, coming from elders in my family who have really made Christianity their own, it inspired me to put a little pagan twist on it in my adult life.”Wedl discovered riot grrrl when she attended the She Rock She Rock summer camp at age 12, and found common ground between the church and punk in their impetus to come together “to build the new era of something”. Minnesota offered a great local soundtrack in the Jayhawks, the Replacements and Prince (members of the New Power Generation, his backing band, played in her grandparents’ church band), and Wedl played both with her uncle and her first band, Sex Education. (Not something they actually got much of in high school.) “As I get older and listen to these artists, it really makes me dream of the day that I’ll move back to Minnesota and be with family,” she says.She first left home to attend Berklee College of Music in Boston, where she studied songwriting and co-founded the playful queercore outfit Dazey and the Scouts. They lasted just over two years, then became a minor pandemic TikTok sensation when a kid dancing to a Morrissey-impersonating verse in their song Wet went viral. “TikTok has its dark sides, but the discovery factor does bring people together – being able to see yourself in some kind of art or music, no matter where you are, is magic,” says Wedl.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |WeeklySign up toSleeve NotesGet music news, bold reviews and unexpected extras. Every genre, every era, every weekPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionWaxahatchee on success and sobriety: ‘You don’t have to be tortured to make interesting art’Read moreMusic also helped Wedl see herself more clearly. Her album’s rockiest moment, Pretty Little Fantasy, attacks perceptions of her as “somebody’s daughter … prim and proper”. Growing up as the eldest and only girl, she says, “I wanted to be so helpful, and I think girls are taught that a lot. But then I was introduced to riot grrrl and Marina Abramović and disgraced women throughout history, and it took most of my 20s to come to the conclusion that I was still carrying the self-imposed life’s work of being delightful, and that doesn’t help anyone – actually, it really hurts me. I think ‘delightful’ is the mask I wore out of not dealing with my own shit – and so if I was a delight, no one would have to investigate further.”On stage, Wedl cultivated being “dead serious about having fun – the ambition to live the life I want”. It filtered into the rehearsal space, then her everyday confidence. Recently, she was reminiscing on an earlier version of her solo project. “There was an upright bass player and an electric bass player because I didn’t want to hurt someone’s feelings by saying they couldn’t play,” she says, laughing. “I’m sure that sounded horrible!” That was 10 years ago now. “Being the leader of a band is a constant crash course in people-pleasing, self-assuredness and respect, and I’m so grateful that I get to learn these huge life lessons through music.”She also credits “various 12-step programmes” with breaking these moulds. “Twenty-one has found me sober,” she sang on her last album, 2019’s Holy Water Branch. “A lot of my life boils down to: how do my daily habits make up who I am?” she says. “When I was drinking, I was really focused on the next time I was gonna do that, who was gonna be there – this mental obsession with the wrong things.” Quitting drinking didn’t erase her problems, but it made them clearer. She realised she had become someone who wasn’t writing, neglecting “this shortcut for when I couldn’t really explain what’s on my heart to others; I could explain it to my guitar and to my journal.”Writing, she says, “can be how we communicate with what we don’t see, what contracts we sign with ourselves. What we want to bring into this reality can be done with pen and paper a lot of the time. It’s really magical.”Waxahatchee on Brennan WedlView image in fullscreenWaxahatchee.Photograph: Sonja Horsman/The GuardianFrom first hearing Brennan to making the record to now, I kind of still can’t fully wrap my head around her existence. She’s unique. Three or four years ago, Lindsey Jordan [AKA Snail Mail] was at my house and we were going back and forth, playing each other stuff. She put on I Wanna Be Your TV, by Brennan, and didn’t give me any context, and I had to stop her and be like, what is this? Is it recent? Did somebody young make this or is this an old song I’ve never heard? She told me Brennan was from Nashville. I couldn’t stop listening to her – I listened to that song a million times.Shortly thereafter, she put out some singles with [west coast label] Kill Rock Stars, a handful of which we re-recorded. Eventually, we got in contact and I got to know her, then got to watch her play music in a studio, her technique, her approach. At every turn, she impresses me – she’s so talented and so humble but not in a grating, overly precious,shucks, I shouldn’t be herekinda way. She has the absolute perfect amount of confidence. She can really write a hook. The melodies are amazing. As I got to know her better, [I saw that] her lyric writing is really strong, too. It’s all influenced by my favourite stuff: it’s Kathleen Edwards, Lucinda Williams, Tom Petty – the people’s songwriters.Brennan Wedl is released via Anti- on 21 AugustExplore more on these topicsMusicCountryPop and rockIndieinterviewsShareReuse this content

title: ‘Unbearably sexy in his rodenty way’: Benedict Cumberbatch’s 20 best films – ranked! | Movies | The Guardian
description: To mark the two-time Oscar nominee’s recent 50th birthday, we rate his finest screen roles – from hyper-British patricians and seething serpents, to ghastly Yanks, tragic codebreakers and sci-fi scoundrels
Author: Peter Bradshaw
Headline: To mark the two-time Oscar nominee’s recent 50th birthday, we rate his finest screen roles – from hyper-British patricians and seething serpents, to ghastly Yanks, tragic codebreakers and sci-fi scoundrels
Content: Benedict Cumberbatch as the persecuted mathematician, Alan Turing, in 2014’s The Imitation Game.Photograph: Black Bear Pictures/AllstarView image in fullscreenBenedict Cumberbatch as the persecuted mathematician, Alan Turing, in 2014’s The Imitation Game.Photograph: Black Bear Pictures/AllstarRankedMovies‘Unbearably sexy in his rodenty way’: Benedict Cumberbatch’s 20 best films – ranked!To mark the two-time Oscar nominee’s recent 50th birthday, we rate his finest screen roles – from hyper-British patricians and seething serpents, to ghastly Yanks, tragic codebreakers and sci-fi scoundrelsPeter BradshawThu 23 Jul 2026 08.00 EDTLast modified on Thu 23 Jul 2026 10.57 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on Google20.The Roses (2025)Benedict Cumberbatch does his considerable best opposite the equally prestigious Olivia Colman in this souped-up marital-breakdown dramedy, essentially remade from the 1989 movie with Michael Douglas and Kathleen Turner. This time, though, it’s all about the Britons – indicating the supposed classiness of the affair. But the film is overwrought and unconvincing; it never convinces us that the couple were once passionately in love and now passionately in hate. The below-par script has the effect of bringing out an unrewarding kind of coldness in Cumberbatch, technically assured though he always is.19.The Thing With Feathers (2025)View image in fullscreenIt’s behind you … The Thing With Feathers.Photograph: TCD/Prod.DB/AlamyHere is an example of Cumberbatch’s presence signalling a film’s purposeful seriousness – and he does indeed give an honest, intelligent and well-intentioned performance in an otherwise self-conscious and contrived study of grief and emotional breakdown. He is the writer and illustrator whose wife has died, and who is visited by a gigantic hallucinatory crow (raspingly voiced by David Thewlis) symbolising the agony of loss. Cumberbatch’s performance, however, looks a little refined and dry for the material.18.August: Osage County (2013)An interesting, subdued appearance from Cumberbatch in this film version of Tracy Letts’s award-winning play about an explosive family reunion. It is an example of how his theatrical accomplishment, technique and distinctive appearance, when married up to material that doesn’t overstate things, can work well. In fact, it is the rest of the cast, headed up by Meryl Streep, who bring the fireworks; Cumberbatch, playing the shy, depressed cousin Little Charles, delivers a less-is-more premium.17. The Hobbit trilogy (2012-2014)Popcorn movies like this, as opposed to upmarket Masterpiece Theatre-type productions, allow Cumberbatch to have some fun – and here he is, playing in motion-capture as the terrifying dragon Smaug and the equally daunting Necromancer, in Peter Jackson’s LOTR prequel-triple. Within the confines of motion-capture, his Smaug – a little like Ian McKellen’s Gandalf – delivers authentic British vocal richness and theatrical hamminess, evil and yet lonely.16.The Other Boleyn Girl (2008)View image in fullscreenSaucy romping … The Other Boleyn Girl.Photograph: Bbc Films/AllstarNowadays, a Tudorbethan romp with Cumberbatch would have to cast him as Henry VIII or Thomas Cromwell at the very least. In Justin Chadwick’s enjoyably gossipy and saucy what-if period drama about Henry’s illicit relationship with Anne Boleyn’s sister Mary (played by Scarlett Johansson), Cumberbatch has to content himself with the more self-effacing role of Henry’s courtier and Mary’s husband, William Carey, who is a step away from the real action. But Cumberbatch’s theatrical credentials are important to the film’s integrity.15.The Courier (2020)Cumberbatch gives some serious moustache action in this espionage drama-thriller, based on a true story. It’s not as good as his appearance in Tinker Tailor Soldier Spy, but Cumberbatch is very good at conveying something that for most actors is just the pretext for comedy – a certain kind of simmeringly significant British uptightness. He plays Greville Wynne, a mousy 1960s businessman, always going to Moscow on boring business and who is asked by MI6 to make contact with a possible Soviet defector. And so, with his fusty homburg and moustache, he makes his appointment with destiny.14.Atonement (2007)View image in fullscreenHazy memories … Atonement.Photograph: Working Title/AllstarOne of Cumberbatch’s distinctive supporting roles is in this bold and starrily cast version of the Ian McEwan bestseller about a tragic lie that changes lives. Cumberbatch is Paul Marshall, a well-spoken young man who visits an aristocratic country house before the second world war, and whose sexual assault of a girl there forms part of a calamitous misunderstanding and untruth. Cumberbatch does sterling work, but this is one of the lesser roles that he tended to get before TV’s Sherlock catapulted him into the big league.13.Four Lions (2010)Cumberbatch can play comedy, although he rarely gets the chance. He displays those skills in this small but perfectly deadpan role, as the special branch negotiator who has to talk to the potential suicide bombers in Chris Morris’s acidic black comedy about jihadist extremism. The character’s stolid professionalism coexists with a certain kind of cluelessness almost comparable to that of the bumbling terrorists themselves.12.Black Mass (2015)View image in fullscreenCrime-adjacent … as Billy Bulger in Black Mass.Photograph: Courtesy of Warner Bros. Pictures/Warner Bros./AllstarNo self-respecting English actor shrinks from the challenge of an accent – and in Scott Cooper’s Black Mass, Cumberbatch does an entertainingly studied version of south Bostonian: posh, but with a hint of the mean streets. In this true-story mob drama, he is Massachusetts senator Billy Bulger, at home in the cigar-smoky gentleman’s clubs and bars exchanging worldly badinage with the great and the good. But his brother is gangster Whitey Bulger, played by Johnny Depp, and Billy finds himself under pressure to persuade Whitey to become an informant. Cumberbatch is very good at showing how infatuated Billy is with his own prestige.11.War Horse (2011)As befits the title, this movie demonstrated Cumberbatch’s horse-riding chops and the elegant figure he cuts in the saddle. As the blusteringly patriotic Maj Jamie Stewart, he is every inch a clenched member of the officer class, giving a fierce speech to his men before a deeply misjudged cavalry charge in which the British are cut down by German machine guns. Cumberbatch looks and sounds the part, and you can fully imagine that gimlet-eyed fanaticism on the faces of actual officers of the time. But it’s a slightly cartoony character in a slightly cartoony film.10.Starter for 10 (2006)View image in fullscreenFingers on buzzers … Starter for 10.Photograph: Icon Film/AllstarA very early movie role for Cumberbatch, in which – perhaps before the industry quite realised what a thoroughbred they were dealing with – he was cast in an almost slapstick role as the pop-eyed, geeky butt-of-the-joke. In this version of David Nicholls’ novel about students competing in the TV quizshow University Challenge, James McAvoy is the fresher at Bristol Uni, mad keen to be on the team. But he is unbearably patronised by Cumberbatch’s team captain – a pompous postgrad – and their confrontation ends in chaotic violence. It’s a very tasty, broad comic role for Cumberbatch – and it’s a shame he hasn’t developed that side of his career.9.1917 (2019)View image in fullscreenA fierce martinet … 1917.Photograph: François Duhamel/APCumberbatch’s second performance as a ramrod-straight commanding officer in the first world war. His contribution here is smaller than in Steven Spielberg’s War Horse, but it is a very powerful cameo and in some ways more realistic. He is Col Mackenzie, to whom two desperate young lance corporals have to deliver a vital message to call off an attack the cunning Germans are attempting to lure the British into making. His Mackenzie is a fierce martinet, careworn by slaughter and by the burden of command, and with a poignant hint of despair.8.Tinker Tailor Soldier Spy (2011)Cumberbatch had one of the trickiest, knottiest roles of his career in this John le Carré thriller, playing Peter Guillam – an intelligence officer low enough on the food chain to have escaped suspicion of double-dealing, but nonetheless little liked by his superiors. He has, however, a close relationship with Gary Oldman’s spy chief George Smiley and, by virtue of the semi-protected status this gives him, has a somewhat cool and insolent manner. Cumberbatch amusingly carries off a cuff-shooting style with a rather absurd mop of a haircut, and a dandruffy three-piece suit.7.Star Trek Into Darkness (2013)View image in fullscreenUnprecedented villainy … as Khan in Star Trek Into Darkness.Photograph: Paramount Pictures/AllstarCumberbatch deserves a massive supervillain role and perhaps the sheer obviousness of this possibility means that he hasn’t really had one. (He could be a Bond villain – but, then again, he may be more interesting as Bond himself.) At all events, he was a watchable turn as the blankly named John Harrison, AKA Khan Noonien Singh – the enigmatically cool foe of Kirk and Spock in JJ Abrams’ 2013 follow-up to his great reboot of the sci-fi pop classic. His role here is a little light on dialogue, and Abrams’ intention was probably more to let us simply luxuriate in that mysterious face: unbearably sexy in its rodenty way.6.Wreckers (2011)Writer-director Dictynna Hood’s little-known indie drama gives us one of Cumberbatch’s most interesting and fully realised performances, notable because there is less of the slant, quirk or mannerism that many other directors and writers would contrive for him. He plays David, a schoolteacher and decent guy married to Claire Foy’s Dawn, living in a quiet English village. Their home life is upended when his troubled brother Nick (Shaun Evans) returns from the army and reveals some uncomfortable family secrets. Cumberbatch’s David shows that he himself is not the cool customer we thought. A finely detailed and well-judged performance.5.Doctor Strange (2016)Cumberbatch was genius casting as Doctor Strange, arriving at what now, perhaps, looks like the end of the Marvel superhero golden age. Strange is a brilliant but arrogant neurosurgeon who achieves his superpowers – in time-honoured fashion – through an accident, but has to be schooled in a distinctively Zen kind of mastery by an enigmatic mentor (played by Tilda Swinton). With this, and 2022 follow-up Doctor Strange in the Multiverse of Madness, Marvel gave Cumberbatch a massive global cineplex audience for his unique charisma, theatricality and unconventional handsomeness.4.The Wonderful Story of Henry Sugar (2023)View image in fullscreenMagical stuff … The Wonderful Story of Henry Sugar.Photograph: Courtesy of NetflixPerhaps it is odd that Cumberbatch has only been in two Wes Anderson films: this Oscar-winning short for Netflix, and The Phoenician Scheme two years later. The elegance and British idiosyncrasy of Cumberbatch would make him an obvious candidate. Henry Sugar gives Cumberbatch another side to his underappreciated comedy style: the deadpan pose, which is here an almost Keatonian stoneface. His Sugar is a wealthy, greedy man with an inscrutable manner, who discovers in himself magic powers (which are, however, not quite superpowers in the Marvel sense) that he is able to exploit for gain in casinos all over the world.3.12 Years a Slave (2013)View image in fullscreenComplicit and self-pitying … 12 Years a Slave.Photograph: Jaap Buitendijk/New Regency Pictures/AllstarCumberbatch played the most insidiously culpable role in Steve McQueen’s Oscar-winning slavery drama – the true story of Solomon Northup (Chiwetel Ejiofor), a black man abducted in 1841 from freedom in the US north into slavery in the south. Northup has two masters: the apparently thoughtful and liberal William Ford, played by Cumberbatch, who then sells him to the sadistic and odious Edwin Epps, played by Michael Fassbender. Ford is supposedly the good guy, and yet he is all the more despicable for being so blandly complicit in the situation, and for his weakness in selling Solomon to a man he knows is evil just to clear his personal debt. His chief emotion is a kind of sorrowing and worldly self-pity.2.The Imitation Game (2014)Here is the one movie that really gave Cumberbatch a chance to develop the Sherlockian persona that had made him an international star. He plays Alan Turing, the genius mathematician and wartime codebreaker who was later persecuted as a gay man in the 1950s, in the middle of a seedy moral panic led by Churchill’s home secretary, David Maxwell Fyfe. Cumberbatch is excellent at showing the young Turing’s brilliance, his abruptness and tactlessness (which may now be attributed to autism spectrum disorder), and also his sensitivity and loneliness. There is a rich, romantic melancholy in the fake heterosexual relationship that Turing confects with his fellow codebreaker and friend Joan Clarke, played with energy and charm by Keira Knightley. Sherlock was fictional, but the equally exceptional Turing was real, and achieved more than Sherlock ever could.1.The Power of the Dog (2021)View image in fullscreenOrnery and sociopathic … The Power of the Dog.Photograph: Kirsty Griffin/APIronically, Cumberbatch’s greatest film to date is a western – and this most English of actors plays the nastiest US roughneck imaginable. He is the boorish and charmless Phil Burbank, who runs a ranch in remote Montana with his brother George (Jesse Plemons). Phil is more than just ornery; he is a sociopath and a bully, obsessed with mocking his brother’s pretensions to culture and refinement, but profoundly dependent on him emotionally. And so, when George marries sensitive widow and single mother Rose Gordon (Kirsten Dunst), Phil starts harassing and abusing her – there is a bizarre scene where he strums derisively along on a five-string banjo while she is trying to play the piano. In his dysfunctional, childlike way, he is clearly unable to stop thinking about the fact that his brother and this woman are enjoying sexual relations. Phil himself would appear to be a virgin. Or is he? He also begins to take an oppressively strange interest in Rose’s teenage son, offering to teach him to ride and take him out into the remote hills to school him in the rancher ways of manly self-reliance. It is a huge role for Cumberbatch, and his wild-eyed magnetic and sinister aura floods the screen.Explore more on these topicsFilmRankedBenedict CumberbatchComedy filmsRomance filmsThrillersFilm adaptationsComedyShareReuse this content

title: There Are No Ghosts at the Grand – stripping back the physical and metaphorical layers of a haunted hotel | Games | The Guardian
description: Renovate rooms, investigate the supernatural and watch people burst into song in this surreal British ghost story
Author: Jordan Erica Webber
Headline: Renovate rooms, investigate the supernatural and watch people burst into song in this surreal British ghost story
Content: A very British sensibility … screenshot from There Are No Ghosts at the Grand.Illustration: Friday SundaeView image in fullscreenA very British sensibility … screenshot from There Are No Ghosts at the Grand.Illustration: Friday SundaeSummer games previewsGamesThere Are No Ghosts at the Grand – stripping back the physical and metaphorical layers of a haunted hotelRenovate rooms, investigate the supernatural and watch people burst into song in this surreal British ghost storyJordan Erica WebberThu 23 Jul 2026 09.00 EDTLast modified on Thu 23 Jul 2026 09.01 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleAyoung American man arrives in a dilapidated British seaside town to take on the renovations of an equally run-down hotel, inherited from a mysteriously absent father he never knew. His power tools talk to him in a Scottish accent, the local people speak in baffling slang, and occasionally people burst into song. Oh, and there are ghosts.There Are No Ghosts at the Grand is a novel mix of genres – no bad thing in these times of sequels and remakes – but, according to the creative director of independent developersFriday Sundae, Anil Glendinning, it grew organically from a single goal: to tell a very British ghost story.Based in Bristol, Anil and his partner and co-founder Rachel Glendinning have ready access to the UK’s abundance of stately homes. “We spent a lot of time wandering around National Trust properties,” says Anil. “Peeking around some of the back rooms that are guarded furiously by red ribbon tape and pensioners who tell you not to go back there. What we discovered was rooms that were half in disrepair and half returning to a state that they may have looked [like] back in Edwardian or Victorian ages. And we thought: wouldn’t that be such a huge amount of fun, to do that in a video game?”View image in fullscreenSecret spaces … There Are No Ghosts at the Grand.Illustration: Friday SundaeHence the main interaction: using a range of power tools to strip away mouldering wallpaper, rearrange furniture, vacuum up detritus, and apply a fresh coat of paint. (And, at night, to fight ghosts.) As you work, other characters chat about your choices, the room, its history. Many are based on real people, even the animated paintbrush in your toolkit, inspired by people Anil knew growing up in Scotland: “On the surface, our Robert C McBrushy character is an ode to Microsoft’s Clippy. But actually, he is a father figure, but one that spans a spectrum of different fathers, from one who is thoughtful and gives you wonderful advice, to one who loses his temper and berates you.”Several of the game’s quirkier features evolved in similar ways. Notably, the musical numbers were another British twist: “There’s a very blurry line between comedy and horror, particularly within UK movies and culture. We have always written our own music for the game, and during one sequence we thought we would play with the idea of the character actually singing it. It felt quite natural, being quite surreal, and having the players question if what they were seeing was real.”The game features voice talent including musical theatre performer Alex Brightman (of Beetlejuice the Musical fame) and Marcia Richards (from London band the Skints), but there is an option to skip the musical numbers. Drawing from his experience working on motion-controlled games for the Microsoft Kinect, Anil is keen to offer customisations to suit a range of players, such as an autocomplete slider for those unwilling or unable to paint every centimetre of a wall.Where they won’t make accommodations is, again, in the Britishness of the game, with no glossary for any slang. The actors collaborated in the creation and expression of their characters, leading to a natural style that might not always translate, even to other English-speakers, as with the (unannounced) British-Jamaican singer who voices Colin: “We allowed them to improvise and speak naturally in their own mix of patois and British English and find this really interesting natural code-switching, depending on which character they’re speaking to. So it feels really authentic.”The aim is for the characters you meet and the town they inhabit to feel like the real Britain that the developers know, in all its diversity. If, like the American protagonist, you feel a bit out of your depth; well, that’s kind of the point.There Are No Ghosts at the Grand is in development for PC and Xbox Series X/S. Release date late 2026.Explore more on these topicsGamesSummer games previewsRole playing gamesPuzzle gamesfeaturesShareReuse this content

title: Beyond the body: Wayne McGregor stretches the definition of dance at this year’s Venice Dance Biennale | Dance | The Guardian
description: With the British dance legend at the helm, this year’s themes ranged from quantum gravity to slightly muted geopolitics
Author: Lyndsey Winship
Headline: With the British dance legend at the helm, this year’s themes ranged from quantum gravity to slightly muted geopolitics
