Content: Great visual drama … Five Days in the Sun at the Venice Dance Biennale.Photograph: Julia GatView image in fullscreenGreat visual drama … Five Days in the Sun at the Venice Dance Biennale.Photograph: Julia GatDanceBeyond the body: Wayne McGregor stretches the definition of dance at this year’s Venice Dance BiennaleWith the British dance legend at the helm, this year’s themes ranged from quantum gravity to slightly muted geopoliticsLyndsey WinshipThu 23 Jul 2026 08.18 EDTLast modified on Thu 23 Jul 2026 08.20 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleThe most memorable performance in the opening days of this year’sVeniceDance Biennale had barely any dance in it at all. Italian choreographer Andrea Salustri’s Invisible is built around movement, but it’s movement of light, air, water and sound waves, with Salustri and co-creator Alice Rende as shadowy figures conducting the elements. They brew up storms like Zeus and Poseidon, with flashing veins of light, sounds of pummelling rain (in fact small drips and bubbles amplified by powerful microphones) and an enormous amount of billowing smoke that ultimately whips itself into a spinning tornado.View image in fullscreenConducting the elements … Andrea Salustri in Invisible.Photograph: Cosmin CirsteaSalustri, who won the Italian callout to create a new work for this year’s biennale, is a circus artist and philosophy graduate, and he is also a choreographer – a definition so much wider than one who devises dance. This year marks the 20th dedicated dance festival in Venice (dance was previously seen as part of the theatre or music programmes here until 1999, since when it has been an annual, then biennial, and now annual feature of Venice’s arts bonanza). In a talk to mark this anniversary, current artistic director Wayne McGregor was joined by four former directors to discuss their aims for the event. The question of “what dance is” kept arising as the ongoing line of inquiry, a question “so ordinary that it is still necessary somehow”, said choreographer Marie Chouinard.A new film, Life Lines, put together by McGregor and filmmaker Ravi Deepres, compiles archive clips from festivals past, showing that the range of dance here has been vast and multifarious, from the fairly conventional to the theatrically wild. There’s clearly no single story of contemporary dance’s development, and McGregor’s theme for this year’s festival is Time Does Not Exist. Influenced by physicist Carlo Rovelli and the theory of quantum gravity, his is not a linear view of time, and without doubt, in dance (and in life), we carry our embodied pasts constantly into the present. That’s no more evident than in Soa Ratsifandrihana’s piece Fampitaha, Fampita, Fampitàna (meaning “comparison, transmission, rivalry” in Malagasy, the language of Madagascar, from where the choreographer is from). It’s a piece about living in the diaspora and concerned with the sometimes joyful, sometimes painful, memories that link back home – especially the memories stored in our bodies, in songs, stories and dance steps. It’s a gentle piece, conversational between the three dancers and fabulous guitarist Joël Rabesolo, in the sense that like many conversations it meanders, then touches on the profound, then gets self-absorbed, connects again, tails off. But it has its moments.View image in fullscreenMemories stored in dance steps … Fampitaha, fampita, fampitàna by Soa Ratsifandrihana.Photograph: Harilay RabenjaminaSouth African Mamela Nyamza is the recipient of this year’s Silver Lion award, for an outstanding emerging artist (the Golden Lion, for lifetime achievement, will be awarded to Aboriginal Australian company Bangarra Dance theatre later in the festival). Nyamza’s work is generally shot through with political activism, although the politics in her piece performed at the biennale, The Herd/Less, is of the quiet kind. A clan of brightly clothed characters arrive on tin-can stilts in rhythmic strides, seemingly a close-knit, cohesive unit. Individuals emerge to fill the role of leader and changes of allegiance seem to happen by stealth rather than force – traditional symbols of status or control including spears and knobkerries are present, but not violently wielded – and the sense of where power lies subtly shifts.A more straightforward piece of contemporary dance comes from Emanuel Gat, the Israeli choreographer who has been based in France for nearly 20 years. The last of his works seen in the UK wasset to the music of Tears for Fears, this one, called Five Days in the Sun, is danced to Mahler’s Fifth Symphony – now that’s range. But if you’re choosing a piece of music as epic as Mahler’s Fifth (a recording sadly; who can afford a live orchestra?), then the question is: what are you going to do with it? In parts, the dance feeds off the music, elsewhere it’s insensitive to it, or entirely uninterested, casually brushing past some of the most exquisite moments in late Romanticism. It’s all deliberate – Gat’s a trained musician, and has spoken before about not choreographing to the obvious beat – but to what end?View image in fullscreenQuiet politics … The Herd/Less by Mamela Nyamza.Photograph: Kim Stevens/LimelightfotoThe symphony is said to have been influenced by both Mahler’s brush with death and falling in love with his wife, some big feelings, but much of the dance here feels bloodless, although the movement is clever, playful, sometimes comical; specific as well as freewheeling. The standout performer is Anaïs Van Caekenberghe , who eats up the stage dancing with such delight in her physicality.A highlight of Five Days in the Sun is the costumes in the first movement, the dancers in long flowing gowns, the gauzy peach fabric rippling with the slightest movement, swooshing in the dancers’ slipstream. This, along with the expert lighting (also designed by Gat) does bring great visual drama. Andrea Salustri is not the only choreographer who recognises that the power of movement can mean more than dancing bodies.The Biennale Danza 2026 continues until 1 AugustLyndsey Winship’s trip was provided by the Venice Dance BiennaleExplore more on these topicsDanceVenice Biennale 2026VenicefeaturesShareReuse this content

title: A grief therapist told me I should live to honor my son. How would I do that? | Autobiography and memoir | The Guardian
description: After my 22-month-old child died, music and poetry brought catharsis and comfort
Author: Guardian staff reporter
Headline: After my 22-month-old child died, music and poetry brought catharsis and comfort
Content: Illustration: Guardian Design/Getty ImagesView image in fullscreenIllustration: Guardian Design/Getty ImagesAutobiography and memoirA grief therapist told me I should live to honor my son. How would I do that?After my 22-month-old child died, music and poetry brought catharsis and comfortMatthew SchnipperThu 23 Jul 2026 07.00 EDTLast modified on Thu 23 Jul 2026 13.23 EDTShareThe day Renzo had his aneurysm, in the afternoon before it happened, I distinctly remember telling him that it was going to be a pleasure to watch him grow up. It seems ridiculous to write that here, the foreshadowing so on the nose as to be unbelievable. But it’s true. To him and my wife Allegra, I said, “I love our little family.” After the struggles around his birth, job loss, the pandemic, it felt, finally, like we had made it to the other side. We had, I suppose, just not how I envisioned it.My friends Jayson and Stacy recommended I get to knowDavid Kessler’s work. According to Harvard Business Review, Kessler is “the world’s foremost expert on grief”. He’d worked closely with Elisabeth Kübler-­Ross, who was the reigning expert on grief before her own death in 2004. A large part of Kübler-­Ross’s legacy is her classification of what she calls the five stages of grief: denial, anger, bargaining, depression and acceptance, as described in her 1969 book On Death and Dying. After you experience a death, she wrote, these stages can occur in any order, with one or more possibly not occurring at all. Just weeks after Renzo’s death, I’d tricked myself into thinking I had jumped all the way to acceptance when really I was trying to outrun anger and depression. Bargaining hasn’t been a big one for me, nor has denial. The facts have been pretty hard to ignore.In 2019, Kessler updated Kübler-Ross’s five stages with a sixth: finding meaning. He made this addition after the death of one of his two sons, who accidentally overdosed at 21 years old. On Kessler’s website you can find all kinds of useful material. There are straightforward sections, like chapters in a handbook, called A Loved One’s Belongings, Grief & Holidays, and When a Child Dies. When a Child Dies is a series of videos, all of which I dragged my feet on watching until Allegra urged me to. In the videos, Kessler has a gentle presence. He’s grandfatherly but young, in his 60s, with a healthy head of hair. His voice is squeaky, but he speaks softly. He’s wearing a dark blue vest and a turquoise button-­down shirt, standing in front of a piece of abstract art and white shutters. They are closed. A green glass vase with pink flowers peeks out from the side of the screen. VeryNancy Meyersmeets Mister Rogers. The presentation has the effect of making Kessler trustworthy, if anonymous. All smooth edges, no distracting elements, no sudden movements, just a nice guy, shot authoritatively in the center of the frame, talking calmly about unthinkable disaster. Welcome to the rest of your miserable life, he may as well have been saying, this non-threatening emissary from hell broadcasting straight to my laptop.The first video in the series is called The Loss –­ the Moment Everything Changed. Kessler focuses on the “moment” part of the loss as he explains the concept of “anticipatory grief”. This is the kind I did not get an opportunity to have. For your parents, your grandparents, your pets, there is a built-­in expectation that they will predecease you. So you imagine their death, imagine your life without them, as that is the natural order of things. That imagining helps prepare you for the actual event, like a practice run before the big race. With a sudden death, it’s the surprise that gets you. You’re unprepared. You wouldn’t run a marathon if you’d never taken a jog.double quotation markI’ve likened grieving my son’s death to falling off a cliffAs I watched Kessler’s videos, about a month after Renzo’s death, I found myself feeling resentful toward people who had the luxury of pregrieving their loved ones. It was strange and sad to find myself not only envying but begrudging those also in mourning, these straw men against whom I measured my own level of suffering, unfairly or otherwise. On the one hand, pain is pain is pain. On the other, it’s not.Kessler seemed to be reading my mind as, partway through the first video, he broke the third wall. “We deal with so many unwanted surprises as a parent dealing with loss,” he says. “It’s so much more brutal. I don’t have to tell you how brutal it is. You know.” I do.I’ve likened grieving Renzo’s death to falling off a cliff. I’ve found that others who have mourned unexpected deaths, of any kind, have felt a similar drop. Their loved one’s existence was terra firma, and then they toppled over the edge into freefall.I’m rewatching these videos as I write this. It’s eerie to see them again after a few years. Watching for the first time, I was totally bewildered, stumbling around in the dark. Kessler was describing the death world like a store manager showing me the ropes on my first day on the job. Now I’ve been working there for a long time. I know where everything is.View image in fullscreenRise Above by Matthew Schnipper.Photograph: Random HouseGrief has rules, Kessler explains in the second video. He likens it to blindness or the loss of a limb. You have to learn to navigate this new terrain. He spends a while talking about laughter. Is laughing disloyal? No, it’s not. Social media, he says, bringing with it many photos of other people’s obliviously happy families, can be a risk; be mindful. And don’t expect your spouse to be your support system; they are as out of gas as you. This was ominous. “How can two empty tanks support each other?”A number of times he refers to the pitfalls of conversations with strangers in the checkout line. People will disappoint you; be prepared. “People say stupid things in life, and they don’t quit saying them in grief.” He says it out the side of his mouth in a funny, conspiratorial kind of way, like a stand­up comic.Surprisingly, he starts off the third video with a joke that seems more for the grief counselors’ group text than for the newly bereaved. “People always ask me, ‘How long will I grieve?’ I say, ‘How long will your child be dead?’”But as the video goes on, he regains his seriousness. “The goal of grief work is to remember our child with more love than pain.” I dreaded this idea. The pain I felt mourning Renzo’s death was evidence of his existence. Missing him was the closest I could get to being with him. The worse I felt, the truer our love. The better I’d begin to feel, the further he’d fade. Time moves in only one direction: the wrong one. “No one wants to be the bereaved parent,” Kessler says. But “we’ve been given more life. So what are we going to do with the rest of our lives?”It reminded me of two lines from Mary Oliver’s poemPoem 133: The Summer Daythat have been so oft quoted as to become cliche.double quotation markTell me, what is it you plan to dowith your one wild and precious life?What so many saw as an invitation I now saw as a threat.Tell me.I’d never read the entire poem, and so I sought it out. I was not surprised to find out that most of it is about Oliver traipsing through the grass, appreciating the anatomy of a grasshopper. I was, however, surprised to see that the line before those famous final two is “Doesn’t everything die at last, and too soon?”If Kessler was right, I had a choice to make about how to use the time I’d been given. He said I should live in a way that honored Renzo. How would I do that?One thing I did know about myself was that I’d always wanted to be a parent. I love kids. They’re excitable, they’re weird, they love to shove food in their mouths and make big messes and not clean them up. They remind me of myself. Devoting myself to a child of my own seemed like an ideal scenario in which to disappear. And it was. Renzo’s life served to define my own in a way that felt much healthier than my relationship with work. While some of this was emotional, a lot of it was logistical. He was a baby; he had to be watched at every moment. It was exhausting but euphoric.After his daughter’s death, Jayson had become close with Kessler, and he offered to introduce us. We took him up on it, and he connected us over email. Kessler responded quickly. He said he was willing to talk.“My heart sank when I read about your sweet son Renzo. Would Thursday, January 13th at 6 PM EST work for you?” It would. His assistant sent us a Zoom link.For a brief time, Allegra and I had the living room set up backward from how it is now, with the couch where the chairs are now and the chairs where the couch is. So when I remember our conversation with Kessler, the two of us on the couch together, huddled over my laptop, the memory feels inverted, wrong. We should have been facing the other way.I had high hopes for our bespoke treatment. Once he heard our story, I imagined, Kessler would have some silver bullet to decimate our grief. Everyone grieves differently, he’d said in his videos. Now was the time for him to see how we did it. And then fix it.Kessler was warm and patient, but most of what he said was the same as in his videos. The one addition was that he kept telling us to be resilient. That’s it? That’s the big secret? We’d climbed the mountain, seen the oracle, and he was out of ideas.I know now it’s not anyone’s fault. Certainly not Kessler’s. He had done more than anyone to help us heal in those early days from that for which there is no cure. But if even he had nothing for us other than “resilience”, it was difficult to not feel hopeless. We made plans to check in and talk again but never did.double quotation markThe grief expert kept telling us to be resilient. That’s it?Despite my capriciousness in regard to accepting the wisdom and advice of others, I continued seeking it out. I’ve always looked for guides, gurus, in times of difficulty, hoping they would offer a vista into another, better way of life. I was reading whatever I could, my antennae up for transmissions of wisdom. Online, I stumbled across the audio recording of an interviewToni Morrisongave to the Paris Review in 1993. One thing she said was “I think of beauty as an absolute necessity. I don’t think it’s a privilege or an indulgence. It’s not even a quest. I think it’s almost like knowledge, which is to say, it’s what we were born for. I think finding, incorporating, and then representing beauty is what humans do.” This quote, surfaced years after the original interview, when the entire unedited conversation was turned into a podcast, was not included in the original print interview; it must have been edited out. Why? Too moony? Too frivolous? Perhaps, but this was the first bit of insight that truly made me reconsider what I’d need to do in order to heal.I’d never lived the life of an aesthete, but I was suddenly starved for beauty. Without Renzo’s pure presence, it was like I’d become beauty anemic and I needed to over-index in order to even approach neutral.My usual sources were running dry. Music had become an albatross. Even my ears were grieving. I’d put on a record and recoil. Everything suddenly sounded sour. I was an idiot for ever having liked techno, unfeeling and inhuman. Indie rock felt whiny and immature. Jazz, an interminable screed of noisomeness. Hardcore was the music of belligerent blowhards.Bill Callahan’s was the only singing voice I could handle. He advised me well in his guttural drawl. “No matter how far wrong you’ve gone / You can always turn around.” But after weeks of nonstop Callahan, I needed to find diversity in sages.My friend Brian had recently visited and before he left had given me a few records from his storage space, including an album of Indian santoor music. A santoor, I learned, is essentially a hammer dulcimer, a stringed instrument played with tiny mallets. Imagine someone strumming the high notes of a harp at a warp speed. Giving Callahan a break, I listened to the santoor. The songs were long and open, unstructured but soft, with no dissonance and no words. When the santoor played, it sounded like the room was glowing. Beauty, finally beheld.View image in fullscreenBill Evans.Photograph: David Redfern/RedfernsUnfortunately, it’s difficult to locate santoor records in America, so after dozens of plays of my sole recording, I did finally have to move on. I explored different pianists, looking for music that was close to imperceptible. I discovered an album of piano music literally called Walk in Beauty. The music was gentle, like snow falling. Then I started spending a lot of time with music by the pianistBill Evans. His albums were often gently elegiac, as though he knew, when he recorded them more than half a century earlier, that I’d one day need them. One song I gravitated toward, a solo piece performed in 1966 at New York’s Town Hall, was dedicated to Evans’s father after his then-recent death. Long and laconic, it was not a lament, nor was it a celebration. It was a recitation of facts, of pain, of admiration, and of love, translated into music and banged out on the keys.I started going to record stores to buy every Bill Evans record I could find. A second- floor shop in Chinatown that­ specialized in Japanese music for some reason contained a bounty. Undercurrent, Explorations, Conversations with Myself, Alone, You Must Believe in Spring. I played each album over and over until its songs were a part of me, and then I’d get a new one and begin the process again. As I delved deeper into the back half of Evans’s career, the portraits on the albums’ covers got increasingly haggard. He went from handsome and clean-­shaven in the 50s and 60s to sallow, hunched and skinny in the 70s –­ his body rotting away from the effects of heroin use. In 1980, Evans, in New York to perform a series of gigs, died from a fatal hemorrhage at 51 after a lifetime of drug and alcohol abuse. A tragedy. He was so young, depending on how you look at it.Though it was recorded in 1977, You Must Believe in Spring wasn’t released until after Evans died. It’s a playful record that includes a recording of Freddie Freeloader fromMiles Davis’s landmark 1959 album Kind of Blue, on which Evans was the pianist. I wonder if he recorded it again, almost 20 years later, to remind everyone, or maybe himself, of his presence in history. It’s a jaunty rendition, one loosened by time. Elsewhere on the album, Evans covers the M*A*S*H theme, also known as Suicide Is Painless. I tried not to get any ideas.Renzo, of course, died on the wintriest day, Christmas Eve. Thinking of his cold-weather death, I liked the idea of believing in spring and not summer, the hedonist’s season. Spring is a reasonable time of year. On its face, believing in spring doesn’t promise much other than a realist’s acknowledgment of the change of season. A reckoning with the inevitable passage of time, a reckoning with which I was struggling. Spring would come, a fact; believing in its renewal was up to me.This is an edited extract fromRise Above by Matthew Schnipper, out now from Random House.

title: The music in Nolan’s Odyssey is as well-crafted – and bloodlessly forgettable – as the film itself | The Odyssey | The Guardian
description: Ludwig Göransson’s meticulous score uses instruments of the era to create an earthy, raw and bleak soundscape, but it merely supports the portrayal of an uncomplicated hero
Author: Guardian staff reporter
Headline: Ludwig Göransson’s meticulous score uses instruments of the era to create an earthy, raw and bleak soundscape, but it merely supports the portrayal of an uncomplicated hero
Content: Struggling to be heard … a battle scene from The Odyssey.Photograph: Melinda Sue Gordon/Universal PicturesView image in fullscreenStruggling to be heard … a battle scene from The Odyssey.Photograph: Melinda Sue Gordon/Universal PicturesThe OdysseyThe music in Nolan’s Odyssey is as well-crafted – and bloodlessly forgettable – as the film itselfTom SmailLudwig Göransson’s meticulous score uses instruments of the era to create an earthy, raw and bleak soundscape, but it merely supports the portrayal of an uncomplicated heroThu 23 Jul 2026 05.01 EDTLast modified on Thu 23 Jul 2026 10.58 EDTShareAfew weeks ago I came across a video of Professor Armand D’Angour, associate professor of classics at Oxford university. He explained how, through groundbreaking research involving fragments of melody and rhythms found on papyrus and stone and the reconstruction of ancient instruments, it is now possible to hear for the first time in 2,000 years what ancient Greek music would have sounded like.Given Ludwig Göransson’s beautiful theme for theaulos(an ancient double-barrelled flute) in Christopher Nolan’s latest blockbuster, The Odyssey, and his reputation for dedicated research into his subject matter, it seems very likely that he too has heard these calls from the ancient past.Over time, a successful film score becomes synonymous with its film, but before its creation, the potential for its direction is endless. “None at all is better than the wrong music,” Alfred Hitchcock famously said. In The White Ribbon Michael Haneke opts for none at all with the exception of music from diegetic sources (performances within the film itself).But that is a fairly austere and rare standpoint. Generally music is preferred, a decision has to be made and a direction established.View image in fullscreenThe Odyssey’s composer Ludwig Göransson.Photograph: Eamonn M McCormack/Getty ImagesAccording to a 2026 article in Vanity Fair, Nolan asked Ludwig Göransson not to use an orchestra. He wanted a recognisable world for the audience, but not one which they had encountered before. Göransson needed to expand his horizons and work with sounds he had not used previously.Bronze gongs were employed, metallic objects of all shapes and sizes were struck. The sound world was to be earthy, raw, bleak and to add further to this mix, instruments of the era were used. Like Professor D’Angour, Göransson reconstructed the ancientaulosand early lyres, adding synths and vocals – not massed ranks of voices, but smaller, more intimate groupings.I am a composer of contemporary classical music, who also writes the occasional film score. There are two reasons why I am in an interesting position to comment on Göransson’s score.Firstly, I am writing an opera ofThe Odyssey, to be premiered in Athens in 2027, directed by Isabella Bywater and based on the translation by Emily Wilson which inspired Nolan. Secondly, my most recent film was Jonathan Nossiter’s Last Words, set in a post-apocalyptic world not dissimilar to the hostile landscape of The Odyssey, for which the brief was strikingly similar to Nolan’s for Göransson: “The orchestra should consist primarily of stone, wind and metal.”View image in fullscreen‘An orchestra of stone, wind and metal’ … a scene from Last Words.Photograph: TCD/Prod.DB/AlamySo I understand. And personally, I love Göransson’s plan: the natural sounds of the earth combined with ancient instruments and a layer of synth and vocals. But the resulting score did not resound as it might have.Many felt there was a lack of emotional heft, a lack of recognisable music. Some said they didn’t notice the music at all. This in itself need not be a bad thing. The purpose of a film score is to enhance the film, not to be memorable.If it can serve its purpose and also be memorable, then that is good news for all concerned. Indeed sometimes the music is just so memorable that the film has no choice but gracefully and gratefully to accept the fact – I think of the main theme in Schindler’s List, or the shower scene in Psycho.But to be memorable should never be the primary aim of a film score. And Göransson’s score for The Odyssey is certainly not memorable. But does it enhance the film?The ingredients were all there, but I have to agree with the dissenting voices that the final soundtrack, with moments of exception, was unconvincing.Part of the blame for this must be laid at the door of the mix. A hefty layer of sound design was added and very often the music struggled to be heard. This is clearly not the composer’s fault.But listening to the soundtrack on its own, I found that the inspiration I had hoped for was rarely to be found. With some exceptions – in particular the Sirens and one of Calypso’s scenes where the singing was adventurous and exquisite – the vocal writing I found to be predictably Hollywood. The extensive use of large drums likewise belongs to the familiar world I thought Göransson was attempting to avoid. Indeed, a lot of the bigger moments sound like a traditional score, just with synth instead of orchestra.There were even moments when I felt the music fighting against the film. Sometimes, of course, this is a message to the audience that all is not as it seems. But in these moments it just felt misplaced. The merging of the soundscape of the ancient world with elements of a more traditional approach was, for me, not a successful one.View image in fullscreenProven pedigree … from left, Michael B Jordan, Autumn Durald Arkapaw, Ludwig Göransson and Ryan Coogler with their Oscars for Sinners.Photograph: Mario Anzuoni/ReutersOdysseus, in Wilson’s translation, is famously, and to some controversially, a “complicated man”. Nolan’s epic, in my opinion, does not focus enough on this complicated nature, the flaws, the diametrically opposed elements of his character. Yes there is loss, but mainly this relates to his men and his memory. But there is so much more. Here is a man who had power and glory, kingdom, reputation, home, wife, son – and he has lost everything. He sits alone on Calypso’s beach, a no one staring across the “fruitless sea”.This lack of emotional complication in Odysseus himself, so integral to Homer’s story, was reflected in the music. It seems that, for all Göransson’s laudable efforts to recreate the sounds of the ancient world and to marry them with more recognisable elements, they too got lost in that fruitless sea.Göransson is a skilful composer. He has won three Oscars and is clearly much loved by film audiences. In The Odyssey he has, in my opinion, succeeded in serving the film, but I do not think he has enhanced it.Explore more on these topicsThe OdysseyChristopher NolanClassicsHomercommentShareReuse this content

title: Raymond Briggs’s library goes on sale at his local Oxfam shop | Books | The Guardian
description: Books belonging to the beloved creator of The Snowman and When the Wind Blows were donated by his estate after his death
Author: Ella Creamer
Headline: Books belonging to the beloved creator of The Snowman and When the Wind Blows were donated by his estate after his death
Content: ‘A wonderful glimpse of the visual and intellectual world of one of Britain’s most distinctive illustrators’ … Raymond Briggs in 2008.Photograph: Anthony Devlin/PAView image in fullscreen‘A wonderful glimpse of the visual and intellectual world of one of Britain’s most distinctive illustrators’ … Raymond Briggs in 2008.Photograph: Anthony Devlin/PABooksRaymond Briggs’s library goes on sale at his local Oxfam shopBooks belonging to the beloved creator of The Snowman and When the Wind Blows were donated by his estate after his deathElla CreamerThu 23 Jul 2026 12.52 EDTLast modified on Thu 23 Jul 2026 15.23 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleThe book collection of the creator of The Snowman and Fungus the Bogeyman has gone on sale at Oxfam’s Hove bookshop in East Sussex andonline.More than 1,000 books from the collection of Raymond Briggs, the beloved British author and cartoonist, were donated by his estate after hisdeath aged 88 in 2022.Briggs’s personal library spans comics, illustration, novels and social history, including works by Peanuts creator Charles Schulz and Winnie-the-Pooh illustrator EH Shepard as well as a signed first edition of Graham Rawle’s Woman’s World, the 2005 book composed of fragments from women’s magazines.Other illustrators featured in Briggs’s library include Shirley Hughes, Aubrey Beardsley, Edward Gorey and Posy Simmonds. There are about 30 comic annuals from the 50s, offering “a glimpse into the visual world that shaped Briggs’s style”, according toOxfam.View image in fullscreenThe Raymond Briggs collection in Hove’s Oxfam bookshop.Photograph: Oxfam“What’s fascinating is the range,” said Oxfam Hove bookshop volunteer Martin Hill. “You begin to see connections between the books Briggs created and the books and artists he chose to have around him.”“There are great illustrators and cartoonists, but then you find books about ageing, bathrooms, domestic life, old annuals and wartime milkmen,” he added. “It is serious, funny, ordinary and sometimes rather dark – which feels very familiar to anyone who knows Briggs’s work.”The collection includes pamphlets on nuclear disarmament, a subject explored in Briggs’s 1982 graphic novel When the Wind Blows. A 1974 book by Monty Python’s Terry Jones and Michael Palin, Bert Fegg’s Nasty Book for Boys and Girls, is also listed, along with a set of John Constable’s reproduced sketches and a first edition copy of Riddley Walker, the post-apocalyptic novel by Russell Hoban.Born in Wimbledon in 1934, Briggs later spent more than 50 years living beneath the South Downs near Hove. The area inspired the 1978 picture book The Snowman, which features Brighton’s Royal Pavilion. Briggs taught illustration at Brighton School of Art from the early 60s to 1986.View image in fullscreenHe became known for picture books that subverted conventions of children’s storytelling, in the likes of 1977’s Fungus the Bogeyman and 1973’s Father Christmas, which imagines a cantankerous Santa Claus.“I liked to find fantastical creatures and treat them realistically,” said Briggs in an interviewwith the Guardianin 2019, on the publication of his illustrated memoir Time for Lights Out. “I don’t see any difference between doing children’s and adult books. My attitude is exactly the same. I just do the book as I want it to be.”The animated adaptation of The Snowman first hit screens in 1982, and has been broadcast almost every Christmas since.After Briggs’s death, children’s authorMichael Rosen saidthat “we have been lucky to have read and watched such great work from a great artist with a great heart”.The Oxfam collection “offers a wonderful glimpse of the visual and intellectual world of one of Britain’s most distinctive illustrators”, said Hill. Prices for the books on theonline storerange from £5.99 to £400 for the first edition of Rawle’s Woman’s World.Explore more on these topicsBooksRaymond BriggsChildren and teenagersOxfamnewsShareReuse this content

title: Owner of Viking ship used in The Odyssey says studio has not paid bill for damage  | Sweden | The Guardian
description: Swedish nonprofit alleges cost to repair replica was $6,000 but Universal – which made $264m on film’s opening weekend – has not stumped up
Author: Miranda Bryant
Headline: Swedish nonprofit alleges cost to repair replica was $6,000 but Universal – which made $264m on film’s opening weekend – has not stumped up
Content: The Glad av Gillberga was used as one of the escort vessels in the flotilla, which are thought to have been similar to Nordic Viking ships.Photograph: GCMC/Peter OlaussonView image in fullscreenThe Glad av Gillberga was used as one of the escort vessels in the flotilla, which are thought to have been similar to Nordic Viking ships.Photograph: GCMC/Peter OlaussonSwedenOwner of Viking ship used in The Odyssey says studio has not paid bill for damageSwedish nonprofit alleges cost to repair replica was $6,000 but Universal – which made $264m on film’s opening weekend – has not stumped upMiranda BryantNordic correspondentThu 23 Jul 2026 11.28 EDTLast modified on Thu 23 Jul 2026 21.30 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleThe Swedish owners of a Viking longship replica that features in the Hollywood blockbusterThe Odysseysay they feel “forgotten” after film-makers allegedly returned the vessel damaged and failed to foot the bill for repairs.In nearly three decades at sea, the warship Glad av Gillberga – based on a wreck from around 1040 found in Denmark’s Roskilde fjord – has successfully sailed the North Sea, the Baltic and even voyaged to Canada.But after six months in the hands of Universal, which according to its owners promised to cover the cost of any repairs, it allegedly sustained considerable damage including to the stern and the mast.Vikingaleden, a nonprofit Viking centre in Värmland, westernSweden, said that when it invoiced Universal for the materials used to repair their ship (they did not charge for their volunteer labour), no payment arrived.View image in fullscreenMatt Damon stars as Odysseus in The Odyssey.Photograph: Melinda Sue Gordon/Universal PicturesA year later, while the Christopher Nolan epic starring Matt Damon, Tom Holland, Anne Hathaway and Zendayastorms global box offices, taking $264m (£196m) in its opening weekend alone, it says it is still waiting for its $6,000 payment.Peter Olausson, chair of the Vikingaleden association, said that although he felt proud that the ship was featured in the film, that feeling had been tinged with injustice after the film giant’s alleged failure to cover the cost of damages.Some of the damage allegedly caused to the boat’s woodwork.Photograph: SVT“We are only charging for the material cost, not the labour,” he said. “Of course Universal can afford this $6,000. We feel forgotten.”The ship is now back in action but Olausson accused Universal of going back on their word. “The agreement was that we would fix it and get financially reimbursed.”Olausson said that to Universal, the outstanding sum would be comparatively small, but for the 200-member association that runs the Viking “village” and museum, it is a significant amount.Paint damage allegedly caused to the boat.Photograph: SVTThecritically acclaimed film, which reportedly cost $250m to make, is based on Homer’s ancient Greek epic poem about the king of Ithaca, Odysseus, on a 10-year sea journey home from the Trojan war.The Glad av Gillberga was used as one of the escort vessels in the flotilla, which are thought to have been similar to Nordic Viking ships.The main ship, Odysseus’s boat, was the 35-metre-long Norwegian replica Draken Harald Hårfagre.Its Swedish captain, Björn Ahlander, said life on the ships filming for six months around Greek and Italian islands was not easy for the 30-person crew. He told the broadcasterSVT: “We rebuilt the ship into an ancient Greek ship. We removed the tents and all the modern things and had an open deck that we lived on during this time. It was quite tough.”A Universal spokesperson said: “Our records show that all invoices relating to the Glad av Gillberga were paid in full, including repairs. The studio and the production have contacted the association to clear up any misunderstanding.”Explore more on these topicsSwedenThe OdysseyEuropeChristopher NolannewsShareReuse this content

title: Murdoch and Musk in the crosshairs at Venice film festival | Venice film festival | The Guardian
description: Danny Boyle’s drama about the acquisition of the Sun and a four-hour Elon Musk documentary will premiere alongside new films from Martin McDonagh and Werner Herzog – but only one female director has a movie in competition
Author: Philip Oltermann
Headline: Danny Boyle’s drama about the acquisition of the Sun and a four-hour Elon Musk documentary will premiere alongside new films from Martin McDonagh and Werner Herzog – but only one female director has a movie in competition
Content: Paper tiger … Jack O'Connell in Ink by Danny Boyle, the opening film of this year’s Venice film festival.Photograph: StudiocanalView image in fullscreenPaper tiger … Jack O'Connell in Ink by Danny Boyle, the opening film of this year’s Venice film festival.Photograph: StudiocanalVenice film festivalMurdoch and Musk in the crosshairs at Venice film festivalDanny Boyle’s drama about the acquisition of the Sun and a four-hour Elon Musk documentary will premiere alongside new films from Martin McDonagh and Werner Herzog – but only one female director has a movie in competitionPhilip OltermannEuropean culture editorThu 23 Jul 2026 10.58 EDTLast modified on Thu 23 Jul 2026 21.30 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleTheVenice film festivalpromises to take a swing at media moguls of the print and digital era this year, with Trainspotting director Danny Boyle’s new film about Rupert Murdoch’s acquisition of the Sun and a new documentary about Elon Musk included in the lineup of the films for the festival’s 83rd edition.In Boyle’s Ink, which will open the festival on the Lido on 2 September, Australian actor Guy Pearce plays compatriot newspaper tycoon Murdoch, whose purchase of the Sun in 1969 sensationalised the media landscape and transformed public life in Britain. The script is written by Olivier award-winning playwright and screenwriter James Graham (Dear England, Brexit: The Uncivil War).Oscar-winning veteran film-makerAlex Gibneyprofiles the wealthiest person in the world, rightwing agitator and owner of social network X in his four-hour documentary Musk, which will be screened out of competition in Venice.In recent years, Venice has sought to profile itself as the glitziest and starriest of the big three European film festivals when compared with artistically challenging Cannes and politically charged Berlin.This year’s lineup, presented by long-running artistic director Alberto Barbera this week, will still draw major stars to Venice’s waterways.View image in fullscreenSam Rockwell (left) and John Malkovich in Wild Horse Nine.Photograph: Diego Araya Corvalán/Searchlight Pictures/APBritish-Irish director Martin McDonagh will showcase his new film Wild Horse Nine, in which John Malkovich and Sam Rockwell are two unlikely CIA agents in action in Chile on the eve of the coup led by Augusto Pinochet. Steve Buscemi, Tom Waits and Parker Posey also star in the awaited return of the director of In Bruges, Three Billboards Outside Ebbing, Missouri and The Banshees of Inisherin.Robert Pattinson will grace the Lido as the lead in A24’s Primetime, a psychological thriller inspired by the US reality TV show To Catch a Predator, directed by Lance Oppenheim.Real-life spouses Penélope Cruz and Javier Bardem star in French playwright-director Florian Zeller’s Bunker, in which a publisher-architect couple move to London and are invited by a millionaire to design a building that could withstand a nuclear bomb.Real-life sisters Kate and Rooney Mara star in Bucking Fastard, the first dramatic feature film in seven years by German cult director Werner Herzog. The film was originally announced as a biopic of Freda and Greta Chaplin, twins from York who spoke and moved in unison and gained notoriety after a brush with the law. It seems to have since veered off into a direction more familiar from the maker of Aguirre, the Wrath of God and Fitzcarraldo, centred around a mission to dig a tunnel through a mountain range “in search of an imaginary land”.Barbera said that Liam and Noel Gallagher had confirmed their attendance at the festival, for the premiere of Oasis reunion documentary Don’t Look Back in Anger, while Russell Crowe is expected to attend a screening of What Love Builds, a feature-length documentary about his band Indoor Garden Party.But there is some more serious fare on the Lido this year. Russian-born film-maker Ilya Khrzhanovsky will show the latest film to emerge out of hisDar project, likened to a Stalinist Truman Show and inspired by a Soviet research institute that contributed to the creation of the first atomic bomb.Ukrainian director Sergei Loznitsa will bring his documentary Imperium, about a film that was commissioned by the Italian Communist party to glorify the triumph of the Soviet Union but ended up revealing its contradictions.Laura Poitras’ latest documentary They’re Here, made with Rachel Mueller, dives into ICE’s intervention in Minneapolis and the killing of Renée Good, with some previously undisclosed footage.Tragicomedies such as Caro Diario and The Son’s Room have madeNanni Morettione of Italy’s most revered film-makers, but for the last two decades he has preferred to show his new works at Cannes rather than Venice. Showing in competition this year, Succederà Questa Notte (It Will Happen Tonight) will be Moretti’s first film in Venice since 1989.After Tunisian director Kaouther Ben Hania made a mark on last year’s festival with The Voice of Hind Rajab, there is a dramatic drop in female directors in this year’s competition. After six female directors competed for the Golden Lion in 2025, Danish-Egyptian director May El-Toukhy’s drama Woman Unknown is the only female-directed film among the 20 titles in the main competition.Venice 2026 official selection: the full listInkDanny BoyleCompanyCasey AffleckA Bit of LightAli AsgariRitorno a Buenos AiresMarco BechisUn Bon Petit SoldatStéphane BrizéIl Fuoco Che Ti title: Children’s literature festival criticised after requests that comments on diversity be deleted | Books | The Guardian
description: John McLay, one of the founders of the festival in Bath, contacted writers who had noted a lack of diversity in the programme to request they remove comments posted to social media
Author: Guardian staff reporter
Headline: John McLay, one of the founders of the festival in Bath, contacted writers who had noted a lack of diversity in the programme to request they remove comments posted to social media
Content: Photograph: ViewStock/Getty Images/View Stock RFView image in fullscreenPhotograph: ViewStock/Getty Images/View Stock RFBooksChildren’s literature festival criticised after requests that comments on diversity be deletedJohn McLay, one of the founders of the festival in Bath, contacted writers who had noted a lack of diversity in the programme to request they remove comments posted to social mediaLiv FaceyThu 23 Jul 2026 08.58 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleBath children’s literature festivalis facing criticism after its founder emailed authors asking them to delete social media posts about the festival’s lack of diversity in its 2026 programme.Authors said that their comments criticising the festival for its lack of representation under a post on its Instagram page were deleted, with the original post later removed and comments limited on other posts.Several authors said they received emails from one of the founders, John McLay, on Monday evening asking them to delete their comments because they hadn’t “investigated the full line-up before hitting the keyboard”.John McLay, a children’s literary scout, founded the festival 19 years ago alongside his wife Gill, an agent at Bath Literary Agency.Gill McLay defended the decision to contact authors directly, saying the festival wanted a post with incorrect information, which claimed there were no writers of colour in the programme, to be removed.The email cited seven authors of colour in the programme, one of whom is UK-Cypriot, as evidence that the complaints were inaccurate. The programme features almost 100 events in total, and bills itself as the UK’s largest children’s literature festival.The festivalalso contacted one author’s agent to ask them to retract their comments.“Maybe it wasn’t the right way to respond to it, and we have since apologised for that,” said Gill McLay. “It certainly wasn’t our intent to silence anybody at all. It was purely to just try and ensure that it was the right information.”Children’s author Sophie Anderson, who received the email, told the Guardian: “To have someone so powerful in the industry sending these quite threatening and bullying emails is just horrible.”Alexandra Sheppard, an author whose video about the situation has amassed more than 20,000 views, said that “the fear of being blacklisted for critiquing powerful organisations feels very real”. She is now organising an open letter to be sent to the festival.In anInstagramstatement, the festival said it is “very proud of this year’s line-up”, but had “listened to the comments made”. While it “chose authors and illustrators from the selection put forward by publishers”, it said it will “work harder to ensure we have greater representation”, adding that it has “already brought new people into the management of the festival to help us with this”.Authors argue the issue reflects a broader pattern, with similar concerns raised about other events including the London children’s literature festival.“In 2026, we need to do better,” said author Iqbal Hussain. “We are not living in Regency Bath, nor 1950s London.”The row comes amid wider concerns about representation in children’s books. RecentDepartment for Education figuresshow that 38.7% of school-age pupils in England are from minority ethnic backgrounds, but theeighth Reflecting Realities survey, run by the Centre for Literacy in Primary Education,found that only 24% of books had main characters from racial minorities, down from 30% in 2022.“I’ve seen countless conversations, panels, articles and social media posts about how publishing can better reflect the young readers it serves,” said Sheppard. “If an organisation like BCLF still lacks meaningful inclusion, the reason is simple: they don’t want it.”Campaigners warn that limited representation can further alienate young readers who struggle to see their lives reflected in books.Gill McLay told the Guardian that the festival is working to improve, citing steps such as introducing a steering committee. “All we’re asking of people today is that they give us the opportunity to do it better,” she added.Explore more on these topicsBooksFestivalsSocial mediaDigital mediaChildren's books: 8-12 yearsChildren's books: 7 and underTeen booksnewsShareReuse this content

title: Seinfeld’s Jason Alexander apologizes to Courtney Stodden for ‘inappropriate’ sketch made when she was a teenager | Television | The Guardian
description: The actor, who is one of the writers credited for the Funny or Die sketch, said he was ‘deeply sorry for any harm or distress’ caused by the 2012 skit
Author: Sian Cain
Headline: The actor, who is one of the writers credited for the Funny or Die sketch, said he was ‘deeply sorry for any harm or distress’ caused by the 2012 skit
Content: Jason Alexander pictured in Los Angeles in 2025. The Seinfeld actor has apologized to Courtney Stodden for his part in a 2012 Funny or Die sketch, in which he rubbed a phone on her breasts.Photograph: Gilbert Flores/Variety/Getty ImagesView image in fullscreenJason Alexander pictured in Los Angeles in 2025. The Seinfeld actor has apologized to Courtney Stodden for his part in a 2012 Funny or Die sketch, in which he rubbed a phone on her breasts.Photograph: Gilbert Flores/Variety/Getty ImagesTelevisionSeinfeld’s Jason Alexander apologizes to Courtney Stodden for ‘inappropriate’ sketch made when she was a teenagerThe actor, who is one of the writers credited for the Funny or Die sketch, said he was ‘deeply sorry for any harm or distress’ caused by the 2012 skitSian CainWed 22 Jul 2026 21.25 EDTLast modified on Wed 22 Jul 2026 21.27 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleSeinfeld star Jason Alexander has apologized to the model Courtney Stodden for a decade-old comedy sketch in which he rubbed a mobile phone on her breasts when she was 17, saying, “It was inappropriate and I truly regret it”.Stodden made international headlines in 2011 when she married her ex-husband, The Green Mile actor Doug Hutchison, when she was 16 and he was 51. Though she was a minor, the marriage was legal due to her parents giving their permission. Stodden and Hutchison divorced in 2020.In the years since, Stodden, now 31, has been outspoken against child marriage in the US, whichremains legal in 33 stateswith parental consent or a judicial waiver. Child marriage was legal in every US state until 2018.On Monday,Stodden posted a statement about the 2012 Funny or Die sketch, published when she was 17. In the sketch she plays herself and wears a red bikini while Alexander, playing a daytime television doctor, rubs a phone on her breasts while saying he has “full bars” in her chest but that he is “not getting anything” in her head. After making several suggestive remarks, Alexander’s character then invites Stodden to come back on to his talk show when she has turned 18.Alexander, who played George Costanza in Seinfeld, is one of the writers credited for the sketch and was 52 when it was filmed.View image in fullscreenCourtney Stodden pictured in December.Photograph: MEGA/GC Images“Looking back now, I don’t see comedy,” Stodden wrote. “I see a 17-year-old girl whose body became part of an adult joke. People still say, ‘You chose to be there.’ Legally, I couldn’t make those decisions for myself. That’s the point. I can’t change what happened to me but I can ask myself: how did so many adults look at a minor and decide this was acceptable?“That’s exactly why I speak out today. Children deserve laws that protect them. They shouldn’t have to become adults before realising the law should have protected them all along.”Alexander apologized on Wednesday,telling the Hollywood Reporter:“Looking back at the comedy sketch in which Ms Stodden and I participated in 2012, I completely agree that it was inappropriate and I truly regret it. But more importantly, I am deeply sorry for any harm or distress it has caused Ms Stodden. I offer her my sincerest apologies.”Stodden latertold the Hollywood Reporter, “I appreciate Jason Alexander’s public apology. People keep asking if he’s apologized privately, and he hasn’t. A private apology would be meaningful, but meaningful action would be even more powerful.“If he truly regrets what happened, I’d invite him to support [non-profit] Unchained At Last and the fight to end child marriage through AB 1267. That’s how we turn accountability into lasting change.”Since their divorce, Hutchison hassaidhe was “blacklisted” for marrying Stodden but said he had “no regrets”, calling their marriage “a beautiful and powerful journey. A bittersweet journey with Courtney”.Explore more on these topicsTelevisionTV comedyComedySeinfeldUS televisionnewsShareReuse this content

title: The end of Heartstopper is a bittersweet loss – it’s never felt scarier to be young and queer | Television | The Guardian
description: The gay Netflix romance maintained a political edge despite rollbacks to queer and trans rights
Author: Alim Kheraj
Headline: The gay Netflix romance maintained a political edge despite rollbacks to queer and trans rights
Content: Joe Locke and Kit Connor in Heartstopper Forever.Photograph: NetflixView image in fullscreenJoe Locke and Kit Connor in Heartstopper Forever.Photograph: NetflixTelevisionThe end of Heartstopper is a bittersweet loss – it’s never felt scarier to be young and queerAlim KherajThe gay Netflix romance maintained a political edge despite rollbacks to queer and trans rightsThu 23 Jul 2026 05.00 EDTLast modified on Thu 23 Jul 2026 05.01 EDTShareAfter six books, three seasons of television and afilm-length finale, Heartstopper has come to an end. Often dubbed the most wholesome show on TV, Netflix’s adaptation ofAlice Oseman’s young adult LGBTQ+ graphic novel series, which itself began life as a web comic, is a legitimate cultural phenomenon, turning its two leads –Kit Connorand Joe Locke – into household names, while racking up tens of millions of views. In print, meanwhile, the Heartstopper series has sold over 10m copies worldwide, including about 1.3m in the UK alone.Yet these figures only tell one side of the story of Heartstopper’s impact. At the queer and feminist bookshop where I work part-time in Sheffield, the young people who come in are immediately drawn to copies of Oseman’s books, and often take pictures of the now permanent window display depicting Nick and Charlie, the couple at the story’s heart. I see young queer people’s faces light up when they see we have the book in stock, even though they probably already have a copy at home.‘It would be weird not to show the sex’: Kit Connor and Joe Locke on Heartstopper’s queer teen curtain callRead moreI witnessed something similar in 2023 when I was invited to speak to an LGBTQ+ group at a secondary school in London. I was there to speak about queer history and my career as a journalist. When the students learned that I had once interviewed Oseman, though, all they wanted to discuss was Heartstopper. I could see how much the varied and positive queer representation in Oseman’s work meant to them, how Nick and Charlie’s delicate and sweet love story continued to bring them so much joy.For adults, Heartstopper has had a similar effect. When the show premiered in 2022, many older queer people voiced their love for the show, while acknowledging the grief they felt that such a thing hadn’t existed during their own adolescence. While Heartstopper was not made for a mature audience – it is, ostensibly, a children’s TV show – for queer people like myself, who grew up under the tyranny of Section 28, which prohibited the so-called “promotion” of homosexuality in school, it was hard not to feel a little wistful at the love and acceptance the queer characters in Heartstopper often experienced.That both the books and TV show were offering LGBTQ+ people of all ages, but particularly young people, a lifeline was not lost on those involved – as Connor said in a recentinterview: “It’s the biggest thing that will ever happen to any of us.”Heartstopper Forever review – sanitized sex scenes won’t let the Netflix lovebirds grow upRead moreIt’s perhaps why, despite its syrupy reputation, Heartstopper maintained a political edge. While it lacked the bite of, say,Russell T Davies’sIt’s a Sinor a film like120 BPM, Oseman and the show’s executive producer, Patrick Walters, didn’t skirt around serious topics. The third season, for example, highlighted the bait-and-switch tactics employed by the media after Elle, a young trans character played by Yasmin Finney, is invited to discuss her art on a local radio station, only for the host to try to drag Elle into a toxic debate about transgender identities. Likewise, in Heartstopper Forever, Netflix’s film conclusion, Elle brings up the ban on hormone blockers for young trans people, as well as the rollback of trans rights in the UK more broadly. “If I was a few years younger,” she says, “I wouldn’t even have been able to take hormone blockers legally.”View image in fullscreenThe cast of Heartstopper in season one.Photograph: NetflixHeartstopper touched on other serious topics, too, such as mental health issues, self-harm and disordered eating. The TV show was sometimes criticised for feeling like an afterschool special, its rose-tinted representation of larger issues more focused on educating and reassuring its young audience than delivering hard-hitting drama. But as Oseman said in a recentinterview: “It was always my intention with Heartstopper to find the middle line, the balancing point, between showing these issues in a realistic way while also keeping that tone of, like, ‘It’s going to be OK and things will work out.’”LGBTQ+ inclusion in film at a three-year low, Glaad survey suggestsRead moreThe cynicism surrounding Heartstopper’s sanitised depiction of queer adolescence is understandable, though, especially when so many younger LGBTQ+ people do still feel isolated and while also engaging in risky behaviours like using sex apps while underage. Dramas like the recent adaption of Paris Lees’s memoir,What It Feels Like for a Girl, and even older shows likeSkins, are perhaps more accurate representations of the messiness of growing up queer.Young LGBTQ+ people deserve realistic portrayals of their lives. But they also deserve joyful escapism, too. Young queer people are still more likely to experience mental health problems, with a 2024 study byThe Trevor Projecthighlighting how the majority of LGBTQ+ young people reported symptoms of depression, anxiety or self-harm. At times, Heartstopper might seem like make-believe, but the significance of seeing queer teens not only surviving but also thriving shouldn’t be sneered at.View image in fullscreenConnor and Locke with cast members Tobie Donovan and Sebastian Croft at London Pride, 2022.Photograph: Chris J Ratcliffe/Getty ImagesIn fact, this is perhaps more imperative than ever. According toGlaad’s annual studytracking LGBTQ+ representation in film, there has been a continued decline of queer characters on the silver screen over the last three years. This follows a similar trend in TV, where the number of LGBTQ+ characters on screen has also decreased since 2021. Pair this with a global rise in anti-LGBTQ+ rhetoric, rising hate crime, especially against trans people, and the rollback on trans rights in the UK, and it must feel like a particularly volatile and scary time to be a young queer person.When I consider this, I think back to that LGBTQ+ secondary school group I spoke to in 2023. With recentreportsof schools cancelling talks by queer authors and of growing censorship in libraries, both in the UK and the US, I hope their enthusiasm for queer stories can be maintained and protected, and that they continue to see themselves on screen and on bookshelves for years to come.Whether Heartstopper is truly forever, as the film’s title suggests, remains to be seen: Oseman has suggested she would like to write for adults next, while Locke and Connor are headed to Hollywood. But even as Nick and Charlie’s story reaches its conclusion, the need for joyful queer stories remains. Having seen the impact firsthand, for young queer people to lose out on the hope and reassurance they can offer would be a profound shame.Explore more on these topicsTelevisionNetflixLGBTQ+ rightsComics and graphic novelsAlice OsemanKit ConnorfeaturesShareReuse this content

title: Pompeii: Out of Time With Tom Hiddleston review – you will cringe so hard you turn to cheese | Television | The Guardian
description: The actor emotes his way through Vesuvius’s catastrophic eruption in a docudrama that is embarrassing and exhausting – but undeniably entertaining. Prepare to be Hiddlestoned!
Author: Lucy Mangan
Headline: The actor emotes his way through Vesuvius’s catastrophic eruption in a docudrama that is embarrassing and exhausting – but undeniably entertaining. Prepare to be Hiddlestoned!
Content: Crikey … Pompeii: Out of Time With Tom Hiddleston.Photograph: Disney+View image in fullscreenCrikey … Pompeii: Out of Time With Tom Hiddleston.Photograph: Disney+TV reviewTelevisionReviewPompeii: Out of Time With Tom Hiddleston review – you will cringe so hard you turn to cheeseThe actor emotes his way through Vesuvius’s catastrophic eruption in a docudrama that is embarrassing and exhausting – but undeniably entertaining. Prepare to be Hiddlestoned!Lucy ManganThu 23 Jul 2026 00.00 EDTLast modified on Thu 23 Jul 2026 10.56 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleThere’s such a fine line between “cheese” and “cringe”, is there not? Come, walk it with me andTom Hiddlestonin Pompeii: Out of Time With Tom Hiddleston. Let’s see how many of us survive the eruptions of embarrassment that attend our journey!This is a National Geographic documentary-drama, which means that even the factual bits are emotionally scripted and there’s CGI money and speaking parts for the re-enactments. And there is Tom (he has looked very deeply into my eyes throughout all three episodes, so I feel we are on first-name terms now), wringing every drop of feeling from every moment with the untrammelled appetite of a born actor.We begin! Dramatically! With Tom! Telling us – in weirdly ungrammatical fashion – that Pompeii was destroyed in “a single day that are the most harrowing anyone on this Earth has ever experienced”. Then he drops his voice. And explains that although a day is not long, it does mean there is hope for individual survival. He looks deep into my eyes, into your eyes, into all eyes, into eyes past and not yet born, and intones: “A day. A chance.”You have been Hiddlestoned. While you recover, opening credits roll.As the programme proper begins, informal Tom greets us. He vouchsafes us the intimate personal knowledge that he actually visited Pompeii with two friends in 1998, when he was 17. There are photos to prove it! Tom is young and happy and just like us except taller and handsomer and a born actor destined for a career that blends lucrative and prestigious work in perfect proportions! Pompeii, he confides in us and our eyes, inspired him to learn Latin, study classics at an unnamed university and pursue acting “to bring people back”. Crikey. It’s lucky he didn’t just bugger off to Ibiza for a month. His life and Britain’s cultural export ledger could have been so different.Tom meets Prof Steven L Tuck, leader of the Pompeii Survivors’ Project and labelled in the onscreen caption as “Helpful Classicist”, which, once further experts have been cheesily introduced as “Helpful Archaeologist”, “Helpful Geoscientist” and so on, I very much enjoy. He is there to help Tom find three Pompeiians whose stories he can trace through that fateful day in AD79 when Mount Vesuvius began to rain tonnes of rock and magma down on the defenceless towns below.Prof Tuck shows Tom some ancient records and inscriptions and Tom, who has the Latin, translates them. The first candidate they find is a boy called Lucius Avianius (which, if my own Latin does not desert me, translates as “Lucy Birdyus”). Tom looks – nay, surely is! – deep in thought. “If I’m going to tell Avianius’s story,” he says at last, raising his soulful eyes to our bog-standard eyes, “I need to combine the historical evidence with what I do as an actor. I need” – voice drop, eyes soulfuller – “to walk alongside him, through the streets in his shoes.” The Romans had the same word for “story” and “history”, he says. “Evidence and imaginationintermingled,” he continues, knotting his fingers together. Consider meawed.View image in fullscreenConsider me awed! … Pompeii: Out of Time With Tom Hiddleston.Photograph: DisneyThe second potential survivor is a bathhouse owner called Julia – mistakenly translated by Tom at first as “Julius”. Oh, Tom! But then another expert explains that women held property and power in the old days and that Julia even did it with the stigma of illegitimacy attached to her. This causes Tom to sigh in compassion and admiration and name her “Impossible Julia”. At this point, I find myself cringing so hard I nearly turn to cheese, which is an unexpected turn of events but not an entirely unpleasant sensation.On we go, with Tom interviewing more Helpful Experts, including a disaster psychologist, who explains why people don’t necessarily run for the hills literally or metaphorically at the first sign of trouble but might try to find, say, family first. “Of course,” breathes Tom. “That’s who you’d go to. It’s who I’d go to.” I regretfully make a mark in the “cringe” column.Tom enthuses educatedly over Pliny, stands aside graciously for more ash- and pumice-occluded re-enactments, and hopes wildly for success in the rescue mission launched by the Roman fleet from the Bay of Naples. Occasionally, though, he is overcome by the thought of the suffering unfolding over the day as the full force of the volcano is unleashed. Born actors are easily stricken. But they are very brave. They soldier on until the contract is fulfilled and the money inarguably due.I suppose I would have to look back at my first documentary-drama presented by a born actor and profess myself entertained but exhausted by the constant prospect of the whole teetering thing collapsing into utter humiliation for all involved. But if I were younger, less cynical, a nicer person in any way, I would have loved it and it would have got me a bit more interested in the ancient world. Contract fulfilled.Pompeii: Out of Time With Tom Hiddleston is on Disney+ now.Explore more on these topicsTelevisionTV reviewTom HiddlestonreviewsShareReuse this content

title: TV tonight: documentary reveals the shocking truth behind model family blogger Ruby Franke | Television | The Guardian
description: First of a three-part series exposing the hidden horror behind a picture-perfect image created for the cameras. Plus Adeel Akhtar traces his roots in Who Do You Think You Are? Here’s what to watch this evening
Author: Guardian staff reporter
Headline: First of a three-part series exposing the hidden horror behind a picture-perfect image created for the cameras. Plus Adeel Akhtar traces his roots in Who Do You Think You Are? Here’s what to watch this evening
Content: Ruby Franke in The Devil in the Family.Photograph: BBC/Disney/Passion PicturesView image in fullscreenRuby Franke in The Devil in the Family.Photograph: BBC/Disney/Passion PicturesTV tonightTelevisionTV tonight: documentary reveals the shocking truth behind model family blogger Ruby FrankeFirst of a three-part series exposing the hidden horror behind a picture-perfect image created for the cameras. Plus Adeel Akhtar traces his roots in Who Do You Think You Are? Here’s what to watch this eveningThu 23 Jul 2026 01.20 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleThe Devil in the Family: The Fall of Ruby Franke9pm, BBC TwoAt its peak, Ruby Franke’s parenting YouTube channel was earning £100,000 a month. But, as this documentary series shows, her ostensibly model family was a house of cards. As her ex-husband puts it: “The devil knocked and we opened the door.” Their downfall culminated in Franke’s arrest for child abuse in 2023 after her emaciated son escaped and summoned help. Thanks to the plentiful footage recorded by Franke herself, it’s easy to sense the roots of this horror.Phil HarrisonThe Chase Around the World9pm, ITV1The shrewd expansion of the Chase universe into a travel contest with a general knowledge quiz attached continues. This time, the teams are in Barcelona where they must answer questions that will help them navigate the city and avoid the flight home. The last-placed team face a terrifying head-to-head showdown with the Chasers.PHThe Traitors Canada9pm, BBC ThreeThe Canadian version sees actor Karine Vanasse taking the Claudia Winkleman role. Expect poker faces, big buildings, gratuitous shower scenes and lots of Lana Del Rey-esque cover versions of songs. The Traitorfication of telly (hi Nobody’s Fool) continues apace.Priya ElanWho Do You Think You Are?9pm, BBC OneView image in fullscreenLike the plot of a sweeping doorstopper novel … Adeel Akhtar in Who Do You Think You Are?Photograph: BBC/Wall to Wall Media Ltd/Stephen PerryActor Adeel Akhtar’s maternal family originally hailed from India, but his mother came to the UK from Kenya. The explanation of how and why is like the plot of a sweeping doorstopper novel, with the road leading to Mombasa and Nairobi – the latter city being, at the dawn of the 20th century, blighted by the threat of colonial segregation.Jack SealeThe Five-Star Weekend9pm, Sky AtlanticSurely, the impending revelation that Gigi (Gemma Chan) had an affair with the recently dead husband of Hollis (Jennifer Garner) is going to blow up this perfect girls’ weekend? But while we wait for that to happen, it’s a whole load of fun watching the rest of this wealthy group of women slowly reveal their own secrets. Moreish summer fun that slips down far too easily.HRCIA9pm, Sky WitnessThis spin-off of long-running US police procedural FBI gets a belated UK air date. Its dynamic is very much an odd-couple cop duo, with by-the-book FBI agent Bill Goodman partnered up with loose-cannon CIA case officer Colin Glass. Will they find the stolen top-secret weapon before they drive each other up the wall?Alexi DugginsExplore more on these topicsTelevisionTV tonightfeaturesShareReuse this content

title: The Midnight Train by Matt Haig audiobook review – a cosy, feelgood journey  | Books | The Guardian
description: Actor James Norton brings a sense of gentle longing to this tale of an octogenarian looking back on the life he’s departed
Author: Fiona Sturges
Headline: Actor James Norton brings a sense of gentle longing to this tale of an octogenarian looking back on the life he’s departed
Content: Shades of Dickens … Matt Haig.Photograph: Peter FludeView image in fullscreenShades of Dickens … Matt Haig.Photograph: Peter FludeAudiobook of the weekBooksReviewThe Midnight Train by Matt Haig audiobook review – a cosy, feelgood journeyActor James Norton brings a sense of gentle longing to this tale of an octogenarian looking back on the life he’s departedFiona SturgesThu 23 Jul 2026 11.00 EDTLast modified on Thu 23 Jul 2026 11.01 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleThe latest novel from the Reasons to Stay Alive author Matt Haig begins with an octogenarian man having a heart attack. Former business magnate Wilbur Budd is already feeling a bit peculiar when he receives a phone call from his ex-wife, Maggie, who he hasn’t heard from in years. “I wanted to hear your voice,” she tells him wistfully. Minutes after getting off the phone, Wilbur collapses on his front lawn and dies.What sounds like the end of a story is, in fact, the beginning as Wilbur finds himself standing on a train platform with his mind “reverberating as if from some giant cosmic cymbal crash”. A train arrives and a woman disembarks and introduces herself as Agnes. She is, she says, the ghost of a bookseller who once ran a shop Wilbur visited as a child. She will be guiding him in a voyage through his past, pausing at formative moments including his brother’s premature death and Wilbur and Maggie’s honeymoon in Venice. “This is death,” Agnes tells him. “Where your life flashes before your eyes. This is how it happens. Fast and slow. Like life … ”With shades of Dickens’s A Christmas Carol, The Midnight Train is a cosy feelgood tale, despite the ostensibly bleak themes of death and the afterlife. The narrator is the Happy Valley and McMafia actor James Norton who brings a rare softness and gentle longing to his performance. As Wilbur reflects on times gone by, he comes to realise the things in life he could have done better.Available via Canongate, 8hr 36minFurther listeningYou With the Sad EyesChristina Applegate, Headline, 8hr 27minA candid memoir in which the actor best known as Kelly Bundy from Married … With Children reflects on her childhood raised by a heroin-addicted mother, becoming the family breadwinner as a child actor, and her recent diagnosis with multiple sclerosis. Read by the author.Birnam WoodEleanor Catton, Audible Studios, 12hr 47minSaskia Maarleveld reads this smartly plotted eco-thriller from the Booker prize-winning author of The Luminaries. It tells of a guerrilla gardening collective in New Zealand, named after the moving forest in Macbeth, who set their sights on a plot of land owned by an American billionaire.Explore more on these topicsBooksAudiobook of the weekreviewsShareReuse this content

title: Beethoven: The Last Three Sonatas/Mitsuko Uchida album review – the music emerges as if new  | Classical music | The Guardian
description: There are no major surprises in this live recording, just the simple but sublime sense that the music is being passed from composer, through pianist, on to us
Author: Erica Jeal
Headline: There are no major surprises in this live recording, just the simple but sublime sense that the music is being passed from composer, through pianist, on to us
Content: Profoundly affirmative … Mitsuko Uchida performing at Suntory Hall, Tokyo.Photograph: © Naoya Ikegami/Suntory HallView image in fullscreenProfoundly affirmative … Mitsuko Uchida performing at Suntory Hall, Tokyo.Photograph: © Naoya Ikegami/Suntory HallClassical musicReviewBeethoven: The Last Three Sonatas/Mitsuko Uchida album review – the music emerges as if newDeccaThere are no major surprises in this live recording, just the simple but sublime sense that the music is being handed from composer, through pianist, to usErica JealThu 23 Jul 2026 10.00 EDTLast modified on Thu 23 Jul 2026 14.01 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleTwenty years ago, Mitsuko Uchida released a studio recording of Beethoven’s last three piano sonatas that remains a benchmark. Now comes this performance recorded live in Tokyo last October.Those who have loved her previous recording won’t find big surprises – her interpretations remain similar, albeit at times a little broader or more elastic, and with less obvious urgency in, say, the brief second movements of Op 109 and 110. Yet, even from a pianist who has been playing these pieces for so many decades, the music emerges as if new: nothing sounds predetermined. Nor did Suntory Hall, which seats about 2,000, get exactly the same performance as she had given to a much smaller audience at Wigmore Hall in London,two weeks earlier. Most audience noise has been edited out, but the atmosphere of collective concentration remains.View image in fullscreenBeethoven: The Last Three Sonatas – Mitsuko Uchida, Live at Suntory Hall.Photograph: DeccaUchida’s Beethoven remains unassumingly wonderful. The contrasts between loud and soft can be dramatic but are never shocking; the quiet passages offer a kind of communion. The long finales of these sonatas emerge in huge, world-encompassing spans, naturally and organically, but to powerful effect: when in the final movement of Op 110, the solemn, Bach-like fugal theme – with all its almost ecclesiastical certainty – dissolves into Beethoven at his most mysterious, only to return joyfully renewed, the result is profoundly affirmative. There’s no signposting of interpretation, no sense that this music needs a mediator. Instead, it’s as if she is turning over leaf after leaf of the music, and simply handing us what she finds there: from Beethoven, through Uchida, to us.Allow content provided by a third party?This article includes content hosted onembed.music.apple.com. We ask for your permission before anything is loaded, as the provider may be using cookies and other technologies.
