title: ‘We didn’t care what the client wanted!’: Sheffield’s Designers Republic on their art for Aphex Twin, Pulp … and Pringles | Art and design | The Guardian
description: Their eye-popping designs burst out of Sheffield in 1986, making them the toast of the music scene. Founder Ian Anderson reveals why some fans think his art is worth remortgaging the house for
Author: Daniel Dylan Wray
Headline: Their eye-popping designs burst out of Sheffield in 1986, making them the toast of the music scene. Founder Ian Anderson reveals why some fans think his art is worth remortgaging the house for
Content: It’s like I’ve got a private museum that nobody visits – not even me … a collection of flyers from the Designers Republic archive.Photograph: The Designers RepublicView image in fullscreenIt’s like I’ve got a private museum that nobody visits – not even me … a collection of flyers from the Designers Republic archive.Photograph: The Designers RepublicArt and design‘We didn’t care what the client wanted!’: Sheffield’s Designers Republic on their art for Aphex Twin, Pulp … and PringlesTheir eye-popping designs burst out of Sheffield in 1986, making them the toast of the music scene. Founder Ian Anderson reveals why some fans think his art is worth remortgaging the house forDaniel Dylan WrayThu 23 Jul 2026 00.00 EDTLast modified on Thu 23 Jul 2026 12.40 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleForty years ago inSheffield, Ian Anderson founded one of the most influential UK design companies by mistake. “The name the Designers Republic was a joke, because we weren’t designers,” he recalls. “Early on, everything we did was in black and white because we didn’t know how to make it colour.”With co-founder Nick Phillips, The Designers Republic (TDR) quickly became synonymous with music, designing record covers, packaging, logos and campaigns for everyone fromAphex Twinto Autechre, Pulp to Nine Inch Nails. They worked on groundbreaking video games, such as Wipeout and Grand Theft Auto, collaborated with brand behemoths like Coca-Cola and Adidas, and their work is held in the MoMA and the V&A. There exist such obsessive collectors of their work that Anderson cites one person who remortaged his house twice to keep up with their output.View image in fullscreen‘We weren’t designers’ … Nick Phillips, left, and Ian Anderson in 1986.Photograph: Jeremy LawrenceTo commemorate the milestone, Anderson – now 65, who runs the company solo – has been selling a load of original TDR pieces (stickers, flyers etc), as well as launching a new merch line. After keeping five original copies of everything, he has amassed a staggering archive. “Storage is an issue,” he says, showing me around his Sheffield studio, which is stuffed with records, posters, flyers, books and artworks. “It’s like I’ve got a private museum that nobody visits – not even me. Yet there are people who would value some of those things and exhibit it on their wall. That seems to be the right thing to do, in honour of the work.”Despite TDR’s naive, inexperienced origins – Anderson had been in bands and was managing one called Person to Person, while Phillips was a sculpture student who also played the organ in local band World of Twist – their self-taught experimentations in designs for local bands soon saw them in demand. Their aspiration, says Anderson, was simply to be like a northern version of Gilbert and George. “We went out and got matching Nehru suits made by the same tailor that made ABC’s gold lamé suits,” he recalls.Their start in 1986 coincided with the birth of a new record label in Sheffield called Fon. They worked on covers for them, the first being Chakk’s 10 Days in an Elevator, which was inspired by photographer William Klein’s cover for his book New York 1954-55 and used woodblock typography to “mimic visually the onomatopoeia” of the word Chakk. It became the first in a series of designs for the label’s covers that utilised distinctive black and white strips over the spines, so that all Fon records could be easily identified on a shelf.Inspired by Russian constructivism, modern art, Japanese letterform and pop art, TDR made eye-popping work for groups such as Pop Will Eat Itself (for whom they designed a band logo that riffed on Pepsi’s) and Age of Chance, as maximal as Peter Saville’s designs for Factory records over in Manchester were minimal.View image in fullscreenMaximalist … the cover art for Age of Chance’s single Kiss.Photograph: The Designer’s RepublicAnderson viewed TDR’s brightly coloured, text-crammed sleeves as an antidote to what he calls the “heaven knows we’re miserabilists now” brigade – meaning the Smiths and the bands they influenced. “Just fucking cheer up,” he says. “The majority of people who liked the Smiths had the luxury of being miserable. It was white middle-class students who could afford to moan. Council estate kids didn’t want someone saying they were miserable. They were the people going to clubs and doing jobs to get out of that life. I liked that ‘let’s have it’ attitude. Our world was very different. We were into dance and electronic music.”TDR coined the term SoYo – an abbreviation of South Yorkshire, while the tagline for the company is: North of Nowhere. “We coined SoYo not as a great exercise in regional branding, just as a fuck off to a bunch of knobs in Soho,” says Anderson, who had moved from Bracknell New Town in Berkshire to study philosophy at Sheffield University. “We had a sense of us versus the world.” So when a new Sheffield record label started in 1989 – Warp – TDR created the label’s logo and a striking purple visual identity, becoming the primary driving aesthetic driving force behind its first 10 years. A hip, successful, and hugely influential label, with killer sleeves to boot – from LFO’s minimal yet striking covers to the psychedelic colour explosion of Nightmares on Wax’s A Word of Science, which doubled up as a visual tracklisting of the album–it put them on the global map as a design powerhouse.View image in fullscreenSells for thousands … Work. Buy. Consume. Die.Photograph: The Designer’s Republic (tDR)Their work was so immediately impactful that they never even needed to pitch to potential clients, and Anderson developed a correspondingly spiky attitude. “We really didn’t care what the client wanted,” Anderson says. “We weren’t those people that would go and play golf with them.” Being in Sheffield meant that they were largely free to get on with the job. “No one was gonna jump on a train to come up and check on us,” Anderson says. “For most of them, the thought of going this far north was dizzying.”In the mid-1990s, TDR experimented with postmodernism and irony, playing with themes of capitalism and consumerism, even establishing a faux corporation called Pho-Ku. They produced posters with slogans such as “Work. Buy. Consume. Die.” A recent reprint of this instantly sold out, and originals sell for thousands.Anderson says that his approach was influenced by his philosophy studies. “A lot of our design is about asking questions,” he says. “If I’m designing a record cover, I don’t want it to look like a record cover. If I’m designing a corporate logo then I’d rather it look like a record cover. For me, things work, and they have more gravity, if you challenge people’s preconceptions.” When Aphex Twin had his music used in an advert for the mobile phone company Orange, an accompanying remix CD was released in 1997 with a format-twisting design from TDR, in which the visuals-free sleeve simply read: “An image of children chasing after an ice-cream van from an Orange™ TV commercial advertising Text Messaging.”While TDR was very much rooted in Sheffield – they designed flyers and posters for numerous local nights and clubs such as Gatecrasher, NY Sushi, and Warp’s Blech – big names were now knocking on their door. By the 2000s, they had worked with countless huge brands and corporations. So, when this staunchly anti-conformist company started designing for Coca-Cola and Pringles, did they get criticised? “Yeah, it was all like, ‘You sold out’,” says Anderson. “People were like, ‘We thought you were all, up the revolution’. But from our point of view, it gave us all a lot of new opportunities and everybody has to draw the line somewhere. We refused a lot of stuff.”View image in fullscreenNew horizons … Coca-Cola bottles designed by The Designer’s Republic.Photograph: The Designer’s Republic (tDR)By the late 2000s, TDR was a growing company with a staff of about 20, including several designers, Anderson having taken on a more hands-off role. They found themselves working during a time of shrinking marketing budgets post financial crisis, and in 2009 went into voluntary liquidation. “When I took a back seat to allow TDR to grow beyond me, it died,” Anderson said at the time. “Its creative spark was crushed under the weight of business self-interest.”It returned as a slimmed down operation, just Anderson, and continued to make innovative work. Autechre’s 2010 album Oversteps remains some of Anderson’s favourite work, while Aphex Twin’s hugely anticipated return album, Syro (2014), saw TDR employed to create a conceptually ambitious packaging design in which the album cover doubled up as a huge receipt for every penny spent on the album, alongside a detailed infographic listing the 138 pieces of audio equipment used to record it.View image in fullscreenDown to the penny … the cover art for Aphex Twin’s 2014 album.Photograph: The Designer’s RepublicIn many ways, Anderson has come full circle, and he’s back to working on record covers for interesting artists – recent work includes the electronic outfit Fluke, experimental hip-hop group Clipping, and longtime collaborators and Warp mainstays Seefeel. Meanwhile, the reputation of TDR has endured, with popular social media accounts digging into and celebrating the work for younger audiences. “I went into Covid with some uncertainty as a designer, well known or not,” says Anderson. “Then I came out of it and suddenly I was perceived as the Goat, the OG, like an elder statesman of design.”Despite the blast of nostalgia around the 40th anniversary, Anderson says he’s more concerned about what’s next. “When I should be celebrating the past along with the rest of our new followers, at the same time I don’t know if I’ve ever been less bothered about it,” he says. “It’s like having a fantastic house full of stuff, but actually preferring to sit in your shed thinking about new things.”Explore more on these topicsArt and designDesignGraphic designSheffieldAphex TwinfeaturesShareReuse this content

title: Ken Turner, the 100-year-old artist who flattened a Tesla with a tank: ‘I’m not a revolutionary, I’m a simple rebel’ | Art | The Guardian
description: The painter, sculptor and performance artist has devoted his life to making art available to the masses. Now with a new exhibition, the centenarian discusses inequality, inspiration, and why he won’t be stopping any time soon
Author: Eddy Frankel
Headline: The painter, sculptor and performance artist has devoted his life to making art available to the masses. Now with a new exhibition, the centenarian discusses inequality, inspiration, and why he won’t be stopping any time soon
Content: Drawn from life … Ken Turner.Photograph: Patrick ShanahanView image in fullscreenDrawn from life … Ken Turner.Photograph: Patrick ShanahanArtKen Turner, the 100-year-old artist who flattened a Tesla with a tank: ‘I’m not a revolutionary, I’m a simple rebel’The painter, sculptor and performance artist has devoted his life to making art available to the masses. Now with a new exhibition, the centenarian discusses inequality, inspiration, and why he won’t be stopping any time soonEddy FrankelThu 23 Jul 2026 03.00 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on Google‘Life is short, art long,” says Ken Turner, reading from a book of aphorisms by ancient Greek physician Hippocrates. And he’s half right, really, because at 100 years old – and very possibly the world’s oldest performance artist – art might have been long, but life hasn’t been all that short for him, either. And he has filled those years to the brim with radicalism, experimentalism and a singular dedication to pushing ideas as far as he can make them go.In the 1960s, Turner moved from painting towards a socially conscious form of public art, creating giant inflatable sculptures that visitors could inhabit and interact with decades before immersive art became a thing. He was taught by Fernand Léger, he was mates with John Berger, he made frames for Frank Auerbach and Leon Kossoff. Last year, he drove a tank over a Tesla in a performance meant as a damning indictment of corporate greed and rising fascism. His has been a life of rebellion and revolution, of obstinately following his own artistic path no matter what obstacles he encounters, no matter how much easier it would have been to walk down a more well-trodden one.View image in fullscreenCrushing it … A still from My Ride Over a Tesla.Photograph: Huw WahlHe’s written two books: Crashing Culture and the brilliantly named A Life Being Ken Turner. Those two titles sum him up perfectly; he smashes art conventions to bits, all while being uniquely, resolutely and utterly himself. And he’s still at it: still performing, still painting, still making. Ahead of an exhibition of new work at Wharfside Arts Hub in Penzance, he sits in his kitchen talking about his life and work, dressed in a blue work shirt and cap. A Michael Eavis-esque moustache-less beard makes him look every inch the Cornwall creative that he is. We’re joined by his son, the film-maker Huw Wahl, whose tender, grainy filmic portrait of his father plays on a loop in the show. “I think his painting is getting better and better, in a way, because he’s becoming more and more playful,” says Wahl. “But Ken’s always been childish, in the best sense.”“I was always interested in imaginative ideas, and the idea of play as an education in itself,” Turner says. “The more you go through education into university, the more you forget to play, and playing is incredibly important.”double quotation markThere’s no difference between performance and painting for me. I perform when I’m painting and I’m painting when I performTurner has a lot to say, a lot of stories to tell, as you’d expect after 80 years of art-making. Dates and names may escape him now, but the anecdotes tumble out in an endless stream. He struggles to talk about the emotions and reasoning behind most of the work, but perhaps even at 100 years old he’s still processing it all. His art isn’t a fait accompli. He doesn’t seem comfortable looking backwards when there’s so much still to look forward to.Turner was drafted into the mines at the outbreak of the second world war, and eventually into the Royal Engineers. He emerged desperate to make art, but deeply suspicious of the art establishment and turned down a place at London’s Slade School of Fine Art because he didn’t like the colour of the walls. “If they can’t get the colour of the walls right, how can they teach people?” he says. Later, he refused to do his final exam at Regent Street Polytechnic because they expected him to paint in an impressionist style, and turned down invites to show in the art galleries of the West End. “I didn’t like the dealers, I thought they smelled foul, they looked crooked, and they were monsters in their own right.” At every stage, Turner has pushed back against expectations: “I’m not a revolutionary, I’m a simple rebel, really.”View image in fullscreenBlank canvas … Turner in a still from My Ride Over a Tesla.Photograph: © Huw WahlInstead, Turner worked as a framer before taking a position as a teacher at Barnet College and eventually Central Saint Martins. In the late 1960s, an encounter with a public project by radical theatre director Joan Littlewood inspired him to form Action Space, an art collective hellbent on taking art out of the galleries and putting it where the public could actually reach it. They described themselves as “an association of artists working in the community, through education and play, developing art forms relevant to a wider public”. They created vast inflatable sculptures you could walk through and perform in, they used light, sound, movement and drama, they put on events for children. This was socially conscious, community-engaged art well ahead of its time.“Well, that’s the social conscience I have,” Turner says when asked about making art for a wider public. “I almost cry about the inequality that’s happening.” Action Space was about finding ways of connecting with people outside traditional, conservative art contexts. But rejecting the established gallery model to work in disadvantaged urban areas was also a continuation of his rebellious streak. “It was something intuitive, it was in my bones to reject institutional thinking.” Action Space still exists today, in another guise, as a charity working with learning-disabled artists, including Turner prize-winner Nnena Kalu.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |WeeklySign up toThe GuideGet our weekly pop culture email, free in your inbox every FridayPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotiondouble quotation markI still need to sell paintings, but I don’t want to go into the West End to sell them. I just intensely dislike criticsIt was also around this time that Turner discovered his love of performance art, a passion that peaked last year when he drove that 15-tonne tank over a Tesla in a project coordinated by guerrilla art provocateurs Led By Donkeys. “There’s no difference between performance and painting for me,” he states. “I perform when I’m painting, and I’m painting when I perform.” And age is no barrier to that – he’ll be performing at the opening of the new exhibition alongside his daughter.Turner moved to Cornwall in the 1990s and rediscovered his love of playing around with brushes and oils, rekindling his passion for painting. It’s still his main form of creative output, though that comes with its own pressures: “I’m still at that point where I need to sell paintings, but I don’t want to go into the West End to sell them. That’s the art industry. I just intensely dislike the critics, and you’re not an art critic, are you?”He looks disappointed at my response. That old suspicion of the art establishment rearing its head again. “Well, have a look at the paintings, and then decide who should buy them,” he says. So I do. They’re big, grandiose, semi-abstract things, often chaotic, sometimes violent, deeply emotional and overwrought. They deal with the destruction of Gaza, the impact of the climate emergency; they don’t make for easy viewing. “I think museums should buy them,” Turner says. “They instruct people, they’re about the sadness of the planet, the development of cruelty and inequality that’s happening.”He cares deeply about the world, and he genuinely thinks art can help change things for the better. Is it that belief in art’s power that pushes him forward? “Even though I’m 100, I’ll get to about 110 and I’ll still go on painting, because it’s signposted: ‘This is the way, this is where you should go.’ It’s a voice inside.”It’s also a necessity. “If I didn’t [make art], I’d die. It gives me life. If I don’t paint, I get sick, I really get sick in my mind, and that’s no good.” Even at 100, you get the feeling that as long as he’s got art in his life, Ken Turner could go on for ever.Ken Turner at 100: A Century of Crashing Cultureis at Wharfside Arts Hub, Penzance, to 4August.Explore more on these topicsArtExhibitionsfeaturesShareReuse this content

title: Why Scotland’s cinematic Moray coast made it a perfect location to shoot The Odyssey | Scotland holidays | The Guardian
description: ‘Legends cling like barnacles to these shores,’ says our local writer, who reveals how the lore of these craggy cliffs and beaches resonates with the Homeric classic
Author: Guardian staff reporter
Headline: ‘Legends cling like barnacles to these shores,’ says our local writer, who reveals how the lore of these craggy cliffs and beaches resonates with the Homeric classic
Content: Matt Damon as Odysseus, on location in Scotland.Photograph: Melinda Sue Gordon/Universal PicturesView image in fullscreenMatt Damon as Odysseus, on location in Scotland.Photograph: Melinda Sue Gordon/Universal PicturesScotland holidaysWhy Scotland’s cinematic Moray coast made it a perfect location to shoot The Odyssey‘Legends cling like barnacles to these shores,’ says our local writer, who reveals how the lore of these craggy cliffs and beaches resonates with the Homeric classicEstella ShardlowThu 23 Jul 2026 02.00 EDTLast modified on Thu 23 Jul 2026 08.11 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleThe incoming tide has made a labyrinth of the beach. As silvery rivulets spread across Culbin Sands to meet the mudflats, my walk becomes increasingly circuitous: weaving inland towards the pine forest, backtracking closer to the waves, skirting tidal pools and scaling dunes constantly resculpted by the North Sea breeze.In this shapeshifting stretch of north-east Scotland’s Moray coast, there’s not another soul in sight, nothing to anchor me to the modern day. It’s easy, then, to imagine a longship bringing weary warriors ashore, or a band of man-eating giants marauding from the woodland. Which is exactly what happens here – on screen – in Christopher Nolan’s adaptation ofThe Odyssey.At first glance, Scotland is not an obvious backdrop for a nearly 3,000-year-old Greek epic. While the poem describes the Ithacan king Odysseus and his men being cast adrift on mysterious, far-flung shores, its imagined geography is the Mediterranean (unsurprisingly, Sicily, Morocco and the Peloponnese are the movie’s other locations). Few people who live in Moray, including me, anticipated Hollywood actors Matt Damon, Zendaya and Tom Holland descending on our local beaches and sleepy fishing villages last summer. The Moray coast isn’t even well known domestically, overshadowed by the likes of the Isle of Skye and the North Coast 500 driving route in the Highlands.double quotation markIn this shapeshifting stretch of north-east Scotland’s coast, there’s not another soul in sight, nothing to anchor me to the modern dayThe more I think about it, though, the more Nolan’s decision makes sense. Unfurling due east of Inverness in a series of vast sand dunes, shingle bars, pine-edged marshes and sandstone skerries, this coastline is both cinematic and unusually varied. The absence of tourist crowds and coastal development must be a boon to film-makers.Yet the resonances between the world ofThe Odysseyand these Scottish seafaring communities run deeper than scenery. Legends cling like barnacles to Moray’s shores. Superstitions intermingle with real histories of sieges and tragedies. Take the “Atlantis of the North” at Culbin Sands, the RSPB nature reserve and forest where Matt Damon’s Odysseus clashes with the giant-like Laestrygonians. A thriving agricultural estate and village stood here until 1694, when a freak sandstorm buried the settlement in a single night. Locals blamed either a witch’s curse or their reckless laird’s bargain with the devil.View image in fullscreenCulbin Sands, where Matt Damon’s Odysseus clashes with the giant-like Laestrygonians.Photograph: Jan Holm/Alamy“The lore you hear along this coast is worthy of a Homeric epic,” says Moray-based author and classicist VJ Randle, whose novel The Athenian Murders is inspired by Greek mythology. She leads me along a section of the 50-mileMoray Coast Trail. Through an early morning Stygian sea-mist, we set off from the fishing village of Hopeman, the sandy footpath weaving between tangles of gorse and heather, before we clamber over the rock pools of Clashach Cove to a shadowy opening between the golden sandstone cliffs.“This cave always reminds me of Calypso’s home,” Randle says. “Although locally, it’s said that a scarier sort of sea-spirit lives here.” She’s talking about theGrin Iron Wife– a witch with pale-green skin, rusted-red teeth and hair of seaweed who drags children and the elderly down to her underwater lair.double quotation markLocal storyteller Michelle is helping to revive fading traditions. Her walking tours introduce figures with striking parallels to classical mythologySuch tales were once shared byseanchaidhean– oral historians who preserved heroic battles, local legends and clan genealogy. They were to Scottish Gaelic culture what the wandering bards were to ancient Greece.It’s a fading tradition that local storytellers like Michelle van den Hout ofOtherworldly Talesare helping to revive. Her walking tours introduce figures with striking parallels to classical mythology: theScottishcreator goddess and giantessCailleach, a titan if ever there was one, islands and mountains formed from the rocks that fell from her creel basket; or the siren-likeCeasg, luring sailors who listened to her song to a watery grave.View image in fullscreenBurghead harbour is where The Odyssey’s film crew docked a longship alongside the usual fishing vessels.Photograph: Helen Hotson/AlamySuch tales were never simply about frightening people, van den Hout explains: “For isolated coastal communities, storytelling was a way to pass down lessons about survival, respecting nature and how to treat strangers. Rather than warning a child with, ‘Careful, that place has a riptide,’ it was more effective to say that a witch lives there.”Threats coming from the North Sea could sometimes be made of flesh and blood, of course. In the town of Burghead, I stand on the grassy promontory overlooking granite cottages huddled around a working harbour. This is where The Odyssey’sfilm crew docked a 35-metre (115ft) longship last year alongside the usual fishing vessels. My attention turns to the raised earthworks beneath my feet: the remains of one of the largest Pictish forts ever built, burnt down in the 10th century, most likely during a Viking raid.double quotation markThe Moray coast’s fishwives were no idle, passive Penelopes. While the menfolk were at sea, they trudged miles to sell their catchAlong the neighbouring beach of Roseisle, dozens of barnacle-covered concrete blocks and pillboxes are wedged in the sand, half buried, like the ruins of a forgotten civilisation – and resembling the Trojan horse in the movie. But these relics are not ancient; they’re part of a vast defensive frontier from the second world war, when Moray became a secret training ground for the D-day landings. This legacy continues beneath the waves, where several sunken tanks from the wartime exercises can be explored on scuba diving excursions.Heading further east, the cliffs steepen and craggy outcrops have long stirred up stories of metamorphosis. The soaring, 50ft sea-arch of Bow Fiddle Rock, for instance, is a giant that’s fallen beneath the waves, only the edge of his fiddle still visible, and the cries of nesting seabirds are its mournful music.A few miles further on, the ruins of Findlater Castle cling to a narrow promontory. Befitting its turbulent past, from Viking occupation to being besieged by troops loyal to Mary, Queen of Scots, this is where Nolan has the sorceress Circe bewitch Odysseus’ men into swine.View image in fullscreenFindlater Castle, where Christopher Nolan has the sorceresss Circe bewitch Odysseus’ men into swine.Photograph: John Bracegirdle/AlamyThere’s magic on my visit, too, only it erupts from the ocean below. The setting sun is a bronze shield, the sea uncharacteristically calm, when a dozen dorsal fins pierce the surface. Bottlenose dolphins. They’re a regular sight along these shores, with the Moray Firth home to Europe’s most northerly resident pod. Minke whales, humpbacks and orcas sometimes pass through, too, making this one of the UK’s best cetacean-spotting locations.A motor launch scuds across the bay, heading for shore apace as dusk falls. My thoughts turn to homecomings. That, after all, is the essence of The Odyssey, more than monsters or battles. The hero’s yearning to return. His wife Penelope anxiously waiting. Countless women through the centuries have surely done the same from this very clifftop, staring out to sea for a sign of a familiar sail.But a visit toBuckie and District Fishing Heritage Centrethe following day reveals that the Moray coast’s fishwives were no idle, passive Penelopes. While the menfolk were at sea, they baited thousands of hooks by hand, gutted and packed herring, and trudged 13 miles inland to sell their catch.One faded, sepia photograph in the display is unforgettable: it shows the female fishers, barefoot and skirts hitched, carrying their husbands on their backs through the surf, ensuring the men’s boots stayed dry before they boarded their boats. These are, perhaps, the true heroes of these Scottish shores: not a cunning soldier-king, as Homer would have it, but the stalwart, unsung “fisher lassies”, sharing a sea-swell of stories and ballads as they worked.Where to stayThe Odyssey’scast and crew made their base atThe Seafield Armsin Cullen (doubles from £150). “The production team turned our meeting rooms into a working base for editing the film,” says owner Charles Milne, “while our grand ballroom became a private cinema where Christopher Nolan and the crew watched the day’s footage each evening.” Its refurbished self-catering accommodation Bow Cottage, where Matt Damon stayed, has been decked out with ancient Greek-inspired touches and movie memorabilia for visiting fans (sleeps seven from £300 a night, minimum three-night stay.) While you’re in Cullen, browse the shelves atBeinn Booksfor collections of Scottish folklore or perhaps a translation of Homer’s original.How to get thereNational rail services run to Inverness and Aberdeen, with onward train and bus connections to larger coastal towns. Hiring a car is the easiest way to reach its more remote beaches, castles and walking trails.Explore more on these topicsScotland holidaysFilm-inspired holidaysThe OdysseyUnited Kingdom holidaysBeach holidaysfeaturesShareReuse this content

title: ‘Barbecue plus one’: My friend’s genius solution for feeding the masses without sweltering next to the coals | Food | The Guardian
description: Throw one thing on the grill and serve it with one huge bowl of something else – spicy roasted aubergines, rich ratatouille, or a fresh and tangy Greek salad
Author: Rachel Roddy
Headline: Throw one thing on the grill and serve it with one huge bowl of something else – spicy roasted aubergines, rich ratatouille, or a fresh and tangy Greek salad
Content: Quick and easy … Rukmini Iyer's roasted aubergine, chickpeas and pomegranate with a lemon tahini sauce.Photograph: Matthew Hague/The GuardianView image in fullscreenQuick and easy … Rukmini Iyer's roasted aubergine, chickpeas and pomegranate with a lemon tahini sauce.Photograph: Matthew Hague/The GuardianFeastFood‘Barbecue plus one’: My friend’s genius solution for feeding the masses without sweltering next to the coalsThrow one thing on the grill and serve with a huge bowl of something else – from spicy roasted aubergines to a Greek saladSign up here for our weekly food newsletter, FeastRachel RoddyThu 23 Jul 2026 08.00 EDTLast modified on Thu 23 Jul 2026 08.03 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleThe Guardian’s journalism is independent. We will earn a commission if you buy something through an affiliate link.Learn more.While I am glad to be home in Rome, it was particularly hard to leave England this year. After a few days in London, we spent two weeks between Dorset and Devon, in gardens or on the pebble beaches of West Bay, Lyme Regis and Branscombe. Thrashing around in the sea has never felt so necessary. There was also plenty of good eating, the air filled with suggestive scents – of fat dripping on to hot coals, sun cream, insect spray and lavender. Which brings me to the theme of this newsletter: a barbecue plus one.The Guardian’s journalism is independent. We will earn a commission if you buy something through an affiliate link.Learn more.The term is actually borrowed from an Australian friend. It is her solution to feeding lots of people well without too much work or clearing up: one thing on the barbecue (be it sausages, chicken, slices of cheese, kebabs or vegetables) plus one enormous bowl of something else. The solution came about after a particularly chaotic afternoon when her kids were all young, for which she made loads of dishes and compromises, and still didn’t manage to make everyone happy. Clearing up, she thought: “Thats it – next time it is going to be one or two things on the barbecue plus the biggest bowl in the house, filled with one substantial dish.” If I am being precise it should be ’barbecue plus one’, plus pitta bread, so helpful people can stuff the item from the grill, plus the bowl ‘thing’ into a pitta pouch, avoiding the need of a plate, plus sauces in squeezy bottles.The following is her list of suggestions for that plus one, along with some things we made in Branscombe – jollof rice, roasted aubergine with tahini dressing, coleslaw, Greek salad, tabbouleh, ratatouille and caponata.Jollof takes many different forms (and names) depending on the region. Nigerian chef Aji Akokomihas a particularly good version, which includes basmati rice, curry, thyme, ginger, red peppers and fish sauce, and has a crispy, toasty layer that forms on the bottom of the pot. Akokomi explains that jollof rice’s rich, complex combination of sweet, umami and spice goes well with marinated lamb, barbecued carrots and halloumi. Kate Young developed a jollof rice recipe after reading about it inAmericanah by Chimamanda Ngozi Adichie: “She made him the kind of jollof rice he liked, flecked with bits of red and green peppers and, as he ate, fork moving from the plate to his mouth, saying, ‘This is pretty good,’ as he always had in the past, she felt her tears and her questions gathering.”View image in fullscreenA shoo-in for the ‘Big Bowl’ … David Carter’s Greek salad with galomizithra.Photograph: Kate Whitaker/The ObserverThe next suggestion for the “big bowl” is Claudia Roden’sbatinjan bil rumman wal laban, which is grilled aubergine on a base of yoghurt and tahini with pomegranate seeds. Rukmini Iyer also provides us with a quick and easy version of this with herroast aubergines, chickpeas and pomegranate traybake(pictured top). The third suggestion is coleslaw – Felicity Cloake haslots of thoughtsabout that.If you can find tasty cucumbers (the small Lebanese ones are particularly good), great tomatoes and meaty olives, Greek salad is the ticket. Nigel Slater’s recipe ispretty classic, while David Carter’s versionhas real zingfrom the galomizithra, a soft sheep-and-goat’s-milk cheese from Greece’s largest island, while carob rusks add crunch.Most of us visualise tabbouleh as bulgur with specks of herbs – however, in Lebanon it is the other way round, a mass of green with specks of bulgur. At their restaurant Moro, Sam and Samantha Clark sit somewhere in the middle withtheir tabbouleh, which they describe as a mineral tonic of a salad with finely chopped parsley, soaked bulgur, tomatoes, mint and onion, seasoned with olive oil, lemon juice, salt and pepper.Chef Joudie Kalla, whose family comes from Palestine, brings together the many versions eaten andprepares tabbouleh with maftoul, a large-grain couscous which soaks up flavours. She also adds dried mint for sweetness, pomegranate molasses for its sticky sourness, depth, richness and colour, and chilli flakes for heat.I am happy if the bowl is full of ratatouille. Felicity combines aspects from Raymond Blanc, Gui Gedda and Marie-Pierre Moine, and American chef Thomas Keller for herperfect version. Similar in style is Nigel’sclassic recipe, while Adam Byattis inspired by confit byaldi, a ratatouille-variation by French chef Michel Guérard. He arranges slices of aubergine, courgette, onion and tomato in a fallen-domino-like spiral for a dish that “allows all of the vegetables to cook at the same time and melt into each other”.Combining aspects of ratatouille with the sweet and sour dishes so typical in Italy is caponata.My versionis pretty much the recipe from the Anna Tasca Lanza cooking school – a foundation of celery and just a little tomato, plus aubergine that has been fried separately – while Jacob Kenedy reminds us that caponata is as generic a word as soup, as much of a way of cooking and seasoning as it is a dish itself. He provides afantastic templatethat illustrates the elasticity of the dish, listing possible additions: red peppers, ginger, courgettes, chilli flakes, artichoke, raisins, green olives, flaked almonds, pine nuts, dark chocolate, herbs. And finally, my last and equally delicious suggestion for the giant bowl: Meera Sodha’soven-baked caponatais an ideal plus one.If you want to read the complete version of this newsletterplease subscribeto receive Feast in your inbox every Thursday.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |WeeklySign up toFeastRecipes from all our star cooks, seasonal eating ideas and restaurant reviews. Get our best food writing every weekPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionMy week in foodView image in fullscreenSouvenir supper … Some of Rockfish’s excellent tinned fish headed back to Rome with Rachel.Photograph: Ed Ovenden/RockfishPacked like sardines|As well as being a beautifully situated restaurant that shines a light on the diversity of British seafood, Mitch Tonks’s Rockfish in Lyme Regis is anonline fishmonger. You can buy a range of fresh and tinned fish and seafood: British crab meat, British mackerel, escabeche, south coast squid in ink sauce. A tin of sardines came back to Rome with me and will be mixed with spaghetti, thinly sliced fennel, lemon zest and red chilli.Dorsetmeets Rome|We also had a hugely enjoyable, multigenerational evening at theWest Beach Cafe Bar. Now run by Adam Donald and Romla Walker, the cafe serves pizza with bases that remind me of Roman pizza bianca, oblong in shape, the dough tender and cushion-like, baked on semolina, with firm crisp edges and bases. The classic tomato, mozzarella and oregano,andthe spinach, egg, rocket and parmesan stood out.Worththeir salt|Recipes close each chapter in Sudi Pigott’sConsider the Anchovyand they are great: butter and anchovy bagna cauda, braised lamb cacciatore, minced pork balls ‘boles de picolat’, salad niçoise. The chapters themselves – Athens, Sicily, Cantabria, the Basque Country, Costa Brava – hold a fascinating, obsessive and witty memoir-travelogue in search of a small fish with a huge flavour.Explore more on these topicsFoodFeastBarbecueSaladnewslettersShareReuse this content

title: Cool your boots: icy cuppa substitutes for summer | Food | The Guardian
description: Iced tea doesn’t have to be the super sweet, sugar-packed mainstream brands – there are plenty of offerings ready to convert even the staunchest tea purist
Author: Guardian staff reporter
Headline: Iced tea doesn’t have to be the super sweet, sugar-packed mainstream brands – there are plenty of offerings ready to convert even the staunchest tea purist
Content: Legend has it that English tea merchant Richard Blechynden was responsible for popularising iced tea in the American south.Photograph: creacart/Getty ImagesView image in fullscreenLegend has it that English tea merchant Richard Blechynden was responsible for popularising iced tea in the American south.Photograph: creacart/Getty ImagesFoodCool your boots: icy cuppa substitutes for summerIced tea doesn’t have to be the super sweet, sugar-packed mainstream brands – there are plenty of offerings ready to convert even the staunchest tea puristMina HollandThu 23 Jul 2026 08.00 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleThe Guardian’s journalism is independent. We will earn a commission if you buy something through an affiliate link.Learn more.As a very prescriptive tea drinker – piping hot“Stephen Fry” everyday Twinings, thank you, brewed strong with a dash of milk and no sugar under any circumstances– iced tea has always been lost on me. But there’s nothing like an unprecedented heatwave to encourage this sleepless mother to review her position on something as vital as tea.The Guardian’s journalism is independent. We will earn a commission if you buy something through an affiliate link.Learn more.Many of us associate iced tea with the American south, but legend has it that it was actually an English tea merchant who popularised it there. Richard Blechynden met countless visitors who were unwilling to drink hot tea in the summer heat of 1904’sLouisiana Purchase Exposition, so instead he served it over ice. With the American penchant for iced drinks being already well-documented (Mark Twainwrote that if there was one thing that could be called “American”, it was “the national devotion to ice-water”), it took off.Iced tea has evolved in myriad ways, but the southern style, for which black tea is heavily sweetened and often flavoured before serving on ice, reigns supreme in the mainstream. Until recently, I had tried onlyLipton’s iterations, which tick precisely zero boxes for me, mostly because they use only the faintest trace of actual tea (0.12% of black tea extract, to be precise), combined with fruit concentrate (peach, lemon) and abundant sugars and sweeteners. IMHO, something as sweet as that is refreshing only if it’s fizzy.I realised during my testing, however, that I could get on board with the still, sweet iced teas that dominate the market if they had a bit more, well,teaabout them. As well as sugar and peach concentrate, Harry Brompton’s Peach IcedTea(see today’s pick) contains 96% brewed Kenyan black tea, and it really delivers: I can really feel the grip of the tannins. The ChariTea range, also in my pick, uses a similarly high proportion of tea, as well as agave syrup in place of refined sugar and organic fruit juices for flavourings – the Red one, made with rooibos, passionfruit and elderberry juice, is everything I want on a hot day.The handful of sparkling teas I tried are pushing the definition of iced tea a little.Twining’s Refreshdescribes itself as a “juicy raspberry lemonade with hibiscus” (unsurprisingly, I couldn’t taste the 1% white tea infusion), andHolos’ organic Jasmine Hopsnumber is really more a kombucha.Anyone who is overheated and in search of a cuppa substitute, in fact, might best be advised to make their own iced tea, perhaps using Brew Tea Co’s kit in today’s pick, which I brewed hot and served over ice with no milk (obvs) and a little dash of maple syrup. Don’t tell me a girl can’t change.Four iced teas for a hot summer’s dayM&S Peach Iced Tea No Added Sugar£2.10 (750ml) Ocado. White tea infused with real peach juice – still too sweet for my palate but, unlikeSnappleand Lipton’s, it has no added sugars or sweeteners, so take the win.ChariTea Red Iced Tea£1.95 (330ml) Waitrose. An original and moreish blend of South African rooibos and organic passionfruit; a percentage of proceeds goes to social projects in the tea-growing region.Harry Brompton’s Peach Iced Tea£1.25 (500ml; on offer) Sainsbury’s. Satisfyingly tannic and very refreshing. Serve with lemon wedges and a good fan.Brew Tea Co Peach & Orange Iced Tea£12.50. Ceylon tea meets dried peach and citrus rind in a giant bag designed for home infusions, so great for determining strength and sweetness yourself.Explore more on these topicsFoodTeaAmerican food and drinkfeaturesShareReuse this content

title: Rachel Roddy’s ratatouille and potato gratin recipe | Stew | The Guardian
description: The classic Provencal stew, but with potato for ballast and topped with a cheesy herb crumb
Author: Rachel Roddy
Headline: The classic Provencal stew, but with potato for ballast and topped with a cheesy herb crumb
Content: Rachel Roddy’s ratatouille potato gratin.Photograph: Rachel Roddy/The GuardianView image in fullscreenRachel Roddy’s ratatouille potato gratin.Photograph: Rachel Roddy/The GuardianA kitchen in RomeStewRachel Roddy’s ratatouille and potato gratin recipeThe classic Provencal stew, but with potato for ballast and topped with a cheesy herb crumbRachel RoddyThu 23 Jul 2026 01.00 EDTSharePrefer the Guardian on GoogleWe swapped the heatwave and melting pavements in Rome for a similar situation in London, which meant catching a train to Dorset felt something of a relief. Not that it wasn’t hot there too, but it was offset by the sea, a garden and a dark, cool house. The weather also meant that the always good July produce was superb and abundant: all the berries, cherries, peas, runner and french beans; but also potatoes, aubergines, courgettes, tomatoes and herbs – all of which go into this week’s recipe.My template for ratatouille is a mix of Jane Grigson’s recipe in herVegetable Bookand Robert Carrier’s version in Great Dishes of the World, plus the advice from a French friend to cook the aubergine separately, either by deep-frying (by far the tastiest option), baking, or air frying. The benefit of this step is not just flavour; it ensures the aubergine holds its shape better, meaning distinct chunks in the final stew.What I like about Grigson’s recipe is the initial lively simmer (with a lid on), which kickstarts the cooking, followed by an uncovered simmer, then a final livelier simmer so any wateriness disappears. You will have a good idea of how sweet or acidic the tomatoes are from tasting them raw, but taste again towards the end of cooking and, if necessary, add a pinch of sugar or jot of red-wine vinegar.Ratatouille is a Provençal word, which, according to the digital dictionary Orthodidacte, is linked to two terms:tatouiller,which means “to handle repeatedly” or “grope,” as well as “to stir in water’; andratouiller, which means ‘to stir in water,” “mix for a long time,” or “stir up the mud at the bottom of the water”. I am at a loss as to how “grope” can be applied here, but the rest of the terms can be applied in some way to the making of ratatouille, especially the stirring of the mud at the bottom of the water, which is a reminder to swirl the spoon deeply so the vegetables don’t clump and stick to the bottom of the pan.Potatoes are delicious when simmered along with tomatoes until soft, but their addition does turn it into another dish, which is why I have called this ratatouille and potato gratin – the gratin being a topping of soft breadcrumbs, grated cheese, minced herbs and olive oil, which, under the grill, turns into a crusty top.If you are eating straight away, let the dish sit for about 10 minutes, so the juices that have run free are reabsorbed, before serving with sausages, grilled cheese, slices of roast chicken or simply a green salad. While the top will lose some of its crusty edge, the dish is just as good and even more velvety at room temperature.Ratatouille and potato gratinServes41 large aubergine,cut into 2cm chunksOlive oilSalt1 large onion,peeled and sliced1garlic clove,peeled2 courgettes, cut into chunks1 red or yellow pepper,cut into chunks1all purpose large potato,peeled and cut into chunks3big tomatoes,peeled, or 1 x 400g tin tomatoesSugar and red-winevinegar(optional)For the topping100g of soft breadcrumbs2 tbspgrated parmesan2 tbspminced herbs(parsley, thyme, marjoram, say)Either deep-fry the aubergine chunks until golden, or spread them on a baking tray, rub them with oil and salt and bake for 25 minutes, until soft and golden, or use an air fryer. Set aside.In a heavy-based pan, warm four to six tablespoons of olive oil and the onion and garlic. After about five minutes, add the courgettes, peppers and a pinch of salt, and stir. Five minutes later, add the potato and tomato, cover and simmer for 20 minutes.skip past newsletter promotionFree newsletter |WeeklySign up toFeastRecipes from all our star cooks, seasonal eating ideas and restaurant reviews. Get our best food writing every weekPreview latestEnter your emailSign upafter newsletter promotionCheck the amount of liquid and add a little water so that the vegetables are surrounded, then reduce to a simmer for 40-45 minutes, stirring every now and then.Ten minutes before the end of cooking, raise the heat a little so wateriness bubbles away for a dense stew. Then, in the last minutes of cooking, stir in the aubergine and taste for salt, adding a jot of sugar or red-wine vinegar, if you wish. Leave to cool a little.Tip the stew into a roughly 30cm x 20cm rectangular dish. Combine the topping ingredients with some olive oil, then spread the crumbs over the top of the ratatouille and put under the grill for a few minutes, until the top is crusty.Explore more on these topicsStewA kitchen in RomeItalian food and drinkFoodFrench food and drinkVegetablesrecipesShareReuse this content
